We introduce CDER-SME, a cross-device Event-RGB dataset collected under a multi-level stress induction framework (cognitive and social) to elicit spontaneous emotional leakage. To enable reproducible acquisition with independent, decoupled sensors, we provide a hardware-agnostic alignment pipeline for temporal synchronization and landmark-guided spatial registration. CDER-SME adopts a three-tier structure with 92 subjects and 1,963 expert-annotated samples (Action Units and emotions), including 790 Event-RGB pairs and 210 high-fidelity aligned pairs. We further report a reproducible multimodal baseline, where cross-modal fusion improves performance over single-modality counterparts, supporting the complementarity of event dynamics and RGB cues. By removing the need for coaxial calibration, CDER-SME offers a practical benchmark for cross-device alignment and deployable Event-RGB MER in real-world affective intelligence.

</details>


### [26] [Semantic Browsing: Controllable Diversity for Image Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23679)
*Sara Dorfman,Maya Vishnevsky,Omer Dahary,Or Patashnik,Daniel Cohen-Or*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A method for controlled diversity in text-to-image generation that induces structured, semantically meaningful variation at the text level using a VLM agentic workflow, enabling Semantic Browsing of interpretable image galleries.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern text-to-image models suffer from low diversity in generated samples, collapsing into single visual interpretations. Existing diversity methods produce incidental rather than meaningful variations, motivating a need for structured, semantically controlled diversity.

Method: Exploits that modern text-to-image models are trained on elaborated captions, decoupling semantic decision-making from pixel generation. Diversity is induced at the text level (not stochastic latent variation) by using a Vision Language Model (VLM) with an agentic workflow that enforces structured variation attuned to the original prompt.

Result: Produces diverse and navigable design spaces where every variation corresponds to a specific, user-understandable semantic decision.

Conclusion: Shifts paradigm from stochastic latent diversity to controlled text-level semantic variation, enabling Semantic Browsing for creative exploration.

Abstract: Modern text-to-image models excel in visual fidelity and prompt adherence. However, this strict adherence comes at the cost of diversity: generated samples tend to collapse into a single visual interpretation. Existing methods to improve diversity produce outputs driven by incidental variations rather than meaningful design choices. This motivates a new variant of the diversity task where structure is enforced on the generated samples. We introduce a method for controlled diversity that enables Semantic Browsing, where users can navigate structured image galleries and experience creative exploration through a systematic traversal of meaningful, interpretable axes of variation. Achieving this level of semantic control requires a deep understanding of the scene. We exploit the fact that recent text-to-image models are trained on elaborated captions, effectively decoupling semantic decision-making from pixel generation. This enables a paradigm shift: instead of relying on stochastic variation within the text-to-image model, we induce diversity directly at the text level. By leveraging rich textual representations, we allow a Vision Language Model (VLM) to operate on the full scene context. To overcome the generic outputs typical of standard VLMs, we employ an agentic workflow that explicitly enforces structured variation attuned to the original prompt. We demonstrate that our method produces diverse and navigable design spaces where every variation corresponds to a specific, user-understandable semantic decision.

</details>


### [27] [MIRAGE: Stealthy Visual Prompt Injection for Vulnerability Detection in Web Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20717)
*Xuelong Dai,Jianyu Ma,Boyang Ma,Biwei Yan,Yijun Yang,Yue Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MIRAGE is a visual indirect prompt injection framework that uses diffusion models to generate stealthy adversarial images within small, attacker-controlled regions (e.g., ad slots) to hijack the next action of MLLM-based web agents, evaluated against SeeAct and OpenClaw.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing adversarial evaluations for MLLM-based web agents rely on permissive threat models and visually conspicuous artifacts, failing to address realistic constrained scenarios where an attacker controls only a small, semantically legitimate region (e.g., an ad slot) on a trusted web platform.

Method: MIRAGE uses diffusion models to generate perceptually benign adversarial images within attacker-controlled boundaries, combined with a robust optimization technique that incorporates curvature-aware adversarial diffusion guidance and sparse, dark-pixel residual perturbations.

Result: Comprehensive evaluations against SeeAct and OpenClaw empirically demonstrate the potency, realism, and stealth of MIRAGE for targeted next-action hijacking.

Conclusion: MIRAGE exposes a realistic and stealthy vision-based vulnerability in MLLM web agents under constrained threat models, highlighting the need for robust defenses against visual indirect prompt injection from unprivileged third parties.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM)-based web agents provide practical, high-precision solutions for visual browser automation; however, they inherently expand the attack surface, introducing novel vision-based vulnerabilities. Existing adversarial evaluations targeting these agents frequently rely on permissive threat models and visually conspicuous artifacts. In this paper, we investigate a constrained vulnerability detection setting: a trusted web platform where the evaluator acts solely as an unprivileged third party, such as a merchant or advertiser, controlling only a semantically legitimate, spatially constrained region, such as an ad slot, a sponsored card, or a localized widget. Operating under these realistic constraints, we propose MIRAGE, a novel visual indirect prompt injection framework for targeted next-action hijacking. Our approach leverages diffusion models to generate perceptually benign adversarial images strictly confined to the attacker-controlled boundaries permitted by the trusted service provider. To maximize attack efficacy within such a restrictive setting, we introduce a robust optimization technique combining curvature-aware adversarial diffusion guidance with sparse, dark-pixel residual perturbations. Comprehensive evaluations against prominent MLLM web agent frameworks, specifically SeeAct and OpenClaw, empirically demonstrate the potency, realism, and stealth of our proposed MIRAGE.

</details>


### [28] [Evaluation of Medical Vision Language Models HuluMed and MedGemma, and general purpose chatbots Gemma 3, ChatGPT Plus, and Claude Pro on real previously unseen wound images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20723)
*Yunzhe Xue,Mohammed Saim Ahmed Quadri,Neal Panse,Justin W. Ady,Usman Roshan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This study evaluates six Vision-Language Models (VLMs) on clinical wound assessment using 20 diverse wound cases and 240 clinician-graded decisions. ChatGPT (72.50%) and Claude (62.08%) outperformed medically specialized models like HuluMed (40.00%), MedGemma 27B (17.50%), highlighting that general-purpose multimodal reasoning currently surpasses domain-specific alternatives for wound analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Chronic wound assessment is clinically challenging, requiring interpretation of morphology, tissue composition, vascular characteristics, and infection risk. Recent VLMs offer potential for automated multimodal wound analysis, motivating the need to benchmark their performance against clinical standards.

Method: Six VLMs (ChatGPT, Claude, HuluMed, Gemma 3, MedGemma 4B, MedGemma 27B) were evaluated on 20 clinically diverse wound cases (vascular, surgical, ischemic, venous, lymphedema, amputation-related) using a structured 12-question framework covering classification, infection risk, vascular intervention, debridement urgency, therapy selection, and management planning. Performance was measured against 240 clinician-graded decisions.

Result: ChatGPT achieved 174/240 correct (72.50%), Claude 149/240 (62.08%), HuluMed 96/240 (40.00%), Gemma 3 81/240 (33.75%), MedGemma 4B 62/240 (25.83%), MedGemma 27B 42/240 (17.50%).

Conclusion: General-purpose frontier VLMs substantially outperform medically specialized models for wound analysis, emphasizing the value of broad multimodal reasoning. However, current VLMs still exhibit limitations in advanced wound-management reasoning, procedural planning, and autonomous clinical reliability.

Abstract: Chronic wound assessment remains a clinically challenging task that requires accurate interpretation of wound morphology, tissue composition, vascular characteristics, and infection risk. Recent advances in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have introduced the possibility of automated multimodal wound analysis through image understanding combined with clinical reasoning. This study evaluates the performance of several general-purpose and medically specialized open-source and proprietary VLMs for clinical wound assessment using an expanded, curated dataset of 20 clinically diverse wounds spanning vascular, surgical, ischemic, venous, lymphedema, and amputation-related etiologies. Six VLMs were evaluated using a structured twelve-question clinical framework covering wound classification, infection risk, vascular intervention recommendations, debridement urgency, wound therapy selection, and advanced management planning. Across 20 wound cases and 240 clinician-graded wound-analysis decisions, ChatGPT achieved the highest overall performance with 174/240 correct responses (72.50%), followed by Claude with 149/240 (62.08%). Among the open-source and medically specialized models, HuluMed achieved the strongest performance with 96/240 correct responses (40.00%), followed by Gemma 3 (81/240, 33.75%), MedGemma 4B (62/240, 25.83%), and MedGemma 27B (42/240, 17.50%). The findings suggest that frontier general-purpose multimodal systems currently demonstrate substantially stronger wound-analysis performance than medically specialized alternatives, highlighting the continued importance of broad multimodal reasoning capabilities alongside domain-specific medical knowledge. Although current VLMs demonstrate promising potential for clinical decision support, substantial limitations remain in advanced wound-management reasoning, procedural planning, and autonomous clinical reliability.

</details>


### [29] [D2HDMap: Non-visible Driveline Map Prior for Online Vectorized HD Map Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20725)
*Seojun Shon,Chikao Tsuchiya,Dhaval Bhanderi,David Ilstrup,Hsinmin Cheng,Christopher Ostafew*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: D2HDMap is an online mapping system that uses a lightweight, non-visible driveline prior to guide estimation of visible road structures, reducing reliance on costly HD maps while improving generalization and robustness. It achieves 44.8 mAP on geographically disjoint splits and demonstrates resilience to localization noise.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing autonomous vehicle mapping systems rely on either costly, maintenance-heavy HD maps (which compromise safety when outdated) or purely sensor-based online mapping (which struggles with long-range reliability and occlusion). There is a need for a lightweight prior that combines the benefits of both approaches without the overhead of full HD map priors.

Method: D2HDMap injects a lightweight, non-visible driveline prior to guide the estimation of visible road structures (lane dividers, road boundaries, crosswalks). The system uses noise-aware training to increase robustness to realistic localization error, and is trained such that it can generalize to inference scenarios where no prior is available.

Result: On a geographically disjoint split, D2HDMap achieves 44.8 mAP, surpassing recent state-of-the-art. Models trained with a driveline prior largely retain performance even when priors are not available at inference. Noise-aware training substantially increases robustness to realistic localization error.

Conclusion: D2HDMap demonstrates that a lightweight driveline prior can effectively replace heavy HD map priors for online mapping, improving generalization and robustness while reducing creation and maintenance effort. It outperforms prior methods on geographically disjoint data and shows resilience to localization noise through noise-aware training.

Abstract: Accurate, up-to-date representations of road structures are critical for the safe operation of autonomous vehicles. Existing systems rely either on costly, maintenance-heavy high-definition (HD) maps which compromise safety when outdated, or purely sensor-based online mapping which struggles with long-range reliability and occlusion. Systems incorporating map prior information into online mapping seek to overcome drawbacks of both approaches by combining them in some way. We propose 'Driveline To HD Map' (D2HDMap), an online mapping system that injects a lightweight, non-visible driveline prior to guide the estimation of visible road structures such as lane dividers, road boundaries and crosswalks. This prior incurs less effort to create and update compared to full HD map priors used in other approaches. We also show that training with such a prior can improve generalization at inference time when no prior is available. Ablation studies conducted on the nuScenes and Argoverse 2 dataset demonstrate that models trained using a driveline prior largely retain performance even when priors are not available. On a geographically disjoint split, D2HDMap achieves 44.8 mAP, surpassing recent state-of-the-art. Additionally, noise-aware training substantially increases robustness to realistic localization error.

</details>


### [30] [How Well Can Your Video Model Remember? Measuring Memory-Budget Trade-offs in Long Video Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20726)
*Yixian Tian*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A compact empirical law is derived showing that logit-accuracy in long video understanding scales linearly with log-budget, with a distance-dependent exponent α(D) that decays log-linearly with temporal distance D. This exponent quantifies the marginal value of additional frames, revealing large differences across models (α(1000) ranges from 0.17 to 1.26).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: No prior framework predicts how answer accuracy degrades as frame budget shrinks and events recede in long video understanding, despite long-form models operating under strict budget constraints.

Method: Weighted least-squares model fitted on ~155,000 binary predictions across ten models and three sampling strategies. Models logit(accuracy) as linear in log(budget) with an exponent α(D) that decays log-linearly with distance.

Result: Cell-level weighted R² = 0.05–0.75 across models. Budget effectiveness at D=1000s differs ~7.4× between best streaming (α=1.26) and base models (α=0.17). A 10× budget boost yields +29pp for STREAMINGVLM vs +4pp for Qwen3-VL at D=1000s.

Conclusion: α(D) enables principled budget allocation and reveals model-ranking reversals at long distances; proposed as a diagnostic metric for streaming video models.

Abstract: We introduce a compact empirical model that quantifies how answer accuracy degrades as a function of frame budget B and temporal distance D in long video understanding -- analyzing performance when recalling content from D seconds in the past using a fraction B of total frames. Long-form models operate under strict budgets, yet no prior framework predicts how accuracy degrades as B shrinks and events recede. We fit a weighted least-squares model on ~155,000 binary predictions across ten models and three sampling strategies, deriving a law where logit-accuracy scales linearly in log-budget with a distance-dependent exponent that decays log-linearly with distance. This budget exponent α(D) captures the marginal value of extra frames at distance D. The law achieves cell-level weighted R^2 = 0.05-0.75 across models. Notably, budget effectiveness at D = 1000 s differs by \approx 7.4\times between the best streaming and base models. STREAMINGVLM achieves α(1000) = 1.26 (95% CI: [1.06, 1.58]), meaning a tenfold budget increase substantially improves long-distance accuracy, while the best Qwen3-VL base model reaches only α(1000) = 0.17 (CI: [0.04, 0.34]). In accuracy space, a 10\times budget increase at D = 1000 s yields +29 percentage points for STREAMINGVLM versus +4 pp for the base model. Sampling strategies show model-dependent trade-offs: random sampling yields higher base sensitivity but steeper distance decay. We demonstrate how α(D) enables principled budget allocation, including a model-ranking reversal at long distance, and propose it as a diagnostic metric for streaming video models.

</details>


### [31] [VTOS: Learning to Orchestrate Vision Tools by Co-Searching Solutions and Observers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20728)
*Jinchao Ge,Lingqiao Liu,Shuwen Zhao,Lei Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VTOS is a framework that jointly searches for executable vision-tool pipelines and observer programs that diagnose failures, enabling adaptive orchestration of tools like Grounding DINO, SAM, and NMS. It outperforms static pipelines on dense object counting and zero-shot plant-disease segmentation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing visual-programming agents generate fixed solution pipelines that are brittle under dense objects, occlusion, small targets, and domain shift. The authors aim to enable adaptive tool orchestration that can diagnose and recover from failures.

Method: VTOS co-searches solution programs (composing vision tools like Grounding DINO, SAM, NMS, slice-and-detect) and observer programs that diagnose candidate solutions, identify failure modes, and generate actionable feedback. Observations are accumulated in a shared VisionThoughts knowledge base to guide subsequent search.

Result: VTOS outperforms static tool pipelines and agentic visual-programming baselines on dense object counting (LVIS-Count) and zero-shot plant-disease segmentation (PlantSeg-OOD).

Conclusion: Co-searching solutions and observers is an effective strategy for adapting vision tools to challenging computer vision tasks with varying visual conditions.

Abstract: Vision foundation tools such as open-vocabulary detectors, segmentation models, and post-processing operators are powerful building blocks for computer vision, but their effectiveness depends heavily on how they are orchestrated: which tools are used, in what order, with what parameters, and under what visual conditions. Existing visual-programming agents typically generate a fixed solution pipeline, making them brittle under dense objects, occlusion, small targets, and domain shift. We introduce VTOS (Vision Tools Orchestration Search), a framework for adaptive visual tool orchestration through joint solution--observer search. VTOS co-searches executable solution programs that compose vision tools such as Grounding DINO, SAM, NMS, and slice-and-detect, together with observer programs that diagnose candidate solutions, identify failure modes, and generate actionable feedback. These observations are accumulated in a shared VisionThoughts knowledge base to guide subsequent search. We evaluate VTOS through two case studies: dense object counting on LVIS-Count and zero-shot plant-disease segmentation on PlantSeg-OOD, which stress different orchestration challenges including threshold calibration, NMS, slicing, mask refinement, and domain generalization. Across both tasks, VTOS outperforms static tool pipelines and agentic visual-programming baselines, showing that co-searching solutions and observers is an effective strategy for adapting vision tools to challenging computer vision tasks.

</details>


### [32] [XmoPipe: A Pipeline for Large-Scale In-the-Wild Human Motion Dataset Construction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20731)
*Nathan Salazar,Emmanuel Dellandréa,Mathieu Lefort,Alexandre Meyer*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper presents a scalable pipeline that automatically constructs large-scale in-the-wild human motion datasets from online videos by retrieving, extracting 3D body and facial motions, and generating textual descriptions, achieving performance comparable to models trained on traditional marker-based motion capture data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional marker-based motion capture is costly and limited in scale and diversity, creating a need for more scalable and diverse human motion datasets to train robust motion models.

Method: A scalable pipeline that retrieves videos from keywords, extracts 3D body and facial motion using monocular motion capture, and generates high-level textual descriptions, enabling targeted collection of diverse motions, multi-person interactions, or expressive behaviors.

Result: Motion reconstruction and generation models trained on the resulting dataset achieve performance comparable to those trained on traditional motion capture datasets, with strong cross-dataset generalization.

Conclusion: Monocular motion capture from unconstrained online videos can effectively replace costly marker-based systems for building large-scale, diverse human motion datasets without sacrificing model quality.

Abstract: Large-scale human motion datasets are essential for training robust motion models for analysis, synthesis, and understanding. While marker-based motion capture provides precise data, it is costly and limited in scale and diversity. Recent advances in monocular motion capture and video-language understanding open the way to extract plausible motion from unconstrained online videos. We present a scalable pipeline for constructing in-the-wild human motion datasets. From a few keywords, the system retrieves videos, extracts 3D body and facial motion, and generates high-level textual descriptions. The pipeline is flexible, enabling targeted collection of various motions, multi-person interactions, or expressive behaviors. We demonstrate its quality by training motion reconstruction and motion generation models, showing performance comparable to models trained on traditional motion capture datasets and strong cross-dataset generalization.

</details>


### [33] [Robust Zero-Shot Generalization for Open-Vocabulary Action Recognition via Task Arithmetic](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20734)
*Francesca Morandi,Omayma Moussadek,Federico Venturini,Mauro Suardi,Alessandro Banzatti,Francesco Cannarile,Angelo Porrello,Simone Calderara*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a zero-shot Open Vocabulary Action Recognition (OVAR) method that uses model merging and task arithmetic to combine knowledge from multiple fine-tuned models on public datasets, achieving superior out-of-distribution generalization without domain-specific fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional OVAR requires costly domain-specific fine-tuning for real-world scenarios, which raises privacy and regulatory concerns. The authors aim to bypass target-domain training by recombining knowledge from existing public datasets and models.

Method: The method leverages model merging and task arithmetic to extract and combine task vectors from models fine-tuned on diverse public OVAR datasets, creating a merged model without requiring target-domain training.

Result: The merged model achieves superior zero-shot generalization in out-of-distribution settings compared to the pre-trained base model.

Conclusion: Model merging and task arithmetic provide an effective alternative to domain-specific fine-tuning for OVAR, enabling robust zero-shot generalization while avoiding costly training and privacy concerns.

Abstract: Open Vocabulary Action Recognition (OVAR) enables the recognition of novel actions by leveraging vision-language representations, overcoming the limitations of traditional closed-set approaches. However, achieving robust performance in real-world scenarios typically requires domain-specific fine-tuning, which is often costly and raises privacy and regulatory concerns. In this work, we propose an alternative paradigm that bypasses target-domain training and recombines knowledge from existing datasets and models. Leveraging model merging and task arithmetic, we extract and combine task vectors from models fine-tuned on diverse public OVAR datasets. We show that, in out-of-distribution settings, the resulting merged model achieves superior zero-shot generalization to the pre-trained base model. Code is available at https://github.com/omaymaMoussadek/robust-ovar

</details>


### [34] [REKEY: Metadata-Grounded Visual-Key Regeneration for Contamination-Resilient VQA Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20736)
*Tengjie Lin,Yutao Sun,Jingwei Ni,Shuhan Ge,Hao-Xuan Ma,Yanting Miao,Wangyue Lu,Mingshuai Chen,Tiancheng Zhao,Jianwei Yin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ReKey is a live benchmark protocol that regenerates visual keys in images at evaluation time to prevent VQA benchmark leakage, revealing that current VLMs score 9.5-18.8% higher on leaked static benchmarks than on fresh regenerated variants.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Static VQA benchmarks suffer from data leakage where items become memorized rather than testing genuine visual ability, and rebuilding high-quality benchmarks like V*Bench is expensive and still results in static releases that can be leaked again.

Method: ReKey uses human-validated edit slots to randomly regenerate the answer-bearing local detail (visual key) in real images at evaluation time, producing fresh instances with new answers, construction-grounded labels, and controlled visual-search difficulty.

Result: On V*Bench, original leaked items score 9.5-18.8 percentage points higher than ReKey's regenerated variants across eight frontier vision-language models, demonstrating that static benchmarks overestimate true VLM visual ability.

Conclusion: ReKey keeps evaluation fresh as models and training data evolve by making the visual key renewable, providing a more reliable measure of genuine visual ability in VLMs.

Abstract: Static visual question answering (VQA) benchmarks age quickly: Once the items leak into training corpora, scores can reflect memorization rather than genuine visual ability, thus obscuring real progress. Rebuilding high-quality benchmarks such as V*Bench requires substantial human annotation, yet each static release can quickly become another leaked artifact. We propose ReKey, a live benchmark protocol that randomly regenerates the answer-bearing local detail, or visual key, in real images at evaluation time. Using human-validated edit slots, ReKey samples fresh instances with new answers, construction-grounded labels, and controlled visual-search difficulty. On V*Bench, the ReKey regenerated benchmark reveals a sharp score jump across eight frontier vision-language models (VLMs): The original items score 9.5--18.8 percentage points higher than the regenerated variants. By making the visual key renewable, ReKey keeps evaluation fresh as models and training data evolve.

</details>


### [35] [An approach with Visual and Tabular Mamba to multimodal medical data using Mixed Fusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20738)
*Matheus B. Rocha,Gustavo B. Dettogni,Renato A. Krohling*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a multimodal medical data fusion approach for cancer classification using Mamba-based state space models. A Mixed Fusion architecture combines a vision Mamba (for lesion image classification) with a tabular Mamba (integrating image probabilities with clinical/sociodemographic data). Evaluated on PAD-UFES-20 (skin lesions) and NDB-UFES (oral cancer), the method shows slightly lower balanced accuracy than Transformers on the former but superior performance on the latter, with notable recall gains and enhanced interpretability via SHAP.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop an interpretable multimodal fusion framework for cancer classification that leverages state space models (Mamba) as an alternative to Transformer-based approaches, particularly in scenarios where recall/sensitivity is critical.

Method: A Mixed Fusion architecture using two Mamba variants: one for visual processing (classifying lesion images) and another for tabular processing (combining image class probabilities with clinical/sociodemographic data for final diagnosis). SHAP is used for interpretability.

Result: Slightly lower balanced accuracy than Transformers on PAD-UFES-20; superior performance on NDB-UFES; substantial gains in recall metric; enhanced interpretability via SHAP.

Conclusion: Mamba-based state space models are a suitable alternative for multimodal medical data classification, especially when sensitivity (recall) is a key requirement.

Abstract: This article presents a complementary approach for integrating multimodal medical data in cancer classification, based on state space models represented by the Mamba architecture. To this end, a mixed multimodal fusion architecture, called Mixed Fusion, was employed and developed to enhance the interpretability of the decision-making process. The proposed approach explores two variants of Mamba: one dedicated to visual processing, responsible for classifying the lesion image and generating probabilities associated with the target classes, and another focused on tabular processing, which uses these probabilities together with clinical and/or sociodemographic data to produce the final diagnosis. The experiments were conducted on two medical datasets: PAD-UFES-20, composed of clinical images and information associated with skin lesions, and NDB-UFES, consisting of histopathological images and sociodemographic data related to oral cancer. The results indicate slightly lower performance in balanced accuracy, compared with Transformer-based approaches, on PAD-UFES-20, and superior performance on NDB-UFES. Additionally, substantial gains were observed in the recall metric. Furthermore, the adoption of the Mixed Fusion architecture enables the application of the Shapley Additive Explanations (SHAP) method, increasing the interpretability of the results. These findings indicate that Mamba-based models constitute a suitable alternative for multimodal classification in medical data, especially in scenarios in which sensitivity is a relevant requirement.

</details>


### [36] [Mirage: a Clean-Label Backdoor against LiDAR 3D Object Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20752)
*Ziba Parsons,Ang Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Mirage is a black-box, clean-label backdoor attack against LiDAR 3D object detection models, achieving 73% misclassification with only 0.5% poisoning rate.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep neural network-based LiDAR 3D object detection is vulnerable to backdoor attacks, but existing attacks require white-box access or label modification and focus on geometric attacks.

Method: Mirage injects a small number of label-consistent poisoning samples into the training set, causing the model to learn a malicious association between a trigger pattern and an attacker-chosen target class while preserving normal training semantics.

Result: 73% misclassification success rate with a poisoning rate of only 0.5%, while maintaining detection performance close to benign models.

Conclusion: Mirage demonstrates a practical black-box backdoor attack on LiDAR 3DOD that is effective with minimal poisoning and without label modification.

Abstract: Deep neural network-based LiDAR 3D object detection serves as a critical perception component in safety-critical autonomous systems. However, recent studies have revealed its vulnerability to backdoor attacks. Existing attacks typically require white-box access or label modification and focus on geometric attacks such as object disappearance or bounding-box manipulation. In this paper, we present Mirage, a black-box and clean-label backdoor attack against deep neural network-based LiDAR 3DOD. Mirage injects a small number of label-consistent poisoning samples into the training set, causing the model to learn a malicious association between a trigger pattern and an attacker-chosen target class while preserving normal training semantics. As a result, the compromised model behaves normally on benign inputs yet systematically misclassifies triggered objects as the target class during deployment. We evaluate Mirage on multiple state-of-the-art LiDAR 3DOD models and benchmark datasets. Experimental results show that Mirage achieves a 73% misclassification success rate with a poisoning rate of only 0.5%, while maintaining detection performance close to that of benign models.

</details>


### [37] [UniSLAD: A Unified Framework for Structural and Logical Industrial Visual Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20768)
*Changyi Li,Chao Yang,Yu Xiao,Kari Tammi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: UniSLAD is a unified, training-free framework that jointly detects both structural and logical anomalies by combining CNN and Transformer dual-feature extraction with dual-granularity feature representation (patch-level Mahalanobis Transform memory banks and image-level Lower-Upper Mean & Power Mean Pooling), achieving 99.4% and 93.1% on two industrial benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While structural anomaly detection has progressed, logical anomaly detection remains underexplored, and in practice both types co-occur. Existing solutions lack a unified approach to handle both simultaneously, motivating a single framework capable of comprehensive anomaly detection.

Method: The method, UniSLAD, has two main components: (1) a dual-feature extractor combining a CNN backbone (local texture perception) with a Transformer backbone (global contextual reasoning), and (2) dual-granularity feature representation modules—patch-level memory banks enhanced by Mahalanobis Transform for discriminative scoring, and image-level distribution maps aggregated via Lower-Upper Mean (LUM) and Power Mean Pooling (PMP) for robust global representation. The entire framework operates without additional training.

Result: On two industrial benchmarks, UniSLAD achieves 99.4% and 93.1% in comprehensive anomaly detection, demonstrating competitive performance. Ablation studies confirm the individual contributions of each proposed component.

Conclusion: UniSLAD effectively addresses both structural and logical anomaly detection in a unified, training-free manner, providing a practical solution for dynamic industrial environments and advancing comprehensive anomaly detection research.

Abstract: Visual anomaly detection is a fundamental task in industrial automation. While existing approaches have achieved notable progress in identifying structural defects, the detection of logical anomalies remains relatively underexplored. In practice, structural and logical anomalies frequently co-occur in industrial workflows. Therefore, a solution capable of detecting both structural and logical anomalies is crucial for advancing comprehensive anomaly detection research. To address this limitation, we propose a unified framework, termed UniSLAD, which jointly addresses logical and structural anomalies without additional training, enabling a practical solution for dynamic industrial environments. First, we introduce a dual-feature extractor that synergistically integrates a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) backbone for local texture perception with a Transformer backbone for global contextual reasoning, yielding richer and more comprehensive representations. Building on this foundation, we design dual-granularity feature representation modules. At the patch level, memory banks enhanced by the Mahalanobis Transform (MT) preserve representative features and support more discriminative anomaly scoring. At the image level, distribution maps are aggregated using Lower-Upper Mean (LUM) and Power Mean Pooling (PMP), yielding a more robust global representation than conventional average pooling. Extensive experiments on the two industrial benchmarks demonstrate that UniSLAD achieves competitive performance in comprehensive anomaly detection, achieving 99.4% and 93.1%, respectively. Furthermore, ablation studies verify the individual contributions and effectiveness of each proposed component.

</details>


### [38] [TriMotion: Modality-Agnostic Camera Control for Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20774)
*Seunghyun Shin,Jifei Song,Wooseok Jeon,Hae-Gon Jeon,Jiankang Deng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TriMotion is a modality-agnostic framework for camera-controlled video generation that maps video, pose, and text inputs describing the same camera trajectory into a shared motion embedding space, using a Motion Triplet Dataset and latent motion consistency objective for training, achieving high-quality generation across modalities with flexible applications like motion composition and interpolation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for camera motion control in generative systems are limited to conditioning on a single modality (e.g., pose trajectories or reference videos), which restricts support for heterogeneous user inputs.

Method: TriMotion introduces: (1) a modality-agnostic framework mapping video, pose, and text inputs into a shared motion embedding space; (2) a Motion Triplet Dataset built from Multi-Cam Video Dataset with geometry-grounded motion descriptions from camera extrinsics; (3) a latent motion consistency objective for efficient training without pixel-space decoding.

Result: TriMotion generates high-quality videos that accurately follow target camera trajectories across all three modalities (video, pose, text), and enables applications like sequential motion composition and cross-modal motion interpolation.

Conclusion: A shared motion embedding space across modalities effectively addresses the single-modality limitation, enabling flexible camera-controlled video generation and novel applications.

Abstract: Camera motion control is essential for directing viewpoint changes in generative systems. However, existing methods typically condition the generation process on a single specific modality, such as explicit pose trajectories or reference videos, limiting their ability to support heterogeneous user inputs. To address this limitation, we present TriMotion, a modality-agnostic framework for camera-controlled video generation that maps video, pose, and text inputs, describing the same camera trajectory into a shared motion embedding space. Learning such a space requires synchronized supervision across modalities. Therefore, we build the Motion Triplet Dataset by extending a Multi-Cam Video Dataset with geometry-grounded motion descriptions derived from camera extrinsics. We further introduce a latent motion consistency objective that leverages the motion embedding space to encourage the generated video to follow the target camera trajectory directly in latent space, avoiding the cost of pixel-space decoding. Extensive experiments show that TriMotion generates high-quality videos that accurately follow the target camera trajectories across all three modalities. Beyond standard generation, the shared motion embedding space also enables flexible applications such as sequential motion composition and cross-modal motion interpolation.

</details>


### [39] [GroundShot: Visually Consistent Multi-Shot Long Video Generation via Entity-Grounded Shot Scheduling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20799)
*Yixuan Lai,Tianjia Shao,Kun Zhou,Weijia Dou,Siyu Zhu,Jingdong Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GroundShot is a training-free, model-agnostic framework that improves multi-shot video consistency by building an online entity-level visual memory to schedule, ground, verify, and retrieve entity references across shots, evaluated on the new GroundBench diagnostic benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-shot video generation suffers from accumulated inconsistencies across shots, causing entities (characters, objects, locations) to drift from their initial appearances; existing methods lack entity-centered consistency management.

Method: GroundShot operates as an agentic framework that: (1) schedules shot generation order by expected usefulness as entity references, (2) grounds entities from generated videos, (3) verifies entity reliability before adding to memory, and (4) retrieves suitable entity references from memory before each shot generation — all without training or model modification.

Result: GroundShot improves multi-shot consistency over existing methods on the proposed GroundBench benchmark, which measures entity-level consistency across controlled challenge dimensions.

Conclusion: Entity-grounded visual memory with online scheduling, verification, and retrieval significantly enhances multi-shot video consistency without requiring additional training or architectural changes.

Abstract: Generating visually consistent multi-shot videos remains an open challenge. As videos span more shots, inconsistencies can accumulate across shots, causing entities that reappear across shots -- characters, objects, and locations -- to drift away from how they first appear. We observe that viewers judge consistency by comparing each later appearance of an entity with its first clear appearance; the visual quality of this initial appearance sets the consistency ceiling for all that follows. Motivated by this, we present \textbf{GroundShot}, a training-free, model-agnostic agentic framework for entity-grounded multi-shot generation. GroundShot builds an entity-level visual memory online from accepted generated shots: it schedules shots' generation order by their expected usefulness as entity references, grounds entities from generated videos, verifies their reliability before adding them to memory, and retrieves suitable entity references from memory before each shot is generated. To evaluate this entity-centered view of consistency, we further introduce \textbf{GroundBench}, a diagnostic benchmark that measures consistency at the entity level while isolating controlled challenge dimensions. Experiments show that GroundShot improves multi-shot consistency over existing methods while requiring no additional training or model modification.

</details>


### [40] [NeoLoc-68: End-to-end 68-point neonatal facial landmark localisation in neonatal clinical environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20823)
*Abdullah Bin-Obaid,Maria M. Cobo,Rebeccah Slater,Lionel Tarassenko,Mauricio Villarroel*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes the first end-to-end 68-point facial landmark detector specifically designed for neonatal faces in clinical environments, adapting a YOLO-based keypoint model trained on a large combined dataset (37,459 public images + 1,123 manually annotated clinical frames). It achieves state-of-the-art performance on public benchmarks and strong generalisation on clinical neonatal data, with further improvements after fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing facial landmark detectors trained on adult faces perform poorly on neonates in clinical settings due to occlusions from medical equipment, varied head poses, and motion blur from sudden pain-related movements. Reliable neonatal landmark detection is needed for automated, non-contact pain assessment.

Method: Combined 37,459 single-face images from 11 public datasets (standardised to 68-point markup) with 1,123 manually annotated frames from a neonatal research dataset. Adapted a YOLO-based keypoint model initialised with weights from a pretrained neonatal face detector to regress 68 facial landmarks.

Result: On public datasets: NME=5.37, FR=12.5%, AUC0.08=38.00%, AUC0.1=48.70%. On clinical neonatal test set before fine-tuning: DFR=5.3% (lowest among baselines). After fine-tuning: NME=6.36, FR=22.30%, DFR=1.77%, AUC0.08=29.24%, AUC0.1=40.25%.

Conclusion: This is the first end-to-end 68-point neonatal facial landmark detection model. With further dataset expansion and refinement, it could support downstream tasks in neonatal health monitoring and pain-related facial analysis.

Abstract: Facial landmark localisation is a prerequisite for developing automated, non-contact neonatal pain assessment methods. Clinicians use pain scales to judge the severity of pain, many of which rely on facial expression. However, facial landmark detectors trained on adult faces perform poorly in neonatal clinical environments due to frequent occlusions caused by medical equipment, varied head poses, and challenging imaging conditions, including motion blur triggered by sudden pain-related movements. We propose an end-to-end facial landmark detector capable of predicting 68 landmarks on neonatal faces in clinical environments. We combined 37,459 single-face images from 11 public datasets, standardised to 68-point markup, with 1,123 manually annotated frames from a neonatal research dataset (totalling over 76,000 landmarks). A YOLO-based keypoint model was adapted to regress the facial landmarks, initialised with weights from a pretrained neonatal face detector. On public datasets, our proposed model achieved state-of-the-art performance: Normalised Mean Error (NME) = 5.37, Failure Rate (FR) = 12.5%, Area Under the Cumulative Error Curve (AUC) at AUC0.08 = 38.00% and AUC0.1 = 48.70%. On the clinical neonatal test set, before fine-tuning, the model achieved the lowest Detection Failure Rate (DFR) = 5.3% among all baselines and showed strong generalisation. After fine-tuning, performance improved further to NME = 6.36, FR = 22.30%, DFR = 1.77%, AUC0.08 = 29.24% and AUC0.1 = 40.25%. To the best of our knowledge, this represents the first end-to-end 68-point neonatal facial landmark detection model. With further dataset expansion and refinement, it could support downstream tasks in neonatal health monitoring and pain-related facial analysis.

</details>


### [41] [From Uncertainty to Stability and Fidelity: Guiding Sparse-View 3D Gaussian Splatting with Fisher Information](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20842)
*Junbao Zhou,Qingshan Xu,Yuan Zhou,Xiaolong Shen,Beier Zhu,Kesen Zhao,Yiming Zeng,Chen Bai,Cheng Lu,Hanwang Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes a sparse-view 3D Gaussian Splatting method that uses Fisher Information to guide stereo augmentation (selecting informative supporting views) and uncertainty-aware regularization (adjusting Gaussian removal probabilities), mitigating overfitting and achieving state-of-the-art rendering fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) requires dense input views for high-quality rendering; in sparse-view scenarios it overfits, producing artifacts. Existing methods use random priors or random Dropout without principled guidance, introducing uncertainty and instability.

Method: Two components: (1) Stereo augmentation with Fisher Information — actively selects the most informative supporting views and leverages depth priors for reliable pseudo ground truths. (2) Uncertainty-aware regularization — uses Fisher Information to measure each Gaussian's uncertainty and adaptively adjusts its removal probability during Dropout-based regularization.

Result: The method achieves state-of-the-art performance on sparse-view novel view synthesis benchmarks, with superior rendering fidelity and reduced overfitting.

Conclusion: Fisher Information provides a principled way to guide both augmentation and regularization in sparse-view 3DGS, improving stability and rendering quality.

Abstract: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has emerged as a promising technique for novel view synthesis. However, 3DGS requires dense input views to achieve high-quality rendering. In sparse-view scenarios, 3DGS often prones to overfitting, resulting in noticeable artifacts and degraded rendering quality. Previous methods explore to address this issue by introducing additional priors (e.g. depth priors) or integrating regularization techniques (e.g. Dropout). However, these methods are often applied without principled guidance. In particular, prior-based augmentation typically samples novel viewpoints randomly, while Dropout-based regularization randomly removes Gaussians. The compounded randomness introduces uncertainty and instability, limiting the fidelity of novel view synthesis. In this paper, we propose a novel method for sparse-view 3DGS that incorporates Fisher Information to quantitatively guide the utilization of geometric priors and regularization. Specifically, our method comprises two key components: (1) Stereo augmentation with Fisher Information. By leveraging Fisher Information, we actively select most informative supporting views and use depth priors to curate reliable pseudo ground truths, which reduces randomness in augmentation and improves stability and rendering fidelity; (2) Uncertainty-aware regularization. We reduce the instability of Dropout-based regularization by using Fisher Information to quantitatively measure the uncertainty of each 3D Gaussian, and adaptively adjust the removal probability, leading to more stable and effective regularization. With these two components, our method effectively mitigates overfitting and improves the stability of optimization in sparse-view 3DGS, resulting in superior rendering fidelity. Extensive experiments show that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance in sparse-view novel view synthesis benchmarks.

</details>


### [42] [Translating Inference-Time Control to Radiology Vision-Language Models: Activation Steering for Pneumonia Classification on Chest X-rays](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20852)
*Eduardo Moreno Judice de Mattos Farina,Mateus A. Esmeraldo,Felipe Akio Matsuoka,Paulo Eduardo de Aguiar Kuriki,Felipe Campos Kitamura*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CAA (Contrastive Activation Addition) can improve pneumonia classification in chest radiograph VLMs without fine-tuning, with meaningful gains in one of three models tested.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To evaluate whether inference-time activation steering (CAA) can improve diagnostic performance in medical vision-language models without updating model weights, addressing the need for lightweight adaptation methods.

Method: Three frozen chest radiograph VLMs (MedGemma-4B-IT, NV-Reason-CXR-3B, CheXOne-3B) were evaluated on the Kermany pneumonia test set. Classification used logits of Yes/No tokens under binary prompts. Steering vectors included answer-bias control, pneumonia text contrast, and image-conditioned contrast from 30 positive/negative samples. A 200-image development set was used for layer/scale selection (100 images) and threshold calibration (100 images). Performance was assessed via ROC-AUC, PR-AUC, F1, threshold analyses, reverse/random-vector controls, and conditional bootstrap confidence intervals.

Result: NV-Reason-CXR-3B showed strongest benefit: calibrated F1 improved from 0.7692 (zero-shot) to 0.8619 (pneumonia-text steering) and 0.8727 (image-conditioned steering). CheXOne-3B showed modest improvement (F1: 0.8528→0.8666) but confidence intervals crossed zero. Fixed-threshold F1 improvements were common but did not consistently indicate better diagnostic performance.

Conclusion: CAA substantially alters prediction score distributions and operating characteristics without fine-tuning. Meaningful performance gains were observed in one of three evaluated VLMs, suggesting activation steering may serve as a lightweight approach for adapting medical VLM behavior, though benefits are model-dependent.

Abstract: Inference-time engineering can alter model behavior without fine-tuning. However, its utility for improving diagnostic performance in medical vision-language models (VLMs) remains unclear. We aim to evaluate whether Contrastive Activation Addition (CAA) can improve pneumonia classification in chest radiograph VLMs without updating model weights. Three frozen chest radiograph VLMs (MedGemma-4B-IT, NV-Reason-CXR-3B, and CheXOne-3B) were evaluated on the public Kermany pneumonia test set. Classification was based on the logits of the tokens Yes and No under a binary prompt. Steering vectors included a 30-pair answer-bias control, a 30-pair pneumonia text contrast, and an image-conditioned contrast derived from 30 pneumonia and 30 normal development images. A deterministic 200-image development set was used for layer and scale selection (100 images) and threshold calibration (100 images). Performance was assessed using ROC-AUC, PR-AUC, F1 score, threshold analyses, reverse-vector controls, random-vector controls, and conditional bootstrap confidence intervals. Fixed-threshold F1 improvements were frequently observed but did not consistently indicate improved diagnostic performance. For MedGemma-4B-IT. NV-Reason-CXR-3B showed the strongest benefit: calibrated F1 improved from 0.7692 in the zero-shot setting to 0.8619 with pneumonia-text steering and to 0.8727 with image-conditioned steering. For CheXOne-3B, pneumonia-text steering increased calibrated F1 from 0.8528 to 0.8666, although the confidence interval crossed zero. On this public pneumonia benchmark, CAA substantially altered prediction score distributions and operating characteristics without fine-tuning. Meaningful performance gains were observed in one of three evaluated VLMs, suggesting that activation steering may serve as a lightweight approach for adapting medical VLM behavior.

</details>


### [43] [FOCA: Future-Oriented Conditioning for Data-Efficient Vision-Language-Action Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20867)
*Duc Minh Nguyen,Nghiem Tuong Diep,Binh Gia Nguyen,Trong-Bao Ho,Doanh Le,Tan Q. Nguyen,Thien-Loc Ha,Nhiem Tran,Bao Thach,Nhat X. Tran,Tuan A. Tran,Artur Habuda,Philip Lund Møller,Tran Nguyen Le,Daniel Sonntag,Matthias Niepert,Khoa D. Doan,Vu Duong,Hung Ngo,Minh N. Vu,Duy M. H. Nguyen,An Thai Le,Ngo Anh Vien*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FOCA is a future-oriented conditioning framework that improves few-shot VLA adaptation by combining explicit future interaction prediction with implicit goal alignment, achieving state-of-the-art results with very few demonstrations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLA models suffer from sharp performance degradation under few-shot imitation learning settings, revealing a key weakness in current adaptation strategies that limits their practical deployment.

Method: FOCA combines explicit prediction of task-grounded future interaction embeddings with implicit alignment to future goal observations, enabling long-horizon reasoning in latent space without pixel-level prediction. It also supports action-free co-training with synthetic videos from world models, interpreted as learning a future-conditioned value-like representation.

Result: 95.7% success with 20 demonstrations on LIBERO, 7-12% improvement on RoboCasa, and up to 26% absolute gains on real robots, establishing state-of-the-art in few-shot VLA adaptation.

Conclusion: FOCA effectively addresses the few-shot limitation of VLA models by leveraging future-oriented conditioning, significantly improving data efficiency and enabling practical robot learning with minimal demonstrations.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models enable general-purpose robotic control via large-scale multimodal pretraining, yet their effectiveness under few-shot imitation learning remains limited. We conduct a systematic stress test of state-of-the-art VLA models and show that performance degrades sharply as demonstrations are reduced, revealing a key weakness of existing adaptation strategies. To address this, we introduce FOCA, a future-oriented conditioning framework for data-efficient VLA adaptation. FOCA combines explicit prediction of task-grounded future interaction embeddings with implicit alignment to future goal observations, enabling long-horizon reasoning in latent space without pixel-level prediction. This formulation naturally supports action-free co-training with synthetic videos from video world models and can be interpreted as learning a future-conditioned value-like representation. Extensive experiments demonstrate FOCA achieves 95.7% success with 20 demonstrations on LIBERO, improves 7-12% on RoboCasa, and delivers up to 26% absolute gains on real robots, establishing a new state of the art in few-shot VLA adaptation.

</details>


### [44] [Toward Parking Spot Occupancy Recognition: A Self-Supervised Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20886)
*Luan Marko Kujavski,Rayson Laroca,Paulo Lisboa de Almeida*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A self-supervised approach for parking spot occupancy recognition that requires no labeled target data, using a two-stage self-supervised transfer learning protocol (SimCLR + ResNet-50), achieving 97.2% accuracy (97.8% with two-stage deployment) across three public datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable scalable and label-efficient parking occupancy monitoring in urban areas without requiring labeled samples from each target parking lot.

Method: A self-supervised transfer learning fine-tuning protocol with two self-supervised stages (on unlabeled generic data then unlabeled target-specific data), followed by supervised fine-tuning using only generic parking lot labels. Uses SimCLR with ResNet-50 encoder. Evaluated under leave-one-out cross-environment on PKLot, CNRPark-EXT, and PLds. Includes a two-stage deployment strategy: Strong General Model first, then Specialized Model fine-tuned with unlabeled target data from first N days.

Result: Strong General Model achieves 97.2% average accuracy, outperforming supervised and self-supervised baselines. Two-stage strategy further improves to 97.8%.

Conclusion: Self-supervised learning enables a scalable and label-efficient solution for real-world parking occupancy monitoring.

Abstract: As urban areas expand, automatic monitoring of parking lots becomes essential for efficient and sustainable cities. This work proposes a self-supervised approach for parking spot occupancy recognition that requires no labeled samples from the target parking lot. Building upon a self-supervised transfer learning fine-tuning protocol, the proposed training strategy consists of two self-supervised stages: first on unlabeled generic data and then on unlabeled target-specific data, followed by supervised fine-tuning using only generic parking lot labels. We adopt SimCLR with a ResNet-50 encoder and evaluate the method under a leave-one-out cross-environment protocol on three public datasets: PKLot, CNRPark-EXT, and PLds. We also introduce a two-stage deployment strategy in which a Strong General Model is initially deployed, followed by a Specialized Model that incorporates unlabeled images collected during the first N days of deployment in a self-supervised manner. Experimental results show that the Strong General Model alone outperforms supervised and self-supervised baselines, achieving an average accuracy of 97.2%, which further improves to 97.8% with the proposed two-stage strategy. These results demonstrate that self-supervised learning enables a scalable and labelefficient solution for real-world parking occupancy monitoring. Our trained models and source code are publicly available at https://github.com/LoanMaikon/Parking-Spot-Occupancy-Recognition.

</details>


### [45] [Fine-grained Human Motion Understanding with Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20888)
*Thomas Markhorst,Zhi-Yi Lin,Jouh Yeong Chew,Jan van Gemert,Xucong Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes an LLM-based model for fine-grained human motion understanding that uses explicit temporal encoding of skeletal poses with timestamps, achieving SOTA across multiple benchmarks with diverse pose- and motion-level supervision, and supporting both 2D and 3D inputs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable fine-grained human motion understanding (reasoning about motion order, duration, rhythm) which requires explicit temporal grounding of poses, and to study what supervision is needed for motion-language reasoning beyond existing methods that rely on ground-truth 3D motion capture.

Method: Represents motion as a sequence of skeletal poses with explicit timestamp tokens; uses a unified pose encoder supporting both 2D and 3D; constructs a diverse training mixture (pose captioning, pose QA, motion captioning, motion QA); optionally incorporates video for context.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on BABEL-QA, HuMMan-QA, CompMo, NTU-RGB+D, and QEVD-Coach; even with 2D skeletal input, surpasses previous 3D-based methods. Ablations show primary gains from diversity of pose- and motion-level supervision, with smaller additional benefit from staged training.

Conclusion: Explicit temporal encoding and diverse pose- and motion-level supervision are highly effective for fine-grained human motion understanding; 2D skeletal input can outperform previous 3D methods, reducing reliance on costly 3D motion capture.

Abstract: In this work, we propose \methodname, an LLM-based model for fine-grained human motion understanding that represents motion as a sequence of skeletal poses with explicit timestamps for each pose. Each pose encodes body joint positions and is temporally grounded with timestamp tokens, allowing the model to reason about motion order, duration, and rhythm. To study what supervision is needed for motion-language reasoning, we construct a diverse training mixture spanning pose captioning, pose question answering, motion captioning, and motion question answering. Our ablations show that the primary gains come from the diversity of pose- and motion-level supervision, while staged training provides a smaller additional benefit. Different from previous works that rely on ground-truth 3D motion capture, our approach supports both 2D and 3D skeletal motion representations through a unified pose encoder, and can optionally incorporate video to provide contextual information. Extensive experiments on BABEL-QA, HuMMan-QA, CompMo, NTU-RGB+D, and QEVD-Coach demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple benchmarks, highlighting the effectiveness of explicit temporal encoding and diverse pose- and motion-level supervision for fine-grained human motion understanding. Notably, even when using only 2D skeletal input, our approach surpasses previous 3D-based methods.

</details>


### [46] [Go-with-the-Track: Video Compositing and Motion Control with Point Tracking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20891)
*Koichi Namekata,Yash Kant,Zhizheng Liu,Ryan D Burgert,Yuancheng Xu,Kuan Heng Lin,Emmett Steven,Julien Philip,Li Ma,Andrea Vedaldi,Paul Debevec,Ning Yu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Go-with-the-Track unifies motion control and reference image compositing in video generation by jointly conditioning on multiple reference images and reference-anchored point-tracks, using spatially-aware point-track embeddings injected via a lightweight adapter into a video diffusion transformer with a hybrid training strategy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods treat motion control and reference compositing separately: point-track-conditioned I2V models restrict content insertion to the first frame, while reference-to-video models lack fine-grained spatial-temporal control. There is no unified framework that achieves both precise compositing and motion control throughout a video.

Method: (1) Extends conventional point-tracks to reference-anchored point-tracks that establish correspondences between generated frames and reference images. (2) Introduces spatially-aware point-track embeddings via a coordinate-wise MLP followed by temporal pooling, where embedding similarity correlates with spatial proximity. (3) Injects embeddings into a video diffusion transformer through a lightweight adapter to resolve pixel-to-patch resolution mismatch. (4) Employs hybrid training on dynamic, static, and synthetic scene video datasets.

Result: Achieves superior motion and reference control in a single model. Enables multi-reference conditioned video generation with point-track driven compositing, and camera control for both static and dynamic scenes.

Conclusion: Go-with-the-Track successfully unifies motion control and reference image compositing, surpassing existing methods that treat these capabilities separately, and enables new video generation capabilities not previously possible.

Abstract: Filmmaking demands precise motion control and reference image compositing -- capabilities that existing methods treat separately. Point-track-conditioned image-to-video models restrict content insertion to the first frame, while reference-to-video models lack fine-grained spatial-temporal control over how reference content integrates across frames.
  We present Go-with-the-Track, which unifies both capabilities by jointly conditioning on multiple reference images and reference-anchored point-tracks -- extending conventional point-tracks to explicitly establish correspondences between generated frames and reference images, thus enabling precise compositing and motion control throughout the video.
  To achieve this, we introduce spatially-aware point-track embeddings that encode the full sequence of point-track coordinates using a coordinate-wise MLP followed by temporal pooling. This representation captures the spatial characteristics of each point-track (serving as a unique identifier), while the embedding similarity correlates directly with spatial proximity, enhancing the model's ability to distinguish and associate point-tracks. We inject these point-track embeddings into a video diffusion transformer via a lightweight adapter, resolving the pixel-to-patch resolution mismatch while avoiding the substantial motion detail loss inherent in naive point-track subsampling.
  We use a hybrid training strategy to train jointly on dynamic, static, and synthetic scene video datasets to boost motion controllability. Experiments demonstrate that Go-with-the-Track achieves superior motion and reference control in a single model and enables new capabilities: multi-reference conditioned video generation with point-track driven compositing, as well as camera control for both static and dynamic scenes. Project Page: https://eyeline-labs.github.io/Go-with-the-Track/

</details>


### [47] [BELDE: Building a Large-scale Earth-observation Land-cover Dataset for Europe](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20909)
*Ümit Mert Çağlar,Alptekin Temizel*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces BELDE, a large-scale publicly available land-cover segmentation dataset for Europe built from Sentinel-2 RGB imagery and ESA WorldCover annotations, containing over 1 million image-mask pairs across 7 classes at 10m resolution, along with cross-region benchmarks (BELDE-K for South Korea, BELDE-CA-NV for US). Models trained on BELDE achieve 83.0% F1 on European test sets but drop to 66.4% and 58.3% on out-of-distribution regions, highlighting geographic domain shift challenges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing land-cover segmentation datasets are limited in geographic coverage, scale, and public accessibility. While RGB imagery is widely used due to cost and deployment constraints, there is a lack of large-scale, publicly available RGB land-cover datasets, especially for cross-region generalization studies.

Method: The dataset was constructed from Sentinel-2 true-color images and ESA WorldCover data annotations. It contains 1,088,385 image-segmentation map pairs covering Europe with 7 land-cover classes at 10m resolution. Additional benchmarks include BELDE-K (16,607 pairs for South Korea) and BELDE-CA-NV (88,155 pairs for California/Nevada). Multiple semantic segmentation architectures were used to establish baseline results, evaluating both in-domain and cross-domain performance.

Result: Models trained on BELDE achieve 83.0% F1 on the European test set, 66.4% on BELDE-CA-NV, and 58.3% on BELDE-K, demonstrating significant performance degradation due to geographic domain shift.

Conclusion: BELDE provides a continental-scale RGB segmentation benchmark that supports the development of robust and transferable Earth observation models. The dataset and benchmark resources will be publicly released.

Abstract: Earth observation imagery plays a critical role in environmental monitoring, urban planning, disaster assessment, and climate analysis. While multi-spectral sensors are increasingly available, true-color (RGB) imagery remains widely used due to the power, cost, and deployment constraints of many satellite and aerial platforms. However, existing land-cover segmentation datasets are often limited in geographic coverage, scale, or public accessibility.
  To bridge this gap, we introduce BELDE (Building a Large-scale Earth-observation Land-cover Dataset for Europe), a publicly available dataset tailored for RGB-based remote sensing semantic segmentation. Constructed from Sentinel-2 true-color images and ESA WorldCover data annotations, BELDE contains 1,088,385 curated image-segmentation map pairs spanning Europe with 7 land-cover classes at 10 m spatial resolution, making it one of the largest publicly available RGB land-cover segmentation datasets for Earth observation. To facilitate cross-region generalization studies, we additionally introduce BELDE-K (16,607 pairs) covering the Republic of Korea and BELDE-CA-NV (88,155 pairs) covering California and Nevada in the United States.
  We establish baseline results using multiple semantic segmentation architectures and evaluate both in-domain and cross-domain performance. Models trained on BELDE achieve an F1 score of 83.0% on the European test set, while performance decreases to 66.4% on BELDE-CA-NV and 58.3% on BELDE-K, highlighting the challenges posed by out-of-distribution geographic domain shift. By providing a continental-scale RGB segmentation and evaluation benchmark, BELDE supports the development of robust and transferable Earth observation models. The dataset and benchmark resources will be publicly released.

</details>


### [48] [PROTON: Prototype-Based Test-Time Online OOD Detection for Medical VLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20913)
*Abhijit Das,Nichula Wasalathilaka,Yifan Lu,Adinath Dukre,Dwarikanath Mahapatra,Shadab Khan,Imran Razzak*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PROTON is a lightweight post-hoc OOD detection module for medical VLMs that maintains an online prototype bank from high-confidence test predictions and adaptively fuses prototype distance with MCM scoring via stream-level variance statistics, significantly improving OOD detection across covariate shift (+23.9 AUROC), semantic shift (+8.8), and far-OOD (+8.1) on the FLAIR + FIVES benchmark without requiring model modification, training data, or prompt engineering.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Medical VLMs enable zero-shot clinical image classification but fail to reliably detect out-of-distribution (OOD) inputs at deployment. No static scoring method works across all shift types; e.g., MCM on FLAIR achieves 76.4% AUROC for far-OOD but only 42.4% (random) for covariate shifts like ultra-wide-field fundus images. This is due to a structural mismatch: covariate-shifted inputs are indistinguishable in softmax space but occupy distinct regions in VLM embedding space.

Method: PROTON (PROtotype-based Test-time ONline OOD detection) is a lightweight post-hoc module that: (1) maintains an online prototype bank from high-confidence test predictions, (2) adaptively fuses prototype distance with MCM scoring using stream-level variance statistics, and (3) requires no model modification, training data, or prompt engineering.

Result: On FLAIR + FIVES benchmark, PROTON improves MCM by +23.9 AUROC on covariate shift, +8.8 on semantic shift, and +8.1 on far-OOD, making it the only zero-shot method to improve all three OOD categories without hierarchical prompts or labeled data.

Conclusion: PROTON effectively addresses the structural mismatch between softmax and embedding spaces for OOD detection in medical VLMs, achieving state-of-the-art zero-shot OOD detection across diverse shift types with minimal overhead.

Abstract: Medical vision-language models (VLMs) enable zero-shot clinical image classification, yet reliably detecting out-of-distribution (OOD) inputs at deployment remains an open problem. No static scoring method works across all shift types: Maximum Concept Matching (MCM) on FLAIR achieves 76.4% AUROC for far-OOD but only 42.4% for covariate shifts such as ultra-wide-field fundus images, effectively random. We trace this to a structural mismatch: covariate-shifted inputs are indistinguishable from in-distribution samples in softmax space, yet occupy distinct regions in the VLM embedding space. To exploit this untapped signal, we propose PROTON (PROtotype-based Test-time ONline OOD detection), a lightweight post-hoc module that maintains an online prototype bank from high-confidence test predictions and adaptively fuses prototype distance with MCM scoring via stream-level variance statistics, requiring no model modification, training data, or prompt engineering. On the ophthalmology benchmark FLAIR + FIVES, PROTON improves MCM by +23.9 AUROC on covariate shift, +8.8 on semantic shift, and +8.1 on far-OOD, making it the only zero-shot method to improve all three without hierarchical prompts or labeled data. Code is available at https://github.com/GenMI-Lab/PROTON, and the project page is available at https://genmi-lab.github.io/PROTON.

</details>


### [49] [GIM-ENDO: A Multimodal Endoscopic Image and Video Dataset for Gastric Intestinal Metaplasia Morphology and Pathology](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20919)
*Mojgan Forootan,Mahziar Setayeshfar,Ali Darvishi,Mohammad Tashakoripour,Hamidreza Bolhasani*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GIM-ENDO is a publicly available, histopathologically validated dataset for gastric intestinal metaplasia detection using endoscopic imaging, featuring detailed annotations from 24 patients across multiple imaging modalities, endoscopic signs, and histological subtypes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Early detection of GIM is crucial for preventing gastric cancer, but development of reliable AI models has been hindered by the lack of publicly available, histopathologically validated datasets with comprehensive annotations combining endoscopic signs, histological subtypes, grading systems, and normal controls.

Method: The dataset was acquired using the Olympus EVIS X1 system with WLE and IEE (NBI, M-NBI) from 24 patients (22 GIM-positive, 2 normal controls). It includes demographic data, endoscopic findings, histopathological results, H. pylori status, images, and video clips. Annotations cover six primary IEE endoscopic signs, two additional findings, GIM subtypes, and OLGA/OLGIM staging.

Result: The GIM-ENDO dataset is publicly accessible at Zenodo (https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.20707267) and the DataBioX website, providing a comprehensive resource for AI model development in GIM detection.

Conclusion: GIM-ENDO fills a critical gap in publicly available datasets for GIM, enabling the development of more reliable AI models for real-time endoscopic detection and characterization of gastric intestinal metaplasia.

Abstract: Gastric intestinal metaplasia (GIM) is a precursor lesion to gastric dysplasia and adenocarcinoma whose early detection is crucial for intervening in the carcinogenesis cascade. Artificial intelligence (AI) holds considerable promise for real-time endoscopic detection and characterization of GIM. However, development of reliable AI models has been constrained by the absence of publicly available, histopathologically validated datasets that combine detailed endoscopic annotations, histological subtype (complete and incomplete), standardized grading systems, and normal mucosal patterns. GIM-ENDO was designed to fill this gap. The dataset comprises demographic data, endoscopic findings, histopathological results, and H. pylori status acquired using the Olympus EVIS X1 system with white-light endoscopy (WLE) and image-enhanced endoscopy (IEE), including narrow-band imaging (NBI) and magnifying NBI (M-NBI), along with images and video clips from 24 patients (22 GIM-positive, 2 normal controls). Annotations cover six primary IEE endoscopic signs -- light blue crest (LBC), marginal turbid band (MTB), white opaque substance (WOS), TV pattern (Fusion), atrophy, and map-like erythema (MLE) -- plus two additional endoscopic findings (AHP and GA) recorded where present. GIM subtypes (complete and incomplete) are annotated for all GIM-positive cases; OLGA and OLGIM staging are provided where complete histological sampling was available. The dataset is publicly accessible at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.20707267. For the latest updates and further information regarding this dataset, readers are referred to the DataBioX website: https://databiox.com
  A short version of this work has been submitted to MICCAI 2026 Open Data Track.

</details>


### [50] [ELDiff: When Evidential Learning Meets Text-to-Image Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20924)
*Qingtao Pan,Kai Ye,Zhihao Dou,Bing Ji,Shuo Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ELDiff proposes an evidential learning-supervised T2I diffusion model that uses uncertainty metrics and conflict detection to improve object-wise semantic consistency in multi-object text-to-image generation, addressing segmentation map bias and semantic overlap conflicts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multi-object T2I diffusion models struggle with semantic consistency between text prompts and generated images. Token supervised models attempt to learn object-wise consistency via segmentation maps but suffer from segmentation map bias and semantic overlap conflicts, especially with multiple objects.

Method: ELDiff introduces two key components: (1) a pixel evidence loss with evidential regularization to restrain overconfidence in unreliable segmentation map labels, and (2) a token conflict loss that optimizes a measured conflict factor to weaken semantic contradictions between objects.

Result: Outperforms existing training-based and training-free T2I diffusion models across SD v1.4, SD v2.1, SDXL, SD v3.5, and Qwen-Image without requiring additional inference-time manipulations. The method is also seamlessly extensible to existing training pipelines.

Conclusion: ELDiff effectively enhances fault tolerance to unreliable segmentation maps and suppresses semantic conflicts, strengthening object-wise consistency learning for multi-object T2I generation.

Abstract: In multi-object text-to-image (T2I) diffusion, ensuring semantic consistency between textual prompts and generated visual content is crucial for image synthesis. However, such consistency constraint is often underemphasized in the denoising process of diffusion models.
