Motivation: Practitioners lack a unified geospatial-native, cloud-optimized, and ML-integrated tool for end-to-end forest imagery workflows (annotation, prediction, visualization, analysis) that can handle multi-task vision problems across varying regions, sensors, and acquisition conditions.

Method: AwakeForest integrates model-assisted inference, automatic annotation, and human-in-the-loop refinement; supports plug-and-play pretrained models; handles scalable interaction with imagery from aerial scenes to large orthomosaics (GB to hundreds of GB); produces analysis-ready outputs for downstream use and iterative model/annotation updates.

Result: Demonstrated on the PALMS dataset, showing support for end-to-end workflow for practical forest management and analysis.

Conclusion: AwakeForest provides a unified, scalable platform for large-scale forest imagery analysis, bridging the gap between ML models and practical forest management workflows.

Abstract: Forest imagery analysis often involves multiple tightly coupled vision tasks, which must be performed under substantial variation in geographic regions, sensors, and acquisition conditions. However, practitioners often lack a unified tool that is geospatial-native, cloud-optimized, and ML-integrated for end-to-end workflows spanning annotation, prediction, visualization, and downstream analysis at scale. We present AwakeForest, an interactive end-to-end platform designed for large-scale forest imagery that integrates model-assisted inference, automatic annotation, and human-in-the-loop refinement within a single workflow. Our platform supports plug-and-play integration of pretrained models and enables scalable interaction with forest imagery ranging from standard aerial scenes to large orthomosaics that can span several gigabytes to hundreds of gigabytes. AwakeForest produces analysis-ready outputs that can be directly used for downstream analysis and to support iterative model and annotation updates on new scenes. We demonstrate the system on the PALMS dataset and illustrate how AwakeForest supports an end-to-end workflow for practical forest management and analysis.

</details>


### [77] [Beyond Damage Assessment: Recyclable Material Detection in Aerial Disaster Imagery Using a Lightweight Patch-Based Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21279)
*Mahmoud Hazem,Karim Hammoudi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper presents a lightweight approach for detecting recyclable materials in damaged areas from aerial images after disasters, along with a publicly available labeled dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The increasing frequency of disasters creates damaged areas containing recyclable materials. Efficient detection of these materials is needed to support ecological recycling efforts.

Method: A lightweight approach designed for efficient detection of recyclable materials from aerial images of disaster-affected areas.

Result: Experimental results demonstrate the potential of the proposed approach for localizing recyclable materials in disaster imagery.

Conclusion: The proposed lightweight method shows promise for recyclable material detection, and the publicly released labeled dataset will support further development in this domain.

Abstract: Nowadays, more and more disasters of different natures are appearing. Several disaster assessment approaches have been developed in order to identify damaged areas from aerial images. These damaged areas contain rich material that could be recycled towards several ecological purposes. In this paper, we present a lightweight approach that permits the efficient detection of recyclable material. Experimental results show the potential of the proposed approach towards localizing recyclable materials. Accordingly, we provide a rare dataset of material images that we labeled towards supporting the development of recyclable material detectors. The dataset of labeled material images is publicly available at: anonymous.

</details>


### [78] [Unsupervised Domain Adaptation for Sim-to-Real Object Pose Estimation with Contrastive Alignment and Pseudo-Label Refinement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21287)
*Nidhal Eddine Chenni,Arunkumar Rathinam,Djamila Aouada*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CAPLR introduces a novel unsupervised domain adaptation method for object pose estimation that aligns pose-sensitive features in localized regions using cross-domain pairing, contrastive alignment, and pseudo-label refinement, achieving state-of-the-art results across multiple benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing UDA methods for object pose estimation rely on global feature matching, multi-stage frameworks, or image translation, which overlook pose-specific information embedded in feature representations.

Method: Three key components: (1) Efficient Cross-Domain Pairing using intermediate features to identify pose-similar image pairs across domains; (2) Contrastive Alignment for feature alignment at localized regions in both intermediate and task-specific representations; (3) Consistency-Based Pseudo-Label Refinement to encourage stable target predictions.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple well-known object pose estimation benchmarks featuring diverse and challenging scenarios.

Conclusion: Localized, pose-sensitive feature alignment is more effective than global feature matching for domain adaptation in object pose estimation.

Abstract: Unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA) enables robust transfer of knowledge from simulated to real environments while exploiting a subset of unlabeled target data to improve real-world performance. Existing UDA methods for Object pose estimation often rely on global feature matching, multi-stage larger frameworks, or image translation pipelines, which tend to overlook the pose-specific information embedded in feature representations. To bridge this limitation, we introduce CAPLR that targets the adaptation of pose-sensitive features in localized regions, ensuring that domain alignment preserves the geometric cues essential for accurate pose estimation. CAPLR achieves UDA with three key components: (1) Efficient Cross-Domain Pairing strategy leveraging intermediate features to identify pose similar image pairs across domains without supervision; (2) Contrastive Alignment to perform feature alignment at localised regions in both intermediate and task-specific representations; and (3) Consistency-Based Pseudo-Label Refinement to improve reliability by encouraging stable target predictions. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CAPLR achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple well-known object pose estimation benchmarks featuring diverse and challenging scenarios.

</details>


### [79] [NoduLoCC2026: Lung Nodule Localization and Classification Contest from Chest X-Ray Images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21290)
*Adnan Mustafic,Halim Benhabiles,Adnane Cabani,Kristhian André Oliveira Aguilar,Romain Amigon,Clément Bardin,Chiara Bentifece,Marin Boehm,Kévin Bouchard,Laura Burattini,Diedre Carmo,Fahima Idiri,Matthis Lahargoue,Ilaria Marcantoni,Hicham Messaoudi,Cyril Meyer,Farid Meziane,Léon Morales,Letícia Rittner,Agnese Sbrollini,Léonard Zipper,Karim Hammoudi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: NoduLoCC2026 is a challenge on lung nodule detection and localization in chest X-rays, with 5 international teams submitting solutions. The best method achieved 0.72 balanced accuracy and 0.79 AUC-ROC for classification, while localization proved more challenging (53% correct nodule count, 12.83mm median distance).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To establish a benchmark for lung nodule detection and localization in chest X-ray images through a standardized challenge, enabling comparison of different approaches and identifying current limitations.

Method: Organized a challenge (NoduLoCC2026) with a provided dataset for classification and localization tasks; received submissions from 5 international teams; evaluated on an external test dataset.

Result: Best classification performance: balanced accuracy 0.72, AUC-ROC 0.79. Best localization performance: correct nodule count prediction on 53% of test images, median distance of 12.83mm.

Conclusion: Classification shows good performance, but localization remains a more challenging task with significant room for improvement.

Abstract: We propose NoduLoCC2026, a challenge on lung nodule detection and localization in chest X-ray images. We have provided a dataset for both tasks and received submissions from 5 international teams. The participating teams' solutions are presented in this work along with results on an external dataset used for testing. Proposed methods show good performance on the classification task. The best method shows a balanced accuracy score of 0.72 and AUC-ROC of 0.79. We highlight the limitations of current approaches for the localization task, with the best approach having predicted the correct number of nodules on 53\% of the test images with a median distance of 12.83mm, showing that it is a more challenging task than the first one. The challenge website is available via https://gt-i2mdp.github.io/website/nodule_challenge.html.

</details>


### [80] [Lightweight 3D Feature Pretraining by Bayesian Inversion of 2D Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21292)
*Marwane Hariat,Gianni Franchi,David Filliat,Antoine Manzanera*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Casper3D is a lightweight probabilistic framework that converts noisy multi-view 2D foundation-model embeddings into a latent 3D semantic representation using a set-based variational model with relative pose reasoning, achieving stable open-vocabulary 3D understanding across different backbone embeddings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the instability and noise in converting multi-view 2D semantic embeddings into coherent 3D semantic representations, particularly in ambiguous settings where simple pooling methods fail.

Method: A probabilistic framework that models view-level semantic features as noisy observations of an underlying 3D semantic state, inferred via a set-based variational model incorporating relative pose information during multi-view reasoning. The framework is trained by predicting held-out semantic observations from novel viewpoints, remaining aligned with visual and text semantic spaces.

Result: Casper3D produces more stable 3D semantics compared to simple multi-view pooling, particularly in ambiguous and noisy settings. It works effectively with both language-aligned and self-supervised embeddings across different backbone architectures.

Conclusion: The proposed backbone-agnostic probabilistic approach is effective for open-vocabulary 3D understanding by fusing noisy 2D foundation-model embeddings into a robust latent 3D semantic representation.

Abstract: We present Casper3D, a lightweight probabilistic framework for converting noisy multi-view 2D foundation-model embeddings into a latent 3D semantic representation. We model view-level semantic features as noisy observations of an underlying 3D semantic state and infer this state with a set-based variational model that incorporates relative pose during multi-view reasoning. Casper3D is trained by predicting held-out semantic observations from novel viewpoints, while remaining aligned with visual and text semantic spaces for open-vocabulary 3D understanding. The framework is backbone-agnostic and applies to both language-aligned and self-supervised embeddings. Experiments show that Casper3D produces more stable 3D semantics than simple multi-view pooling, especially in ambiguous and noisy settings.

</details>


### [81] [SCOPE: Scale-Consistent One-Pass Estimation of 3D Geometry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21300)
*Zheng Zhang,Lihe Yang,Tianyu Yang,Chaohui Yu,Yixing Lao,Xiaoyang Guo,Biao Gong,Fan Wang,Hengshuang Zhao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SCOPE is a novel method for estimating consistent 3D geometry from long monocular video sequences, achieving significant error reductions (24.2% point map error, 34.9% temporal alignment error) through affine-invariant point maps, viewpoint-invariant geometry, appearance-invariant learning, and frequency-modulated positioning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods struggle to maintain geometric accuracy and temporal consistency when estimating 3D geometry from extended monocular video sequences (hundreds of frames).

Method: SCOPE generates affine-invariant 3D point maps with shared parameters across entire sequences, incorporating three key innovations: (1) viewpoint-invariant geometry for multi-perspective alignment, (2) appearance-invariant learning for consistency across exponential timescales, and (3) frequency-modulated positioning for extrapolation beyond training sequence lengths.

Result: On ScanNet, SCOPE reduces relative point map error by 24.2% and temporal alignment error by 34.9% compared to state-of-the-art methods. It handles complex camera trajectories and lighting variations efficiently in a single pass.

Conclusion: SCOPE enables scale-consistent 3D geometry estimation from long monocular videos, outperforming existing methods on both accuracy and temporal consistency while supporting extrapolation to sequences longer than those seen during training.

Abstract: We present SCOPE (Scale-Consistent One-Pass Estimation of 3D Geometry), a novel approach for estimating 3D geometry from extended monocular video sequences, where existing methods struggle to maintain both geometric accuracy and temporal consistency across hundreds of frames. Our approach generates affine-invariant 3D point maps with shared parameters across entire sequences, enabling consistent scale-invariant representations. We introduce three key innovations: viewpoint-invariant geometry aligning multi-perspective points in a unified reference frame; appearance-invariant learning enforcing consistency across exponential timescales; and frequency-modulated positioning enabling extrapolation to sequences vastly exceeding training length. Experiments across diverse datasets demonstrate significant improvements, reducing relative point map error by 24.2% and temporal alignment error by 34.9% on ScanNet compared to state-of-the-art methods. Our approach handles challenging scenarios with complex camera trajectories and lighting variations while efficiently processing extended sequences in a single pass. Project page: https://scope3d.github.io/.

</details>


### [82] [A Test-time Actor-Critic Approach to News Images Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21304)
*Damianos Galanopoulos,Vasileios Mezaris*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces ACIG, a test-time, model-agnostic Actor-Critic Image Generation approach inspired by reinforcement learning's Actor-Critic paradigm. ACIG generates prompts, produces images, evaluates results, and refines prompts in a feedback loop. It achieved the best results in the MediaEval NewsImages 2026 challenge.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve the relevance and quality of images generated from news headlines by introducing a feedback-driven refinement mechanism, addressing the limitation of static one-shot image generation methods.

Method: ACIG adopts the Actor-Critic paradigm: the Actor generates and refines image creation prompts, the image generator produces corresponding images, and the Critic evaluates the generated results. If the evaluation is unsatisfactory, the prompts are iteratively refined in a closed feedback loop at test time, without requiring model retraining.

Result: ACIG achieved the best results in the MediaEval NewsImages 2026 challenge according to the challenge's leaderboard.

Conclusion: The proposed test-time, model-agnostic feedback loop approach effectively generates high-quality images from news headlines, outperforming competing methods in the challenge.

Abstract: This paper introduces the CERTH-ITI solution for the MediaEval NewsImages 2026 challenge, which focuses on generating images related to news headlines. Inspired by the Actor-Critic paradigm in reinforcement learning, we present a test-time, model-agnostic Actor-Critic Image Generation approach (ACIG). ACIG generates prompts for image creation, produces the images, evaluates the generated results, and if needed refines the image generation prompts accordingly in a feedback loop. ACIG achieved the best results in the NewsImages 2026 challenge, according to the challenge's leaderboard.

</details>


### [83] [WildBox: A Dataset and Benchmark for Aerial Monocular 3D Detection of African Savanna Wildlife](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21309)
*Vandita Shukla,Kilian Meier,Lucie Laporte-Devylder,Camille Rondeau Saint-Jean,Jenna M. Kline,Blair R. Costelloe,Devis Tuia,Fabio Remondino,Benjamin Risse*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: WildBox is a dataset of 237,505 3D bounding box annotations for seven African savanna species from drone video. Zero-shot monocular 3D detection fails completely (0.00 AP), with depth contributing 84-99% of error. Fine-tuning recovers performance to ~8.68 AP-BEV and ~13.17 AP3D macro. A coarse-to-fine curriculum (pretraining on merged zebra then fine-tuning on subclasses) improves performance efficiently.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing monocular 3D detection benchmarks (e.g., KITTI, Omni3D) focus on urban/indoor scenes with rigid objects. Wildlife monitoring from drones lacks annotated 3D detection datasets, making it unclear whether open-vocabulary 3D architectures can generalize to aerial wildlife scenarios.

Method: WildBox dataset: 237,505 3D bounding boxes across 7 species (6 classes) from drone video, KITTI/Omni3D-compatible format, per-segment scale-normalized camera frame, instance tracking across segments. Evaluation of OVMono3D-LIFT and DetAny3D under zero-shot, GT 2D box prompt, and supervised fine-tuning protocols. Coarse-to-fine curriculum training on zebra subclasses.

Result: Zero-shot 3D detection: 0.00 AP across both architectures and all 2D-input conditions. Fine-tuning: 8.68±0.47 AP-BEV@0.50, 13.17±0.69 AP3D macro. Depth contributes 84% of normalized Hausdorff distance after fine-tuning, >99% in zero-shot. Coarse-to-fine curriculum improves macro 3D performance with less total compute.

Conclusion: Monocular aerial depth estimation is the dominant bottleneck for 3D wildlife detection from drones. Open-vocabulary 2D localization works reasonably (50.55 AP@50) but 3D fails entirely in zero-shot. WildBox provides a foundation for progress in this regime.

Abstract: We introduce WildBox, a dataset and benchmark for monocular 3D detection of wildlife from drone video, comprising 237,505 3D bounding box annotations across seven African savanna species grouped into six benchmark classes. Annotations follow a KITTI/Omni3D-compatible format in a per-segment scale-normalised camera frame, with instance identities maintained across each segment. We evaluate two open-vocabulary monocular 3D architectures, OVMono3D-LIFT and DetAny3D, under zero-shot, ground-truth 2D box prompt, and supervised fine-tuning protocols. Open-vocabulary 2D foundation models provide usable zero-shot wildlife localisation (50.55 AP@50), but zero-shot 3D detection collapses to 0.00 AP across both architectures and every 2D-input condition tested, including ground-truth 2D box prompts, thus isolating the failure to the 3D stage. Fine-tuning on WildBox recovers performance to 8.68 +/- 0.47 AP-BEV@0.50 and 13.17 +/- 0.69 AP3D macro. Depth contributes 84% of normalised Hausdorff distance after fine-tuning and over 99% in zero-shot, identifying monocular aerial depth as the dominant open problem in this regime. A coarse-to-fine curriculum, i.e. pretraining on a merged zebra class before fine-tuning on the Grevy's/plains split, improves macro 3D performance with less total compute, with the largest gains on the two zebra subclasses. WildBox is released with video-level splits, evaluation code, and baseline checkpoints to enable progress in 3D wildlife perception from drone video.

</details>


### [84] [LEViL: Label-Efficient Video Learning via Zero-Shot Distillation over VLM-Generated Pseudo-Label Spaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21358)
*Aslı Çelik*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a label-efficient video learning framework combining annotation-free video pretraining (using VLM-generated semantic pseudo-labels and zero-shot distillation) with target-label-set-aware fine-tuning, outperforming semi-supervised methods on UCF101 and HMDB51 under limited-label regimes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Supervised video pretraining requires large-scale labeled datasets, which are costly and domain-dependent. The paper aims to eliminate the need for manual source annotations while maintaining effective transfer learning for downstream action recognition.

Method: (1) Annotation-free pretraining: VLM generates text descriptions from unlabeled videos → constructs semantic pseudo-label space → frozen video-language model produces zero-shot soft targets → student video encoder learns rich representations. (2) Target-label-set-aware fine-tuning: combines supervised learning on labeled target data with zero-shot distillation over the actual target label set, preserving VLM semantic guidance.

Result: Outperforms compared semi-supervised video action recognition methods on UCF101 and HMDB51 across all limited-label regimes. The annotation-free pretraining provides effective initialization for full-data fine-tuning despite using a modest unlabeled pretraining pool.

Conclusion: Label-efficient video learning is achievable without manual source annotations by leveraging VLMs for semantic pseudo-labeling and zero-shot distillation, offering a scalable alternative to supervised video pretraining.

Abstract: Supervised video pretraining is a common transfer learning practice for improving downstream action recognition performance. However, it requires large-scale labeled source datasets, and the effectiveness of the learned initialization is influenced by the similarity between the source and target domains. Constructing such labeled pretraining datasets for different target domains is costly and difficult to scale. To address these limitations, this study proposes a label-efficient video learning framework that combines annotation-free video pretraining with target-label-set-aware fine-tuning. During pretraining, a vision-language model (VLM) generates textual descriptions of unlabeled videos, which are processed to construct an interpretable semantic pseudo-label space. A frozen video-language model then produces zero-shot soft target distributions over this space, allowing a student video encoder to learn semantically rich representations without manual source annotations. During downstream adaptation, target-label-set-aware fine-tuning combines supervised learning from labeled target videos with zero-shot distillation over the actual target label set, helping preserve VLM-derived semantic guidance while adapting the pretrained encoder to the target task. Experiments on UCF101 and HMDB51 show that the proposed framework outperforms the compared semi-supervised video action recognition methods across all evaluated limited-label regimes. Moreover, the annotation-free pretraining stage learns transferable representations that provide an effective initialization for full-data fine-tuning, despite relying on a comparatively modest unlabeled pretraining pool.

</details>


### [85] [Graph-of-Differences: Anatomy-Structured Difference Alignment for Medical Image Re-Identification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21368)
*Nichula Wasalathilaka,Abhijit Das,Imran Razzak,Dwarikanath Mahapatra*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Graph-of-Differences (GoD) improves medical image re-identification by grounding identity comparisons in explicit anatomical structure via soft node correspondence and graph-level difference alignment, achieving significant accuracy gains on fundus and CXR benchmarks with verifiable structure-level explanations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Medical image re-identification suffers from shortcut learning and produces decisions that clinicians cannot audit against named anatomy, lacking anatomically grounded and interpretable identity matching.

Method: Each image is represented as an anatomy graph with nodes corresponding to named anatomical regions; soft node correspondence is established between image pairs; differences are computed over matched anatomy; a graph-level difference alignment objective ties anatomy-matched differences to the global backbone difference; explanations are defined over named graph nodes with node insertion/deletion tests.

Result: GoD improves Rank-1 by +7.1 pp on fundus and +3.1 pp on CXR over a strong frozen-backbone baseline, with further gains on zero-shot external transfers confirming improved accuracy and generalization.

Conclusion: Anatomy grounding via GoD improves both accuracy and generalization in medical image re-identification, replacing unstable pixel heatmaps with verifiable structure-level evidence.

Abstract: Medical image re-identification (MedReID) enables longitudinal patient linkage but remains vulnerable to shortcut learning and often produces decisions that clinicians cannot audit against named anatomy. We propose Graph-of-Differences (GoD), which grounds identity comparisons in explicit anatomical structure. Each image is represented as an anatomy graph whose nodes correspond to named anatomical regions; given an image pair, soft node correspondence is established, and differences are computed over matched anatomy. A graph-level difference alignment objective ties these anatomy-matched differences to the global backbone difference, ensuring the retrieval signal is anchored in homologous structures rather than arbitrary spatial tokens. Explanations are defined over named graph nodes and quantitatively audited via node insertion/deletion tests, replacing unstable pixel heatmaps with verifiable structure-level evidence. On internal benchmarks, GoD improves Rank-1 by +7.1 pp on fundus and +3.1 pp on CXR over a strong frozen-backbone baseline, with further gains on zero-shot external transfers confirming that anatomy grounding improves both accuracy and generalization. Code is available at https://github.com/GenMI-Lab/GoD.git.

</details>


### [86] [FLM-Occ: Feed-forward Likelihood Maximization for Efficient Indoor Occupancy Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21373)
*Guangcheng Chen,Lihuang Fang,Huaqi Tao,Yicheng He,Li He,Hong Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FLM reformulates occupancy prediction as voxel distribution estimation using a feed-forward likelihood maximization framework, achieving superior accuracy with 32 superquadrics (2.7% of prior SoTA) while running 3.7x faster.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Gaussian primitive-based occupancy prediction methods rely on voxel classification with only local constraints, lacking global supervision, which leads to spurious primitives in empty regions, harming representational and computational efficiency.

Method: FLM trains a network to predict a mixture model maximizing likelihood over ground-truth occupied voxels in a feed-forward manner. It defines mixture weights as normalized primitive volumes for simplex constraints and derives novel voxelization formulas for end-to-end training.

Result: On Occ-ScanNet, FLM-Occ achieves superior accuracy using only 32 superquadrics (2.7% of prior SoTA) while running 3.7 times faster.

Conclusion: FLM effectively eliminates spurious primitives and improves efficiency by reformulating occupancy prediction as likelihood maximization, enabling accurate modeling with far fewer primitives.

Abstract: Recent indoor occupancy prediction methods adopt Gaussian primitives as a sparse 3D representation for computational efficiency. However, their training relies on voxel classification, which imposes only local constraints and lacks global supervision on the distribution of the primitives. Therefore, they inevitably predict spurious primitives in empty regions, undermining both representational and computational efficiency. To address this, we propose Feed-forward Likelihood Maximization (FLM), a novel framework that reformulates occupancy prediction as voxel distribution estimation. In FLM, a network is trained to predict a mixture model that maximizes the likelihood over ground-truth occupied voxels in a feed-forward manner. To enable end-to-end training of networks and voxelization of a standard mixture model, we define mixture weights as normalized primitive volumes to implicitly enforce simplex constraints and derive novel voxelization formulas. Based on FLM, our FLM-Occ, a novel method that is capable of relocating randomly initialized primitives over long distances to model a scene. On Occ-ScanNet, FLM-Occ achieves superior accuracy using only 32 superquadrics, 2.7% of the prior SoTA, while running 3.7 times faster.

</details>


### [87] [EnTrust: Modeling Inter-Modal Conflict for Trustworthy Multimodal Medical Image Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21384)
*Dwarikanath Mahapatra,Abhijit Das,Behzad Bozorgtabar,Zongyuan Ge,Sudipta Roy,Deepak Nayak,Mauricio Reyes,Imran Razzak*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EnTrust is a multimodal medical image segmentation framework that explicitly models inter-modal conflict as the primary source of predictive uncertainty. It uses an EnFuse module to decompose multimodal features into shared consensus, modality-specific cues, and conflict signals; a SegDiff diffusion model that generates segmentation hypotheses diverging in regions of disagreement; and a TrustMap that produces calibrated, pixel-wise uncertainty maps that explain why predictions are unreliable. It achieves SOTA accuracy with 40% lower calibration error than baselines, and outperforms 5x deep ensembles with half the memory.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current multimodal segmentation models handle inter-modal disagreement poorly—deterministic fusion averages away conflict, while post-hoc uncertainty estimation is decoupled from fusion—both obscuring why predictions are unreliable. The need is to explicitly treat inter-modal conflict as the primary uncertainty source and provide clinically interpretable uncertainty.

Method: 1) EnFuse module: decomposes multimodal features into three disentangled components—shared consensus (F_c), modality-specific cues (F_{u,m}), and conflict signals (F_{cf})—enforced via a cross-covariance objective. 2) SegDiff: a diffusion-based generative segmentation model that samples hypotheses diverging specifically in conflict regions. 3) TrustMap: translates hypothesis divergence into calibrated pixel-wise uncertainty via ensemble entropy, conflict-guided perturbation probing, and a learned calibration head.

Result: SOTA segmentation accuracy across four benchmarks (brain, cardiac, lesion, oncology). 40% reduction in calibration error vs. strongest baseline. Outperforms 5x deep ensembles using a single model at roughly half the memory footprint.

Conclusion: Explicitly modeling inter-modal conflict as the primary uncertainty source yields both higher accuracy and better-calibrated, interpretable uncertainty, enabling clinicians to understand why predictions are unreliable.

Abstract: Multimodal medical imaging fuses complementary anatomical and functional information, yet modalities frequently disagree in pathologically heterogeneous regions. Current segmentation models handle this in one of two inadequate ways: deterministic fusion that averages away disagreement, or post-hoc uncertainty estimation decoupled from the fusion process that produces it. Both obscure the clinically critical question: why is this prediction unreliable? We present EnTrust, a framework that treats inter-modal conflict as the primary source of predictive uncertainty. Our EnFuse module decomposes multimodal features into three disentangled components: shared anatomical consensus (F_c), modality-specific cues (F_{u,m}), and spatially localized conflict signals (F_{cf}), with independence enforced via a cross-covariance objective. This structured decomposition conditions SegDiff, a diffusion-based generative segmentation model whose sampled hypotheses diverge specifically in regions of modal disagreement. TrustMap then translates this hypothesis divergence into calibrated, pixel-wise uncertainty using ensemble entropy, conflict-guided perturbation probing, and a learned calibration head, enabling clinicians to understand not only where predictions are uncertain, but why. Across four benchmarks spanning brain, cardiac, lesion, and oncology domains, EnTrust achieves state-of-the-art segmentation accuracy while reducing calibration error by 40% compared to the strongest baseline. Notably, it outperforms 5x deep ensembles using a single model at roughly half the memory footprint. Code and checkpoints are available at https://github.com/GenMI-Lab/EnTrust.git.

</details>


### [88] [MIRCaps: A Large-Scale Mixed-Domain Dataset with Image-Level and Region-Level Captions for Fine-Grained Vision-Language Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21419)
*Arlindo Luciano Tulumba Roberto,Hyungjoon Kim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces a large-scale multimodal dataset (141K images, ~1M image-level captions, ~1.7M region-level captions, ~1.4M bounding boxes) for mixed-domain image-caption tasks spanning general-purpose and CCTV surveillance. Each image has ~7 captions and each bounding box has ~7 region captions covering fine-grained attributes. Fine-tuning lightweight VLMs (SmolVLM, BLIP, BLIP2, Qwen2.5-VL) on this dataset improves image captioning and object detection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current mixed-domain (general-purpose + CCTV surveillance) image-caption datasets are limited, hindering VLM performance across these domains.

Method: A large-scale multimodal dataset with 141,364 images, 981,947 image-level captions, 1,742,264 region-level captions, and 1,391,779 bounding boxes, offering an average of 7 captions per image and 7 region captions per bounding box covering object categories, sizes, colors, actions, states, and environmental context.

Result: Lightweight VLMs (SmolVLM-256M-Instruct, BLIP, BLIP2, Qwen2.5-VL 3B-Instruct) effectively fine-tuned on the dataset show improved performance on image captioning and object detection.

Conclusion: The proposed dataset and code are publicly available, and they address the gap in mixed-domain image-caption resources for both general and surveillance applications.

Abstract: Despite recent progress in Vision-Language Models (VLMs), mixed-domain image-caption datasets for both general-purpose and CCTV-based video surveillance systems remain limited. To address this gap, we introduce a large-scale multimodal dataset comprising 141,364 images, 981,947 image-level captions, 1,742,264 region-level captions, and 1,391,779 bounding box annotations. Each image is associated with an average of seven image-level captions describing different aspects of the overall scene, as well as seven region-level captions for each annotated bounding box. These complementary caption types are designed to help VLMs learn fine-grained visual attributes, including object categories, estimated sizes, colors, actions, states, and surrounding environmental context. We demonstrate the effectiveness of the dataset on two important downstream tasks: image captioning and object detection. Experimental results show that lightweight VLMs, including SmolVLM-256M-Instruct, BLIP, BLIP2, and Qwen2.5-VL 3B-Instruct, can be effectively fine-tuned using our dataset. Our dataset and code are publicly available at https://zenodo.org/records/20418601.

</details>


### [89] [Synergistic Dual-Branch Adaptation for Multi-modal Generalized Category Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21446)
*Yuxun Qu,Minyu Zhou,Yongqiang Tang,Chenyang Zhang,Wensheng Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SDBA is a plug-and-play dual-branch framework for Generalized Category Discovery that enhances cross-modal synergy by inserting adapters with visual information injection into text encoding and enforcing consistent local neighborhood distributions between modalities, achieving SOTA on six benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multi-modal GCD methods suffer from coarse cross-modal synergy: modalities are encoded independently without correcting bias/noise from derived texts, and mutual learning is limited to global class-level anchors lacking fine-grained relational supervision.

Method: SDBA has two components: (1) cross-modal synergistic adapter that inserts lightweight adapters into both visual and text branches and injects visual information into the text adapter at each encoder layer; (2) neighborhood mutual learning module that enforces consistent local neighborhood distributions between branches via bidirectional KL divergence for fine-grained relational supervision.

Result: State-of-the-art performance on six benchmarks with consistent improvements across different baselines (GET, TextGCD), demonstrating broad scalability.

Conclusion: The proposed plug-and-play SDBA framework effectively addresses cross-modal synergy limitations in dual-branch GCD methods by providing fine-grained relational supervision and enhancing text feature learning during encoding.

Abstract: Generalized Category Discovery (GCD) aims to classify old categories and discover new ones from unlabeled data. Recent multi-modal approaches introduce retrieved or synthesized texts into a dual-branch architecture to provide semantic cues complementary to visual features. However, the cross-modal synergy in existing dual-branch methods remains coarse and incomplete: the two modalities are encoded independently with the bias and noise in the derived text left unaddressed during encoding, and existing mutual learning strategies operate only on global class-level anchors, lacking fine-grained relational supervision. To address these limitations, we propose the Synergistic Dual-Branch Adaptation (SDBA) framework, which serves as a plug-and-play enhancement compatible with existing dual-branch methods such as GET and TextGCD. SDBA comprises two components: the cross-modal synergistic adapter inserts lightweight adapters into both branches and further injects visual information into the text adapter at each encoder layer to enhance text feature learning during encoding; the neighborhood mutual learning module enforces consistent local neighborhood distributions between the two branches via bidirectional KL divergence, providing fine-grained relational supervision for both old and new classes. Extensive experiments on six benchmarks demonstrate state-of-the-art performance, and consistent improvements on different baselines validate the broad scalability of the proposed framework.

</details>


### [90] [Technical Report for ICRA 2026 GOOSE 2D Fine-Grained Semantic Segmentation Challenge: Exploring Query-Based Segmentation and Increased Spatial Context for Outdoor Scene Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21456)
*David Pascual-Hernández,Roberto Calvo-Palomino,Inmaculada Mora-Jiménez,Jose María Cañas-Plaza*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper presents a submission to the GOOSE 2D Fine-Grained Semantic Segmentation Challenge, using SegFormer as baseline and improving with larger crop sizes, Mask2Former architecture, and test-time augmentation, achieving 69.6% mIoU on the test set.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of fine-grained semantic segmentation in outdoor environments using the GOOSE and GOOSE-Ex datasets from 4 camera setups with 56 fine-grained classes and 11 broader categories.

Method: Starting from SegFormer baseline, progressively improved by increasing training crop sizes, switching to query-based Mask2Former architecture, and applying test-time augmentation.

Result: Final submission achieves 69.6% mIoU on the challenge test set, with query-based segmentation and larger crop sizes providing substantial gains.

Conclusion: Query-based segmentation with larger training crops significantly improves fine-grained segmentation; code and weights are released publicly for reproducibility.

Abstract: In this report, we present our submission to the GOOSE 2D Fine-Grained Semantic Segmentation Challenge, organized as part of the Workshop on Field Robotics at ICRA 2026. The challenge combines data from the GOOSE and GOOSE-Ex datasets, which comprise more than 13k images captured from 4 distinct camera setups, annotated using a hierarchical taxonomy of 56 fine-grained classes and 11 broader categories. Starting from SegFormer as a baseline, we progressively improve segmentation performance through increased training crop sizes, a transition to the query-based Mask2Former architecture, and test-time augmentation. Our experiments show that query-based segmentation significantly outperforms the baseline model. Furthermore, increasing the crop size used during training yields substantial gains, highlighting the relevance of preserving scene context for fine-grained semantic disambiguation. Our final submission, using test-time augmentation, achieves an mIoU of 69.6% on the challenge test set, providing a strong baseline for fine-grained semantic segmentation in outdoor environments. To facilitate reproducibility and future research, code and weights will be made publicly available at https://github.com/RoboticsLabURJC/outdoor-fine-grained-segmentation .

</details>


### [91] [Native space based pipelines outperform template space based pipeline in subcortical segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21463)
*Tomás Lima,Daniel Novák,Eduard Bakštein*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This study develops UNet-based segmentation pipelines for subcortical brain structures (STN, RN, SN) in native space, demonstrating superiority over template-based methods at 7T, but revealing a persistent domain gap when transferring to 3T clinical images that synthetic data augmentation only modestly addresses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Template-based coregistration for subcortical segmentation may compromise patient-specific accuracy, especially in small structures. The need to evaluate whether native-space approaches offer measurable advantages for movement disorder applications (e.g., Parkinson's Disease) motivated this study.

Method: Two UNet-based pipelines were developed for segmenting STN, RN, and SN using 7T and 3T MRI from five public datasets. Native-space vs. template-space segmentation was compared, template resolution effects were investigated, and 7T-to-3T transferability was tested with synthetic 3T data generated via disentangled representation learning.

Result: Native-space outperformed template-space segmentation at 7T (STN Dice: 0.775 vs. 0.713; HD95: 0.79 mm vs. 1.17 mm). Increasing template resolution did not improve accuracy. All models showed significant performance drops when applied to 3T images; synthetic 3T data yielded only modest improvements without degrading 7T performance.

Conclusion: Native-space segmentation is preferable for patient-specific subcortical segmentation in surgical planning for Parkinson's Disease. Bridging the 7T-to-3T domain gap remains an open challenge, motivating future work on domain adaptation for subcortical structures.

Abstract: Accurate segmentation of subcortical regions is critical for neurosurgical planning and functional research. Most automated methods rely on template space coregistration, which may compromise patient-specific accuracy, particularly in small structures. We identify a need to evaluate whether native space approaches offer a measurable advantage, which we evaluate in the context of movement disorders. We developed two UNet-based segmentation pipelines of the Subthalamic Nucleus (STN) - a common surgical target in Parkinson's Disease - and the neighbouring Red Nucleus (RN) and Substantia Nigra (SN). We collected 7T and 3T MRI data from five public datasets. The pipelines were evaluated in the native-space against manual labels. We further investigated the effect of the template resolution. Motivated by the hypothesis that models may better learn target boundaries in higher field, we tested the transferability of 7T-trained models to 3T clinical images, and whether synthetic 3T training data - generated via a disentangled representation learning method - could help bridging this domain gap. On held-out 7T data, the native pipeline consistently outperformed the template one. For the STN, native-space Dice reached 0.775 +- 0.055 versus 0.713 +- 0.051 (1 mm template), with HD95 of 0.79 +- 0.24 mm versus 1.17 +- 1.10 mm, respectively. Similar advantages were observed for the RN and SN. Increasing template resolution did not improve accuracy. When applied to 3T images, all models showed a considerable performance drop. Adding synthetic 3T data yielded only modest improvements, though without degrading 7T performance. Native-space segmentation is preferable for applications requiring patient specific anatomical fidelity, such as the surgical planning in PD. Bridging the 7T-to-3T domain gap remains an open challenge, motivating future work on domain adaptation tailored to subcortical structures.

</details>


### [92] [Semi-Supervised Vision-Language-Action Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21493)
*Hongyang He,Jiuming Liu,Victor Sanchez*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SemiVLA is a self-distilled teacher-student framework for semi-supervised VLA robot adaptation that uses a reliability controller and Bottleneck-Projected Alignment Update to generate reliable pseudo-actions from unlabeled trajectories, achieving 89.0% success on LIBERO with only 10% labeled data, outperforming supervised LoRA by 8.0 points.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Adapting Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models to new environments requires costly action-labeled demonstrations. The authors aim to reduce this dependence by enabling semi-supervised adaptation where only a small portion of trajectories have action labels, while leveraging abundant unlabeled vision-language observations.

Method: The authors propose SemiVLA, a self-distilled teacher-student framework that: (1) generates reliable pseudo-actions on unlabeled trajectories, (2) introduces a VLA-specific reliability controller that assesses vision-language alignment, action feasibility, and temporal transition consistency, and (3) updates the teacher via Bottleneck-Projected Alignment Update to prevent noisy feedback contamination.

Result: Using OpenVLA as the backbone, SemiVLA consistently improves multiple PEFT strategies across LIBERO and CALVIN benchmarks. With 10% labeled trajectories, SemiVLA with Selective LoRA achieves 89.0% average success on LIBERO, outperforming supervised LoRA by 8.0 points without extra inference cost.

Conclusion: SemiVLA effectively addresses semi-supervised VLA adaptation by leveraging unlabeled trajectories through reliable pseudo-action generation, significantly reducing dependence on costly action-labeled demonstrations while improving performance across benchmarks.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models enable robots to predict actions directly from visual observations and language instructions, but adapting them to new environments still depends on costly action-labeled demonstrations. To reduce this dependence, we study semi-supervised VLA adaptation under limited supervision signals, where only a small portion of trajectories contain robot actions and the remaining trajectories provide action-unlabeled vision-language observations. Unlike standard semi-supervised learning, the missing supervision is an embodied action signal that must be visually grounded, language-consistent, physically feasible, and temporally stable. To address this problem, we propose SemiVLA, a self-distilled teacher-student framework that learns from reliable pseudo-actions on unlabeled trajectories. SemiVLA introduces a VLA-specific reliability controller to assess vision-language alignment, action feasibility, and temporal transition consistency, and further updates the teacher through a Bottleneck-Projected Alignment Update to avoid noisy feedback contamination. With OpenVLA as the backbone, SemiVLA consistently improves multiple PEFT strategies across LIBERO and CALVIN. Under 10\% labeled trajectories, SemiVLA with Selective LoRA achieves 89.0\% average success on LIBERO, outperforming supervised LoRA by 8.0 points without extra inference cost.

</details>


### [93] [Compressing Observation History into Agent Memory: Distilling Transformers into Recurrent Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21562)
*Philippe Weinzaepfel,Christian Wolf,Bülent Mert Sariyildiz,Guillaume Bono,Gianluca Monaci*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes a distillation method to transfer the compression strategy of full-history transformers to recurrent transformers, enabling linear-time complexity for long-horizon streaming tasks while narrowing the performance gap between the two architectures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Transformers are computationally prohibitive for long sequences in streaming vision and robotics applications (e.g., map-free pose estimation), and recurrent transformers with fixed-size memory underperform full-history transformers due to the harder learning problem of explicit compression.

Method: A distillation approach where a teacher model (full-history transformer) compresses observation history into a fixed-size bottleneck representation, which is then used to directly supervise the student's (recurrent transformer) memory mechanism, aligning the two compression strategies.

Result: The approach trains a recurrent latent robotic memory with linear-time complexity that substantially narrows the performance gap to full-history transformers.

Conclusion: The performance gap between recurrent and full-history transformers is not due to architectural limitations but differences in learned compression; distillation of compression strategies effectively bridges this gap.

Abstract: Transformers are AI's workhorse with strong performance in modeling sequential data, but their computational cost becomes prohibitive when processing long sequences. We target long-horizon streaming vision and robotics applications like map-free pose estimation, where it is particularly impractical to store and maintain a history of observations. Recurrent Transformers address this limitation by maintaining fixed-size memory but their performance lags behind that of transformers operating over the full observation history. We argue that this gap does not stem from architectural limitations, but from differences in how these models learn to compress past information. Without access to an observation history, recurrent models must explicitly decide what to retain in memory at each step, a significantly harder learning problem. In this work, we propose a distillation approach that transfers the compression strategy of a classical full-history transformer to a recurrent variant. We enable this by designing a teacher model that explicitly compresses its observation history into a fixed-size bottleneck representation. By directly supervising the student's memory with this bottleneck representation, we align the two compression mechanisms. We show that this approach allows to train a recurrent latent robotic memory with linear-time complexity while substantially narrowing the performance gap to full-history transformers.

</details>


### [94] [A Smart Classroom Behavior Analysis Framework with a New Highly Congested Classroom Dataset](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21568)
*Wei Xu,Maoxiang Chu,Yuelong Fan,Guanghao Liao,Yinxiang Yu,Zhi Chen,Haotian Wang,Yutian Zhu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces the HCCB dataset (50,229 instances across 7 behavior categories in crowded classrooms) and proposes ODER-HSFNet, a YOLO-based detection framework with three novel modules (ODER, HSSF, OCDetect) to address challenges of dense co-occurrence, occlusion, scale variation, and fine-grained semantics, achieving state-of-the-art results on HCCB and SCB-D3-S datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Student behavior detection in large-class scenarios is challenging due to dense instance co-occurrence, asymmetric occlusion, depth-wise scale variation, and fine-grained semantic degradation in distant targets. Existing datasets and general-purpose detectors are insufficient to characterize and address these challenges.

Method: The authors construct the HCCB dataset (50,229 instances, 7 categories) and propose ODER-HSFNet, a YOLO-based detector with three novel components: (1) Occlusion-aware Deformable Edge Rectifier (ODER) for strengthening boundary evidence under occlusion, (2) Hypergraph-State Spatial Fusion (HSSF) module integrating local structure enhancement, state-space contextual modeling, and high-order relation aggregation, and (3) Occlusion-Calibrated Detection Head (OCDetect) for suppressing low-quality Pre-NMS candidates and reducing false positives.

Result: ODER-HSFNet achieves 60.60%/80.12% mAP50:95/mAP50 on HCCB and 57.36%/74.65% on SCB-D3-S, outperforming mainstream YOLO-series methods. Ablation studies verify the effectiveness of each proposed component.

Conclusion: The HCCB dataset and ODER-HSFNet framework effectively address the key challenges of highly crowded classroom behavior detection, providing a new benchmark and superior detection performance compared to existing methods.

Abstract: Student behavior detection is important for intelligent classroom analysis but remains challenging in large-class scenarios due to dense instance co-occurrence, asymmetric occlusion, depth-wise scale variation, and fine-grained semantic degradation in distant targets. Existing classroom behavior datasets and general-purpose detectors are insufficient to characterize and address these challenges. This paper constructs the Highly Congested Classroom Behavior (HCCB) dataset, containing 50,229 student behavior instances across seven categories: reading, writing, heads up, sleeping, looking around, bowing head, and using phone. HCCB provides a challenging benchmark that integrates dense distributions, severe occlusion, scale variation, and fine-grained behavioral semantics. To address these issues, we propose ODER-HSFNet, a YOLO-based detection framework tailored to highly crowded classrooms. At its core, ODER-HSFNet introduces three task-specific innovations: the Occlusion-aware Deformable Edge Rectifier (ODER), which strengthens boundary evidence under occlusion; the Hypergraph-State Spatial Fusion (HSSF) module, which integrates local structure enhancement, state-space contextual modeling, and high-order relation aggregation; and the Occlusion-Calibrated Detection Head (OCDetect), which suppresses low-quality Pre-NMS candidates and reduces false positives from occlusion boundaries and neighboring instances. Experiments on two classroom behavior detection datasets show that ODER-HSFNet outperforms mainstream YOLO-series methods, achieving 60.60%/80.12% mAP50:95/mAP50 on HCCB and 57.36%/74.65% on SCB-D3-S. Ablation studies further verify the effectiveness of the proposed design for highly crowded classroom behavior detection.

</details>


### [95] [The Unreasonable Effectiveness of VLMs for Zero-shot Procedural Mistake Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21579)
*Serdar Ozsoy,Lars Doorenbos,Federico Spurio,Gianpiero Francesca,Juergen Gall*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ZeProM is a zero-shot framework using a single pre-trained Video-Language Model to jointly perform procedural mistake detection and temporal action segmentation, eliminating the need for supervised multi-stage pipelines and achieving competitive or superior performance on EgoPER and CaptainCook4D benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing procedural mistake detection methods rely on multi-stage pipelines with supervised components (e.g., temporal action segmentation, error detection modules) that require task-specific training and tailored datasets, limiting their general applicability. The authors aim to overcome this by introducing zero-shot procedural mistake detection.

Method: ZeProM (Zero-shot Procedural Mistake detection) is a unified framework built on a single pre-trained Video-Language Model (VLM) that jointly solves both procedural mistake detection and temporal action segmentation without any task-specific fine-tuning or separate supervised modules.

Result: On the EgoPER benchmark, ZeProM achieves a 4.4 point improvement in EDA and a 2.0 point improvement in F1@.5 on average over all five tasks compared to the strongest supervised methods. It approaches or outperforms fully supervised methods across both EgoPER and CaptainCook4D benchmarks.

Conclusion: The results demonstrate the potential of unified zero-shot methods for procedural mistake detection, suggesting the field should move away from complex multi-stage pipelines toward more generalizable solutions.

Abstract: Procedural mistake detection is important for quality control and user assistance across many disciplines. Recent work in this field has achieved significant gains by using the reasoning capabilities of Video-Language Models (VLMs) as components within multi-stage pipelines, which consist of separate modules for supervised temporal action segmentation, error detection, and explainability. Consequently, they remain dependent on tailored training datasets and require task-specific training, limiting their wider applicability. To remedy this, we introduce zero-shot procedural mistake detection and propose a unified Zero-shot Procedural Mistake detection (ZeProM) framework that jointly solves procedural mistake detection and temporal action segmentation with a single pre-trained VLM. By evaluating our framework on two canonical mistake detection benchmarks, EgoPER and CaptainCook4D, we find that ZeProM can perform these tasks successfully, while approaching, or even outperforming, the performance of fully supervised methods. For instance, we achieve a 4.4 point improvement in EDA and a 2.0 point improvement in F1@.5 on average over all five EgoPER tasks compared to the strongest supervised methods. Overall, our results show the potential of unified methods for procedural mistake detection, and we hope this will steer the field away from highly complex pipelines and toward more generally applicable solutions.

</details>


### [96] [Radial Basis Function Networks as Projection Heads in Self-Supervised Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21590)
*Andreas Schliebitz,Heiko Tapken,Martin Atzmueller*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes replacing the MLP projection head in SSL with a Radial Basis Function Network (RBFN), enabling label-free quality assessment via a novel Scale-Normalized Separation (SNS) metric. RBFN heads are competitive drop-in replacements across multiple SSL architectures and datasets, and SNS strongly correlates with standard logistic regression metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard SSL discards the projection head after training and requires costly linear probing with labeled data to assess backbone quality. This is computationally wasteful and inefficient.

Method: Replace the MLP projection head with an RBFN (three RBF layers, Gaussian radial basis function). Derive SNS (Scale-Normalized Separation) metric from learned kernel centers and shapes to evaluate representation quality without labels or a separate classifier.

Result: RBFN heads are competitive drop-in replacements for MLP projectors across MoCo, SimCLR, BYOL, SwAV, SimSiam and four datasets. SNS shows strong to very strong positive correlation with logistic regression metrics. A new PyTorch-compatible Open Images V7 dataset is also published.

Conclusion: RBFN projectors can serve as reliable proxies for backbone representation quality while reducing computational waste, and the SNS metric enables label-free quality assessment.

Abstract: Self-supervised learning (SSL) typically relies on a backbone encoder followed by a small multilayer perceptron (MLP) projection head, which is conventionally discarded after training, while backbone quality is assessed via costly linear probing on labeled data. We argue that this approach including discarding the projector is rather computationally wasteful. Instead, we propose replacing the MLP head with a radial basis function network (RBFN), whose interpretable center and shape parameters can be exploited to judge representation quality without labels or a separate classifier. To this end, we introduce Scale-Normalized Separation (SNS), a novel label-free quality metric derived solely from the kernel centers and shapes learned during training. Across five canonical SSL architectures (MoCo, SimCLR, BYOL, SwAV and SimSiam) and four image classification datasets, we show that RBFN projection heads are competitive drop-in replacements for standard MLP projectors. We recommend constructing them with three RBF layers activated by the Gaussian radial basis function. Moreover, SNS exhibits strong to very strong positive correlation with established logistic regression metrics, demonstrating that a trained RBFN projector can act as a reliable proxy for backbone representation quality. We additionally publish a novel PyTorch compatible image classification dataset based on Google's Open Images V7 to facilitate reproducible research into representation learning.

</details>


### [97] [Boundary-by-Mask: Few-Shot Instance Segmentation with Mask-Conditioned Boundary Learning for Texture-Poor Industrial Parts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21594)
*Yutaka Yoshinaga,Naoya Chiba,Koichi Hashimoto*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Boundary-by-Mask is a few-shot instance segmentation framework that uses a foundation-model encoder and a lightweight Signed Distance Function (SDF) head to predict boundary-aware distance maps from RGB images and instance masks, enabling robust segmentation on low-texture industrial surfaces with application-specific instance definitions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing instance segmentation models perform well on general images but fail in industrial scenarios due to (1) application-specific and inconsistent instance definitions, and (2) large domain gaps caused by weak textures and limited contextual cues in industrial imagery.

Method: The framework uses a foundation-model encoder to extract visual features from few RGB images and instance masks, trains a lightweight SDF head to predict boundary-aware distance maps, and reconstructs segmentation masks via SDF-to-mask. A pixel-wise shallow MLP head enables fast training. Instance definition is conditioned by the input mask, allowing flexible target specification.

Result: Strong few-shot generalization, robustness in feature-poor (low-texture, color-uniform) conditions, and precise control over mask-level targets, validated on industrial parts and food items with ambiguous boundaries.

Conclusion: Supervising boundaries rather than interior appearance via SDF prediction enables reliable instance segmentation in challenging industrial settings, with flexible instance definition through mask conditioning.

Abstract: Recent advances in large pre-trained models have led to remarkable progress in instance segmentation on general images. However, industrial scenarios remain challenging. Instance definitions are often application-specific and inconsistent, and the domain gap from general imagery is substantial due to weak textures and limited contextual cues. Consequently, a direct application of existing models is unreliable. We propose Boundary-by-Mask, a few-shot instance segmentation framework that supervises boundaries instead of interior appearance. Given a few RGB images and corresponding instance masks, the method extracts rich visual features using a foundation-model encoder and trains a lightweight Signed Distance Function (SDF) head to predict boundary-aware distance maps. Segmentation masks are obtained through an SDF-to-mask reconstruction process. By explicitly estimating contours, the framework achieves reliable instance separation even on low-texture and color-uniform surfaces. The instance definition is conditioned by the instance mask. Replacing the mask specifies the segmentation target, such as the whole object or a sub-part. A pixel-wise shallow MLP head enables rapid training. Experiments on industrial parts and food items with ambiguous boundaries show strong few-shot generalization, robustness in feature-poor conditions, and precise control over mask-level targets.

</details>


### [98] [$φ$-Scene: Physically Grounded Image-to-3D Scene Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21596)
*Haodong Li,Lulu Shao,Haolin Lu,Yu Fu,Yen-Ru Chen,Seemandhar Jain,Manmohan Chandraker*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: φ-Scene is a physically grounded approach for open-vocabulary compositional image-to-3D scene reconstruction that treats scenes as stable physical systems, using topology-driven physical assembly with SDF-based penetration resolution and rigid-body simulation to produce physically valid 3D scenes with reduced artifacts and improved stability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing single-image 3D scene reconstruction methods treat the problem as purely visual/geometric prediction, producing outputs with floating objects, interpenetrations, and unstable-contact artifacts that lack physical validity and limit usability in simulation, robotics, and interactive environments.

Method: φ-Scene formulates reconstruction as topology-driven physical assembly: it infers object support relationships, orders objects topologically, and progressively settles each object against its stabilized support context. For each object, SDF-based optimization resolves penetrations against the pre-settled support context, followed by rigid-body simulation to achieve stable contact under real-world physical constraints.

Result: On 3D-Front, φ-Scene achieves the strongest overall performance among out-of-domain methods and remains highly competitive with in-domain baselines. Human and VLM evaluations show strong preference for φ-Scene in visual quality, reference alignment, and physical plausibility. Dedicated metrics show substantial reduction in penetration artifacts and lower post-simulation drift.

Conclusion: Treating scene reconstruction as physical assembly rather than pure geometric prediction yields more physically valid, stable 3D scenes, enabling better downstream usability in simulation and robotics.

Abstract: Reconstructing compositional 3D scenes from a single image is a fundamental challenge in 3D world modeling. Recent methods can recover high-fidelity, complete 3D objects and predict plausible scene arrangements, but most still treat scene reconstruction primarily as a visual and geometric prediction problem. Their outputs may therefore contain floating objects, interpenetrations, or unstable-contact artifacts, limiting their physical validity and downstream usability in simulation, robotics, and interactive environments. We present $φ$-Scene, a physically grounded approach to open-vocabulary and compositional image-to-3D scene reconstruction. The key premise is that a reconstructed scene should not be treated merely as a set of objects with predicted poses, but as a stable physical system. Accordingly, $φ$-Scene formulates reconstruction as topology-driven physical assembly: it infers how objects support one another, orders them accordingly, and progressively settles each object against its already stabilized support context. For each object in topological order, SDF-based optimization first resolves penetrations against the pre-settled support context, and rigid-body simulation then settles the object into a stable contact configuration under real-world physical constraints. Experiments on 3D-Front show that $φ$-Scene achieves the strongest overall performance among out-of-domain methods and remains highly competitive with in-domain baselines. Human and VLM evaluations further show strong preference for $φ$-Scene in visual quality, reference alignment, and physical plausibility. Finally, dedicated physical plausibility metrics covering static contact quality and dynamic stability demonstrate that $φ$-Scene substantially reduces penetration artifacts while producing much lower post-simulation drift, indicating more stable and physically grounded 3D scenes.

</details>


### [99] [$μ$Match: Foundation Models for Semi-supervised Learning and Domain Adaptation in EM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21605)
*Marei Freitag,Olesia Korchevaia,Luca Freckmann,Anwai Archit,Constantin Pape*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: μMatch is a semi-supervised learning and domain adaptation framework that leverages vision foundation models (SAM, SAM2, μSAM, DINOv2/v3) with student-teacher methods to improve electron microscopy segmentation, reducing annotation effort while achieving consistent improvements over baselines on mitochondrion, nucleus, and neurite segmentation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Electron microscopy (EM) segmentation relies heavily on supervised learning requiring extensive manual annotation due to the diversity of segmentation tasks and scarcity of comprehensively annotated data, limiting ultrastructural analysis. Foundation models have been successful in other biomedical imaging modalities but their application in EM has been limited to specific organelles.

Method: μMatch: a semi-supervised learning and domain adaptation framework that implements state-of-the-art student-teacher-based methods and leverages multiple vision foundation models (SAM, SAM2, μSAM, DINOv2/v3) for EM segmentation tasks including mitochondrion, nucleus, and neurite segmentation.

Result: Consistent improvements over strong baselines on challenging EM segmentation tasks, demonstrating a path toward substantially reducing annotation effort in EM.

Conclusion: The proposed framework effectively addresses the gap of foundation model application in EM by combining semi-supervised learning with foundation models, offering a viable approach to reduce manual annotation burden while improving segmentation performance across multiple ultrastructural targets.

Abstract: Vision foundation models have substantially advanced computer vision, enabling state-of-the-art performance in zero- and few-shot settings. They have been successfully applied to biomedical imaging tasks ranging from organ segmentation in computed tomography to cell segmentation in light microscopy. Electron microscopy (EM) is a central modality for analyzing cellular ultrastructure due to its nanometer-scale resolution. However, the application of foundation models in EM has so far been limited to specific organelles, such as mitochondria, largely due to the diversity of segmentation tasks and the scarcity of comprehensively annotated data. As a result, EM segmentation still predominantly relies on supervised learning, requiring extensive manual annotation and limiting ultrastructural analysis. To address this gap, we propose $μ$Match, a framework for semi-supervised learning and domain adaptation that leverages foundation models. We implement state-of-the-art student-teacher-based methods and evaluate multiple foundation models (SAM, SAM2, $μ$SAM, DINOv2/v3) on challenging EM tasks, including mitochondrion, nucleus, and neurite segmentation. Our results demonstrate consistent improvements over strong baselines and highlight a path toward substantially reducing the annotation effort in EM.

</details>


### [100] [T-MOR: Learning Motion-Aware Skeleton Representations for Human Action Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21607)
*Di Yang,Mahmoud Ali,Quan Kong,Gianpiero Francesca,Francois Bremond*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: T-MOR learns transferable action representations from skeleton sequences using multi-modal contrastive learning (skeleton, video, text). It introduces PoseCap-1M dataset (1M+ video-skeleton-text triplets) and achieves strong performance on action classification and temporal detection benchmarks, including few-shot and zero-shot settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing vision-language models like CLIP rely on appearance-level supervision and do not explicitly model human motion, which is essential for fine-grained action recognition where actions are defined by temporally structured body movements.

Method: T-MOR uses a multi-modal contrastive learning scheme that aligns skeleton motion representations with visual (video) and textual (language) representations during training. Inference requires only lightweight skeleton inputs. The method is pre-trained on PoseCap-1M, a large-scale dataset of over one million synchronized video, skeleton, and text triplets.

Result: T-MOR achieves consistent performance improvements across multiple action recognition benchmarks (Toyota Smarthome, Penn Action, UAV-Human, TSU, Charades) for both action classification and frame-wise temporal detection. It also shows strong generalization in few-shot and zero-shot settings.

Conclusion: T-MOR demonstrates the effectiveness of motion-centric and embodied representations for transferable action understanding, enabling strong performance with lightweight skeleton inputs at inference while benefiting from multi-modal supervision during training.

Abstract: Vision-language models such as CLIP have recently achieved strong performance on a wide range of visual understanding tasks. However, most existing models rely primarily on appearance-level supervision from images or videos, and do not explicitly model human motion, which is essential for fine-grained and human-centric action recognition task as actions are defined by temporally structured and physically grounded body movements. To address this problem, we propose Transferable skeleton MOtion Representation (T-MOR), a motion-aware framework that learns transferable action representations from skeleton sequences with the aid of video and language supervision during training. T-MOR adopts a multi-modal contrastive learning scheme that aligns skeleton motion with visual and textual representations, while performing inference using only lightweight skeleton inputs. To support large-scale pre-training, we construct PoseCap-1M, a new dataset that contains over one million synchronized video, skeleton, and text triplets covering diverse human activities. We evaluate T-MOR on a range of human-centric action recognition benchmarks, including action classification and frame-wise temporal detection. Experimental results show that T-MOR consistently improves performance across multiple datasets, such as Toyota Smarthome, Penn Action, UAV-Human, TSU, and Charades. In addition, T-MOR demonstrates strong generalization ability in few-shot and zero-shot settings, highlighting the effectiveness of motion-centric and embodied representations for transferable action understanding.

</details>


### [101] [CurvSegFlow: Time-Conditioned Flow Matching for Robust Segmentation of Curvilinear Structures in Noisy Biomedical Images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21608)
*Sidi Mohamed Sid'El Moctar,Achraf Ait Laydi,Alexandre Beber,Marcus Braun,Zdenek Lansky,Yousef El Mourabit,Helene Bouvrais*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CurvSegFlow uses time-conditioned flow matching to progressively refine noisy initializations into accurate segmentations of curvilinear structures (e.g., microtubules, vessels, nerves, arteries), achieving superior performance under low SNR conditions across multiple biomedical imaging modalities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate segmentation of curvilinear structures in biomedical imaging is challenging due to thin geometry, complex topology, and noise, especially in microscopy of cytoskeletal networks where low SNR and filament crossings cause fragmented results.

Method: CurvSegFlow models segmentation as a dynamic process using time-conditioned flow matching, combining a U-Net backbone with triple-term loss function and temporal embeddings to progressively refine noisy initializations into target structures via a learned velocity field.

Result: Evaluated on synthetic and real microtubule datasets, plus public benchmarks of retinal vessels, corneal nerves, and coronary arteries. Achieves competitive/superior performance with consistent improvements in precision and structural continuity, especially under low SNR conditions.

Conclusion: Flow-based iterative refinement provides a robust and general framework for curvilinear structure segmentation, improving quality in challenging imaging conditions and generalizing across modalities without architectural changes.

Abstract: Accurate segmentation of curvilinear structures remains challenging in biomedical imaging due to their thin geometry, complex topology, and sensitivity to noise. This is particularly critical for microscopy images of cytoskeletal network, where low signal-to-noise ratios and dense filament crossings often lead to fragmented or inaccurate segmentation. In this work, we propose CurvSegFlow, a segmentation framework based on time-conditioned flow matching. Instead of predicting a segmentation mask in a single pass, the method models segmentation as a dynamic process that progressively refines a noisy initialization into the target structure through a learned velocity field. The proposed model combines a U-Net backbone with triple-term loss function and temporal embeddings to guide the refinement process across reconstruction stages. This formulation enables gradual error correction and improves the continuity of thin structures. CurvSegFlow is evaluated on multiple synthetic and real microtubule datasets, as well as on public benchmarks of retinal vessels, corneal nerves and coronary arteries. Across datasets, the method achieves competitive or superior performance compared to established segmentation models, with consistent improvements in precision and structural continuity, particularly under low signal-to-noise conditions. These results show that flow-based iterative refinement provides a robust and general framework for curvilinear structure segmentation. Overall, the proposed approach improves segmentation quality in challenging imaging conditions and generalizes effectively across modalities without architectural changes.

</details>


### [102] [Cross-Modal Corroboration for Annotation-Free Wildlife Monitoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21613)
*Bharath Pillai,Varun Viswapriyan,Christopher Stewart,Tanya Berger-Wolf,Jenna Kline*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes an annotation-free multimodal wildlife monitoring pipeline that validates itself by aligning independently derived hourly activity curves from vision and acoustic sensors with published behavioral priors, demonstrated on Milu deer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scaling wildlife monitoring for real-world conservation is hindered by severe annotation scarcity; the authors aim to eliminate the need for costly manual labels by leveraging expert knowledge as a validation signal.
