Method: The pipeline combines (1) a vision module using BioCLIP 2 for zero-shot species detection, sliced inference for constrained camera angles, and geometry-based localization; (2) an acoustic module with a fine-tuned classifier for vocalization detection; and (3) a three-way convergence metric that aligns hourly activity curves from both modalities with published behavioral priors to serve as annotation-free validation.

Result: Both vision and acoustic modalities independently recovered activity patterns consistent with known Milu deer behavioral ecology, requiring minimal manual annotation.

Conclusion: The framework offers a practical, self-validating path for scaling conservation monitoring across species detectable in both visual and acoustic modalities with documented behavioral priors.

Abstract: Scaling wildlife monitoring for real-world conservation deployments requires automated analysis of smart sensors that operate under severe annotation scarcity. We propose leveraging expert knowledge of species activity patterns as an annotation-free validation signal for multimodal monitoring pipelines. We operationalize agreement as the alignment of independently derived hourly activity curves both with each other and with published behavioral priors-a three-way convergence that rules out shared-data confounds and dataset-internal correlation as alternative explanations. Our vision pipeline combines zero-shot species detection via BioCLIP 2, sliced inference to handle deployment-constrained camera positioning, and geometry-based geographic localization from camera trap imagery. Our acoustic pipeline detects species vocalizations via a fine-tuned classifier. We validate the pipeline on a breeding herd of Milu deer and demonstrate that both modalities independently recover activity patterns consistent with known deer behavioral ecology with minimal manual annotation. The framework applies to species detectable in both visual and acoustic modalities for which behavioral priors are documented in the literature, suggesting a practical path toward self-validating wildlife-monitoring pipelines at conservation scale.

</details>


### [103] [A DVDrive Approach for doScenes Instructed Driving Challenge](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21623)
*Zijian Fu,Xiangyang Chu,Mengshi Qi,Huadong Ma,Guanghao Zhang,Wei Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper presents an adaptation of OmniDrive for the doScenes Instructed Driving Challenge, introducing a divided-view perception module (DVPE-style) that performs visibility-aware cross-attention within local view spaces to improve language-instruction grounding for trajectory prediction, achieving better alignment between natural language commands and multi-view visual features.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the emerging problem of instruction-conditioned trajectory prediction in autonomous driving, where models must predict future ego trajectories based on visual context, historical motion, and natural-language maneuver instructions.

Method: Adapts OmniDrive (a vision-language-action driving agent) to the doScenes setting by training on instruction-annotated nuScenes scenes and generating 6-second ego trajectories (12 waypoints). Introduces a DVPE-style divided-view perception module that groups query features and image tokens into divided local view spaces and performs visibility-aware cross-attention within each view, replacing global cross-attention over all camera features.

Result: The divided-view perception module reduces irrelevant cross-view interference and improves alignment between language instructions and local driving-relevant visual evidence, leading to better multi-view visual grounding for instruction-conditioned trajectory prediction.

Conclusion: The proposed approach effectively adapts OmniDrive for the doScenes challenge, and the divided-view perception module enhances the model's ability to ground language instructions in multi-view visual context for trajectory prediction.

Abstract: Instruction-conditioned trajectory prediction is an emerging problem in autonomous driving, where a model predicts the future ego trajectory not only from visual scene context and historical motion, but also from a natural-language maneuver instruction. This paper presents our submission to the doScenes Instructed Driving Challenge, built upon OmniDrive, a vision-language-action driving agent with 3D perception, reasoning, and planning capabilities. We adapt OmniDrive to the doScenes setting by training it on instruction-annotated nuScenes scenes and generating a 6-second ego trajectory represented by 12 future waypoints. To improve multi-view visual grounding, we further introduce a DVPE-style divided-view perception module into the OmniDrive perception head. Instead of attending globally to all camera features, the proposed module groups query features and image tokens into divided local view spaces and performs visibility-aware cross-attention within each view. This design reduces irrelevant cross-view interference and helps the model better align language instructions with local driving-relevant visual evidence. The code is publicly available at: https://github.com/feel12348/doscenes-omnidrive.

</details>


### [104] [Chehre: An Emoji-Prompted Video Dataset for Perceptually Diverse Facial Expression Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21657)
*Bita Azari,Zoe Stanley,Avneet Batra,Poorvi Bhatia,Hali Kil,Manolis Savva,Angelica Lim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Chehre is a novel emoji-prompted video dataset for dynamic facial expression recognition, featuring 2,111 videos from 203 performers with 902 annotator validations. It introduces two benchmarks: dominant (top-1 accuracy) and distributional (spread ratio) expression recognition. Current vision-language models perform poorly (best 32.5% Top-1 accuracy), highlighting the challenge of capturing inter-individual perceptual diversity in facial expressions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing facial expression recognition datasets rely on static images, basic emotion categories, or single deterministic annotations, failing to capture the dynamic nature and inter-individual perceptual diversity of real-world facial expressions. Chehre addresses these gaps by providing a video dataset with diverse, distributional annotations.

Method: Participants were prompted to express and record 40 facial emojis. Facial motions were transferred onto synthetic faces for privacy. A separate group provided emoji and label annotations. Two tasks defined: dominant expression recognition (top-1 accuracy) and distributional expression recognition (Spread Ratio). Vision-language models evaluated via random sampling and persona prompting.

Result: Best-performing model achieves only 32.5% Top-1 accuracy on dominant expression recognition. Spread Ratio on distributional recognition is well below the human reference, indicating models fail to capture perceptual diversity.

Conclusion: Both benchmark tasks are challenging for current vision-language models, and Chehre serves as a valuable benchmark for evaluating diverse, dynamic, and distributional facial expression recognition.

Abstract: Facial expressions are nonverbal social signals used in human interaction, but facial expression recognition datasets often focus on static images, basic emotion categories, or single deterministic annotations. We introduce Chehre, an emoji-prompted video dataset for analyzing dynamic facial expressions across a wide range of expressions for exploring inter-individual perceptual diversity. In Chehre, participants were prompted to express and record 40 facial emojis. Later, their facial motions were transferred onto synthetic faces to preserve privacy. A separate group of annotators analyzed the anonymized videos using emoji and label annotations, resulting in 2,111 high quality videos collected from 203 performers and validated by 902 annotators. We define two benchmark tasks: dominant expression recognition, which tests whether models recover the top human-rated labels, and distributional expression recognition, which tests whether models capture the diversity of human responses. We benchmark recent vision-language models using random sampling and persona prompting to generate multiple predictions per video. Results show that both tasks are challenging: among the models evaluated, the best-performing model achieves only 32.5% Top-1 accuracy on dominant expression recognition and a Spread Ratio well below the human reference on distributional recognition. Chehre provides a benchmark for evaluating diverse, dynamic, and distributional facial expression recognition

</details>


### [105] [UnityShots: Memory-Driven Multi-Shot Audio-Video Generation with Boundary-Aware Gating](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21661)
*Jiehui Huang,Yuechen Zhang,Bin Xia,Jiahao Wang,Xu He,Zhenchao Tang,Meng Chu,Xin Tao,Pengfei Wan,Jiaya Jia*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: UnityShots is a memory-driven multi-shot audio-video generation system using fixed-size long-term and short-term memory slots, a boundary-conditioned gate, and a discrete cut-type prior to achieve coherent cross-shot consistency, outperforming open-source baselines and matching closed-source systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multi-shot video generation methods either cannot scale beyond fixed-length sequences, use linearly growing memory banks, or lack multi-shot-aware backbones. The goal is to achieve persistent cross-shot coherence in subject appearance, scene context, and speaker identity.

Method: UnityShots builds on LTX-2.3 with two fixed-size memory slots: a long-term memory (LTM) slot anchored to the opening shot and a short-term memory (STM) slot for the preceding tail, updated via a boundary-conditioned gate fusing visual cut probability and beat-tracker signals. Audio uses a reference speaker token per shot. A discrete cut-type prior learned via AdaLN enables inference-time transition control.

Result: Evaluated on I2V, T2V, and R2V tasks, UnityShots leads open-source baselines on all cross-shot coherence metrics and matches the strongest closed-source system on multi-shot axes. A benchmark of 200 multi-cultural multi-shot sequences across six regions and ten+ languages is also released.

Conclusion: The fixed-size memory architecture with boundary-conditioned gating and discrete cut-type control effectively enables scalable, coherent multi-shot audio-video generation without linear memory growth.

Abstract: Generating a coherent multi-shot video requires structured cross-shot memory. Subject appearance, scene context, and speaker identity must persist across cuts. Existing approaches either train end-to-end over fixed-length sequences and cannot scale, generate shot-by-shot with memory banks that grow linearly, or orchestrate pretrained generators under an LLM planner without a multi-shot-aware backbone. We present UnityShots, a memory-driven multi-shot audio-video generation system built on LTX-2.3, trained on annotated cinematic and music-video shots. The video stream maintains two fixed-size slots, a long-term memory (LTM) slot anchored to the opening shot and a short-term memory (STM) slot holding the immediately preceding tail, both updated at every cut by a boundary-conditioned gate that fuses visual cut probability and beat-tracker signals. The audio stream injects a reference speaker token at every shot to preserve vocal timbre without a sliding audio bank. A discrete cut-type prior, learned through AdaLN, becomes an inference-time control knob over transition strength. We release a benchmark of $200$ multi-cultural multi-shot sequences spanning six ethnic regions and ten or more languages, with per-shot reference identities, reference audio, and per-boundary transition labels. Evaluated across I2V, T2V, and R2V conditioning modes, UnityShots leads open-source baselines on every cross-shot coherence metric and matches the strongest closed-source system on the multi-shot axes.

</details>


### [106] [Enlight: Fast Low-Light Image Enhancement via Multi-Objective Optimization and Shadow-Aware Refinement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21674)
*Nirjhor Datta,M. Sohel Rahman*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ENLIGHT is a training-free, zero-shot framework for low-light image enhancement that directly optimizes a perceptual objective at inference time using a two-stage global-to-local strategy, achieving competitive perceptual quality with low inference time.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing deep learning methods for low-light image enhancement require large-scale training data and supervision, lack interpretability, and incur high computational costs. ENLIGHT aims to provide a fast, training-free, and interpretable alternative.

Method: The method uses a two-stage global-to-local optimization: (1) global illumination adjustment maintaining structural consistency and noise control, (2) shadow-aware refinement via masked local optimization. Two modes exist: Fast (multi-objective with entropy, gradient preservation, noise regularization) and Ultrafast (lightweight approximation). The framework is optimizer-agnostic, supporting evolutionary and local search methods.

Result: Evaluated on BAID, Backlit300, LIME, MEF, NPE, and DICM; achieves competitive perceptual quality (MUSIQ, NIQE, BRISQUE) with significantly lower inference time compared to learning-based methods. Qualitative results show improved contrast, preserved structural details, and controlled noise amplification.

Conclusion: ENLIGHT is a practical, interpretable, and efficient alternative to learning-based low-light enhancement methods, offering zero-shot operation with competitive perceptual quality and fast inference.

Abstract: We present ENLIGHT, a fast and training free framework for low-light image enhancement based on direct optimization of a perceptual objective. Unlike deep learning approaches that require large scale training data and supervision, ENLIGHT operates in a zero-shot manner by optimizing image quality at inference time. The method employs a two stage global to local optimization strategy. In the first stage, ENLIGHT performs global illumination adjustment to improve visibility while maintaining structural consistency and avoiding excessive noise enhancement. In the second stage, a shadow aware refinement selectively improves low-intensity regions through masked local optimization, enhancing visibility without overexposure. To balance quality and efficiency, we introduce two modes: Fast, which uses a multi-objective formulation combining entropy, gradient preservation, and noise regularization, and Ultrafast, which reduces computational cost via a lightweight approximation of the same objective. The framework is optimizer agnostic and supports both evolutionary and lightweight local search methods. Experiments on BAID, Backlit300, LIME, MEF, NPE, and DICM demonstrate that ENLIGHT achieves competitive perceptual quality (MUSIQ, NIQE, BRISQUE) with significantly lower inference time. Qualitative results further show improved contrast, preserved structural details, and controlled noise amplification, making ENLIGHT a practical and interpretable alternative to learning based methods.

</details>


### [107] [VT-DUDA: Visual Token Conditioning for Diffusion-guided Unsupervised Domain Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21700)
*Xuan Qi,Daniele Berardini,Dario Serez,Vito Paolo Pastore,Vittorio Murino*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VT-DUDA improves diffusion-guided unsupervised domain adaptation by using visual tokens from source images as instance-level conditioning, enabling better target-style synthetic data generation and achieving state-of-the-art accuracy on multiple benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing diffusion-based UDA methods rely on coarse text prompts or domain adaptation modules that primarily control appearance, leaving target-style synthesis insufficiently specified. The authors aim to provide richer, instance-level conditioning to improve the quality and downstream usefulness of synthetic target-style data.

Method: VT-DUDA maps each source image to a compact sequence of visual tokens and concatenates them with text embeddings along the cross-attention context dimension of a latent diffusion model, creating a hybrid conditioning context. This enables instance-dependent conditioning while preserving the standard diffusion objective and allowing inference-time manipulation via token selection and token-strength adjustment.

Result: On Office-31, Office-Home, and VisDA-2017, VT-DUDA improves average target-domain accuracy over strong discriminative and diffusion-based UDA baselines.

Conclusion: In generation-based UDA, a stronger conditioning interface (visual tokens providing instance-level context) can improve the downstream usefulness of synthetic target-style data.

Abstract: Unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA) aims to learn a target-domain classifier from labeled source data and unlabeled target data under distribution shift. Recent diffusion-based UDA methods approach this problem by synthesizing labeled target-style images and training on the resulting synthetic data. However, their performance depends heavily on the conditioning design: class prompts provide only coarse guidance, while domain adaptation modules mainly control appearance, which may leave target-style synthesis insufficiently specified. We propose VT-DUDA, a visual-token conditioning framework for diffusion-guided UDA. Instead of relying only on text prompts, VT-DUDA uses source images to provide additional instance-level visual context for target-style synthesis. Specifically, VT-DUDA maps each source image to a compact sequence of visual tokens and forms a hybrid conditioning context by concatenating these tokens with the corresponding text embeddings along the cross-attention context dimension of a latent diffusion model. This provides instance-dependent conditioning beyond text alone, while synthesis is performed with the target-domain adapter branch. Because guidance is represented explicitly as a token sequence, the same interface also permits inference-time manipulation of the conditioning signal through token selection and token-strength adjustment. The proposed method preserves the standard diffusion objective and can be integrated into existing adapter-based diffusion frameworks without modifying the backbone. Across Office-31, Office-Home, and VisDA-2017, VT-DUDA improves average target-domain accuracy over strong discriminative and diffusion-based UDA baselines. The results suggest that, in generation-based UDA, a stronger conditioning interface can improve the downstream usefulness of synthetic target-style data.

</details>


### [108] [Structural Assessment for Understanding and Guiding Dataset Distillation in Discrete Token Space](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21705)
*Yue Cao,Jianyang Gu,Vyacheslav Kungurtsev,Yu Hu,Jozsef Hamari,Zheng Liu,Mohsen Zardadi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper investigates dataset distillation (DD) effectiveness through discrete visual tokenizers, introducing a structural score to measure token composition adequacy. It finds that balanced token compositions improve validation performance, and high structural scores can guide diffusion-based DD, offering a principled complement to distributional similarity considerations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The factors that make one distilled dataset more effective than another remain poorly understood. The authors aim to investigate this question by analyzing the compositional structure of semantic concepts captured in distilled datasets.

Method: The paper uses discrete visual tokenizers to provide a finite vocabulary for statistical analysis of compositional structure. It introduces a structural score to measure token composition adequacy. The approach involves quantitative analysis of token-level statistics across distilled datasets.

Result: Distilled datasets with balanced token composition yield higher validation performance. Divergence from original data distribution does not necessarily harm performance. Samples with high structural scores in discrete token space can effectively guide diffusion-based DD.

Conclusion: Token composition is a critical factor in dataset effectiveness. The structural score provides a principled complement to existing distributional similarity considerations in dataset distillation.

Abstract: Dataset distillation (DD) has proven to reduce training cost while preserving accuracy. While promising, the factors that make one distilled dataset more effective than another remain poorly understood. In this work, we investigate this question through the lens of discrete visual tokenizers. Whereas many prior DD efforts emphasize matching global data distributions, we suggest that the effectiveness depends on which semantic concepts are captured and how they are composed. Discrete visual tokenizers provide a finite vocabulary that enables direct statistical analysis of such compositional structure. Through quantitative analysis of token-level statistics, we introduce the structural score to measure the adequacy of token compositions. We observe that distilled datasets with balanced token composition yield higher validation performance. On the other hand, divergence from the original data does not necessarily harm performance. We further show that samples with high structural scores in the discrete token space can effectively guide diffusion-based DD. Our findings highlight the importance of token composition in dataset effectiveness, offering a principled complement to distributional similarity considerations in DD.

</details>


### [109] [HPP: Hierarchical Programmatic Probing for Long Video Understanding by Decoupling Perception and Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21734)
*Awais Rauf,Ahmed Hasssan,Greg Slabaugh*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HPP decouples perception from reasoning in long-video understanding by using a coding-capable LLM to programmatically probe hierarchically segmented videos via iterative queries to a VLM, achieving strong results on multiple benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLMs couple perception and multi-step reasoning within a single forward pass, causing a bottleneck due to the LLM's limited capacity to discover and execute multi-step strategies in latent representations for long videos.

Method: HPP reformulates long-video understanding as iterative programmatic exploration: a coding-capable LLM plans and executes multi-step strategies in an interactive coding environment, probing a hierarchically segmented video via a VLM for localized perception. Three key components are: (1) information-density-aware hierarchical segmentation, (2) late-interaction semantic retrieval, and (3) structured probing functions for coarse-to-fine temporal localization.

Result: HPP achieves substantial gains on LongVideoBench (requiring fine-grained perception and long-range relational reasoning), and demonstrates effectiveness on EgoSchema, VideoMME, and MLVU benchmarks.

Conclusion: Decoupling semantic perception from higher-order temporal reasoning via iterative programmatic probing effectively addresses the bottleneck of coupled VLMs for long-video understanding.

Abstract: Understanding long videos requires fine-grained perception and multi-step, higher-order reasoning over complex, long-range spatio-temporal dynamics. Vision-language models (VLMs) encode video frames into visual tokens and attempt to perform both perception and multi-step planning latently, within a single forward pass. This coupled formulation, however, is bottlenecked by the LLM's limited capacity to discover and execute multi-step strategies in its latent representations. To address this bottleneck, we propose Hierarchical Programmatic Probing (HPP), a framework that decouples semantic perception from higher-order temporal reasoning by reformulating long video understanding as iterative, programmatic exploration of a hierarchically segmented video. Specifically, a coding-capable LLM plans and executes a multi-step strategy in an interactive coding environment, probing the video for information and invoking a VLM for localized perception on demand. To make probing tractable over long videos, we introduce three components: information-density-aware hierarchical segmentation, late-interaction semantic retrieval, and structured probing functions for coarse-to-fine temporal localization. We validate HPP on LongVideoBench, which requires both fine-grained perception and long-range relational reasoning, and show that decoupling the two via iterative programmatic probing yields substantial gains. Further results on EgoSchema, VideoMME, and MLVU demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach across diverse long-video benchmarks.

</details>


### [110] [Adversarial Domain Prompt Tuning and Generation for Single Domain Generalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21736)
*Zhipeng Xu,De Cheng,Xinyang Jiang,Nannan Wang,Dongsheng Li,Xinbo Gao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes PAPT, a Progressive Adversarial Prompt Tuning framework that leverages pre-trained text-to-image diffusion models to generate diverse training data for single domain generalization by learning abstract prompts (domain-invariant category prompts and domain-specific style prompts) through adversarial learning, eliminating the need for manual prompt engineering.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Single domain generalization (SDG) suffers from limited training data diversity. Existing augmentation methods are insufficient. While AIGC offers a solution, manually designing textual prompts for all possible domains is impractical, and some domain characteristics are too abstract to describe with words.

Method: PAPT framework uses pre-trained T2I diffusion models with two learnable abstract prompt sets: (1) domain-invariant category prompts capturing category information, and (2) domain-specific style prompts modeling domain styles. Adversarial learning between these prompts enables generation of diverse domain styles while preserving categorical features, without requiring manual prompt design.

Result: Extensive experiments demonstrate superior performance over state-of-the-art single-domain generalization approaches.

Conclusion: Leveraging pre-trained T2I foundation models with learnable abstract prompts via adversarial tuning is an effective strategy for SDG, overcoming the impracticality of manual prompt engineering and enabling generalization to unseen domains.

Abstract: Single domain generalization (SDG) aims to learn a robust model, which could perform well on many unseen domains while there is only one single domain available for training. One of the promising directions for achieving single-domain generalization is to generate out-of-domain (OOD) training data through data augmentation or image generation. Given the rapid advancements in AI-generated content (AIGC), this paper is the first to propose leveraging powerful pre-trained text-to-image (T2I) foundation models to create the training data. However, manually designing textual prompts to generate images for all possible domains is often impractical, and some domain characteristics may be too abstract to describe with words. To address these challenges, we propose a novel Progressive Adversarial Prompt Tuning (PAPT) framework for pre-trained diffusion models. Instead of relying on static textual domains, our approach learns two sets of abstract prompts as conditions for the diffusion model: one that captures domain-invariant category information and another that models domain-specific styles. This adversarial learning mechanism enables the T2I model to generate images in various domain styles while preserving key categorical features. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method, achieving superior performances to state-of-the-art single-domain generalization approaches.

</details>


### [111] [Quantile Adaptive Temperature Scaling for Confidence Calibration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21749)
*Omprakash Chakraborty,Leo Fillioux,Ismail Ben Ayed,Jose Dolz*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: QaTS introduces quantile-adaptive temperature scaling for post hoc neural network calibration, addressing heterogeneous miscalibration across the confidence spectrum, and consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard Temperature Scaling (TS) applies a global, uniform rescaling of logits, failing to correct the highly heterogeneous structure of miscalibration across the confidence spectrum. The largest correctness-confidence discrepancies occur in different quantile regions (especially low-confidence predictions), which TS leaves unaddressed.

Method: Quantile Adaptive Temperature Scaling (QaTS) adapts the temperature as a function of a prediction's empirical confidence quantile. It maps confidences into quantile space to normalize the calibration problem, making miscalibration structure explicit and enabling a monotone temperature function that adapts across quantiles while preserving well-calibrated high-confidence predictions. The formulation aligns with a reparameterized ECE objective.

Result: Across a broad range of datasets, architectures, evaluation scenarios, and diverse tasks, QaTS consistently and substantially outperforms state-of-the-art post hoc calibration methods under challenging scenarios such as class imbalance and distributional shifts.

Conclusion: QaTS delivers more reliable and trustworthy confidence estimates without modifying model predictions, preserving high-confidence behavior while addressing miscalibration in low-confidence regions.

Abstract: Deep neural networks often produce poorly calibrated confidence estimates, overstating their certainty even when predictions are incorrect. Temperature Scaling remains the most widely used posthoc calibration method due to its simplicity and effectiveness, yet its global, uniform rescaling of logits fails to correct the highly heterogeneous structure of miscalibration observed across the confidence spectrum. In particular, the largest correctness confidence discrepancies arise in different quantile regions depending on the setting, low confidence predictions, where uncertainty matters most, tend to exhibit the largest correctness confidence discrepancies, which standard TS leaves largely unaddressed. We introduce Quantile Adaptive Temperature Scaling (QaTS), a simple and efficient post hoc calibration method that adapts the temperature as a function of a predictions empirical confidence quantile. By mapping confidences into the quantile space, QaTS normalizes the calibration problem, makes the structure of miscalibration explicit and enables a monotone temperature function that adapts across quantiles while leaving well calibrated high confidence predictions largely unchanged. preserving high confidence behavior. This quantile aware formulation aligns naturally with a reparameterized Expected Calibration Error (ECE) objective and yields a sample wise temperature that is robust across a variety of challenging scenarios, such as class imbalance and distributional shifts. Across a broad range of datasets, architectures, evaluation scenarios and diverse tasks, QaTS consistently, and substantially, outperforms state of the art post hoc calibration methods, delivering more reliable and trustworthy confidence estimates without modifying model predictions.

</details>


### [112] [From Gradient Clipping to Structural Refinement: Improving DPSGD for Medical Image Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21763)
*Shiva Parsarad,Parth Shandilya,Isabel Wagner*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper evaluates recent DPSGD variants (Auto-S, NSGD, PSAC) for differentially private medical image segmentation, revealing that prior assumptions (especially for PSAC) don't consistently hold, and proposes an adaptive DP-Morph method combining clipping strategies with morphological refinement to improve multi-class segmentation under privacy constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Medical image segmentation uses sensitive data, raising privacy concerns as trained models can leak information. Differential privacy (via DPSGD) offers a solution but reduces utility, and recent DPSGD variants' behavior in medical segmentation remains underexplored.

Method: Evaluated DPSGD variants (Auto-S, NSGD, PSAC) on binary and multi-class medical segmentation tasks; analyzed gradient alignment; combined clipping strategies with morphological refinement; proposed adaptive DP-Morph variant for class-specific structures in multi-class settings.

Result: Prior assumptions, particularly for PSAC, do not consistently hold in medical segmentation. Combining clipping strategies with morphological refinement improves segmentation quality under privacy constraints. The adaptive DP-Morph variant enhances performance in multi-class settings.

Conclusion: DPSGD variants behave differently in medical segmentation than in classification, necessitating domain-specific evaluation. Adaptive DP-Morph effectively balances privacy and utility for multi-class medical image segmentation.

Abstract: Medical image segmentation is widely used for disease detection but relies on sensitive data, raising privacy concerns as trained models can leak information. Differential privacy, typically implemented via Differential Private Stochastic Gradient Descent (DPSGD), provides a solution, though at the cost of reduced utility. Recent DPSGD variants, including Automatic clipping (Auto-S), Normalised SGD with perturbation (NSGD), and Per-sample adaptive clipping (PSAC), have shown promise in image classification, but their behavior in medical segmentation remains underexplored. We evaluate these methods across binary and multi-class tasks and analyze gradient alignment, showing that prior assumptions, particularly for PSAC, do not consistently hold. We further demonstrate that combining clipping strategies with morphological refinement improves segmentation quality under privacy constraints. Finally, we propose an adaptive DP-Morph variant that captures class-specific structures and enhances performance in multi-class settings.

</details>


### [113] [Motion-Aware Reinforcement Learning For Object Localization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21764)
*Prithvi Raj Singh,Satyendra Singh*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MARLNet is a PPO-based bounding-box refinement agent that uses a constant-velocity motion prior and action smoothness penalty to improve detection (up to +0.011 IoU≥0.5 on VOC, +0.007 on VisDrone), while revealing reward interference issues and representational ceilings in crop-feature refinement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing bounding-box refinement agents suffer from instability, overshooting, and reward design issues that limit detection accuracy. The authors aim to improve refinement by incorporating motion priors and smoother action penalties in a reinforcement learning framework.

Method: MARLNet is a PPO-based agent with a 268-dimensional observation (current proposal, kinematic prediction, previous action, 256-dim EfficientNet-B0 crop feature) and a 5D policy for coordinate adjustments and termination. It uses a constant-velocity motion prior and action smoothness penalty in the reward.

Result: +0.011 gain on Pascal VOC 2012 (λ_phys=0.10) and +0.007 on VisDrone 2019 (λ_phys=0.70) at IoU≥0.5. Plain PPO regresses on VOC metric; unconstrained PPO achieves +0.025 on VisDrone. Reward interference (constant-velocity penalty + absolute IoU) causes trigger collapse; action smoothness penalty resolves it.

Conclusion: Motion priors prevent overshooting in refinement agents; reward design critically affects stability; crop-feature agents sharing a backbone with the base detector face a representational ceiling confirmed by global-plus-local ablation.

Abstract: We present MARLNet (Motion-Aware Reinforcement Learning Network), a PPO-based bounding-box refinement agent that incorporates a constant-velocity motion prior into the observation state and an action smoothness penalty into the reward function. The agent operates on 268-dimensional observations encoding the current proposal, a kinematic prediction, the previous action, and a 256-dimensional EfficientNet-B0 crop feature, and learns a five-dimensional policy controlling coordinate adjustments and a binary termination trigger. Evaluated on Pascal VOC 2012 and VisDrone 2019, MARLNet trains stably across all regularization strengths tested and achieves consistent gains in detection success rate at $\text{IoU} \geq 0.5$: up to $+0.011$ on VOC ($λ_\text{phys}{=}0.10$), where the motion prior prevents the overshooting that causes plain PPO to regress on this metric, and $+0.007$ on VisDrone ($λ_\text{phys}{=}0.70$), where unconstrained PPO achieves a larger gain ($+0.025$) owing to the weaker base detector. Through reward design ablations and training dynamics analysis, we identify a reward interference in which combining a constant-velocity deviation penalty with an absolute IoU term causes trigger collapse, and show that replacing it with the action smoothness penalty resolves this failure. We further characterize a representational ceiling facing crop-feature refinement agents that share a backbone with their base detector, confirmed through a global-plus-local observation ablation. Project page: https://prithviraj97.github.io/marl-net

</details>


### [114] [RAPID: A Reproducible Multi-Agent Pipeline for Interpretable Disaster Damage Assessment from Satellite and Street-View Imagery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21819)
*Yifan Yang,Wenjing Gong,Kaili Zhang,Lei Zou,Zhengzhong Tu,Hao Li,Zongrong Li,Xinyue Ye*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RAPID is a multi-agent pipeline for zero-shot, interpretable disaster damage assessment that coordinates specialized agents to analyze heterogeneous geospatial data (satellite and street-view imagery) without task-specific fine-tuning, achieving 0.92 accuracy for multi-disaster classification and up to 0.627 for cross-view damage severity prediction across hurricanes, floods, wildfires, and earthquakes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing disaster damage assessment methods rely on supervised learning and task-specific fine-tuning, which fail to generalize under domain shifts, long-tailed data distributions, and heterogeneous geospatial data sources. They also lack the ability to integrate and reason across multimodal geospatial information (e.g., satellite and street-view images), creating a need for intelligent, scalable, and autonomous approaches.

Method: RAPID is a reproducible multi-agent pipeline that coordinates specialized agents for cross-view understanding, image restoration, structured damage recognition, and geographical reasoning across heterogeneous data modalities (remote sensing and ground-level perspectives). It operates in zero-shot manner without task-specific fine-tuning, jointly using complementary information from pre- and post-disaster street-view images, post-disaster remote sensing imagery, and street-view image pairs.

Result: RAPID achieves 0.92 overall accuracy for multi-disaster type classification and up to 0.627 for cross-view damage severity prediction across hurricanes, floods, wildfires, and earthquakes.

Conclusion: RAPID demonstrates strong potential as a foundational framework for autonomous disaster intelligence, producing fine-grained, interpretable assessments and automatically generating location-specific, decision-relevant disaster reports to support early-stage emergency response.

Abstract: Due to the increasing frequency and intensity of extreme climate events, there is a clear demand for intelligent, scalable, and autonomous approaches to disaster damage assessment. Existing methods, largely based on supervised learning and task-specific fine-tuning, struggle to generalize under domain shifts, long-tailed data distributions, and heterogeneous geospatial data sources, especially in disaster scenarios. They also often lack the ability to integrate and reason across multimodal geospatial information, such as satellite images and street-view images. In this paper, we introduce RAPID, a reproducible multi-agent pipeline for interpretable disaster damage assessment, including damage-level assessment, damage-type interpretation, and actionable suggestions for response, remediation, and recovery. RAPID coordinates specialized agents to perform cross-view understanding, image restoration, structured damage recognition, and geographical reasoning across heterogeneous data modalities. Without task-specific fine-tuning, RAPID supports zero-shot damage assessment by jointly using complementary information from remote sensing and ground-level perspectives. The system produces fine-grained, interpretable assessments and automatically generates location-specific, decision-relevant disaster reports to support early-stage emergency response. We evaluate RAPID across hurricanes, floods, wildfires, and earthquakes using multiple cross-view imagery inputs, including pre- and post-disaster street-view images, post-disaster remote sensing imagery, and street-view image pairs. Experiments show that RAPID achieves 0.92 overall accuracy for multi-disaster type classification and up to 0.627 for cross-view damage severity prediction, highlighting its potential as a foundational framework for autonomous disaster intelligence.

</details>


### [115] [Beyond Flat Labels: Level-Restricted Contrastive Learning for Hierarchical Fine-Grained Vision Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21838)
*Zhiyuan Tao,Srikumar Sastry,Matthew J Thompson,Elizabeth G Campolongo,Net Zhang,Ziheng Zhang,Hilmar Lapp,Yu Su,Tanya Berger-Wolf,Nathan Jacobs,Wei-Lun Chao,Jianyang Gu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A method to improve hierarchical consistency in zero-shot visual classification by restricting contrastive comparisons to same taxonomic levels and using group-balanced design, achieving 30.47% improvement on iNat21.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multimodal contrastive learning methods produce predictions inconsistent across taxonomic levels (e.g., predicting a fine-grained category whose parent contradicts the predicted higher-level label), due to false negative labels when contrastive comparison involves multiple taxonomic levels.

Method: Restrict contrastive comparisons to categories within the same taxonomic level, and adopt a group-balanced design to ensure each taxonomic level receives adequate optimization.

Result: Improves hierarchical consistency and classification accuracy from coarse to fine granularity; on iNat21, improves average accuracy across levels by 30.47% over BioCLIP baseline.

Conclusion: The proposed framework significantly improves hierarchical consistency in both Euclidean and hyperbolic spaces for hierarchical zero-shot classification.

Abstract: Multimodal contrastive learning has enabled zero-shot visual classification by aligning images with textual categories. However, in hierarchically structured label spaces, existing methods often produce predictions that are inconsistent across taxonomic levels. For example, a model may predict a fine-grained category whose parent category contradicts its simultaneously predicted higher-level label. By analysis, the issue originates from false negative labels when contrastive comparison involves multiple taxonomic levels. To this end, we propose to restrict contrastive comparisons to categories within the same taxonomic level. In addition, we adopt a group-balanced design, ensuring each taxonomic level receives adequate optimization. As a result, the proposed framework improves both hierarchical consistency and classification accuracy from coarse to fine granularity. We train our model with TreeOfLife-10M based on BioCLIP and evaluate it across multiple hierarchical classification benchmarks, where the model demonstrates significantly improved hierarchical consistency in both Euclidean and hyperbolic spaces. Notably, on iNaturalist 2021 (iNat21), our method improves average accuracy across levels by 30.47% over the baseline, highlighting its effectiveness for hierarchical zero-shot classification.

</details>


### [116] [Zero-Shot Vision-Language Models for Classroom Engagement Recognition: A Benchmark Study of Prompt Sensitivity and Cross-Dataset Generalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21861)
*Aman Goyal,Kshama Nitin Shah,Kemmannu Vineet Venkatesh Rao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper benchmarks five VLMs (CLIP, BLIP-VQA, GPT-4o, LLaVA-1.5-7B, Qwen2.5VL-7B-Instruct) for zero-shot classroom engagement recognition on DAiSEE (individual-student video) and SCB (scene-level images) datasets, revealing near-random individual performance (kappa≤0.10), class collapse (85-100% single-label predictions), extreme prompt sensitivity (≤32% accuracy swings), but moderate scene-level success (kappa~0.60 for CLIP/GPT-4o with rubrics), and a 98% rejection rate for GPT-4o on student face chain-of-thought prompts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To systematically evaluate the suitability of modern VLMs for zero-shot automated classroom engagement recognition, a domain with substantial potential for scalable learning analytics that remains largely unexplored in the VLM context.

Method: Benchmarked five VLMs (CLIP, BLIP-VQA, GPT-4o, LLaVA-1.5-7B, Qwen2.5VL-7B-Instruct) on DAiSEE (300 individual-student video clips) and SCB (1,168 scene-level images) using three prompt variants (minimal, rubric-anchored, chain-of-thought), with evaluation metrics including Cohen's kappa, prediction distribution analysis, and accuracy swing measurement.

Result: Three failure modes identified: (1) near-random performance on individuals (kappa≤0.10 on DAiSEE); (2) class collapse (85-100% predictions to single engagement level); (3) extreme prompt sensitivity (≤32% accuracy swings). Scene-level classification (SCB) was more tractable: CLIP and GPT-4o achieved kappa~0.60 with behavioral rubrics. GPT-4o safety filters rejected 98% of chain-of-thought requests on individual student faces.

Conclusion: Current zero-shot VLMs are unreliable for individual student engagement recognition but show moderate promise for scene-level classification with careful prompt design; the findings establish a calibrated baseline and highlight critical design considerations (privacy/ethics, prompt engineering, task granularity) for deploying VLMs in educational observation systems.

Abstract: Automated classroom engagement recognition holds substantial promise for scalable learning analytics, yet the suitability of modern Vision-Language Models (VLMs) for this task under zero-shot conditions remains largely unexplored. We present a systematic benchmark that evaluates five widely-used VLMs: CLIP, BLIP-VQA, GPT-4o, LLaVA-1.5-7B, and Qwen2.5VL-7B-Instruct across two complementary educational datasets: DAiSEE, an individual-student video dataset (300 sampled test clips), and the Student Classroom Behaviour dataset (SCB, 1,168 scene-level images). Each model is probed with three prompt variants spanning minimal, rubric-anchored, and chain-of-thought designs. Our experiments reveal three primary failure modes of zero-shot VLMs for engagement recognition: (1) near-random performance on individual students, with Cohen's kappa never exceeding 0.10 on DAiSEE; (2) severe class collapse, where models assign 85-100% of predictions to a single engagement level regardless of visual content; and (3) extreme prompt sensitivity, with accuracy swings of up to 32 percentage points on identical images depending solely on prompt phrasing. Remarkably, scene-level classification on SCB is substantially more tractable: CLIP and GPT-4o achieve kappa approximately 0.60 when prompted with behaviorally-grounded rubrics. We also document a practical barrier for deployment: GPT-4o's safety filters reject 98% of chain-of-thought requests involving individual student faces. Our findings provide a calibrated baseline and surface critical design considerations for the use of VLMs in educational observation systems.

</details>


### [117] [Prompt-Calibrated SAM 3 for Open-Vocabulary Remote Sensing Semantic Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21863)
*Yanghui Song,Nanqing Liu,Haonan Yin,Yingjie Gao,Chengfu Yang,Qi Ming*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ProC-SAM3 is a training-free method for open-vocabulary semantic segmentation in remote sensing that improves SAM3 by constructing offline prompt pools with MLLM-generated candidates, caching text embeddings for efficiency, and using Presence-Guided Residual Fusion to suppress noisy activations, achieving 56.1% average mIoU (+3.9% over prior best).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing SAM3-based training-free OVSS in remote sensing suffers from three issues: single class-name prompts lack semantic coverage, expanding to multiple prompts causes redundant online text encoding, and aggregating multiple prompt responses propagates noisy activations into final predictions.

Method: (1) Offline prompt pool with Category Matcher (groups MLLM-generated candidates) and Expansion Constraints (refines sets with category-specific priors); (2) Caching and reusing text embeddings across all test images to avoid repeated encoding; (3) Presence-Guided Residual Fusion that gates decoder outputs by prompt presence/confidence, plus peak-preserving class aggregation to retain fine-grained activations for small/sparse objects.

Result: Average mIoU of 56.1% across eight benchmarks, outperforming previous best training-free method by 3.9 percentage points.

Conclusion: ProC-SAM3 effectively calibrates SAM3's prompt interface for remote sensing OVSS through offline prompt engineering, caching, and gated fusion, achieving state-of-the-art training-free performance.

Abstract: Open-vocabulary semantic segmentation (OVSS) in remote sensing images aims to segment categories beyond a fixed label space. Recent SAM 3-based methods provide a promising training-free foundation, yet three key issues remain: (1) a single class-name prompt lacks sufficient semantic coverage for complex remote sensing categories; (2) expanding each category into multiple prompts introduces redundant online text encoding; and (3) directly aggregating multiple prompt responses propagates noisy activations into the final prediction. To address these issues, we propose ProC-SAM3, which calibrates SAM 3's prompt interface for remote sensing OVSS from three complementary aspects. First, we construct an offline prompt pool where a Category Matcher groups MLLM-generated candidates into per-category sets, and Expansion Constraints further refine each set using category-specific prior knowledge. Second, the resulting text embeddings are cached and reused across all test images, eliminating repeated text encoding. Third, we introduce Presence-Guided Residual Fusion to gate unreliable decoder outputs by prompt presence and confidence, followed by peak-preserving class aggregation that retains fine-grained activations for small and sparse objects. Experiments on eight benchmarks show that ProC-SAM3 achieves an average mIoU of 56.1%, outperforming the previous best training-free method by 3.9 percentage points. Code will be available at https://github.com/YanghuiSong/ProC-SAM3.

</details>


### [118] [Fidelity- and Perception-Aware Local Implicit Attention for Arbitrary-Scale Image Super-Resolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21910)
*Yu-Syuan Xu,Hao-Lun Sun,Hao-Wei Chen,Hsien-Kai Kuo,Chun-Yi Lee*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FPLIA proposes a Fidelity- and Perception-Aware Local Implicit Attention framework that integrates fidelity-oriented features into a diffusion pipeline for arbitrary-scale image super-resolution, achieving realistic textures without structural hallucinations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional pixel-regression ASISR methods produce overly smooth results lacking realistic details, while diffusion methods generate sharper textures but risk structural hallucinations. The goal is to produce both realistic and faithful reconstructions across continuous upscaling factors.

Method: FPLIA introduces a Fidelity and Perception Attention Module (FPAM) using self-attention and cross-attention on fidelity-oriented and perceptual features, plus a Fidelity and Perception Select Module (FPSM) that adaptively selects representative features for RGB prediction.

Result: Extensive experiments show FPLIA delivers superior perceptual realism while maintaining reconstruction accuracy on standard ASISR benchmarks.

Conclusion: FPLIA effectively balances fidelity and perceptual quality for arbitrary-scale super-resolution, addressing the smoothness vs. hallucination trade-off.

Abstract: Arbitrary-scale image super-resolution (ASISR) aims to reconstruct high-resolution images from low-resolution inputs over a continuous range of upscaling factors. While traditional pixel-regression approaches often produce overly smooth results that lack realistic details, recent diffusion methods can produce sharper and more realistic textures. However, these diffusion techniques frequently introduce the risk of structural hallucinations. To address these issues, we propose Fidelity- and Perception-Aware Local Implicit Attention (FPLIA), a framework that effectively integrates fidelity-oriented features into a diffusion pipeline to produce realistic and faithful reconstructions for ASISR. We introduce a Fidelity and Perception Attention Module (FPAM), which applies both self-attention and cross-attention to fidelity-oriented and perceptual features to enhance representational capacity. To further exploit their complements, we design a Fidelity and Perception Select Module (FPSM) that adaptively selects the most representative features for RGB values prediction. We conduct extensive experiments to validate the effectiveness of these components. Both qualitative and quantitative results show that FPLIA delivers superior perceptual realism while maintaining reconstruction accuracy on standard ASISR benchmarks. The source code is accessible at the following repository: https://github.com/XUSean0118/FPLIA.

</details>


### [119] [Rethinking the Adaptation of Vision Foundation Models for Efficient Cell Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21913)
*Qing Xu,Xiangjian He,Wenting Duan,Jiebo Luo,Zhen Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EffiCell-Seg is a highly efficient cell segmentation framework that leverages frozen Vision Foundation Models by extracting structural priors (global saliency and local morphology) via a Cell Structure Prompt Encoder and a Synergistic Mask Decoder, achieving state-of-the-art performance with only ~5M trainable parameters (130x fewer than fine-tuned counterparts).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing cell segmentation methods rely on fine-tuning heavy visual encoders, causing extensive computational overhead and dependency on large-scale annotations. The authors aim to unlock the potential of pretrained VFMs for cellular imaging without re-training the visual encoder.

Method: The framework has two key components: (1) Cell Structure Prompt Encoder (CSP-Encoder) that synthesizes semantic-aware saliency and principal morphological features from frozen VFM representations into explicit structural prior maps; (2) Synergistic Mask Decoder (SM-Decoder) that jointly predicts geometric distance fields and semantic maps via mutual cross-guidance for contextual consistency.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art methods across diverse cell imaging modalities while requiring only ~5M trainable parameters, which is over 130x fewer than fully fine-tuned VFM counterparts.

Conclusion: EffiCell-Seg demonstrates that pretrained VFMs contain complementary structural priors that can be efficiently harnessed for cell segmentation without fine-tuning, offering a practical and resource-efficient paradigm for computational pathology.

Abstract: Cell segmentation is critical for computational pathology and biomedical discovery. While recent Vision Foundation Models (VFMs) have demonstrated remarkable universal feature representations, unlocking their full potential for cellular imaging is currently bottlenecked by resource-intensive adaptation paradigms. Existing methods typically rely on fine-tuning heavy visual encoders, leading to extensive computational overhead and a dependency on large-scale annotations. To address this, we propose the EffiCell-Seg framework for highly efficient cell segmentation without re-training the visual encoder. Our core insight is that pretrained VFMs intrinsically encode complementary structural priors: global saliency for localizing potential cells, and local morphological patterns for delineating cellular structures. To harness these priors, we devise a Cell Structure Prompt Encoder (CSP-Encoder) that synthesizes semantic-aware saliency and principal morphological features from frozen VFM representations into explicit structural prior maps. Moreover, we propose a Synergistic Mask Decoder (SM-Decoder) that enforces contextual consistency by jointly predicting geometric distance fields and semantic maps via mutual cross-guidance. Extensive experiments demonstrate that EffiCell-Seg outperforms state-of-the-art methods across diverse cell imaging modalities while requiring only ~5M trainable parameters, over 130x fewer than fully fine-tuned VFM counterparts. The code is available at https://github.com/xq141839/EffiCell-Seg.

</details>


### [120] [GTA-Net: Cooperative Game Theory for Vision-Language Alignment in Chest X-Ray Report Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21915)
*Saif ur Rehman Khan,Imad Ahmed Waqar,Sebastian Vollmer,Muhammad Nabeel Asim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GTA-Net formulates chest X-ray report generation as a cooperative game-theoretic alignment problem, using binary and ternary game aligners to enforce explicit region-word correspondence and disease-level consistency, achieving SOTA on CheXpertPlus and IU-XRay.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing vision-language models for chest X-ray report generation rely on implicit attention mechanisms that fail to enforce explicit region-word correspondence and disease-level consistency, leading to clinically unreliable descriptions.

Method: GTA-Net proposes a Game-Theoretic Alignment Network with (1) a BinaryGameAligner using similarity-based payoff matrices and Shapley-inspired importance weighting for image region-text token interactions, (2) a Disease-Aware Ternary Aligner for joint interactions among images, reports, and structured disease concepts, (3) a Swin-based visual encoder with LoRA-adapted LLM, trained with a unified generation and alignment objective.

Result: State-of-the-art performance on standard generation metrics and improved clinical consistency on CheXpertPlus and IU-XRay datasets.

Conclusion: Explicit game-theoretic alignment is effective for improving cross-modal grounding and clinical consistency in medical vision-language generation.

Abstract: Automated chest X-ray report generation requires precise cross-modal grounding to ensure clinically reliable descriptions. However, existing vision-language models rely on implicit attention mechanisms that fail to enforce explicit region-word correspondence and disease-level consistency. We propose Game-Theoretic Alignment Network (GTA-Net), a vision-language framework that formulates report generation as a cooperative game-theoretic alignment problem. The model introduces a BinaryGameAligner that models interactions between image regions and text tokens using similarity-based payoff matrices with Shapley-inspired importance weighting. To enforce clinical semantics, we further develop a Disease-Aware Ternary Aligner, which captures joint interactions among images, reports, and structured disease concepts. GTA-Net combines a Swin-based visual encoder with a LoRA-adapted large language model and is trained with a unified objective for generation and alignment. Experiments on CheXpertPlus and IU-XRay demonstrate state-of-the-art performance across standard generation metrics and improved clinical consistency, highlighting the effectiveness of explicit game-theoretic alignment for medical vision-language generation.

</details>


### [121] [CoSA: Correlation-Guided Change Attention with Learnable Residual Gating for Remote Sensing Change Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21932)
*Abdirashid Omar,Jonghyuk Park*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CoSA is a lightweight decoder-side refinement module that uses explicit bi-temporal feature correlation as a control signal for change-aware feature enhancement, achieving 1.5-2.6% F1 gains on CD benchmarks with negligible overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robust change localization in remote sensing bi-temporal imagery remains challenging under sparse changes, noisy labels, and appearance variations. Existing attention mechanisms rely on implicit feature weighting without explicit temporal control.

Method: CoSA computes normalized same-location cross-correlation between paired decoder features, converts low correlation into a change gate, and injects gated residuals at native 1/8 and 1/16 feature scales via learnable residual scaling within an FC-Siam framework.

Result: 1.5-2.6% gains in changed-class F1 across LEVIR-CD, S2Looking, DSIFN, and CLCD datasets with negligible parameter overhead. Multiscale placement and learnable residual gating are both essential for peak performance.

Conclusion: CoSA establishes a practical and effective refinement paradigm for enhancing temporal discriminability in Siamese change detection frameworks.

Abstract: Remote sensing change detection (CD) from bi-temporal imagery is critical for applications such as urban monitoring, disaster assessment, and environmental management, yet robust localization remains challenging under sparse changes, noisy labels, and appearance variations. In this paper, we propose Context Sampling Attention (CoSA), a lightweight decoder-side refinement module that explicitly leverages bi-temporal feature correlation as a control signal for adaptive change-aware feature enhancement. This differs from conventional attention mechanisms that rely on implicit feature weighting without explicit temporal control. In the implemented FC-Siam setting, CoSA computes normalized same-location cross-correlation between paired decoder features, converts low correlation into a change gate, and injects the resulting gated residual at native 1/8 and 1/16 feature scales through learnable residual scaling. This design enables effective discrimination between stable and ambiguous regions without relying on computationally expensive global attention. Extensive experiments on four benchmark datasets (LEVIR-CD, S2Looking, DSIFN, and CLCD) demonstrate consistent improvements over strong baselines, achieving 1.5-2.6% gains in changed-class F1 while introducing negligible parameter overhead. Ablation studies confirm that multiscale placement and learnable residual gating are both important for peak performance. These results indicate that CoSA establishes a practical and effective refinement paradigm for enhancing temporal discriminability in Siamese change detection frameworks.

</details>


### [122] [Artic-O: End-to-End Articulated Object Reconstruction via Latent Geometry Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21938)
*Xuyang Wang,Zhenyu Li,Jian Ding,Habib Slim,Peter Wonka,Hongdong Li,Mohamed Elhoseiny*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Artic-O is an end-to-end feed-forward framework for articulated object reconstruction from sparse images, using latent geometry learning and a frozen flow-matching decoder to achieve high-quality geometry and articulation estimation in ~0.3 seconds per object, drastically outperforming prior methods like LARM (9 min per object).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods separate geometry reconstruction, part reasoning, and articulation estimation into different stages, which weakens consistency between shape, active parts, and motion, and incurs substantial inference cost.

Method: Artic-O maps sparse multi-state observations into a pretrained latent geometry space with a frozen flow-matching decoder for complete-shape prior; uses an image-grounded part-reasoning module fusing visual tokens, geometry latents, and decoder features for active-part segmentation and articulation prediction; trained with a geometry-to-articulation curriculum and a decoupled two-pass strategy.

Result: On PartNet-Mobility, Artic-O achieves strong reconstruction quality (lower Chamfer Distance, improved F-score) and comparable or better articulation accuracy across most joint metrics, while reducing inference time from 9 minutes (LARM) to ~0.3 seconds per object.

Conclusion: End-to-end feed-forward latent geometry learning enables efficient, consistent articulated object reconstruction with high quality and substantially faster inference.

Abstract: Reconstructing articulated objects from sparse images requires recovering complete geometry, movable parts, and motion parameters. Recent methods typically separate geometry reconstruction, part reasoning, and articulation estimation into different stages. This separation can weaken consistency between shape, active parts, and motion, while also incurring substantial inference cost. We introduce Artic-O, an end-to-end, feed-forward framework for articulated object reconstruction via latent geometry learning. Instead of fitting geometry in image or view space, Artic-O maps sparse multi-state observations into a pretrained latent geometry space, where a frozen flow-matching decoder provides a complete-shape prior for recovering visible and occluded structures. To connect geometry with articulation, Artic-O fuses visual tokens, geometry latents, and point-wise decoder features in an image-grounded part-reasoning module for active-part segmentation and articulation prediction. We further train the model with a geometry-to-articulation curriculum and a decoupled two-pass strategy to balance reconstruction and part-level supervision. On PartNet-Mobility, Artic-O achieves strong reconstruction quality while being substantially more efficient than LARM, a strong prior method. It reduces Chamfer Distance, improves F-score, and achieves comparable or better articulation accuracy across most joint metrics, while reducing inference time from 9 minutes to about 0.3 seconds per object.

</details>


### [123] [ScalePredictor: Instance-aware Scale Learning for Accurate Quantization of Vision Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21947)
*Changjun Li,Runqing Jiang,Lian Xu,Ye Zhang,Qingyong Hu,Yulan Guo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ScalePredictor is a dynamic quantization framework for Vision Transformers that leverages shallow-layer activation distributions to predict optimal quantization scales for deeper layers using a Taylor-motivated polynomial projection, achieving superior accuracy-efficiency trade-offs in PTQ.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing PTQ methods use static quantization uniformly applied to all instances, which is suboptimal because activation distributions vary significantly across diverse natural image samples.

Method: 1) Reveals a hidden correlation between shallow-layer activation distribution ranges and optimal scales for deeper layers. 2) Develops an efficient range extraction approach to capture robust range statistics from shallow stages. 3) Feeds these statistics into a Taylor-motivated polynomial scale projection module to generate all quantization scales simultaneously, avoiding costly just-in-time calibration.

Result: Extensive experiments on ImageNet show ScalePredictor consistently outperforms prior PTQ methods, achieving a more favorable accuracy-efficiency trade-off with insignificant computational overhead.

Conclusion: Dynamic quantization via shallow-to-deep scale prediction is an effective and efficient paradigm for deploying Vision Transformers on edge devices.

Abstract: Vision Transformers have achieved remarkable success in many fields, yet their deployment on edge devices remains challenging due to their substantial computational demands. Post-Training Quantization (PTQ) offers an attractive solution by compressing models using a small calibration set with minimal training overhead. However, most existing PTQ works adopt a static quantization paradigm that is uniformly applied to all instances. Given the substantial diversity of natural images, the activation distributions vary significantly across samples, making these methods inherently suboptimal. In this paper, we propose ScalePredictor, a dynamic quantization framework for accurate and efficient quantization scale learning of ViTs. We first reveal a hidden correlation between the distribution range of shallow-layer activations and the optimal scales of deeper layers. Based on this, we develop a scale learning mechanism that integrates an efficient range extraction approach to capture robust range statistics at the shallow stage, which are then fed into a Taylor-motivated polynomial scale projection module to generate all quantization scales simultaneously. With the efficiency of polynomial approximation, ScalePredictor introduces insignificant computational overhead while avoiding costly just-in-time calibration. Extensive experiments on ImageNet demonstrate that ScalePredictor consistently outperforms prior PTQ methods, achieving a more favorable accuracy-efficiency trade-off. Code and additional results are shown in the supplementary materials.

</details>


### [124] [CapRiCorn-1K: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Video Captioning and Subject Referential Consistency Across Temporal Scales](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21949)
*Xinlong Chen,Jiafu Tang,Yue Ding,Yizhuo Jia,Bozhou Li,Bohan Zeng,Yang Shi,Shihao Li,Yiyan Ji,Qiang Liu,Weihong Lin,Yuanxing Zhang,Pengfei Wan,Liang Wang,Tieniu Tan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CapRiCorn-1K is a new benchmark for evaluating video caption quality and subject referential consistency across varying durations and domains, revealing that current models struggle with long-form videos and that its metrics correlate well with downstream task performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks cannot objectively and comprehensively evaluate video caption accuracy, completeness, and consistent subject references across diverse durations and scenarios, hindering progress in video captioning models.

Method: CapRiCorn-1K benchmark supports both audiovisual and visual-only settings, evaluates caption quality and subject referential consistency across long temporal horizons and diverse video domains.

Result: Current models struggle to generate accurate, comprehensive captions with consistent subject references; caption quality and subject consistency decline as video duration increases; the proposed metrics strongly correlate with downstream task performance.

Conclusion: CapRiCorn-1K effectively evaluates video captioning and subject consistency, and its metrics are validated by downstream task correlations, highlighting the need for improved long-form video captioning models.

Abstract: Accurate and comprehensive video captions with consistent subject references are critical for downstream understanding and generation tasks. However, few existing benchmarks can objectively and comprehensively evaluate these properties across diverse durations and scenarios, thereby hindering the advancement of video captioning models. To bridge this gap, we propose CapRiCorn-1K, a comprehensive benchmark designed to evaluate both video captioning quality and subject referential consistency across long temporal horizons and diverse video domains. To accommodate varied evaluation needs, our benchmark supports both audiovisual and visual-only settings. Extensive experiments on CapRiCorn-1K reveal that current models generally struggle to generate accurate and comprehensive captions while maintaining consistent subject references. Moreover, as video duration increases, both the overall caption quality and subject referential consistency decline. Notably, our evaluation metrics exhibit strong correlations with the performance of downstream understanding and generation tasks conditioned on the generated captions, further validating their effectiveness. The project is available at https://github.com/xlchen0205/CapRiCorn-1K .

</details>


### [125] [Denoising-Enhanced Coarse-to-Fine Infrared Small Target Detection with Attention Prior-Guided Knowledge Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21956)
*Houzhang Fang,Ruixuan Huang,Qiuhuan Chen,Xiaolin Wang,Yi Chang,Luxin Yan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ECFNet is a coarse-to-fine infrared small target detection framework using attention prior-guided knowledge distillation, region binary classification, denoising-assisted training, and lightweight detection to achieve state-of-the-art performance with high efficiency on three real datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: IRSTD in high-resolution images is crucial for UAV surveillance and ground monitoring but remains challenging due to small target size, weak features, and complex dynamic background interference. Existing methods suffer from redundant computations on non-target regions and insufficient exploitation of target context.

Method: (1) Coarse stage: Region Binary Classification Network (RBCN) on grid-based multi-scale feature maps to identify target-containing context region proposals; (2) Denoising-assisted training strategy using noisy GT masks for better proposal discrimination and faster convergence; (3) Fine stage: Lightweight target detector operating on coarse stage proposals; (4) Teacher-student cross-attention prior knowledge distillation to focus on critical target regions.

Result: Outperforms existing single-stage and two-stage approaches on three real infrared datasets while maintaining high real-time processing efficiency.

Conclusion: The proposed coarse-to-fine framework with attention-guided knowledge distillation effectively addresses IRSTD challenges by reducing redundant computation and enhancing target feature representation, achieving superior accuracy-efficiency trade-offs.

Abstract: Infrared small target detection (IRSTD) in high-resolution images is crucial for many practical applications, such as surveillance of unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) and UAV-based ground monitoring. However, IRSTD remains challenging due to the small size and weak features of targets, as well as significant interference from complex dynamic backgrounds. Existing detection methods often suffer from redundant computations on non-target background regions and insufficient exploitation of target context information, which limits their performance in complex backgrounds. To address these issues, we propose an efficient coarse-to-fine infrared small target detection framework with attention prior-guided knowledge distillation, termed ECFNet. In the coarse stage, we design a region binary classification network (RBCN) on grid-based multi-scale feature maps to efficiently recognize target-containing context region proposals while suppressing complex backgrounds. Moreover, we introduce a novel denoising-assisted training strategy that incorporates noisy ground-truth (GT) masks into the feature maps of RBCN and trains the network to reconstruct the GT masks through a denoising task, thereby enhancing its ability to distinguish target proposals from background regions and accelerating convergence. In the fine stage, we customize a lightweight target detector to the coarse stage's region proposals for balancing accuracy and efficiency. Furthermore, we propose a knowledge distillation strategy guided by the teacher-student cross-attention prior. This mechanism directs the student to focus on critical target regions, thereby enhancing the discriminative feature representation for infrared small targets. Extensive experiments on three real infrared datasets demonstrate that our method outperforms both existing single-stage and two-stage approaches while maintaining high real-time processing efficiency.

</details>


### [126] [Look Before You Zoom: Adaptive Routing for the Resolution-Context Trade-off in Visual RAG](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21968)
*Oanh N. Tran,Thanh Quoc Hung Le,Oscar Chew,Kuan-Hao Huang,Khoa D. Doan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ViRGo is a lightweight routing framework that adaptively selects between global perception, patch-based retrieval, and attention-based retrieval based on object scale estimates from VLM localization heads, improving the accuracy-efficiency trade-off across varying object sizes in VQA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLMs degrade on small objects, but existing training-free retrieval approaches indiscriminately zoom into local regions, ignoring the resolution-context trade-off: patch-based zooming destroys large-object context, attention-based retrieval fails on tiny details, and global perception is fastest when retrieval is unnecessary.

Method: ViRGo estimates object scale from the VLM's intrinsic localization heads during the initial forward pass, combines it with semantic token confidence, and adaptively routes to one of three strategies: global perception, patch-based retrieval, or attention-based retrieval.

Result: ViRGo matches patch retrieval accuracy on small objects, leverages attention-based retrieval for larger objects, and reduces inference time by routing to the global baseline when zooming is unnecessary, improving the accuracy-efficiency trade-off across multiple VQA benchmarks and object-size groups.

Conclusion: Adaptive routing based on object scale estimates effectively resolves the resolution-context trade-off in VLM visual retrieval, achieving better accuracy-efficiency balance than indiscriminate retrieval approaches.

Abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) struggle as query-relevant objects become smaller. To address this, recent training-free approaches dynamically retrieve and zoom into local image regions. However, we show that indiscriminately applying retrieval ignores a critical vulnerability: the resolution-context trade-off. Patch-based zooming recovers details for small targets, but can split large objects and destroy global spatial context; attention-based retrieval better preserves large objects, but remains less reliable on tiny details; and global perception is often fastest when retrieval is unnecessary. Motivated by these failure modes, we introduce ViRGo (Visual Retrieval or Global Perception), a lightweight framework that formulates visual retrieval as an adaptive routing problem. ViRGo estimates object scale from the VLM's intrinsic localization heads during the initial forward pass and combines it with semantic token confidence to select between global perception, patch-based retrieval, and attention-based retrieval with minimal additional computation.
