Models output structured decisions that are snapped to the graph and scored on validity, legality, optimality, constraint satisfaction, and cross-zoom consistency.

Result: Seven VLMs were tested. Models can read maps and perform simple routing, but fail at graph-cost reasoning (e.g., single-facility pin placement is near chance even for frontier reasoning models) and are frequently scale-inconsistent.

Conclusion: Current VLMs lack the ability to perform graph-grounded reasoning for mobility decisions, highlighting a gap between map perception and actionable decision-making that respects road network constraints.

Abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) are increasingly used to read maps for logistics, delivery, and accessible navigation, where the output is an actionable decision (a route, a pin, a parking choice) that must respect the road network. Yet most map benchmarks grade free-text or multiple-choice answers that cannot be verified against the underlying graph. We present \textbf{MapReason-OSM}, a benchmark and evaluation harness for graph-verifiable mobility decisions on self-rendered OpenStreetMap panels. We render fixed-style maps for ten U.S. downtowns at two aligned zoom scales, overlay a consistent marker grammar, and pair each panel with a hidden street graph and exact oracles, yielding 6{,}000 instances (12{,}000 panels across the two zooms) over 12 routing, facility-location, and visual-disambiguation tasks. Models return structured decisions that we snap back to the graph and score for validity, legality, optimality, and constraint satisfaction, plus \emph{cross-zoom consistency}. Across seven VLMs, models read maps and route simply but fail at graph-cost reasoning (single-facility pin placement is near chance even for frontier reasoning models), and are frequently scale-inconsistent. We release the benchmark, harness, and deterministic generator.

</details>


### [178] [Automated sign detection across the Electronic Babylonian Library: A large-scale dataset and end-to-end cuneiform OCR pipeline](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22608)
*Wentao Che,Esteban Garcés Arias,Asim Niaz,Andreas Bender,Enrique Jiménez*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper presents a DETR-based object detection model trained on the largest annotated cuneiform sign dataset (173/106 classes), integrating tablet-side extraction, line grouping, and n-gram evaluation. It achieves 28-37% improvement over prior work, generates ~2.9M detections across 87,668 tablet fragments, and provides a scalable foundation for corpus-wide cuneiform analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cuneiform tablet decipherment is extremely demanding, and only a small fraction of excavated tablets has been analyzed. Large annotated datasets are needed for computer vision approaches, which are lacking.

Method: A Deformable Detection Transformer (DETR)-based object detection model is evaluated under two class granularities (173 and 106 classes). The system integrates automatic tablet-side extraction, heuristic line grouping, and n-gram-based textual similarity evaluation to bridge visual sign detection and textual structure.

Result: Achieves consistent improvements of up to 28-37% over prior work on COCO-style detection metrics. Applied to 87,668 tablet fragments, producing nearly 2.9 million sign detections.

Conclusion: The approach provides a scalable and interpretable foundation for corpus-wide cuneiform analysis, operates without linguistic priors, and supports future integration with multimodal and linguistic modelling frameworks, though it remains sensitive to tablet damage and layout variability.

Abstract: Learning to read cuneiform tablets is an extremely demanding task; consequently, of the roughly half million excavated tablets, only a small fraction has been analysed by Assyriologists. Computer vision offers a promising avenue for decipherment but requires large, densely annotated datasets. To address this limitation, the largest annotated cuneiform sign dataset to date is used, and a Deformable Detection Transformer (DETR)-based object detection model is evaluated under two class granularities of 173 and 106 classes. The proposed system integrates automatic tablet-side extraction, heuristic line grouping, and n-gram-based textual similarity evaluation to bridge visual sign detection and textual structure, and achieves consistent improvements of up to 28-37% over prior work on COCO-style detection metrics. At inference, the method is applied to 87,668 tablet fragments from the Electronic Babylonian Library (eBL) corpus, producing nearly 2.9 million sign detections. Although the approach operates without linguistic priors and remains sensitive to tablet damage and layout variability, it provides a scalable and interpretable foundation for corpus-wide cuneiform analysis and supports future integration with multimodal and linguistic modelling frameworks.

</details>


### [179] [OmniSpace: Efficient Geometry Awareness for Autonomous Vehicles MLLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22617)
*Hao Vo,Phu Loc Nguyen,Khoa Vo,Sieu Tran,Duc Minh Nguyen,Ngo Xuan Cuong,Nghi D. Q. Bui,Anh Nguyen,Duy Minh Ho Nguyen,Ngan Le*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: OmniSpace is a plug-and-play framework that enhances MLLMs' geometric spatial reasoning for autonomous driving purely from 2D inputs by injecting camera pose information, multi-view epipolar attention, and 3D geometric distillation, outperforming prior methods across multiple AV benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing geometry-aware MLLMs rely on auxiliary 3D models at inference time, which increases pipeline complexity and risks cascading failures. The authors identify that current MLLMs are bottlenecked by weak cross-view correspondence and depth estimation in spatial reasoning tasks for autonomous driving.

Method: OmniSpace introduces three components: (1) a Camera Pose Injector to provide explicit geometric context, (2) a Multi-view Epipolar Attention module to enhance cross-view correspondence, and (3) a 3D Geometric Distillation objective to transfer geometric knowledge into the model—all operating purely from 2D observations in a plug-and-play manner.

Result: OmniSpace surpasses existing methods on planning (nuScenes, Bench2Drive), risk detection (nuInstruct), language (Omnidrive), and generalization (DriveBench) benchmarks.

Conclusion: OmniSpace provides a simple, effective, plug-and-play solution to equip MLLMs with geometry-aware spatial reasoning for autonomous driving without relying on auxiliary 3D models at inference time.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have achieved remarkable performance on 2D visual tasks, yet enhancing their spatial intelligence for real-world applications such as Autonomous Vehicles (AV) remains an open challenge. Existing geometry-aware MLLMs typically rely on auxiliary 3D models at inference time, introducing pipeline complexity and the risk of cascading failures. In this paper, we present OmniSpace, a simple yet effective plug-and-play paradigm for geometry-aware spatial reasoning from purely 2D observations. Motivated by our finding that current MLLMs are bottlenecked by weak cross-view correspondence and depth estimation, OmniSpace introduces a Camera Pose Injector, a Multi-view Epipolar Attention module, and a 3D Geometric Distillation objective that jointly address these two limitations by transferring geometric knowledge into the model. Extensive experiments show that OmniSpace surpasses existing methods on planning benchmarks (nuScenes, Bench2Drive), risk detection (nuInstruct), language (Omnidrive), and generalization (DriveBench).

</details>


### [180] [DR-Mamba: Automatic Inference-Time Domain Adaptation for Document Image Binarization via Sample-Conditioned Detail-Background Suppression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22625)
*Sheng-Wei Chan,Jen-Shiun Chiang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DR-Mamba is a sample-conditioned, inference-time domain adaptation framework for degraded document image binarization that uses fast-slow Mamba routing and input-dependent subtractive gates to suppress background interference, achieving strong cross-domain robustness without fine-tuning or test-time parameter updates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Degraded document image binarization suffers from domain shifts caused by various degradation types (aging, bleed-through, stains, shadows, uneven illumination), and existing learning-based methods become unstable on unseen degradation domains.

Method: DR-Mamba reinterprets Mamba-style selective scanning as fast-slow route modeling: a fast detail route captures local stroke structures, and a slow background route accumulates spatially persistent degradation responses. The two routes are integrated via an input-dependent subtractive gate that explicitly suppresses background interference. It also incorporates full-resolution detail-guided reconstruction and thin-stroke-aware supervision.

Result: Evaluated under a leave-one-year-out protocol on DIBCO-style benchmarks, DR-Mamba demonstrates that per-document, per-location subtractive suppression improves cross-domain robustness, with particularly strong performance on the most severely degraded held-out fold.

Conclusion: DR-Mamba provides robust automatic inference-time domain adaptation for document image binarization without needing target-domain labels, fine-tuning, or test-time parameter updates.

Abstract: Degraded document image binarization is sensitive to domain shifts caused by paper aging, bleed-through, stains, shadows, and uneven illumination, and the foreground-background separation of recent learning-based methods can become unstable on unseen degradation domains. We propose DR-Mamba, a sample-conditioned detail-background suppression framework that performs automatic inference-time domain adaptation for document image binarization. Unlike test-time adaptation methods that require gradient updates or auxiliary data at inference, DR-Mamba adapts to each input document through input-dependent gates within a single forward pass, requiring no target-domain labels, no fine-tuning, and no test-time parameter updates. Instead of using Mamba-style selective scanning as a single generic feature path, DR-Mamba reinterprets it as fast-slow route modeling: a fast detail route captures local stroke structures, while a slow background route accumulates spatially persistent degradation responses. The two routes are integrated through an input-dependent subtractive gate that explicitly suppresses background interference rather than fusing features by addition or concatenation. We further add full-resolution detail-guided reconstruction and thin-stroke-aware supervision to recover fine strokes lost during downsampling. Evaluated under a leave-one-year-out protocol on DIBCO-style benchmarks, where each held-out year is treated as an unseen degradation domain, DR-Mamba shows that per-document, per-location subtractive suppression improves cross-domain robustness, with particularly strong performance on the most severely degraded held-out fold.

</details>


### [181] [Automatic Vehicle Detection using DETR: A Transformer-Based Approach for Navigating Treacherous Roads](https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.17843)
*Istiaq Ahmed Fahad,Abdullah Ibne Hanif Arean,Nazmus Sakib Ahmed,Mahmudul Hasan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This study is the first to apply Detection Transformer (DETR) with a Collaborative Hybrid Assignments Training (Co-DETR) scheme for automatic vehicle detection in complex driving environments. Using the BadODD dataset, the method outperforms traditional YOLO and Faster R-CNN approaches by enhancing feature learning and attention mechanisms through versatile label assignment and parallel auxiliary heads, demonstrating improved accuracy and robustness for real-world autonomous navigation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional vehicle detection methods (YOLO, Faster R-CNN) struggle with diverse driving environments involving varying lighting, road types, and vehicle types. This study explores combining CNNs with Transformers (specifically DETR) to address these limitations.

Method: The authors employ a Collaborative Hybrid Assignments Training (Co-DETR) scheme, which enhances DETR by introducing versatile label assignment strategies and multiple parallel auxiliary heads to improve supervision during training and extract positive coordinates for better training efficiency.

Result: Extensive experiments on DETR variants and YOLO models using the BadODD dataset show that the proposed Co-DETR approach achieves superior results and improved accuracy under diverse conditions compared to traditional methods.

Conclusion: This work demonstrates the potential of DETR for robust and efficient vehicle detection in challenging driving environments, contributing to autonomous navigation technology and opening new research directions in object detection for autonomous vehicles.

Abstract: Automatic Vehicle Detection (AVD) in diverse driving environments presents unique challenges due to varying lighting conditions, road types, and vehicle types. Traditional methods, such as YOLO and Faster R-CNN, often struggle to cope with these complexities. As computer vision evolves, combining Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) with Transformer-based approaches offers promising opportunities for improving detection accuracy and efficiency. This study is the first to experiment with Detection Transformer (DETR) for automatic vehicle detection in complex and varied settings. We employ a Collaborative Hybrid Assignments Training scheme, Co-DETR, to enhance feature learning and attention mechanisms in DETR. By leveraging versatile label assignment strategies and introducing multiple parallel auxiliary heads, we provide more effective supervision during training and extract positive coordinates to boost training efficiency. Through extensive experiments on DETR variants and YOLO models, conducted using the BadODD dataset, we demonstrate the advantages of our approach. Our method achieves superior results, and improved accuracy in diverse conditions, making it practical for real-world deployment. This work significantly advances autonomous navigation technology and opens new research avenues in object detection for autonomous vehicles. By integrating the strengths of CNNs and Transformers, we highlight the potential of DETR for robust and efficient vehicle detection in challenging driving environments.

</details>


### [182] [4DVLT: Dynamic Scene Understanding with Worldline-Centered Vision-Language Tracking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22631)
*Chaoyue Li,Boxue Yang,Shengyao Zhou,Haoyang Wu,Rui Qian,Linfeng Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: 4DVLT introduces a worldline-centered task and benchmark (Instruct-4D) for instruction-conditioned 4D dynamic scene understanding, along with 4DTrack, a graph-conditioned worldline inference method that outperforms adapted VLT baselines by 19.62 TGA points on the new benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing paradigms (large multimodal models and vision-language tracking) capture only fragmented aspects of 4D scene understanding — they either lack metric topology or remain tied to local 2D/3D tracking without persistent identity binding across space, time, and views.

Method: 4DTrack casts instruction-conditioned tracking as graph-conditioned worldline inference using: (1) an object-centric 4D state graph, (2) metric-guided routing, (3) bidirectional decoding, and (4) kinematic calibration.

Result: On Instruct-4D (129.4K QA pairs, 64.7K targets, 851 scenes), 4DTrack-Qwen3.5-9B achieves 62.68 TGA_Top1, surpassing the best adapted VLT baseline by 19.62 points.

Conclusion: Worldline-centered modeling improves both target grounding and recovered worldline quality, demonstrating the value of persistent identity binding for 4D dynamic scene understanding.

Abstract: 4D dynamic scene understanding requires grounding language to a persistent worldline that binds identity, metric 3D motion, and synchronized multi-view 2D projections. Existing paradigms capture only part of this structure: large multimodal models reason over rich visual evidence but rarely preserve metric topology, while vision-language tracking remains tied to fragmented 2D or 3D outputs and local continuation. We therefore introduce \textbf{4DVLT}, a worldline-centered task for instruction-conditioned 4D dynamic scene understanding in fully observed multi-view video, and \textbf{Instruct-4D}, a benchmark with 129.4K question-answer pairs, 64.7K target entities, 851 scenes, and 9 reasoning-oriented query types. To address this setting, we present \textbf{4DTrack}, which casts instruction-conditioned tracking as graph-conditioned worldline inference through an object-centric 4D state graph, metric-guided routing, bidirectional decoding, and kinematic calibration. On Instruct-4D, 4DTrack-Qwen3.5-9B reaches 62.68 $\mathrm{TGA}_{\mathrm{Top1}}$ and surpasses the best adapted VLT baseline by 19.62 points. These results show that worldline-centered modeling improves both target grounding and recovered worldline quality. The project page is available at https://github.com/mikubaka88/4DVLT.

</details>


### [183] [Learning Entropy Signature for Image Representation and Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22634)
*Jan Glaser,Ivo Bukovsky,Noriyasu Homma,Marcel Jirina*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper extends Learning Entropy to images via Spatial Learning Entropy Maps (SLEMs) and proposes Learning Entropy Signatures (LES)—a compact image descriptor based on the K largest LE locations—demonstrating that these signatures capture discriminative information for image classification, linking neural weight learning to information relevance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To extend Learning Entropy (originally for time series) to image analysis, and to investigate whether learning activity patterns in neural networks can serve as meaningful image descriptors for classification tasks.

Method: Spatial Learning Entropy Maps (SLEMs) are generated by sequentially presenting local pixel neighborhoods to a pretrained feedforward MLP in a fixed spatial order to predict central pixels. Learning Entropy Signatures (LES) are then derived by selecting the K locations with the largest LE values from the SLEM, forming a compact representation of image content based on learning weight behavior.

Result: A relatively small number of K largest LE locations preserves substantial discriminative information. LES provides effective image classification performance, showing that learning activity hotspots correlate with information-relevant image structures.

Conclusion: There is a close relationship between neural weight learning dynamics and information relevance in images. LES extends Learning Entropy's role from time series to images, and from structural point extraction to compact image representation and classification.

Abstract: Learning Entropy (LE) has recently been extended to image analysis through Spatial Learning Entropy Maps (SLEMs), which are two-dimensional LE distributions that highlight unusually high learning activity across an image. Unlike conventional image descriptors, SLEMs are generated by incremental, sample-wise learning of a pretrained feedforward MLP network, where local pixel neighborhoods are presented sequentially in a fixed spatial order to predict the corresponding central pixels. Consequently, the learning activity at each image location depends not only on its local structure but also on the knowledge acquired from previously processed locations. This paper introduces Learning Entropy Signatures (LES), an image descriptor derived from SLEM using the K largest LE locations. LES captures the spatial organization of learning-relevant image structures and provides a compact representation of image content based on learning weight behavior. Experimental evaluation on image classification tasks shows that a relatively small number of K largest LE locations preserve substantial discriminative information. The results indicate a close relationship between the learning of neural weights and information relevance, extending the role of Learning Entropy from time series to images and, within images, from structural point extraction to compact image representation and classification.

</details>


### [184] [MaRS: Robust Out-of-Distribution Detection via Mahalanobis Residual Scoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22649)
*Francesco Di Salvo,Sebastian Doerrich,Christian Ledig*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MaRS improves out-of-distribution detection in medical images by using Mahalanobis distance on reconstruction residuals in latent space, outperforming existing methods across modalities and distribution shifts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Foundation models for medical images degrade under distribution shifts (e.g., different patients, devices, acquisition conditions). Reliable OOD detection is essential for safe deployment, but existing reconstruction-based methods in latent space underperform. The authors hypothesized this is due to how reconstruction errors are scored (L2 residual norms collapse anisotropic residual structure), not poor reconstruction quality.

Method: MaRS (Mahalanobis Residual Scoring): a post-hoc, label-free OOD detector that (1) learns an in-distribution manifold via a lightweight autoencoder on frozen embeddings, (2) measures deviation via Mahalanobis distance on reconstruction residuals, yielding variance-aware OOD scores that preserve anisotropic residual structure.

Result: Across three imaging modalities, multiple distribution shift types, and different model families and scales, MaRS outperforms established confidence-, distance-, and reconstruction-based baselines while remaining fully post-hoc and lightweight.

Conclusion: The limitation of reconstruction-based OOD detection in latent space is due to scoring (L2 norm collapses anisotropic residuals), not reconstruction quality. MaRS addresses this with Mahalanobis residual scoring, achieving state-of-the-art OOD detection performance.

Abstract: Foundation models provide highly descriptive representations for medical images, yet their reliability degrades under distribution shifts arising from changes in patients, devices, or acquisition conditions. Reliable out-of-distribution (OOD) detection is therefore essential for safe deployment. Recent post-hoc detectors efficiently exploit frozen embeddings (\emph{e.g.}, kNN), whereas reconstruction-based OOD detection in latent feature space has seen limited adoption due to inconsistent performance. In this work, we show that the limitation of reconstruction-based methods in latent space does not stem from poor reconstruction quality, but from how reconstruction errors are scored. Standard $L_2$ residual norms collapse the anisotropic residual structure, thereby suppressing informative deviations. To address this limitation, we introduce \texttt{MaRS} (Mahalanobis Residual Scoring), a label-free OOD detector that learns an in-distribution manifold using a lightweight autoencoder and measures deviation via a Mahalanobis distance on reconstruction residuals, yielding variance-aware OOD scores. Across three imaging modalities, multiple types of distribution shift, and different model families and scales, \texttt{MaRS} outperforms established confidence-, distance-, and reconstruction-based baselines, while remaining fully post-hoc and lightweight. The code is available at https://github.com/francescodisalvo05/mars.

</details>


### [185] [Prompting Diffusion Models for Zero-Shot Instance Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22660)
*Irem Zeynep Alagöz,Nils Morbitzer,Andrea Ramazzina,Nassir Navab,Federico Tombari,Stefano Gasperini*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Prompt2Seg introduces a spatial conditioning framework for diffusion-based segmentation that takes explicit spatial prompts (2D Gaussians or confidence maps) to guide a frozen diffusion segmentation model, achieving superior zero-shot generalization across diverse domains by training on a limited set of object categories and outperforming the underlying diffusion backbone consistently.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Foundation models for promptable segmentation suffer from false positives and over-segmentation when accurately segmenting an object's region. Early attempts leveraging generative priors only use prompts during post-processing, yielding suboptimal segments agnostic to user input.

Method: Prompt2Seg augments a frozen diffusion segmentation model with a spatial conditioning branch. It takes explicit spatial prompts (2D Gaussians or confidence maps) as input signals, training the model to respond directly to user intent via spatial conditioning. Fine-tuned on a constrained set of object categories from Hypersim and Virtual KITTI 2.

Result: Evaluated on seven datasets spanning standard benchmarks, paintings, egocentric views, and X-ray data. Prompt2Seg consistently outperforms the underlying diffusion segmentation backbone across all benchmarks and generalizes zero-shot to unseen object types and visual domains.

Conclusion: Rich priors from generative pretraining combined with principled spatial conditioning offer a compelling path toward broadly generalizing interactive segmentation without requiring large-scale mask supervision.

Abstract: Several disruptive research directions have recently emerged in computer vision, including foundation models achieving previously unseen zero-shot performance in scene understanding, even interactively, and generative models that synthesize extremely realistic images. The latter have also been shown to be highly effective in scene understanding tasks thanks to their rich priors. However, for promptable segmentation, foundation models struggle with accurately segmenting an object's region, leading to false positives and over-segmentation. Notably, early attempts that leverage generative priors use prompts only during post-processing, yielding suboptimal segments because the process is agnostic to the user input. In this paper, we target these limitations with Prompt2Seg, a spatial conditioning framework for diffusion-based segmentation. Prompt2Seg augments a frozen diffusion segmentation model with a conditioning branch. Our approach takes spatial prompts, represented as 2D Gaussians or confidence maps, as explicit input signals, training the model to respond directly to user intent. Fine-tuned on a deliberately constrained set of object categories drawn from Hypersim and Virtual KITTI 2, Prompt2Seg generalizes zero-shot to a wide range of unseen object types and visual domains. We evaluate on seven datasets ranging from standard benchmarks to more challenging domains, including paintings, egocentric views, and X-ray data. Furthermore, we demonstrate that Prompt2Seg consistently outperforms the underlying diffusion segmentation backbone across all benchmarks. Our results suggest that the rich priors encoded in generative pretraining, combined with principled spatial conditioning, offer a compelling path toward broadly generalizing interactive segmentation without large-scale mask supervision.

</details>


### [186] [SATURN: Symbolic Spatial Reasoning for Multi-Perspective Grounding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22694)
*Danial Kamali,Tanawan Premsri,Shreya Rajpal,Amir Zadeh,Chuan Li,Parisa Kordjamshidi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SATURN is a neuro-symbolic framework that combines approximate 3D scene reconstruction with soft perspective-aware predicates and a Pythonic symbolic executor to achieve robust, compositional spatial reasoning across varying frames of reference. It introduces the 3D FORCE benchmark and outperforms VLMs and existing methods on both synthetic and real-world tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLMs fail at compositional spatial reasoning when relations depend on frames of reference, and existing neuro-symbolic methods rely on brittle geometric procedures and hard decisions over noisy perception.

Method: SATURN reconstructs an approximate 3D scene, derives soft perspective-aware spatial predicates, and composes them via a training-free Pythonic symbolic executor, preserving uncertainty through multi-hop inference.

Result: On 3D FORCE, SATURN remains stable and outperforms VLMs and spatially trained models as depth/perspective complexity grows. On MindCube, it achieves 78.57% accuracy, outperforming the strongest baseline by 14 percentage points.

Conclusion: Decoupling perception from reasoning with soft predicates and symbolic execution yields robust, perspective-aware compositional spatial reasoning without training.

Abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) remain unreliable when spatial reasoning requires composing relations whose meanings depend on frames of reference. Existing neuro-symbolic methods make reasoning more explicit, but often depend on brittle geometric procedures and hard decisions over noisy perception. We propose SATURN, a neuro-symbolic framework for perspective-aware compositional spatial reasoning. SATURN reconstructs an approximate 3D scene, derives soft perspective-aware spatial predicates, and composes them with a training-free Pythonic symbolic executor, separating perception from reasoning while preserving uncertainty through multi-hop inference. We also introduce 3D FORCE, a diagnostic benchmark that controls reasoning depth, view, and perspective composition across spatial arrangement grounding (SAG) and referring expression grounding (REF). On 3D FORCE, VLMs and spatially trained models degrade sharply as depth and perspective complexity increase, whereas SATURN remains stable and outperforms strong baselines. On the real-world MindCube benchmark, SATURN achieves 78.57% overall accuracy, outperforming the strongest baseline by 14 pp.

</details>


### [187] [NullFlow: One-Step Generative Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22696)
*Xiao Shi,Edward P. Chandler,Chicago Y. Park,Shirin Shoushtari,Ulugbek S. Kamilov*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: NullFlow is a one-step generative image reconstruction framework that constrains generative flow to a measurement-consistent subspace, eliminating the need for separate data-fidelity corrections and reducing inference from hundreds of network evaluations to a single one, matching state-of-the-art diffusion solvers on image inpainting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing generative reconstruction solvers require separate data-fidelity corrections and iterative (step-by-step) integration, leading to high computational cost. NullFlow aims to achieve one-step inference without compromising reconstruction quality.

Method: NullFlow confines the generative flow to a measurement-consistent subspace, so the flow never violates data fidelity. It learns the flow's average velocity via a single network evaluation, bypassing traditional step-by-step flow matching integration. The training objective's global minimizer is proven to be a one-step posterior sampler.

Result: On image inpainting, NullFlow matches state-of-the-art diffusion solvers in quality while reducing inference cost from hundreds of network evaluations to just one.

Conclusion: NullFlow provides a principled, one-step framework for generative image reconstruction that is both efficient and theoretically grounded, with strong empirical performance on inpainting tasks.

Abstract: We propose NullFlow, a principled framework for one-step generative image reconstruction. Our key idea is to confine the generative flow to a measurement-consistent subspace. Because the flow never leaves this subspace, NullFlow needs no separate data-fidelity corrections, unlike existing solvers. NullFlow samples in a single network evaluation by learning the flow's average velocity, avoiding the step-by-step integration of traditional flow matching methods. We prove that the average velocity of this constrained flow yields a training objective whose global minimizer is a one-step posterior sampler. We show on image inpainting that NullFlow matches state-of-the-art diffusion solvers while cutting inference from hundreds of network evaluations to one.

</details>


### [188] [Modular Diffusion Models for Structured Visual Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22702)
*Siddhesh Khandelwal,Björn Ommer,Leonid Sigal*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Modular Diffusion Models (MDMs) decompose the diffusion process into task-specific modules to learn distributions over structured outputs (object detection, segmentation, scene graph generation), capturing prediction uncertainty and multi-modality in complex visual scenes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional supervised methods produce deterministic point estimates that fail to capture inherent uncertainty in structured visual recognition tasks, including natural ambiguities (e.g., occlusion, boundary ambiguity) and data noise/sparsity.

Method: MDMs decompose the diffusion process into distinct, task-specific modules, each capturing a different aspect of structured information (categories, locations, relationships). Modules are learned independently and integrated at inference without additional training, handling heterogeneous output spaces (continuous + discrete).

Result: Experimental validation on object detection, instance segmentation, and scene graph generation demonstrates the benefits of the MDM framework.

Conclusion: The modular diffusion framework effectively captures prediction uncertainty and multi-modality in structured visual recognition tasks by decomposing the output space into independently learnable components.

Abstract: Traditional supervised methods for structured visual recognition tasks -- such as object detection, segmentation, and scene graph generation -- often produce deterministic, fixed outputs, limiting their ability to capture the inherent uncertainty in complex visual scenes. As a consequence, such point estimates are unable to capture the prediction uncertainty (or multi modality) intrinsic to these problems, often arising from natural ambiguities (e.g., ambiguity in size of partially occluded objects, local ambiguity of exact segmentation boundary, etc.) as well as noise and sparsity of training data. To address this limitation, we present Modular Diffusion Models (MDMs), a simple and novel framework that learns a distribution over structured outputs for a given input image. MDMs decompose the diffusion process into distinct, task-specific modules, each focused on capturing a different aspect of the structured information space, such as object categories, spatial locations, and inter-object relationships. This modular design allows each component to be learned independently, with seamless integration at inference without additional training. Furthermore, the modularity of MDMs enables the diffusion process to easily operate over the heterogeneous output space common in many structured learning tasks (e.g., a continuous bounding boxes and discrete class labels). Experimental results over three distinct structured tasks -- object detection, instance segmentation, and scene graph generation -- highlight the benefits of our proposed framework.

</details>


### [189] [Generative Relightable Avatars](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22718)
*Kunwar Maheep Singh,Christian Theobalt,Rishabh Dabral*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GRA is a person-specific method for photorealistic free-view rendering and environment-map relighting of full-body humans, combining physics-grounded relighting with generative refinement and a video-to-video diffusion model for temporally coherent, high-detail outputs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modeling fine-grained appearance details is inherently a one-to-many problem that can benefit from a generative formulation, as opposed to fully regressive approaches.

Method: The method follows a hybrid approach: (1) optimize material parameters in UV-space from a tracked animated mesh and render a coarse relit appearance under target HDR environment maps; (2) refine textures with a feed-forward model to capture pose-dependent dynamics and complex illumination effects; (3) use a fine-tuned video-to-video diffusion model with an error-recycling strategy for temporally coherent, high-detail videos while preserving 3D control.

Result: Improved perceptual quality over prior relightable avatar baselines demonstrated through experimental evaluations.

Conclusion: The hybrid generative-relighting approach outperforms fully regressive methods in perceptual quality for free-view relighting of full-body humans.

Abstract: We present Generative Relightable Avatars (GRA), a person-specific method for photorealistic free-view rendering and environment-map relighting of full-body humans. We postulate that modeling fine-grained appearance details is inherently a one-to-many problem that can benefit from a generative formulation. In contrast to fully regressive relightable avatar methods, GRA follows a hybrid approach that combines controllable, physics-grounded relighting with probabilistic refinement. Starting from a tracked animated mesh, we optimize material parameters in UV-space and render a coarse relit appearance under a target HDR environment map. Next, we refine the textures with a feed-forward model to capture pose-dependent texture dynamics and illumination effects beyond simplified reflectance assumptions. Finally, a fine-tuned video-to-video diffusion model transforms the physically grounded renderings into temporally coherent, high-detail videos while preserving 3D control, with an error-recycling strategy for generating long videos. Experimental evaluations demonstrate our method's improved perceptual quality over prior relightable avatar baselines. Project Page: https://vcai.mpi-inf.mpg.de/projects/GRA/

</details>


### [190] [Interpretable Uncertainty Routing Separating Emotion Ambiguity from Distribution Shift in Facial Expression Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22725)
*Keito Inoshita,Takato Ueno*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes Uncertainty-Aware Routing (UAR) for facial expression recognition, which decomposes uncertainty into aleatoric (annotator disagreement) and epistemic (distribution shift) components using a Deep Ensemble of DINOv2 models. Aleatoric recovers annotator disagreement (Spearman 0.66), epistemic detects corruption shifts (AUROC 0.699), and UAR retains 1.8x more ambiguous in-distribution faces than single-uncertainty routing at matched OOD rejection rates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: FER faces inherent ambiguity where human annotators disagree (in-distribution) and distribution shift (out-of-distribution) demand different downstream actions—reporting ambiguity vs. rejection—but a single uncertainty score conflates both, motivating the need for uncertainty decomposition.

Method: Uncertainty is decomposed into aleatoric and epistemic components from a Deep Ensemble of fully fine-tuned DINOv2 models. Each component is validated against independent external signals: aleatoric against human annotator disagreement, epistemic against corruption-induced distribution shift. Then UAR, an inference-time routing mechanism, uses the separated uncertainties to route inputs differently.

Result: Aleatoric recovers annotator disagreement (Spearman 0.66, 95% CI: 0.64-0.68); epistemic detects corruption shifts (avg AUROC 0.699 at highest severity). UAR retains ~1.8x more ambiguous in-distribution faces than single-uncertainty routing at matched OOD rejection rate.

Conclusion: Uncertainty decomposition enables interpretable and differentiated action selection for FER—ambiguity reporting vs. OOD rejection—which a single uncertainty score or label-distribution-learning baseline cannot achieve, as the latter cannot separate ambiguity from shift and thus cannot route.

Abstract: Facial expression recognition (FER) is inherently ambiguous: human annotators frequently disagree, and models deployed in real environments face distribution shift. Crucially, these two conditions demand different downstream actions, as ambiguous in-distribution faces should be reported with their ambiguity whereas out-of-distribution inputs should be rejected. However, a single uncertainty score conflates the two. In this study, uncertainty decomposition into aleatoric and epistemic components for FER is investigated, and Uncertainty-Aware Routing (UAR), an inference-time routing mechanism that exploits the separation, is introduced. Specifically, aleatoric and epistemic uncertainties are obtained from a Deep Ensemble of fully fine-tuned DINOv2 models and are each validated against an independent external signal: aleatoric against human annotator disagreement, and epistemic against distribution shift induced by image corruptions. The proposed dual-validation protocol reveals that aleatoric recovers annotator disagreement with Spearman correlation 0.66 (95% CI: 0.64-0.68), and epistemic detects corruption-induced shifts, achieving average AUROC of 0.699 at the highest corruption severity. UAR retains approximately 1.8 times more ambiguous in-distribution faces than single-uncertainty routing at a matched out-of-distribution rejection rate. A strong label-distribution-learning baseline achieves comparable disagreement recovery but cannot separate ambiguity from shift and therefore cannot route, establishing that the value of decomposition lies in the separation enabling interpretable and differentiated action selection.

</details>


### [191] [RaysUp: Ultra-light Universal Feature Upsampling via Geometry-Aware Ray Representation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22749)
*Yuchuan Ding,Linfei Li,Lin Zhang,Ying Shen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RaysUp is an ultra-lightweight, task-agnostic, and VFM-agnostic feature upsampling framework that reconstructs high-resolution feature maps by lifting reconstruction into a geometry-aware ray domain, using Spatially Decoupled Guidance Encoder, Any-Resolution Cross-Attention, Ray Positional Encoding (RayPE) with 6D Plücker coordinates, and Geometry-Aware Neighborhood Attention. It achieves SOTA performance with only 16% parameters of AnyUp and ~7x faster inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pre-trained VFMs produce inherently low-resolution patchified/pooled outputs, limiting fine-grained pixel-level reasoning. Existing upsampling approaches degrade semantic fidelity or rely on VFM-specific retraining and heavy architectures, hindering efficiency and scalability.

Method: RaysUp introduces a geometry-aware ray-domain reconstruction paradigm with four key components: (1) Spatially Decoupled Guidance Encoder for direction-aware encoding; (2) Any-Resolution Cross-Attention for resolution-flexible reconstruction; (3) Ray Positional Encoding (RayPE) that injects implicit 3D geometric priors via 6D Plücker ray coordinates; (4) Geometry-Aware Neighborhood Attention for content-adaptive bilateral aggregation with geometric consistency.

Result: RaysUp achieves state-of-the-art performance across diverse dense prediction tasks, using only 16% of the parameters of AnyUp and delivering approximately 7x faster inference.

Conclusion: RaysUp establishes a substantially improved accuracy-efficiency trade-off and serves as a practical and scalable solution for universal feature upsampling.

Abstract: Pre-trained Vision Foundation Models (VFMs) have become central to modern computer vision due to their powerful semantic representations and strong generalization ability. However, their patchified or pooled outputs are inherently low-resolution, limiting their effectiveness in tasks requiring fine-grained, pixel-level reasoning. Existing feature upsampling approaches either degrade semantic fidelity or rely on VFM-specific retraining and heavy architectures, hindering efficiency and scalability. To address these challenges, we propose RaysUp, an ultra-lightweight, task-agnostic, and VFM-agnostic feature upsampling framework that reconstructs high-resolution feature maps at arbitrary resolutions. Unlike conventional 2D interpolation or attention-based schemes, RaysUp lifts feature reconstruction into a geometry-aware ray domain. Specifically, we introduce a Spatially Decoupled Guidance Encoder for direction-aware guidance encoding, an Any-Resolution Cross-Attention mechanism for resolution-flexible reconstruction, and a novel Ray Positional Encoding (RayPE) that injects implicit 3D geometric priors via 6D Plucker ray coordinates. Finally, a Geometry-Aware Neighborhood Attention module further ensures content-adaptive bilateral aggregation while preserving geometric consistency. Extensive experiments across diverse dense prediction tasks demonstrate that RaysUp achieves state-of-the-art performance while using only 16% of the parameters of AnyUp and delivering approximately 7x faster inference. These results highlight a substantially improved accuracy-efficiency trade-off and establish RaysUp as a practical and scalable solution for universal feature upsampling. Code is available at https://github.com/MAP-RaysUp/RaysUp.

</details>


### [192] [READ More than What You See: Reinforcement Learning for Accurate and Coherent Audio Description Generations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22766)
*Bo Fang,Xinyao Zhang,Yuxin Song,Hui Zhang,Hang Zhou,Antoni B. Chan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: READ introduces the first reinforcement-learning framework for Audio Description generation, optimizing sequence-level outputs with reference-matching, length, format, and coherence rewards, achieving state-of-the-art results on multiple benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing AD methods suffer from style mismatch when prompting off-the-shelf models or produce generic, biased descriptions due to suboptimal next-token prediction training that fails to fully leverage model capacity.

Method: READ formulates AD as a sequence-level optimization problem using RL with multiple reward functions: reference-matching, length, format, and a novel context-aware coherence reward for narrative coherence.

Result: Substantially outperforms prior methods across MAD-Eval, CMD-AD, and TV-AD benchmarks on diverse evaluation metrics.

Conclusion: RL is a promising paradigm for producing accurate and coherent AD generation, with codes, models, and benchmarks to be released publicly.

Abstract: Audio Description aims to generate concise narrations of essential visual content in audio-visual media for blind and low-vision audiences. Existing methods either rely on prompting off-the-shelf multimodal models, which often mismatch AD style, or partially optimize training-based systems with next-token prediction, which under-explores model capacity and biases generation toward generic expressions. We present READ, the first reinforcement-learning (RL) framework for training-based AD generation. READ formulates AD as sequence-level optimization with reference-matching, length, and format rewards, and further introduces a dedicated coherence reward under context-aware supervision to promote narratively coherent descriptions. Experiments on MAD-Eval, CMD-AD, and TV-AD show that READ substantially outperforms prior methods across diverse evaluation metrics. Our results highlight RL as a promising paradigm for accurate and coherent AD generation. Our codes, models, and benchmark results will be publicly available.

</details>


### [193] [LoCC: Detection and Localization of Lip-Syncing Deepfakes via Counterfactual Frame Consistency](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22772)
*Soumyya Kanti Datta,Shan Jia,Siwei Lyu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LoCC is a detection framework for lip-syncing deepfakes that identifies localized inconsistencies in lip motion by evaluating frame-level alignment with counterfactual estimates from temporal neighbors, using a teacher-student learning paradigm, and outperforms SOTA methods on multiple benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Lip-syncing deepfakes are challenging to detect because artifacts are localized to the mouth region and evolve dynamically over time, requiring precise temporal and spatial modeling of lip motion that holistic video analysis fails to capture.

Method: LoCC performs fine-grained detection by evaluating whether each frame aligns with a counterfactual estimate generated from its temporal neighbors, following a teacher-student learning paradigm to capture frame-level discrepancies in lip motion consistency.

Result: Achieves superior performance over state-of-the-art methods on LAV-DF, AVDF1M, FakeAVCeleb, and KODF datasets, and generalizes well across different compression levels and datasets.

Conclusion: Real videos exhibit strong and stable temporal consistency, while lip-sync deepfakes introduce localized inconsistencies; the counterfactual-based frame-level analysis effectively detects these artifacts.

Abstract: Lip-syncing deepfakes are among the most challenging forms of manipulated media because their artifacts are localized almost exclusively to the mouth region and evolve dynamically over time. Detecting such deepfakes requires precise temporal and spatial modeling of lip motion. In this paper, we propose LoCC, a novel detection framework that performs fine-grained detection and localization of lip-syncing deepfakes at both segment and frame levels. Unlike prior approaches that analyze videos holistically, our method evaluates whether each frame aligns with a counterfactual estimate generated from its temporal neighbors. Real videos exhibit strong and stable consistency, whereas lip-sync deepfakes introduce localized inconsistencies. Following a teacher-student learning paradigm, our model effectively captures these frame-level discrepancies and achieves superior performance over state-of-the-art methods on multiple benchmark lip-syncing deepfake datasets, including LAV-DF, AVDF1M, FakeAVCeleb, and KODF, and generalizes well across compression levels and datasets.

</details>


### [194] [Visual Geometry Transformer in the Wild: Distractor-Free 3D Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22787)
*Tianbo Pan,Xingyi Yang,Shizun Wang,Xinchao Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VGTW is an end-to-end framework for multi-view 3D reconstruction that handles real-world inconsistencies (transient distractors, occlusions) by isolating and suppressing distractor-affected regions via a Distractor-aware Training strategy with auxiliary mask supervision, producing clean point clouds without extra 3D supervision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing end-to-end multi-view 3D reconstruction methods rely on a restrictive static assumption (distractor-free scenes with perfect cross-view geometry), causing failure in real-world settings where transient distractors and occlusions are present.

Method: Proposes Visual Geometry Transformer in the Wild (VGTW) with a Distractor-aware Training (DAT) strategy that separates clean features from distractor-contaminated ones in the attention mechanism while enforcing feature consistency across views. Uses an auxiliary mask prediction head trained on a new dataset with pixel-level distractor masks.

Result: State-of-the-art performance and robust generalization in diverse real-world scenarios, producing clean, distractor-free point clouds directly without additional 3D supervision.

Conclusion: VGTW is computationally efficient, compatible with existing pipelines, and enables robust 3D reconstruction from inconsistent real-world multi-view inputs.

Abstract: Current end-to-end multi-view 3D reconstruction methods achieve impressive results, but rely on a restrictive static assumption: the scenes is entire distractor-free with perfect cross-view geometry. This reliance on idealized inputs causes even the most advanced methods to fail in real-world settings, where transient distractors and occlusions present. To address this, we propose Visual Geometry Transformer in the Wild (VGTW), an end-to-end framework for robust reconstruction from inconsistent views. At its core, we isolate and suppress distractor-affected regions while preserving the consistent components across views. Specifically, we introduce a Distractor-aware Training (DAT) strategy that separates clean features from distractor-contaminated ones in the attention mechanism while enforcing feature consistency across images. To enable this, we train the model with an auxiliary mask prediction head, using supervision from a new dataset we collected with pixel-level distractor masks. The resulting VGTW model is a feed-forward network that directly outputs clean, distractor-free point clouds. Remarkably, it requires no additional 3D supervision, remains computationally efficient, and is compatible with existing pipelines. Extensive experiments validate our approach, demonstrating state-of-the-art performance and robust generalization in diverse, real-world scenarios.

</details>


### [195] [Learning Adaptive Dynamical Features via Multi-$τ$ Liquid-Mamba for All-in-one Image Restoration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22801)
*Hu Gao,Changshuo Wang,Yulong Chen,Lizhuang Ma*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes Multi-τ Liquid-Mamba, an adaptive state space module with input-conditioned multi-timescale liquid discretization for all-in-one image restoration, achieving SOTA results while preserving Mamba's linear complexity and plug-and-play integrability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Mamba-based image restoration models rely on a single state-evolution timescale, limiting their adaptability to spatially heterogeneous and task-dependent degradation patterns in all-in-one image restoration.

Method: Introduces input-conditioned multi-timescale liquid discretization into selective state space modeling, modulating effective discretization steps of multiple dynamical branches and adaptively fusing responses via degradation-aware gating weights, while preserving the original selective parameterization and hardware-efficient scan mechanism.

Result: MLMIR achieves state-of-the-art performance in all-in-one image restoration and remains highly competitive in task-aligned restoration settings across extensive benchmarks.

Conclusion: Multi-τ Liquid-Mamba is a plug-and-play module that enhances adaptability to heterogeneous degradation patterns while maintaining linear complexity, offering a practical advancement for Mamba-based architectures in image restoration.

Abstract: Image restoration aims to recover high-quality images from degraded observations. Recent Mamba-based image restoration models have demonstrated strong potential in modeling long-range dependencies with linear complexity. However, most existing designs still rely on a single state-evolution timescale, which limits their adaptability to spatially heterogeneous and task-dependent degradation patterns in all-in-one image restoration. In this paper, we propose Multi-$τ$ Liquid-Mamba, an adaptive state space module that introduces input-conditioned multi-timescale liquid discretization into selective state space modeling. Instead of changing the overall selective scan pipeline, the proposed module modulates the effective discretization steps of multiple dynamical branches and adaptively fuses their responses according to degradation-aware gating weights. This design allows the model to capture both fast-varying local details and slowly evolving global structures while preserving the linear scaling property of Mamba with respect to sequence length. Importantly, Multi-$τ$ Liquid-Mamba modulates the effective transition dynamics while preserving the original selective parameterization and hardware-efficient selective scan mechanism, making it a plug-and-play module that can be seamlessly integrated into existing Mamba-based architectures. Built upon this framework, we develop a Multi-$τ$ Liquid-Mamba Image Restoration Network (MLMIR) for all-in-one image restoration. Extensive experiments on a wide range of restoration benchmarks demonstrate that MLMIR consistently achieves state-of-the-art performance in all-in-one image restoration while remaining highly competitive in task-aligned restoration settings.

</details>


### [196] [CoVStream: Edge-Cloud Collaboration for Understanding of Long Video Streams](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22804)
*Xu Liu,Guikun Chen,Zihao Yan,Kanzhi Wu,Wenguan Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CoVStream is an edge-cloud collaborative framework for long video understanding that reduces bandwidth by 87.6% while retaining 99.2% of cloud-level accuracy by distilling video on edge devices and only activating heavy reasoning on the cloud upon user query.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing long video understanding approaches overlook the deployment constraint that video streams are often produced by computationally constrained edge devices, creating an untenable trade-off between cloud offloading (high bandwidth) and on-device processing (limited capacity).

Method: CoVStream employs a two-tier architecture: (1) edge node distills raw video into compact visual features and semantic captions to minimize bandwidth; (2) cloud server constructs an entity graph and global visual context, activating a heavy reasoning model only upon user query arrival.

Result: On VideoMME-Long, LVBench, and RTV-Bench, CoVStream reduces bandwidth by 87.6% while retaining 99.2% of cloud baseline accuracy on LVBench.

Conclusion: Edge-cloud collaborative streaming with selective heavy reasoning activation can effectively resolve the bandwidth-accuracy trade-off in long video understanding on resource-constrained devices.

Abstract: Long, continuous video streams are an increasingly critical driver of multimedia intelligence. Existing efforts often handle long videos with a sample-encode-reason approach using large models. However, they overlook a crucial deployment fact: the stream is often produced by computationally constrained devices. This forces an untenable compromise: cloud offloading unlocks strong reasoning but incurs prohibitive bandwidth overhead, while on-device processing remains limited by edge hardware capacity. Therefore, we propose CoVStream, the first edge-cloud collaborative framework for understanding long video streams. The edge node distills raw video streams into compact visual features and semantic captions for transmission to the cloud, minimizing bandwidth costs, while the cloud server integrates this data into an entity graph and global visual context, activating the heavy reasoning model only when a user query arrives. Experiments on VideoMME-Long, LVBench, and RTV-Bench show that CoVStream reduces bandwidth usage by 87.6% while retaining 99.2% of the cloud baseline accuracy on LVBench.

</details>


### [197] [Policy-as-Data: Learning Generalizable HOI Diffusion Models from Simulated Physics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22806)
*Shujia Li,Jianshu Hu,Haiyu Zhang,Yunpeng Jiang,Haoyuan Jin,Xinyuan Chen,Yaohui Wang,Yutong Ban*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes a physics-simulator-based framework (ours) that uses RL-trained policies to generate synthetic task-oriented HOI data, bridging representation gaps via coarse-to-fine retargeting, achieving better generalization, long-horizon generation, dynamic diversity, and physical plausibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current data-driven HOI generation is limited by expensive, functionally narrow motion capture datasets, causing poor generalization to unseen objects and lack of physical consistency over long horizons.

Method: (1) RL-trained policies in a physics simulator generate task-oriented synthetic HOI data; (2) a coarse-to-fine retargeting process bridges the representation gap between simplified simulator models and standard parametric body models; (3) a generative model is trained on the augmented dataset.

Result: Enhanced generalization to unseen objects, capability of long-horizon generation, greater dynamic diversity, and improved physical plausibility.

Conclusion: Physics-simulated synthetic data can effectively overcome the data-scarcity bottleneck in HOI generation, enabling scalable and physically consistent generation.

Abstract: Synthesizing realistic Human-Object Interactions (HOI) is critical for creating embodied avatars and functional virtual environments. However, current data-driven approaches primarily rely on motion capture datasets, which are expensive to scale and limited in functional diversity. Models trained with these datasets fail to generalize to unseen objects and maintain physical consistency over long horizons. In this paper, we propose a novel framework that leverages a physics simulator to overcome the data-scarcity bottleneck in HOI generation. Specifically, we propose a scalable pipeline, called \ours, which leverages policies trained with reinforcement learning in a physics simulator for task-oriented data generation and trains a generative model on the augmented dataset for generalizable HOI generation. To seamlessly utilize the synthetic data, we introduce a coarse-to-fine retargeting process that bridges the representation gap between the simplified model used in physics simulator and the standard parametric body models required for generative training. Validated through comprehensive experiments, our method demonstrates enhanced generalization to unseen objects and the capability of long-horizon generation, while exhibiting greater dynamic diversity and physical plausibility.

</details>


### [198] [DBT-Bleed: Dual-Branch Temporal Modeling with Key-Frame Selection for Surgical Bleeding Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22829)
*Sudhanshu Mishra,Jialang Xu,Jensen Ang,Evangelos B. Mazomenos,Beng Ti Ang,Yueming Jin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DBT-Bleed is a dual-branch multi-scale temporal modeling framework for detecting intraoperative bleeding adverse events in long surgical videos. It uses layer-wise temporal adapters (short/long-term) and a hierarchical entropy-driven frame selection (HiRED) to reduce redundancy while preserving temporal dynamics. It achieves ~6.5% F1 improvement on MultiBypass and zero-shot transferability (+6% F1) on the new EndoPit-IAE neurosurgery dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Intraoperative Adverse Event (IAE) detection, especially bleeding, is critical for surgical safety. Existing methods fail to distinguish bleeding from visually similar residual blood due to limited temporal reasoning, and processing long surgical videos with fine-grained temporal dynamics is computationally prohibitive.

Method: (1) DBT-Bleed: a dual-branch framework with layer-wise temporal adapters that disentangle bleeding vs. normal representations across short- and long-term temporal scales. (2) HiRED (Hierarchical Entropy-Driven frame selection): an efficient strategy that retains temporally informative segments while discarding redundancy for processing long videos.

Result: On MultiBypass: +6.53% F1, +5.62% Recall, +9% MCC over video-level baselines. On a new neurosurgery dataset (EndoPit-IAE), zero-shot transfer gains +6% F1 and +8% MCC.

Conclusion: DBT-Bleed with HiRED effectively solves bleeding IAE detection by modeling multi-scale temporal dynamics, achieves state-of-the-art results on two surgical video datasets, and introduces EndoPit-IAE, the first IAE-annotated neurosurgery dataset, enabling future cross-procedure generalization research.

Abstract: Intraoperative Adverse Events (IAEs) detection is critical for improving surgical safety, with bleeding being among the most frequent events across many surgery types. Existing methods struggle to distinguish bleeding IAE from visually similar residual blood due to limited temporal reasoning. Moreover, modeling long surgical videos while preserving fine-grained temporal dynamics remains computationally challenging. We propose DBT-Bleed, a dual-branch multi-scale temporal modeling framework disentangling bleeding and normal representations using layer-wise temporal adapters for short- and long-term bleeding progression. To efficiently process long surgical videos without sacrificing fine-grained temporal information, we introduce HiRED, a Hierarchical Entropy-Driven frame selection strategy that retains temporally informative segments while removing redundancy. Experiments on the MultiBypass dataset demonstrate gains of 6.53% in F1, 5.62% in Recall and 9% in MCC values for bleeding IAE detection, consistently outperforming video-level baselines. Additionally, we evaluate cross-procedure generalization on a newly curated dataset from a different surgical procedure type, where DBT-Bleed demonstrates robust transferability by achieving gain of 6% in F1 and 8% in MCC under zero-shot setting. To support this evaluation, we introduce EndoPit-IAE, an Endonasal Pituitary Surgery dataset annotated for IAEs, representing the first IAE-annotated dataset in neurosurgery. Code will be made publicly available upon acceptance.

</details>


### [199] [Homographic Navigation: Geometry-Driven Camera Guidance for Deterministic Planar Capture](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22834)
*Dominik Kroupa,Marek Vaško,Muh Yuzril Ihza Baharuddin,Adam Herout*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A geometry-centric framework called homographic navigation uses homography as an organizing variable to guide camera acquisition for precise planar region capture, enabling single-shot artifact recognition and localization from minimal supervision via synthetic data augmentation, two-pass inference, and Stable Warp training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To achieve precise planar region capture from minimal supervision (single annotated reference image) by unifying learning, alignment, and evaluation through homography as a core organizing variable rather than just an output.

Method: 1) Homographic augmentation generates unlimited synthetic training data from a single annotated reference image. 2) Single-shot model jointly recognizes and localizes artifacts via sparse keypoint prediction. 3) Two-pass inference (global detection + localized refinement) addresses limited input resolution. 4) Stable Warp training strategy improves high-precision accuracy. 5) Model predicts confidence estimates per keypoint and per sample.

Result: Accurate planar alignment can be achieved from minimal supervision, with significant accuracy improvements in the high-precision regime via Stable Warp training and two-pass inference.

Conclusion: The framework provides a foundation for geometry-driven camera guidance and future learning from in-the-wild video data, demonstrating that precise planar alignment is feasible with minimal supervision.

Abstract: We present homographic navigation, a geometry-centric framework for guiding camera acquisition toward precise capture of planar regions. Rather than treating homography as an output, we use it as an organizing variable that unifies learning, alignment, and evaluation. From a single annotated reference image, we generate unlimited synthetic training data via homographic augmentation and train a single-shot model for joint recognition and localization of multiple artifacts (physical objects with a rectangular planar target) through sparse keypoint prediction. To address precision under limited model input resolution, we introduce a two-pass inference scheme with global detection followed by localized refinement, and a Stable Warp training strategy that significantly improves accuracy, particularly in the high-precision regime. The model also predicts confidence estimates per predicted keypoint and per the whole sample. Experimental results demonstrate that accurate planar alignment can be achieved from minimal supervision, providing a foundation for geometry-driven camera guidance and future learning from in-the-wild video data.

</details>


### [200] [OrthoMotion:Disentangling Camera and Subject Motion via Geometry Semantics Orthogonal Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22835)
*Zijie Meng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: OrthoMotion achieves guaranteed disentanglement of camera and subject control in video generation by algebraically orthogonalizing their respective operators (RoPE rotation for camera, gated value injection for subject), cutting cross-talk by >2.4x with state-of-the-art accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Controllable video generation requires independent control of camera and subject motion, but 2D conditioning inherently entangles them because both produce optical flow with the same inverse-depth (1/Z) scaling, making the 2D camera/object split a non-identifiable inverse problem.

Method: OrthoMotion decouples camera and subject at the attention operator level: camera motion is encoded as a norm-preserving rotation of the RoPE phase (geometric channel), while subject motion is encoded as gated value injection in cross-attention (semantic channel). A lightweight decoupling regularizer drives their response subspaces to orthogonality.

Result: State-of-the-art camera and subject accuracy simultaneously; cross-talk reduced by more than 2.4x measured by a new Cross-Talk Error (CTE) metric, with no loss in fidelity and generalization across backbones.

Conclusion: OrthoMotion is the first method to guarantee disentanglement by construction rather than relying on emergent properties, resolving the representational entanglement via algebraically complementary operator design.

Abstract: Controllable video generation demands independent command of the camera and the subject, yet 2D conditioning entangles them: camera- and object-induced optical flow share the same inverse-depth (1/Z) scaling and cannot be separated from image evidence alone. We first prove that this entanglement is representational, not architectural -- the 2D camera/object split is a non-identifiable inverse problem -- and therefore reframe decoupling as a question of operator design. We resolve it at the level of the attention operator. OrthoMotion routes camera motion into a geometric channel, a norm-preserving rotation of the rotary position embedding (RoPE) phase, and subject motion into a semantic channel, a gated value injection in cross-attention. Because these sub-operators are algebraically complementary -- a rotation versus a translation of the affine action on tokens -- a lightweight decoupling regularizer provably drives their response subspaces to orthogonality, so the two controls stop interfering. To our knowledge OrthoMotion is the first method to guarantee disentanglement by construction rather than hope for it to emerge. It attains state-of-the-art camera and subject accuracy at once while minimizing cross-talk, which we quantify with a new Cross-Talk Error (CTE) metric, cutting cross-talk by more than 2.4x with no loss in fidelity and generalizing across backbones.

</details>


### [201] [G-MASt3R-SfM: Graph-based View Pruning and Multi-stage Optimization for Robust SfM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22856)
*Toshiki Watanabe,Shintaro Ito,Natsuki Takama,Koichi Ito,Takafumi Aoki*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: G-MASt3R-SfM improves Structure from Motion accuracy by introducing Graph-based View Pruning and Multi-Stage Optimization modules to filter out unreliable matches from MASt3R, achieving state-of-the-art results on ETH3D.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MASt3R generates robust correspondences but produces incorrect matches for non-overlapping image pairs, which degrades pose estimation accuracy when directly used in SfM pipelines like MASt3R-SfM.

Method: Proposes two modules: (1) Graph-based View Pruning (GVP) constructs a scene graph from matching confidence and geometrically removes outlier views; (2) Multi-Stage Optimization (MSO) progressively refines camera parameters from local to global consistency.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on ETH3D dataset for both camera pose estimation and 3D reconstruction, effectively suppressing noise from outlier correspondences.

Conclusion: G-MASt3R-SfM successfully addresses the non-overlapping image pair matching issue in MASt3R-based SfM, enhancing robustness and accuracy.

Abstract: Structure from Motion (SfM) is essential for multi-view 3D reconstruction, however, its accuracy heavily relies on the accuracy of image matching. While the recent correspondence matching method, MASt3R, enables robust matching even under challenging conditions, it tends to generate incorrect correspondences for non-overlapping image pairs. Consequently, existing SfM methods using MASt3R, such as MASt3R-SfM, suffer from significant degradation in pose estimation accuracy as they incorporate these unreliable matches directly into optimization. To address this issue, we propose G-MASt3R-SfM, a novel SfM pipeline that enhances robustness through two key modules. First, the Graph-based View Pruning (GVP) module constructs a scene graph from matching confidence and geometrically prunes outlier views. Second, the Multi-Stage Optimization (MSO) module progressively refines camera parameters by expanding the optimization scope from local consistency to the global consistency. Experiments on the ETH3D dataset demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art accuracy in both camera pose estimation and 3D reconstruction, effectively suppressing noise caused by outliers.

</details>


### [202] [Chains That See, Answers That Don't: A Multi-Aspect Evaluation Recipe for Forced Chain-of-Thought on Video-MME](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22862)
*Zhichao Fan,Yanhang Li,Zexin Zhuang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Forced chain-of-thought (CoT) does not improve video QA accuracy for Qwen2.5-VL despite chains being genuinely video-conditioned; a three-probe evaluation reveals no benefit and even a small drop for the 7B model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To test the widespread assumption that forced chain-of-thought (CoT) makes vision-language models more reliable on video question answering, by designing a controlled evaluation recipe that separates chain faithfulness from downstream accuracy.

Method: A three-probe evaluation recipe: (1) paired accuracy across direct, CoT, answer-first, and no-video conditions; (2) a counterfactual video-swap diagnostic over CoT chains; (3) a four-rung visual-degradation ladder. All probes are reported under strict and permissive regex scorers with multiplicity correction. Applied to Qwen2.5-VL on Video-MME subsets.

Result: CoT chains are strongly video-conditioned (swapping video collapses chain overlap and flips most answers), but forced CoT does not improve MCQ accuracy; on the 7B model it produces a small but statistically supported drop under a post-hoc primary scorer choice.

Conclusion: The assumption that forced CoT improves video QA reliability is not supported for Qwen2.5-VL on Video-MME; findings are limited to this instantiation and will be released with reproducible code and data.

Abstract: Forced chain-of-thought (CoT) is widely assumed to make vision-language models more reliable on video question answering. We propose a small three-probe evaluation recipe to test that assumption: paired accuracy across direct, CoT, answer-first, and no-video conditions; a counterfactual video-swap diagnostic over the CoT chains; and a four-rung visual-degradation ladder. Each probe is reported under both a strict and a permissive regex scorer, with multiplicity correction over a manuscript-declared primary family. Applied to Qwen2.5-VL on Video-MME subsets, the recipe returns a two-part finding. The CoT chains are strongly video-conditioned: swapping the input video collapses chain overlap and flips most final letters, the opposite of what a "boilerplate-chain" null would predict. Yet on the same data, forced CoT does not improve MCQ accuracy, and on the smaller 7B model it produces a small but statistically supported drop under a post-hoc primary scorer choice. We do not claim this generalizes beyond the Qwen2.5-VL / Video-MME instantiation; the raw responses and a single recomputation script will be released with the supplementary material so every number can be re-derived.

</details>


### [203] [VideoLatent: Video-Language Learning via Latent Self-Forcing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22870)
*Zi-Yuan Hu,Zicong Tang,Shijia Huang,Yanyang Li,Michael R. Lyu,Liwei Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VideoLatent introduces a latent injection module and self-forcing training paradigm for efficient video understanding and reasoning in MLLMs, achieving superior performance on 14 benchmarks while reducing training/inference overhead by ~6x/~68x compared to Video-R1, using only standard video-question-answer triplets without additional supervision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing CoT-based MLLMs require labor-intensive CoT annotations and high training/inference overhead; visual latent reasoning methods focus on image tasks and rely on additional supervision signals (CoT traces, auxiliary images, fine-grained annotations), limiting scalability and transferability to video tasks.

Method: VideoLatent introduces a latent injection module tailored for video understanding and reasoning, trained with a latent self-forcing paradigm comprising latent alignment and latent diversity objectives, using only standard video-question-answer triplets without additional supervision signals.

Result: Outperforms existing standard and latent MLLMs across 14 benchmarks on general video understanding and complex video reasoning; achieves ~6x training and ~68x inference efficiency gains over Video-R1; demonstrates strong generalizability across different MLLM backbones and model scales.

Conclusion: VideoLatent effectively bridges the gap between efficient latent reasoning and video understanding, offering a scalable and transferable alternative to CoT-based MLLMs without requiring costly annotations or auxiliary supervision.

Abstract: Recent advancements in chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning have shown promise in enhancing video understanding and reasoning capabilities of multimodal large language models (MLLMs). However, existing CoT-based MLLMs require labor-intensive CoT annotations and incur substantial training and inference overhead. While visual latent reasoning has emerged as a more efficient alternative, existing methods primarily focus on image tasks and heavily rely on additional supervision signals for visual latent generation (e.g., CoT traces, auxiliary images, or fine-grained annotations), limiting their scalability and transferability to video tasks. To bridge this gap, we introduce VideoLatent, a novel MLLM equipped with a latent injection module tailored for video understanding and reasoning. Specifically, VideoLatent learns to perform visual latent reasoning using a new latent self-forcing training paradigm, which comprises latent alignment and latent diversity objectives, and relies solely on standard video-question-answer triplets. Extensive experiments across 14 benchmarks demonstrate that our model consistently outperforms existing standard and latent MLLMs on general video understanding and complex video reasoning. Compared with Video-R1, our VideoLatent achieves superior computational efficiency, reducing training/inference overhead by $\sim$6$\times$/$\sim$68$\times$.
