Moreover, experiments demonstrate that our method has strong generalizability to different MLLM backbones and different model scales.

</details>


### [204] [Fursee: Hybrid YOLO-DINOv3 Framework for Fursuit Identity Retrieval and Clustering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22872)
*Jundi Wu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces Fursee, a three-stage hybrid pipeline (YOLO detection + ArcFace-optimized DINOv3 embeddings + DBSCAN with silhouette-coefficient search) for fursuit identity retrieval and clustering, along with a new benchmark dataset, outperforming GPT5.5, Claude Opus 4.8, and Qwen3.7-Plus.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Global furry conventions produce massive fursuit photographs, and manual sorting is labor-intensive, yet no dedicated multimodal model or public benchmark exists for automated fursuit identity retrieval and clustering.

Method: A three-stage pipeline: (1) YOLO detects and crops high-resolution fursuit head patches for improved small/overlapping target localization; (2) ArcFace optimizes DINOv3 embeddings to enlarge angular separation between identities on the feature hypersphere; (3) DBSCAN performs unsupervised clustering with silhouette-coefficient-driven search for automatic hyperparameter selection.

Result: The pipeline outperforms GPT5.5, Claude Opus 4.8, and Qwen3.7-Plus on all evaluation metrics for both retrieval and clustering tasks, achieving competitive performance on fursuit head retrieval and grouping.

Conclusion: The proposed specialized dataset and pipeline effectively address the fursuit identity retrieval and clustering problem, surpassing general multimodal models on this domain-specific task.

Abstract: Global furry conventions produce massive fursuit photographs, while manual sorting brings heavy labor costs and calls for automatic identity retrieval and clustering solutions. General multimodal models lack dedicated optimization for complex fursuit scenes, and no public benchmark dataset exists for this task. To fill this gap, we build a specialized fursuit image dataset and present a three-stage hybrid pipeline Fursee for fursuit identity retrieval and clustering. First, YOLO detects and crops high-resolution fursuit head patches to improve localization of small and overlapping targets. Second, ArcFace optimizes DINOv3 embeddings to enlarge angular separation between different identities on the feature hypersphere. Third, DBSCAN performs unsupervised clustering, with silhouette-coefficient-driven search automatically selecting optimal hyperparameters rather than fixed manual radius. Retrieval and clustering experiments verify that our pipeline outperforms mainstream multimodal models including GPT5.5, Claude Opus 4.8 and Qwen3.7-Plus on all evaluation metrics, achieving competitive performance for fursuit head retrieval and grouping.

</details>


### [205] [SingGuard: A Policy-Adaptive Multimodal LLM Guardrail with Dynamic Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22873)
*SingGuard Team*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SingGuard is a policy-adaptive multimodal guardrail model family for safety assessment in conversations, supporting dynamic runtime policy rules with fast-to-slow reasoning regimes and achieving SOTA results across 35 datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLMs are increasingly deployed across diverse domains (consumer, medical, financial, enterprise), broadening the safety surface with risks from multimodal QA, assistant responses, and cross-modal composition. Existing guardrails rely on fixed taxonomies or narrow interaction settings, lacking adaptability when safety rules change at deployment time.

Method: SingGuard treats active policy as a runtime input, checking target content rule-by-rule with natural-language rules. It supports three inference regimes (fast, hybrid, slow) along a reasoning spectrum, optimized via fast-slow decoupled reinforcement learning. SingGuard-Bench is also introduced with 56,340 examples across 80+ risk types, including cross-modal joint-risk cases.

Result: Across six benchmark families (35 datasets), SingGuard achieves state-of-the-art average F1 in every family. Dynamic-rule evaluation shows policy-following accuracy improvement from 0.6465 to 0.7415 under runtime policy shifts.

Conclusion: SingGuard enables adaptive, interpretable, and efficient multimodal safety assessment that can flexibly accommodate policy changes at deployment time, addressing a key limitation of existing guardrails.

Abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) are increasingly deployed in consumer, medical, financial, and enterprise applications. This broad deployment expands the safety surface: risks can arise from multimodal question answering, assistant responses, and cross-modal composition, while moderation policies may vary across products, regions, and deployment stages. Most existing guardrails either rely on fixed taxonomies or target only a narrow set of interaction settings, which limits their adaptability when safety rules change at deployment time. We present \textbf{SingGuard}, a policy-adaptive multimodal guardrail model family for safety assessment in multimodal conversations. SingGuard treats the active policy as a runtime input: given natural-language rules, it checks the target content against the active policy rule by rule and predicts both the safety label and the triggered rule. To balance efficiency and interpretability, SingGuard supports fast, hybrid, and slow inference regimes along a fast-to-slow reasoning spectrum, ranging from direct safety judgments to policy-grounded deliberation. We further optimize this behavior with fast--slow decoupled reinforcement learning. We also introduce \textbf{SingGuard-Bench}, a multimodal guardrail benchmark with 56{,}340 examples spanning 80+ fine-grained risk types across multimodal QA, adversarial attack, and dynamic-rule evaluation settings, including cross-modal joint-risk cases where each modality is harmless in isolation but their composition implies unsafe intent. Across six benchmark families (35 datasets), SingGuard achieves state-of-the-art average F1 in every family. Dynamic-rule evaluation further shows improved policy-following accuracy from 0.6465 to 0.7415 under runtime policy shifts. Our code is available at https://github.com/inclusionAI/Sing-Guard.

</details>


### [206] [FedOT: Ownership Verification and Leakage Tracing via Watermarks for Federated LDMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22875)
*Wenlong Cheng,Yuan Gan,Yunqiu Xu,Jiaxu Miao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FedOT is the first framework for ownership verification and leakage tracing in federated Latent Diffusion Models, using a chunked watermark and Latent Vector Transformation (LVT) to defend against VAE replacement attacks while enabling both ownership verification and malicious client identification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VAE-based watermarking techniques for LDMs in FL fail to address threats from malicious clients because they (1) support ownership verification but cannot trace model leakage to a specific client, and (2) are vulnerable to VAE replacement attacks that can easily remove watermarks.

Method: FedOT proposes two key components: (1) a chunked watermark with two parts—one for ownership verification and another for client identification (traceability); (2) Latent Vector Transformation (LVT) that modifies the original latent distribution of the VAE to strengthen the connection between VAE and U-Net latent spaces, making VAE replacement cause significant image quality degradation.

Result: Extensive experiments demonstrate that FedOT achieves superior performance in both ownership verification and traceability, effectively securing federated LDMs against model leakage and unauthorized distribution.

Conclusion: FedOT successfully addresses the dual challenges of ownership verification and leakage tracing in federated LDMs, with the LVT mechanism providing robust protection against VAE replacement attacks.

Abstract: Training Latent Diffusion Models (LDMs) within Federated Learning (FL) has attracted increasing attention due to its ability to combine the powerful generative capacity of LDMs with the privacy-preserving properties of FL. However, FL requires sharing the global model with multiple participants, which risks unauthorized model distribution or resale by malicious clients. While an intuitive approach is to adopt existing VAE-based watermarking techniques for LDMs in FL, this strategy falls short in addressing such threats due to two fundamental challenges: (1) Existing methods support ownership verification but lack the ability to trace model leakage to a specific malicious client; (2) VAE-based watermarks are vulnerable, as they can be removed simply by replacing the decoder with a clean counterpart. In this paper, we propose FedOT, the first framework for ownership verification and leakage tracing in federated LDMs. Specifically, to address the first challenge, we design a chunked watermark, where the first part is for ownership verification, and the second part is used for client identification. Furthermore, to overcome the second challenge and secure the model against VAE replacement attack, we introduce Latent Vector Transformation (LVT), which strengthens the connection between the VAE and U-Net latent spaces by modifying the original latent distribution of the VAE. Consequently, any attempt to replace the VAE for watermark removal leads to significant image quality degradation, making the LDM model unusable. Extensive experiments demonstrate that FedOT achieves superior performance in both ownership verification and traceability. Project page: https://spyzixuan.github.io/FedOT/.

</details>


### [207] [Full-Body Golf Swing Kinematic Reconstruction From a Smartwatch IMU](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22876)
*Yuanshuo Tan,Kezhe Zhu,Xiujie Sun,Chunping Liang,Shuoyang Zhu,Chenquan Xu,Licheng Zhong,Huiming Pan,Yinri Jin,Chang Liu,Bo Xiao,Shenglong Le,Bryndan W. Lindsey,Peter B. Shull*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes WIT-KinNet, a single wrist-worn IMU approach using deep learning to estimate full-body joint angles during golf swings, achieving 8.11° mean error and strong correlations for key kinematic parameters (pelvic/torso rotation, X-factor, S-factor).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing golf swing measurement methods (optical motion capture, camera-based, multi-sensor IMU systems) are impractical for on-course use due to lab constraints, camera placement requirements, or complex multi-segment setup/calibration. A practical, wearable solution is needed.

Method: WIT-KinNet uses modality-specific IMU embeddings and temporal kinematic encoding to learn wrist-to-body motion dependencies. Data from 36 golfers (beginner to skilled) performing swings of three amplitudes (full/half/quarter) with seven club types was collected using a smartwatch IMU and validated against optical motion capture.

Result: Mean absolute error of 8.11±1.84° across full-body joint angles. High temporal correlation for pelvic rotation (r=0.98) and upper torso rotation (r=0.97). X-factor and S-factor both r=0.96. Swing amplitude, skill level, and club type significantly affected error (p<0.05).

Conclusion: Establishes the first single wrist-worn IMU approach for full-body golf swing kinematics estimation, enabling practical, unobtrusive swing analysis during real gameplay.

Abstract: Quantitative measurement of the golf swing is critical for evaluating technique and enabling individualized feedback. However, existing methods are impractical to use on the golf course: optical motion capture is laboratory-bound, camera-based methods require impractical camera placement, and multi-sensor inertial measurement unit (IMU) systems require multi-segment setup and calibration. We thus propose a single wrist-worn IMU approach for estimating full-body joint angles during golf swings. The proposed Wrist-IMU Temporal Kinematic Network (WIT-KinNet) leverages modality-specific IMU embeddings and temporal kinematic encoding to learn wrist-to-body motion dependencies and estimate full-body joint angles during golf swings. Thirty-six golfers spanning beginner and skilled players, performed full, half, and quarter swings using seven club types: driver, 3-wood, 5-hybrid, 5-iron, 7-iron, 9-iron, and sand wedge. The proposed WIT-KinNet was evaluated under subject-wise cross-validation using synchronized smartwatch IMU data and ground-truth kinematics derived from an optical motion capture system. The proposed approach achieved a mean absolute error of 8.11 $\pm$ 1.84$^\circ$ across full-body joint angles. High temporal correlation was observed for pelvic rotation and upper torso rotation (r = 0.98 and 0.97, respectively), with X-factor and S-factor also showing strong correlation (r = 0.96 and 0.96). Linear mixed-effects models of the error revealed that swing amplitude, skill level, and club type all significantly affected measurement differences (p $<$ 0.05). The results establish the first single wrist-worn IMU approach for estimating full-body golf swing kinematics, enabling practical swing analysis during real gameplay.

</details>


### [208] [PHOEBI: An Open-World Benchmark for Bacterial Identification in Phase-Contrast Microscopy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22890)
*Aaditya Baranwal,Md Jahid Hasan,Shruti Vyas*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PHOEBI is a large benchmark dataset for multi-label species identification from phase-contrast microscopy of polymicrobial mixtures. It reveals that standard per-image aggregators fail on unseen species combinations (open-world recognition), while proposed anchor-based decoders generalize better by geometrically modeling species presence over frozen features.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Field samples in microbiology are routinely polymicrobial and contain species never seen during training. No existing computer-vision benchmark tests multi-label species identification from phase-contrast microscopy under such open-world conditions, motivating the need for a dedicated dataset and evaluation protocol.

Method: The authors built a wet-lab dataset of 120,000 PCM images across 40 combinations of six rod-shaped bacteria. They introduced a leave-combinations-out (LCO) evaluation protocol that holds out entire species combinations, simulating open-world generalization. They tested gradient-trained per-image aggregators and proposed three lightweight anchor-based decoders that capture per-species presence geometrically over a shared frozen tile-feature pool.

Result: Gradient-trained per-image aggregators dropped 0.39–0.57 F1 from in-distribution to held-out splits (open-world failure). A linear probe across 13 encoders showed only ~6 percentage points F1 variation, confirming representation quality is not the issue. The proposed anchor-based decoders scored higher on held-out combinations than on in-distribution validation.

Conclusion: The open-world recognition failure lies in the aggregator, not the visual representation. Anchor-based decoders that geometrically model species presence over frozen feature pools can generalize to unseen species combinations, outperforming standard aggregators on held-out data.

Abstract: Optical microscopy enables rapid, label-free imaging of live bacteria and is the standard instrument for species identification across clinical, environmental, and industrial microbiology. Yet field samples are routinely polymicrobial and may contain organisms that were never seen during system training, and no computer-vision benchmark tests multi-label species identification from phase-contrast microscopy (PCM) of such mixtures. We introduce Phase-contrast Optical bEnchmark for Bacterial Identification ($\textbf{PHOEBI}$), a wet-lab-prepared dataset of $120{,}000$ PCM images covering $40$ combinations of six rod-shaped species, paired with a leave-combinations-out (LCO) evaluation protocol that holds out entire species combinations to mirror the practical scenario of a model trained on catalogued mixtures that must generalise to unseen ones. On LCO, every gradient-trained per-image aggregator we test drops $0.39$ to $0.57$ F1 from the in-distribution to the held-out split, a systematic open-world recognition failure in the aggregator, not the visual representation. A linear probe of thirteen different encoders over the same features spreads only about six percentage points of F1 across general-purpose and biomedical pretraining objectives, confirming the representation is sound. We propose three lightweight $\textit{anchor-based}$ decoders that capture per-species presence geometrically over a shared frozen tile-feature pool, scoring $\textit{higher}$ on held-out combinations than on in-distribution validation.

</details>


### [209] [InteractiveAvatar: Real-Time Streaming Video Generation for Consistent and Intent-Aware Avatars](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22905)
*Quanyue Song,Yishan He,Yanfei Zhang,Shihao Cheng,Zhixiang He,Zhizhi Guo,Chi Zhang,Xuelong Li,Caigui Jiang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: InteractiveAvatar is a real-time infinite-streaming video generation framework that achieves visually consistent avatar video generation and intent-aware interactions using autoregressive distillation, Long-Short Visual Memory (LSVM) for visual consistency, and a Reasoning-Reaction Module (RRM) with State-Cycling and Cache-Switching mechanisms for user intent alignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing diffusion-based audio-driven avatar generation methods struggle with visual temporal consistency and cannot explicitly perceive user intent in complex interactive streaming scenarios.

Method: The framework uses (1) autoregressive distillation for real-time infinite-streaming generation, (2) Long-Short Visual Memory (LSVM) mechanism to compress historical visual information into compact tokens for both short-range coherence and long-term consistency, and (3) Reasoning-Reaction Module (RRM) with State-Cycling strategy and Cache-Switching mechanism for intent-aware interaction.

Result: State-of-the-art visual consistency in long-duration generation, enabling complex user-avatar interaction in real time across diverse scenarios.

Conclusion: InteractiveAvatar successfully addresses visual temporal consistency and intent perception challenges, achieving real-time infinite-streaming avatar video generation with both visual coherence and interactive responsiveness.

Abstract: Recent diffusion-based models have enabled realistic audio-driven avatar generation in real-time streaming. However, existing approaches struggle to maintain visual temporal consistency and fail to explicitly perceive user intent in complex interactive streaming scenarios. To address these challenges, we propose InteractiveAvatar, a real-time infinite-streaming video generation framework that supports visually consistent avatar video generation and intent-aware interactions. With autoregressive distillation, InteractiveAvatar achieves real-time str-eaming generation of human avatars over arbitrarily long durations. For visual consistency, we introduce a Long-Short Visual Memory (LSVM) mechanism that flexibly compresses historical visual information into compact tokens, preserving both short-range coherence and long-term consistency. To generate avatars with speeches and actions aligned with user intent, we propose a Reasoning-Reaction Module (RRM), which incorporates a State-Cycling strategy and a Cache-Switching mechanism. Extensive experimental results over diverse scenarios demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art visual consistency in long-duration generation, while enabling complex user-avatar interaction in real time.

</details>


### [210] [Intend, Reflect, Refine: An Adaptive Multimodal Reflection Framework for Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22913)
*Zisheng Chen,Yuping Qiu,Jianhua Han,Tao Tang,Xiuwei Chen,Likui Zhang,Ying-Cong Chen,Hang Xu,Xiaodan Liang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: IRR-Drive proposes an adaptive multimodal reflection framework for autonomous driving that generates preliminary textual intentions, predicts future semantic BEV representations for self-correction, and uses an adaptive reflection reward to balance performance and efficiency, achieving state-of-the-art on NAVSIM benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLA models for autonomous driving directly generate final trajectories without explicitly examining future consequences, limiting their reliability in complex and dynamic environments.

Method: IRR-Drive first generates a preliminary textual intention and predicts future semantic BEV representations (dual-modality reflection space: Text + BEV), then uses this to self-correct and refine intent before final trajectory generation. It also constructs reflection-oriented training data and designs an adaptive reflection reward to balance planning performance and computational efficiency based on scene complexity.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on the NAVSIM benchmark in both PDMS and EPDMS metrics.

Conclusion: The multimodal reflection framework and adaptive reflection strategy effectively improve planning reliability and efficiency in autonomous driving.

Abstract: Recent Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have advanced end-to-end autonomous driving by incorporating reasoning for better interpretability and planning quality. However, most existing approaches directly generate the final trajectory without explicitly examining its future consequences, which limits their reliability in complex and dynamic environments. To address this limitation, we propose IRR-Drive (Intend, Reflect, Refine), an adaptive multimodal reflection framework for autonomous driving. Specifically, to tightly couple high-level reasoning with physical constraints, IRR-Drive first generates a preliminary textual intention and anticipates potential interactions by predicting future semantic bird's-eye view (BEV) representations. This dual-modality (Text + BEV) reflection space explicitly models anticipated scene evolution, enabling the model to rigorously self-correct and refine its initial intent before generating the final trajectory. Furthermore, to balance planning performance and computational efficiency, we construct reflection-oriented training data and design an adaptive reflection reward, enabling the model to adaptively select its reasoning mode according to scene complexity. Instead of using reasoning primarily as an auxiliary interpretation, IRR-Drive directly integrates an adaptive reflection mechanism into the planning framework, enabling grounded, decision-aware trajectory correction that is driven by scene complexity. Our method achieves state-of-the-art performance on the NAVSIM benchmark in both PDMS and EPDMS. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of our multimodal reflection framework and validate the efficacy of the proposed adaptive reflection strategy.

</details>


### [211] [Each Judge Its Own Yardstick: Discovering Per-VLM Taxonomies for Physical Video Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22918)
*Yu Cao,Ziquan Liu,Zhensong Zhang,Jiankang Deng,Shaogang Gong,Jifei Song*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: JudgeFit is an iterative refinement procedure that discovers per-VLM evaluation taxonomies for assessing physical consistency in video generators. It constructs initial taxonomies via VLM self-prompting, then iteratively calibrates and repairs them using human ratings. Applied to 16 VLMs, it achieves ~32% mean improvement over global-schema baselines and reveals model-specific blind spots.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing approaches use a single global evaluation schema for all VLMs when judging physical consistency, ignoring that VLMs have distinct training data, architectures, and internal representations. This leads to unreliable and non-individualized evaluation.

Method: JudgeFit has an iterative refinement procedure: (1) initial taxonomy constructed by prompting the target VLM to enumerate physics errors on a small video set and clustering descriptions; (2) diagnostic step calibrating per-dimension scores to human ratings, identifying unreliable/redundant dimensions; (3) LLM prompted to repair the taxonomy; (4) iterate until convergence.

Result: Applied to 16 VLMs across 8 model families. The refined taxonomy outperforms global-schema baselines on held-out videos for every VLM, with ~32% mean relative improvement. Per-VLM profiles expose model-specific blind spots and reliability patterns that overall rankings miss.

Conclusion: VLMs require individualized evaluation taxonomies for reliably assessing physical consistency. JudgeFit's iterative discovery of per-VLM taxonomies significantly improves evaluation accuracy and reveals model-specific weaknesses that global rankings cannot capture.

Abstract: Maintaining physical consistency in video generators and world models increasingly relies on vision-language models (VLMs) as automated judges that provide reward signals, ranking decisions, and data-filtering criteria. Yet VLMs differ substantially in training data and architecture, encoding physical phenomena through distinct internal representations. A single global evaluation schema therefore gives every VLM the same axes of competence, regardless of what each can actually perceive. We propose JudgeFit, an iterative refinement procedure that discovers a per-VLM evaluation taxonomy. An initial taxonomy is constructed by prompting the target VLM to enumerate physics errors on a small set of videos and clustering the resulting descriptions. The taxonomy is then refined through a diagnostic step: we calibrate the VLM's per-dimension scores to human physical-commonsense ratings, diagnose which dimensions it scores unreliably or redundantly, and prompt an LLM to repair them, iterating until convergence. We further instantiate this procedure as a benchmark and apply it to 16 VLMs spanning eight model families. The refined taxonomy outperforms the global-schema baseline on held-out videos for every VLM tested, with a mean relative improvement of approximately 32%. Beyond aggregate accuracy, the per-VLM profiles expose model-specific blind spots that overall rankings cannot anticipate, with reliability patterns differing markedly across model families.

</details>


### [212] [MythraGen: Two-Stage Retrieval Augmented Art Generation Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22924)
*Quang-Khai Le,Cong-Long Nguyen,Minh-Triet Tran,Trung-Nghia Le*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MythraGen is a retrieval-augmented generation framework that combines art retrieval from an external database with LoRA-based fine-tuning of Stable Diffusion to generate artistically styled images that closely match textual prompts, outperforming existing methods on the WikiArt dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Text-to-image generation still struggles with producing high-quality, contextually accurate images that faithfully match textual descriptions, particularly in the domain of artistic generation.

Method: The framework extracts features from a large-scale art dataset, retrieves images with highest similarity to the query prompt from an external art database, and uses these retrieved images to fine-tune Stable Diffusion via LoRA for desired art generation.

Result: Experimental results and user studies on the WikiArt dataset demonstrate that MythraGen generates artworks closely matching user input, significantly outperforming existing solutions.

Conclusion: The retrieval-augmented approach with LoRA fine-tuning effectively bridges text-to-image generation with artistic style transfer, producing contextually accurate and stylistically faithful outputs.

Abstract: Text-to-image generation has seen rapid advancements, especially with the development of generative models. However, challenges remain in achieving high-quality, contextually accurate image outputs that faithfully match the provided textual descriptions, especially in artistic generation. In this paper, we present a simple yet efficient retrieval augmented generation framework, namely MythraGen, for text-to-artistic image generation by integrating an art retrieval mechanism with LoRA-based model fine-tuning. Our method extracts features from a large-scale art dataset, optimizing the generation process by combining artist-specific styles and content. Particularly, retrieved images from an external art database that have the highest similarity to the query prompt are used to finetune Stable Diffusion using LoRA for desired art generation. Experimental results and user studies on the WikiArt dataset show that our proposed method can generate artworks that closely match the user's input, significantly outperforming existing solutions.

</details>


### [213] [BEV-Denoise: Learning Intrinsic Noise for Accurate Bird's-Eye-View Semantic Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22931)
*Dooseop Choi,Kyounghwan An,Kyoung-Wook Min*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: BEV-Denoise uses a UNet-based noise estimation module (inspired by DDPM) to estimate and remove intrinsic noise from BEV features, improving BEV semantic segmentation. It employs a Task Decomposition learning paradigm with a pre-trained BEV map autoencoder. The framework works across four existing models and three VT paradigms, validated on nuScenes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Learned BEV features contain intrinsic noise that degrades semantic segmentation accuracy; existing methods lack explicit mechanisms to estimate and remove this noise.

Method: (1) A UNet-based noise estimation module inspired by DDPM to estimate noise from BEV features; (2) subtracting estimated noise from BEV features before decoding; (3) Task Decomposition (TD) sequential learning paradigm using a pre-trained BEV map autoencoder to supervise the noise estimation module.

Result: Applied to four existing models covering three major VT paradigms, achieving improved BEV semantic segmentation on the nuScenes dataset.

Conclusion: Explicit noise estimation and removal from BEV features is effective for improving segmentation; the TD paradigm enables effective supervision for noise estimation.

Abstract: In this paper, we present a framework dubbed \textbf{BEV-Denoise} that estimates and removes intrinsic noise from learned Bird's-Eye-View (BEV) features to achieve accurate BEV semantic segmentation. Inspired by the noise estimation capability of Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPM), we design a UNet-based noise estimation module that learns to estimate the noise from the learned BEV features. The estimated noise is then subtracted from the BEV features and fed to BEV map decoders for the final prediction results. To facilitate supervision for the noise estimation module, we follow a sequential learning paradigm called Task Decomposition (TD) where a pre-trained BEV map autoencoder is employed to train a view transformation (VT) encoder. We share three key insights learned from our intensive experiments that are critical for improved performance. We apply our framework to four existing models, encompassing the three major VT paradigms. Experimental results on a large-scale real-world dataset, nuScenes, demonstrate the effectiveness of our framework.

</details>


### [214] [Hybrid Compression: Integrating Pruning and Quantization for Optimized Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22935)
*Minh-Loi Nguyen,Long-Bao Nguyen,Van-Hieu Huynh,Minh-Triet Tran,Trung-Nghia Le*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes a two-phase model compression method combining pruning/quantization with Mixture of Experts (MoE) to reduce CNN model size while maintaining performance with minimal accuracy loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep neural networks face deployment challenges on embedded and edge devices due to limited memory and computational resources, necessitating effective model compression that balances model size and performance.

Method: A two-phase approach: (1) apply compression techniques (pruning and quantization) to reduce model size; (2) use Mixture of Experts (MoE) to route compressed models for enhanced performance while maintaining inference efficiency.

Result: Achieves substantial reductions in FLOPs and parameters on CNN benchmark datasets with negligible accuracy drop.

Conclusion: The proposed two-phase compression method effectively reduces CNN model size for edge deployment while preserving accuracy, demonstrating the viability of combining compression with MoE routing.

Abstract: Deep neural networks have witnessed remarkable advancements in recent years and have become integral to various applications. However, alongside these developments, training and deployment of neural network models on embedding and edge devices face significant challenges due to limited memory and computational resources. These problems can be addressed with deep neural network compression, which involves a trade-off between model size and performance. In this paper, we propose a novel method for model compression through two phases. First, we utilize model compression techniques, such as pruning and quantization, to significantly reduce the model size. Then, we use Mixture of Experts to route the previously compressed models to enhance performance while maintaining a balance in inference efficiency. MoEs consist of multiple expert models (i.e., compressed models) that are moderately sized and deliver stable performance. Experimental results on several benchmark datasets show that our method successfully compresses CNN models which achieves substantial reductions in FLOPs and parameters with a negligible accuracy drop.

</details>


### [215] [Evaluating self-supervised echocardiographic representations across downstream extraction strategies for left-ventricular segmentation and ejection fraction estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22943)
*Sylwia Majchrowska,Philip Teare*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper systematically evaluates self-supervised representations for echocardiography (segmentation and EF estimation) and shows that conclusions about representation quality depend heavily on the downstream extraction strategy used. Both DINOv3 and BYOS representations, when evaluated with a frozen lightweight decoder, approach supervised U-Net performance, while simpler heuristic extraction substantially underestimates their quality. The authors advocate for multi-strategy evaluation in dense medical image analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Self-supervised learning in medical imaging is typically judged using a single downstream evaluation setting, which for dense clinical tasks can confound representation quality with the capacity of the downstream model used to recover task-relevant information. This work seeks to decouple these factors and provide a more rigorous evaluation methodology.

Method: The authors compare a hierarchy of downstream extraction strategies with increasing expressivity: heuristic extraction (no mask-supervised training), frozen linear probes, frozen lightweight decoder probes, and partial fine-tuning. This framework is applied to two SSL representation families: DINOv3 (generic frozen self-distillation features) and BYOS (task-adapted dense self-supervised representation) on EchoNet-Dynamic for left-ventricular segmentation and EF estimation.

Result: Heuristic extraction substantially understated what was recoverable from frozen representations. DINOv3 improved from Dice 0.684/EF MAE 13.01 (heuristic) to Dice 0.906/EF MAE 9.65 (frozen lightweight decoder), approaching supervised U-Net (Dice 0.915, EF MAE 9.72). BYOS improved from Dice 0.687/EF MAE 17.83 to Dice 0.902/EF MAE 8.74 with the same evaluation strategy.

Conclusion: Conclusions about self-supervised representation quality in dense echocardiographic analysis depend strongly on the downstream extraction strategy. Multi-strategy evaluation is an important methodological consideration for SSL in dense medical image analysis.

Abstract: Self-supervised learning (SSL) is increasingly used in medical imaging to reduce annotation requirements, but representation quality is often judged using a single downstream evaluation setting. For dense clinical tasks, this can confound representation quality with the capacity of the downstream model used to recover task-relevant information. We present a systematic evaluation of self-supervised representations for left-ventricular segmentation and ejection fraction (EF) estimation from apical four-chamber echocardiography on EchoNet-Dynamic. Rather than relying on a single downstream probe, we compare a hierarchy of extraction strategies with increasing expressivity: heuristic extraction without mask-supervised training, frozen linear probes, frozen lightweight decoder probes, and partial fine-tuning. We apply this framework to two complementary representation families: generic frozen self-DIstillation with NO labels (DINOv3) features and a task-adapted dense self-supervised representation, Bootstrap Your Own Segmentation (BYOS). In both families, heuristic extraction substantially understated what was recoverable from the frozen representation. For DINOv3, performance improved from Dice 0.684 and EF mean absolute error (MAE) 13.01 under heuristic extraction to Dice 0.906 and EF MAE 9.65 with a frozen lightweight decoder, approaching a supervised U-Net baseline (Dice 0.915, EF MAE 9.72). For BYOS, performance improved from Dice 0.687 and EF MAE 17.83 under heuristic extraction to Dice 0.902 and EF MAE 8.74 with a frozen lightweight decoder. These results show that conclusions about self-supervised representation quality in dense echocardiographic analysis depend strongly on the downstream extraction strategy used for evaluation. We therefore argue that multi-strategy evaluation is an important methodological consideration for SSL in dense medical image analysis.

</details>


### [216] [Evo-RAD: Navigating Rare Retinal Disease Diagnosis via Self-Evolving Agentic Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22955)
*Wangding Xia,Ye Du,Jiashi Lin,Meng Wang,Danli Shi,Shujun Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Evo-RAD is a self-evolving agentic framework that reframes retrieval-augmented diagnosis as a Markov Decision Process, using a graph-based agent to dynamically curate evidence sets for rare disease diagnosis, achieving +21.04% improvement over retinal foundation models and +3.56% over retrieval/PEFT methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large-scale foundation models perform well on common diseases but struggle with rare diseases due to underrepresentation in clinical data. Static retrieval-augmented diagnosis suffers from the hubness problem, retrieving visually similar but semantically incorrect common diseases instead of rare ones.

Method: Formulates retrieval as a Markov Decision Process (MDP) with a graph-based agent that observes the reference set state and takes actions: DELETE (removing discordant evidence), INSERT (adding pathologically consistent samples), or TERMINATE (concluding evolution). Optimized via Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with a homogeneity-aware reward to maximize diagnostic homogeneity of the support set.

Result: Outperforms retinal foundation models by +21.04%, and surpasses retrieval-based and parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods by +3.56% on retinal disease benchmarks.

Conclusion: Evo-RAD demonstrates that dynamic, self-evolving evidence acquisition can effectively mitigate the hubness problem and significantly improve rare disease diagnosis over both foundation models and existing retrieval/PEFT approaches.

Abstract: Large-scale pretrained foundation models have revolutionized general medical screening, but often falter on rare diseases because such conditions are underrepresented in real-world clinical datasets. While retrieval-augmented diagnosis attempts to mitigate this, conventional static methods frequently succumb to the hubness problem, retrieving visually similar but semantically incorrect common diseases. To address this, we propose Evo-RAD, a self-evolving agentic framework that transforms evidence acquisition into a dynamic decision-making task. We formulate retrieval as a Markov Decision Process (MDP) where a graphbased agent observes the reference set state and executes actions to purge discordant evidence (DELETE), acquire pathologically consistent samples (INSERT), or conclude the evolution (TERMINATE). Optimized via Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with a homogeneityaware reward, the agent learns to maximize the diagnostic homogeneity of the support reference set. Experiments on retinal disease benchmarks show that Evo-RAD substantially improves rare-disease diagnosis, outperforming retinal foundation models by +21.04%, while also surpassing retrieval-based and parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods by +3.56%. Code is available at https://github.com/SDH-Lab/Evo-RAD.

</details>


### [217] [Concept Alignment Contrast and Long-Short Prompt Memory for Test-Time Adaptation of SAM3 in Medical Image Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22963)
*Yubo Zhou,Jianghao Wu,Ping Ye,Shaoting Zhang,Guotai Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CM-TTA proposes Concept Alignment Contrast (CAC) metric and Long-Short Prompt Memory (LSPM) module to adapt SAM3 for medical image segmentation via test-time adaptation, outperforming existing methods on prostate and skin lesion segmentation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: SAM3 generalizes well on natural images but degrades on medical images due to domain gap. Existing vision-language TTA methods rely on image-level uncertainty minimization that fails to capture region-level semantic correctness, and lack stability mechanisms for continual one-pass adaptation.

Method: (1) Concept Alignment Contrast (CAC): uses textual-visual semantic consistency to evaluate prediction quality across multiple augmentations and selects the best augmented view as supervision. (2) Long-Short Prompt Memory (LSPM): short memory fuses recent prompts based on CAC scores for agile local adaptation, long memory maintains a stable global prompt for enhanced pseudo-labels. (3) Densely Supervised Prompt Update (DSPU): optimizes prompt embeddings with enhanced pseudo-labels as dense supervision.

Result: CM-TTA significantly outperforms existing TTA methods for SAM3 on prostate and skin lesion segmentation benchmarks.

Conclusion: The proposed framework effectively bridges the domain gap for SAM3 in medical imaging by combining semantic-aware contrastive selection with dual-memory prompt management for stable yet adaptive test-time optimization.

Abstract: Concept segmentation models like Segment Anything Model 3 (SAM3) show strong generalization on natural images, yet their performance degrades in medical imaging due to the domain gap caused by different imaging principles and styles. Test-Time Adaptation (TTA) is essential for improving the testing performance by updating the model on the fly without annotations. However, existing vision-language TTA methods are mainly driven by image-level uncertainty minimization, which does not necessarily reflect region-level semantic correctness in medical segmentation. Moreover, they often lack mechanisms to maintain stability in continual one-pass adaptation, leading to limited performance when reliable dense supervision is missing for segmentation. To address these issues, we propose Concept Alignment Contrast and LongShort Prompt Memory for Test-Time Adaptation (CM-TTA) of SAM3 for medical images. First, for a test sample with multiple augmentations, we introduce a novel Concept Alignment Contrast (CAC) metric, which leverages textual-visual semantic consistency to robustly evaluate prediction quality to select the best augmented view as the supervision. Second, to balance rapid and stable adaptation, we design a Long-Short Prompt Memory (LSPM) module. The short memory dynamically fuses recent prompts based on CAC scores for agile local adaptation, while the long memory maintains a stable global prompt to generate enhanced pseudo-labels. Finally, a Densely Supervised Prompt Update (DSPU) strategy is proposed to optimize the prompt embeddings with enhanced pseudo labels as dense supervision. Extensive experiments on prostate and skin lesion segmentation demonstrate that our CM-TTA framework significantly outperforms existing methods for TTA of SAM3.

</details>


### [218] [Subject-Level Unknown-Identity Identification from Leap Motion Controller 2 Hand Landmarks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22986)
*Bahar Moharrer,Susanna Cifani,Marco Raoul Marini,Luigi Cinque,Maria De Marsico*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper evaluates subject recognition and unknown-identity rejection on LMC2 hand landmark data using geometric and angular descriptors under a LOSO protocol. Extra Trees outperforms neural approaches (cosine-similarity embedding and MLP+OpenMax), showing that score separation between known and unknown subjects is the key challenge.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore whether compact, interpretable hand landmark-based descriptors from Leap Motion Controller 2 can support contactless unknown-subject rejection and identification in a small-cohort setting.

Method: Uses original LMC2 geometric landmarks enriched with fingertip-to-palm distances and palm-normalized inter-finger angular features. Evaluated under Leave-One-Subject-Out (LOSO) protocol with inner validation for threshold selection. Compares Extra Trees, learned embedding (centroid + cosine-similarity rejection), and MLP+OpenMax.

Result: Extra Trees achieves the best overall performance; neural methods underperform, indicating the primary difficulty is robust known-vs-unknown score separation rather than enrolled-class discrimination.

Conclusion: Compact, interpretable landmark-based descriptors are feasible for contactless unknown-subject rejection and identification on small-cohort data, with tree-based ensembles proving most effective.

Abstract: This work studies subject recognition from Leap Motion Controller 2 (LMC2) hand landmark data under a subject-level unknown-identity identification protocol on the Multi View Leap2 Hand Pose (ML2HP) dataset. Using only the landmark modality, we retain the original geometric representation and enrich it with fingertip-to-palm distances and palm-normalized inter-finger angular descriptors. Evaluation is performed under a Leave-One-Subject-Out (LOSO) protocol in which, for each outer fold, one subject is excluded from the enrolled set and treated as unknown at test time. To avoid tuning on the true outer unknown subject, the unknown-rejection threshold is selected in an inner validation step by temporarily withholding one enrolled subject from the inner gallery and using it only for threshold estimation. We compare a tree ensemble baseline with two neural alternatives: a learned embedding baseline based on centroid matching and cosine-similarity-based rejection, and an MLP+OpenMax model, which represents a more established open-set recognition approach. Under this evaluation setup, Extra Trees remains the strongest overall method, indicating that the main challenge on this benchmark is not enrolled-subject discrimination alone, but robust score separation between known and unknown probes. The results support the feasibility of compact, interpretable landmark-based descriptors for contactless hand-based unknown-subject rejection and identification on a small-cohort dataset.

</details>


### [219] [Can Single-View Mesh Reconstruction Generalize to Robot Camera Rotation?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22987)
*Yu Zhan,Guangcheng Chen,Hanjing Ye,Zhiqin Cheng,Zanjia Tong,Wenjun Xu,Hong Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Single-view mesh reconstruction fails under robot camera rotation due to view-dependent priors; this paper introduces a rotation-sweep evaluation protocol and a Gravity-Aware Refinement that reduces layout-orientation error by 47.1%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robot-mounted cameras rotate during manipulation and navigation, but learned single-view reconstruction models rely on view-dependent priors and generalize poorly to out-of-distribution camera rotations, causing 3D inconsistencies, incorrect layouts, and physical constraint violations. This failure mode is under-evaluated.

Method: (1) An evaluation protocol with controlled axis-wise roll, pitch, and yaw sweeps to trace errors in MDE, canonical object meshes, camera-space layout, and physical plausibility within a SAM3D-style pipeline. (2) A two-stage SAM3D+FoundationPose pipeline and a Gravity-Aware Refinement to reduce layout-orientation error.

Result: Camera rotations induce MDE distortion, layout drift, and collision penetration; canonical mesh predictions remain stable. Two-stage SAM3D+FoundationPose is more robust than one-stage feed-forward layout prediction. Gravity-Aware Refinement reduces pairwise ICP-based layout-orientation error by 47.1%.

Conclusion: Current single-view mesh reconstruction methods generalize poorly to robot camera rotation; explicit gravity cues are important for reliable robotic single-view mesh reconstruction.

Abstract: Single-view mesh reconstruction predicts object meshes and spatial layouts from a single observation, making it attractive for fast robot spatial reasoning and real-to-sim digital twins. However, robot-mounted cameras naturally rotate during manipulation and navigation, while learned single-view reconstruction models often rely on view-dependent priors and may generalize poorly to out-of-distribution camera rotations. Such rotations can introduce 3D inconsistencies, incorrect layouts, and violations of physical constraints, but this failure mode remains under-evaluated. We introduce an evaluation protocol with controlled axis-wise roll, pitch, and yaw sweeps to trace errors in monocular depth estimation (MDE), canonical object meshes, camera-space layout, and physical plausibility within a representative SAM3D-style pipeline. On the Aria Digital Twin dataset and a real Franka wrist-camera sequence, camera rotations induce MDE distortion, layout drift, and collision penetration, while canonical mesh predictions remain relatively stable. A two-stage SAM3D+FoundationPose pipeline is more robust than one-stage feed-forward layout prediction, and our Gravity-Aware Refinement reduces one-stage pairwise ICP-based layout-orientation error by 47.1$\%$. Our evaluation reveals that current single-view mesh reconstruction methods generalize poorly to robot camera rotation, and suggests that explicit gravity cues are important for reliable robotic single-view mesh reconstruction.

</details>


### [220] [Black-Box Continual Learning for Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22999)
*Yuting Li,Weihang Fang,Haoyuan Gao,Linghe Kong,Yexin Li,Lichao Sun,Weiran Huang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Black-CL is a realistic black-box continual learning benchmark for VLMs, and BETA is a lightweight method using only textual prototypes (SPA, LDR, TTPA) that matches/exceeds white-box CL with 0.05M parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional CL relies on white-box access (weights, gradients), which is unrealistic for cloud-hosted VLMs. The paper aims to bridge the gap between CL research and real-world deployment by introducing a benchmark with weight/architecture inaccessibility, constrained computation, and task-agnostic inference.

Method: Proposes BETA with three components: (1) Semantic Projection Accumulation (SPA) for incremental knowledge acquisition, (2) Latent Distribution Replay (LDR) to anchor embedding space against forgetting, and (3) Test-Time Prototype Adaptation (TTPA) for dynamic instance-aware boundary refinement. Only output embeddings/logits are queried with no backbone gradient flow.

Result: BETA outperforms existing black-box tuners across 10 datasets and various backbones with only 0.05M trainable parameters (180-3000× reduction vs. competitors), achieving performance on par with or exceeding white-box CL methods.

Conclusion: Black-CL and BETA provide a foundational framework for advancing continual learning toward real-world systems by addressing realistic black-box constraints.

Abstract: The rapid deployment of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) in dynamic environments necessitates the ability to learn continuously without forgetting. However, traditional continual learning (CL) settings often rely on white-box paradigms, which is increasingly invalidated by the shift toward cloud-hosted models. In this paper, we introduce Black-CL, a more realistic benchmark for VLMs that enforces three primary real-world challenges: weight and architecture inaccessibility, constrained computation, and task-agnostic inference. The learner can query only output embeddings or logits, with no gradient flow through or structural modification of the backbone. Current CL methodologies, which rely on backbone backpropagation or complex parameter expansion, are fundamentally incompatible with these constraints. Under this setting, we propose BETA, a simple yet effective baseline built on the key insight that solely optimizing textual prototypes can navigate the complexities of CL. BETA integrates three core components: Semantic Projection Accumulation (SPA) for incremental knowledge acquisition, Latent Distribution Replay (LDR) for anchoring the embedding space against catastrophic forgetting, and Test-Time Prototype Adaptation (TTPA) for dynamic, instance-aware boundary refinement. Extensive experiments across ten diverse datasets and various backbones demonstrate that BETA significantly outperforms existing black-box tuners. Remarkably, with only 0.05 M trainable parameters, a 180--3000$\times$ reduction compared to competitive methods, BETA achieves performance on par with or even exceeding white-box CL methods. We believe Black-CL and BETA provide a foundational framework for future advancements in continual learning and accelerates the transition of continual learning from academia to real-world systems.

</details>


### [221] [MotionMAR: Multi-scale Auto-Regressive Human Motion Reconstruction from Sparse Observations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23000)
*Yuhua Luo,Junsheng Zhang,Mengyin Liu,Xincheng Lin,Ming Yan,Zhudi Chen,Chenglu Wen,Lan Xu,Siqi Shen,Cheng Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MotionMAR is a coarse-to-fine autoregressive framework for human motion reconstruction from sparse observations, using multi-scale tokenization, autoregressive modeling, scale-aware control, and refinement modules to achieve state-of-the-art accuracy on AMASS.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Human motion has a temporal hierarchical structure (low-frequency global trajectories to high-frequency details). Existing methods lack a structure-aware approach to reconstruct motion from sparse observations, motivating a multi-level autoregressive framework inspired by computer vision.

Method: MotionMAR has four components: (1) Temporal Multi-scale Tokenization (TMT) VQ-VAE to encode motion at multiple temporal resolutions; (2) Motion Autoregressive Network (MAN) to predict coarse-to-fine latent indices; (3) Scale-Aware Control (SAC) to align outputs with sparse tracking data; (4) Motion Refinement Network (MRN) to smooth poses and remove quantization artifacts.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on the AMASS dataset for motion reconstruction from sparse observations.

Conclusion: MotionMAR provides a reliable, structure-aware coarse-to-fine approach for human motion reconstruction, validated by SOTA results on AMASS. Code is publicly available.

Abstract: Human motion follows a temporal hierarchical structure, transitioning from low-frequency global trajectories to high-frequency details. Inspired by the success of multi-level autoregressive models in computer vision, we propose MotionMAR, a coarse-to-fine framework for motion reconstruction from sparse observations. It first estimates the global trajectory of human motion and then gradually refines the temporal details. This architecture consists of four integrated components. The Temporal Multi-scale Tokenization (TMT) VQ-VAE encodes the data at multiple temporal resolutions, separating semantic motion from minor jitters. The Motion Autoregressive Network (MAN) operates in this latent space, predicting motion across scales. It first establishes the global structure through coarse indices and then generates finer indices to recover specific details. Meanwhile, the Scale-Aware Control (SAC) module integrates sparse tracking data to ensure the generated output aligns with actual observations. The Motion Refinement Network (MRN) subsequently smooths consecutive poses and eliminates quantization artifacts. Experiments show that MotionMAR achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on the AMASS dataset, providing a reliable and structure-aware approach for motion reconstruction. The source code is publicly available at http://www.lidarhumanmotion.net/motionmar/.

</details>


### [222] [From Point Estimates to Distributions: GMM Pooling for MIL in Preterm Birth Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23005)
*Hussain Alasmawi,Numan Saeed,Soha Said,Mohammad Yaqub*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) pooling for multiple instance learning to predict preterm birth from multiple TVUS images per patient, improving PR-AUC from 0.44 to 0.56, and achieving SOTA on a lymph node benchmark (0.91 F1, 0.89 ROC-AUC, 0.18 MAE).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing ultrasound-based PTB prediction models use only a single selected TVUS frame per patient, ignoring the rich information from multiple cervical images routinely acquired during exams.

Method: Formulates PTB prediction as an MIL problem where each patient is a variable-sized bag of TVUS images. Proposes GMM pooling to summarize the bag by modeling the feature distribution of all images, capturing intra-patient variability beyond standard point-estimate aggregators.

Result: PTB prediction: PR-AUC improved from 0.44 to 0.56 over instance-based model. Lymph node benchmark: 0.91 F1, 0.89 ROC-AUC for classification, 0.18 MAE for regression (SOTA).

Conclusion: GMM pooling effectively models intra-patient variability from multiple images, outperforming standard MIL aggregators for PTB prediction and achieving SOTA on a lymph node metastasis benchmark.

Abstract: Preterm birth (PTB) prediction can enable targeted surveillance and timely intervention, yet most ultrasound-based models use a single selected transvaginal ultrasound (TVUS) frame per patient despite routine exams acquiring multiple cervical images. We formulate PTB prediction as a multiple instance learning (MIL) problem, representing each patient as a variable-sized bag of TVUS images with a single outcome label. To move beyond standard MIL aggregators that collapse a bag into a point estimate, we propose a Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) pooling, which summarizes all images in a bag into a fixed-length representation by modeling their feature distribution. This design captures intra-patient variability. We evaluate the method on a private clinical cohort and on a public lymph node metastasis benchmark. For PTB prediction, GMM pooling improves over the instance-based model PR-AUC from 0.44 to 0.56. On the lymph node benchmark, it achieves state-of-the-art performance with 0.91 F1-score and 0.89 ROC-AUC for classification and 0.18 MAE for regression. The code is publicly available at https://github.com/HussainAlasmawi/GMM_Pooling.

</details>


### [223] [ScalingAttention: Discovering Intrinsic Sparse Attention Topology for Video Diffusion Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23019)
*Ruiliang Zhou,Xuecheng Wu,Kang He,Guangyun Han,Bin Liu,Qinqin Chen,Wende Xu,Qingjie Zhao,Chengru Song*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ScalingAttention is a training-free framework that accelerates Diffusion Transformers for video generation by leveraging weight-encoded Intrinsic Sparse Topology to achieve up to 1.90X end-to-end speedup with superior fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) suffer from a quadratic computational bottleneck due to 3D full attention. Existing sparse methods face a dilemma: dynamic pruning has prohibitive runtime overhead and memory fragmentation, while static heuristics fail to capture fine-grained dependencies.

Method: ScalingAttention decouples topology discovery from sparsity control via two components: (1) WEST (Weight-Encoded Sparse Topology) extracts a robust block-sparse prior mask offline to eliminate runtime search; (2) FAST (Fidelity-Aware Sensitivity Tuning) adaptively tunes head-wise sparsity based on diffusion fidelity requirements. A hardware-aligned bit-wise block-sparse kernel is co-designed for practical acceleration.

Result: Experiments on Wan2.1 show up to 1.90X end-to-end speedup with superior fidelity, establishing a new Pareto frontier over state-of-the-art baselines.

Conclusion: ScalingAttention demonstrates that attention in DiTs has a weight-encoded, scale-invariant Intrinsic Sparse Topology that can be extracted offline, enabling training-free acceleration that outperforms existing sparse attention methods.

Abstract: While Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) have revolutionized high-fidelity video generation, their reliance on 3D full attention creates a quadratic computational bottleneck. Existing sparse methods face a dilemma: dynamic pruning suffers from prohibitive runtime overhead and memory fragmentation, while static heuristics fail to capture fine-grained dependencies. In this work, we propose ScalingAttention, a training-free framework grounded in a key inductive bias: while individual activations are input-dependent, the high-mass attention regions for each head rapidly converge to a stable, prompt-agnostic Intrinsic Sparse Topology. This topology is weight-encoded, scale-invariant, and efficient to extract. ScalingAttention decouples topology discovery from sparsity control via: (1) WEST (Weight-Encoded Sparse Topology), which extracts a robust block-sparse prior mask offline to eliminate runtime search; (2) FAST (Fidelity-Aware Sensitivity Tuning), which adaptively tunes head-wise sparsity based on diffusion fidelity requirements. To ensure practical acceleration, we co-design a hardware-aligned bit-wise block-sparse kernel. Experiments on Wan2.1 show up to 1.90X end-to-end speedup with superior fidelity, establishing a new Pareto frontier over state-of-the-art baselines.

</details>


### [224] [Boosting Neural Video Codec via Scale-Driven Online Flow Refinement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23023)
*Tiange Zhang,Rongqun Lin,Haocheng Tang,Xiandong Meng,Weijia Jiang,Zhimeng Huang,Siwei Ma*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes a training-free, plug-and-play module (SOFR) that refines optical flow across scales to improve neural video codec generalization on unseen motion patterns, achieving 2.84% (PSNR) and 4.05% (MS-SSIM) bitrate savings with negligible overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: State-of-the-art neural video codecs (NVCs) generalize poorly to complex, unseen motion patterns; online fine-tuning is too expensive, so a training-free method is needed to bridge this domain gap.

Method: SOFR is a plug-and-play module that integrates motion information from coarse and fine scales and dynamically fuses them based on warping accuracy, with a rate-aware strategy that selects fusion strategies per bitrate mode and a warping-error-based reliability check.

Result: On USTC-TD dataset, SOFR brings average 2.84% (PSNR) and 4.05% (MS-SSIM) bitrate savings across NVC frameworks (DCVC-SDD, DCVC-FM, EHVC) with negligible coding time increase.

Conclusion: The proposed training-free, scale-driven flow refinement effectively and efficiently improves NVC generalization to complex motion without fine-tuning, demonstrating broad applicability across multiple NVC architectures.

Abstract: Although state-of-the-art neural video codecs (NVCs) have achieved remarkable performance, they suffer from limited generalization when encountering complex motion patterns unseen during training. To bridge this domain gap without the expensive cost of online fine-tuning, we propose a Training-Free Scale-Driven Online Flow Refinement (SOFR) method. Serving as a plug-and-play module, SOFR integrates motion information from coarse and fine scales and dynamically fuses them according to warping accuracy, effectively rectifying motion estimation errors with negligible computational overhead. Furthermore, we design a rate-aware strategy that selects different dynamic fusion strategies according to bitrate modes, and employs a reliability check based on warping error to ensure robustness. Extensive experiments on the USTC-TD dataset verify the effectiveness and generalization of SOFR across various NVC frameworks, including DCVC-SDD, DCVC-FM, and EHVC. Notably, it brings an average of 2.84% and 4.05% bitrate savings in terms of PSNR and MS-SSIM, respectively, to DCVC-FM with negligible coding time increase. Our code is available at https://github.com/SunnyMass/SOFR.

</details>


### [225] [Learning Stable Canonical Worlds for Novel View Synthesis and Beyond](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23027)
*Xiaoyu Xu,Jian Zou,Sheyang Tang,Zhihua Wang,Jing Liao,Kede Ma*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CanonicalGS is a feed-forward pipeline that maps multi-view observations into a stable scene-centric representation using uncertainty-aware fusion of depth, semantic features, and uncertainty estimates, achieving up to 2.5 dB PSNR improvement for novel view synthesis and 11% gain in semantic segmentation accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing feed-forward Gaussian splatting methods remain tied to view-dependent predictions and accumulate noisy or redundant evidence when more input views are added, instead of converging to a stable scene representation.

Method: CanonicalGS extracts view-centric evidence from depth, semantic features, and uncertainty estimates, then aggregates this evidence in a canonical latent world using uncertainty-aware fusion, emphasizing reliable observations while suppressing uncertain or redundant ones.

Result: Up to 2.5 dB improvement in PSNR for novel view synthesis and an 11% gain in semantic segmentation accuracy.

Conclusion: The proposed scene-centric representation scales more effectively for novel view synthesis and transfers well to downstream visual perception tasks.

Abstract: Feed-forward Gaussian splatting (FFGS) facilitates real-time novel view synthesis, yet current methods often remain tied to view-dependent predictions. As more input views are added, they may accumulate noisy or redundant evidence instead of converging to a stable scene representation. In this paper, we introduce CanonicalGS, a feed-forward pipeline that maps cluttered multi-view observations into a stable, scene-centric representation. CanonicalGS first extracts view-centric evidence from depth, semantic features, and uncertainty estimates, and then aggregates this evidence in a canonical latent world using uncertainty-aware fusion. By emphasizing reliable observations while suppressing uncertain or redundant ones, CanonicalGS produces representations that scale more effectively for novel view synthesis and transfer to downstream visual perception tasks. Experiments show up to a $2.5$ dB improvement in peak signal-to-noise ratio for synthesizing novel views and an $11\%$ gain in semantic segmentation accuracy.

</details>


### [226] [Physics-Guided Spatiotemporal State Space Modeling for Lookahead Molten Pool Segmentation in Laser Wire-Feed Welding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23028)
*Sen Li,Haichao Cui,Changhao Yin,Chendong Shao,Yaqi Wang,Xinhua Tang,Fenggui Lu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: WeldMamba is a physics-guided spatiotemporal state space network that predicts weld-pool segmentation 500ms ahead using coaxial images, process parameters, and electrical signals, achieving 74.63% mIoU on a 43-sequence laser welding dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-time weld-pool perception is critical for closed-loop control in laser wire-feed welding, but sensing, computation, and actuator response introduce unavoidable delays that hinder effective control.

Method: The model uses a visual encoder, process- and sensor-conditioned feature normalization, patch-level temporal state space modeling, horizon-conditioned latent prediction, dense future feature prediction, and a motion-aware mask decoder. Auxiliary losses include signed-distance-function supervision, temporal consistency, feature distillation, and fine-grained keyhole losses.

Result: 74.63% mIoU at 500 ms lookahead on a 43-sequence laser welding dataset. Ablation shows temporal history, patch-level state space modeling, and keyhole motion awareness are the main contributors.

Conclusion: The proposed WeldMamba effectively enables lookahead weld-pool segmentation, compensating for system delays in laser welding, with strong contributions from temporal modeling and motion awareness.

Abstract: Real-time weld-pool perception is critical for closed-loop control in laser wire-feed welding, where sensing, computation, and actuator response introduce unavoidable delay. This paper presents a physics-guided spatiotemporal state space network for lookahead weld-pool segmentation. The model uses historical coaxial grayscale images, welding process parameters, and aligned wire-state electrical signals to predict the future semantic layout of three physically meaningful regions: keyhole, wire, and molten pool. It combines a visual encoder, process- and sensor-conditioned feature normalization, patch-level temporal state space modeling, horizon-conditioned latent prediction, dense future feature prediction, and a motion-aware mask decoder. Auxiliary signed-distance-function supervision, temporal consistency, feature distillation, and fine-grained keyhole losses further constrain the predicted geometry and local motion. Experiments on a 43-sequence laser welding dataset show that the proposed WeldMamba reaches 74.63\% mIoU at a 500 ms lookahead. Ablation studies further show that temporal history, patch-level state space modeling, and keyhole motion awareness are the main contributors to robust future segmentation.

</details>


### [227] [DrivingVoxels: Compositional Sparse Voxel Rasterization for Dynamic Driving Scene Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23031)
*Tania Aguirre,Luis Roldão,Moussab Bennehar,Nathan Piasco,Dzmitry Tsishkou,Simone Rossi,Pietro Michiardi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DrivingVoxels is a compositional sparse voxel rendering framework for dynamic driving scenes that uses multiple independent octrees (one per rigid dynamic object plus a static background octree) with LiDAR-guided initialization, achieving comparable perceptual quality, better structural metrics, and shorter training times than prior 3DGS-based methods on PandaSet.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods struggle with dynamic driving scenes: sparse voxel methods are mostly static-only, while 3D Gaussian Splatting approaches suffer from long training times and uncontrollable memory growth in large, unbounded environments with multiple dynamic objects.

Method: A compositional sparse voxel rendering framework that jointly rasterizes sparse voxels from multiple independent octrees in a single rendering pass. Each rigid dynamic object is represented by a local-coordinate octree, and the static background is modeled by a separate octree. A fully explicit, neural-free representation is used with LiDAR-guided structural initialization to capture scene geometry efficiently.

Result: On PandaSet, DrivingVoxels achieves on-par perceptual metrics and better structural metrics for novel view synthesis and reconstruction, while requiring shorter training times than prior 3DGS-based methods.

Conclusion: The proposed compositional sparse voxel approach effectively handles dynamic driving scenes, balancing rendering fidelity with training efficiency and memory control, outperforming 3DGS-based methods on structural metrics with faster optimization.

Abstract: Reconstructing dynamic urban scenes remains challenging due to the unbounded nature of driving environments and the presence of multiple dynamic objects. Currently, potentially faster sparse voxel methods are mainly designed for static scenarios. On the other hand, dynamic approaches based on 3D Gaussian Splatting, despite their high-fidelity, are often time-consuming for driving scenarios and exhibit uncontrollable memory growth in large scenes. To address these limitations, we present DrivingVoxels, a compositional sparse voxel rendering framework for dynamic driving scenes. Our method jointly rasterizes sparse voxels from multiple independent octrees within a single rendering pass. Each rigid dynamic object is represented by an octree defined in its local coordinate frame, while a separate static octree models the stationary background. DrivingVoxels adopts a fully explicit, neural-free representation together with a LiDAR-guided structural initialization that efficiently captures scene geometry. We evaluate our framework on the PandaSet benchmark, demonstrating that DrivingVoxels performs on par on perceptual metrics and better on structural metrics for NVS and reconstruction while requiring shorter training times than previous 3DGS-base methods to an efficient optimization workflow anchored by a strong LiDAR prior.

</details>


### [228] [SPAR: Semantic-Pixel Self-Alignment and Adaptive Routing for Unified Multimodal Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23041)
*Hongxiang Li,Hongxu Chen,Chenyang Zhu,Xiaoshuang Huang,Jiayin Cai,Xiaolong Jiang,Yao Hu,Long Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SPAR introduces a unified multimodal framework with Semantic-Pixel self-alignment and Adaptive Routing that bridges the gap between visual understanding and generation by using an asymmetric dual-stream tokenizer and a self-aligned generation paradigm, achieving state-of-the-art results in both tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MLLMs excel at visual understanding but fail at visual generation due to the fundamental discrepancy between semantic perception features and pixel-level reconstruction features. Existing approaches rely on external teacher models to bridge this gap, which creates dependencies and limits efficiency.

Method: (1) An asymmetric dual-stream unified tokenizer: a lightweight semantic stream for discriminative features and a Transformer-augmented pixel stream for fine-grained visual details, unified into a compact latent space. (2) A self-aligned generation paradigm that uses the optimized tokenizer as an internal alignment teacher for the diffusion model, eliminating external dependencies. (3) Dynamic Token Routing that adaptively aggregates multi-layer MLLM features per token based on semantic demands.

Result: SPAR achieves state-of-the-art performance among unified architectures, delivering exceptional generation and reconstruction quality while preserving foundational visual understanding capabilities.

Conclusion: The proposed semantic-pixel self-alignment and adaptive routing effectively reconcile the feature discrepancy between perception and generation, establishing a new SOTA for unified multimodal frameworks without external teacher dependence.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have achieved remarkable success in visual understanding but remain constrained in visual generation due to the fundamental feature discrepancy between semantic perception and pixel-level reconstruction. Bridging this gap requires overcoming two core challenges: endowing semantic encoders with high-fidelity reconstruction capabilities, and effectively aligning generative models with semantic spaces without relying on external teachers. To this end, we propose a novel unified multimodal framework featuring \textbf{S}emantic-\textbf{P}ixel self-alignment and \textbf{A}daptive \textbf{R}outing (\textbf{SPAR}). First, to reconcile semantic perception with pixel-level reconstruction, we introduce an asymmetric dual-stream unified tokenizer. A lightweight semantic stream anchors discriminative features, while a Transformer-augmented pixel stream recovers fine-grained visual details into a unified compact latent space. Second, to eliminate external dependencies, we propose a self-aligned generation paradigm that natively leverages this optimized tokenizer as an internal alignment teacher for the diffusion model. Furthermore, to facilitate flexible multimodal interaction within this unified space, we introduce Dynamic Token Routing, which enables each token to adaptively aggregate multi-layer MLLM features based on its distinct semantic demands. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SPAR establishes the state-of-the-art for unified architectures, achieving exceptional generation and reconstruction quality while preserving foundational visual understanding capabilities.

</details>


### [229] [UECP: Uncertainty-Enhanced Collaborative Perception](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23046)
*Kang Yang,Tianci Bu,Peng Wang,Deying Li,Wen Jie,Yongcai Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes UECP, a collaborative perception framework for autonomous driving that uses a physically grounded uncertainty map (supervised by LiDAR point density) to replace traditional confidence maps for unbiased agent contribution weighting, and introduces an Uncertainty-Aware Pyramid Fusion (UAPF) module with Uncertainty-Weighted Downsampling and Uncertainty-Guided Residual Fusion for robust cooperative perception.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing collaborative perception methods rely on confidence maps co-trained with detection heads, which are biased and correlated with detection noise. Additionally, there is no principled way to deeply integrate physical evidence into cooperative fusion.

Method: Proposes an uncertainty map supervised by real-time LiDAR point density (physically grounded, decoupled from detection noise). Develops UECP framework with UAPF module using a coarse-to-fine strategy: Uncertainty-Weighted Downsampling (UWD) for high-fidelity feature preservation, and Uncertainty-Guided Residual Fusion (UGRF) to reinforce ego features and suppress noise.

Result: UECP outperforms state-of-the-art methods in effectiveness and robustness on real-world datasets.

Conclusion: Embedding a physically grounded uncertainty map into fusion significantly improves collaborative perception quality and robustness.

Abstract: Collaborative perception serves as a pivotal solution to enhance the perception capability of individual agents in autonomous driving, where a core challenge lies in seeking reliable evidence to quantify and weight the contribution of each participating agent. Existing methods typically rely on a confidence map, which is co-trained with the detection head, but it is inherently correlated with the detection results and thus fails to provide unbiased physical evidence. Furthermore, how to deeply integrate evidence into the cooperative fusion process remains an open question.
