To address these issues, this paper first proposes an uncertainty map, a physically grounded and unambiguous metric for evaluating perception quality. This map is directly supervised by real-time sensor signals, i.e., LiDAR point density, ensuring decoupling from detection noise and thereby providing physical scenario-aware evidence for weighting agent contribution. Based on this map, we develop the Uncertainty-Enhanced Collaborative Perception (UECP) framework, centered on the Uncertainty-Aware Pyramid Fusion (UAPF) module. UAPF uses a coarse-to-fine strategy, with two key components: Uncertainty-Weighted Downsampling (UWD) for high-fidelity feature preservation, and Uncertainty-Guided Residual Fusion (UGRF) to reinforce ego features, suppressing noise and ensuring robust fusion. Extensive experiments on real-world datasets show UECP outperforms state-of-the-art methods in effectiveness and robustness by embedding the uncertainty map into fusion. Code will be publicly available.

</details>


### [230] [Unlimited OCR Works](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23050)
*Youyang Yin,Huanhuan Liu,YY,Qunyi Xie,Chaorun Liu,Shiqi Yang,Shaohua Wang,Zhanlong Liu,Hao Zou,Jinyue Chen,Shu Wei,Jingjing Wu,Mingxin Huang,Zhen Wu,Guibin Wang,Tengyu Du,Lei Jia*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Unlimited OCR introduces Reference Sliding Window Attention (R-SWA) to replace standard attention in LLM-based OCR decoders, maintaining a constant KV cache throughout decoding to eliminate the memory and speed degradation issues plaguing long-sequence OCR tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: End-to-end OCR models using LLM decoders suffer from accumulated KV cache memory consumption and progressive slowdown as output sequences lengthen, contrasting with human efficiency in long-horizon copying tasks.

Method: Proposes Reference Sliding Window Attention (R-SWA), which replaces all attention layers in the decoder to reduce attention computation costs and maintain a constant KV cache throughout the entire decoding process. Built upon DeepSeek OCR as baseline.

Result: Combining DeepSeek OCR's high-compression encoder with constant KV cache design allows transcription of dozens of pages in a single forward pass under a standard 32K maximum length.

Conclusion: R-SWA is a general-purpose parsing attention mechanism applicable beyond OCR to tasks such as ASR and translation. Code and weights are publicly released.

Abstract: Recently, end-to-end OCR models, exemplified by DeepSeek OCR, have once again thrust OCR into the spotlight. A widely held view is that employing a large language model (LLM) as the decoder allows the model to leverage the prior distribution of language, leading to improved OCR performance. However, the downside is equally evident: as the output sequence lengthens, the accumulated KV cache drives up memory consumption and progressively slows down generation. This stands in stark contrast to humans, who exhibit no such decline in efficiency during long-horizon copying tasks. In this technical report, we propose Unlimited OCR, a model designed to emulate human parsing working memory. Taking DeepSeek OCR as the baseline, we replace all attention layers in the decoder with our proposed Reference Sliding Window Attention (R-SWA), which reduces attention computation costs while maintaining a constant KV cache throughout the entire decoding process. By combining the high compression rate of DeepSeek OCR's encoder with our constant KV cache design, Unlimited OCR can transcribe dozens of pages of documents in a single forward pass under a standard maximum length of 32K. More importantly, R-SWA is a general-purpose parsing attention mechanism - beyond OCR, it is equally applicable to tasks such as ASR, translation, etc. Codes and model weights are publicly available at http://github.com/baidu/Unlimited-OCR.

</details>


### [231] [Three-Step Hierarchical Transformer for Multi-Pedestrian Trajectory Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23058)
*Raphaël Delécluse,Hazem Wannous,Laurent Grisoni,Laurent Guimas*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A three-step hierarchical Transformer that separates temporal encoding, multimodal fusion, and social interaction reasoning for pedestrian trajectory prediction, achieving SOTA on real-world datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods either address temporal dynamics, multimodal cues, and social interactions separately or entangle them in costly attention blocks, limiting scalability, flexibility, and interpretability.

Method: A three-step hierarchical Transformer with (1) lightweight GRU summaries for efficient cross-modal attention, (2) explicit separation of temporal encoding, multimodal fusion, and scene-level interaction reasoning, and (3) social attention over time-agent tokens for inter-pedestrian influence modeling.

Result: State-of-the-art performance on real-world datasets (JRDB, Urban) and competitive results on JTA; ablation confirms contribution of each stage; qualitative analysis shows ability to anticipate complex behaviors like early turning.

Conclusion: Explicitly separating temporal, multimodal, and social reasoning in a hierarchical Transformer improves scalability, flexibility, and interpretability for pedestrian trajectory prediction while achieving strong empirical performance.

Abstract: Pedestrian trajectory prediction requires modeling temporal dynamics, multimodal cues, and social interactions in crowded environments. Existing methods often address these factors separately or entangle them in costly attention blocks, limiting scalability, flexibility, and interpretability. We propose a three-step hierarchical Transformer that explicitly separates temporal encoding, multimodal fusion, and scene-level interaction reasoning. Lightweight GRU summaries enable efficient cross-modal attention, while social attention over time--agent tokens captures inter-pedestrian influences at manageable cost. Experiments on JTA, JRDB, and the Pedestrians and Cyclists in Road Traffic dataset show state-of-the-art performance on real-world datasets (JRDB, Urban) and competitive results on JTA. Ablation and qualitative analyses confirm the contribution of each stage and the model's ability to anticipate complex behaviors such as early turning.

</details>


### [232] [MotionHalluc: Diagnosing Kinematic Hallucinations in Fine-Grained Motion Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23061)
*Weile Guo,Shenghong He,Danying Mo,Chengdong Xu,Xuexun Liu,Chao Yu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces MotionHalluc, a benchmark for evaluating motion hallucinations in cross-video comparison, identifies three hallucination types (directional, attributional, temporal), and proposes a training-free baseline (PPV) that injects explicit kinematic measurements, achieving a 10.6% average performance gain across models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing models for cross-video motion instruction generation suffer from motion hallucinations—generating descriptions that do not reflect actual kinematic differences between paired videos. There is no dedicated benchmark to systematically evaluate these hallucinations.

Method: (1) MotionHalluc benchmark: 1540 fine-grained questions over 553 video pairs, evaluating three hallucination dimensions (directional, attributional, temporal). (2) Perceive-Parse-Verify (PPV): a training-free baseline that converts candidate instructions into executable measurement queries and supplies kinematic measurements at inference time.

Result: State-of-the-art large multimodal models show high susceptibility to motion hallucinations. The PPV baseline achieves an average 10.6% performance gain across models through explicit quantitative measurement injection.

Conclusion: Motion reasoning with explicit quantitative measurements is a key factor in reducing hallucinations in cross-video comparison.

Abstract: Motion instruction generation in cross-video comparison aims to produce corrective feedback that describes the differences between a query and a reference motion. However, existing models often generate instructions that exhibit motion hallucinations, failing to reflect actual kinematic differences between paired videos. To systematically investigate these hallucinations, we introduce MotionHalluc, a dedicated benchmark for evaluating motion hallucinations in paired-video comparison. MotionHalluc comprises 1540 fine-grained questions over 553 video pairs, evaluating hallucinations along three core dimensions: (1)directional hallucination, (2)attributional hallucination, and (3)temporal hallucination. Extensive evaluations of state-of-the-art large multimodal models demonstrate high susceptibility to these hallucinations. Furthermore, we provide Perceive-Parse-Verify (PPV) as a training-free measurements extraction and verification baseline that converts candidate instructions into executable measurement queries and supplies kinematic measurements at inference time. Our results show that this simple measurements injection yields an average 10.6% performance gain across models, suggesting that motion reasoning with explicit quantitative measurements is a key factor in reducing hallucinations in cross-video comparison. Our code and dataset will be made publicly available upon acceptance.

</details>


### [233] [Attention-Spectrum Regularization for Replay-Free Continual Multimodal LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23063)
*Chuangxin Zhao,Canran Xiao,Siyuan Ma,Mengyao Lyu,Yanbiao Ma,Jun Xia,Guiguang Ding,Yang Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ASR is a replay-free continual learning framework for MLLMs that preserves cross-modal attention structures via spectral regularization of attention maps, outperforming existing methods on multiple benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MLLMs suffer from catastrophic forgetting when continually fine-tuned on non-stationary streams of visual domains, question types, and instructions, and existing continual vision-language methods provide limited control over how internal cross-modal attention patterns supporting old skills drift during adaptation.

Method: ASR treats cross-modal attention maps as 2D signals, compresses them into compact spectral statistics (Fourier power spectra), stores only skill-wise prototype distributions (no replay data or old snapshots), and applies a phase-invariant spectral regularizer to constrain harmful drift while allowing instance-level attention adaptation.

Result: Experiments on continual VQA and multimodal instruction-tuning benchmarks (VQA v2, VQACL, CLT-VQA, CoIN, UCIT) show ASR consistently improves final performance and reduces forgetting over replay-, regularization-, and adapter-based baselines.

Conclusion: Preserving skill-level attention structure via spectral regularization is an effective and lightweight mechanism for continual learning in multimodal large language models.

Abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) are increasingly required to adapt to non-stationary streams of visual domains, question types, and user instructions, yet continual fine-tuning often causes severe forgetting of previously acquired multimodal skills. Existing continual vision-language methods mainly preserve outputs, replay data or pseudo-data, regularize embedding geometry, or allocate task-specific parameters, but they provide limited control over how internal cross-modal attention patterns supporting old skills drift during adaptation. We propose Attention-Spectrum Regularization (ASR), a replay-free continual learning framework that preserves skill-conditioned structures of cross-modal attention. ASR treats cross-attention maps as two-dimensional signals, summarizes their scale and directional properties into compact spectral statistics, and stores only skill-wise prototype distributions instead of replaying past image-question pairs, generated pseudo-examples, or old-stage teacher snapshots. In later stages, a phase-invariant spectral regularizer constrains harmful drift of these prototypes while allowing instance-level attention to adapt to new tasks. We provide theoretical analysis showing that skill-conditioned spectral drift controls forgetting under a spectral sufficiency assumption, and that Fourier power spectra are stable to spatial translations and bounded perturbations. Experiments on continual VQA and multimodal instruction-tuning benchmarks, including VQA v2, VQACL, CLT-VQA, CoIN, and UCIT, show that ASR consistently improves final performance and reduces forgetting over strong replay-, regularization-, and adapter-based baselines. Preserving skill-level attention structure is an effective and lightweight mechanism for continual MLLMs. Code is available at https://github.com/Creative-zcx/attention-spectrum-replay

</details>


### [234] [Rethinking Prototype-based Similarity Learning for Few-Shot Object Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23069)
*KunHo Heo,Seungjae kim,Wongyu Lee,SuYeon Kim,MyeongAh Cho*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes TSMa (Text-Anchored Semantic Mask) and SHARe (Stage-Aligned Hierarchical Autoregressive Regression) to address class confusion and insufficient spatial localization in few-shot object detection, achieving +10.1 nAP improvement on COCO.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing prototype-based similarity learning approaches for few-shot object detection suffer from (i) class confusion due to inter-class similarity margin collapse, and (ii) insufficient visual cues for precise localization since similarity scores capture only class-level semantic affinity with limited spatial information.

Method: Two complementary components: (1) Text-Anchored Semantic Mask (TSMa) uses class-level text features as semantic anchors to identify semantically aligned channels via channel-wise interaction between visual and text features, suppressing style-induced spurious responses and emphasizing class-intrinsic signals. (2) Stage-Aligned Hierarchical Autoregressive Regression (SHARe) reformulates localization as a hierarchical autoregressive process that progressively refines bounding boxes across multiple stages, leveraging ViT layer-wise characteristics by aligning feature abstraction levels with regression stages.

Result: State-of-the-art performance on COCO, outperforming previous best by +10.1 nAP, with extensive analysis validating each component.

Conclusion: The proposed TSMa and SHARe effectively mitigate class confusion and improve localization precision in few-shot object detection, achieving significant gains over prior methods.

Abstract: Few-shot object detection aims to detect novel object categories from only a few labeled examples, avoiding costly large-scale annotation. Recent prototype-based similarity learning approaches enable training-free adaptation by matching query features with class prototypes. However, they suffer from two fundamental limitations: (i) class confusion arising from inter-class similarity margin collapse, and (ii) insufficient visual cues for precise localization, as similarity scores capture only class-level semantic affinity while providing limited spatial information. To address these issues, we introduce two complementary components. Text-Anchored Semantic Mask (TSMa) leverages class-level text features as semantic anchors to identify semantically aligned channels through channel-wise interaction between visual and text features. By suppressing style-induced spurious responses and emphasizing class-intrinsic signals, TSMa enlarges inter-class similarity margins and mitigates class confusion. We further propose Stage-Aligned Hierarchical Autoregressive Regression (SHARe), which reformulates localization as a hierarchical autoregressive process that progressively refines bounding boxes across multiple stages. SHARe leverages the layer-wise characteristics of ViT representations by aligning feature abstraction levels with regression stages: deeper layers guide early coarse localization, while shallower layers rich in edge and texture cues refine spatial details in later stages. Experiments on COCO demonstrate a new state of the art, outperforming the previous best by +10.1 nAP, with extensive analysis validating each component. The code is available at https://github.com/VisualScienceLab-KHU/ReSet.

</details>


### [235] [Poisson2Gaussian: Noise Gaussianization to Enhance Image Denoising](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23098)
*Xirou Zhou,Zijing Xu,Yibo Qu,Qi Zhang,Xiaowan Hu,Xinyang Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Poisson2Gaussian (P2G) is a noise Gaussianization method that converts complex Poisson-mixed real-world noise into i.i.d. Gaussian noise via probability density matching, enabling existing denoisers to achieve state-of-the-art performance across diverse datasets without requiring paired clean data or explicit noise parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Poisson-mixed noise in photon detection is signal-dependent, heteroscedastic, and statistically asymmetric, making it difficult for existing denoisers to learn, whereas i.i.d. Gaussian noise is easier for networks to model.

Method: P2G converts complex real-world noise to i.i.d. Gaussian noise through probability density matching beyond low-order moments, paired with an unbiased denoising framework that ensures convergence to the underlying signal without paired clean data or explicit noise parameters.

Result: P2G achieves state-of-the-art performance across diverse datasets, improving PSNR by up to 0.75 dB in challenging scenarios where noise strongly deviates from Gaussian statistics.

Conclusion: P2G is architecture-agnostic, provides universal improvements for various denoisers, and the source code will be publicly available.

Abstract: The quantum nature of light determines the inherent Poisson stochasticity of photon detection, which is ubiquitous in photography, microscopy, and astronomy. However, our controlled numerical studies reveal that the signal-dependency, heteroscedasticity, and statistical asymmetry of Poisson-mixed noise make it challenging for existing denoisers to learn. In contrast, i.i.d. Gaussian noise, with its statistical independence and symmetric distribution, is easier to model for networks. To address this gap, we propose Poisson2Gaussian (P2G), a noise Gaussianization method that explicitly converts complex real-world noise to i.i.d. Gaussian noise via probability density matching beyond low-order moments. We also design an unbiased denoising framework that synergizes P2G with downstream denoisers, ensuring convergence to the underlying signal without requiring paired clean data or explicit noise parameters. Extensive experiments demonstrate that P2G consistently achieves state-of-the-art performance across diverse datasets. In challenging scenarios where noise strongly deviates from Gaussian statistics, our method improves the PSNR by up to 0.75 dB. Notably, P2G is architecture-agnostic and can provide universal improvements for various denoisers. The source code will be publicly available.

</details>


### [236] [Scene-agnostic ALS boresight self-calibration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23101)
*Aurélien Brun,Jan Skaloud*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper presents a new method for ALS boresight calibration that replaces traditional plane-based constraints with scene-agnostic point-to-point correspondences from overlapping strips, using two formulations (parametric adjustment and factor-graph integration), tested across five inertial systems, showing boresight recovery is possible during routine mapping with only 3-4 overlapping strips.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional ALS boresight calibration requires dedicated flight patterns over structured scenes containing planar surfaces, which limits applicability during routine mapping missions. The authors aim to relax these scene and operational constraints.

Method: Two formulations are proposed: (1) a parametric adjustment using INS/GNSS trajectory, and (2) a rigorous factor-graph formulation that adds boresight as a parameter to an existing dynamic network integrating raw GNSS/IMU data. Both use automatically extracted point-to-point correspondences from overlapping ALS strips instead of plane constraints.

Result: Evaluated across four ALS flights with five inertial systems, varying altitudes, overlap geometries, terrain types, and sensor classes. The lightweight formulation works for tactical/navigation-grade IMUs, while the factor-graph approach is superior when inertial errors are less observable. Boresight recovery is possible with minimum 3-4 overlapping strips during routine mapping.

Conclusion: For sufficiently high-quality INS/GNSS trajectories, boresight calibration can be performed without specific scene prerequisites during routine mapping operations. The proposed methods draw a clear boundary between legacy plane-based conditioning and the new scene-agnostic approach.

Abstract: ALS boresight calibration has relied for two decades on dedicated flight patterns over structured scenes containing planar surfaces of varied aspect and slope. While reliable, this approach imposes constraints on the scene content and operations, which limits its applicability to boresight recovery within routine mapping missions. We present a practical approach that substantially relaxes these requirements by replacing plane-based constraints with scene-agnostic point-to-point correspondences extracted automatically from overlapping ALS strips. Two complementary formulations are proposed to estimate boresight with laser vector observations: (i) a simpler parametric adjustment utilizing INS/GNSS trajectory; (ii) a rigorous formulation treating GNSS and raw inertial data within an existing factor-graph, i.e. a dynamic network, where boresight is added as an additional parameter. Both formulations are evaluated across four operational ALS flights equipped with five inertial systems, covering a wide range of flight altitudes, overlap geometries, terrain types and inertial sensor classes. The analysis draws a clear boundary between the legacy plane-based conditioning that falls short outside the calibration scenario and the proposed formulations, which either recover or absorb boresight effects under conventional mapping geometry. Among them, the lightweight formulation is sufficient for boresight recovery using tactical and navigation grade inertial sensors, while the general factor-graph approach is clearly superior when the inertial sensor errors are less observable within an optimal smoother. This supports the hypothesis that, for INS/GNSS trajectory of sufficient quality, the boresight calibration can be performed without particular scene prerequisites during routine mapping operations using a minimum of 3-4 overlapping strips, with either proposed formulation...

</details>


### [237] [Compression and Retrieval: Implicit Memory Retrieval for Video World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23105)
*Zhan Peng,Jie Ma,Huiqiang Sun,Chong Gao,Zhijie Xue,Zhiyu Pan,Zhiguo Cao,Jun Liang,Jing Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes CaR (Compression and Retrieval), an attention-driven implicit memory retrieval mechanism with a lightweight context compression network for long-horizon video world models. It also introduces SceneFly, a large-scale synthetic dataset with realistic camera trajectories. The approach achieves SOTA results on benchmarks and generalizes well to open-domain scenes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing video world models struggle with maintaining consistent long-term memory across complex camera trajectories, relying on computationally expensive context scaling or rigid heuristic retrieval mechanisms that lack generalization to varying trajectories and environments.

Method: Proposes CaR (Compression and Retrieval), an attention-driven implicit memory retrieval mechanism that injects viewpoint information via positional encoding and performs flexible memory retrieval through attention computation. Introduces a lightweight context compression network for efficient extended context processing, and constructs SceneFly dataset for training/evaluation.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art results on established benchmarks and demonstrates strong generalization to open-domain scenes.

Conclusion: The attention-driven implicit memory retrieval with context compression effectively addresses long-term memory challenges in video world models, outperforming existing methods.

Abstract: Video world models hold promise for simulating interactive environments, yet maintaining consistent long-term memory across complex camera trajectories remains a critical challenge. Existing methods typically rely on computationally expensive context scaling or rigid heuristic retrieval mechanisms, which lacks generalization to varying camera trajectories and environments. In this paper, we propose Compression and Retrieval (CaR), an attention-driven implicit memory retrieval mechanism to overcome these limitations. By injecting viewpoint information via positional encoding, our method performs flexible memory retrieval through attention computation. To efficiently process extended contexts with minimal computational overhead, we further introduce a lightweight context compression network. Furthermore, we construct SceneFly, a large-scale synthetic dataset featuring realistic camera trajectories and frame-level annotations to train and evaluate long-horizon video world models. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach achieves state-of-the-art results on established benchmarks and exhibits strong generalization to open-domain scenes.

</details>


### [238] [Technical Report for the ICRA 2026 GOOSE 2D Fine-Grained Semantic Segmentation Challenge: Pretraining-Diverse Ensemble of Foundation Vision Encoders for Robust Outdoor Scene Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23113)
*Boyan Wang,Yongxi Huang,Wenjing Li,Tianrui Hui,Shaofei Huang,Nan Pu,Zhun Zhong*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Our solution for the ICRA 2026 GOOSE challenge uses a pretraining-diverse ensemble of DINOv3, SigLIP2, and InternImage encoders paired with a Mask2Former decoder, trained with a strong recipe (long schedules, EMA, larger crop size, multi-scale + flip TTA). Achieves 75.40% mIoU (2nd place). Key finding: encoder pretraining recipe dominates accuracy, not parameter count or decoder design.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address fine-grained semantic segmentation of unstructured outdoor scenes from four camera platforms into 56 categories, pushing the limits of parsing complex outdoor environments.

Method: Ensemble of three foundation vision encoders (DINOv3, SigLIP2, InternImage) with complementary pretraining objectives, fed into a Mask2Former decoder. Training uses long schedules, EMA, larger crop size, and multi-scale + flip TTA. Encoders are combined via per-class validation-IoU weighting.

Result: 75.40% composite mIoU on the GOOSE test set, winning second place in the challenge.

Conclusion: The dominant factor for accuracy is the encoder's pretraining recipe rather than its parameter count or decoder design.

Abstract: This report presents our solution for the ICRA 2026 GOOSE 2D Fine-Grained Semantic Segmentation Challenge, which requires parsing unstructured outdoor scenes from four camera platforms into 56 fine-grained categories. Our approach pairs foundation vision encoders (including DINOv3, SigLIP2, and InternImage) with a Mask2Former decoder, and trains them with a strong recipe including long training schedules, exponential moving average, a larger crop size, and multi-scale plus flip test-time augmentation. The three encoders, chosen for their complementary pretraining objectives, are combined into a pretraining-diverse ensemble through per-class validation-IoU weighting. Evaluated on the official GOOSE test set, our submission achieves 75.40% composite mIoU and wins the second place of the challenge. Our study further shows that the encoder's pretraining recipe, rather than its parameter count or the decoder design, is the dominant factor for accuracy on this benchmark.

</details>


### [239] [LUMINA-26: Low-Light Understanding for Modeling and Interpreting Night-time Actions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23118)
*Aman Kumar Pandey,Anil Singh Parihar*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces LUMINA-26, a diverse low-light action recognition dataset with 6,784 clips across 26 classes, and proposes Illumi-Net, an illumination-adaptive Mixture-of-Experts network that achieves state-of-the-art results on ELLAR and establishes a strong baseline on LUMINA-26.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Low-light human action recognition is challenging due to poor illumination, noise, motion ambiguity, and existing datasets lacking action diversity, capture realism, and balanced class distribution.

Method: Proposes LUMINA-26 dataset (6,784 clips, 26 action classes, 22 subjects, 20 locations under natural low-light) and Illumi-Net, an Illumination-Adaptive Mixture-of-Experts Network using video-level illumination cues for adaptive enhancement, transformer-based spatio-temporal feature extraction, and expert-conditioned decision fusion.

Result: Surpasses state-of-the-art on ELLAR (Top-1: 55.13%, Top-5: 78.87%) and establishes baseline on LUMINA-26 (Top-1: 75.95%, Top-5: 93.58%).

Conclusion: Provides a practical benchmark and strong baseline for future low-light action recognition research.

Abstract: Low-light human action recognition remains a challenging problem due to poor illumination, amplified noise, motion ambiguity, and diverse real-world scenes. Existing low-light datasets often lack sufficient action diversity, capture realism, or balanced class distribution, limiting the development of robust models. To address this, we introduce LUMINA-26: Low-Light Understanding for Modeling and Interpreting Night-time Actions, comprising 6,784 clips across 26 action classes, recorded from 22 subjects across 20 indoor and outdoor locations under naturally occurring low-light conditions. We also propose Illumi-Net: An Illumination-Adaptive Mixture-of-Experts Network, which leverages video-level illumination cues to guide adaptive enhancement and transformer-based spatio-temporal feature extraction, with expert-conditioned decision fusion. Our method surpasses previous state-of-the-art performance on ELLAR (Top-1: 55.13%, Top-5: 78.87%) and establishes a strong baseline on LUMINA-26 (Top-1: 75.95%, Top-5: 93.58%), offering a practical benchmark for future low-light action recognition research.

</details>


### [240] [MambaADv2: Evolving Duality-enhanced State Space Model for Unsupervised Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23126)
*Xiaobin Hu,Haoyang He,Bo Yin,Yu He,Lei Xie,Jiangning Zhang,Yu-Gang Jiang,Shuicheng Yan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MambaADv2 is a multi-class unsupervised anomaly detection framework that uses Duality-enhanced State Space (DSS) modules, Hybrid State Space (HSS) blocks with position-aware modeling, and a semantics-adaptive progressive scanning strategy to combine linear complexity with superior long-range dependency modeling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CNNs fail to capture long-range dependencies, and Transformers suffer from quadratic computational complexity. Mamba-based architectures offer a middle ground, but existing Mamba models are not directly optimized for anomaly detection—specifically for reconstructing normal representations while amplifying anomalies.

Method: A pre-trained encoder + Mamba-inspired decoder with Duality-enhanced State Space (DSS) modules across multiple scales. DSS integrates parallel-cascaded Hybrid State Space (HSS) blocks (SSD-based Mamba lineage + Mamba3-style position-aware state-space modeling with dual computational paths: linear recurrence and parallel matrix formulation) and frequency-enhanced convolution operations. A semantics-adaptive progressive scanning strategy reduces scanning complexity along the feature pyramid.

Result: The approach combines global dependency modeling (long-range) and local representation learning with linear computational complexity, tailored for precise normal reconstruction and anomalous deviation amplification.

Conclusion: MambaADv2 advances Mamba-based architectures for unsupervised anomaly detection by addressing the core duality of modeling local continuity and global context, while maintaining efficiency via progressive scanning.

Abstract: While recent advancements in anomaly detection have demonstrated the efficacy of CNN- and Transformer-based approaches, these architectures face inherent limitations: CNNs struggle to capture long-range dependencies, whereas Transformers suffer from quadratic computational complexity. Consequently, Mamba-based architectures have attracted considerable attention, as they successfully combine superior long-range dependency modeling with linear computational complexity. By critically rethinking the structural evolution across the Mamba lineage 1-3 series, this paper proposes MambaADv2, a framework tailored for multi-class unsupervised anomaly detection. MambaADv2 comprises a pre-trained encoder and a Mamba-inspired decoder, equipped with Duality-enhanced State Space (DSS) modules across multiple scales. The proposed DSS module effectively models both global dependencies and local representations by integrating parallel-cascaded Hybrid State Space (HSS) blocks and frequency-enhanced convolution operations. The structure of the Hybrid State Space (HSS) block is tailored by following the SSD-based Mamba lineage and incorporating Mamba3-style position-aware state-space modeling, leveraging the dual computational paths of linear recurrence and parallel matrix formulation to model local continuity and global contextual comparison, thereby better serving the core anomaly detection objective of precisely reconstructing normal representations while magnifying anomalous deviations. Additionally, we propose a semantics-adaptive progressive scanning strategy that decays scanning complexity along the feature pyramid.

</details>


### [241] [Spectral Gating via Damped Oscillations for Adaptive Implicit Neural Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23129)
*Alex Costanzino,Pierluigi Zama Ramirez,Giuseppe Lisanti,Luigi Di Stefano*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes modeling neuron activations as steady-state responses of sinusoidally-forced damped harmonic oscillators, enabling adaptive spectral selectivity that resolves the spectral dilemma in Implicit Neural Representations without hyperparameter tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing INRs face a spectral dilemma: periodic activations capture fine details but memorize noise (all-pass filters), while spatially compact activations regularize well but suffer from low-frequency bias. Current solutions introduce computational overhead or are fragile to tune.

Method: Each neuron's activation is modeled as the steady-state response of a sinusoidally-forced damped harmonic oscillator. The amplitude of this response governs spectral selectivity. Oscillator parameters and network weights are jointly optimized. The network is initialized in the stopband, naturally enforcing a coarse-to-fine learning curriculum that progressively expands the spectral gate.

Result: The method achieves state-of-the-art or competitive results against established INRs across comprehensive experiments, without requiring any task-specific hyperparameter tuning.

Conclusion: Modeling activations as harmonic oscillator responses provides an elegant, parameter-free solution to the spectral dilemma in INRs, enabling adaptive spectral filtering that captures low frequencies first and high frequencies only when justified by the reconstruction objective.

Abstract: Implicit Neural Representations (INRs) have been proven successful in encoding continuous signals through coordinate-based networks, yet facing a spectral dilemma: periodic activations capture fine details but act as all-pass filters that memorise noise, while spatially compact activations regularise effectively but suffer from low-frequency bias. Existing attempts to resolve this trade-off introduce computational overhead or tuning frailty. We propose to model each neuron's activation as the steady-state response of a sinusoidally-forced damped harmonic oscillator, whose amplitude naturally governs the network's spectral selectivity during training. By jointly optimising the oscillator parameters alongside the network weights, our method adapts to the target signal's spectral content without explicit regularisation. Initialised in the stopband, the network exhibits a coarse-to-fine learning curriculum that progressively expands its spectral gate, capturing low-frequency structures first and high-frequency details only when justified by the reconstruction objective. Comprehensive experiments show that our approach consistently achieves state-of-the-art or competitive results against established INRs, while requiring no task-specific tuning of any hyperparameters.

</details>


### [242] [Expert Consensus on Criteria for the Automated Assessment of Laparoscopic Camera Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23131)
*Amir Ebrahimzadeh,Nazila Esmaeili,Michael Ghadimi,Jannis Hagenah*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper establishes a framework for automated laparoscopic camera navigation (LCN) assessment by defining 14 key aspects, surveying 23 surgeons to rate their clinical importance, and mapping them against the technological readiness of computer vision (CV) methods. The result is a "Clinical Importance vs. CV Technological Readiness" matrix identifying high-priority targets for developing automated assessment tools.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LCN assessment relies on manual rating systems that are time-consuming, subjective, and difficult to scale, limiting their use in surgical training. Automated feedback could provide immediate, standardized metrics to accelerate training.

Method: The authors developed a taxonomy of 14 key camera navigation aspects across five categories (Framing & Composition, Visibility & Clarity, Orientation & Stability, Motion & Dynamics, Safety & Awareness). They assessed the technological readiness of each aspect using current SoTA in CV, and conducted a survey where 23 practicing laparoscopic surgeons rated the importance of each aspect on a 5-point Likert scale and selected the five most critical skills.

Result: Foundational aspects (Field of View, Focus, Centering) were rated most important by surgeons. A "Clinical Importance vs. CV Technological Readiness" matrix was produced, highlighting aspects that are both clinically crucial and technologically ready for automated measurement.

Conclusion: This framework provides a roadmap for developing automated LCN skill assessment by aligning surgeon priorities with CV capabilities, enabling AI-driven assistance tools to accelerate learning curves and improve surgical safety and efficiency.

Abstract: Background: Laparoscopic camera navigation (LCN) is a critical skill, yet its current assessment typically relies on manual rating systems which are time-consuming and difficult to scale. Automated feedback could significantly enhance surgical training by providing immediate, standardized metrics. This study aims to define, clinically evaluate the relevance, and establish the technical readiness of a set of approaches for LCN assessment.
  Methods: We developed a detailed taxonomy of 14 key aspects of camera navigation, categorized into Framing & Composition, Visibility & Clarity, Orientation & Stability, Motion & Dynamics, and Safety & Awareness. For each aspect, we assessed the technological readiness of automated measurement based on the current state of the art (SoTA) in computer vision (CV). To establish clinical relevance, we designed a survey for practicing laparoscopic surgeons to rate the importance of each aspect on a 5-point Likert scale and to select the five most critical skills.
  Results: 23 surgeons participated in the survey. Foundational aspects like Field of View, Focus and Centering were rated as most important by surgeons. We present a "Clinical Importance vs. CV Technological Readiness" matrix, identifying high-priority targets for development--aspects that are both clinically crucial and technologically ready to measure.
  Conclusion: This work establishes a foundational framework for quantifying LCN skills. By aligning surgeon priorities with CV capabilities, we provide a clear roadmap for automatic skill assessment. This foundation enables the development of AI-driven assistance tools that can accelerate the learning curve for surgical assistants and potentially improve surgical safety and efficiency.

</details>


### [243] [T-VSS: Test-Time Visual Subspace Steering for Adversarial Robustness of Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23132)
*Jaehyuk Jang,Minseok Seo. Seungju Cho,Kangwook Ko,Changick Kim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: T-VSS is a lightweight test-time defense that improves adversarial robustness of VLMs by directly correcting corrupted visual features within a sample-specific low-rank subspace, outperforming prior methods in efficiency and robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing test-time adaptations for VLMs either adapt text prompts or optimize pixels/padding, which are indirect and expensive. No prior work directly adapts the corrupted visual representation itself in an efficient manner.

Method: T-VSS builds a sample-specific low-rank subspace from multi-view feature residuals anchored at the attacked image, then learns a shared feature correction within this subspace via reliability-weighted entropy minimization.

Result: On fine-grained, ImageNet, and ImageNet-OOD benchmarks, T-VSS improves adversarial robustness while maintaining competitive clean accuracy and achieves better efficiency than prior test-time adaptations.

Conclusion: Constraining test-time adaptation to a compact visual geometry (low-rank subspace) effectively steers attacked features toward stable and discriminative predictions while avoiding noisy full-space updates, offering a more direct and efficient defense path.

Abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) achieve strong zero-shot recognition, but they remain highly vulnerable to adversarial perturbations. Recent test-time adaptations improve robustness without retraining, but they do not directly adapt the corrupted visual representation itself. Prompt-based methods adapt the learnable text prompts, while input-space methods optimize pixels or padding at test time. These approaches can improve predictions, but they do so through an indirect and expensive optimization path. We propose Test-time Visual Subspace Steering (T-VSS), a lightweight defense that performs test-time adaptation directly in the visual feature space. T-VSS first builds a sample-specific low-rank subspace from multi-view feature residuals anchored at the attacked image. It then learns a shared feature correction within this subspace using reliability-weighted entropy minimization. By constraining adaptation to a compact visual geometry, T-VSS steers attacked features toward more stable and discriminative predictions while avoiding noisy full-space updates. Experiments on fine-grained, ImageNet, and ImageNet-OOD benchmarks show that T-VSS improves adversarial robustness while maintaining competitive clean accuracy and better efficiency than prior test-time adaptations.

</details>


### [244] [Koshur Pixel: a large-scale synthetic ocr dataset for kashmiri](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23144)
*Haq Nawaz Malik,Faizan Iqbal,Nahfid Nissar*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Koshur Pixel is the first large-scale synthetic OCR dataset for Kashmiri (Perso-Arabic Nastaliq script), comprising 613,078 image-text pairs generated via SynthOCR-Gen from the KS-PRET-5M corpus, with multiple fonts, granularities, and 25+ augmentation strategies to address low-resource OCR challenges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: OCR for low-resource languages like Kashmiri is constrained by lack of annotated training data and complexity of Nastaliq script (contextual glyph shaping, dense ligatures, orthographic variability), motivating a scalable synthetic alternative to manual annotation.

Method: Generated 613,078 synthetic image-text pairs from the KS-PRET-5M corpus using the SynthOCR-Gen framework, spanning multiple fonts and textual granularities (words to full-page documents), with 25+ augmentation strategies simulating real-world document degradations.

Result: Produced the first large-scale synthetic OCR dataset for Kashmiri, providing a cost-effective and scalable resource for training OCR systems without manual annotation.

Conclusion: Koshur Pixel establishes a foundational resource for digitizing Kashmiri textual heritage and advancing language technologies for a severely under-resourced language.

Abstract: Optical Character Recognition (OCR) for low-resource languages is often constrained by the lack of annotated training data and the complexity of script-specific rendering. Kashmiri, written primarily in the Perso-Arabic Nastaliq script, presents additional challenges due to contextual glyph shaping, dense ligatures, and orthographic variability. We introduce Koshur Pixel, the first large-scale synthetic OCR dataset for Kashmiri, comprising 613,078 image-text pairs generated from the KS-PRET-5M corpus using the SynthOCR-Gen framework. The dataset spans multiple fonts and textual granularities, ranging from individual words to full-page documents, and incorporates more than 25 augmentation strategies that emulate real-world document degradations. Koshur Pixel provides a scalable and cost-effective alternative to manual annotation, establishing a foundational resource for training OCR systems, digitizing Kashmiri textual heritage, and advancing language technologies for a severely under-resourced language.

</details>


### [245] [Interpretable Probabilistic Medical Image Segmentation via Gaussian Process with Explicit Modelling of Annotation Bias and Variability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23177)
*Qi Li,Yuliang Huang,Shaheer U. Saeed,Qianye Yang,Vasilis Stavrinides,Zachary M. C. Baum,Dean C. Barratt,J. Alison Noble,Tom Vercauteren,Yipeng Hu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A logit-space probabilistic segmentation framework using stochastic variational Gaussian Process that explicitly decomposes predictions into image-dependent reference logits and annotator-specific bias/variance perturbations, enabling analysis of intra- and inter-rater variability propagation while improving uncertainty calibration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multi-rater probabilistic segmentation models encode annotator characteristics implicitly in deep latent feature space, making direct analysis of annotator influence on predictive distributions difficult and less interpretable.

Method: Proposes a logit-space probabilistic segmentation framework based on stochastic variational Gaussian Process that explicitly decomposes predictions into: (1) an image-dependent reference logit distribution, and (2) annotator-specific perturbations parameterized by bias and variance, enabling explicit modeling of intra- and inter-rater variability.

Result: The method achieves improved uncertainty calibration with comparable segmentation accuracy compared to state-of-the-art multi-rater probabilistic segmentation methods on a multi-annotator medical image dataset. Learned bias and variance parameters quantitatively reflect annotator-specific behavior, and controlled perturbation experiments show systematic influence on predictive performance.

Conclusion: Explicitly modeling annotator-specific bias and variance perturbations in logit space via Gaussian Process provides better interpretability and uncertainty calibration for multi-rater medical image segmentation, with public code available.

Abstract: Deep learning-based medical image segmentation models are trained using annotations that exhibit systematic bias and variability across raters. While probabilistic multi-rater approaches can emulate annotator-specific delineations, annotator characteristics are typically encoded implicitly in deep latent feature space, making direct analysis of their influence on predictive distributions less straightforward. We propose a logit-space probabilistic segmentation framework based on stochastic variational Gaussian Process that explicitly decomposes predictions into an image-dependent reference logit distribution and annotator specific perturbations parameterised by bias and variance. This formulation enables more explicit analysis on how intra- and inter-rater variability propagate to predictive distributions. We evaluate the method on a multi-annotator medical image dataset, which shows that explicitly modelling annotator specific perturbations improves uncertainty calibration while maintaining comparable segmentation accuracy, compared with state-of-the-art multi-rater probabilistic segmentation method. The learned bias and variance parameters quantitatively reflect annotator-specific behaviour. Furthermore, controlled perturbation experiments over bias and variance demonstrate how changes in annotator parameters systematically influence predictive performance. The code used in this paper is made publicly available at https://github.com/QiLi111/GPS-Var.

</details>


### [246] [StreamPPG: Low-Latency rPPG Estimation via Consistent Privileged Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23186)
*Yiming Li,Yihan Yang,Yuguang Chu,Yuanhui Hu,Si-Yuan Cao,Xiaohan Zhang,Xiaokai Bai,Zhe Wu,Hui-Liang Shen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: StreamPPG is a unified architecture for real-time, low-latency frame-wise remote photoplethysmography (rPPG) estimation that achieves clip-wise competitive accuracy by using a Consistent Privileged Learning (CPL) strategy with ground-truth BVP signals as privileged information.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional clip-wise rPPG methods introduce seconds of delay (due to requiring 100+ frames before inference), hindering real-time use. Frame-wise methods fail to capture long-range temporal/periodic features of physiological rhythms, resulting in lower accuracy.

Method: StreamPPG uses a unified architecture trained with a Consistent Privileged Learning (CPL) strategy, where ground-truth rPPG signals serve as privileged information during training to improve representation learning without requiring them at inference.

Result: StreamPPG achieves state-of-the-art accuracy across multiple datasets while maintaining real-time throughput on edge devices.

Conclusion: StreamPPG bridges the gap between low-latency frame-wise estimation and high-accuracy clip-wise approaches, enabling practical real-time contact-free health monitoring.

Abstract: Remote photoplethysmography (rPPG) estimates the blood volume pulse (BVP) signal from facial videos, enabling contact-free health monitoring. Conventional clip-wise approaches, which use video clips as input, require capturing over one hundred frames before inference, thus introducing several seconds of delay and hindering real-time use. Meanwhile, frame-wise approaches struggle to capture long-range temporal and periodic features of physiological rhythms, and therefore lead to reduced estimation accuracy. To overcome these issues, we propose StreamPPG, a unified architecture that enables low-latency frame-wise physiological signal estimation while achieving competitive accuracy compared with clip-wise approaches. StreamPPG is trained under a consistent privileged learning (CPL) strategy, which leverages ground-truth rPPG signals as privileged information to enhance the model's representation capability. Extensive experiments demonstrate that StreamPPG achieves state-of-the-art accuracy across multiple datasets while maintaining real-time throughput on edge devices.

</details>


### [247] [Unmasking LAION-5B: Age, Gender, Race, and Emotion Biases in Large-Scale Image Datasets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23204)
*Iris Dominguez-Catena,Daniel Paternain,Mikel Galar*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper analyzes demographic biases (age, gender, race, emotion) in LAION-2B-en and LAION-2B-multi components of LAION-5B using FairFace, DeepFace, and Emo-AffectNet, finding overrepresentation of young adults, White individuals, and males, with stereotypical emotion-gender associations and consistent underrepresentation of minorities and middle-aged/older women.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large-scale image-text datasets like LAION-5B are foundational to modern AI but their uncurated nature raises concerns about demographic and stereotypical biases that could propagate to downstream models.

Method: Using FairFace, DeepFace, and Emo-AffectNet models to analyze faces detected in LAION-2B-en and LAION-2B-multi, examining biases across age, gender, race, and expressed emotion dimensions.

Result: Substantial overrepresentation of young adults (20-39), White individuals, and males; consistent underrepresentation of minority racial groups and middle-aged/older women; stereotypical associations (e.g., Anger with males, Happiness with females); patterns consistent across two demographic models and both dataset components.

Conclusion: Biases are deeply embedded in LAION-5B, one of the most widely-used training datasets, and given its scale in training generative models, these demographic imbalances could shape the behavior and outputs of numerous downstream AI systems.

Abstract: Large-scale image-text datasets, such as LAION-5B, are foundational to modern AI systems, yet their vast scale and uncurated nature raise significant concerns about demographic and stereotypical biases. This study presents a comprehensive analysis of the demographic composition and representational, stereotypical, and intersectional biases in LAION-2B-en and LAION-2B-multi, the two main components of the LAION-5B dataset. Using state-of-the-art models -- FairFace, DeepFace, and Emo-AffectNet -- we analyze faces detected in the dataset to identify biases across age, gender, race, and expressed emotion. Our findings reveal substantial overrepresentation of young adults (20--39), White individuals, and males, alongside consistent underrepresentation of minority racial groups and middle-aged or older women across both dataset components. We also observe stereotypical associations between demographic attributes and emotions, such as ``Anger'' being predominantly linked to males and ``Happiness'' to females, pointing to systemic imbalances in the data. The consistency of these patterns across two demographic models and both components of LAION-5B demonstrates that these biases are deeply embedded in one of the most widely-used training datasets. Given the scale at which LAION-5B is used to train generative models, these demographic imbalances could shape the behavior and outputs of numerous downstream AI systems.

</details>


### [248] [CFPO: Counterfactual Policy Optimization for Multimodal Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23206)
*Zhangyuan Yu,Wanran Sun,Guangjing Yang,Xiaohu Wu,Qicheng Lao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CFPO is a reinforcement learning framework for LVLMs that enforces causal consistency between visual and textual reasoning via cross-modal counterfactual enhancement, achieving 3.17%-6.25% gains over standard RL baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current RL paradigms for LVLMs lack counterfactual enhancement and causal learning mechanisms, leading to grounding failures where models ignore visual evidence or exhibit hallucination drift during long chain-of-thought reasoning.

Method: CFPO introduces a cross-modal counterfactual enhancement mechanism that regularizes the policy by maximizing the discrepancy between the model's predictions and those from a counterfactual state where critical visual cues are suppressed. It integrates with standard algorithms like GRPO and DAPO without requiring external reward models or additional supervision.

Result: CFPO achieves consistent gains of 3.17%-6.25% over standard RL baselines and 1.32%-2.13% over the state-of-the-art perception-aware method (PAPO).

Conclusion: CFPO significantly improves reasoning fidelity in LVLMs by enforcing causal consistency between visual perception and textual reasoning, addressing grounding failures and hallucination drift effectively.

Abstract: Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in multimodal reasoning. However, prevailing reinforcement learning (RL) paradigms lack explicit counterfactual enhancement and causal learning mechanisms. This fundamental deficiency results in severe grounding failures, manifesting as a tendency to ignore visual evidence in favor of language priors or exhibiting hallucination drift during long chain-of-thought reasoning. To address this root cause, we propose CounterFactual Policy Optimization (CFPO), a novel framework that enforces causal consistency between visual perception and textual reasoning. CFPO introduces a cross-modal counterfactual enhancement mechanism, which regularizes the policy by maximizing the discrepancy between the model's predictions and those from a counterfactual state where critical visual cues are suppressed. This approach seamlessly integrates with standard algorithms like GRPO and DAPO without requiring external reward models or additional supervision. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CFPO significantly improves reasoning fidelity, achieving consistent gains of 3.17%-6.25% over standard RL baselines and 1.32%-2.13% over the state-of-the-art perception-aware method (PAPO). Code is available at https://github.com/Raven-July/CFPO.

</details>


### [249] [Temporally Aware Densification for Dynamic 3D Gaussian Splatting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23212)
*Vikram Sandu,Mayurdeep Pathak,Rajiv Soundararajan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes Visibility-Aware Densification (VAD) for dynamic 3DGS, integrating temporal visibility, adaptive thresholding, and offset warping to improve dynamic region reconstruction quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing dynamic 3DGS methods use static densification strategies that neglect temporal behavior, causing under-reconstructed and blurry dynamic regions because short-lived Gaussians receive sparse supervision and fail to densify effectively.

Method: (1) Visibility-Aware Densification (VAD): integrates temporal visibility into densification so Gaussians are refined based on actual temporal presence. (2) Temporally-Adaptive Thresholding (TAT): adjusts each Gaussian's densification threshold according to its temporal lifespan. (3) Temporal Offset Warping (TOW): enhances deformation capacity around temporal centers to extend the lifespan of highly dynamic Gaussians.

Result: Achieves substantial improvements in visual quality of dynamic regions, outperforming existing methods across three dynamic multi-view benchmark datasets. VAD generalizes as a plug-and-play component across diverse dynamic 3DGS methods.

Conclusion: The proposed temporal-aware densification framework effectively addresses the static densification limitation in dynamic 3DGS, consistently improving dynamic reconstruction quality.

Abstract: Despite modeling temporal motion, dynamic 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) methods still inherit a static densification strategy that is ill-suited for dynamic scenes. This neglect of temporal behavior leads to under-reconstructed and blurry dynamic regions, as short-lived Gaussians receive sparse supervision and fail to densify effectively. We propose a Visibility-Aware Densification (VAD) framework that integrates temporal visibility into the densification process, ensuring that Gaussians are refined based on their actual temporal presence. A Temporally-Adaptive Thresholding (TAT) mechanism further adjusts each Gaussian's densification threshold according to its temporal lifespan, promoting balanced refinement of both static and dynamic regions. Finally, a Temporal Offset Warping (TOW) design enhances deformation capacity around temporal centers, extending the lifespan of highly dynamic Gaussians and facilitating more effective densification. Our approach achieves substantial improvements in the visual quality of dynamic regions, outperforming existing methods across three dynamic multi-view benchmark datasets. Moreover, the proposed VAD module generalizes across diverse dynamic 3DGS methods, consistently improving dynamic reconstruction as a plug-and-play component.

</details>


### [250] [RS-Gen: A Multi-Stage Agentic Framework for Reasoning and Search-Augmented Image Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23221)
*Feifei Bian,Zhimin Zheng,Wei Deng,Daiguo Zhou,Jian Luan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RS-Gen is a plug-and-play, training-free multi-stage image agentic framework that introduces a "Questioning-and-Solving" closed-loop mechanism to address ambiguous intentions, logical reasoning gaps, and OOD knowledge issues in image generation/editing, achieving SOTA results on WISE Verified and RISEBench benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing image generation and editing models struggle with ambiguous intentions, logical reasoning, and out-of-distribution (OOD) knowledge due to lack of deep reasoning capabilities and real-time external information. Even emerging unified understanding-and-generation models are constrained by their intrinsic parameter scales and static knowledge gaps.

Method: RS-Gen is a plug-and-play, training-free, multi-stage image agentic framework that employs a "Questioning-and-Solving" closed-loop mechanism to identify logical issues and knowledge gaps, then autonomously plans actions to bridge information deficits and execute deep logical reasoning.

Result: On WISE Verified benchmark, RS-Gen achieves 0.313 absolute gain for Qwen-Image; on RISEBench, it achieves 19.70 absolute gain for Qwen-Image-Edit-2511, elevating both to SOTA level among open-source models.

Conclusion: RS-Gen significantly expands the capability boundaries of foundational image generation and editing models through its agentic, plug-and-play design without requiring additional training.

Abstract: Recent years have witnessed remarkable progress in image generation and editing, particularly regarding instruction following and visual fidelity. However, when handling ambiguous intentions, logical reasoning, and Out-of-Distribution (OOD) knowledge, existing image models often yield sub-optimal results due to a lack of deep reasoning capabilities and real-time external information. Although emerging unified understanding-and-generation models attempt to bridge this gap, they remain constrained by their intrinsic parameter scales and static knowledge gaps. Inspired by agentic paradigms, we propose RS-Gen: a plug-and-play, training-free, multi-stage image agentic framework. RS-Gen innovatively introduces a "Questioning-and-Solving" closed-loop mechanism to accurately identify logical issues and knowledge gaps, autonomously planning actions to bridge information deficits and execute deep logical reasoning. Extensive experiments demonstrate that RS-Gen significantly expands the capability boundaries of foundational image generation and editing models. Specifically, on the WISE Verified and RISEBench benchmarks, RS-Gen yields substantial absolute performance gains of 0.313 for Qwen-Image and 19.70 for Qwen-Image-Edit-2511, respectively, successfully elevating both to the state-of-the-art (SOTA) level among open-source models.

</details>


### [251] [PhysFlow: Frequency Decoupled with Dual-Field Rectified Flow for Remote Photoplethysmography](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23226)
*Zixu Li,jianjun Qian,Hang Shao,Lei Luo,Jian Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PhysFlow is a frequency-decoupled dual-field rectified flow framework for robust rPPG estimation, which decomposes the ground-truth rPPG signal into trend and amplitude components and models them separately using component-specific conditional velocity fields, achieving state-of-the-art performance in heart-rate estimation and waveform reconstruction under complex disturbances.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current deep learning rPPG methods struggle under complex disturbances (varying illumination, facial expressions, head movements) because they model the rPPG signal in a unified manner, coupling different signal components and making weak pulse-related variations easily dominated by external interference.

Method: PhysFlow decomposes the ground-truth rPPG signal into trend and amplitude components as separate supervisory targets, then learns two component-specific conditional velocity fields to model these components separately based on extracted facial features, using a rectified flow formulation that enables efficient waveform reconstruction with few ODE integration steps.

Result: Extensive experiments on multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate that PhysFlow outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both heart-rate estimation and rPPG waveform reconstruction across diverse challenging scenarios.

Conclusion: Frequency-decoupled dual-field rectified flow improves rPPG robustness under complex disturbances by reducing mutual interference between different signal components, enabling efficient and reliable contactless pulse estimation.

Abstract: Remote Photoplethysmography (rPPG) enables contactless pulse estimation from facial videos, serving as a vital tool for health monitoring. However, current deep learning methods often struggle under complex disturbances, particularly varying illumination, facial expressions, and unconstrained head movements. In such scenarios, subtle physiological signals are easily dominated by external interference, making the recovered rPPG waveform unstable and unreliable. One important reason is that most existing methods directly model the rPPG signal in a unified manner, where different signal components are coupled during reconstruction. This makes it difficult to preserve weak pulse-related variations when strong disturbance-induced changes are present. To address this challenge, we propose PhysFlow, a frequency-decoupled dual-field rectified flow framework tailored for robust rPPG estimation. Specifically, the ground-truth rPPG signal is decomposed into trend and amplitude components, which are used as separate supervisory targets. Based on the extracted facial features, PhysFlow learns two component-specific conditional velocity fields to model the two components separately. This design reduces mutual interference between different components and improves the robustness of rPPG reconstruction under complex disturbances. Moreover, the rectified flow formulation enables efficient waveform reconstruction with only a few ordinary differential equation (ODE) integration steps. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate that PhysFlow outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both heart-rate estimation and rPPG waveform reconstruction across diverse challenging scenarios.

</details>


### [252] [Privacy-Preserving Person Re-Identification from Temporal Sequences with Transformer and Hungarian Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23230)
*Raphaël Delécluse,Hazem Wannous,Laurent Guimas*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a privacy-preserving person re-identification method using depth images (which obscure facial features) combined with a Transformer encoder architecture that processes temporal sequences of RGB and depth modalities, using the Hungarian algorithm for multi-view association and batch hard triplet loss for discriminative learning, achieving competitive performance on top-view datasets while prioritizing privacy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional RGB-based Re-ID methods raise privacy concerns and are highly sensitive to lighting variations and occlusion, motivating the need for a privacy-preserving approach.

Method: The method uses depth images for privacy preservation, applies the Hungarian algorithm for multi-view association via global cost minimization, employs a Transformer encoder architecture processing temporal sequences of RGB and depth modalities, and uses batch hard triplet loss for discriminative feature learning.

Result: Depth-only Re-ID achieves competitive performance compared to state-of-the-art methods on TVPR2, GODPR, and BIWI RGBD-ID datasets, measured by CMC and mAP metrics.

Conclusion: Depth-only re-identification can achieve competitive performance while prioritizing privacy preservation, offering a viable alternative to RGB-based methods.

Abstract: Person re-identification (Re-ID) is a crucial task in surveillance and human behavior analysis, often used in public spaces such as transport hubs. Traditional RGB-based Re-ID methods raise privacy concerns and are highly sensitive to lighting variations and occlusion. In this paper, we propose a novel Re-ID approach that leverages depth images, which inherently obscures facial and other identifiable features, making it a privacy-preserving solution. Our method addresses the association problem between multiple views of individuals by applying the Hungarian algorithm, optimizing the matching process through minimization of the global cost across the distance matrix. We further enhance the approach by introducing temporal sequences of frames as input to a Transformer encoder architecture, which exploits both RGB and depth modalities. This architecture captures dynamic movement patterns, improving feature extraction and re-identification accuracy. Additionally, we employ batch hard triplet loss to enhance discriminative feature learning by focusing on the hardest samples. We evaluate both depth-only and RGB-D models on several top-view datasets, including TVPR2, GODPR, and BIWI RGBD-ID. Our results demonstrate that depth-only re-identification can achieve competitive performance compared to state-of-the-art methods, as measured by standard metrics such as Cumulative Matching Characteristics (CMC) and Mean Average Precision (mAP), while prioritizing privacy preservation.

</details>


### [253] [SteerVTE: Seamless Video Text Editing with Style and Glyph Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23254)
*Kai Zeng,Moran Li,Zhengwei Wang,Yingchen Yu,Yiheng Lin,Ruichuan An,Ming Lu,Qi She,Wentao Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SteerVTE is a unified framework for video text editing that steers frozen video diffusion models using style and glyph control, achieving precise, temporally coherent text modifications through lightweight adapters, novel loss functions, and progressive training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Video text editing remains largely unexplored despite advances in image-domain text editing. It is a localized task demanding stroke-level precision within small text regions, compounding challenges of cross-frame accuracy, temporal coherence, and stylistic fidelity, and existing video foundation models inherently lack strong text rendering priors.

Method: Built on a frozen diffusion transformer, SteerVTE attaches a lightweight text context adapter with a style encoder (capturing original text visual attributes) and dual-granularity glyph encoders (line-level and character-level target text encoding). A glyph-aware spatial-focal loss and a three-stage progressive training curriculum (image→video) are used, along with an automatic synthesis pipeline to construct the SteerVTE-1M dataset.

Result: SteerVTE substantially outperforms existing video editing baselines across text accuracy, style consistency, and temporal coherence on the SteerVTE-1M dataset.

Conclusion: The proposed unified framework effectively addresses the unique challenges of video text editing by combining style and glyph control with progressive training, demonstrating significant improvements over prior methods.

Abstract: Visual text editing aims to precisely modify text in images and videos while preserving stylistic consistency and visual realism. Despite significant advances in the image domain, video text editing remains largely unexplored: it is a localized task demanding stroke-level precision within small text regions, which compounds the challenges of cross-frame accuracy, temporal coherence, and stylistic fidelity. We introduce SteerVTE, a unified framework that \underline{\textbf{steer}}s a frozen video diffusion model to perform precise \underline{\textbf{V}}ideo \underline{\textbf{T}}ext \underline{\textbf{E}}diting through style and glyph control. Built on a frozen diffusion transformer, SteerVTE attaches a lightweight text context adapter with two complementary modules: a style encoder capturing the original text's visual attributes, and dual-granularity glyph encoders encoding the target text at both the line and character levels. To overcome the inherently weak text rendering priors of video foundation models, we further propose a glyph-aware spatial-focal loss and a three-stage progressive training curriculum that scales from image to video data. To support large-scale training, we also develop an automatic synthesis pipeline and construct SteerVTE-1M, a dataset of one million triplets spanning diverse scenes, fonts, and stylistic effects. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SteerVTE substantially outperforms existing video editing baselines across text accuracy, style consistency, and temporal coherence.

</details>


### [254] [P-JEPA: Procedural Video Representation Learning via Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23256)
*Felix Tristram,Stefano Gasperini,Benjamin Killeen,Marcel Walch,Christian Benz,Nassir Navab,Ghazal Ghazaei*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: P-JEPA is a backbone-agnostic approach for learning long-duration procedural video representations by predicting pooled masked latent vectors in a dense, frame-aligned action space, enabling real-time understanding of videos over 30 minutes long with state-of-the-art results on EgoExo4D fine-grained action classification using an order of magnitude fewer parameters than LLM-based methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Procedural videos contain actions with long-range dependencies that existing video foundation models fail to capture due to the quadratic complexity of self-attention, and visually similar actions (e.g., turning stove on vs. off) appear at different procedural steps, requiring models to understand long-range temporal context.

Method: P-JEPA reduces long-duration video learning to a dense, frame-aligned action space and predicts pooled masked latent vectors, using a backbone-agnostic design that integrates with features from VJEPA2.1, TSM, and I3D.

Result: P-JEPA achieves state-of-the-art results on EgoExo4D fine-grained action classification, consistently improves linear separability, streaming inference, and temporal action segmentation on EgoExo4D, EgoProceL, and Assembly101, runs in real time, and uses an order of magnitude fewer parameters than LLM-based methods.

Conclusion: P-JEPA enables effective long-form understanding of procedural videos over 30 minutes long, achieving strong performance with high efficiency and real-time inference.

Abstract: The increasing maturity of embodied AI platforms has driven a growing interest in procedural video representation learning to support intelligent assistance systems for complex, multi-step tasks. Leveraging large-scale latent predictive training, video foundation models capture video dynamics, enabling downstream tasks such as activity understanding, spatiotemporal localization, and predictive control. However, procedural videos include actions with long-range dependencies that these models do not support, due to the quadratic complexity of self-attention. Distinct actions, for example, may be visually similar despite appearing at different points in the procedure, such as turning the stove on versus off. Here, we propose a backbone-agnostic approach that learns long-duration video representations by reducing the problem to a dense, frame-aligned action space and predicting pooled masked latent vectors. This approach allows our Procedural Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture (P-JEPA) to ingest videos over 30 minutes long, enabling effective long-form understanding of procedural steps. We evaluate P-JEPA using features extracted with VJEPA2.1, TSM, and I3D over the EgoExo4D, EgoProceL, and Assembly101 datasets, finding that it consistently improves linear separability, streaming inference, and temporal action segmentation performance, achieving state-of-the-art results on EgoExo4D fine-grained action classification while using an order of magnitude fewer parameters than LLM-based methods and running in real time.

</details>


### [255] [Safe Few-Step Generation via Velocity Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23267)
*Yujin Choi,Jaehong Yoon*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VESFlow and VESFlow+ are training-free safety methods for flow matching-based text-to-image generation that edit the velocity field to steer outputs away from unsafe content while preserving benign prompt quality, achieving low attack success rates (e.g., 6.3% on Ring-A-Bell, 6.8% on MMA-Diffusion) with few sampling steps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Flow matching-based T2I models lack effective safety/concept removal mechanisms because prior safety methods rely on iterative trajectory steering (constrained by few sampling steps) or CLIP-centric prompt embedding manipulation (ineffective with modern context-aware text encoders).

Method: VESFlow directly edits the velocity field via a safe-conditional posterior, leveraging that flow matching models learn marginal velocity. VESFlow+ adds risk score-based filtering to skip velocity editing for benign prompts and pushes the trajectory away from the unsafe direction in addition to steering toward safe outputs.

Result: VESFlow+ reduces attack success rate to 6.3% on Ring-A-Bell and 6.8% on MMA-Diffusion for the 4-step MeanFlow model (measured by NudeNet), while preserving fidelity on benign prompts.

Conclusion: The proposed training-free velocity editing approach effectively enables safety in flow matching-based T2I generation with extremely few sampling steps, overcoming limitations of prior iterative or embedding-based safety methods.

Abstract: Flow matching has recently emerged as a strong paradigm for state-of-the-art text-to-image (T2I) generation, enabling high-quality generation with a small number of sampling steps. As these models are increasingly integrated into real-world applications, ensuring safe and non-sensitive content generation has become a critical requirement. However, adapting safety and concept removal methods to this new generation framework remains an open challenge. Specifically, prior methods largely rely on iterative trajectory steering across a number of denoising steps or on CLIP-centric prompt embedding manipulation.
