These design assumptions pose fundamental bottlenecks for safety in flow matching-based T2I generation, where limited sampling steps constrain iterative correction and modern context-aware text encoders diminish the effectiveness of embedding-level interventions. In this paper, we propose VESFlow, a training-free safety method tailored to flow matching with extremely few sampling steps. Leveraging the fact that flow matching models learn the marginal velocity, we directly edit the velocity field via a safe-conditional posterior. VESFlow steers the trajectory toward safe outputs while leaving the conditioning prompt unchanged. Building on the observation that VESFlow leaves outputs unchanged under benign prompts, we further introduce a risk score-based filtering that bypasses velocity editing to reduce computational cost while preserving benign prompt generation. Based on this filtering, we propose VESFlow+, a stronger variant of VESFlow that not only edits the velocity toward the safe direction, but also pushes it away from the unsafe direction. Experimental results show that VESFlow+ removes the target concept, reducing the attack success rate by NudeNet to 6.3% on Ring-A-Bell and 6.8% on MMA-Diffusion on the 4-step MeanFlow model, while preserving fidelity on benign prompts.

</details>


### [256] [BoxCtrl: 3D-Aware Visual Prompting for Geometric Image Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23270)
*Feifei Wang,Shiyuan Yang,Xiaoyu Li,Jing Liao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: BoxCtrl introduces a 3D-aware visual prompting framework using RGB-coded 3D bounding boxes as visual prompts for precise geometric image editing (translation, rotation, scaling). It employs a two-stage training paradigm (SFT + RL) with synthetic data for SFT and unpaired real-world data for RL, achieving state-of-the-art geometric editing performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing instruction-based editing and multimodal models struggle with precise and consistent 3D geometric image editing (translating, scaling, rotating in 3D space). Text-only or coarse 2D-guided approaches cannot accurately encode complex geometric transformations.

Method: BoxCtrl uses RGB 3D bounding boxes projected onto 2D images as visual prompts, where each box's three orthogonal faces are painted with distinct RGB colors to encode position, size, and orientation compactly. This decouples geometric control from appearance control, allowing the model to learn consistent correspondences between faces of the same color in latent space. A two-stage training paradigm is employed: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) on a large-scale synthetic dataset for foundational editing, followed by Reinforcement Learning (RL) on unpaired real-world data guided by a reward function evaluating geometric accuracy and visual fidelity to bridge the synthetic-to-real domain gap.

Result: BoxCtrl achieves state-of-the-art performance across translation, rotation, scaling, and composite editing tasks, demonstrating significant improvements in geometric precision while maintaining photorealistic quality.

Conclusion: The well-designed RGB 3D bounding box visual prompts effectively decouple geometric from appearance control, and the SFT-RL training paradigm successfully addresses the challenge of precise 3D geometric image editing, outperforming existing methods.

Abstract: As instruction-based editing models and multimodal large language models advance, diverse image editing tasks have become feasible. However, achieving precise and consistent geometric image editing, such as translating, scaling, and rotating in 3D space, remains a major challenge. In this work, we introduce BoxCtrl, a 3D-aware visual prompting framework. Unlike text-only or coarse 2D-guided approaches, our method introduces informative RGB 3D bounding boxes projected onto 2D images as visual prompts. The three orthogonal faces of each box are painted with distinct RGB colors, simultaneously encoding position, size, and orientation to provide a compact, intuitive in-context visual example. The key to BoxCtrl's success lies in these well-designed bounding boxes, which decouple geometric control from appearance control. This enables the model to learn consistent correspondences between faces of the same color in the latent space, leading to a precise understanding of geometric intentions and accurate editing results. We introduce a two-stage training paradigm: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) followed by Reinforcement Learning (RL). To address paired data scarcity, we construct a large-scale synthetic dataset for SFT, equipping the model with fundamental editing capabilities. To bridge the synthetic-to-real domain gap, we incorporate an online RL stage leveraging unpaired real-world data. Guided by a reward function evaluating geometric accuracy and visual fidelity, our SFT-RL strategy significantly enhances geometric precision while maintaining photorealistic quality. Extensive experiments demonstrate that BoxCtrl achieves state-of-the-art performance across translation, rotation, scaling, and composite editing tasks.

</details>


### [257] [Transfer learning-based method for automated ewaste recycling in smart cities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23286)
*Nermeen Abou Baker,Paul Szabo-Müller,Uwe Handmann*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper applies transfer learning (fine-tuned AlexNet) to classify smartphones from 6 brands (12 classes) for automated e-waste recycling, achieving ~98% accuracy using SGD with Momentum at 3e-4 learning rate.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The growing e-waste stream requires automated, accurate sorting solutions beyond traditional methods, and AI-driven classification can enable circular smart cities by improving recycling efficiency.

Method: Transfer learning via fine-tuning AlexNet's output layers on a small dataset of 12 classes (6 smartphone brands), with learning rate tuning, optimizer selection (SGD with Momentum), and data augmentation to prevent overfitting.

Result: SGD with Momentum optimizer and 3e-4 learning rate achieved approximately 98% model accuracy with generalization.

Conclusion: Transfer learning is an effective scenario for automated e-waste classification, reducing sorting error rates and supporting circular smart city initiatives.

Abstract: Sorting a huge stream of waste accurately within a short period can be done with the support of digitalization, particularly Artificial Intelligence, instead of traditional methods. The overlap of Artificial Intelligence and Circular Economy can flourish many services in the environmental technology domain, in particular smart ewaste recycling, resulting in enabling circular smart cities. We analyse the growing need for automated ewaste recycling as an essential requirement to cope with the fast growing ewaste stream and we shed the light on the impact of Artificial Intelligence in supporting the recycling process through smart classification of devices, where the smartphone is our case study. Our study applies transfer learning as a special technique of Artificial Intelligence by finetuning the output layers of AlexNet as a pretrained model and perform the implementation on a small size dataset that contains 12 classes from 6 smartphone brands. We evaluate the performance of our model by tuning the learning rate, choosing the best optimizer, and augmenting the original dataset to avoid overfitting. We found that the optimizer of Stochastic Gradient Descent with Momentum and 3e-4 as a learning rate brings almost 98% model accuracy with generalization. Our study supports automated ewaste recycling in decreasing the error rate of ewaste sorting and investigates the advantages of applying transfer learning as the best scenario to overcome the rising challenges.

</details>


### [258] [Flow6D: Discrete-to-Continuous Flow Matching for Efficient and Accurate Category-Level 6D Pose Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23293)
*Mingyu Mei,Li Zhang,Zibo Dai,Han Sun,Xinyue Zhao,Huiliang Shen,Zaixing He*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Flow6D proposes a hierarchical flow matching framework for category-level 6D pose estimation that first discretizes pose parameters into bins for coarse localization via discrete flow matching, then regresses continuous pose residuals via continuous flow matching, achieving real-time performance at 70 FPS with state-of-the-art results on synthetic and real datasets, including articulated objects.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing category-level 6D pose estimation methods suffer from limited accuracy due to noise and local optima in high-dimensional continuous regression, and inefficient infinite-space search hindering real-time performance.

Method: Flow6D uses a two-stage strategy: (1) discrete flow matching first discretizes rotation and translation into bins to localize the latent space near the true pose, reducing search complexity; (2) continuous flow matching samples in the latent space to predict local pose residuals for refined, accurate regression.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art methods on synthetic and real datasets for both rigid and articulated objects, with real-time inference at 70 FPS.

Conclusion: The hierarchical flow matching framework effectively addresses accuracy and efficiency challenges in category-level 6D pose estimation, and naturally extends to articulated objects.

Abstract: 6D pose estimation is a key task in computer vision and embodied AI, widely used in robotic manipulation, augmented reality, etc. Existing methods directly regress in a high-dimensional continuous space, facing two key challenges in category-level pose estimation: limited accuracy due to noise and local optima, and inefficient search over an infinite space that hinders real-time performance. This paper proposes Flow6D, a hierarchical flow matching framework with a two-stage discrete latent space localization-continuous pose regression strategy. Rotation and translation parameters are first discretized into bins, with a discrete flow matching model locking the latent space around the true pose to reduce search complexity. Then, by sampling in the latent space, a continuous flow matching model predicts local pose residuals to optimize the estimate and regress to an accurate pose. The framework also naturally extends to articulated objects, outperforming state-of-the-art methods on synthetic and real datasets with real-time inference at 70 FPS. Project website: https://flow6d.github.io/.

</details>


### [259] [Ocean4D: Generative Underwater 4D Reconstruction via Medium-Aware Video Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23298)
*Yuqiang Huang,Yuxi Wang,Junyu Dong,Zhaoxiang Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Ocean4D proposes a generative framework for underwater 4D reconstruction that combines 4D geometrically consistent conditioning (4D-GCC) and a Medium-Aware Block for implicit denoising, achieving state-of-the-art performance on dynamic and static underwater benchmarks from monocular video.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing underwater reconstruction methods rely on in-air assumptions and near-static assumptions, failing to account for underwater absorption, backscatter, drifting particles, and dynamic distractors, leading to unstable geometry and inconsistent cross-view results.

Method: Ocean4D consists of two components: (1) 4D-GCC for constructing 4D geometrically consistent conditioning with improved cross-frame coverage, and (2) a Medium-Aware Block that performs implicit medium-aware denoising in the latent diffusion process to handle absorption and scattering. It takes monocular video and target cameras to generate videos along target trajectories.

Result: Extensive experiments on both dynamic and static underwater benchmarks demonstrate state-of-the-art performance on underwater reconstruction.

Conclusion: The proposed generative framework effectively addresses the challenges of underwater 4D reconstruction by jointly handling degraded light transport and dynamic water variations, outperforming existing methods.

Abstract: Underwater 4D reconstruction remains challenging due to the coupling between degraded light transport in participating media and dynamic water variations. Most existing Methods are developed under in-air assumptions and do not explicitly account for underwater absorption and backscatter. Additionally, near-static assumptions make these approaches sensitive to drifting particles and dynamic distractors , leading to unstable geometry and inconsistent cross-view results. To address these issues, we propose a generative framework for underwater 4D reconstruction, named Ocean4D, which is built on two complementary components. Specifically, 4D-GCC constructs 4D geometrically consistent conditioning with improved cross-frame coverage, while the Medium-Aware Block performs implicit medium-aware denoising in the latent diffusion process to stabilize underwater appearance under absorption and scattering. Given a monocular video and target cameras, our method generates videos along the target trajectories while preserving global structure and cross-view consistency. Extensive experiments on both dynamic and static underwater benchmarks demonstrate state-of-the-art performance on underwater reconstruction.

</details>


### [260] [VideoAgent: All-in-One Framework for Video Understanding and Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23327)
*Hengji Zhou,Lingxuan Huang,Jian Wang,Bing Zhou,Si Wu,Lianghao Xia,Chao Huang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VideoAgent is an all-in-one agentic framework for automated video editing that handles diverse operations and long-video coherence through shot planning, cross-modal retrieval, and multi-agent orchestration of 30+ specialized editors, achieving 87-95% success rates with 60% cost reduction and near-human quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing automated video editing systems are limited to short segments and domain-specific tasks, lacking ability to handle diverse video comprehension/editing operations and long-video understanding for coherent narrative creation.

Method: VideoAgent introduces two innovations: (1) automated video shot creation with shot planning agents for coherent narratives and cross-modal retrieval for aligned visual content; (2) multi-agent orchestration framework with 30+ specialized editing agents, intent parsing for filter tools, and textual-gradient graph optimization for assembling complex editing pipelines.

Result: VideoAgent achieves 87-95% orchestration success rates, reduces API costs by 60%, and human evaluation shows only 4% below human-created videos across six categories.

Conclusion: VideoAgent demonstrates superiority over existing multimodal LLMs and agentic systems, producing professional-quality content approaching human-level performance on the proposed VideoEdit benchmark and public datasets.

Abstract: Video editing has become essential in digital media creation, yet existing automated systems are restricted to short segment processing and domain-specific tasks. They face two critical limitations: i) inability to handle diverse video comprehension and editing operations, and ii) lack of long-video understanding for coherent narrative creation. We propose VideoAgent, an all-in-one agentic framework addressing these challenges through two key innovations. First, we develop automated video shot creation with shot planning agents for coherent narratives and cross-modal retrieval for aligned visual content. Second, we design a multi-agent orchestration framework integrating over thirty specialized editing agents. Intent parsing filters relevant tools while textual-gradient graph optimization assembles complex editing pipelines. Extensive experiments on our newly-proposed VideoEdit benchmark and public datasets demonstrate VideoAgent's superiority over existing multimodal LLMs and agentic systems. VideoAgent achieves 87-95% orchestration success rates while reducing API costs by 60%. Human evaluation across six video categories shows VideoAgent produces professional-quality content approaching human-level performance, with ratings only 4% below human-created videos. We release our code at https://github.com/HKUDS/VideoAgent.

</details>


### [261] [RT-DocLayout: Real-Time End-to-End Document Layout Analysis with Reading Order in the Wild](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23344)
*Cheng Cui,Tingquan Gao,Xueqing Wang,Changda Zhou,Hongen Liu,Ting Sun,Yubo Zhang,Zelun Zhang,Jiaxuan Liu,Manhui Lin,Yue Zhang,Suyin Liang,Yiqing Xiang,Yi Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RT-DocLayout is a 33M-parameter end-to-end framework for document layout analysis that unifies classification, detection, segmentation, and reading order prediction within a single query-based decoder (built on RT-DETR), achieving SOTA performance at 132.1 FPS and improving downstream OCR-based document reconstruction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing document layout analysis methods rely on fragmented multi-stage pipelines or computationally heavy generative Transformers, leading to error propagation and limited efficiency. The intricate coupling among heterogeneous layout elements, geometric distortions, and reading order further complicates the task.

Method: RT-DocLayout is built upon RT-DETR with a unified multi-task formulation within a single query-based decoder. It jointly performs classification, bounding box regression, mask generation, and reading order relationship construction in one 33M-parameter architecture, with multi-task optimization to learn geometric and structural representations simultaneously.

Result: State-of-the-art performance on public benchmarks for document layout analysis, real-time inference speed of 132.1 FPS, and significant improvement in full-document reconstruction quality when coupled with downstream OCR engines.

Conclusion: RT-DocLayout provides a scalable and practical end-to-end solution for document layout analysis, serving as an efficient front-end for real-world document intelligence systems.

Abstract: Accurate document layout analysis remains a critical bottleneck for document parsing systems, due to the intricate coupling among heterogeneous document layout elements, geometric distortions (\eg, paper warping and bending, perspective variations), and reading order within diverse layout structures. Existing approaches typically rely on fragmented multi-stage pipelines or computationally heavy generative Transformer architectures, leading to error propagation and limited efficiency.
  In this paper, we present RT-DocLayout, a highly efficient end-to-end framework for document layout analysis, designed as a front-end for document parsing tasks. The proposed model unifies classification, detection, pixel-level segmentation, and reading order prediction for layout elements within a single 33M-parameter architecture. Built upon the RT-DETR, our key contribution is a unified multi-task formulation within a single query-based decoder that simultaneously classifies, regresses bounding box, generates masks, and constructs relationship to reason reading order.
  By jointly learning geometric and structural representations, RT-DocLayout introduces multi-task optimization that substantially improves robustness under real-world document distortions. Extensive experiments on public benchmarks demonstrate state-of-the-art performance in document layout analysis while maintaining real-time inference speed(132.1 FPS). When coupled with downstream OCR engines, RT-DocLayout significantly improves full-document reconstruction quality, providing a scalable and practical foundation for real-world document intelligence systems.

</details>


### [262] [Faithful Grounded Visual Reasoning via Learned Proxy-Tokens](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23354)
*Tom Hodemon,Mohamed Chaouch,Aboubacar Tuo,Angelique Loesch*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Composer introduces proxy-token-based visual grounding for MLLMs, achieving performance parity with coordinate-based methods while improving grounding accuracy by +9.0 points.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MLLMs achieve success in VQA but their black-box nature hinders deployment in critical domains. Existing GVR approaches use textual coordinates for visual grounding, which lack learnable semantic links to visual features, causing a semantic-spatial gap and hallucinated coordinates.

Method: Composer uses learned proxy-tokens as discrete symbolic pointers that explicitly index the image latent space, enabling the model to manipulate visual regions as addressable, semantically manipulable sets. The authors also constructed ComposerGCoT, a synthesized dataset for holistic assessment of reasoning consistency and grounding accuracy.

Result: Composer achieves performance parity with coordinate-based counterparts in final answer accuracy, while improving visual grounding accuracy by +9.0 points.

Conclusion: Discrete proxy-tokens capture spatial semantics more effectively than typical textual coordinates, and visual grounding mechanisms with learnable semantic links represent a promising path toward trustworthy and reliable MLLMs.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have achieved remarkable success in Visual Question Answering (VQA), yet their "black-box" nature hinders deployment in critical domains. Grounded Visual Reasoning (GVR) approaches attempt to improve interpretability by explicitly couple textual rationales with visual grounding information, which are typically textual coordinates. This mechanism lacks a learnable semantic link to the visual features, often resulting in a semantic-spatial gap where the model hallucinates coordinates that do not correspond to image evidences. In this work, we introduce Composer, a MLLM that leverages a novel visual grounding mechanism based on learned proxy-tokens to promote faithful interpretability. These discrete symbolic pointers explicitly index the image latent space, allowing the model to manipulate visual regions as addressable, semantically manipulable sets. To rigorously validate our novel grounding mechanism, we constructed ComposerGCoT, a dataset synthesized to enable holistic assessment of reasoning consistency and grounding accuracy. Experimental results indicate that Composer achieves performance parity with its coordinate-based counterpart in final answer accuracy, while improving visual grounding accuracy by +9.0 points. By demonstrating that discrete proxy-tokens capture spatial semantics more effectively than typical textual coordinates, we establish that visual grounding mechanisms with learnable semantic links represent a promising path toward trustworthy and reliable MLLMs.

</details>


### [263] [Changing Modalities: Adapting Remote Sensing Models to New Satellites and Sensors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23356)
*Tim G. Zhou,Anthony Fuller,Geoff Pleiss,Evan Shelhamer*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DeluluNet is a modular neural architecture that enables remote sensing models to handle changing input modalities (substitution, addition, or subset) via modality hallucination from a unimodal teacher using unlabeled multimodal data, eliminating the need for full retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Remote sensing models are designed for static modality sets, but satellite sensors change over time (new sensors added, old ones retired), requiring practical ways to deploy existing models on different modality configurations without extensive retraining or relabeling.

Method: DeluluNet uses modular components trained end-to-end via modality hallucination: given unlabeled multimodal data, it learns to predict missing modality representations from available ones, guided by a unimodal teacher model, enabling operation under modality substitution, addition, and subset scenarios.

Result: DeluluNet can maintain predictions when input modalities change across three scenarios (Modality Transfer, Addition, Peeking), providing a practical alternative to full re-labeling and re-training.

Conclusion: DeluluNet offers a scalable and practical solution for evolving sensor ecosystems, allowing deployed models to adapt to modality changes with minimal overhead, which is critical for real-world remote sensing applications.

Abstract: Machine learning models for remote sensing are trained and deployed on a static set of modalities. However, as we equip newer satellites with novel sensors and retire old ones, practitioners may wish to deploy an existing model on a substitution, superset, or subset of modalities with minimal retraining given data availability or practical computational constraints. We study the setting of updating existing models to changing modalities and identify three main scenarios: Modality Transfer (substitution), Addition (superset), and Peeking (subset). We propose DeluluNet, an architecture with modular components for all three changing modality scenarios. DeluluNet is trained end-to-end, learning a multi-modal model from a unimodal teacher and unlabeled multimodal data via modality hallucination--predicting missing modality representations from those that are present. As a result, DeluluNet can keep predicting even when input modalities change, providing a practical alternative to re-labeling and re-training in a changing world.

</details>


### [264] [Polynomial Dice Loss for Medical Image Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23373)
*Hiroaki Aizawa*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes Polynomial Dice Loss, a Taylor-expansion-based extension of Dice Loss that adjusts higher-order component contributions to improve medical image segmentation, particularly for data imbalance and small lesion detection. It achieves competitive performance across multiple segmentation settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Medical image segmentation suffers from data imbalance and small lesion detection challenges. Existing Dice Loss mitigates these issues but has limited flexibility in controlling loss shape contributions.

Method: The authors propose Polynomial Dice Loss by applying Taylor expansion to Dice Loss, formulating it as a polynomial representation that allows adjustable weighting of higher-order components to control the loss function shape.

Result: The polynomial formulation provides a simple way to control loss shape and achieves competitive performance across multiple segmentation settings compared to conventional Dice and Tversky coefficient-based losses.

Conclusion: Polynomial Dice Loss offers an effective and flexible extension of Dice Loss for medical image segmentation, particularly advantageous for handling data imbalance and small lesions.

Abstract: Medical image segmentation is a fundamental task for medical image processing and computer-assisted intervention, yet data imbalance and small lesion detection pose significant challenges. Dice Loss, which measures the overlap between predicted and ground truth regions, is widely used to mitigate these issues. To further emphasize its properties, we propose Polynomial Dice Loss, a polynomial extension of Dice Loss. Specifically, by leveraging the geometric characteristics of Dice Loss and formulating the loss function as a polynomial representation via Taylor expansion, we enable the adjustment of the contribution of higher-order components to the loss function. In our experiments, we evaluate the proposed method against loss functions derived from conventional Dice and Tversky coefficients. Experimental results and further analysis show that the polynomial formulation provides a simple way to control the loss shape and achieves competitive performance across multiple segmentation settings.

</details>


### [265] [Rethinking Object-Centric Representations for Video Dynamics Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23436)
*Amaury Wei,Ismail Nejjar,Olga Fink*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: STAITUS introduces appearance-pose disentanglement in slot-based video object tracking to resolve conflicts between temporal consistency and object motion, achieving sharper masks and more stable tracking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing slot-based video object tracking methods enforce temporal consistency on entangled appearance-pose embeddings, causing slots to lock onto static backgrounds and fragment foreground objects under motion or viewpoint changes.

Method: STAITUS disentangles each slot into appearance and geometric pose (position/scale), applies temporal alignment only in appearance space, enforces within-frame spatial separation, and uses an adaptive gating mechanism to dynamically adjust the number of active slots.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in segmentation quality and tracking stability on synthetic and real-world benchmarks.

Conclusion: Disentangling appearance from pose in slot representations resolves the consistency-motion conflict, enabling persistent object tracking without over-segmentation.

Abstract: Unsupervised video object tracking aims to decompose dynamic scenes into persistent, object-centric entities without manual annotations. Many recent approaches rely on slot-based representations, where a fixed set of latent variables ("slots") represent individual objects across frames. To preserve object identity, these models enforce temporal consistency on slot embeddings. However, when appearance and pose are entangled, this consistency objective conflicts with object motion and viewpoint changes. As a result, slots tend to lock onto static regions (e.g., background) to satisfy the consistency objective, while foreground objects become fragmented across multiple slots or frequently swap identities. To address these limitations, we propose STAITUS, a unified framework that explicitly disentangles each slot into appearance and geometric pose (position/scale). Leveraging this disentanglement, STAITUS enforces within-frame spatial separation and applies temporal alignment only in appearance space, yielding sharper masks and more persistent identities under motion, occlusion, and object entry/exit. Furthermore, to mitigate over-segmentation, we introduce an adaptive gating mechanism that dynamically adjusts the number of active slots to match scene complexity. Extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world benchmarks demonstrate that STAITUS substantially outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in segmentation quality and tracking stability.

</details>


### [266] [MeGAS: Thermomechanical Dynamic Gaussian Splatting for Thermophysical Scene Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23455)
*Zesong Yang,Yuanhang Lei,Liyuan Cui,Yihang Chen,Jiaer Huang,Boming Zhao,Peter Yichen Chen,Hujun Bao,Zhaopeng Cui*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MeGAS integrates thermomechanical phase-change dynamics (melting, solidification) into 3D Gaussian Splatting using temperature attributes, heat advection-diffusion, MPM dynamics, and topology-adaptive rendering for physically plausible photorealistic synthesis of thermophysical phenomena.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing physics-integrated neural rendering frameworks only model mechanically driven dynamics, ignoring temperature—a fundamental factor behind thermomechanical processes like melting and solidification.

Method: (1) Thermomechanical dynamic Gaussian Splatting representation augmenting 3DGS with temperature attributes; (2) Heat advection-diffusion solver with MPM dynamics incorporating phase transitions; (3) Topology-adaptive Gaussian rendering strategy to handle cracking and floaters under extreme deformation.

Result: MeGAS produces physically consistent thermomechanical behavior while maintaining high-fidelity photorealistic rendering, as demonstrated by extensive experiments.

Conclusion: MeGAS advances toward physics-integrated world models by bridging thermophysical phenomena with neural rendering, enabling plausible synthesis of phase-change processes.

Abstract: Recent advances integrate physically grounded Newtonian dynamics with neural rendering frameworks, narrowing the gap between photorealistic scene reconstruction and physics-based animation. However, existing approaches focus on mechanically driven dynamics while neglecting temperature, a fundamental yet invisible physical factor underlying phenomena such as melting, solidification, and other thermomechanical processes. In this paper, we propose MeGAS, a novel framework that incorporates thermomechanical phase-change dynamics into 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS). Specifically, we propose a new thermomechanical dynamic Gaussian Splatting representation that augments 3DGS with temperature attributes and employs a heat advection-diffusion solver with MPM dynamics incorporating phase transitions, enabling physically plausible and visually realistic synthesis of thermophysical phenomena. Furthermore, a new topology-adaptive Gaussian rendering strategy is proposed to mitigate cracking and floaters under extreme deformation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MeGAS produces physically consistent thermomechanical behavior while maintaining high-fidelity photorealistic rendering, advancing toward physics-integrated world models.

</details>


### [267] [C^2GR: Coupled Comprehensive Generative Replay for a Continually Learnable Universal Segmentation Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23473)
*Wei Li,Jingyang Zhang,Guoan Wang,Junzhi Ning,Yang Chen,Guang Yang,Lixu Gu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: C²GR is a Coupled Comprehensive Generative Replay framework for Task-Incremental Learning in universal segmentation models that uses Bayesian Joint Diffusion and Relation-aware Unified Prompt Synchronization to mitigate catastrophic forgetting under concurrent appearance and objective shifts across sequential medical tasks, achieving only a 2.44% performance drop compared to joint training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Universal segmentation models face catastrophic forgetting when trained sequentially on tasks from different medical departments, where consecutive tasks exhibit concurrent shifts in both image appearance and segmentation objective. Existing methods fail to address this dual-shift problem effectively.

Method: Proposes C²GR framework with two key components: (1) Bayesian Joint Diffusion (BJD) that formulates image-mask correspondence as conditional distributions optimized via conditional denoising for structure-realistic generation; (2) Relation-aware Unified Prompt Synchronization (RUPS) that simultaneously modulates the generator and segmentor via a shared task-relation-aware prompt to synchronize their optimization.

Result: Experiments on 20 tasks across diverse modalities and objectives show C²GR achieves only a 2.44% drop in overall performance compared to joint training, effectively alleviating forgetting from concurrent appearance and objective shifts.

Conclusion: C²GR effectively addresses catastrophic forgetting in task-incremental learning for universal segmentation by jointly generating realistic image-mask pairs of past tasks and synchronizing generator-segmentor optimization, providing a privacy-preserving continual learning solution for medical imaging.

Abstract: Universal segmentation models exhibit significant potential for diverse tasks involving different imaging modalities and segmentation objectives. Task-Incremental Learning provides a privacy-preserving approach to continually evolve a universal model on tasks from sequentially-arriving medical departments. However, training the model solely on the incoming task induces forgetting on past tasks, since consecutive tasks exhibit concurrent shifts in image appearance and segmentation objective. To address this problem, we propose a novel Coupled Comprehensive Generative Replay (C^2GR) framework that simultaneously synthesizes image-mask pairs of previous tasks to mitigate forgetting under concurrent appearance and objective shifts. This requires preserving image-mask correspondence for structure-realistic generation and bridging asynchronous optimization of the generator and segmentor for segmentation-oriented generation. Specifically, we propose a Bayesian Joint Diffusion (BJD) method that formulates the correspondence as conditional distributions optimized via conditional denoising. Furthermore, we develop a Relation-aware Unified Prompt Synchronization (RUPS) scheme to simultaneously modulate the generator and segmentor via a shared task-relation-aware prompt for synchronizing their optimization. Experiments on 20 tasks spanning diverse modalities and objectives demonstrate that C^2GR exhibits only a 2.44% drop in overall performance compared to joint training with all task data, effectively alleviating forgetting from the concurrent shifts. Our code will be made publicly available at https://github.com/mar-cry/C2GR.

</details>


### [268] [From Reconstruction to Decision: A Post-Encoder Plug-in Adapter for Curvilinear Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23486)
*Qin Lei,Jiang Zhong,Xin Xiao,Yuming Yang,Hao Wu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PEPA is a lightweight post-encoder plug-in adapter for curvilinear object segmentation that addresses reconstruction and binarization bottlenecks via Target-Conditioned Snake Upsampling (TCSU) and Target-Adaptive Differentiable Thresholding (TADT), improving topological connectivity with minimal parameters (~0.26M).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Curvilinear object segmentation (e.g., vessels, cracks) suffers from spatial sparsity and topological fragility where small errors cause structural disconnections. Modern pipelines rely on frozen foundation encoders with heavy downsampling that limits fine structural recovery, motivating a post-encoder solution that addresses both reconstruction and decision bottlenecks.

Method: PEPA couples two components: (i) Target-Conditioned Snake Upsampling (TCSU) for recovering thin/tortuous structures via target-conditioned continuous snake-like sampling, and (ii) Target-Adaptive Differentiable Thresholding (TADT) for predicting target-specific thresholds with a soft-threshold surrogate that prevents trivial bias shifting. It operates at the post-encoder stage and works with both prompt-based decoders and dense predictors.

Result: On five medical and industrial benchmarks, PEPA attached to frozen-encoder baselines yields consistent improvements, with topological connectivity gains (clDice) typically exceeding region overlap gains (IoU). It adds only ~0.26M parameters.

Conclusion: PEPA offers a practical, lightweight post-encoder enhancement for structure-centric segmentation that improves structural continuity without modifying the foundation encoder.

Abstract: Curvilinear object segmentation, including vessels and cracks, is challenging due to extreme spatial sparsity and topological fragility, where small local errors can cause severe structural disconnections. Meanwhile, modern segmentation pipelines increasingly rely on strong but hard-to-modify foundation encoders whose heavy downsampling limits fine structural recovery. Motivated by this, we focus on the post-encoder stage and study two recurring and actionable failure modes: a reconstruction bottleneck in high-resolution feature restoration and a decision bottleneck in binarization. We present PEPA, a lightweight Post-Encoder Plug-in Adapter for 2D curvilinear segmentation pipelines with accessible decoder/head features and target, query, or class descriptors. PEPA couples (i) Target-Conditioned Snake Upsampling (TCSU), which uses target-conditioned continuous snake-like sampling to better recover thin and tortuous structures during upsampling, and (ii) Target-Adaptive Differentiable Thresholding (TADT), which predicts target-specific thresholds and optimizes a soft-threshold surrogate with explicit safeguards against trivial bias shifting. Under this post-encoder interface, PEPA can be attached to both prompt-based decoders and conventional dense predictors. Experiments on five medical and industrial benchmarks show that adding PEPA to frozen-encoder baselines yields consistent improvements, with gains in topological connectivity (clDice) typically exceeding those in region overlap (IoU), indicating improved structural continuity. With only $\sim$0.26M additional parameters, PEPA offers a practical post-encoder enhancement for structure-centric segmentation.

</details>


### [269] [Brain-Adapter: A Dual-Stream Vision-Language MIL Framework for Comprehensive 3D CT Diagnosis of Acute Intracranial Pathologies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23494)
*Zhenyu Yi,Zhiyun Song,Yusong Sun,Zelin Liu,Manman Fei,Zhenhao Li,Jiaxuan Zhao,Xu Han,Lichi Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Brain-Adapter is a dual-stream MIL framework that adapts 2D biomedical VLMs to 3D brain CT diagnosis using raw diagnostic reports, achieving SOTA performance without dense annotations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Automated 3D brain CT diagnosis is critical yet challenging due to reliance on manual annotations and limited semantic understanding of conventional models. Transferring 2D foundation VLMs to 3D volumes remains an open problem.

Method: Brain-Adapter proposes a dual-stream MIL framework with: (1) Text-Conditioned Attention (TCA) that uses raw diagnostic sentences as semantic queries to align visual cues with disease concepts; (2) a parallel visual MIL stream capturing global scan characteristics supervised by LLM-extracted structured labels; (3) a consistency constraint for representation coherence; and (4) an Uncertainty-Aware Refinement (UAR) module for dynamic calibration and fusion of predictions during inference.

Result: Brain-Adapter significantly outperforms state-of-the-art 3D models and standard MIL approaches on multi-label classification of 3D brain CT scans.

Conclusion: The method eliminates reliance on dense annotations, providing a highly scalable and clinically viable solution for 3D acute intracranial pathology analysis.

Abstract: Automated diagnosis of 3D brain CT scans is essential for critical care, yet it remains challenging due to the heavy reliance on manual annotations and the limited semantic understanding of conventional models. While 2D foundation vision-language models (VLMs) have shown remarkable generalization, effectively transferring their representational power to 3D volumes remains an open problem. In this paper, we propose Brain-Adapter, a novel dual-stream multiple instance learning (MIL) framework that leverages pre-trained 2D biomedical VLMs and raw diagnostic reports for robust scan-level multi-label classification. Specifically, we introduce a Text-Conditioned Attention (TCA) mechanism, utilizing raw diagnostic sentences as semantic queries to dynamically align visual cues with specific disease concepts. Concurrently, a parallel visual MIL stream captures global scan characteristics, supervised by structured labels extracted via a Large Language Model (LLM). To ensure representation coherence, a consistency constraint enforces synergy between the two streams. During inference, an Uncertainty-Aware Refinement (UAR) module dynamically calibrates and fuses these dual-stream predictions to resolve ambiguous cases. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms state-of-the-art 3D models and standard MIL approaches. By eliminating the reliance on dense annotations, Brain-Adapter provides a highly scalable and clinically viable solution for 3D acute intracranial pathology analysis.

</details>


### [270] [UniverSat: Resolution- and Modality-Agnostic Transformers for Earth Observation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23503)
*Yohann Perron,Guillaume Astruc,Nicolas Gonthier,Clement Mallet,Loic Landrieu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: UniverSat introduces a Universal Patch Encoder for Vision Transformers that handles arbitrary spatial, spectral, and temporal resolutions across optical and non-optical sensors, enabling training on heterogeneous Earth Observation data via self-supervision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vision Transformers rely on rigid patch projectors that fail to generalize across the diverse input modalities, scales, and resolutions found in Earth Observation data.

Method: A Universal Patch Encoder that maps patches from arbitrary spatial, spectral, and temporal resolutions (from both optical and non-optical sensors) into a shared embedding space using a shared set of weights, enabling self-supervised training on heterogeneous multimodal corpora.

Result: Strong performance on classification and segmentation tasks across GeoBench, PANGEABench, and SpectralEarth benchmarks.

Conclusion: The approach yields robust, sensor-agnostic spatial features, enabling a single model to handle diverse EO data without rigid patch projectors.

Abstract: Vision Transformers (ViT) dominate computer vision. However, their reliance on rigid patch projectors hinders transfer to Earth Observation (EO), where input modalities, scales, and resolutions vary widely. We introduce UniverSat, a ViT-style backbone built around a Universal Patch Encoder that maps patches from arbitrary spatial, spectral, and temporal resolutions, and from both optical and non-optical sensors, into a shared embedding space with a shared set of weights. This enables training a single model on heterogeneous multimodal corpora via self-supervision, yielding robust, sensor-agnostic spatial features. We validate this approach with strong results across classification and segmentation on standard EO benchmarks from GeoBench, PANGEABench, and SpectralEarth. Our code and models are available at https://github.com/gastruc/UniverSat.

</details>


### [271] [Scaling State-Space Models from Lines to Paragraphs: An Ablation of Mamba-based OCR](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23524)
*Merveilles Agbeti-Messan,Pierrick Tranouez,Stéphane Nicolas,Clément Chatelain,Thierry Paquet*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper investigates Mamba-based SSMs for OCR, finding they rival Transformers on clean synthetic paragraphs (both <1% CER) with 1.4-4.5× speedup, but underperform on real handwriting (8.2% vs 4.2% on IAM lines, 10.0% vs 3.5% on IAM paragraphs), with the gap largely attributed to data scarcity rather than architectural limitations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While SSMs like Mamba offer linear-time decoding and match Transformer accuracy on printed historical lines, their behavior on longer sequences (paragraphs) and generalization to handwriting remain poorly understood.

Method: Systematic exploration of four core hyperparameters (decoder depth, state dimension, expansion factor, connector depth) on synthetic paragraphs (100-1,000 chars); controlled comparison against Transformer baseline under identical protocol on both synthetic and real handwritten data (IAM lines and paragraphs).

Result: On clean synthetic paragraphs, both models achieve <1% CER at all lengths, with SSM running 1.4-4.5× faster. On real handwriting, SSM underperforms: 8.2% vs 4.2% CER (IAM lines) and 10.0% vs 3.5% CER (IAM paragraphs). Data scarcity accounts for a substantial part of the gap.

Conclusion: SSMs are practically viable for large-scale document transcription on clean, synthetic-like data with significant speed advantages, but they are markedly data-hungry on long handwritten sequences, where Transformers still dominate.

Abstract: End-to-end OCR increasingly relies on autoregressive sequence models, where the quadratic cost of Transformer attention limits efficient transcription of long, paragraph-level text. State-Space Models (SSMs) such as Mamba offer linear-time decoding and have recently been shown to match Transformer accuracy on printed historical lines, but their behavior as sequences grow from short lines to full paragraphs, and their generalization to handwriting, remain poorly understood. We study how a Mamba-based OCR recognizer scales from lines to paragraphs. We first conduct a systematic exploration of its four core hyperparameters (decoder depth, state dimension, expansion factor, and connector depth) on synthetic paragraphs from 100 to 1,000 characters, identifying the recurrent state dimension and the expansion factor as the dominant levers for long-sequence accuracy. We then compare the recognizer against a Transformer baseline trained under an identical protocol. On clean synthetic paragraphs, both models stay below 1% CER at every length while the SSM runs 1.4 to 4.5 times faster, the speedup growing with sequence length. On real handwriting, however, the SSM lags clearly behind: it reaches 8.2% CER on IAM lines and 10.0% on IAM paragraphs, against 4.2% and 3.5% for the Transformer baseline. Through controlled experiments we show that a substantial part of this gap stems from data scarcity rather than from an intrinsic architectural limit: the autoregressive SSM decoder is markedly data-hungry on long sequences. Our study clarifies when SSMs are a practical choice for large-scale document transcription and when they are not.

</details>


### [272] [LightSTAR: Efficient Visual Document Retrieval via Lightweight Selection with Vision-Adaptive Refinement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23539)
*Tongkun Guan,Haocheng Wang,Wei Shen,Xiaokang Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LightSTAR is an efficient visual document retrieval framework that decomposes retrieval into LLM-free visual selection (keyword-anchored candidate narrowing) and vision-adaptive semantic refinement (fine-grained matching on top candidates), achieving SOTA accuracy with several-fold latency reduction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing MLLM-based visual document retrieval methods suffer from prohibitive computational costs by applying intensive MLLM encoding to every page. The observation that user queries are typically keyword-anchored offers an opportunity for efficient candidate narrowing.

Method: Two-stage framework: 1) LLM-free Visual Selection uses content-grounded query encoding focusing on informative words and LLM-free visual embeddings to produce a high-recall candidate set; 2) Vision-adaptive Semantic Refinement performs fine-grained semantic matching on top candidates via adaptive region-wise feature fusion combining textual and layout cues, optimized via hardness-aware contrastive objective.

Result: LightSTAR achieves state-of-the-art retrieval accuracy while reducing end-to-end latency by several-fold, offering a practical solution to the accuracy-efficiency trade-off.

Conclusion: The proposed decomposition strategy effectively balances accuracy and efficiency in visual document retrieval, providing a highly practical solution validated by SOTA results and significant latency reduction.

Abstract: Visual document retrieval requires rapidly locating relevant pages from large multi-modal corpora in response to user queries. While recent methods powered by Multi-modal Large Language Models (MLLMs) show competitive accuracy, they suffer from prohibitive computational costs by applying intensive MLLM encoding to every single page. Meanwhile, we observe that user queries are typically keyword-anchored, containing semantically rich words that are expected to appear directly in the visible text of relevant pages, offering an efficient cue for quickly narrowing down candidate pages. Building on this insight, we propose LightSTAR, an efficient framework that decomposes visual document retrieval into: 1) LLM-free Visual Selection, which utilizes content-grounded query encoding to focus on informative words and employs LLM-free visual embeddings to produce a high-recall candidate set; and 2) Vision-adaptive Semantic Refinement, which further performs fine-grained semantic matching exclusively on these top candidates via adaptive region-wise feature fusion to effectively combine textual and layout cues, optimized through a hardness-aware contrastive objective. Experimental results demonstrate that LightSTAR achieves state-of-the-art retrieval accuracy while reducing end-to-end latency by several-fold, offering a highly practical solution to the accuracy-efficiency trade-off in visual document retrieval. Code is available at https://github.com/bokufa/LightSTAR.

</details>


### [273] [Dense Reward for Multi-View 3D Reasoning with Global Maps and Local Views](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23557)
*Jiho Choi,Seonho Lee,Seojeong Park,Hyunjung Shim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DR-MV3D introduces dense, verifiable rewards to supervise multi-view 3D VQA reasoning by decomposing the task into map construction, view-trajectory planning, and egocentric grounding, using global consistency and local trajectory rewards optimized via GRPO, achieving consistent improvements over baselines on MindCube, VSI-Bench, and BLINK (MV).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current multimodal LLMs trained with sparse, answer-level supervision yield inconsistent cross-view reasoning and brittle view selection in MV3D-VQA.

Method: Decomposes MV3D-VQA into three steps: allocentric global map construction, question-conditioned view-trajectory planning, and egocentric grounding. Uses two dense rewards (global consistency reward from frozen 3D vision models like VGGT+SAM3, local trajectory reward) and optimizes via trajectory-level policy optimization (GRPO).

Result: Consistently improves over strong multi-image baselines on MindCube, VSI-Bench, and BLINK (MV).

Conclusion: Process-level dense supervision is effective for multi-view 3D reasoning.

Abstract: Multi-view 3D Visual Question Answering (MV3D-VQA) requires integrating partial observations into a coherent 3D scene representation and selecting informative viewpoints for multi-step spatial reasoning. However, current multimodal LLMs are typically trained with sparse, answer-level supervision, which often yields inconsistent cross-view reasoning and brittle view selection. We present DR-MV3D (Dense Reward for MV3D-VQA), a map-grounded learning framework that provides dense, verifiable rewards to supervise the reasoning process. Our approach decomposes MV3D-VQA into (i) allocentric global map construction, (ii) question-conditioned view-trajectory planning, and (iii) egocentric grounding for answer prediction. To make intermediate steps learnable without manual annotations, we introduce two rewards: a global consistency reward that aligns the predicted map with geometry-consistent pseudo targets from frozen 3D vision foundation models (e.g., VGGT + SAM3), and a local trajectory reward that supervises ordered viewpoint selection. We optimize the full pipeline with trajectory-level policy optimization (GRPO). Experiments on MindCube, VSI-Bench, and BLINK (MV) show that DR-MV3D consistently improves over strong multi-image baselines, supporting the effectiveness of process-level dense supervision for multi-view 3D reasoning.

</details>


### [274] [Polycepta: Object-Centric Appearance Estimation for Multi-Object Tracking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23604)
*Mohamed Nagy,Naoufel Werghi,Jorge Dias,Majid Khonji*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Polycepta introduces an object-centric appearance state estimation framework that treats appearance modeling as a recursive estimation problem, progressively refining appearance representations over time. It achieves 90.57 Hz inference speed and 92.27% MOTA on KITTI, reducing identity switches across multiple benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional MOT systems using tracking-by-detection rely on static, frame-independent appearance descriptors that lack robustness. Real-time systems often abandon appearance cues entirely due to computational cost, relying solely on motion and geometry, which limits tracking accuracy.

Method: Polycepta reformulates appearance modeling as a recursive estimation problem, constructing and continuously updating an independent appearance state per tracked object. It uses a learning strategy that encourages learning appearance-state construction (object-specific representations) rather than memorization, enabling generalization to unseen classes.

Result: Consistent reductions in identity switches and improved tracking performance on KITTI, Waymo Open Dataset, and MOT17. Operates at 90.57 Hz, achieves 92.27% MOTA on KITTI when integrated into RobMOT.

Conclusion: Polycepta demonstrates that appearance estimation can be progressively refined over time, outperforming static or degrading appearance descriptors. The approach is both effective and efficient, suitable for real-time MOT applications.

Abstract: The tracking-by-detection paradigm in multi-object tracking (MOT) typically relies on static appearance descriptors to complement motion estimation. However, these descriptors are frame-independent, limiting their robustness as visual cues. Since such descriptors are often obtained from computationally intensive pretrained backbones, real-time MOT systems frequently abandon appearance cues altogether and rely solely on motion prediction and geometric association. In this work, we introduce Polycepta, an object-centric appearance state estimation framework that reformulates appearance modeling as a recursive estimation problem rather than a frame-wise matching task. Polycepta constructs and continuously updates an independent appearance state for each tracked object, enabling future appearance representations to be estimated from accumulated observations. Polycepta is encouraged to learn the appearance-state construction of object-specific representations rather than memorize them through a proposed learning strategy, enabling appearance estimation for unseen classes. A key property of Polycepta is that the quality of appearance estimation improves as object states evolve during inference. While conventional appearance descriptors remain static or degrade over time, Polycepta progressively refines appearance estimates as additional observations are accumulated. Extensive experiments on KITTI, the Waymo Open Dataset, and MOT17 demonstrate consistent reductions in identity switches and improvements in tracking performance when integrated into the tracking-by-detection pipelines. Polycepta operates at 90.57 Hz and delivers state-of-the-art performance on the KITTI benchmark when integrated into the RobMOT framework, achieving a MOTA of 92.27\%.

</details>


### [275] [Vera: A Layered Diffusion Model for Content-Preserving Video Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23610)
*Hongkai Zheng,Ta-Ying Cheng,Benjamin Klein,Yisong Yue,Zhuoning Yuan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Vera is a layered diffusion framework for content-preserving video editing that separates creative editing from content preservation by generating an edit layer and alpha matte for compositing with the source video, using a Mixture-of-Transformers architecture and a high-quality layered dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing video diffusion models regenerate every pixel and often alter elements that should remain unchanged (characters, background scenes), making content preservation a core challenge in video editing.

Method: Vera uses a layered diffusion framework that generates an edit layer and alpha matte for compositing with the source video. It extends text-to-video DiT into a Mixture-of-Transformers (MoT) architecture with separate DiTs for each layer interacting through joint self-attention. Training uses a high-quality layered dataset with accurate alpha mattes, diverse scenes, dynamics, and visual effects.

Result: Vera outperforms leading open-source video editing models in content preservation while remaining competitive in edit quality, using only 486K frames of layered training data.

Conclusion: The layered diffusion approach with compositing effectively separates creative editing from content preservation, offering a promising direction for video editing where source content integrity is critical.

Abstract: Video diffusion models have enabled remarkable progress in video generation and editing. However, content preservation remains a core challenge: existing methods regenerate every pixel and often alter elements that should remain unchanged, such as characters or background scenes. We introduce Vera, a layered diffusion framework for content-preserving video editing. Instead of regenerating the entire video, Vera generates an edit layer along with an alpha matte for compositing with the source video, separating creative editing from content preservation by design. To encourage coherent composition with the source video, we extend the text-to-video DiT into a Mixture-of-Transformers (MoT) architecture, with separate DiTs for each layer that interact through joint self-attention. To support the training of Vera, we further construct a high-quality layered dataset with accurate alpha mattes, diverse scenes and dynamics, and visual effects. Across our quantitative benchmark and human preference study, Vera outperforms leading open-source video editing models in content preservation while remaining competitive in edit quality, using 486K frames of layered training data.

</details>


### [276] [Data Selection Through Iterative Self-Filtering for Vision-Language Settings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23611)
*Andrei Liviu Nicolicioiu,Sarvjeet Singh Ghotra,Morgane M. Moss,Aaron Courville*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A novel Self-Filtering method for vision-language models that iteratively trains a CLIP model on an evolving, self-selected dataset balancing clean and diverse samples, improving downstream performance without needing additional data or pre-trained models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large-scale vision-language datasets are noisy, and existing mitigation methods (heuristics, curated reference datasets, pre-trained models) are insufficient or costly. The authors aim to address this without relying on external resources.

Method: A bootstrapped, iterative approach where a CLIP model trains on a dynamically self-selected dataset that balances high-probability clean samples with diverse samples from the full distribution, alternating between training and improving the data mixture.

Result: Training on datasets filtered via Self-Filtering improves downstream performance compared to baselines, without requiring additional data or pre-trained models.

Conclusion: Self-filtering through iterative bootstrapping is an effective data cleaning strategy for vision-language models, offering a self-supervised alternative to heuristic or externally-supervised filtering approaches.

Abstract: The availability of large amounts of clean data is paramount to training neural networks. However, at large scales, manual oversight is impractical, resulting in sizeable datasets that can be very noisy. Attempts to mitigate this obstacle to producing performant vision-language models have so far involved heuristics, curated reference datasets, and using pre-trained models. Here we propose a novel, bootstrapped method in which a CLIP model is trained on an evolving, self-selected dataset. This evolving dataset constitutes a balance of filtered, highly probable clean samples as well as diverse samples from the entire distribution. Our proposed Self-Filtering method iterates between training the model and selecting a subsequently improved data mixture. Training on vision-language datasets filtered by the proposed approach improves downstream performance without the need for additional data or pre-trained models.

</details>


### [277] [Hedgementation = Hedgerow Segmentation: A Remote Sensing Benchmark](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23615)
*Nathan Senyard,Salem Hamdani,Astrid Zhang,Derek Wang,Evan Shelhamer,Mathias Lécuyer,Joséphine Gantois*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Hedgementation introduces a benchmark for country-scale hedgerow mapping from remote sensing data at 10m² resolution, combining multiple data sources and ground truth labels from France. It evaluates three baseline models on spatial and climatic generalization, testing both supervised and self-supervised learning approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To fill the gap in evaluating machine learning models for fine-scale hedgerow mapping at country scale, which is of high agricultural importance but lacks standardized benchmarks for model generalization across spatial distances and climatic zones.

Method: Harmonizing multiple remote sensing data products with ground truth labels from a hedgerow inventory in France, and measuring three baseline models' generalization performance across spatial distance and climatic zones using both supervised and self-supervised learning approaches.

Result: A new benchmark (Hedgementation) with baseline model performance results for hedgerow mapping at 10m² resolution, assessing generalization across spatial and climatic domains.

Conclusion: Hedgementation provides a standardized evaluation framework for hedgerow mapping models, highlighting the challenges of spatial and climatic generalization for fine-scale agricultural feature extraction from remote sensing data.

Abstract: We propose Hedgementation: a new benchmark to evaluate machine learning models for hedgerow mapping from remote sensing data at country scale and 10m$^2$ spatial resolution. We combine and harmonize multiple remote sensing data products and ground truth labels sourced from a hedgerow inventory in France. We measure the ability of three baseline models to generalize across spatial distance, and across climatic zones, a more explicitly challenging task. Our benchmark tests both supervised and self-supervised learning approaches for remote sensing, applied to tracking fine-scale features of high agricultural importance. The code to reproduce the benchmark and baselines results is available at https://github.com/hedgementation/hedgementation.

</details>


### [278] [Pose Anything Anywhere:Model-free Object Poses from Arbitrary References](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23634)
*Hongli Xu,Jiaqi Hu,Junwen Huang,Boyang Zhong,Peter KT Yu,Nassir Navab,Benjamin Busam,Slobodan Ilic*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PANY is a unified model-free framework for 6D pose estimation of unseen objects that supports both RGB and RGB-D inputs, leverages multi-view transformer geometry to go beyond pairwise matching, aggregates unposed assist views via pose-graph canonical registration, and achieves state-of-the-art performance with +12% on YCB-V and +20% on LM-O benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing model-based methods are accurate but require CAD models or heavy onboarding; model-free approaches are limited to pairwise single-anchor matching and fail under occlusion, large viewpoint changes, and low query-reference overlap. A unified model-free approach generalizing to novel objects without CAD assets is needed.

Method: PANY uses a multi-view transformer geometry backbone to learn view-consistent geometry and cross-view alignment cues beyond pairwise matching. When additional unposed assist views are available, it aggregates them via pose-graph canonical registration to improve geometric coverage and refine final pose estimation.

Result: PANY achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple benchmarks, improving pose accuracy by +12% on YCB-V and over +20% on LM-O, outperforming existing model-free methods in both single-reference and sparse-reference settings.

Conclusion: PANY demonstrates that a unified, model-free multi-view transformer approach can robustly estimate 6D poses of novel unseen objects without CAD dependencies, showing strong real-world robustness under occlusion and limited overlap.

Abstract: Estimating the 6D pose of unseen objects is a fundamental yet challenging problem for open-world robotics and embodied perception. Model-based methods are accurate but depend on CAD assets or heavy onboarding, while most model-free approaches are still limited to pairwise single-anchor matching and thus fail under occlusion and large viewpoint changes with low query-reference overlap. Therefore, we present PANY, a unified model-free framework that seamlessly supports both RGB and RGB-D inputs, operates on one or sparse pose-free reference views, and generalizes effectively to novel objects. Built on a multi-view transformer geometry backbone, PANY moves beyond pairwise matching by learning view-consistent geometry and cross-view alignment cues that remain stable under wide baselines and limited overlap. When additional unposed assist views are available, PANY aggregates them via pose-graph canonical registration to increase geometric coverage and reinforce the final pose. Extensive experiments show that PANY achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple benchmarks, substantially outperforming existing model-free methods, improving pose accuracy by +12% on YCB-V and over +20% on LM-O. Furthermore, PANY consistently performs well under both single-reference and sparse-reference settings, demonstrating strong robustness in real-world environments.

</details>


### [279] [Lightweight Neural Framework for Robust 3D Volume and Surface Estimation from Multi-View Images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23653)
*Diego E. Farchione,Ramzi Idoughi,Peter Wonka*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A feed-forward framework that directly regresses scale-normalized volume, surface area, and uncertainties from multi-view images using point cloud and 2D feature fusion via a graph-based decoder, outperforming state-of-the-art methods on sparse/noisy data across coral monitoring, dietary analysis, and anthropometry tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate volume and surface area estimation is critical for diverse applications (marine ecology, medical diagnostics), but existing methods suffer from high computational costs and poor performance with sparse and noisy data.

Method: A fully feed-forward framework that regresses scale-normalized volume and surface area with uncertainties from multi-view images. It fuses 3D point cloud reconstructions with view-aligned 2D features through a graph-based decoder, bypassing iterative optimization.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art methods, especially with low numbers of input images. Validated on coral monitoring, dietary analysis, and anthropometry tasks.

Conclusion: Provides a high-speed, scalable alternative for precise geometric estimation from visual data, maintaining high performance even in resource-constrained or sparse-view scenarios.

Abstract: Accurate volume and surface area estimation is critical for diverse applications, from marine ecology to medical diagnostics. However, existing methods often suffer from high computational costs and poor performance with sparse and noisy data. We propose a fully feed-forward framework that regresses scale-normalized volume and surface area and their associated uncertainties directly from multi-view images. By fusing 3D point cloud reconstructions with view-aligned 2D features through a graph-based decoder, our model bypasses iterative optimization, ensuring exceptional scalability and rapid inference. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach outperforms state-of-the-art methods, particularly when operating with a low number of input images. Validated across coral monitoring, dietary analysis, and anthropometry, our proposed framework provides a robust, adaptable solution for quantitative shape analysis. This architecture provides a high-speed, scalable alternative for precise geometric estimation from visual data, maintaining high performance even in resource-constrained or sparse-view scenarios.

</details>


### [280] [GeoFidelity-Bench: Evaluating Segment-Level Geographic Fidelity in Text-to-Image Street-View Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23669)
*Kaizhen Tan,Hanzhe Hong,Siru Tao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GeoFidelity-Bench evaluates whether text-to-image models generate street views that match a specific road segment rather than just a generic city prior. On a benchmark of 109 road segments across 25 cities, adding street/neighborhood names improves top-1 retrieval by 5.5 percentage points over city-only prompts, but the similarity margin to the nearest same-city segment stays near zero, showing models learn broad local plausibility rather than exact segment identity. GPS coordinates provide no clear benefit, revealing a gap between plausible and faithful street-view generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether text-to-image models generate images that correspond to a specific requested road segment or merely reflect a generic city prior, and to systematically measure geographic fidelity in street-view generation.

Method: Constructed a benchmark of 7,117 curated Mapillary images covering 109 named OpenStreetMap road segments in 25 cities across 6 continents. Evaluated six open-weight text-to-image generators under city-only, street-and-neighborhood, and GPS-augmented prompt conditions, using local discrimination (ranking target vs. nearest same-city segment, other same-city segments, and other-city segments) as the primary test.

Result: Adding street and neighborhood names increased top-1 retrieval accuracy by 5.5 percentage points (95% CI: 3.4–7.7) over city-only prompts. However, the similarity margin between the target segment and the nearest same-city segment remained near zero. Incorrect local names still yielded partial gains, and raw GPS coordinates provided no statistically clear benefit. Held-out real images successfully recovered segment identity.

Conclusion: Current text-to-image models achieve city- or neighborhood-plausible street-view generation but fail at faithful generation for a specific road segment, revealing a persistent gap between broad geographic plausibility and precise segment-level fidelity.

Abstract: Text-to-image models can generate visually plausible city streets, but whether their outputs correspond to a requested road segment rather than a generic city prior remains unclear. We introduce GeoFidelity-Bench, a reference-panel benchmark for segment-conditioned geographic fidelity in street-view generation. It contains 7,117 curated Mapillary images covering 109 named OpenStreetMap road segments in 25 cities across six continents. For each generated panel, the benchmark ranks the target reference panel against panels from the nearest segment in the same city, other segments in the same city, and segments from other cities, making local discrimination rather than absolute target similarity the primary test. We evaluate six open-weight text-to-image generators under city-only, street-and-neighborhood, and GPS-augmented prompts. Adding street and neighborhood names is associated with an increase of 5.5 percentage points in top-1 retrieval accuracy over city-only prompts, with a 95% confidence interval from 3.4 to 7.7 percentage points. However, the similarity margin between the target and the nearest segment in the same city remains near zero, indicating that local names improve broad local plausibility more than exact segment identity. Prompts that keep the city fixed but use incorrect street or neighborhood names further show that only part of the gain depends on the correct local names, while appending raw GPS coordinates as ordinary text yields no statistically clear additional benefit. Held-out real-image queries successfully recover segment identity, showing that the curated references contain usable segment-level signal. GeoFidelity-Bench thus reveals a persistent gap between city- or neighborhood-plausible street-view generation and faithful generation for a specific road segment.

</details>


### [281] [IMAGIN-4D: Image-Guided Controllable Interaction Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23675)
*Sai Kumar Dwivedi,Federica Bogo,Buğra Tekin,Chenhongyi Yang,Nadine Bertsch,Tomas Hodan,Michael J. Black,Dimitrios Tzionas,Shreyas Hampali*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: IMAGIN-4D is a diffusion-based HOI generator that uses a reference image decomposed into spatio-temporal tokens (interaction-state tokens for spatial cues, frame-aware tokens for temporal cues) to resolve ambiguity in motion generation from text and waypoints, with role-aware conditioning via separate AdaLN streams and cross-attention, trained on synthetic motion-to-image data from FBM, showing improved fine-grained interaction control over baselines on FBM and BEHAVE.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing HOI generation methods use text, object geometry, and waypoints, but these signals underspecify interactions—the same prompt and trajectory can yield different grasps, approach directions, poses, contacts, and layouts. A single global reference image conflates distinct cues and conditions all frames identically, failing to resolve ambiguity.

Method: IMAGIN-4D decomposes image conditioning spatio-temporally: (1) supervised interaction-state tokens for body pose, object pose, body-object contact, and spatial relationships at the depicted frame; (2) frame-aware tokens computed by querying image patches per generated frame for temporal conditioning; (3) role-aware conditioning using separate AdaLAN streams for text, waypoints, and interaction-state tokens, while frame-aware visual tokens cross-attend with motion tokens. A synthetic motion-to-image rendering pipeline from FBM is built to address the lack of paired images in HOI motion datasets.

Result: On FBM and BEHAVE datasets, IMAGIN-4D improves fine-grained interaction control over single-token and uniformly image-conditioned baselines while preserving waypoint-following and motion quality.

Conclusion: Spatio-temporally decomposed image conditioning with role-aware attention streams effectively resolves underspecification in HOI generation, and the synthetic rendering pipeline enables training without paired real data.

Abstract: Generating human-object interactions (HOI) is central to character animation, robotics, AR/VR, and embodied AI. Recent HOI generation methods synthesize motion from text, object geometry, and sparse waypoints, controlling action semantics and object trajectories. However, these signals underspecify interaction: the same prompt and trajectory can produce different grasps, approach directions, body poses, object poses, contacts, and body-object layouts. We address this ambiguity with a reference image as a visual specification of the desired interaction snapshot. However, a single global image representation conflates distinct cues and conditions all frames on identical visual evidence. We therefore introduce IMAGIN-4D, a diffusion-based HOI generator that decomposes image conditioning spatio-temporally. For spatial conditioning, IMAGIN-4D extracts supervised interaction-state tokens for body pose, object pose, body-object contact, and spatial relationships at the depicted frame. For temporal conditioning, it computes frame-aware tokens by querying image patches per generated frame, allowing sequence segments to attend to different visual cues from the same image. To balance image, text, and waypoint cues, IMAGIN-4D uses role-aware conditioning: text, waypoints, and interaction-state tokens use separate AdaLN streams, while frame-aware visual tokens cross-attend with motion tokens. Since HOI motion datasets lack paired images, we build a synthetic motion-to-image rendering pipeline from FullBodyManipulation (FBM) and introduce an image-adherence metric to evaluate whether generated motions match the reference snapshot.
