Motivation: To improve automotive sound design by extending conventional engine order analysis and enhancement with neural network-derived synthesis parameters and stochastic components, enabling more accurate and tunable engine sound modeling across the full RPM-torque operating range.

Method: Neural network-based approach using gradient-based optimization and analysis-by-synthesis via an end-to-end differentiable implementation; parameterizes engine sounds as compact representations capturing per-order and broadband timbral variation; integrates stochastic components into the synthesis framework.

Result: High reconstruction fidelity confirmed by spectral metrics and listening tests; successful integration into an established automotive audio development platform (EVx Suite); demonstrated technical feasibility on deployment-ready embedded systems.

Conclusion: The proposed neural network-based engine sound modeling approach is effective for automotive sound design, achieving high-quality reconstruction while remaining manually tunable and compatible with existing automotive audio frameworks for embedded deployment.

Abstract: Engine order enhancement is central in automotive sound design, where selective harmonics are synthesized to shape perceptual qualities such as sportiness, refinedness, or power. This paper investigates a neural network-based approach to combustion engine sound modeling that extends conventional engine order analysis and enhancement by deriving synthesis parameters from audio data with machine learning and incorporating stochastic components into the synthesis framework. The system parameterizes engine sounds as a compact representation capturing per-order and broadband timbral variation across the full RPM-torque operating range, while remaining manually tunable and compatible with established automotive audio frameworks. The approach leverages gradient-based optimization and analysis-by-synthesis through an end-to-end differentiable implementation. The resulting synthesis parameter set is directly transferable to conventional DSP implementations for deployment on embedded targets. Spectral metrics and listening tests confirm high reconstruction fidelity, and integration into an established automotive audio development platform (EVx Suite) demonstrates technical feasibility on deployment-ready embedded systems.

</details>


### [360] [When EER Hides Deployment Failure: Auditing Threshold Transfer and Unlabeled Score Calibration for Speech Deepfake Detectors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21584)
*Jingwen Zhou,Mingzhe Wang*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: The paper exposes a critical gap between in-domain EER and real-world performance of speech deepfake detectors. A state-of-the-art model with 0.21% EER suffers 39.5% HTER when deploying its threshold on unlabeled data. Seven popular test-time corrections fail to meaningfully improve EER, and some even degrade performance severely. The authors recommend reporting HTER with transferred thresholds alongside EER.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current speech deepfake countermeasures are evaluated almost exclusively using EER computed at an oracle threshold on labeled test sets, which does not reflect real-world deployment where thresholds must be fixed in advance on unlabeled target data. The authors aim to audit this discrepancy and test whether popular unlabeled test-time corrections can close the gap.

Method: The authors audit a frozen SSL-AASIST detector trained on ASVspoof 2019 LA. They transfer its LA-calibrated threshold to In-the-Wild and ASVspoof 2021 DF corpora. They prove a proposition that any strictly increasing score transform cannot change EER, and empirically test seven test-time corrections: z-norm, temperature/shift calibration, embedding mean alignment, AS-norm, and pseudo-label calibration.

Result: In-domain EER is 0.21% but transferred threshold yields 39.5% HTER on In-the-Wild (78.7% bona fide rejection), despite a moderate 11.2% EER. All seven corrections fail to reduce EER by more than 1% relative. AS-norm collapses EER from 11.2% to 60.2%. Pseudo-label calibration reduces HTER 38% relative on In-the-Wild but degenerates to 50% HTER on DF21 (96% spoof prior).

Conclusion: The gap between oracle EER and deployed performance is substantial and cannot be closed by current test-time adaptation methods. The authors recommend reporting HTER at a transferred threshold alongside EER for a more realistic assessment of CM performance.

Abstract: Speech deepfake countermeasures (CMs) are compared almost exclusively by equal error rate (EER), a metric computed at an oracle threshold chosen on the labeled test set. Deployed CMs enjoy no such oracle: a threshold must be fixed in advance and applied to unlabeled target data. We audit this gap with a frozen state-of-the-art SSL-AASIST detector trained on ASVspoof 2019 LA. While its in-domain EER is 0.21%, transferring its LA-calibrated threshold to the In-the-Wild corpus yields a half total error rate (HTER) of 39.5%, with 78.7% of bona fide speech rejected, even though the In-the-Wild EER (11.2%) appears moderate. We then test whether popular unlabeled test-time corrections close this gap, and first prove a simple proposition: any strictly increasing score transform, including z-norm, temperature/shift calibration, and embedding mean alignment under a frozen linear head, cannot change EER. An audit of seven corrections on In-the-Wild and ASVspoof 2021 DF confirms the proposition empirically and exposes two further failure modes: AS-norm with an unlabeled target cohort collapses (EER 11.2% to 60.2%), and pseudo-label calibration that reduces HTER by 38% relative on In-the-Wild degenerates to 50% HTER on DF21, whose spoof prior is 96%. No audited correction reduces EER by more than 1% relative. We recommend reporting HTER at a transferred threshold alongside EER.

</details>


### [361] [Time-Frequency Weighted Losses for Phoneme Reconstruction in DNN-Based Speech Enhancement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21635)
*Nasser-Eddine Monir,Paul Magron,Romain Serizel*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: Proposes a time-frequency weighting framework for speech enhancement that modulates the SDR loss based on speech presence, SIR, and spectral flux, improving phoneme recognition—especially consonants—and frequency-weighted metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional SDR-based training losses treat all TF regions uniformly, missing fine-grained spectral cues relevant to phoneme intelligibility; the goal is to incorporate local acoustic factors into the loss to better preserve speech content.

Method: A differentiable TF weighting framework that adjusts the SDR objective using local speech presence, speech-to-interference ratio (SIR), and spectral flux to emphasize high-competition TF bins and transient consonant cues.

Result: Improves objective frequency-weighted enhancement metrics and phoneme recognition accuracy, especially for consonants; better reconstruction of mid-frequency structures under less adverse SIR.

Conclusion: The proposed weighting scheme effectively enhances phoneme-level intelligibility by focusing on perceptually and acoustically salient TF regions, outperforming uniform SDR training.

Abstract: Conventional training losses for speech enhancement based on the signal-to-distortion ratio (SDR) treat all time-frequency (TF) regions uniformly, overlooking the fine-grained spectral cues that are relevant to specific phoneme intelligibility. We propose a TF weighting framework that modulates the SDR objective based on local speech presence, speech-to-interference ratio (SIR), and spectral flux. By integrating these factors into a differentiable objective, the framework emphasizes TF bins with high speech-noise competition while also accounting for transient cues such as consonant bursts. Experimental results show that our approach improves objective frequency-weighted enhancement metrics, as well as phoneme recognition accuracy, particularly for consonants. Spectral analysis shows better reconstruction of mid-frequency structures at less adverse SIR.

</details>


### [362] [Improving Text-to-Music Generation with Human Preference Rewards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21670)
*Yonghyun Kim,Junwon Lee,Haiwen Xia,Yinghao Ma,Chris Donahue*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: A 120M-parameter FluxAudio-S model for text-to-music generation is enhanced with a learned human-preference reward (TuneJury), combining training-time reward conditioning (doubling as CFG axis), architecture sweep, expert iteration on top decile, CRPO preference tuning, and inference post-processing (joint CFG, source separation, loudness normalization). Expert iteration is the dominant contributor; reward conditioning helps functionally; preference tuning adds negligible gain.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Beyond the standard FAD-CLAP and CLAP score metrics used in the ATTM challenge, the authors aim to incorporate learned human-preference rewards to better align generated music with human aesthetic judgments.

Method: Five engineering decisions on a 120M-parameter FluxAudio-S backbone: (i) training-time reward conditioning that also serves as inference-time CFG axis, (ii) sweep over five score-conditioning architectures with different train/inference variants, (iii) expert iteration on top decile, (iv) CRPO preference-tuning pass for audio-text alignment, (v) inference post-processing with joint CFG, source separation, and loudness normalization.

Result: Per-stage decomposition on 100 Song Describer prompts reveals: training-time reward conditioning functions as a real conditioning axis, expert iteration is the dominant contributor, CRPO preference tuning provides only noise-level gain, and the inference-time score scalar is already saturated at the end of the pipeline.

Conclusion: Expert iteration on the top decile is the most impactful engineering decision; the learned reward works as a conditioning signal but preference tuning adds little value; the inference score scalar is already saturated, suggesting diminishing returns from further tuning.

Abstract: We describe our entry to the efficiency track of the Academic Text-to-Music (ATTM) Grand Challenge at ICME 2026. Beyond the challenge protocol's FAD-CLAP and CLAP score, we add a learned human-preference reward from TuneJury, a twin pairwise ranker trained over open music-preference datasets. The reward serves both as a training-time conditioning signal and as a sample-selection criterion. The pipeline combines five engineering decisions on a 120M-parameter FluxAudio-S backbone, four at training time and one at inference: (i) training-time reward conditioning that doubles as an inference-time CFG axis, (ii) a sweep over five score-conditioning architectures, where training and inference use different variants, (iii) expert iteration on the top decile, (iv) a short preference-tuning pass (CRPO) for audio-text alignment, and (v) inference post-processing via joint CFG, source separation, and loudness normalization. Per-stage decomposition on 100 Song Describer prompts shows training-time reward conditioning as a functional conditioning axis, expert iteration as the dominant contributor, the preference-tuning pass adding only noise-level gain, and the inference-time score scalar already saturated by the end of the chain.

</details>


### [363] [Streaming T5-based Text-to-Speech Synthesis with Limited Lookahead](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21882)
*Muyang Du,Jason Roche,Junjie Lai*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: S5-TTS is a streaming text-to-speech system that extends T5-TTS with encoder-decoder language modeling and monotonic alignment learning, enabling low-latency, word-by-word incremental speech synthesis without requiring full context, while maintaining quality through lookahead-causal masking, Conv-based auxiliary attention, and interleaved multi-source distillation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Streaming TTS in cascaded LLM-TTS systems still suffers from high latency because most TTS models need full context before starting generation, limiting real-time conversational AI applications.

Method: (1) Encoder-decoder language modeling with monotonic alignment learning for word-by-word incremental synthesis; (2) Lookahead-causal masking with Conv-based auxiliary attention to maintain quality under limited lookahead; (3) Interleaved multi-source distillation to restore naturalness.

Result: S5-TTS achieves quality comparable to full-context T5-TTS, supports zero-shot synthesis with high speaker similarity, and significantly reduces end-to-end latency.

Conclusion: Streaming, low-latency TTS is feasible without sacrificing quality, enabling practical conversational AI systems with reduced end-to-end response time.

Abstract: Streaming text-to-speech synthesis in cascaded LLM-TTS systems still faces latency challenges as most TTS models require full context before initiating generation. We present S5-TTS, a streaming variant of T5-TTS that enables low-latency, word-by-word incremental speech synthesis through encoder-decoder language modeling and monotonic alignment learning. S5-TTS begins generating speech immediately after receiving the first few words, substantially reducing end-to-end response latency. To maintain quality under limited lookahead, we introduce a lookahead-causal masking mechanism with Conv-based auxiliary attention that preserves intelligibility and speaker similarity, and employ interleaved multi-source distillation to further restore naturalness. Experiments show that S5-TTS achieves comparable quality to full-context T5-TTS, supports zero-shot synthesis with high speaker similarity, and significantly reduces end-to-end latency for practical conversational AI systems.

</details>


### [364] [Improving Engine Sound Analysis in Hot-Test Environments via a RAB-U-Net (Residual Attention Block U-Net) Noise Removal Method](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21887)
*Raheleh Mohseni,Mahdi Alyari*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: This paper proposes RAB-U-Net, a deep learning model combining U-Net with Residual Attention Blocks, to remove background noise from engine sound recordings during production line hot tests, improving engine diagnostics accuracy over traditional methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Background noise interferes with accurate engine-sound analysis during production line hot tests, and traditional technician-based listening is prone to significant inaccuracies, leading to potential errors in engine diagnostics.

Method: A U-Net neural network enhanced with Residual Attention Blocks (RAB-U-Net) is used for intelligent noise removal from engine sound recordings in real-time production line environments.

Result: The proposed RAB-U-Net system significantly improves the accuracy of engine noise detection and outperforms traditional noise removal techniques.

Conclusion: The RAB-U-Net approach provides a robust, real-time solution for engine diagnostics in production lines, representing a valuable advancement in applying deep learning to automotive quality control.

Abstract: During hot tests on a production line, engine-sound analysis is crucial to ensuring product quality and performance. However, background noise often interferes with accurate sound analysis, leading to potential errors in engine diagnostics. Traditionally, skilled technicians listen to engine sounds to assess engine health, but this is prone to significant inaccuracies. This study presents an innovative deep learning-based approach to address this issue by removing background noise from engine sound recordings using a U-Net neural network structure enhanced with Residual Attention Blocks (RAB-U-Net). Our intelligent noise removal system significantly improves the accuracy of engine noise detection, outperforming traditional techniques and providing a robust solution for real-time applications in production line environments. This study proposes a novel system for engine noise detection in production lines, marking a valuable advancement for the automotive industry in applying deep learning methods to improve the quality of engine diagnostics.

</details>


### [365] [AugCodec: A Low-Bitrate Disentangled Neural Speech Codec via Data Augmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21893)
*Dongmei Wang,Xiaohang Sun,Yang Liu,Fanjie Kong,Abhishek Yanamandra,Abhinav Jain,Daniel Tompkins,Woohyun Kang,Najmeh Sadoughi,Sunil Hadap,Xiang Hao,Zhu Liu,Caren Chen*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: AugCodec is a low-bitrate neural speech codec using data augmentation to disentangle speech into semantic, speaker, and prosody tokens at 12.5Hz, outperforming SOTA in reconstruction and disentanglement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To achieve low-bitrate speech coding by disentangling speech into independent attribute streams (semantic, speaker, prosody), enabling efficient token rate reduction while preserving perceptual quality.

Method: Uses tailored data augmentation to transform speech into variants for extracting disentangled tokens; introduces an augmentation loss to align semantic encoder outputs between source and voice-converted speech, reducing acoustic mismatch while encouraging speaker-agnostic embeddings.

Result: AugCodec operates at 12.5Hz with three token streams and significantly outperforms SOTA methods on LibriSpeech test-clean in both reconstruction quality and disentanglement.

Conclusion: Data augmentation-driven disentanglement is an effective strategy for building low-bitrate neural speech codecs with strong reconstruction and attribute separation.

Abstract: We propose AugCodec, a low-bitrate disentangled neural speech codec that leverages data augmentation to decompose speech into three distinct components: semantic, speaker, and prosody tokens. Specifically, we employ tailored augmenta tion strategies to transform speech into distinct variants, each serving as input for extracting tokens that preserve the target attribute while suppressing others. This disentanglement strategy enables substantial reduction in token rate. Further more, we introduce an augmentation loss that aligns semantic encoder outputs between source and voice-converted speech, encouraging speaker-agnostic embeddings while mitigating the acoustic mismatch induced by voice conversion. Experiments on LibriSpeech test-clean demonstrate that AugCodec significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both reconstruction quality and disentanglement, while operating at only 12.5Hz with three token streams.

</details>


### [366] [ISCSLP 2026 CoT-TTS Challenge: Chain-of-Thought Reasoning for Context-Aware Text-to-Speech](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21933)
*Wei Xue,Junlan Feng,Shilei Zhang,Yue Wang,Ruosong Yang,Bei Liu,Liumeng Xue,Sitong Cheng,Jiahao Pan,Weizhen Bian,Boyi Kang,Bin Long*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: The ISCSLP 2026 CoT-TTS Challenge evaluates TTS systems on their ability to infer speaking styles from textual or audio context using chain-of-thought reasoning, featuring bilingual training data, two tracks, multimodal evaluation, and a baseline Qwen3-based model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing controllable TTS systems require explicit user-provided style prompts, failing to automatically determine how a sentence should be spoken in complex conversational scenarios. The challenge aims to bridge context understanding, reasoning, and expressive speech generation.

Method: Two tracks (text-context-aware and audio-context-aware CoT-TTS); large-scale bilingual training set from speech-rich media; systems must output both chain-of-thought reasoning and speech waveform; official evaluation combines objective metrics, multimodal LLM evaluation, and human subjective assessment; baseline uses 0.6B Qwen3-based model with three-stage training.

Result: No experimental results presented yet (this is a challenge proposal); the challenge provides inference code, fine-tuning recipe, filtered evaluation data, and leaderboard comparison.

Conclusion: The challenge is expected to advance context understanding, chain-of-thought reasoning, and expressive speech generation for applications including film dubbing, audiobook production, virtual characters, and spoken dialogue agents.

Abstract: Recent advances in text-to-speech (TTS) have greatly improved speech naturalness, speaker similarity, and controllability. However, most existing controllable TTS systems still rely on explicit user-provided style prompts, making it difficult to automatically determine how a sentence should be spoken in long and complex conversational scenarios. This proposal introduces the ISCSLP 2026 CoT-TTS Challenge, which aims to evaluate whether a system can infer the intended speaking manner from contextual information and generate speech consistent with both the reasoning output and the surrounding scene. The challenge contains two tracks: text-context-aware CoT-TTS and audio-context-aware CoT-TTS. We construct a large-scale bilingual training set from speech-rich media and provide carefully filtered evaluation data for leaderboard comparison. Each system is required to output both a chain-of-thought reasoning analysis and the generated speech waveform. The official evaluation combines objective metrics, multimodal LLM-based evaluation, and human subjective assessment. To facilitate reproducibility, we provide inference code together with a fine-tuning recipe for a 0.6B Qwen3-based model trained via a three-stage strategy. This challenge is expected to support research on context understanding, chain-of-thought reasoning, and expressive speech generation for applications such as film dubbing, audiobook production, virtual characters, and spoken dialogue agents. Further information about the associated challenge is available at:https://iscslp2026-cot-tts.github.io/challenge-website/

</details>


### [367] [Toward Open-Set Speaker Attribute Prediction with Keyword-Appended LLM Embeddings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21979)
*Byoungjun So,Jaejun Lee,Kyogu Lee*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: Proposes an LLM-based open-set speaker attribute prediction framework using continuous semantic embeddings, keyword-appending strategy, and top-k negative loss to enable zero-shot generalization beyond fixed categorical labels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional speaker attribute prediction relies on fixed categorical labels, which lack semantic richness and cannot generalize to unseen attributes (zero-shot).

Method: (1) Uses LLM embeddings to represent speaker attributes in a continuous semantic space. (2) Keyword-appending strategy structures broad semantic representations into a compact, discriminative manifold bridging cross-modal gaps. (3) Top-k negative loss establishes robust decision boundaries in crowded semantic regions.

Result: Outperforms closed-set benchmarks on LibriTTS-P and generalizes effectively to unseen synonyms. Geometric analysis shows the manifold regularization balances semantic cohesion with predictive clarity.

Conclusion: The proposed framework successfully achieves open-set, semantically rich speaker attribute prediction with zero-shot generalizability, validated empirically and through geometric analysis.

Abstract: Understanding speaker attributes is crucial for voice-related applications, yet conventional approaches rely on fixed categorical labels, lacking semantic richness and zero-shot generalizability. We propose a novel framework for open-set speaker attribute prediction leveraging Large Language Model (LLM) embeddings to represent attributes in a continuous semantic space. To bridge the cross-modal gap, we introduce a keyword-appending strategy that structures broad semantic representations into a compact, discriminative manifold. Furthermore, we employ a top-k negative loss to establish robust decision boundaries in crowded semantic regions. Experimental results on LibriTTS-P demonstrate that our method outperforms closed-set benchmarks and generalizes effectively to unseen synonyms. Geometric analysis suggests that our strategies regularize the embedding manifold, balancing semantic cohesion with predictive clarity.

</details>


### [368] [InstructFX2FX: A Multi-turn Text-to-Preset Demo for Iterative Audio Effect Refinement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22005)
*Song-Ze Yu,Milan Liessens Dujardin,Yuxuan Cai,Wantong Zhang*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: InstructFX2FX is an interactive system for multi-turn, text-guided audio effect refinement. It uses a hybrid LLM + CLAP-optimization architecture where the LLM handles high-level planning (effect selection, ordering, initial parameters) and CLAP-guided optimization perceptually refines parameters across successive user instructions. Evaluated on SocialFX-derived descriptor transitions, it reduces target-directed DSP-feature MMD by ~24% compared to an LLM-only reprompting baseline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing text-to-preset systems are single-shot, mapping one text descriptor to one preset, but real audio engineering is sequential—engineers iteratively refine an effect chain through successive instructions. This creates a stateful problem of updating parameters while preserving prior changes, which single-shot systems cannot handle.

Method: Hybrid architecture: an LLM serves as a high-level planner to select effects, order the signal chain, and propose initial parameters. CLAP-guided optimization then refines these parameters perceptually, providing more controllable updates than re-prompting the LLM at each turn (which tends to perturb unintended settings).

Result: On SocialFX-derived descriptor transitions, CLAP-guided refinement lowers target-directed DSP-feature MMD on 9 of 10 directed descriptor pairs relative to an LLM-only reprompting baseline, reducing average MMD by roughly 24% (from 0.45 to 0.34).

Conclusion: The hybrid approach (LLM planning + CLAP optimization) effectively addresses stateful multi-turn audio effect refinement, outperforming LLM-only reprompting by preserving prior adjustments while achieving target descriptors more accurately.

Abstract: We present InstructFX2FX, an interactive demonstration of multi-turn, text-guided audio effect refinement. Existing text-to-preset systems are largely single-shot, mapping one textual descriptor to one preset. Real audio engineering is instead sequential: engineers refine an existing effect chain through successive instructions such as "make it warmer" or "too harsh, soften it." This poses a stateful problem that single-shot systems do not address: given the current effect parameters and a new instruction, update the sound while preserving what earlier instructions already achieved. InstructFX2FX tackles this with a hybrid architecture that divides labor between a language model and CLAP-guided optimization. The LLM acts as a high-level planner that selects effects, orders the signal chain, and proposes initial parameters, motivated by recent evidence that LLM initialization outperforms CLAP optimization from a random start; CLAP-guided optimization then refines those parameters perceptually, giving more controllable updates than re-prompting the LLM at each turn, which tends to perturb settings the user did not ask to change. At the demo, attendees drive a dry recording through successive natural-language instructions and inspect the evolving FX chain, intermediate optimization checkpoints, and session state. On SocialFX-derived descriptor transitions, CLAP-guided refinement lowers target-directed DSP-feature MMD on 9 of 10 directed descriptor pairs relative to an LLM-only reprompting baseline, reducing the average MMD by roughly 24% (0.45 to 0.34).

</details>


### [369] [What Do Neural Networks Learn for TDOA Estimation? A Cross-Architecture Probing Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22020)
*Yaozhong Kang,Jiang Wang,Runwu Shi,Takeshi Ashizawa,Benjamin Yen,Kazuhiro Nakadai*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: Neural networks for TDOA estimation internally learn cross-power computation but reject PHAT whitening, instead adopting magnitude-aware frequency weighting that retains reliability information discarded by PHAT.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To uncover the internal strategy of neural networks for TDOA estimation compared to classical GCC-PHAT.

Method: Probed hidden layers of MLP, CNN, and Transformer using diagnostic targets derived from GCC-PHAT steps, complemented by gradient attribution and causal frequency masking.

Result: Cross-power computation emerges across all architectures; PHAT whitening fails to emerge; networks learn magnitude-aware frequency weighting instead.

Conclusion: PHAT acts as an information bottleneck; removing it improves performance; end-to-end networks outperform by learning data-adaptive weighting.

Abstract: Neural networks outperform classical GCC-PHAT for Time-Difference-of-Arrival (TDOA) estimation in noise and reverberation, yet their internal strategy remains unexplored. To uncover it, we turn GCC-PHAT's mathematical steps into diagnostic targets, probing hidden layers of three architectures (MLP, CNN, Transformer) and complementing with gradient attribution and causal frequency masking. We find that cross-power computation consistently emerges across all architectures and conditions, while PHAT whitening, the defining step of GCC-PHAT, fails to emerge. Instead, networks learn a magnitude-aware frequency weighting that preserves per-frequency reliability information discarded by PHAT. This makes PHAT an information bottleneck: removing it from both classical and neural GCC pipelines improves performance under additive noise. On real-world reverberant data, PHAT remains the best classical weighting, but end-to-end networks achieve lower error by learning data-adaptive weighting.

</details>


### [370] [An Analysis of Untrained Deep Reservoir Networks for Audio Surveillance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22218)
*Corrado Baccheschi,Patrizio Dazzi*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: This paper explores untrained recurrent models (Echo State Networks) of varying depth for emergency sound detection in audio surveillance, showing they achieve competitive performance against fully trained BiLSTMs and CRNNs while being more computationally efficient, especially for edge deployment on NVIDIA Orin.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To assess whether untrained reservoir computing models (bidirectional Echo State Networks) can serve as efficient alternatives to fully trained recurrent/convolutional-recurrent architectures for emergency sound event detection in resource-constrained audio surveillance scenarios.

Method: Investigate bidirectional Echo State Networks (biESNs) with varying depths (shallow to deep) on the MIVIA Audio Events dataset for multiclass emergency sound detection under different SNR levels; compare against BiLSTM and CRNN baselines; evaluate across log-Mel spectrogram and MFCC input representations with different resolutions; test on edge devices (NVIDIA Orin).

Result: Deep and shallow reservoir models achieve competitive recognition rates compared to fully trained baselines; deeper variants show more robustness in high-noise conditions; shallower variants offer the best computational efficiency on edge devices; the approach remains robust across different input representations.

Conclusion: Untrained reservoir architectures (biESNs) are a promising solution for resource-constrained audio surveillance, offering a favorable trade-off between recognition performance and computational efficiency.

Abstract: In this paper, we investigate untrained recurrent models from the Reservoir Computing (RC) paradigm for audio surveillance, focusing on bidirectional Echo State Networks with different depths, from shallow to deep configurations, for emergency sound event detection. We evaluate these models on the MIVIA Audio Events dataset in a multiclass setting across different Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) levels, with the goal of assessing the trade-off between depth, recognition performance, and computational efficiency. We compare the proposed architectures against fully trained recurrent and convolutional-recurrent baselines, namely Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory networks (BiLSTMs) and Convolutional Recurrent Neural Networks (CRNNs). Results show that deep and shallow reservoir-based models achieve competitive recognition rates, with deeper variants being more robust in highly noisy conditions and shallower ones offering the most favorable efficiency profile, particularly on edge devices such as the NVIDIA Orin. In addition, the proposed approach remains robust across different input representations, including log-Mel spectrograms and MFCCs with varying resolutions. These findings highlight untrained reservoir architectures as a promising solution for resource-constrained audio surveillance scenarios.

</details>


### [371] [Learning to Evade: Adaptive Attacks on Audio Watermarking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22310)
*Weikang Ding,Hanqing Guo,Rui Duan,Guangjing Wang,Yuanda Wang,Mingzhe Chen,Qiben Yan*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: AWM is an adaptive audio watermark attack that uses two-stage optimization and distribution estimation to bypass detection, achieving high attack success with detection rates below 10% for replacement/creation and 0% for removal.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing audio watermarking methods are vulnerable to adversarial attacks, while current defense strategies exploit the normal distribution property of watermark decoder message probabilities to detect manipulations. There is a need for a method that can bypass these defenses.

Method: A two-stage optimization attack: Stage 1 ensures attack success, Stage 2 improves audio quality. To evade detection, it estimates normal distribution parameters from limited target audio samples and adaptively steers decoded probabilities back into the estimated range.

Result: On two watermarking methods across three voice datasets, detection rates are below 10% for replacement and creation attacks, and 0% for removal attacks, while maintaining high attack success.

Conclusion: The proposed AWM effectively bypasses state-of-the-art detectors, demonstrating that current defense strategies against audio watermark attacks are insufficient.

Abstract: Advances in generative audio have intensified copyright concerns, making audio watermarking increasingly important for asserting ownership. However, existing audio watermarking methods are vulnerable to adversarial attacks. We find that watermark decoder message probabilities follow normal distributions, a property exploited by defenses to detect manipulations. This paper introduces an adaptive audio watermark attack method (AWM) designed to bypass existing defense strategies. AWM uses a two-stage optimization: the first stage ensures attack success, while the second improves audio quality. To evade detection, it estimates normal distribution parameters from limited samples of the target audio, and then adaptively steers decoded probabilities back into the estimated range. Evaluated on two watermarking methods across three voice datasets, AWM achieves high success while bypassing state-of-the-art detectors: detection rates are below 10% for replacement and creation, and 0% for removal.

</details>


### [372] [Physics-Informed Neural Operator for Speech Production Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22364)
*Kazuya Yokota,Xinmeng Luan,Debasish Ray Mohapatra,Gary Scavone,Sidney Fels*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: This paper proposes the first physics-informed neural operator (PINO) method for speech production analysis, learning 1D wave equations without supervised training data. The model predicts f0, glottal volume velocity, and sound pressure for five static vowels, achieving 0.8% error for glottal volume flow and 3.2% error for speech waveforms while enabling efficient GPU-parallelized simulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a fast, efficient numerical simulator for speech production analysis that overcomes the limitations of conventional iterative solvers (e.g., Runge-Kutta/Finite difference methods) by leveraging PINOs as a mesh-free, data-efficient alternative.

Method: A physics-informed neural operator is trained to solve the governing one-dimensional wave equations for speech production. The model takes vocal tract shape data as input features and is trained without pre-computed supervised data, using only the physics constraints (the wave equations) as the loss function. The approach is GPU-parallelized, avoiding iterative calculations.

Result: For five static vowels, the PINO model predicted f0, glottal volume velocity, and sound pressure at the lip. Compared to conventional Runge-Kutta/Finite difference methods, the model achieved 0.8% error for glottal volume flow and 3.2% error for speech waveforms.

Conclusion: The PINO-based method is a promising approach for fast, efficient speech production analysis, offering GPU-parallelized simulation without iterative calculations, with low error rates compared to conventional solvers.

Abstract: Physics-informed neural operators (PINOs) have recently gained attention as fast numerical simulators with potential for solving inverse problems. This study proposes the first PINO-based method for speech production analysis. The model learns the governing one-dimensional wave equations directly without requiring pre-computed supervised training data. Using vocal tract shape data as input features, we compare the proposed model's predicted f0, glottal volume velocity and sound pressure at the lip for five static vowels to a conventional Runge Kutta/Finite difference approach. With errors of 0.8% for glottal volume flow and 3.2% for speech waveforms, the proposed model enables efficient GPU-parallelized simulation without iterative calculations. We conclude that PINO is a promising approach for fast analysis of speech.

</details>


### [373] [Kiwano: A Cutting-Edge Open-Source Toolkit for Speaker Verification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22369)
*Mickael Rouvier,Pierre Michel Bousquet*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: Kiwano is an open-source PyTorch toolkit for speaker verification that provides standardized recipes, pretrained models, benchmarking tools, and reproducible evaluation pipelines under the Apache 2.0 license.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To lower entry barriers and standardize evaluation practices in speaker verification by providing an open-source, lightweight, and extensible toolkit that enables reproducible research and benchmarking.

Method: Built on PyTorch as a lightweight yet extensible framework with standardized recipes, pretrained models, integration of multiple speaker verification architectures, unified evaluation protocols, experiment tracking, and rapid prototyping tools.

Result: A publicly available toolkit (Apache 2.0) with transparent training pipelines, ready-to-use baselines across multiple corpora, benchmarking tools, and comprehensive documentation for reproducible experiments.

Conclusion: Kiwano serves as a valuable resource for both academic research and applied development in speaker verification by lowering entry barriers and standardizing evaluation practices.

Abstract: In this paper, we present Kiwano, an open-source toolkit designed to advance research and evaluation for speaker verification. Kiwano provides a lightweight yet extensible framework built on PyTorch, offering standardized recipes, pretrained models, and integration of several widely used speaker verification architectures. The toolkit emphasizes reproducibility, by delivering transparent training pipelines, unified evaluation protocols and ready-to-use baselines across multiple corpora. Beyond conventional training and inference, Kiwano includes tools for benchmarking, experiment tracking and rapid prototyping of new architectures. To foster community adoption, the toolkit is distributed under the Apache 2.0 license, accompanied by comprehensive documentation and reproducible experiments. By lowering entry barriers and standardizing evaluation practices, Kiwano contributes a valuable resource for both academic research and applied development in speaker verification. The toolkit is publicly available at: https://github.com/kiwano-toolkit/kiwano/

</details>


### [374] [ATCCaps: A Call-Sign-Aware Speech Dataset for Air Traffic Control Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22399)
*Dongdong Li,Jianwei Song,Jianwei Wang,Zhe Wang*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: ATCCaps is a 202.94-hour call-sign-aware ATC speech dataset built from real radiotelephony recordings, featuring 170,385 utterances, 922 unique normalized call signs, and multi-level captions (transcript, call-sign, ATC-style) for ASR, call-sign matching, and audio-text retrieval tasks, with evaluations showing scalable call-sign supervision but noting challenges in numeric fidelity validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Call signs are safety-critical in ATC communications, but existing speech datasets lack explicit call-sign supervision and annotation. The authors aim to bridge this gap by constructing a dataset that provides caption-level audio-text supervision specifically for call-sign-aware modeling.

Method: The construction pipeline includes: (1) confidence-aware transcript parsing, (2) ADS-B-derived call-sign metadata extraction, (3) call-sign normalization, (4) rule-based quality filtering, and (5) LLM-assisted caption generation. Each sample is paired with transcript descriptions, call-sign descriptions, and ATC-style captions.

Result: ATCCaps contains 202.94 hours, 170,385 utterances, and 922 unique normalized call signs. Evaluation on the ATCO2-test-set shows scalable audio-grounded call-sign supervision, but caption analysis indicates needed improvements in call-sign and numeric fidelity validation.

Conclusion: ATCCaps enables call-sign-aware ATC speech modeling with ASR evaluation, call-sign matching, and audio-text retrieval capabilities. Reference ASR and CLAP-based baselines confirm its usability, while highlighting the need for explicit validation of numeric and call-sign fidelity in future work.

Abstract: Call signs are safety-critical entities in air traffic control (ATC) communications because they identify the target aircraft of each spoken instruction. This paper presents ATCCaps, a call-sign-aware ATC speech dataset with caption-level audio-text supervision. Built from real ATC radiotelephony recordings, ATCCaps contains 202.94 hours of curated audio, 170,385 utterances, and 922 unique normalized call signs. The construction pipeline combines confidence-aware transcript parsing, ADS-B-derived call-sign metadata, call-sign normalization, rule-based quality filtering, and LLM-assisted caption generation. Each retained sample is paired with transcript descriptions, call-sign descriptions, and ATC-style captions, supporting ASR evaluation, call-sign matching, and call-sign-aware audio-text retrieval. We further characterize ATCCaps through split statistics, call-sign coverage, seen/unseen call-sign analysis, filtering audits, and caption quality evaluation. The evaluation subset is derived from the human-annotated ATCO2-test-set, enabling reference evaluation with manual transcripts. Results show that ATCCaps provides scalable audio-grounded call-sign supervision, while caption analysis highlights the need to explicitly validate call-sign and numeric fidelity. Reference ASR and CLAP-based baselines demonstrate the usability of ATCCaps for call-sign-aware ATC speech modeling.

</details>


### [375] [Libretto: Giving LLM Agents a Sense of Musical Structure](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22708)
*Yichen Xu*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: Libretto is an agent-facing framework that uses LLM-native grammar with explicit structural slots (onset, voice, bar) and corpus-calibrated statistical evaluation across rhythm, harmony, melody, texture, form, and variation to make symbolic music generation measurable, editable, and revisable for language-model agents.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generative music systems produce impressive audio from text prompts, but their outputs are difficult to inspect, edit, and diagnose as musical structure due to the lack of explicit structural representations.

Method: Libretto employs an LLM-native grammar with explicit onset slots, voices, and bar-level organization, combined with corpus-calibrated statistical evaluation over rhythm, harmony, melody, texture, form, and variation. These structural axes support retrieval, diagnosis, copy-risk control, and iterative self-revision.

Result: Libretto enables gap filling, reference-guided full-piece generation, gradual morphing, and educational music generation, transforming symbolic music from raw token sequences into measurable and editable objects for language-model agents.

Conclusion: By grounding generation in explicit structural axes and statistical evaluation, Libretto makes symbolic music a controllable, diagnosable, and revisable medium for LLM-based agents.

Abstract: Generative music systems can now produce impressive audio from text prompts, but audio outputs are difficult to inspect, edit, and diagnose as musical structure. We introduce Libretto, an agent-facing framework for symbolic music generation and revision. Libretto uses an LLM-native grammar with explicit onset slots, voices, and bar-level organization, then evaluates each piece in a corpus-calibrated statistical space over rhythm, harmony, melody, texture, form, and variation. The same structural axes support retrieval, diagnosis, copy-risk control, and iterative self-revision. Across gap filling, reference-guided full-piece generation, gradual morphing, and educational music generation, Libretto turns symbolic music from a raw token sequence into a measurable and editable object for language-model agents.

</details>


### [376] [Scaling Audio Models Efficiently: A Joint Study of Compute Constraints and Optimization Behavior](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22790)
*Vyom Agarwal,Mokshda Gangrade,Siddharth Pal,Jerry Wu*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: This paper investigates compute-performance tradeoffs for ASR and SER across three dimensions (model size, input length, representation resolution), demonstrating non-linear scaling, an optimal 4-second audio duration for SER, and that reducing token resolution significantly cuts computation with minimal WER degradation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Recent advances in compute-optimal scaling for multimodal models have raised questions about how to optimally allocate computational resources across different dimensions (capacity, temporal context, representational granularity) for speech tasks, motivating a systematic investigation.

Method: A unified framework is proposed analyzing three compute dimensions: model size (x_N), input length (x_T), and representation resolution (x_V). Experiments are conducted on LibriSpeech (ASR) and CREMA-D (SER) datasets, systematically varying these dimensions under fixed computational budgets. LoRA-based adaptation is also evaluated.

Result: (1) Diminishing returns on model scaling: Tiny→Small reduces WER by 8.22%, while Small→Medium reduces WER by only 2.35%. (2) Optimal audio duration of ~4 seconds exists for SER. (3) Reducing encoder token resolution (Large-v3: 750 frames, 2572 GFLOPS vs. 1500 frames, 5228 GFLOPS) yields <3% relative WER increase. LoRA finetuning shows minimal degradation.

Conclusion: The study provides practical guidelines for designing efficient speech models by identifying optimal operating points for compute allocation across model capacity, temporal context, and representational granularity, enabling cost-effective deployment.

Abstract: In this paper, we investigate the tradeoffs between compute allocation and model performance for two speech processing tasks: Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) and Speech Emotion Recognition (SER). We propose a unified framework that analyzes three fundamental compute dimensions: model size ($x_N$), input length ($x_T$), and representation resolution ($x_V$). Motivated by recent advances in compute optimal scaling for multimodal models, we systematically vary these dimensions to examine their influence on task performance under fixed computational budgets. Our study provides insights into how compute resources can be optimally distributed across model capacity, temporal context, and representational granularity, offering practical guidelines for the design of efficient speech models. Through experiments on LibriSpeech and CREMA-D datasets, we demonstrate non-linear scaling behavior and identify optimal operating points. Our results show that (1) increasing model size yields diminishing returns: scaling Tiny (39M) to Small (244M) reduces WER by 8.22%, whereas Small to Medium (769M) reduces WER by only 2.35%; (2) an optimal audio duration of approximately 4 seconds exists for SER; and (3) reducing encoder token resolution provides an effective mechanism for lowering inference cost, Large-v3 (1540M) with 750 frames requires 2572 GFLOPS whereas with 1500 frames requires 5228 GFLOPS, with less than 3% relative increase in WER. Additionally, LoRA-based adaptation enables efficient finetuning with minimal performance degradation.

</details>


### [377] [Cross-lingual Retrieval-Augmented Classification for Dysarthria Severity Assessment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22910)
*Taeyoung Jeong,Insung Lee,Du-Seong Chang,Myoung-Wan Koo*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: CRAC leverages cross-lingual speech via an align-retrieve-fuse pipeline with supervised contrastive learning and cross-attention to improve automatic dysarthria severity assessment under data scarcity, achieving 87.3% (Korean) and 86.7% (Italian) balanced accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Automatic dysarthria severity assessment is limited by the scarcity of labeled pathological speech data, motivating the use of cross-lingual speech resources.

Method: CRAC uses supervised contrastive learning to shape a severity-focused embedding space, builds a vector database from an opposite-language corpus, retrieves top-k references, and fuses them with the input via cross-attention in an align-retrieve-fuse pipeline.

Result: Achieves balanced accuracies of 87.3% on Korean (post-stroke) and 86.7% on Italian (ALS) under a speaker-independent three-class protocol, improving over monolingual baselines by 8.4 and 20.0 percentage points, respectively.

Conclusion: Cross-lingual retrieval-augmented classification effectively alleviates data scarcity in dysarthria assessment, substantially outperforming monolingual approaches.

Abstract: Automatic dysarthria severity assessment is limited by the scarcity of labeled pathological speech data. To address this, we propose Cross-lingual Retrieval-Augmented Classification (CRAC), which leverages speech from a different language via an align-retrieve-fuse pipeline. Supervised contrastive learning first shapes a severity-focused embedding space, then a vector database is built from the opposite-language corpus. During both training and inference, the classifier retrieves top-k references from the aligned space and fuses them with the input via cross-attention. Evaluated on Korean post-stroke and Italian ALS dysarthria datasets under a speaker-independent three-class protocol, CRAC achieves balanced accuracies of 87.3% on Korean and 86.7% on Italian, improving over monolingual baselines by 8.4 and 20.0 percentage points, respectively.

</details>


### [378] [HALAS: A Human-Annotated Dataset of Hallucinations of Modern ASR Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23048)
*Mateusz Barański,Jan Jasiński,Julitta Bartolewska,Marcin Witkowski,Konrad Kowalczyk*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: HALAS is the first human-annotated dataset of naturally occurring ASR hallucinations from real earnings call recordings. Analysis reveals cross-model vocabulary overlap, hallucinations in near-correct transcriptions, and that current detection methods achieve only 53.1% F1 or 81% ROC-AUC.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing hallucination mitigation methods are evaluated on non-speech or artificially corrupted audio, not on naturally occurring hallucinations from real speech. There is a lack of rigorous, non-artificial benchmarks for ASR hallucination detection.

Method: HALAS is constructed by collecting span-level human annotations of hallucinations from seven state-of-the-art ASR models on real, unprocessed earnings call recordings. Cross-model vocabulary overlap and hallucination patterns are analyzed. Character and semantic-level metrics are benchmarked against state-of-the-art detection methods.

Result: Strong cross-model vocabulary overlap is observed. Hallucinations occur even in almost correctly transcribed speech (low WER). Proxy metrics (character/semantic-level) reach 81% ROC-AUC, while state-of-the-art detection methods achieve only 53.1% F1.

Conclusion: HALAS establishes the first rigorous non-artificial benchmark for ASR hallucination detection and mitigation, revealing significant room for improvement in current detection methods.

Abstract: End-to-end Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) systems hallucinate on natural speech, yet existing mitigation methods are typically evaluated on non-speech or artificially corrupted audio. We introduce HALAS, the first human-annotated dataset of naturally occurring hallucinations from seven state-of-the-art ASR models on real unprocessed earnings call recordings. HALAS provides span-level labels, enabling analysis of hallucination patterns and their severity. Our analysis reveals strong cross-model vocabulary overlap and confirms that hallucinations also occur for almost correctly transcribed speech (characterized by a low Word Error Rate). The proposed benchmark with HALAS shows that the character and semantic-level metrics used as a proxy for hallucination detection reach 81% ROC-AUC, while state-of-the-art detection methods achieve an F1 score of only 53.1%. As such, HALAS establishes the first rigorous non-artificial benchmark for the detection and mitigation of ASR hallucinations.

</details>


### [379] [From Text Metrics to Model Internals: A Study of Whisper ASR Hallucination Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23060)
*Jan Jasiński,Mateusz Barański,Julitta Bartolewska,Marcin Witkowski,Konrad Kowalczyk*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: This paper studies hallucination detection in Whisper large v3 ASR models, comparing text-based, LLM-based, and internal decoder state probing methods. Probing internal decoder representations without reference transcripts yields the best performance, and a late-fusion meta-classifier combining text and internal-state outputs achieves optimal detection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hallucinations in ASR models produce fluent but incorrect transcriptions that degrade system performance and pose risks in downstream applications, yet robust detection of such errors remains a challenge.

Method: The study investigates three detection paradigms on real-speech human-annotated data: (1) text-based classifiers using metrics for text evaluation, (2) LLM-based detection with domain-specific prompt conditioning, and (3) probing Whisper's internal decoder representations across intermediate decoding layers. A late-fusion meta-classifier combining text and internal-state outputs is also evaluated.

Result: Text-based methods achieve high recall but degrade without reference transcripts. LLM-based detection improves precision with prompt conditioning but remains less competitive than lightweight text methods. Probing decoder representations without ground-truth references yields the strongest performance. The late-fusion meta-classifier achieves the best overall detection performance.

Conclusion: Hallucination traits are encoded across intermediate decoding layers of Whisper's decoder, and combining text-based features with internal decoder states via meta-classification provides optimal hallucination detection for ASR systems.

Abstract: Hallucinations of ASR models - fluent transcriptions with no basis in audio - degrade system performance and pose risks in downstream applications. Robust detection of such errors remains a challenge. This paper studies Whisper large v3 hallucination detection on real-speech human-annotated data across three paradigms: text-based, LLM-based, and internal decoder state probing. Text classifiers utilizing metrics for text evaluation achieve high recall but degrade without reference transcripts. LLM-based detection improves precision with domain-specific prompt conditioning, yet remains less competitive than the lightweight text-based methods. Probing Whisper's decoder representations, without a ground-truth reference, yields the strongest performance, revealing that hallucination traits are encoded across intermediate decoding layers. A late-fusion meta-classifier combining text and internal-state outputs achieves the best overall detection performance.

</details>


### [380] [Synthesizing the Lombard Effect: Multi-Level Control of Speech Clarity and Vocal Effort in TTS](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23176)
*Seymanur Akti,Alexander Waibel*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: A flow-matching based TTS model with pseudo-labels achieves continuous, disentangled control of vocal effort and articulation, enabling word-level emphasis and simulating Lombard effect (clearer speech in noise) with improved intelligibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To simulate the Lombard effect (humans speaking louder/clearer in challenging environments like noise or when talking to hearing-impaired listeners) in speech synthesis systems.

Method: A flow-matching based TTS model trained with vocal effort and articulation pseudo-labels, enabling continuous and disentangled control of these features plus word-level emphasis.

Result: Effective control of clarity-related acoustic features demonstrated; speech-in-noise experiments show successful simulation of intelligibility gains comparable to human clear speech in noisy conditions.

Conclusion: The proposed model successfully simulates the Lombard effect in TTS, providing fine-grained, interpretable control over vocal effort, articulation, and emphasis for improved speech clarity in challenging listening environments.

Abstract: Humans tend to speak louder and clearer in challenging environments, such as noisy conditions or when addressing hearingimpaired listeners, which is called Lombard effect. To simulate this behavior in speech synthesis systems, we introduce a flow-matching based text-to-speech (TTS) model trained with vocal effort and articulation pseudo-labels. The proposed model achieves continuous and disentangled control of vocal effort and articulation, while also enabling word-level emphasis for clarifying specific segments of an utterance. Experimental results show that these control mechanisms effectively improve clarityrelated acoustic features. Furthermore, speech-in-noise experiments demonstrate that our model successfully simulates the intelligibility gains of human clear speech in noisy conditions.

</details>


### [381] [The Watermark Shortcut: How Provenance Marking Sabotages Audio Deepfake Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23335)
*Nicolas M. Müller,Pascal Debus*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: Provenance watermarks on synthetic speech create a "watermark => fake" shortcut for detectors, causing three failures: generalization degradation, strip-to-evade, and mark-to-frame. This is fixable by watermarking both classes during training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To identify and characterize the previously unexamined liability where watermarking only synthetic speech creates a spurious correlation that degrades detector performance.

Method: White-box experiments with watermark-trained detectors and black-box tests of a commercial API (ElevenLabs), plus a proposed fix of retraining with watermarks on both real and synthetic speech.

Result: Watermark-trained detectors exhibit three failures: generalization degradation, strip-to-evade, and mark-to-frame (e.g., EER rises from 16% to 75%). Adding watermark to real speech disguises it as fake in a commercial API. Retraining with both classes watermarked restores clean behavior.

Conclusion: Provenance watermarking creates a spurious shortcut for detectors, but the issue is correctable by decorrelating the watermark from the fake class via balanced training.

Abstract: Provenance watermarking is increasingly treated as a safeguard for synthetic speech, whether built directly into speech-generation models such as Chatterbox, provided through dedicated techniques such as AudioSeal, or deployed by commercial platforms such as ElevenLabs. We identify a previously uncharacterized liability: when synthetic speech is watermarked and human speech is not, detectors trained alongside latch onto the watermark as a spurious "watermark => fake" shortcut. This single feature yields three coupled failures: generalization degradation (model performance deteriorates on unseen data), strip-to-evade (a watermarked fake escapes once unwatermarked), and mark-to-frame (watermarking a real voice flags it as fake). In a controlled white-box experiment, a watermark-trained detector shows all three (for example, mark-to-frame lifts Equal Error Rate from 16% to 75%). In a black-box test of a commercial API, we show that adding a watermark to real speech disguises it as fake. However, this shortcut is fixable: retraining with the watermark on both classes decorrelates it and restores clean behavior. We release experiment data as a paired clean-versus-watermarked corpus (WASP).

</details>


### [382] [Towards Reliable Audio Deepfake Attribution and Model Recognition: A Multi-Level Autoencoder-Based Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2508.02521)
*Andrea Di Pierno,Luca Guarnera,Dario Allegra,Sebastiano Battiato*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: LAVA is a hierarchical framework using a convolutional autoencoder trained on fake audio plus attention-enhanced latent representations to both detect and attribute audio deepfakes to their source models, achieving F1-scores over 95% for technology attribution and 96.31% macro F1 for model recognition across multiple benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Audio deepfakes threaten digital trust; while detection has advanced, attributing deepfakes to their source generative models remains underexplored yet critical for forensic analysis and accountability.

Method: A two-stage hierarchical framework: (1) a convolutional autoencoder trained solely on fake audio extracts attention-enhanced latent representations; (2) two specialized classifiers operate on these features—ADA for generation technology identification and ADMR for specific model instance recognition. Confidence-based rejection thresholds handle open-set conditions.

Result: ADA classifier achieves F1-scores over 95% across ASVspoof2021, FakeOrReal, and CodecFake; ADMR reaches 96.31% macro F1 across six classes. Robustness confirmed on unseen ASVspoof2019 LA attacks and error propagation analysis.

Conclusion: LAVA advances the field by introducing a supervised attribution and model recognition approach under open-set conditions, validated on public benchmarks with released code/models, demonstrating strong robustness and reliability.

Abstract: The proliferation of audio deepfakes poses a growing threat to trust in digital communications. While detection methods have advanced, attributing audio deepfakes to their source models remains an underexplored yet crucial challenge. In this paper we introduce LAVA (Layered Architecture for Voice Attribution), a hierarchical framework for audio deepfake detection and model recognition that leverages attention-enhanced latent representations extracted by a convolutional autoencoder trained solely on fake audio. Two specialized classifiers operate on these features: Audio Deepfake Attribution (ADA), which identifies the generation technology, and Audio Deepfake Model Recognition (ADMR), which recognize the specific generative model instance. To improve robustness under open-set conditions, we incorporate confidence-based rejection thresholds. Experiments on ASVspoof2021, FakeOrReal, and CodecFake show strong performance: the ADA classifier achieves F1-scores over 95% across all datasets, and the ADMR module reaches 96.31% macro F1 across six classes. Additional tests on unseen attacks from ASVpoof2019 LA and error propagation analysis confirm LAVA's robustness and reliability. The framework advances the field by introducing a supervised approach to deepfake attribution and model recognition under open-set conditions, validated on public benchmarks and accompanied by publicly released models and code. Models and code are available at https://www.github.com/adipiz99/lava-framework.

</details>


<div id='cs.AI'></div>

# cs.AI [[Back]](#toc)

### [383] [On the Identifiability of User Adaptation in Co-Adaptive Neural Interfaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20569)
*Philip Waggoner*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper analyzes identifiability in co-adaptive human-machine systems, showing that closed-loop encoder estimates reflect joint system properties rather than uniquely identifying user adaptation, and discusses conditions for proper identification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the fundamental problem that in co-adaptive systems, closed-loop estimates of user adaptation may be misleading because they capture properties of the entire human-machine system rather than the user's true adaptive behavior.

Method: The authors analyze identifiability theoretically in co-adaptive human-machine systems, examining how closed-loop encoder estimates relate to user adaptation and joint system dynamics.

Result: Closed-loop encoder estimates do not uniquely identify user adaptation; they reflect properties of the joint human-machine system instead.

Conclusion: Interpretation of behavioral adaptation in co-adaptive systems requires careful consideration of identifiability, and specific conditions must be established to achieve proper identification of user adaptation.

Abstract: We analyze identifiability in co-adaptive human-machine systems. We show that closed-loop encoder estimates do not uniquely identify user adaptation, but instead reflect properties of the joint system. We discuss implications for interpreting behavioral adaptation and propose conditions for identification.

</details>


### [384] [Beyond Fixed Budgets: Characterizing the Inelasticity and Limitations of Tree-of-Thought Reasoning Strategies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20599)
*Atkia Mahila,Avinash Maurya,M. Mustafa Rafique,Bogdan Nicolae*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Evaluates two Tree-of-Thought search methods (DPTS and SSDP) under varying compute budgets, model sizes, and problem difficulties, revealing DPTS suffers from cold-start bottleneck at low budgets while SSDP faces frontier depletion, suggesting need for adaptive search strategies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To systematically understand how different Tree-of-Thought search strategies behave under varying compute budgets, model sizes, and problem difficulties—a question that has received little systematic attention despite the growing interest in ToT methods for improving LLM reasoning.

Method: Evaluates two representative ToT methods: DPTS (Monte Carlo tree search based) and SSDP (semantic deduplication based), across two mathematical reasoning benchmarks (Math500 and GSM8K), two model scales (Llama-3B and Llama-8B), and four token budgets (3k–10k).

Result: DPTS shows strong scaling at higher budgets but suffers a cold-start bottleneck at low budgets due to insufficient exploration before value estimates become reliable. SSDP reaches candidate solutions efficiently but suffers from frontier depletion—its aggressive node merging permanently discards unexplored paths, preventing further improvement regardless of remaining budget.

Conclusion: Neither a fixed exploration strategy nor a fixed pruning strategy is sufficient across the compute continuum; effective search for scientific reasoning agents requires strategies that adapt their behavior based on search progress and available resources.

Abstract: Tree of Thought (ToT) search has become a promising direction for improving the reasoning capabilities of large language models, but deploying these methods in practice raises a question that has received little systematic attention: how do different search strategies behave under varying compute budgets, model sizes, and problem difficulties? In this work, we evaluate two representative ToT methods; DPTS, a Monte Carlo tree search based approach, and SSDP, a semantic deduplication based approach, across two mathematical reasoning benchmarks (Math500 and GSM8K), two model scales (Llama-3B and Llama-8B), and four token budgets (3k--10k). Our analysis reveals that the two methods exhibit limitations that pull in opposite directions. DPTS suffers from a cold-start bottleneck at low budgets: it requires sufficient exploration before its value estimates become reliable, making it a poor fit for resource-constrained settings despite strong scaling behavior at higher budgets. SSDP, on the other hand, reaches candidate solutions efficiently but is prone to frontier depletion; its aggressive node merging permanently discards unexplored paths, leaving it unable to improve regardless of how much budget remains. Together, these findings suggest that neither a fixed exploration strategy nor a fixed pruning strategy is sufficient across compute continuum. We argue that effective search for scientific reasoning agents requires strategies that can adapt their behavior based on search progress and available resources.

</details>


### [385] [The New Associationism: Lessons from Deep Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20600)
*Daniel Rothschild*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The success of modern AI, particularly supervised learning with error-driven mechanisms, supports a modest form of associationism as a model of human learning, though it requires more complex architectures than classical associationists envisioned.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To argue that modern AI successes vindicate associationist principles of learning (uniform, gradual, error-driven mechanisms across domains) while showing that deep learning architectures go beyond what classical associationism assumed.

Method: Conceptual analysis of supervised learning as a unifying principle across diverse AI systems (LLMs, game-playing agents), examining how feedback signals vary in difficulty to generate, and contrasting classical associationism with modern deep learning architectures.

Result: Supervised learning is found to underlie a wide range of contemporary AI systems, differing mainly in how much work is needed to produce the feedback signal, supporting associationist ideals while requiring more complex computational architectures.

Conclusion: AI supports a modest, genuine associationism where error-driven learning is central but operates within richer architectures; this defuses the classic objection that associationist mechanisms are too limited to explain human cognition.

Abstract: What can the success of modern AI tell us about how humans learn? This paper argues that taking AI seriously as a model of human learning supports a modest but genuine associationism. The central finding is that supervised learning -- learning driven by evaluative feedback -- underlies a surprisingly wide range of contemporary AI systems, from large language models to game-playing agents, differing primarily in how much work is required to generate the relevant feedback signal. This vindicates associationist ideals of a uniform, gradual, error-driven learning mechanism operating across domains, and defuses the once-influential argument that associationist mechanisms are too limited to account for human cognitive capacities. At the same time, the successes of deep learning depend on computational architectures that go well beyond anything classical associationists envisaged, and supervised learning operates within these as one component rather than a complete account of learning.

</details>


### [386] [Specifying AI-SDLC Processes: A Protocol Language for Human-Agent Boundaries](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20615)
*Ylli Prifti*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes a domain-specific language (DSL) for specifying AI-SDLC (Software Development Lifecycle) processes as formal protocols, defining human-agent responsibility boundaries, approval gates, and governance constraints. The language distinguishes policy from mechanism, introduces primitives like validation tokens and capability boundaries, and formalizes the 2+N team pattern. Key findings include that structural enforcement bounds failure rates better than behavioral compliance, and properties like Kleene closure of orchestration loops emerge naturally from the design.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: No specification language exists for expressing human-agent responsibility boundaries, approval gates, and governance constraints in AI-SDLC collaboration. Existing approaches (agent prompts, workflow management, BPMN, access control) are either subject to drift, target adjacent domains, or address only fragments of the problem.

Method: Design of a DSL with formal syntax, well-formedness conditions, operational semantics, and enforcement invariants. The language separates policy (declared intent) from mechanism (structural enforcement), and uses validation tokens and capability boundaries to bound process non-determinism.

Result: (1) Structural enforcement bounds failure rates at a weighted product of agent and validator rates, while behavioral compliance permits cumulative or near-saturating failure growth. (2) The 2+N team pattern formalizes Separation of Duties for AI-SDLC. (3) Kleene closure of orchestration loops and reflexive protocol-adherence validation emerge as design properties.

Conclusion: The contribution is positioned as a novel integration of concepts from multi-agent frameworks (MetaGPT), workflow specification (FlowAgent, BPMN), and capability-based security (SAGA). A working implementation demonstrates feasibility; empirical evaluation is noted as future work.

Abstract: AI agents now participate as first-class team members across the software development lifecycle, yet no specification language exists for expressing the human-agent responsibility boundaries, approval gates, and governance constraints this collaboration requires. Existing approaches encode process in agent prompts (subject to drift), target adjacent domains (workflow management, business processes), or address only fragments (access control, approval gates).
  We propose a domain-specific language for specifying AI-SDLC processes as protocols, with formal syntax, well-formedness conditions, operational semantics, and enforcement invariants. The language distinguishes policy (declared intent) from mechanism (structural enforcement), enabling implementations to bound process non-determinism through primitives such as validation tokens and capability boundaries.
  Three results follow. A failure rate analysis shows that structural enforcement bounds system failure rates at a weighted product of agent and validator rates, while behavioral compliance permits cumulative or near-saturating growth. The 2+N team pattern (two human-in-control roles plus N specialized agent members) formalizes classical Separation of Duties for AI-SDLC. Kleene closure of orchestration loops and reflexive protocol-adherence validation emerge as design properties rather than special-case constructs.
  We position the contribution against multi-agent frameworks (MetaGPT), workflow specification (FlowAgent, BPMN extensions), and capability-based security (SAGA): the novelty lies in the specific integration, not any single primitive. A working implementation demonstrates feasibility; empirical evaluation is future work.

</details>


### [387] [PEAR: Permutation-Equivariant Adaptive Routing Multi-Agent Debate](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20621)
*Yang Feng,Ziwei Xu,Xia Hu,Fengxiang He*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PEAR is a dynamic multi-agent debate protocol that uses permutation-equivariant adaptive routing to eliminate persistent positional biases and improve LLM reasoning accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fixed topologies in multi-agent debate introduce persistent positional biases, amplify unreliable agents, and cause high sensitivity to role assignments, limiting reliability.

Method: PEAR dynamically reconfigures communication roles and sparse topologies across consecutive debate rounds by strategically switching agent-to-role assignments based on evolving agent states, functioning as an equivariant sparse router.

Result: PEAR significantly improves average accuracy over strongest debate baselines across four reasoning benchmarks and six diverse LLM backbones.

Conclusion: PEAR preserves accuracy under agent relabeling, reduces routing complexity, improves generalization, and mitigates positional bias in multi-agent debate.

Abstract: Multi-agent debate improves the reliability of large language models (LLMs) through iterative peer critiques. However, fixed topologies often introduce persistent positional biases, amplify unreliable agents, and cause high sensitivity to role assignments. We introduce \textit{Permutation-Equivariant Adaptive Routing Multi-Agent Debate (PEAR)}, an inference-time protocol that dynamically reconfigures communication roles and sparse topologies across consecutive debate rounds. By strategically switching agent-to-role assignments based on evolving agent states, PEAR prevents any agent from permanently occupying a privileged network position or distributes influence more evenly across the debate. We theoretically characterize PEAR as an equivariant sparse router: it preserves accuracy under agent relabeling while reducing routing complexity and improving generalization. Comprehensive empirical evaluations across four reasoning benchmarks and six diverse LLM backbones demonstrate PEAR significantly improves average accuracy over the strongest debate baselines. The code is at https://github.com/EVIEHub/PEAR.

</details>


### [388] [Darwin Mobile Agent: A Roadmap for Self-Evolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20622)
*Daniel Beechey,Derek Yuen,Jianheng Liu,Dezhao Luo,Tiantian He,Weilin Luo,Jun Wang,Kun Shao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes that the path to general AI is to remove human priors and let intelligence emerge through interaction with complex environments, using mobile GUI as a proxy. It introduces Darwin Mobile Agent, an open-source infrastructure for autonomous reinforcement learning in mobile GUI domains, addressing data-collection bottlenecks via asynchronous agent-environment loops on parallel cloud phones, and outlines a roadmap to remove human priors from task curricula, outcome verification, and memory management.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
