Motivation: Current AI systems rely heavily on human priors (hand-crafted curricula, human verification, engineered memory), limiting their ability to achieve general, adaptive behaviour. The paper argues for systematically removing these priors to let intelligence emerge naturally from interaction with a sufficiently complex "Big World", following the "Bitter Lesson" philosophy.

Method: The authors propose the mobile GUI as a practical proxy for a "Big World" and develop Darwin Mobile Agent, an open-source infrastructure with an asynchronous agent-environment loop running across parallel cloud-phone instances to overcome data-collection bottlenecks in real-world mobile interactions.

Result: The Darwin infrastructure demonstrates the stability and scalability required for the first stage of the proposed roadmap—policy optimization in the GUI domain—validating its suitability as a foundation for autonomous reinforcement learning.

Conclusion: This work establishes the practical and theoretical foundations for moving toward truly autonomous, self-evolving GUI agents by systematically removing human priors from task curricula, outcome verification, and memory management.

Abstract: The goal of artificial intelligence is to create agents capable of general, adaptive behaviour in open-ended environments. Guided by the "Bitter Lesson", we argue that the most effective path toward this goal is to systematically remove human priors and allow intelligence to naturally emerge through interaction with a "Big World" that is orders of magnitude more complex than the agent itself. We propose the mobile Graphical User Interface (GUI) as a practical proxy for such a world and introduce Darwin Mobile Agent, an open-source infrastructure designed as a foundation for autonomous reinforcement learning in this domain. This framework addresses the data-collection bottleneck in real-world mobile interactions by using an asynchronous agent-environment loop across parallel cloud-phone instances. We further propose a conceptual roadmap to systematically remove human priors from three fundamental pillars of a self-evolving agent: task curricula, outcome verification, and memory management. We validate that the Darwin infrastructure provides the stability and scalability required for the first stage of this roadmap: policy optimisation in the GUI domain. This work establishes the practical and theoretical foundation necessary to move toward truly autonomous, self-evolving GUI agents.

</details>


### [389] [Path-dependent program induction under resource constraints explains human sequence learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20623)
*Hanqi Zhou,David G. Nagy,Peter Dayan,Charley M. Wu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper integrates rate-distortion theory with program induction to model how people build abstract, reusable knowledge from sequential experience under bounded cognitive resources, using a hierarchical Adaptor Grammar (HAG) that balances local and global knowledge. HAG outperforms alternatives in rate-distortion trade-offs and generalization, and fits human melodic sequence learning data better than other models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how humans construct abstract, reusable knowledge from sequential experience while operating under cognitive constraints (bounded memory and computation).

Method: Integrates rate-distortion theory with program induction in a hierarchical Adaptor Grammar (HAG) featuring local (within-task) and global (across-task) libraries constrained by memory and computation. Validated via simulations and an online melodic sequence-learning experiment with behavioral measures (recall errors, reaction times, continuation choices).

Result: HAG achieves better rate-distortion trade-offs and stronger generalization than fixed grammars or shallow chunking methods. In human experiments, recall errors showed systematic simplifications, reaction times increased at inferred program boundaries, and hierarchical libraries best explained individual differences in recall and continuation choices.

Conclusion: Structured learning can be viewed as bounded program induction, where the order of experience shapes future abstractions under cognitive resource constraints.

Abstract: How do people build abstract, reusable knowledge from sequential experience under bounded cognitive resources? To answer this question, we integrate rate-distortion theory with recent advances in program induction to describe how prior knowledge shapes which future structures are cheap to encode and easy to discover. We formalize this in a hierarchical Adaptor Grammar (HAG) with distinct local (within-task) and global (across-task) libraries, governed jointly by constraints on memory and computation. In simulations, HAG achieves better rate-distortion trade-offs and stronger generalization than fixed grammars or shallow chunking methods. In an online melodic sequence-learning experiment, participants' recall errors reflected systematic simplifications and reaction times increased at inferred program boundaries. Trial-by-trial fits further showed that hierarchical libraries best explained individual differences in both recall and out-of-sample continuation choices, outperforming all alternative models. These findings cast structured learning as bounded program induction in which the order of experience shapes future abstractions a learner builds.

</details>


### [390] [In LLM Reasoning, there is Irrationality on top of Value Misalignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20624)
*Kejiang Qian,Fengxiang He*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Even well-aligned LLMs fail to maximize aligned values during reasoning, formalized as "rational value risk"—a gap between deployed and optimal reasoning strategies. The risk is decomposed into estimation errors from finite candidates, prompts, and verifiers. Experiments across multiple model families show the risk is widespread, persists after alignment, is sensitive to inference strategies, and decreases with longer reasoning but with diminishing returns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite progress in aligning LLMs with target values during training, models may still fail to maximize those values during actual reasoning/inference. The paper aims to formalize and empirically demonstrate this misalignment gap.

Method: The authors formalize "rational value risk" as the expected utility discrepancy between a model's actual reasoning strategy and the rational optimal strategy. They decompose estimation error into three components: finite candidates, finite prompts, and imperfect verifiers. Experiments are conducted across Llama-3.1, Qwen-2.5, Tülu-3 (7B-72B), GPT-5.2, GPT-5.5, and DeepSeek-V4 models on UltraFeedback, AlpacaEval, GSM8K, MATH, HumanEval, and MathArena benchmarks.

Result: (1) Rational value risk is widespread across models and tasks. (2) Value alignment reduces but does not eliminate the risk. (3) The risk is highly sensitive to the inference-time reasoning strategy. (4) Longer reasoning improves rationality but with diminishing returns.

Conclusion: Alignment in training is insufficient for ensuring optimal reasoning at inference time. There remains a measurable rationality gap that must be addressed through improved inference strategies, not just post-training alignment.

Abstract: Significant progress has been made in aligning LLMs with target value functions. We argue that, even when an LLM has been well aligned in (post-)training, it may still fail to maximise the aligned value in reasoning. We mathematically formalise this gap as rational value risk: the utility discrepancy between a model's deployed reasoning strategy and its rational counterpart, which is defined to be the responses that maximise expected utility in the steepest direction. The estimation error of rational value risk is further decomposed into three components from finite candidates, finite prompts, and imperfect verifiers. Extensive experiments are conducted, covering models Llama-3.1, Qwen-2.5, T{\"}ulu-3 families (7B-72B), GPT-5.2, GPT-5.5, and DeepSeek-V4, and benchmarks UltraFeedback, AlpacaEval, GSM8K, MATH, HumanEval, and MathArena. The results validate that (1) rational value risk is widespread; (2) value alignment can reduce, but cannot eliminate, it; (3) the risk is highly sensitive to inference-time reasoning strategy; and (4) longer reasoning improves rationality with diminishing returns. The code is at https://github.com/EVIEHub/LLM-Rationality.

</details>


### [391] [AlphaMemo: Structured Search-Process Memory for Self-Evolving Alpha Mining Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20625)
*Hang Yu,Zifan Zheng,Jeff Z. Pan,Tongliang Liu,Zhiyong Wang,Fengxiang He*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AlphaMemo is a self-evolving LLM agent for alpha mining that uses Structured Search-Process Memory to record reusable edit motifs from AST differences, with confidence-gated residual memory and asymmetric veto control, achieving improved performance on CSI 500 and S&P 500.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agents for alpha mining face a combinatorial search space, noisy non-stationary feedback, redundant discoveries, and overfitting risks from naively reusing past successes. Existing approaches that memorize only final factors or full trajectories are insufficient.

Method: AlphaMemo records reusable evidence about which edit motifs work or fail under specific parent-factor contexts. It extracts motifs from AST differences, applies confidence-gated residual memory on top of a search-ledger prior, and uses asymmetric veto control to suppress high-confidence failure patterns.

Result: Experiments on CSI 500 and S&P 500 show improved out-of-sample performance and fixed-budget discovery efficiency. Ablations validate the roles of residual learning, confidence gating, AST-diff motifs, and veto memory.

Conclusion: AlphaMemo effectively addresses key challenges in LLM-based alpha mining through structured memory mechanisms, demonstrating superior factor discovery and robustness against overfitting.

Abstract: LLM agents are promising for alpha mining via combining financial priors, symbolic reasoning, executable factor generation, and feedback-driven refinement. Yet, they face a combinatorial search space, noisy non-stationary feedback, redundant discoveries, and overfitting risks from naively reusing past successes. To address these challenges, we propose AlphaMemo, a self-evolving alpha mining agent with Structured Search-Process Memory. Rather than memorizing only final factors or full trajectories, AlphaMemo records reusable evidence about which edit motifs work or fail under specific parent-factor contexts. It extracts motifs from Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) differences, applies confidence-gated residual memory on top of a search-ledger prior, and uses asymmetric veto control to suppress high-confidence failure patterns. Experiments on CSI 500 and S\&P 500 show improved out-of-sample performance and fixed-budget discovery efficiency, with ablations validating the roles of residual learning, confidence gating, AST-diff motifs, and veto memory. Code is at https://github.com/jarrettyu/AlphaMemo.

</details>


### [392] [Latent Goal Prediction from Language for Model-Based Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20627)
*Samuel Barbeau,Simon Roy,Giovanni Beltrame,Christian Desrosiers,Nicolas Thome*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LAGO is a framework that predicts sequences of latent subgoals from language instructions and uses them for action-conditioned rollouts, enabling robust long-horizon planning by dynamically decomposing instructions into locally tractable latent subgoals with a soft minimum trajectory cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Planning with world models suffers from compounding prediction errors and difficulty defining optimizable goals: visual targets offer precise local gradients but poor distant guidance, while language is flexible yet limited by noisy cross-modal alignment or dependence on large generative models unsuitable for the high-sampling nature of model-based planning.

Method: LAGO predicts both sequences of intermediate goal states from language instructions and action-conditioned rollouts within the same latent space. It dynamically decomposes instructions into explicitly predicted, locally tractable latent subgoals, updating them online and using a soft minimum trajectory cost during planning.

Result: Across multiple environments and planning horizons, LAGO avoids the sharp degradation of prior methods, achieving robust and precise long-horizon planning purely from language.

Conclusion: LAGO bridges the precision of visual goals with the flexibility of text-guided control, enabling coherent latent trajectory following over long horizons without compounding prediction errors.

Abstract: Planning with world models is bottlenecked by compounding prediction errors and the difficulty of defining optimizable goals. Visual targets provide precise local gradients but poor distant guidance, while language is flexible yet limited by noisy cross-modal alignment or dependence on large generative models unsuited for the high-sampling nature of model-based planning. To address these challenges, we introduce Latent Goal Prediction from Language (LAGO), a framework that predicts both sequences of intermediate goal states from language instructions and action-conditioned rollouts, all within the same latent space. Rather than optimizing toward a single global objective, LAGO dynamically decomposes instructions into explicitly predicted, locally tractable latent subgoals. By updating these subgoals online and using a soft minimum trajectory cost during planning, LAGO enables an agent to follow coherent latent trajectories over long horizons. Evaluation across multiple environments planning horizons shows that LAGO avoids the sharp degradation of prior methods. By achieving robust and precise long-horizon planning purely from language, LAGO bridges the precision of visual goals with the flexibility of text-guided control.

</details>


### [393] [Human Decision-Making with AI Assistance under Correlated Features](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20628)
*Yanru Guan,Naveen Raman,Fei Fang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies how AI should recommend diagnostic tests to balance immediate decision quality and long-term human learning when features are correlated. It shows that stationary policies fail under correlation, optimal policies follow an explore-then-commit structure, computing the optimal policy is NP-hard, and a dynamic programming algorithm can solve finite-horizon cases with a near-optimal approximation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Humans increasingly rely on AI assistance for decision-making (e.g., doctors using AI-recommended diagnostic tests). The key question is which tests AI should recommend to balance short-term decision quality with long-term human learning, especially when features (test results) are correlated—a realistic scenario not addressed by prior work assuming independent features.

Method: The authors prove that stationary policies become arbitrarily poor under feature correlations through theoretical analysis. They prove that optimal policies must follow an explore-then-commit structure, derive a dynamic programming algorithm for finite horizons, and develop an approximation using shorter-horizon planning with a stationary suffix. Empirical validation shows that stronger correlation leads to longer exploration.

Result: - Stationary policies are optimal only under independent features but perform arbitrarily poorly under correlation. - Any optimal policy follows an explore-then-commit structure with exploration length depending on correlation degree. - Computing the optimal policy is NP-hard. - The dynamic programming algorithm finds optimal finite-horizon policies. - The approximation achieves near-optimal performance. - Stronger feature correlation empirically leads to longer exploration phases.

Conclusion: Feature correlations fundamentally change the optimal strategy for AI-assisted decision-making: they necessitate an explore-then-commit approach rather than stationary policies. The paper provides both theoretical foundations and practical algorithms for this problem, with the exploration-exploitation trade-off governed by the strength of feature correlations.

Abstract: Humans increasingly make decisions with AI assistance; for example, doctors may follow AI-recommended diagnostic tests and base their diagnoses on the results. A natural question is which tests should AI recommend to balance short-term decision quality and long-term human learning when different features (e.g., test results) are correlated. While prior work establishes that stationary policies that recommend the same tests repeatedly are optimal when features are independent, we prove that feature correlations lead such policies to perform arbitrarily poorly. Instead, we prove that any optimal policy must follow an explore-then-commit structure; initially, the AI should offer diverse tests so humans can learn accurate feature coefficients, then the AI should commit to a single set of tests, with exploration length that depends on the degree of feature correlation. We prove that computing the optimal policy is NP-hard and derive a dynamic programming-based algorithm that finds the optimal policy for finite horizons. We additionally develop an approximation that plans for shorter horizons and appends a stationary suffix, achieving near-optimal performance. Our empirical results complement our theory by showing that stronger feature correlation leads to longer exploration phases.

</details>


### [394] [Harnessing Agent Skills: Architectural Patterns and a Reference Architecture for Skill-Mediated LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20631)
*Boming Xia,Liming Zhu,Zhenchang Xing,Qinghua Lu,Dino Sejdinovic,Xiwei Xu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces the concept of "skill-in-use" for LLM agents, presents ten architectural patterns (five core, five supporting) for skill harnessing, and synthesizes them into a four-layer reference architecture (Supply Chain, Mediation, Execution Control, Evidence & Feedback), evaluated across 8 systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Agent skills externalise reusable behavioural knowledge as static artefacts, but their architectural responsibilities only emerge at runtime when bound to context, authority, and interpreted by a stochastic agent. The gap between static skill artefacts and dynamic "skill-in-use" needs systematic architectural treatment.

Method: Empirical grounding of ten architectural patterns (five core, five supporting) for skill harnessing, synthesized into a reference architecture with four responsibility layers, evaluated through cross-instantiation across 8 selected systems.

Result: A catalogue of ten empirically grounded architectural patterns and a four-layer reference architecture (Supply Chain, Mediation, Execution Control, Evidence & Feedback) for skill harnessing in LLM agent systems.

Conclusion: The patterns and reference architecture provide a vocabulary and diagnostic frame for analysing skill-harnessing responsibilities across agent systems, governing the transition from skill artefacts to skill-in-use.

Abstract: Agent skills externalise reusable agent-facing behavioural knowledge and guidance as persistent artefacts that can be discovered, activated, and interpreted by LLM agents. Although a skill artefact is static at rest, its architectural responsibilities arise in use, when the artefact is selected for a run, bound to context and authority constraints, interpreted by a stochastic agent, and recorded as run evidence. We call this run-specific relation skill-in-use. This paper studies agent skill harnessing: the architectural responsibilities that govern the transition from skill artefacts to skill-in-use, bound the executable consequences associated with skill-in-use, and capture evidence for attribution, verification, repair, and evolution. This paper provides a catalogue of ten empirically grounded architectural patterns (five core, five supporting) for skill harnessing and synthesises them into a reference architecture with four responsibility layers: Supply Chain, Mediation, Execution Control, and Evidence & Feedback. We evaluate the architecture through cross-instantiation across 8 selected systems. The resulting patterns and reference architecture provide a vocabulary and diagnostic frame for analysing skill-harnessing responsibilities across agent systems.

</details>


### [395] [DEMM-Bench: A Cross-Regime Benchmark for Agent-Runtime Governance-Evidence Sufficiency](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20634)
*Oleg Solozobov*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DEMM-Bench is a cross-regime benchmark that evaluates whether agent-runtime evidence records (traces, ledgers, provenance graphs, etc.) are sufficient to reconstruct decision-level governance properties, using the Decision Evidence Maturity Model (DEMM). Across 64 manuscript cases, it shows that existing baselines overclaim on 50-75% of cases, while a property-level scorer achieves zero overclaim with 56.25% mean Property Sufficiency Accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Agent-runtime systems produce various evidence records (traces, ledgers, policy logs, etc.), but these containers do not necessarily answer governance questions about specific decisions. A benchmark is needed to measure whether records are sufficient to reconstruct decision-level properties.

Method: DEMM-Bench normalizes eight evidence regimes through adapters, asks property questions across eight dimensions (actor, authority, action, policy, decision basis, resource touch, lifecycle context, verification strength), and applies eight deterministic degradation conditions. It evaluates 64 manuscript cases with trace-present, schema-present, ledger-present baselines and a redacted property-level candidate scorer.

Result: Trace-present and schema-present baselines overclaim on 75% of cases; ledger-present overclaims on 50%. The redacted property-level candidate scorer achieves zero overclaim with 56.25% mean Property Sufficiency Accuracy.

Conclusion: The deposited package (64-case dataset, construction-oracle labels, baselines, adapters) enables reproducible evaluation of decision-evidence maturity across heterogeneous agent-runtime evidence substrates, demonstrating that current evidence containers are insufficient for governance questions and that property-level assessment is needed.

Abstract: Agent-runtime systems emit traces, ledgers, provenance graphs, policy logs, delegation tokens, cache events, and tool-firewall records, but those containers do not necessarily answer governance questions about a specific decision. DEMM-Bench is a cross-regime benchmark for agent-runtime governance-evidence sufficiency, grounded in the Decision Evidence Maturity Model (DEMM): it measures whether records across eight evidence regimes are sufficient to reconstruct decision-level properties rather than merely present. The benchmark normalizes the regimes through adapters, asks property questions over actor, authority, action, policy, decision basis, resource touch, lifecycle context, and verification strength, and applies eight deterministic degradation conditions. Across 64 manuscript cases, trace-present and schema-present baselines overclaim on 75% of cases, ledger-present overclaims on 50%, and the redacted property-level candidate scorer has zero overclaim with 56.25% mean Property Sufficiency Accuracy. The deposited package provides the 64-case dataset, construction-oracle labels, baselines, and adapters, supporting reproducible evaluation of decision-evidence maturity across heterogeneous agent-runtime evidence substrates.

</details>


### [396] [SkillHarness: Harnessing Safe Skills for Computer-Use Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20636)
*Yurun Chen,Biao Yi,Keting Yin,Shengyu Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SkillHarness is a framework for safe skill learning and reuse in dynamic environments, reducing unsafe skill rates by 57.1% through safety-constrained interaction modeling, skill boundary identification, and selective skill reuse.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing CUA skill learning methods assume static, safe environments, overlooking risks from adversarial interactions (e.g., prompt injections) and environmental dynamics (e.g., pop-ups), leading to risky skill learning and brittle execution in real-world dynamic settings.

Method: SkillHarness models skill learning as a safety-constrained interaction process. It introduces (1) **skill boundary** leveraging multi-source supervision signals to identify safe skills from trajectories, (2) **self-improving safety constraints** throughout the skill lifecycle, and (3) **selective skill reuse** where tasks decompose according to context and complete via selective activation of skill subsets.

Result: SkillHarness reduces the unsafe rate of learned skills by 57.1% and consistently improves execution stability under dynamic environmental changes, outperforming existing baselines.

Conclusion: SkillHarness effectively enables safe skill learning and utilization for CUAs in dynamic environments through safety-constrained interaction modeling and context-aware selective skill reuse.

Abstract: Computer-Use Agents (CUAs) are increasingly deployed in dynamic interactive environments, creating a growing need for continual skill learning during interaction. Recent approaches address this challenge by learning reusable skills from successful trajectories. However, these skill learning methods largely assume static and safe environments, overlooking risks from adversarial interactions (e.g., prompt injections) and environmental dynamics (e.g., pop-ups). In dynamic settings, such assumptions can lead to risky skill learning and brittle execution, undermining the reliability of CUAs. This raises the question: how can CUAs learn and use skills safely in dynamic environments? To address this problem, we propose SkillHarness, a framework for safe skill harnessing in dynamic environments. SkillHarness moves beyond static skill abstractions by modeling skill learning and utilization as a safety-constrained interaction process. Specifically, we introduce the skill boundary that leverages multi-source supervision signals to identify safe skills from interaction trajectories, and construct self-improving safety constraints throughout the skill lifecycle. In addition, SkillHarness introduces selective skill reuse, where tasks are guided to decompose according to context and completed through the selective activation of skill subsets. Our experiments demonstrate that SkillHarness significantly reduces the unsafe rate of learned skills by 57.1% and consistently improves execution stability under dynamic environmental changes, outperforming existing baselines.

</details>


### [397] [Constituency Optimisation Through Hamiltonian Representation Of Mandates (COTHROM): Algorithmic Redistricting of Irish Election Boundaries](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20637)
*Ruaidhrí Campion,Matthew Fenlon,Joshua Cooney Mercedal,Casey Farren-Colloty,Eliza Somerville,Michael A. J. Mitchell*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: First computational framework for Irish electoral redistricting under PR-STV, using statistical physics (Potts Hamiltonian), MCMC, simulated annealing, and MCDA/Pareto optimality to optimise constitutional requirements. Applied to County Cork, it consistently outperforms existing boundaries.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Electoral redistricting in Ireland's PR-STV system involves an enormous configuration space where constitutional objectives (e.g., proportional representation, compactness) are often in tension. No systematic computational framework existed to optimise across these multiple requirements while making trade-offs explicit and quantifiable.

Method: (1) Formulate redistricting as a Potts Hamiltonian with constitutional objectives as energy terms; (2) minimise objective function via MCMC and simulated annealing; (3) use coupling constants as proxies for objective weightings; (4) apply Multi-Criterion Decision Analysis (MCDA) and Pareto Optimality to resolve ambiguity in weighting choices.

Result: In County Cork, COTHROM consistently improves on existing legal constituency boundaries across a range of objective weightings for proportional representation and compactness.

Conclusion: The framework provides a systematic, quantifiable approach to Irish redistricting that makes trade-offs explicit and demonstrably outperforms current boundaries, offering a principled tool for future redistricting processes.

Abstract: Electoral redistricting in Ireland's Proportional Representation Single Transferable Vote (PR-STV) system faces the challenge of selecting an optimally representative set of electoral boundaries from an enormous set of possible configurations, and where ``representative'' is a delicate balance of constitutional objectives that are often in tension with one another. We present the first computational framework for Irish electoral redistricting that systematically optimises across multiple constitutional requirements while making trade-offs explicit and quantifiable. The electoral redistricting problem is parsed using statistical physics, where constitutional objectives are considered as terms in a Potts Hamiltonian. Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) methods and simulated annealing are employed to minimise this objective function, systematically exploring this configuration space, with coupling constants as proxies for objective weightings. Multi Criterion Decision Analysis (MCDA) and Pareto Optimality is then utilised to remedy the ambiguity in choosing a certain objective weighting combination over others. With respect to proportional representation and compactness objectives evaluated in County Cork, COTHROM consistently improves on the existing legal constituency boundaries for a range of objective weightings.

</details>


### [398] [RIZZ: Routing Interactions to Near Zero-Interference Zones for Continual Adaptation of Black-Box Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20638)
*Sonali Goel,Pranav Vaidhyanathan,Lucas Schorling,Natalia Ares,Maike Osborne*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: RIZZ is a continual black-box adaptation framework for compound LLM systems that uses verifier-gated memory, dynamic routing, and prompt compilation to enable online adaptation without model weight access, outperforming baselines on competitive benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing black-box adaptation methods (single prompt optimization, undifferentiated memory, rollout-heavy search) struggle with nonstationary input streams, sparse feedback, and task contamination across families, motivating a need for interference-controlled continual adaptation in long-lived LLM agents.

Method: RIZZ organizes input streams into dynamically spawned memory branches. A context-aware router selects/creates branches at inference, compiling branch-local, global, graph-structured, and working-memory context into a bounded prompt. After model actions, task verifiers score outputs; only verified interactions update memory, promote/demote rules, or create anti-patterns — all via natural-language feedback without weight access.

Result: The framework outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on competitive benchmarks under online adaptation settings with context budgets.

Conclusion: RIZZ enables effective continual adaptation for black-box LLM agents through interference-controlled memory and routing, demonstrating that persistent natural-language feedback can drive improvement without weight access.

Abstract: Large language models are increasingly deployed as long-lived agents that must adapt across users, tasks, domains, modalities, and feedback regimes without access to model weights. Existing black-box adaptation methods typically optimize a single prompt, maintain an undifferentiated memory, or rely on repeated rollout-heavy search. However, these designs struggle when streams of input are nonstationary, feedback is sparse, and failures from one task family can contaminate behavior on another. We introduce RIZZ (Routing Interactions to Near Zero-interference Zones), a continual adaptation framework for compound language-model systems that learns entirely through verifier-gated memory, routing, and prompt compilation. RIZZ organizes input streams into dynamically spawned memory branches. At inference time, either while online or offline, a context-aware router selects or creates a branch that retrieves branch-local, global, graph-structured, and working-memory context, which is compiled into a bounded prompt together with retrieved task evidence. After the model acts, task verifiers score the output, and only verified interactions can update memory, promote reusable rules, demote harmful rules, or create anti-patterns. This yields a black-box agent that improves through persistent natural-language feedback while explicitly controlling interference. RIZZ targets the regime where adaptation must occur online under context budgets. Finally, we demonstrate the effectiveness of our framework against state-of-the-art baselines on competitive benchmarks.

</details>


### [399] [An LLM-Explainable DRL Framework for Passenger-Directed Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20640)
*Ouided Braoui,Meriem Bouali,Nadir Farhi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes a framework combining DRL (Dueling Double Deep Q-Network) for adaptive driving control with LLM-based explainability modules to enhance transparency and trust in autonomous vehicles. The system handles three driving modes (fast, comfort, stop), switches reliably between them, and generates safety-oriented explanations, particularly effective when passenger requests are delayed or overridden for safety.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Public trust in autonomous vehicles is limited due to lack of transparency in their decision-making processes.

Method: Combines DRL (Dueling Double Deep Q-Network) for adaptive driving control with LLM-based explainability modules to interpret passenger requests, determine explanation timing, and generate safety-oriented justifications.

Result: DRL agents demonstrated stable learning, safe traffic rule compliance, and reliable mode switching. The framework balances safety, adaptability, and explainability, and is most effective when requests are delayed or overridden due to safety constraints.

Conclusion: The framework serves as a proof of concept for integrating RL decision-making and LLMs to improve transparency and trust in autonomous driving systems.

Abstract: Autonomous vehicles offer the potential for safer and more efficient mobility, yet public trust remains limited due to the lack of transparency in their decision-making. This work addresses this issue by combining deep reinforcement learning (DRL) for adaptive driving control with large language model (LLM)-based explainability modules designed to communicate agent behavior to passengers. DRL agents were trained in simulation using a Dueling Double Deep Q-Network to follow distinct driving requests: \textit{fast}, \textit{comfort}, and \textit{stop}. They demonstrated stable learning, safe compliance with traffic rules, and reliable switching between modes within a single trip. In parallel, LLM modules were introduced to interpret passenger requests, determine when explanations were needed, and generate concise, safety-oriented justifications. Results show that this framework, serving as a proof of concept for integrating RL decision-making and LLMs, balances safety, adaptability, and explainability, and is most effective when requests are delayed or overridden due to safety constraints.

</details>


### [400] [Hypothesis-Disciplined Multi-Agent Automated Formalization of Asymptotic Statistical Theory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20642)
*Tingzhou Wei,Zeyu Zheng,Ethan X. Fang,Junwei Lu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces a multi-agent, hypothesis-disciplined pipeline for formalizing asymptotic statistical theory in Lean 4, producing axiom-clean, source-faithful proofs of parametric and semi-parametric asymptotic distribution and efficiency results, available on GitHub.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Asymptotic statistical theory is difficult to formalize due to its complex mix of convergence statements, asymptotic expansions, functional analysis, and the large gap between regularity conditions and existing Lean 4 infrastructure.

Method: A hypothesis-disciplined Lean 4 formalization pipeline with multiple agents: a manager coordinating seven specialist roles (proof planning, skeleton scaffolding, Mathlib reconnaissance, proof construction, integration, independent review, audit), with a key Auditor agent enforcing hypothesis-disciplined audit—anchoring every theorem hypothesis and concept-layer field to source mathematical prose, justifying as Lean encoding adapters, marking as source-implied, or rejecting unsupported strengthenings.

Result: A systematic formalization of asymptotic statistical theory, covering parametric and semi-parametric models' asymptotic distribution and efficiency results. The Lean development is axiom-clean and source-faithful, with Lean-checked and human-audited proofs, organized to separate theorem-agnostic infrastructure and statistical concept definitions from theorem-specific assembly.

Conclusion: The proposed multi-agent pipeline successfully overcomes the challenges of formalizing asymptotic statistical theory in Lean 4, producing a clean, faithful, and audited formalization that separates reusable infrastructure from theorem-specific code.

Abstract: Asymptotic statistical theory is a challenging domain for AI-assisted formalization: its central results mix convergence statements, asymptotic expansions, functional analysis, and regularity conditions that have a large gap from existing infrastructure in Lean 4 formalization. To address these challenges, we propose a hypothesis-disciplined Lean 4 formalization pipeline built from multiple agents: a manager that coordinates seven specialist roles for proof planning, skeleton scaffolding, Mathlib reconnaissance, proof construction, integration, independent review, and audit. The main methodological discipline is the hypothesis-disciplined audit, implemented by the Auditor agent: every main-theorem hypothesis and concept-layer field must be anchored in the source mathematical prose, justified as a Lean encoding adapter, marked as source-implied, or rejected as an unsupported strengthening. Using this workflow, we build a systematic formalization of asymptotic statistical theory, especially the parametric and semi-parametric models' asymptotic distribution and efficiency results. The resulting Lean development is axiom-clean and source-faithful, with Lean-checked and human-audited proofs of core parametric and semi-parametric theorems organized so that theorem-agnostic infrastructure and statistical concept definitions are separated from theorem-specific assembly. The formalization results are available at https://github.com/junwei-lu/Lean-Asymptotic-Statistical-Theory.

</details>


### [401] [SPARC: A Multi-Agent System for Electrical Circuit Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20643)
*Mushtari Sadia,Zhenning Yang,Umme Habiba Lamia,Nishat Shawrin,Ang Chen,Amrita Roy Chowdhury*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SPARC is a multi-agent system that answers circuit diagram questions by using LLM agents to create and run physics-based simulations, achieving 83% accuracy with up to 58% absolute improvement over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Electrical circuit diagram QA requires complex mathematical reasoning, which multimodal LLMs struggle with.

Method: SPARC employs a multi-agent framework where LLM agents synthesize, execute, and analyze simulation programs grounded in physics-based executable simulations.

Result: Achieves 83% accuracy, with up to 58% absolute improvement over baselines, and enables systematic error diagnosis.

Conclusion: Grounding reasoning in executable physics-based simulations significantly improves accuracy and reliability for circuit diagram QA tasks.

Abstract: Electrical circuit diagram QA tasks require complex mathematical reasoning, which remains challenging for multimodal LLMs. We present SPARC, a multi-agent system that answers questions over circuit diagrams by grounding reasoning in executable physics-based simulations. SPARC uses LLM agents to synthesize, execute, and analyze simulation programs, improving accuracy and reliability by design. It achieves 83% accuracy, with up to a 58% absolute improvement over baselines, while enabling systematic error diagnosis.

</details>


### [402] [Bridging Multi-Valued Heuristics and Dimensionality Reduction in Multi-Objective Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20644)
*Maya Wolff,Ariel Felner,Oren Salzman*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper addresses the problem of integrating multi-valued heuristics (MVHs) with dimensionality reduction (DR) in multi-objective shortest-path (MOSP) algorithms. It shows that naive combination leads to unsound search, proposes a theoretical baseline (NAMOA*_dr-mvh) enforcing consistency, and introduces L-NAMOA*_dr-mvh, a lazy optimistic approach that preserves correctness with admissible MVHs. The method achieves over 10x speedups on certain benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional MOSP algorithms use single-valued heuristics (SVHs) that provide safe lower bounds but fail to capture Pareto frontier trade-offs, yielding weak search guidance. Multi-valued heuristics (MVHs) offer richer approximations but integrating them with dimensionality reduction (DR)—which modern MOSP algorithms depend on—introduces subtle correctness issues, as naive combination destroys ordering invariants and leads to unsound/incomplete search.

Method: The authors first develop NAMOA*_dr-mvh, a theoretical baseline that enforces heuristic consistency to restore correctness. Then, as the main contribution, they propose L-NAMOA*_dr-mvh, which uses a lazy, optimistic approach to DR: it dynamically detects and repairs local ordering violations, preserving exact correctness with only an admissible MVH without requiring full consistency.

Result: Across benchmarks, L-NAMOA*_dr-mvh matches or improves state-of-the-art MOSP algorithms, achieving speedups of over 10x in instances where the MVH's additional guidance enables stronger pruning.

Conclusion: The paper provides the first theoretical frameworks for safely integrating MVHs with DR in MOSP search, demonstrating that lazy optimistic DR can both preserve correctness and yield substantial performance gains.

Abstract: Multi-objective shortest-path (MOSP) algorithms traditionally rely on single-valued heuristics (SVHs), which associate each state with a single admissible cost vector. While SVHs provide safe lower bounds, they fail to capture the trade-off structure of the Pareto frontier and often yield weak search guidance. Multi-valued heuristics (MVHs) address this limitation by mapping states to sets of cost estimates, enabling a richer approximation of possible trade-offs. Modern MOSP algorithms are highly dependent on dimensionality reduction (DR) techniques to efficiently perform dominance checks. However, integrating MVHs with DR introduces subtle correctness challenges. We show that naively combining DR with MVHs destroys the ordering invariants required for DR, leading to unsound and incomplete search. To address this issue, we develop the first theoretical frameworks for safely integrating MVHs with DR. First, we introduce $\text{NAMOA}^*{\text{dr}\text{-}\text{mvh}}$, a theoretical baseline that restores search correctness by enforcing heuristic consistency. Recognizing the practical limitations of this approach, we then introduce our primary contribution, $\text{L}\text{-}\text{NAMOA}^*{\text{dr}\text{-}\text{mvh}}$. This algorithm employs a "lazy," optimistic approach to DR, preserving exact correctness with only an admissible MVH by dynamically detecting and repairing local ordering violations. Across a range of benchmarks, $\text{L}\text{-}\text{NAMOA}^*{\text{dr}\text{-}\text{mvh}}$ matches or improves over state-of-the-art MOSP algorithms, and achieves speedups of over 10x in instances where the additional guidance provided by the MVH translates into stronger pruning.

</details>


### [403] [Learning Splitting Heuristics for Parallel String Solvers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20656)
*Chenhao Gao,Peisen Yao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces a data-driven approach to automatically generate splitting heuristics for parallel string constraint solving (cube-and-conquer), demonstrating improved performance over handcrafted heuristics in Z3seq and Z3str4.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: String constraint solvers face undecidable constraints and complex real-world problems. Multi-core architectures offer parallelism opportunities, but manually designing splitting heuristics for cube-and-conquer is labor-intensive and often sub-optimal.

Method: The authors frame splitting atom selection as a learning task using features from input formulas and dynamic solver execution data. They implement the learned heuristics in Z3seq and Z3str4 string solvers.

Result: The learned heuristics outperform manually designed heuristics in both number of solved formulas and average solving time.

Conclusion: Data-driven automatic generation of splitting heuristics is effective for parallel string constraint solving, offering a practical alternative to manual heuristic design.

Abstract: String constraint solvers are crucial for reasoning about string-manipulating programs. However, many practical string constraints are undecidable, and real-world applications often present complex constraints that challenge current solvers. The rise of multi-core architectures offers an opportunity for parallel solving. A key parallel solving method is \emph{cube-and-conquer}, in which the quality of splitting heuristics is critical to effectively dividing the search space. Unfortunately, manually designing the heuristics is labor-intensive, and handcrafted heuristics are often sub-optimal. This paper introduces a data-driven approach to automatically generating splitting heuristics. We frame the problem of selecting a splitting atom as a learning task, using features from input formulas and dynamic data from solver execution. We implement this approach in two popular string solvers, Z3seq and Z3str4, demonstrating that the learned heuristics outperform manually designed ones in the number of solved formulas and the average solving time.

</details>


### [404] [A-Evolve-Training: Autonomous Post-Training of a 30B Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20657)
*Zhan Shi,Bing He,Yisi Sang,Hanqing Lu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: An autonomous system post-trained a 30B Nemotron model across four rounds over weeks, achieving a score of 0.86 vs. the top human submission's 0.87 on the Nemotron-Reasoning Challenge leaderboard. Crucially, the loop detected its dev metric had become misleading on the weakest domain and autonomously revised its search policy, demonstrating discovery rather than mere optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Post-training frontier models currently requires weeks of human labor (proposing data/recipe changes, launching runs, reading evals). The authors aim to automate this entire loop with no human in the loop, testing whether an autonomous system can perform end-to-end post-training at scale.

Method: An autonomous loop that iteratively proposes data and recipe changes, launches training runs, reads evaluations, and decides what to keep, running across four rounds over multiple weeks to post-train a 30B Nemotron model. The system can revise its own search policy dynamically (e.g., stop maximizing a dev metric that became misleading).

Result: The autonomously produced model scored 0.86 against the top human submission's 0.87 on the public NVIDIA Nemotron-Reasoning Challenge leaderboard (8th of ~4000 entries). The loop detected its dev metric had decoupled from external performance on the weakest domain, revised its policy to lower the proxy while improving the external target. The system also post-trained 120B and 550B Nemotron models, though no public human baseline exists for comparison.

Conclusion: This is the first publicly reported autonomous post-training run at frontier-model scale (prior public demonstrations were at GPT-2-class ~124M budgets). The loop exhibited discovery (recognizing a misleading metric and changing its approach), not just optimization. The authors defer claims of "first autonomous match of human researchers" and treat effectiveness claims for larger models as pending human baselines.

Abstract: Post-training a frontier model is normally weeks of human work: proposing data and recipe changes, launching runs, reading evals, deciding what to keep. We report an autonomous system that runs this loop with no human in the loop, post-training a 30B Nemotron across four rounds over multiple weeks. The autonomously produced model reaches a held-out score of 0.86 against the top human submission's 0.87 on the public NVIDIA Nemotron-Reasoning Challenge leaderboard, placing 8th of ~4000 at the time of writing. More striking than the number: the loop detected that its own dev metric had stopped tracking external performance on the weakest domain -- candidates drove dev to record highs without moving the external target -- and revised its own search policy, no longer maximizing dev but seeking interventions that lowered the now-misleading proxy while improving the external target. We treat this as direct, auditable evidence that a scaled autonomous loop can produce discovery, not only optimization: it detected that its measurement frame had become misleading and changed what counted as evidence. We take the operational view that any system worth the "recursive self-improvement" label must eventually perform end-to-end post-training of a frontier-class model; this is one datapoint of that bar being cleared. We do not claim a "first autonomous match" of human researchers. The claim we make is narrower and auditable: to our knowledge, this is the first publicly reported autonomous post-training run at this scale, where prior public autonomous-ML-research demonstrations sit at GPT-2-class (~124M) budgets. The same system also post-trains the 120B and 550B Nemotron; with no public human baseline there, this shows only that the loop closes at that scale, not that its output is competitive -- infrastructure evidence, with the effectiveness claim deferred until a comparable human anchor exists.

</details>


### [405] [Expected Free Energy-based Planning as Variational Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20658)
*Wouter W. L. Nuijten,Thijs van de Laar,Bert de Vries*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper reformulates Expected Free Energy-based planning as Variational Free Energy minimization with epistemic priors, demonstrating theoretical consistency with the Free Energy Principle and empirical scalability across three environments of increasing complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing EFE-based planning methods rely on specialized optimization procedures that are difficult to extend or analyze, lacking theoretical integration with the broader Free Energy Principle framework.

Method: The authors formulate EFE-based planning as Variational Free Energy minimization on a generative model augmented with epistemic priors, showing that minimizing this functional decomposes into expected plan costs (EFE) plus a complexity term.

Result: Epistemic priors induce information-seeking behavior; the variational formulation yields policy-based inference outperforming plan-based methods under stochastic transitions; temporal factorization enables scalability beyond existing tabular active inference methods.

Conclusion: Planning under uncertainty can be unified with perception and learning under a single inferential process consistent with the Free Energy Principle, with practical scalability improvements.

Abstract: Planning under uncertainty requires agents to balance goal achievement with information gathering. Active inference addresses this through the Expected Free Energy (EFE), a cost function that unifies instrumental and epistemic objectives. However, existing EFE-based methods typically employ specialized optimization procedures that are difficult to extend or analyze. In this paper, we show that EFE-based planning can be formulated as Variational Free Energy minimization on a generative model augmented with epistemic priors. Our main result demonstrates that minimizing a Variational Free Energy functional with appropriately chosen priors yields a decomposition into expected plan costs (the EFE) plus a complexity term. This formulation reinforces theoretical consistency with the Free Energy Principle by casting planning as the same inferential process that governs perception and learning. We validate our approach on three environments of increasing complexity: a deterministic T-maze, a stochastic Reactivity Maze, and a partially observable MiniGrid DoorKey-8x8 environment. The experiments demonstrate that the epistemic priors induce information-seeking behavior, that the variational formulation yields policy-based inference outperforming plan-based methods under stochastic transitions, and that temporal factorization enables scalability to environments where existing tabular active inference methods cannot operate.

</details>


### [406] [Skill Coverage: A Test Adequacy Metric for Agent Skills](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20659)
*Boyin Tan,Xiaowei Huang,Youcheng Sun*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Skill coverage is a test adequacy metric for LLM agents that measures whether agent trajectories exercise documented behavior constraints in skill artifacts. Applied to SkillsBench, it shows only 39.9–43.98% coverage, revealing that task success doesn't imply thorough skill testing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluations assess skills only through task-level outcomes, but task success alone doesn't reveal which parts of a skill have been exercised or remain untested.

Method: Introduced skill coverage, a test adequacy metric that extracts observable behavior constraints from skill documents and measures whether an agent trajectory provides sufficient evidence to exercise and evaluate each constraint, using binary cover/not-cover judgments without additional outcome labeling.

Result: Applying skill coverage to SkillsBench revealed that existing benchmark executions cover only 39.90–43.98% of skill behavior constraints, indicating that current benchmark tasks leave large portions of documented skill guidance untested.

Conclusion: Successful task completion does not imply adequate testing of the skill artifact; skill coverage provides a measure of how thoroughly agent skills are tested, highlighting untested guidance in existing benchmarks.

Abstract: Agent skills encode reusable procedural knowledge that guides large language model agents across tasks and execution contexts. Existing evaluations primarily assess skills through task level outcomes, yet task success alone does not reveal which parts of a skill have been exercised or which remain untested. We introduce skill coverage, a test adequacy metric that treats the skill artifact as the object under test. Our approach extracts observable skill behavior constraints from skill documents and measures whether an agent trajectory provides sufficient evidence to exercise and evaluate each constraint. Skill coverage uses a binary cover or not cover judgment, which reports whether a documented behavior has been exercised with sufficient observable evidence, without assigning an additional outcome label to the behavior. Applying skill coverage to SkillsBench reveals that existing benchmark executions cover only 39.90 to 43.98% of skill behavior constraints, suggesting that current benchmark tasks leave large portions of documented skill guidance untested. These findings show that successful task completion does not imply adequate testing of the skill artifact, highlighting skill coverage as a measure of how thoroughly agent skills are tested.

</details>


### [407] [From Knowing to Acting: Benchmarking Self-Awareness Capability of LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20661)
*Yifan Li,Shengbin Yue,Boyu Feng,Jinhu Qi,Bo Ke,Zixing Song,Hongru Wang,Zhongyu Wei,Irwin King*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: KAPRO is a framework for evaluating LLM agents' self-awareness (knowing when to use external tools vs. relying on internal knowledge), along with the KAware dataset that partitions tasks into external, internal, and hybrid subspaces. Experiments show self-awareness strongly correlates with task success but degrades in internal-capability settings; open-source and instruction-following models overuse tools, while proprietary and reasoning-oriented models exhibit better cognitive gating.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current benchmarks focus on execution success and neglect self-awareness—the ability to discern whether a problem requires external resources or can be solved via internal parametric knowledge.

Method: KAPRO (Knowing-Acting Quadrant PRObe) evaluates cognitive-behavioral alignment by decoupling metacognitive judgment (Knowing) from spontaneous execution (Acting). KAware dataset partitions tasks into external, internal, and hybrid subspaces to probe epistemic boundaries.

Result: Self-awareness capability is strongly correlated with task success but degrades sharply in internal-capability settings. Open-source and instruction-following models exhibit stronger tool overuse due to shallow pattern matching; proprietary and reasoning-oriented models show more reliable cognitive gating.

Conclusion: The work introduces a systematic framework and dataset for evaluating LLM agent self-awareness, highlighting a critical gap in current benchmarks and revealing behavioral differences across model types in tool-use decisions.

Abstract: The integration of external tools has transitioned LLM agents from passive responders to autonomous systems. However, current benchmarks prioritize execution success, neglecting self-awareness capability, the ability to discern whether a problem requires necessary external resources or can be solved via internal parametric knowledge. To address this, we introduce KAPRO (Knowing-Acting Quadrant PRObe), a framework that evaluates cognitive-behavioral alignment by decoupling an agent's metacognitive judgment (Knowing) from its spontaneous execution (Acting). We further construct KAware, a dataset rigorously partitioning tasks into external, internal, and hybrid subspaces to systematically probe these epistemic boundaries. Extensive experiments across diverse agent architectures show that self-awareness capability is strongly correlated with task success but degrades sharply in internal-capability settings. Moreover, open-source and instruction-following models exhibit stronger tool overuse due to shallow pattern matching, while proprietary and reasoning-oriented models demonstrate more reliable cognitive gating. Benchmark and codes are available at https://github.com/AI-Santiago/KAware.

</details>


### [408] [Confidence Laundering in Agent Systems: Why Uncertainty Needs a Latent Carrier](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20662)
*Kaiwen Shi,Zheyuan Zhang,Han Bao,Colby Nelson,Yanfang Ye*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Agent systems propagate overconfidence due to an interface bottleneck where upstream uncertainty is lost during decision handoffs. The authors identify "confidence laundering" as the failure mode and propose "latent uncertainty" as an uncertainty-bearing carrier attached to handoffs to preserve fragility across components.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the problem that agent systems amplify errors because uncertain upstream decisions are repackaged as clean artifacts, causing downstream components to over-trust them, which leads to system-level error amplification.

Method: The authors conceptualize "uncertain decision handoff" and "confidence laundering" as failure modes, and propose "latent uncertainty" — an uncertainty-bearing carrier attached to decision handoffs that preserves pre-commitment fragility for downstream consumption.

Result: The paper provides a conceptual framework and position, not empirical results. It identifies interface bottleneck as root cause and proposes latent uncertainty as a design principle.

Conclusion: Agent uncertainty propagation should shift from step-wise uncertainty estimation toward uncertainty-preserving interface design to build more recoverable agent systems.

Abstract: Modern agent systems can turn uncertainty into overconfidence. Fragile upstream decisions are often exposed to downstream components as clean intermediate artifacts, while the uncertainty behind those decisions is lost at the interface. As a result, local ambiguity can become system-level error amplification. We argue that this reveals an interface bottleneck in agent uncertainty propagation: uncertainty does not propagate simply because a trajectory contains uncertain steps; it propagates only when it survives the handoff between components. We define uncertain decision handoff as the transfer of an intermediate decision made under uncertainty, and identify confidence laundering as a failure mode in which fragile upstream states are repackaged as procedurally valid artifacts that downstream agents over-trust. To address this bottleneck, we propose latent uncertainty as an uncertainty-bearing carrier attached to decision handoffs. Rather than replacing text with hidden states, latent uncertainty aims to preserve pre-commitment fragility in a form that downstream components can use. This position shifts agent uncertainty propagation from step-wise uncertainty estimation toward uncertainty-preserving interface design for more recoverable agent systems.

</details>


### [409] [DrugBench: Evaluating AI Control Protocols for Medication Harm Mitigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20663)
*Guido Freire,Agustín Martínez-Suñé,Viviana Cotik*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper presents DrugBench, an AI control evaluation benchmark for medication safety, introduces severity-based monitoring for medical AI safety, and demonstrates that existing control protocols can be subverted when severity is considered.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs in medical question-answering pose safety risks since misaligned outputs can cause patient harm. AI control has been effective in other domains but its applicability in medical settings has not been systematically studied.

Method: The authors built DrugBench by combining 3,671 multi-turn medical conversations from HealthBench with official FDA drug labels, covering four categories of medication-related harm. They also proposed severity-based monitoring to address limitations of existing control protocols.

Result: Existing AI control protocols can be subverted when safety is defined to account for severity of unsafe outputs (not just probability). Severity-based monitoring addresses this limitation.

Conclusion: AI control protocols need to account for harm severity in medical settings, and severity-based monitoring offers a promising direction for safer medical AI deployment.

Abstract: Large Language Models have the potential to expand and improve the access to clinical information by enabling new ways of interacting with medical knowledge in natural language. However, their deployment in medical question-answering settings is safety-critical, since misaligned outputs can lead to severe patient harm. AI control is an emerging approach that introduces external safeguards to mitigate unsafe behaviours in misaligned systems and has been shown to be effective in domains such as code generation. However, its applicability and effectiveness in medical settings have not been systematically studied. In this work, we present a pipeline for evaluating AI control protocols to mitigate medication-related harm. To this end, we introduce DrugBench, an AI control evaluation benchmark which combines 3,671 multi-turn medical conversations from HealthBench with drug information from official FDA labels, covering four categories of medication-related harm: drug interactions, contraindications, dosing constraints, and patient action restrictions. Furthermore, inspired by the medical domain, we argue that safety should account for the severity of unsafe outputs, not just their probability. Under this revised definition, we show that existing control protocols can be subverted and propose severity-based monitoring to address this limitation.

</details>


### [410] [A Quantum-Assisted Agentic Distributed Artificial Intelligence Framework for Deadline-Bounded Orchestration of Hybrid Renewable Microgrids](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20667)
*Iacovos I. Ioannou,Saher Javaid,Minella Bezha,Yasuo Tan,Naoto Nagaoka,Vasos Vassiliou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A quantum-assisted agentic distributed AI framework for microgrid dispatch is proposed, using BDIx agents that formulate per-slot dispatch as QUBO problems and select among quantum, quantum-inspired, and classical solvers to meet hard deadlines. A belief-shaped storage valuation mechanism injects intertemporal information. Evaluated on a 24-hour simulation, the framework achieves zero deadline misses, exact optimal dispatch per slot, 146.24 EUR daily cost (matching the theoretical lower bound), 97.83% renewable utilization, and zero unserved energy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-time orchestration of microgrids with renewable sources, dispatchable units, storage, and curtailable consumers requires solving combinatorial dispatch and coalition formation problems under hard control deadlines, motivating the need for a framework that can reliably deliver optimal or near-optimal solutions within strict temporal constraints.

Method: A quantum-assisted agentic DAI framework is proposed where each control slot's dispatch problem is formulated as a QUBO problem by BDIx agents and solved via a portfolio of quantum (QAOA), quantum-inspired, and classical solvers. Solver selection is treated as a first-class agentic deliberation action by the coordinator agent, which maintains learned latency beliefs and commits to the solver expected to meet the deadline. A belief-shaped storage valuation mechanism enables the storage agent to price energy at a discounted future-peak value.

Result: On a 24-hour grid-connected microgrid simulation with PV, wind, battery, genset, and demand response, the framework missed zero deadlines, achieved exact optimum per slot, and attained a daily cost of 146.24 EUR (matching the exact lower bound), with 97.83% renewable utilization and zero unserved energy. Deactivating the storage valuation mechanism increased cost to 152.75 EUR (4.5% increase).

Conclusion: The proposed quantum-assisted agentic DAI framework successfully meets hard real-time constraints for microgrid dispatch, achieving provably optimal solutions with high renewable utilization. The belief-shaped storage valuation mechanism provides meaningful economic benefit by injecting intertemporal information into per-slot optimization.

Abstract: The real-time orchestration of microgrids that combine fluctuating renewable sources, dispatchable units, storage and curtailable consumers requires the repeated solution of combinatorial dispatch and coalition formation problems under hard control deadlines. In this paper, a quantum-assisted agentic distributed artificial intelligence (DAI) framework is proposed in which the dispatch problem of each control slot is formulated as a quadratic unconstrained binary optimization (QUBO) problem by Belief-Desire-Intention extended (BDIx) agents and is solved by a portfolio of quantum, quantum-inspired and classical solvers. Solver selection is elevated to a first-class agentic deliberation action of the coordinator agent. Learned beliefs about solver latencies are maintained and the solver intention that is expected to satisfy the prevailing deliberation deadline is committed in each slot. In addition, a belief-shaped storage valuation mechanism is introduced through which the storage agent prices its energy at a discounted future-peak value, injecting intertemporal information into the otherwise myopic per-slot optimization. The framework is evaluated on a 24-hour simulation of a grid-connected microgrid with photovoltaic, wind, battery, genset and demand-response assets, with the Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm (QAOA) executed by statevector simulation and benchmarked per slot against tabu search, simulated annealing, binary particle swarm optimization, greedy descent and exhaustive enumeration. Zero deliberation deadlines are missed, the committed dispatch attains the exact optimum on every slot and the realized daily cost of 146.24 EUR equals the exact lower bound, with 97.83 percent renewable utilization and zero unserved energy. When the storage valuation mechanism is deactivated, the daily cost is increased to 152.75 EUR, a 4.5 percent increase.

</details>


### [411] [Agent Behavior Mining: Generative AI Agent Governance in Business Processes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20669)
*Hoang Vu,Maximilian Körner,Adrian Rebmann,Gabriel Kevorkian,Michael Perscheid,Gregor Berg,Timotheus Kampik*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces Agent Behavior Mining, a governance capability that applies process mining techniques to generative AI agents, making their decision-making observable through standardized event logs. It includes a data model, a multi-agent order-to-cash implementation, and an evaluation with 18 practitioners showing behavioral transparency is seen as essential for trust and governance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Organizations face a governance dilemma: generative AI agents increase operational flexibility but their non-deterministic nature conflicts with the control and standardization required by Business Process Management, creating an "invisible autonomy risk."

Method: (1) An event data model that translates agent activities (reasoning traces, tool usage, token costs) into standardized process logs; (2) instantiation in a multi-agent order-to-cash implementation for detecting policy deviations and quantifying operational variability; (3) an exploratory study with 18 industry practitioners to evaluate perceived practical utility.

Result: Practitioners view behavioral transparency as a prerequisite for trust and consider the ability to examine agent reasoning as an important governance requirement for AI-driven business processes.

Conclusion: Agent Behavior Mining enables the governance of generative AI agents by rendering their non-deterministic decision-making observable and traceable through process mining techniques, addressing the invisible autonomy risk in AI-driven business process management.

Abstract: As organizations increasingly deploy generative AI agents to automate business processes, they face a governance dilemma: although these agents can increase operational flexibility, their non-deterministic nature challenges the control and standardization that Business Process Management seeks to enforce. This paper addresses this \emph{invisible autonomy risk} by introducing \emph{Agent Behavior Mining}, a governance capability that enables the application of process mining techniques to render generative AI agent decision-making observable and traceable. We (1) improve the understanding of generative AI agent behavior through an event data model that translates granular agent activities -- including reasoning traces, tool usage, and token costs -- into standardized process logs; (2) instantiate the data model in a multi-agent order-to-cash implementation, demonstrating how process managers can leverage agent logs to detect policy deviations and quantify operational variability; and (3) evaluate the perceived practical utility of the approach in an exploratory study with 18 industry practitioners. The results indicate that practitioners view behavioral transparency as a prerequisite for trust and consider the ability to examine agent reasoning as an important governance requirement for the next generation of AI-driven business processes.

</details>


### [412] [Democratizing and accelerating AI-driven pathology research through agentic intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20677)
*Jiabo Ma,Cheng Jin,Yihui Wang,Hao Jiang,Ling Liang,Yingxue Xu,Junlin Hou,Zhengrui Guo,Zhengyu Zhang,Yifei Xia,Hongyi Wang,Fengtao Zhou,Zhe Xu,Huajun Zhou,Jiarui Ouyang,Qian Zeng,On Ki Tang,Eunhyang Park,Carolyn Glass,Ronald Cheong Kin Chan,Li Liang,Hao Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PathLab is an autonomous agentic framework that translates natural-language research objectives into executable computational pathology workflows, achieving non-inferior performance to expert implementations across 12 datasets while enabling domain experts without programming experience to design and execute studies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Computational pathology has advanced with foundation models, but widespread adoption is hindered by substantial technical complexity and programming requirements, creating a barrier for domain experts without coding expertise.

Method: PathLab is an autonomous agentic framework that organizes workflow generation around reusable methodological modules (data preprocessing, model development, evaluation, and interpretation), translating natural-language research objectives into executable and validated workflows through structured composition of domain-specific skills and tools.

Result: Across 12 public datasets covering ROI classification, WSI classification, segmentation, and survival prediction, PathLab achieved non-inferior performance to expert implementations, consistently enforced semantic validity, and proactively rejected incompatible workflows. In user studies, it substantially reduced pipeline generation time and enabled non-programmers to independently conduct studies.

Conclusion: PathLab establishes a reliable interface between biomedical intent and computational execution, lowering technical barriers to advanced AI methodologies and democratizing computational pathology by enabling studies to be designed at the level of scientific questions rather than programming expertise.

Abstract: Computational pathology has advanced rapidly with the emergence of foundation models, yet widespread adoption remains limited by substantial technical complexity and programming requirements. Here we present PathLab, an autonomous agentic framework that translates natural-language research objectives into executable and validated computational pathology workflows through the structured composition of domain-specific skills and tools. By organizing workflow generation around reusable methodological modules, including data preprocessing, model development, evaluation and interpretation, PathLab enables studies to be specified at the level of scientific intent rather than implementation details. We evaluated PathLab across 12 public datasets spanning four representative task families: region-of-interest classification, whole-slide image classification, segmentation and survival prediction. Across all task categories, PathLab achieved non-inferior performance relative to expert implementations, while consistently enforcing semantic validity of user prompts and proactively rejecting incompatible workflow specifications prior to execution. In controlled user studies, PathLab substantially reduced the time required to generate executable analytical pipelines and enabled domain experts without programming experience to independently design, execute and evaluate computational pathology studies. Together, these results establish PathLab as a reliable interface between biomedical intent and computational execution, enabling computational pathology studies to be designed at the level of scientific questions rather than programming expertise. By lowering technical barriers to advanced AI methodologies, PathLab provides a foundation for the broader democratization of computational pathology.

</details>


### [413] [From Question Answering to Task Completion: A Survey on Agent System and Harness Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20683)
*Jianyuan Guo,Zhiwei Hao,Chengcheng Wang,Cheng Fan,Tingzhang Luo,Hongguang Li,Ying Gao,Hefei Mei,Jiankun Peng,Rongjian Xu,Minjing Dong,Han Wu,Mengyu Zheng,Kai Han,Shiqi Wang,Chang Xu,Yunhe Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This survey analyzes LLM-based agents through a model-harness lens, arguing that agent quality (success, efficiency, safety, generalization) emerges from the interaction between model capability, runtime infrastructure (observation, context, control, action, state, verification), task structure, and evaluation design, rather than from model capability alone.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLM-based agents evolve from simple prompt engineering to complex agent-native training, the paper seeks to identify where the performance bottleneck lies: in the foundation model, the execution harness, or their coupling. The goal is to provide a systematic framework for understanding and improving agent systems beyond model-centric scaling.

Method: The survey takes a model-harness lens, first defining agents functionally and then decomposing the execution harness into six coupled runtime responsibilities (observation, context, control, action, state, verification). It traces four paradigms of agent engineering, maps task properties to harness configurations, reviews benchmarks/evaluation practices, and synthesizes evidence on how runtime design affects outcomes.

Result: The paper identifies that agent quality emerges from model-harness-task-evaluation interaction. It provides a structured decomposition of the execution harness, mapping of task properties to harness configurations, and synthesizes evidence on how runtime design affects long-horizon task completion, efficiency, and reliability. Open challenges include value-aware evaluation, safety, harness generalization, and model-harness co-evolution.

Conclusion: The survey concludes that treating agents as merely models with auxiliary tools is insufficient; instead, agent quality emerges from the interaction of model capability, runtime infrastructure, task structure, and evaluation design. Future work should focus on co-evolution of models and harnesses, safety, and more holistic evaluation.

Abstract: LLM-based agents mark a shift from passive question answering to active task completion: they perceive environments, invoke tools, maintain state, and act over extended horizons.
