As agent systems have evolved from prompt engineering to workflows and context engineering, harness engineering, and agent-native training with co-evolution, a central question has become increasingly important: where does the bottleneck in agent performance reside, in the foundation model, in the execution harness, or in the coupling between them? This survey examines LLM-based agents through a model-harness lens. We first clarify the functional definition of agents and the implementation view of an LLM-based agent as a foundation model coupled with an execution harness. We then analyze the limits of model-centric scaling, trace four paradigms of agent engineering, and decompose the execution harness into six coupled runtime responsibilities: observation, context, control, action, state, and verification. Using this decomposition, we map task properties and domain pressures to harness configurations, review benchmark and evaluation practices, and synthesize model-harness evidence on how runtime design affects long-horizon task completion, efficiency, and reliability. Finally, we identify open challenges in value-aware evaluation, safety, harness generalization, and model-harness co-evolution. Rather than treating agents as models with auxiliary tools, this survey argues that agent quality -- including success, efficiency, safety, and generalization -- emerges from the interaction between model capability, runtime infrastructure, task structure, and evaluation design. A collection of papers discussed in this survey is provided in https://github.com/ggjy/Awesome-Agent-Engineering.

</details>


### [414] [Artificial Intelligence as Monism: Ontological, Organisational, and Methodological Implications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20704)
*Bertrand K. Hassani*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes understanding AI as a form of monism—an indivisible, unified essence rather than a collection of separate components (data, algorithms, architectures). It explores implications across epistemology, ethics, organisational structure, project management, and data management, advocating for a paradigm shift away from reductionist approaches toward holistic integration to achieve genuine organisational agility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current reductionist approaches decompose AI into separate elements (data, algorithms, architectures), leading to structural inefficiencies, siloed thinking, and fragmented governance. The paper seeks to reframe AI as a unified, indivisible substance to address these conceptual and practical limitations.

Method: Conceptual philosophical analysis drawing from monism, dualism, and holism traditions. The paper argues by analogy and examines implications across five dimensions: epistemology, ethics/existential concerns, organisational structure, project management, and data/information management.

Result: The monistic view positions AI as the central interpretive force across domains, raises concerns about singularity and power concentration, challenges siloed organisational structures toward transversal problem-centric teams, advocates unified project vision with no single stakeholder dominance, and calls for data architectures reflecting irreducible unity of modelled phenomena.

Conclusion: Embracing AI as monism is necessary for a paradigm shift in AI conceptualisation, governance, and integration. Only through this holistic perspective can organisations achieve genuine agility and avoid inefficiencies inherent to reductionist approaches.

Abstract: This paper argues that Artificial Intelligence should be understood as a form of monism:
  a unified substance that cannot be decomposed into separate elements such as data,
  algorithms, or technical architectures. Drawing from philosophical traditions of monism,
  dualism, and holism, the paper contends that AI is not merely a collection of components
  but a single, indivisible essence reflecting the phenomena it replicates. Treating AI as
  monism has deep implications across multiple dimensions. Epistemologically, it positions
  AI as the central interpretive force across technological, organisational, and societal
  domains, while raising ethical and existential concerns regarding singularity, the
  homogenisation of innovation, and the concentration of decision-making power. At the
  organisational level, a monistic approach challenges traditional siloed structures,
  advocating instead for transversal, problem-centric teams whose mandate derives from
  the integrity of the problem rather than from departmental hierarchy. In project
  management, it implies a unified vision and an integrated evaluation of complexity in
  which no single stakeholder perspective dominates the assessment of outcomes. In data
  and information management, it calls for architectures that reflect the irreducible unity
  of the phenomena being modelled. Ultimately, this paper calls for a paradigm shift in
  how AI is conceptualised, governed, and integrated, suggesting that only by embracing AI
  as monism can organisations achieve genuine agility and avoid the structural
  inefficiencies inherent to reductionist approaches.

</details>


### [415] [Simulated Customers Never Walk Away: Decision Fidelity of LLM User Simulators Measured Against Real Purchase Outcomes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20708)
*Liang Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLM-as-user simulators suffer from a "disengagement deficit": they accurately mimic buyers but fail to reproduce how real non-buyers disengage, overstating interest and understating resistance, which biases evaluation of sales/persuasion agents.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing frameworks only measure communicative fidelity (whether simulators talk like humans), but when user goals are assigned, motivation is exogenous. The authors argue this creates a blind spot: no framework tests whether simulators make decisions like real users whose motivation is endogenous, latent, and decaying.

Method: Using a testbed of 2,790 production conversations (793 with verified payment outcomes) between an LLM sales agent and real customers, the authors introduce a teacher-forced probe protocol to measure decision fidelity — whether simulated populations reproduce the decision-state dynamics of real users facing real, consequential choices.

Result: Simulators reproduce eventual buyers accurately (depth bias +0.09) but inflate eventual non-buyers' engagement (depth bias +0.40; d=0.38, p<0.001), halving expressed resistance (25.1%→13.5%) and nearly doubling deliberation (21.9%→40.1%) without fabricating purchases. The deficit replicates across model families (DeepSeek: d=0.41, p=0.002) and resists the instruction to disengage (d=0.34, p=0.008).

Conclusion: Evaluating or training sales/persuasion agents against such simulators overstates funnel progress precisely where it matters most — the customers who walk away. Real non-buyers say "not now" and stop; simulated non-buyers ask about price.

Abstract: LLM-as-user-simulation has become core infrastructure for conversational AI: agent benchmarks (tau-bench), training pipelines, and a growing body of fidelity studies all rely on LLMs role-playing the human side of dialogue. Existing frameworks measure communicative fidelity -- whether simulators talk like humans -- against ground truth from paid participants role-playing assigned goals. We argue this has a structural blind spot: when the goal is assigned, the user's willingness is exogenous, so no framework can test whether simulators make decisions like real users whose motivation is endogenous, latent, and decaying. We introduce decision fidelity -- whether a simulated population reproduces the decision-state dynamics of real users facing real, consequential choices -- and measure it on a unique testbed: 2,790 production conversations between an LLM sales agent and real customers, including 793 with verified payment outcomes. Using a teacher-forced probe protocol that holds context and instrument fixed, we find a systematic, outcome-correlated failure we call the disengagement deficit: simulators reproduce eventual buyers almost exactly (depth bias +0.09) but inflate eventual non-buyers toward the purchase frame (depth bias +0.40; d=0.38, p<0.001), halving expressed resistance (25.1% to 13.5%) and nearly doubling deliberation (21.9% to 40.1%) while fabricating no purchases. The deficit replicates across model families (DeepSeek: d=0.41, p=0.002) and resists the obvious fix: instructing the simulator that it may disengage cuts marginal bias five-fold but barely moves the outcome-conditioned contrast (d=0.34, p=0.008). Real non-buyers say "not now" and stop; simulated non-buyers ask about price. Evaluating or training sales and persuasion agents against such simulators overstates funnel progress exactly where it matters most -- the customers who walk away.

</details>


### [416] [FairTutor: Equity-Aware Pedagogical LLM Routing for Budget-Constrained AI Tutoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20713)
*Qingyang Xu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: FairTutor is an equity-aware model-routing framework that uses multi-agent orchestration to achieve 97.1% of premium AI tutoring quality while reducing serving costs by 71.6%, addressing the inequity between premium and budget-constrained AI education services.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generative AI tutors create education inequity where students with access to premium AI services receive better explanations, guidance, and scaffolding compared to students limited to free or low-cost services.

Method: FairTutor uses a multi-agent orchestration framework combining query analysis, pedagogical planning, low-cost model generation, evaluator-guided critique and revision, and selective escalation to premium AI models. The authors introduce the AIED Advantage Gap metric and the TutorAccessEval benchmark for evaluation.

Result: FairTutor achieves 97.1% of premium pedagogical quality (floor-adjusted Likert scale) while reducing serving cost by 71.6%. Sensitivity analysis reveals a tunable cost-quality Pareto frontier.

Conclusion: FairTutor provides a cost-effective solution to bridge the AI education quality gap between premium and budget-constrained services, with tunable parameters adaptable to diverse student populations.

Abstract: Generative AI tutors provide real-time, personalized learning support, but also create a new education inequity: students with access to premium AI services may receive clearer explanations, more personalized guidance, and better scaffolding than students limited to free or low-cost services. To address this challenge, we propose FairTutor, an equity-aware model-routing framework that achieves cost-effective AI tutoring via pedagogically motivated multi-agent orchestration. FairTutor combines query analysis, pedagogical planning, low-cost model generation, evaluator-guided critique and revision, and selective escalation to premium AI models. We introduce access-tier AI Education (AIED) Advantage Gap to measure the quality difference between premium-access and budget-constrained tutoring, and TutorAccessEval, a benchmark spanning math, reading, writing, science, and language learning. Empirical evaluations show that FairTutor achieves 97.1% of premium pedagogical quality (in floor-adjusted Likert scale) while reducing serving cost by 71.6%. Sensitivity analysis reveals a tunable cost--quality Pareto frontier, enabling FairTutor to be tailored to the needs of diverse student populations.

</details>


### [417] [Escape from Delusional Echo Trap: Symmetry Breaking, Stochastic Dynamics and Mathematical Mitigation Strategies for Algorithmic Sycophancy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20718)
*Sayantari Ghosh,Saumik Bhattacharya,Partha Pratim Chakrabarti*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A mathematical framework using dynamical systems and stochastic differential equations models how sycophantic AI feedback traps users in rigid, self-reinforcing delusional beliefs via a phase transition in a multi-valley potential landscape, but sufficiently strong authentic external information can reverse this effect.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To rigorously model and understand the mechanisms of algorithmic sycophancy and AI-driven delusional spiraling, where AI chatbots with sycophantic tendencies progressively distort user belief systems.

Method: Dynamical systems theory and stochastic differential equations are used to model evolving conviction as a continuous log-odds state variable navigating a multi-valley potential energy landscape, analyzing phase transitions and attractor dynamics.

Result: Sycophantic feedback enhances baseline perception beyond a critical threshold, inducing a structural phase transition that deepens initial tilt and creates resilient attractor basins trapping users in unshakeable delusional convictions. Strong, authentic external information can overcome the internal feedback barrier and restore objective belief.

Conclusion: AI sycophancy can structurally distort belief dynamics through phase transitions, but objective external evidence of sufficient strength can reverse these effects and restore rational belief states.

Abstract: We propose a rigorous and systematic mathematical framework for tracking the cognitive trajectories of a user, in the context of algorithmic sycophancy and AI-driven delusional spiraling. Using tools from dynamical systems theory and stochastic differential equations, we explore how individuals perceive, interpret, and update their beliefs as they interact with AI chatbots that possess hidden traits of sycophancy. We treat the evolving conviction as a continuous log-odds state variable, coupled into a stochastic differential equation, navigating a multi-valley potential energy landscape. Our analysis reveals several critical observations governing the stability and rigidity of belief dynamics. We demonstrate that the baseline prior perception of the individual is systematically enhanced by sycophantic feedback beyond a critical threshold. Here, the perceptual potential landscape undergoes a structural phase transition that severely deepens any incremental initial tilt present in the baseline state, transforming the landscape and giving rise to deep, highly resilient attractor basins that trap the individual in unshakeable, self-reinforcing, delusional convictions. Finally, we demonstrate that genuine external information can successfully challenge these rigid states. If this incoming evidence is strong and authentic enough to overcome the internal feedback barrier, it can correct the structural asymmetry caused by sycophancy, inducing a perception reversal that successfully restores the objective belief state.

</details>


### [418] [When Web Agents Finish but Still Fail: Reproducible Triggers and Trace Diagnostics for Parallel Web Exploration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20724)
*Aagam Sogani,Botao Rui,Swetha Vaidyanathan,Rishi Agarwal,Minghao Yan,Shivaram Venkataraman*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Parallel WebBench reveals that GRPO-trained web agents dramatically improve task completion (50.7%→96.0%) and element-wise F1 (0.2489→0.4529) but still suffer from a completion-correctness gap due to three persistent failure modes: context-bound search loops, premature termination, and synthesis collapse after retrieving relevant evidence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon web agents often fail in ways hidden by final-answer evaluation; they can produce well-formed answers while still missing fields, including unsupported items, or relying on stale evidence, motivating the need for granular failure analysis beyond binary accuracy.

Method: The authors introduce Parallel WebBench (1,679 verified records: 350 manual + 1,329 reconstructed with verified URL trajectories), train WebExplorer-style agents with GRPO under human-only, balanced human-synthetic, and synthetic-heavy data mixtures, and perform trace-level failure-mode analysis.

Result: Best GRPO model improves completion from 50.7% to 96.0% and element-wise F1 from 0.2489 to 0.4529 vs WebExplorer-8B, but binary accuracy remains far below completion. Three failure modes identified: context-bound search loops, premature termination on partial answers, and synthesis collapse after relevant evidence retrieval.

Conclusion: Synthetic-data GRPO reduces abstention and improves partial correctness but leaves a completion-correctness gap requiring evidence-grounded coverage and synthesis diagnostics.

Abstract: Long-horizon web agents often fail in ways hidden by final-answer evaluation: they may visit useful pages, produce a well-formed answer, and terminate confidently while still missing fields, over-including unsupported items, or relying on stale evidence. We study these failures with Parallel WebBench, a parallel web-exploration benchmark containing 1,679 verified records: 350 manually curated parallel tasks and 1,329 reconstructed records with verified URL-based trajectories. We train WebExplorer-style agents with GRPO under human-only, balanced human-synthetic, and synthetic-heavy data mixtures. At 16k context and 16 interaction rounds, the best GRPO model improves completion over WebExplorer-8B from 50.7% to 96.0% and GPT-4.1-mini-judged element-wise F1 from 0.2489 to 0.4529, but binary accuracy remains far below completion. Trace-level analysis identifies three persistent failure modes: context-bound search loops, premature termination on partial answers, and synthesis collapse after relevant evidence has already been retrieved. These results show that synthetic-data GRPO reduces abstention and improves partial correctness, but leaves a completion-correctness gap that requires evidence-grounded coverage and synthesis diagnostics.

</details>


### [419] [Repeated Shared Access Enables Grokking, but Edit Propagation Depends on a Fine-Grained Addressable Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20737)
*Yanan Niu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper dissociates learning competence from editing affordance in synthetic KG QA. Repeated shared access (loop or memory) enables OOD generalization, but effective factual editing requires fine-grained addressable memory (shared memory architectures) that forward computation can write to and reread, which loop recurrence alone provides only partially.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand what architectural ingredients enable both learning (OOD generalization) and factual edit propagation in knowledge-graph QA systems, and to dissociate these two capabilities.

Method: Compares four architectures (Dense, Loop, Dense+Mem, LMC) on controlled synthetic KG QA with 2-hop compositions. Tests OOD grokking and single-row factual edit propagation using pre-edit-correct probes, with Mann-Whitney statistical tests and HOLDANS interchange diagnostics.

Result: All architectures with repeated shared access (Loop, Dense+Mem, LMC) cross OOD grokking barrier. But edit propagation is strong in memory-bearing cells (LMC 0.78-0.92, Dense+Mem 0.71-0.96) vs weak in non-memory cells (Loop 0.04-0.30, Dense 0.00-0.03). LMC achieves 100% direct edit success with 0.989 intended propagation and ~0.1% unrelated fact drift.

Conclusion: Repeated shared access suffices for learning/grokking, but edit propagation requires fine-grained addressable memory that forward computation can write to and later reread — an affordance loop recurrence provides only partially.

Abstract: We study factual edit propagation in a controlled synthetic knowledge-graph QA setting, comparing four architectures that cross loop recurrence with shared memory access: dense (Dense), looped (Loop), dense with shared memory (Dense+Mem), and looped with shared memory (LMC). Dense fits in-distribution 2-hop compositions but fails OOD; both looped recomputation and memory rereading cross this OOD grokking barrier, indicating that repeated shared access -- not a specific architecture -- is the common ingredient for learning. Editing, however, separates the substrates along a different axis. On a shared pre-edit-correct ID set, a single-row factual edit propagates strongly in the two memory-bearing cells (LMC 0.78-0.92, Dense+Mem 0.71-0.96) and only weakly in the others (Loop 0.04-0.30, Dense 0.00-0.03); the separation is statistically clean (Mann-Whitney p=0.008 between memory and non-memory cells, p=0.55 between the two memory cells, though n=5 vs n=5 is underpowered to rule out a moderate gap). In LMC, atomic facts localize to dominant memory sites that composition rereads, and a one-row value edit on LMC's own pre-edit-correct probes achieves 100% direct success with mean 0.989 intended propagation while moving unrelated facts ~0.1% of the time (matched specificity across substrates pending). A coarse hold-answer-subspace (HOLDANS) interchange diagnostic is consistent with this ordering, suggesting that what separates the substrates is when an edited fact is injected and how much computation remains afterwards to reuse it. These results dissociate learning competence from editing affordance: repeated shared access suffices to grok, but edit propagation depends on whether the substrate exposes a fine-grained addressable memory the forward computation can write to and later reread -- an affordance that loop recurrence provides only partially.

</details>


### [420] [Fara-1.5: Scalable Learning Environments for Computer Use Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20785)
*Ahmed Awadallah,Sahil Gupta,Yash Lara,Yadong Lu,Hussein Mozannar,Akshay Nambi,Zach Nussbaum,Yash Pandya,Aravind Rajeswaran,Corby Rosset,Alexey Taymanov,Luiz do Valle,Vibhav Vineet,Spencer Whitehead,Andrew Zhao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: FaraGen1.5 is a scalable data pipeline for computer use agents that combines live websites, synthetic environments, multiple solver models, user simulators, and three complementary verifiers to generate training data. The resulting Fara1.5 models (4B, 9B, 27B) achieve SOTA on browser-use benchmarks, with the 27B model matching much larger proprietary systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Human demonstrations for computer use data are expensive and slow to collect, motivating the need for scalable automatic generation strategies that require both environments for agents to act in and verifiers to judge success.

Method: FaraGen1.5 uses three modular components: environments (live websites + synthetic environments for gated/irreversible domains), a solver harness (powered by multiple models including GPT-5.4, with a user simulator for multi-turn rollouts), and three complementary verifiers (task correctness, efficiency, critical-point adherence). The resulting data trains Fara1.5 models via SFT with an iterative balancing recipe covering broad coverage, high-value tasks, and target model deficiencies.

Result: Fara1.5-9B: 63.4% on Online-Mind2Web, 86.6% on WebVoyager. Fara1.5-27B: 72.3% on Online-Mind2Web, competitive with much larger proprietary systems. Each model sets SOTA for its size class.

Conclusion: The FaraGen1.5 pipeline enables scalable generation of computer use data, and the Fara1.5 models demonstrate that native CUAs at moderate scales can be highly effective through careful data curation and training.

Abstract: Collecting computer use data from human demonstrations is expensive and slow, motivating the need for scalable generation strategies. This requires two key ingredients: environments in which agents can act and verifiers that can judge whether their demonstrations succeeded. We introduce FaraGen1.5, a scalable data pipeline for computer use agents composed of three modular components: environments, solvers, and verifiers. FaraGen1.5 uses both live websites and synthetic environments that faithfully simulate domains gated by authentication or that require irreversible actions. It employs a solver harness that can be powered by multiple models, including strong frontier models such as GPT-5.4, and also incorporates a user simulator to enable multi-turn rollouts. Finally, FaraGen1.5 scores the resulting trajectories with three complementary verifiers covering task correctness, efficiency, and critical-point adherence. Using data produced by this pipeline, we train Fara1.5, a family of native computer use agents (CUAs) at three scales built on Qwen3.5 (4B, 9B, and 27B). To train these models, we employ a supervised finetuning (SFT) recipe that carefully balances data from FaraGen1.5 for broad coverage, specific high-value tasks, and target model deficiencies in an iterative approach. Each model sets a new state of the art for its size class on browser-use benchmarks: Fara1.5-9B reaches 63.4% on Online-Mind2Web and 86.6% on WebVoyager, while Fara1.5-27B achieves 72.3% on Online-Mind2Web, which is competitive with much larger proprietary systems.

</details>


### [421] [What Shapes Emergent Misalignment? Insights from Training Dynamics, Model Priors, and Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20814)
*Yuchen Zhang,Anietta Weckauff,Diego Garcia-Olano,Maksym Andriushchenko*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper studies emergent misalignment (EM) in fine-tuned models by examining training dynamics, model priors, and data, finding that in-domain loss does not reliably predict alignment, pre-trained activations can forecast post-fine-tuning alignment scores, and activation shifts from training prompts overlap substantially with evaluation prompt shifts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand why narrow fine-tuning can lead to broad misalignment (emergent misalignment) and to investigate the underlying mechanisms through the core components of fine-tuning: training dynamics, model priors, and data.

Method: (1) Analyzed relationship between in-domain training loss and out-of-domain alignment scores across datasets/model families; tested different learning schedules to induce alternative local minima. (2) Compared statistical properties of pre-trained vs. misaligned model scores; used evaluation prompt-only activations from pre-trained and original instruct models to predict fine-grained alignment scores after fine-tuning. (3) Computed activation deltas before/after narrow fine-tuning, measuring subspace overlap and similarity between training and evaluation prompt activation shifts.

Result: (1) No strong runs with better broad alignment scores conditioned on similar/lower training loss were found. (2) Misaligned model scores are statistically different from pre-trained models, with some positive correlation signal; pre-trained/instruct model activations can predict post-fine-tuning alignment scores. (3) Moderate-to-high subspace overlap and similarity between training and evaluation activation shifts; subspace overlaps correlate with shift similarities when measuring last prompt-token activations.

Conclusion: Emergent misalignment can be forecasted from pre-fine-tuning model states and is characterized by shared activation shift patterns between training and evaluation prompts, suggesting the phenomenon is rooted in how fine-tuning perturbations propagate through model representations.

Abstract: Emergent misalignment (EM) is a phenomenon in which models generalize with narrow fine-tuning, leading to broad (yet uneven) misalignment across evaluation questions. We study EM and its variability directly through the components of fine-tuning: training dynamics, model priors, and data. (1) We first explored how in-domain training loss relates to out-of-domain alignment scores across datasets and model families. Then, we tried to induce potential alternative local minima through different learning schedules for one narrow fine-tuning, but did not find strong runs with better broad alignment scores conditioned on similar or lower training loss. (2) We found that although the mean and standard deviations of the misaligned model scores are usually statistically different from those of the pre-trained model, there are some potential signals on overall positive correlation. The evaluation prompt-only activations from both the pre-trained and the original instruct models (prior to narrow fine-tuning) could predict fine-grained alignment scores after narrow fine-tuning. (3) Finally, we compared activation deltas before and after narrow fine-tuning and found moderate-to-high subspace overlap and similarity between the resulting activation shifts for training and evaluation prompts. Subspace overlaps between training and evaluation prompt activations correlate with their shifts' similarities when measuring with the last prompt-token activations. The train-evaluation data prompt overlap is controlled against overlap computed from random vectors and evaluation prompts activations.

</details>


### [422] [Study on Quantitative Dynamic Epistemic Logic for Belief Revision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20837)
*Felipe Nunes de Souza Camargo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces a Dynamic Epistemic Logic (P*) that extends AGM belief revision theory with quantitative "degrees of conviction" from modal logic P. It shows that van Ditmarsch's original revision functions fail to capture AGM's philosophical intuition, and proposes a new revision function (*⁰) that does, complete with an implementation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To bridge the gap between AGM belief revision postulates and Dynamic Epistemic Logic by formalizing AGM within a DEL framework that captures quantitative degrees of conviction, and to identify which revision operators faithfully represent the philosophical intuition behind AGM.

Method: The paper builds on AGM postulates (Gärdenfors, Hansson), extends van Ditmarsch's modal logic P (which captures degrees of conviction), defines revision operators on P to form the DEL system P*, proves theorems about AGM postulate satisfaction within P*, and compares revision functions against philosophical criteria.

Result: Van Ditmarsch's original revision functions do not adequately formalize the philosophical intuition behind AGM. The newly defined function *⁰ (inspired by van Benthem) successfully captures that intuition, and an implementation is provided.

Conclusion: The AGM philosophical intuition is not captured by van Ditmarsch's revisions but is captured by the *⁰ function, which is a better formalization of belief revision from an AGM perspective.

Abstract: Belief revision is a process in which an agent begins to believe in something she previously did not. I begin the paper by presenting, based on (Gärdenfors, 1998; Hansson, 1999), postulates for belief revision that constitute the basis of the AGM theory. I will then briefly show the semantics of a modal logic introduced in (van Ditmarsch, 2005), which I call `$P$'. This logic formalizes static epistemic states and has greater expressive power than AGM in doing so because it captures the quantitative notion of "degrees of conviction". The third step is to introduce revision operators on $P$ and, mostly following (van Ditmarsch, 2005), obtain the Dynamic Epistemic Logic (DEL) I call `$P*$'. It models processes of belief revision in several ways. Original results are presented in the following two sections. The first one of these sections revolves around a formalization of AGM postulates within $P*$ by proving some theorems related to the satisfaction of those postulates by revisions defined in $P*$. The last section features an analysis of $P*$'s revisions that go beyond the mere satisfaction of postulates. I compare their formal behavior with respect to some philosophical criteria. At last, I conclude that the functions presented in (van Ditmarsch, 2005) are not good formalizations of the philosophical intuition behind AGM. Instead, it is captured by the function $*^0$ originally defined in this paper (but highly inspired by (van Benthem, 2007)). An implementation of this function is also provided.

</details>


### [423] [Process-Reward Tactic Evolution for Long-Horizon Bioinformatics Workflows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20839)
*Lingzhi Yang,Yubo Fan,Song Wu,Gilchan Park*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces Process-Reward Tactic Evolution (PRTE), a Galaxy-based training framework that converts verified workflow rollouts into reusable tactics for LLM agents. Using process verifiers and curriculum-organized training in Agent Gym, PRTE improves long-horizon bioinformatics workflow execution, achieving better biological correctness and efficiency than no-memory and reflection baselines on BioWorkflow Bench and BioAgent Bench tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agents struggle with reliable bioinformatics work which demands long-horizon interaction with workflow software, typed data objects, provenance tracking, and biological validation. The goal is to enable agents to construct, execute, debug, and validate Galaxy workflows through structured training and reusable tactic accumulation.

Method: Proposes Process-Reward Tactic Evolution (PRTE) framework: agents train on curriculum-organized Galaxy tasks in Agent Gym; process verifiers score workflow construction, software interaction, execution, and biological correctness; successful and failed traces are distilled into a tactic library. At inference, the executor uses this library to tackle held-out Galaxy workflows from BioWorkflow Bench and BioAgent Bench in isolated environments.

Result: PRTE improves long-horizon bioinformatics workflow completion, biological correctness, and execution efficiency over no-memory and reflection-style baselines on benchmark tasks.

Conclusion: Process-supervised tactic accumulation effectively enhances LLM agent performance on complex, long-horizon bioinformatics workflow tasks in Galaxy, surpassing simpler baseline approaches.

Abstract: LLM agents can write code and call tools, but reliable bioinformatics work requires long-horizon interaction with workflow software, typed data objects, provenance, and biological checks. We study this setting through Galaxy workflow execution. The agent must explore task data, construct or adapt an executable workflow DAG, bind inputs and dataset collections, monitor execution, debug failures, and validate biological outputs. We propose Process-Reward Tactic Evolution, a Galaxy-based training framework that turns verified workflow rollouts into reusable \tactics. During training, agents practice on curriculum-organized Galaxy tasks in Agent Gym; process verifiers score workflow construction, software interaction, execution, and biological correctness; successful and failed traces are distilled into a tactic library. At inference, the trained executor, Process-Reward Tactic Evolution, uses this library to execute held-out peer reviewed Galaxy workflow converted BioWorkflow Bench and BioAgent Bench tasks in isolated environments. The paper evaluates whether process-supervised tactic accumulation improves long-horizon bioinformatics workflow completion, biological correctness, and execution efficiency over no-memory and reflection-style baselines.

</details>


### [424] [SignVLA: Real-Time Sign Language-Guided Robotic Manipulation via Attention LSTM and Vision-Language-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20857)
*Ningwei Bai,Xinyu Tan,Harry Gardner,Zhengyang Zhong,Liuhaichen Yang,Luoyu Zhang,Zhekai Duan,Monkgogi Galeitsiwe,Zezhi Tang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SignVLA introduces a real-time sign-language-guided Vision-Language-Action framework that uses hand landmark features and an attention-enhanced LSTM network to convert sign gestures into semantic instructions for robotic manipulation, enabling accessible human-robot interaction for deaf, hard-of-hearing, and speech-impaired users.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLA interfaces rely on speech or text input, limiting accessibility for deaf, hard-of-hearing, and speech-impaired users.

Method: SignVLA uses a modular sign-to-text interface with hand landmark feature extraction, an attention-enhanced LSTM network for temporal gesture dynamics, and a temporal stabilization module for real-time prediction consistency. The generated instruction sequence is passed to a downstream VLA policy for manipulation.

Result: Experimental results demonstrate stable real-time sign recognition and successful execution of manipulation tasks driven by sign-language inputs.

Conclusion: Lightweight temporal sign recognition can serve as an effective and practical accessibility layer for multimodal embodied intelligence.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models enable robots to execute manipulation tasks from natural-language instructions grounded in visual observations. However, existing VLA interfaces primarily rely on speech or text input, limiting accessibility for deaf, hard-of-hearing, and speech-impaired users. We present SignVLA, a real-time sign-language-guided VLA framework for accessible human-robot interaction. The system introduces a modular sign-to-text interface that converts visual sign gestures into semantic instructions compatible with downstream VLA policies. Given video streams, SignVLA extracts hand landmark features and employs an attention-enhanced Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network to capture temporal gesture dynamics for alphabet- and command-level sign recognition. A temporal stabilization module further improves prediction consistency in real-time interaction settings.The generated instruction sequence is then passed to a downstream VLA policy for sign-conditioned robotic manipulation. Experimental results demonstrate stable real-time sign recognition and successful execution of manipulation tasks driven by sign-language inputs. Our findings suggest that lightweight temporal sign recognition can serve as an effective and practical accessibility layer for multimodal embodied intelligence.

</details>


### [425] [When Do Intrinsic Rewards Work for Code Reasoning? A Comprehensive Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20881)
*Xiaolong Jin,Xuandong Zhao,Wenbo Guo,Xiangyu Zhang,Dawn Song*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper systematically studies intrinsic reward methods (certainty-based RLIF) for code generation in LLMs, finding they produce early gains but inevitably collapse (outputs shorten, reasoning degrades), and offer no benefit as pre-training for RLVR compared to training from scratch.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RLVR relies on ground-truth supervision which is costly/infeasible for code tasks; intrinsic reward methods work for math but whether they transfer to code generation (which involves structural complexity, syntactic diversity, and execution-based verification) is unexplored.

Method: Systematic empirical study on LiveCodeBench evaluating certainty-based RLIF methods across various training scenarios and hyperparameters (sample size, temperature).

Result: Certainty-based methods show early gains but inevitably collapse: models shorten outputs and lose reasoning; collapse speed is sensitive to sample size and temperature. RLIF pre-training offers no improvement over training from scratch when used to initialize RLVR.

Conclusion: Intrinsic rewards have both promise and limitations for code reasoning; the paper provides actionable recommendations for using intrinsic rewards in training code reasoning models.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has driven substantial progress in large language model reasoning, but relies on ground-truth supervision that is costly or infeasible, especially in coding tasks. Recent work addresses this by deriving rewards from a model's own signals, such as majority voting or confidence-based scores, achieving notable success on mathematical reasoning benchmarks. However, code generation poses distinct challenges: programs are structurally complex, semantically equivalent solutions may differ syntactically, and verification typically requires execution. Whether these intrinsic reward methods transfer effectively to code remains unexplored. In this work, we present a systematic empirical study of intrinsic reward methods for code generation. We conduct extensive experiments on LiveCodeBench, systematically evaluating representative certainty-based Reinforcement Learning from Internal Feedback (RLIF) approaches under different training scenarios and hyperparameter settings. Our experiments reveal that certainty-based methods yield early gains but inevitably collapse: models progressively shorten outputs and lose reasoning capability, with collapse speed sensitive to sample size and temperature. When used to initialize RLVR training, RLIF pre-training offers no significant improvement over training from scratch. We also provide actionable recommendations for using intrinsic rewards for training code reasoning models. Our study shows both the promise and limitations of intrinsic reward methods for code, informing future work on code models and agents.

</details>


### [426] [Neurosymbolic Clinical Trial Matching via LLM-Driven Abduction and Logical Verification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20895)
*Baiyang Qu,Leonardo Ranaldi,Xi Wang,Marco Valentino*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: αNeSy-CTM is a hybrid neurosymbolic framework combining LLMs with abductive logical verification for Clinical Trial Matching, achieving up to 30% relative improvement over zero-shot LLM baselines and demonstrating complementarity with Chain-of-Thought reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs struggle with deterministic verification for complex eligibility criteria in Clinical Trial Matching, while purely symbolic methods fail on incomplete/noisy patient records. A hybrid approach is needed to bridge this gap.

Method: αNeSy-CTM (abductive neurosymbolic CTM framework) leverages LLMs' linguistic/world knowledge combined with abductive logical reasoning to handle noisy and underspecified clinical text.

Result: αNeSy-CTM achieves up to 30% relative improvement over zero-shot LLM baselines; exhibits improved accuracy, specificity, and robustness over non-abductive neurosymbolic settings; proves highly complementary with Chain-of-Thought reasoning.

Conclusion: NeuroSymbolic methods significantly impact automating CTM, offering a path toward auditable, LLM-driven clinical applications with a potential hybrid routing policy combining αNeSy-CTM and CoT.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) offer a promising path to automate Clinical Trial Matching (CTM), but still struggle with the deterministic verification required for complex eligibility criteria. Conversely, purely symbolic methods provide formal rigour but break down when faced with incomplete patient records and noisy clinical evidence. To bridge this gap, we investigate a hybrid framework for CTM combining LLMs with logical verification. In particular, we introduce an abductive neurosymbolic CTM framework (αNeSy-CTM), which leverages the linguistic and world knowledge in LLMs to support reasoning over noisy and underspecified clinical text. Extensive evaluation demonstrates that αNeSy-CTM substantially outperforms standalone LLM baselines, achieving up to 30% relative improvement over zero-shot baselines. In addition, our analyses confirm the impact of abductive reasoning on CTM, with αNeSy-CTM exhibiting improved accuracy, specificity, and robustness over a non-abductive neurosymbolic setting. Furthermore, αNeSy-CTM and Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning prove highly complementary, highlighting the potential for a hybrid routing policy. Ultimately, this paper demonstrates the impact of neurosymbolic methods for automating CTM, providing a path toward the next generation of auditable, LLM-driven clinical applications.

</details>


### [427] [Root Cause Analysis with Latent Confounders using Partial Ancestral Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20912)
*Henrique O. Caetano,Rafael Arone,Carlos Dias Maciel*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PAG-RCA is a framework for Root Cause Analysis that handles unobserved latent variables by modeling failures as parametric interventions over Partial Ancestral Graphs (PAGs), using causal identification and partial identification to rank root causes, outperforming state-of-the-art methods on synthetic and real-world benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing data-driven RCA frameworks assume causal sufficiency, failing to account for unobserved latent variables that are prevalent in real-world complex systems, which degrades their performance under data scarcity and latent-variable scenarios.

Method: PAG-RCA models system failures as parametric interventions over Partial Ancestral Graphs (PAGs). It uses causal identification algorithms to quantify causal effects over the PAG; when effects are identifiable, candidates are ranked by exact intervention effects; when structurally unidentifiable, it integrates partial identification (first in RCA literature) to score candidates using analytical causal bounds.

Result: Evaluations on synthetic data, microservice anomaly benchmarks, and power-grid cascading failures show PAG-RCA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art data-driven baselines, especially under data scarcity and latent-variable conditions.

Conclusion: By integrating latent variables and partial identification, PAG-RCA advances reliable automated diagnostics in partially observable complex networks, improving data-driven RCA performance where traditional methods degrade.

Abstract: Finding the source of failures, known as Root Cause Analysis (RCA), is essential for identifying the root causes of anomalies and maintaining the reliability of complex systems. While causal theory has advanced data-driven RCA, existing frameworks assume causal sufficiency, failing to account for the unobserved latent variables prevalent in real-world environments. To address this gap, we propose PAG-RCA. This framework models system failures as parametric interventions over Partial Ancestral Graphs (PAGs) to perform RCA in the presence of latent variables. We use standard causal identification algorithms to find the source of failures by quantifying causal effects over the PAG. When an effect is identifiable, candidate root causes are ranked based on their exact intervention effects. When effects are structurally unidentifiable, our framework (for the first time in the RCA literature) integrates partial identification to evaluate and score candidates using analytical causal bounds. By integrating latent variables and partial identification at once our framework ensures robust RCA even under data scarcity and latent-variable scenarios where traditional methods degrade. Evaluations on synthetic data, microservice anomaly benchmarks and power-grid cascading failures demonstrate that PAG-RCA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art data-driven baselines. By improving data-driven RCA performance under data scarcity, this methodology advances reliable automated diagnostics in partially observable complex networks.

</details>


### [428] [Power Systems Agent Benchmark: Executable Evaluation of AI Agents in Electric Power Engineering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20950)
*Sergei Trashchenkov*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: We introduce the Power Systems Agent Benchmark, an executable benchmark for evaluating tool-using AI agents on power-engineering tasks. It contains 41 task families across eight areas, with on-demand synthesized held-out cases to resist contamination. Reference evaluations show strong scores near the ceiling for top models, with public and held-out performance consistent, and the evaluation doubles as quality control by catching latent bugs missed by self-consistency checks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Electric power engineering lacks an executable benchmark analogous to those in software settings for evaluating tool-using AI agents. Current language-model use in this domain is dominated by retrieval and text QA, while agents acting on power-system artifacts remain mostly academic prototypes.

Method: A benchmark with 41 task families across eight power-engineering areas (power flow, protection, stability, microgrids, reliability, power quality, forecasting). Tasks require structured solutions evaluated deterministically for feasibility, normalized score, and violations. Held-out cases are synthesized on-demand via per-family generators using private seeds to resist contamination. Reference evaluation uses three command-line agents.

Result: Strongest model scored near the compact tier's ceiling; a smaller open model trailed. Public and held-out performance were broadly consistent. A separate public-split grid with OpenCode and Aider revealed harness effects. The evaluation uncovered a latent evaluator bug missed by self-consistency checks.

Conclusion: The benchmark enables executable evaluation of power-engineering agents with resistance to contamination. The evaluators are compact deterministic surrogates but can be upgraded to simulator-backed checks without changing the task contract. Reference evaluation doubles as quality control for defect detection.

Abstract: Executable evaluation -- checking the consequences of an agent's actions with a program rather than grading its prose -- has become a prominent way to assess tool-using AI agents in software settings. Electric power engineering has not yet had an analogous benchmark: language-model use is still dominated by retrieval and text question answering, while agents acting on power-system artifacts remain mostly academic prototypes. We introduce the Power Systems Agent Benchmark, an executable benchmark for power-engineering agents. An agent receives a structured task and returns a structured solution; a deterministic evaluator recomputes the engineering quantities, checks operational constraints, and returns a feasibility flag, a normalized score, and explicit violations.
  The benchmark contains 41 task families across eight areas of power engineering, from power flow and protection to stability, microgrids, reliability, power quality, and forecasting. Each task is grounded in a citable source, standard, or documented engineering formulation. To resist contamination, held-out cases are synthesized on demand by per-family generators from private seeds: the construction is inspectable, but the instances remain private. In a reference evaluation with three command-line agents, the strongest score near the compact tier's ceiling, a smaller open model trails, and public and held-out performance are broadly consistent; a separate public-split grid with OpenCode and Aider probes harness effects. The reference evaluation doubles as quality control: unanimous failures flag candidate task or evaluator defects, and it exposed a latent evaluator bug missed by self-consistency checks. The evaluators are compact deterministic surrogates, but the task contract allows their internals to be upgraded to simulator-backed checks without changing how tasks are posed or solved.

</details>


### [429] [Generative Responsible AI Data Evaluation Schema (GRAIDES) for AI Assurance in Local Government](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20963)
*Ethan Knights,Christopher Conlan,Temilorun Gbolahan,Stephen Waterman,Gurpreet Muctor*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: GRAIDES is a lightweight open-source data model for centralizing generative AI observability across vendors, addressing fragmented evaluation data with a standardized schema. It includes blueprints for code, architecture, and statistical evaluation, demonstrated through Westminster City Council's AI catalogue case study focusing on human-model alignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluation data for generative AI is fragmented across systems, inconsistently structured, and difficult to compare, hindering trustworthy governance and measurable evidence of performance and safety.

Method: The authors propose GRAIDES, an open-source data model that frames evaluations as a data modelling problem, providing practical blueprints for code, architecture, and statistical evaluation to centralize AI observability across vendors.

Result: Illustrative case study results from Westminster City Council's AI catalogue demonstrate measuring human-model alignment, including detecting systematic disagreement between evaluators.

Conclusion: GRAIDES offers a practical pathway toward more consistent and reproducible benchmarking, tuning, and assurance activities for generative AI systems by standardizing evaluation data.

Abstract: Trust in the application of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) relies on well-governed measurable evidence of performance and safety. In practice, however, evaluation data is often fragmented across systems, inconsistently structured and difficult to compare. We introduce the Generative Responsible AI Data Evaluation Schema (GRAIDES) as a lightweight open-source data model for centralising AI observability across popular vendors. Practical blueprints for code, architecture and statistical evaluation are shared as guidance about how to approach generative system assurance at the organisational level. Illustrative case study results are reported from Westminster City Council's AI catalogue with a focus on measuring human-model alignment including detecting systematic disagreement between evaluators. By framing evaluations as a data modelling problem, GRAIDES provides a practical pathway toward more consistent and reproducible benchmarking, tuning and assurance activities for generative AI systems.

</details>


### [430] [AutoACSL: Synthesizing ACSL Specifications by Integrating LLMs with CPG-Based Static Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20969)
*Han Zhou,Yu Luo,Dianxiang Xu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AutoACSL is a framework that combines LLMs with Code Property Graph (CPG)-based static analysis to automatically generate verifiable ACSL formal specifications for C programs. It achieves 98% specification generation success and 96% full proof ratio on 604 programs, improving verification effectiveness by 24.7%-51.7% over code-only baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generating formal specifications for C programs is challenging due to manual effort, expertise requirements, and the need for semantic precision. Existing LLM-based approaches often lack semantic depth and produce unverifiable or incomplete contracts.

Method: AutoACSL integrates LLM prompting with semantic features from Code Property Graphs (CPGs). It performs static analyses to extract key elements (arithmetic operations, loops, recursion, return value propagation), encodes them into structured prompts, and uses a feedback-driven synthesis loop where specifications are verified with Frama-C/WP and iteratively refined until success or termination.

Result: On 604 programs, AutoACSL achieves 98% specification generation success ratio and 96% full proof ratio with Gemini-3. Across four LLMs, it improves the full proof ratio by 24.7%-51.7% compared to a code-only baseline.

Conclusion: Integrating LLMs with CPG-based static analysis substantially enhances both generation robustness and verification effectiveness for automated ACSL specification synthesis.

Abstract: Generating formal specifications for C programs remains a challenge in formal verification due to the manual effort, expertise, and semantic precision required. While recent advancements in large language models (LLMs) offer promise in automating specification synthesis, current approaches often lack semantic depth and produce unverifiable or incomplete contracts. To address these limitations, we introduce AutoACSL, a novel framework that integrates LLM prompting with semantic features extracted from Code Property Graphs (CPGs). AutoACSL performs static analyses to extract key semantic elements, including arithmetic operations, loop and recursion structures, and return value propagation, which are encoded into structured prompts. These prompts enable the LLM not only to generate normal behavioral specifications but also to include constraints that prevent inputs leading to runtime errors. AutoACSL employs a feedback-driven synthesis loop, where candidate specifications are verified using Frama-C/WP and refined iteratively until verification succeeds or a termination condition is met. Evaluated on 604 programs drawn from diverse datasets, AutoACSL achieves a 98% specification generation success ratio and a 96% full proof ratio when paired with Gemini-3. Compared to a code-only baseline, AutoACSL improves the full proof ratio by 24.7% to 51.7% across four LLMs (GPT-o4 Mini, GPT-5.2, Grok-4.1, and Gemini-3), demonstrating that integrating large language models with CPG-based static analysis substantially enhances both generation robustness and verification effectiveness for automated ACSL specification synthesis.

</details>


### [431] [How Should Agents Read Demonstrations? Hierarchical Structure Beats Flat Action Logs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20978)
*Honjar Xing,Jefferson Lin,Henry Lieberman*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Grouping recorded actions into labeled, hierarchical subgoals significantly improves LLM agent performance on vague tasks (pass rate 90.7% vs 76.7%) compared to flat demonstration formats, with the effect driven solely by subgoal grouping rather than additional annotations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To make LLM agent authoring accessible to non-programmers via Programming by Demonstration (PbD), and to investigate how the organizational structure of recorded action logs affects downstream plan quality.

Method: Controlled experiment across 85 web automation tasks comparing zero-shot baseline against four demonstration formats with identical action sequences but differing structure, including hierarchical subgoal grouping, flat formats, and various annotation conditions.

Result: Hierarchically grouped demonstrations improved pass rates from 76.7% to 90.7% (p=0.034) on 43 vague-description tasks; flat demonstrations showed smaller non-significant improvement; no format benefited precise-description tasks; subgoal grouping alone drove the effect without need for preconditions, postconditions, or parameter annotations.

Conclusion: Segmenting action sequences into named subgoal groups rather than flat step lists is a concrete design recommendation for PbD pipelines and any system feeding procedural context to LLM agents.

Abstract: Programming by Demonstration (PbD) offers a human-centered way to author procedural knowledge for LLM agents: users communicate what they want by showing rather than by writing prompts or code, making agent authoring accessible to non-programmers. The natural output of a PbD recording is a flat action log, but how this log is organized before being passed to the agent is an open design question with significant consequences for plan quality. We propose grouping recorded actions into labeled, hierarchical subgoals and evaluate the effect of this organizational structure in a controlled experiment. Across 85 web automation tasks, we compare a zero-shot baseline against four demonstration formats that share identical action sequences but differ in structure. On 43 natural-language tasks with vague descriptions, hierarchically grouped demonstrations improve pass rates from 76.7\% to 90.7\% (paired permutation test $p{=}0.034$; win-loss 6:0), while flat demonstrations show a smaller, non-significant improvement. On 42 tasks with precise descriptions, no format provides any benefit, confirming that the hierarchical advantage arises specifically when descriptions leave procedural details ambiguous. Ablation shows that subgoal grouping alone drives the effect: preconditions, postconditions, and parameter annotations add no measurable benefit. These results offer a concrete design recommendation for PbD pipelines and, more broadly, for any system that feeds procedural context to an LLM agent: segment action sequences into named subgoal groups rather than presenting flat step lists.

</details>


### [432] [BioInsight: Multi-Agent Orchestration for Interactive Biomedical Knowledge Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20997)
*Jieyi Wang,Bingxuan Li,Nanyi Jiang,Desong Meng,Zirui Fan,Yuxin Guo,Jiayu Liu,Kunlun Zhu,Eddie Yang,Xiusi Chen,Pan Lu,Bingxin Zhao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BioInsight is a multi-agent system that transforms static biomedical reports into interactive, evidence-centered interfaces, enabling researchers to inspect evidence, assess uncertainty, and refine hypotheses through ranked pathways, literature packets, and citation-grounded reports.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Static AI-generated biomedical reports are insufficient for research decision-making, as users need to interactively inspect evidence, assess uncertainty, compare mechanisms, and refine hypotheses.

Method: BioInsight organizes disease-specific evidence via typed intermediate artifacts (ranked pathways, literature packets, reasoning notes, citation-grounded reports, dashboard schemas, rendered interactive interfaces), decomposes evidence retrieval from mechanistic reasoning, normalizes citations deterministically, and converts structured evidence into interactive interfaces.

Result: BioInsight achieves best performance on standardized biomedical QA, protein-function reasoning, and end-to-end biomedical evidence synthesis tasks.

Conclusion: Biomedical AI systems should move beyond text-only static reports toward provenance-preserving, interactive evidence artifacts.

Abstract: Biomedical researchers increasingly use AI-generated analyses and reports to interpret protein-level signals, but static outputs are often insufficient for research decision-making, where users need to inspect evidence, assess uncertainty, compare mechanisms, and refine hypotheses. We present \textsc{BioInsight}, a multi-agent system that moves from static biomedical report generation to interactive evidence-centered interactive interface generation. Given a disease name, a protein association table, and optional cohort metadata, BioInsight organizes disease-specific evidence through typed intermediate artifacts, including ranked pathways, literature evidence packets, protein-level reasoning notes, citation-grounded reports, dashboard schemas, and rendered interactive interfaces. The system decomposes evidence retrieval from mechanistic reasoning, normalizes citations through deterministic components, and converts the same structured evidence used in the report into an interactive interface. We evaluate BioInsight on standardized biomedical QA, challenging protein-function reasoning, and end-to-end biomedical evidence synthesis. Results show that BioInsight achieves best, and suggest that biomedical AI systems should move beyond text-only and static reports toward provenance-preserving, interactive evidence artifacts.

</details>


### [433] [Building Agent Harnesses for Scientific Curation from Multimodal Sources](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21005)
*Sheng Zhang,Qin Liu,Renqian Luo,Shufang Xie,Reuben Tan,Sean Hayes,Gregory Bryman,Wendong Ge,Roxy Zhang,Oluwaseun Egbelowo,Kelly Yee,Hoifung Poon*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Beaver is an agent harness for scientific curation that extracts structured information from multimodal scientific papers while preserving provenance. It achieves 81.0 GRAS, outperforming frontier agents by 23+ points, through staged workflows, multimodal tooling, and iterative revision loops.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scientific discovery workflows require structured curation from literature, but current agents struggle because evidence is scattered across long text, dense tables, and figures, and records often need reasoning across multiple evidence fragments rather than simple span extraction.

Method: Beaver combines a frontier agent with multimodal evidence tooling, task scaffolding, and artifact-grounded autoresearch. It structures curation as a staged, auditable workflow with an iterative evaluate-diagnose-revise loop, where persistent run artifacts expose stage-localized failures and guide harness updates.

Result: Beaver achieves 81.0 on Gold-Referenced Attribute Score (GRAS), outperforming frontier agents by over 23 absolute points. Ablations confirm contributions from task scaffolding, multimodal evidence tooling, and provenance traces, with largest gains on high-value attributes requiring cross-modal reasoning and normalization.

Conclusion: Harness design is a central determinant of agent performance for scientific curation from papers with multimodal evidence. The staged, auditable workflow with provenance significantly improves extraction quality.

Abstract: Scientific discovery workflows often depend on structured curation from the literature. This is difficult for current agents because the key evidence is scattered across long text, dense tables, and figures, and the final records often require reasoning across multiple evidence fragments rather than copying a single span. We study scientific curation from multimodal sources and introduce Beaver, an agent harness that extracts structured information from scientific papers while preserving provenance to the supporting evidence. Beaver combines a frontier agent with multimodal evidence tooling, task scaffolding, and artifact-grounded autoresearch. These components turn curation into a staged, auditable workflow and enable an iterative evaluate--diagnose--revise loop, where persistent run artifacts expose stage-localized failures and guide harness updates. Experiments show that Beaver reaches 81.0 on Gold-Referenced Attribute Score (GRAS), an attribute-level measure of agreement with gold curated records, outperforming frontier agents by over 23 absolute points. Ablations show that task scaffolding, multimodal evidence tooling, and provenance traces each contribute meaningfully to performance, while attribute-level analysis shows the largest gains on high-value attributes that require cross-modal reasoning and normalization. These results show that, for scientific curation from papers with multimodal evidence, harness design is a central determinant of agent performance.

</details>


### [434] [Closure of Self-Determining System Based on Causal and Constitutive Relations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21010)
*Yoshiyuki Ohmura,Earnest Kota Carr,Yasuo Kuniyoshi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes defining system boundaries through causal-constitutive loops, where constitutive relations involve at least two independent variables to ensure self-determination and prevent reduction to supervenience, leading to a dual-process organization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address challenges in defining system boundaries for self-determining systems—particularly handling external causes and circular causality when relying solely on causal closure.

Method: Introduces two asymmetric relations (causal and constitutive); defines system boundaries as closures of causal-constitutive loops; imposes a minimal requirement of at least two independent variables in constitutive relations to avoid supervenience.

Result: Self-determination is achieved by constraining constitutive relations to include internal causes. The minimal requirement produces two interdependent loops, implying a dual-process organization.

Conclusion: System boundaries for self-determining systems can be rigorously defined via causal-constitutive loops, and a dual-process structure emerges as a necessary condition for genuine self-determination (non-reductive autonomy).

Abstract: A self-determining system is defined as one in which causes originating within the system influence the system itself. This definition raises the question of how to specify system boundaries. Although the concept of "closure" is commonly used for this purpose, defining boundaries solely in terms of causal relations introduce challenges, such as how to handle external causes and circular causality. To address this issue, we introduce two types of asymmetric relations: causal and constitutive. We propose that system boundaries can be defined as closures of loops formed by these relations, referred to as causal-constitutive loops. By constraining constitutive relations, the resulting system necessarily includes internal causes and thereby satisfies self-determination. Furthermore, to prevent reduction to supervenience, constitutive relations must involve at least two independent variables. This minimal requirement leads to two interdependent loops, which implies a dual-process organization.

</details>


### [435] [Agentic Time Machine as an Infrastructure for Future-Event Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21013)
*Jingyi Chai,Bingyang Zheng,Xiangrui Liu,Hao Lu,Zihang Zhou,Tianchen Wang,Kemeng Zhang,Siheng Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces Agentic Time Machine (TM), an infrastructure that reconstructs past web states for evaluating forecasting agents, and proposes a planner-solver-aggregator multi-agent framework that achieves top performance on the FutureX live leaderboard, including 1st place overall as of June 17.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluating LLM agents on future event forecasting faces a trade-off between efficiency (live benchmarks are slow) and environmental fidelity (retrospective benchmarks use static databases that lack realism). The authors aim to resolve this tension.

Method: The authors build Agentic Time Machine (TM), an infrastructure that reconstructs past web states by filtering post-cutoff content. They also propose a planner-solver-aggregator multi-agent framework that decomposes questions into analytical angles, gathers evidence in parallel, and aggregates results into forecasts.

Result: Offline scores under TM correlate strongly with live FutureX scores. The proposed framework achieves the highest score on FutureX-Past and Polymarket under TM, and achieves 1st place on the FutureX live leaderboard over four consecutive weeks, including 1st in May Week 1 and 1st on the eight-week overall leaderboard as of June 17.

Conclusion: Agentic Time Machine provides a fast yet faithful sandbox for forecasting-agent evaluation, and the proposed multi-agent framework yields state-of-the-art forecasting performance.

Abstract: Forecasting future events is a critical challenge for large language model (LLM) agents, spanning domains from elections and monetary policy to financial markets. However, evaluating progress on this task presents a fundamental trade-off between efficiency and environment fidelity. While live evaluation benchmarks suffer from an inherently slow feedback loop, existing retrospective replays typically restrict agents to static, pre-frozen databases that sacrifice the environmental realism of actual deployments. To tackle this issue, we introduce Agentic Time Machine (TM), an infrastructure that approximately reconstructs the web state at any chosen past time by filtering post-cutoff content. Leveraging this evaluation infrastructure, we further propose a planner-solver-aggregator multi-agent framework that breaks each question into diverse analytical angles, gathers evidence in parallel, and combines the results into a single forecast. Experiments show that offline scores under TM correlate strongly with live FutureX scores, validating that TM offers a fast and reliable sandbox for forecasting-agent evaluation. On FutureX-Past and Polymarket evaluated under TM, our framework achieves the highest score among strong closed-book, tool-augmented, and self-consistency baselines. On the official FutureX live leaderboard, our system achieves the best average rank over four consecutive weeks, including 1st place in May Week 1. As of June 17, it also ranks 1st on FutureX's official eight-week overall leaderboard.

</details>


### [436] [The AI Evaluability Gap: The Missing Layer for Managing Risk and Sustaining Value](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21015)
*Vishal Srivastava,Tanmay Sah*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper identifies the AI Evaluability Gap—the lack of sufficient evidence for high-confidence governance decisions—and proposes Evaluability, a framework distinguishing operational (structural evidence) from investment (causal evidence) certification, with six properties of evaluable evidence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Organizations deploying AI face two governance challenges: managing AI risk and sustaining AI value. Both depend on evidence whose sufficiency cannot be taken for granted, yet existing approaches focus on system properties (safety, fairness, etc.) while neglecting the evidentiary foundations needed to justify governance decisions.

Method: The paper identifies a category error in current AI governance practice, formalizes governance decisions as functions of calibrated confidence Conf(D|E), and introduces six properties of evaluable evidence: observability, attributability, intervenability, verifiability, calibration, and temporal validity. It distinguishes Operational Certification (structural evidence for deployment) from Investment Certification (causal evidence for resource allocation).

Result: A framework defining Evaluability as a system's capability to generate, maintain, and renew sufficient evidence for high-confidence governance decisions, with a clear distinction between two certification types based on different evidentiary standards.

Conclusion: Evidence sufficiency is a missing layer of AI governance; closing the AI Evaluability Gap is a prerequisite for both managing risk and sustaining value in AI-enabled organizations.

Abstract: Organizations deploying AI face two fundamental governance challenges: managing AI risk and sustaining AI value. Both depend on evidence whose sufficiency cannot be taken for granted. We call the shared underlying challenge the AI Evaluability Gap: the condition in which organizations lack sufficient evidence to support high-confidence governance decisions regarding either risk or value.
  We argue that this gap reflects a category error in current practice. Existing governance approaches focus primarily on properties of systems, such as safety, fairness, reliability, compliance, and value, while paying comparatively little attention to the evidentiary foundations required to justify decisions about those properties. We further argue that AI governance encompasses both operational decisions regarding whether a system may operate and investment decisions regarding whether it merits continued organizational resources.
  To address this problem, we introduce Evaluability, defined as the capability of a system to generate, maintain, and renew evidence sufficient to support high-confidence governance decisions over time. We formalize governance decisions as functions of calibrated confidence Conf(D|E) and identify six properties of evaluable evidence: observability, attributability, intervenability, verifiability, calibration, and temporal validity.
  The framework distinguishes Operational Certification, which relies primarily on structural evidence to justify deployment decisions, from Investment Certification, which relies primarily on causal evidence to justify continued resource allocation. We argue that evidence sufficiency is a missing layer of AI governance and that closing the AI Evaluability Gap is a prerequisite for both managing risk and sustaining value in AI-enabled organizations.

</details>


### [437] [Negative Knowledge as Failure-aware Shared Memory for AutoResearch](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21024)
*Hanchun Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a negative knowledge memory layer where failed AI research attempts are curated into a structured bank, enabling downstream agents to learn from failures, improving performance and efficiency on ScienceAgentBench and PDE problems while enabling knowledge transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Failed attempts in AI-assisted research are typically discarded rather than treated as reusable knowledge, limiting cumulative learning from negative outcomes.

Method: A curator agent converts failed attempts into bounded, typed records stored in a shared negative knowledge bank; a downstream research agent explicitly adopts or rejects these records before proposing next experiments.

Result: The negative knowledge layer outperforms vanilla AutoResearch baselines with fewer tokens, enables solving tasks all baselines fail at, and transfers across different PDE problems.

Conclusion: Structured negative knowledge should be maintained as a collective infrastructure alongside positive findings in AI-engaged scientific research.

Abstract: AI-assisted research systems generate many failed attempts, but those failures rarely become a durable, shared knowledge asset. We propose a negative knowledge memory layer: a curator agent converts each failed attempt into a bounded, typed record in a shared bank, and a downstream research agent explicitly adopts or rejects those records before proposing its next experiment. We evaluate this layer in two settings: same-task retry on ScienceAgentBench and cross-task scientific research on two nonlinear math-physics PDE problems. The negative knowledge layer outperforms vanilla AutoResearch baselines while using fewer tokens; agents with the negative knowledge bank solve new tasks that all baselines fail to solve in PDE systems research. We also show that the previous negative knowledge bank can transfer and enhance AutoResearch on different PDE problems. These results suggest that structured negative knowledge is a knowledge asset that should be explicitly maintained in broader AI-engaged scientific research beyond a memory-compression or debugging aid, alongside positive findings, as a collective infrastructure for scientific memory. Code is available at https://github.com/hch-wang/Negative_Knowledge.

</details>


### [438] [Coherence Under Commitment: Probing Generalization and Vacuous Memorization in LLM Logical Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21083)
*Noor Islam S. Mohammad,Mahmudul Hasan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMs can appear coherent by systematically abstaining from decisive answers. The paper introduces Coherence Under Commitment (CUC), a dual-query framework measuring both consistency and decisiveness via commitment scores, deterministic log-probability elicitation, and a 3-way decision (True/False/Uncertain). Experiments on 1B-3B models across FOLIO show a coherence-commitment trade-off (e.g., Qwen2.5-3B has near-zero contradiction but 7.4% coverage; TinyLlama-1.1B reaches 79.4% coverage with violations everywhere). Coherence-only evaluation favors abstention; CUC reveals vacuity. Results generalize to LogiQA v2 (ρ=0.97).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs can achieve apparent logical coherence (e.g., negation consistency) by systematically abstaining from committing to entailment or refutation, providing no practical utility. Existing evaluation metrics fail to penalize this vacuous coherence.

Method: Proposes Coherence Under Commitment (CUC), a dual-query evaluation paradigm with: (1) a commitment score c(φ)=p(φ)+p(¬φ); (2) deterministic elicitation via normalized YES/NO log probabilities; (3) a 3-way decision framework (True/False/Uncertain) to operationalize the coherence-commitment trade-off.
