Result: On 204 FOLIO examples with 4 LLMs (1B-3B): Qwen2.5-3B achieves near-zero contradiction (E[v_neg]=0.025) but only 7.4% coverage; TinyLlama-1.1B achieves 79.4% coverage with violations on every example. Coherence-only evaluation favors the abstaining model; CUC exposes this vacuity. The frontier generalizes to LogiQA v2 (ρ=0.97).

Conclusion: Evaluation must jointly report coherence and non-vacuous commitment. A toolkit for standardized assessment is released.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) deployed for logical reasoning in knowledge-intensive domains exhibit a subtle but critical failure: coherence can be vacuously achieved through systematic abstention. A model that withholds commitment to either entailment or refutation satisfies negation consistency while providing no utility. We introduce Coherence Under Commitment (CUC), a dual-query evaluation paradigm that jointly measures consistency and decisiveness. CUC contributes three innovations: (1) a commitment score $c(\varphi) = p(\varphi) + p(\lnot\varphi)$ quantifying probability mass allocated to decisive outcomes; (2) a \textbf{deterministic elicitation protocol} via normalized YES/NO log probabilities, eliminating sampling variance; and (3) a 3-way decision framework (True/False/Uncertain) operationalizing the coherence-commitment trade-off into metrics. Experiments on four open-weight LLMs (1B-3B) across 204 FOLIO examples expose a sharp frontier. Qwen2.5-3B achieves near-zero contradiction ($\mathbb{E}[v_{\mathrm{neg}}]{=}0.025$) but only $7.4\%$ coverage, while TinyLlama-1.1B reaches $79.4\%$ coverage with violations on every example. Coherence-only evaluation would rank the abstaining model first; CUC exposes this as vacuous, and the frontier generalizes to LogiQA~v2 ($ρ{=}0.97$). We argue that evaluation must report both coherence and non-vacuous commitment and release a toolkit for standardized assessment.

</details>


### [439] [Repeated post-training is not Self-improving: Diagnosing Scientific Amnesia in Continual DPO Pipelines](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21089)
*Jianzhe Lin,Fei Wang,Xiaolin Li,Rajeshkumar Golani,Jubin Chheda*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper identifies and diagnoses "scientific amnesia"—a failure mode in continual DPO training where LLMs fail to accumulate methodological knowledge across training campaigns—and provides a diagnostic suite, benchmark, and comparative study of five intervention strategies, finding that no single approach universally solves the problem and that outcomes depend heavily on chain regime and evaluation design.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Industrial LLM teams frequently ship behavioral updates via sequential DPO training on related preference-data campaigns. The dominant failure mode is not classical catastrophic forgetting but rather a failure to accumulate reusable methodological knowledge about how to train subsequent campaigns—a phenomenon the authors term "scientific amnesia." The paper aims to formalize this practitioner intuition into a measurable problem.

Method: The authors contribute: (i) a diagnostic suite for measuring scientific amnesia; (ii) a Program-based pipeline using FSDP-sharded DPO checkpoints across Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct; (iii) a 30-campaign HumanEval subdomain benchmark; and (iv) a comparative study of five strategy proposers (random memory, rule-based scheduling, retrieval-only memory, warm-start Bayesian optimization, and MSCL) across different chain regimes (single-seed homogeneous, heterogeneous, multi-seed homogeneous).

Result: In the single-seed 5-condition × 3-step chain, 4 of 5 candidates degraded in step-level peak pass@1 (including MSCL); only the rule-based schedule improved. In a heterogeneous chain, MSCL improved while others did not complete. In a multi-seed homogeneous sweep, retrieval-only had the best mean Delta, but no pairwise candidate gap was statistically distinguishable.

Conclusion: Scientific amnesia is observable in production-like continual DPO pipelines. Conclusions about interventions depend sharply on chain regime, evaluator design, and seed coverage. The contribution is primarily diagnostic rather than a claim that MSCL solves the problem.

Abstract: Industrial LLM teams often ship behavior updates by repeatedly DPO-training a base model on sequences of related preference-data campaigns. The dominant failure mode in this regime is not always classical catastrophic forgetting: a pipeline may preserve previously learned behaviors while still failing to accumulate reusable methodological knowledge about how to train the next campaign. We call this failure mode scientific amnesia. This paper turns that practitioner intuition into a measurable industrial problem. We contribute: (i) a diagnostic suite for amnesia, (ii) a Program-based pipeline that chains FSDP-sharded DPO checkpoints across Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct runs, (iii) a 30-campaign HumanEval subdomain benchmark, and (iv) a comparative diagnostic study of five strategy proposers: random memory, rule-based scheduling, retrieval-only memory, warm-start Bayesian optimization, and MSCL, a meta-scientific memory and reasoner candidate. Across a single-seed 5-condition * 3-step real-LM chain, 4 of 5 candidates degrade in step-level peak pass@1, including MSCL; only the deliberately conservative rule-based schedule improves. Follow-up pilots qualify rather than overturn this finding: in a heterogeneous chain, MSCL is the only completed candidate that improves, whereas in a small multi-seed homogeneous sweep, retrieval-only has the best mean Delta and no pairwise candidate gap is statistically distinguishable. The contribution is therefore diagnostic, not a claim that MSCL solves the problem: scientific amnesia is observable in a production-like continual-DPO pipeline, and conclusions about interventions depend sharply on chain regime, evaluator design, and seed coverage.

</details>


### [440] [Self-Improvement Can Self-Regress: The Rise-and-Collapse Failure Mode of LLM Self-Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21090)
*Jianzhe Lin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Self-improvement via REINFORCE post-training for code can self-regress: models quickly improve on pass@1 then collapse within the same training campaign. This is due to within-task policy over-optimization, not cross-task forgetting. Among control approaches, CARE (between-campaign memory) helps smaller models, while ES (early stopping) and GRPO boost larger models, but GRPO does not eliminate the within-campaign collapse cliff.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate and characterize the previously under-explored phenomenon where RL-based self-improvement in code post-training (specifically REINFORCE) leads to a rapid rise-then-collapse pattern in performance within a single training campaign, and to identify effective control mechanisms to mitigate this regression.

Method: Multi-seed testbed using Qwen-2.5-3B/7B on competitive programming tasks with binary CodeGrader reward, across 10 sequential 20-step campaigns. Compares three control approaches: CARE (between-campaign memory with capability posterior, transfer gate, regression-aware belief revision), ES (within-campaign early-stop rolling forward peak checkpoint), and GRPO (group-relative reward normalization). Also conducts a Gemma-3-4B pilot for generalization.

Result: Pass@1 exhibits a robust rise-then-collapse pattern across campaigns, peaking within tens of steps then falling, sometimes to near zero. On 3B, CARE v2 nearly doubles end-of-chain pass@1 (4.9%→9.5%). On 7B, ES reaches 22.2% vs naive 11.8%; GRPO reaches 20.7%. GRPO raises the floor but does not remove the within-campaign cliff (≈17-point peak-to-end gap). GRPO+ES shows mixed results. Pattern replicates on Gemma-3-4B.

Conclusion: The optimal control loop depends on model scale: CARE benefits smaller fragile models, while ES and GRPO are more effective for larger models. The collapse phenomenon is a fundamental property of policy over-optimization, not an artifact of a specific model family, and existing KL/EWC constraints do not prevent it.

Abstract: Self-improvement can self-regress. In REINFORCE post-training for code, a model can quickly improve on its optimized metric and then collapse within the same training campaign. We study this in a controlled multi-seed testbed using Qwen-2.5-3B and Qwen-2.5-7B, trained on competitive-programming tasks with binary CodeGrader reward across 10 sequential 20-step campaigns. Across campaigns, pass@1 shows a robust rise-then-collapse pattern: it peaks within tens of gradient steps and then falls back, sometimes to near zero. This is not cross-task catastrophic forgetting, but within-task policy over-optimization on a fixed distribution; KL- and EWC-style constraints do not prevent it.
  We ask where the control loop should sit. We compare three levels: CARE, a between-campaign memory mechanism with a capability posterior, transfer gate, and regression-aware belief revision; ES, a within-campaign early-stop rule that rolls forward the peak checkpoint and sets the next budget to peak_step+3; and GRPO, which changes the RL update using group-relative reward normalization.
  The answer is regime-dependent. On Qwen-2.5-3B, where naive REINFORCE is fragile, CARE v2 nearly doubles end-of-chain pass@1 from 4.9% to 9.5%, with paired bootstrap 95% CI [+0.4,+8.9] and gains in 4/5 seeds. On Qwen-2.5-7B, CARE reaches parity with naive REINFORCE, 13.8% vs. 11.8%, while ES reaches 22.2% [14.1,28.0]. Out-of-the-box GRPO reaches 20.7% [15.7,25.1], nearly matching REINFORCE+ES.
  GRPO raises the floor but does not remove the cliff. Its 7B gain mainly comes from better between-campaign carryover, while the within-campaign peak-to-end gap remains about 17 points under both REINFORCE and GRPO. GRPO+ES gives mixed evidence: 2/3 seeds improve, but one final cliff lowers the mean to 17.0% [0.0,28.1]. A Gemma-3-4B pilot shows the same signature, suggesting the phenomenon is not limited to Qwen.

</details>


### [441] [Answer Engineering: Local Trajectory Editing for Protocol-Constrained Decision Making in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21121)
*Victor Lavrenko,Anastasiia Molodnitskaia*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Answer Engineering uses deterministic, localized rule-guided interventions on the reasoning trajectory during autoregressive generation to improve protocol compliance, evaluated on a clinical SSNHL benchmark, achieving 80.7% balanced accuracy vs. 42.0% under reasoning-only generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large language models produce confident but protocol-invalid answers in procedurally critical domains (e.g., clinical management of SSNHL), where unguided chain-of-thought reasoning can shift rather than eliminate errors.

Method: Proposes Answer Engineering, a deterministic runtime and authoring layer that applies localized rule-guided interventions to the visible reasoning trajectory during standard autoregressive generation, without retraining, weight modification, or global search.

Result: On a controlled SSNHL benchmark, compliance improved from 54.5% (unguided) to 83.5% (Answer Engineering) for SSNHL cases, and from 1.6% to 77.9% for conductive contrast conditions. Balanced accuracy rose from 42.0% (reasoning-only) to 80.7%.

Conclusion: Protocol adherence can be improved through auditable runtime control of reasoning trajectories; limitations include rule coverage, trigger reliability, and persistent diagnosis-first generation dynamics.

Abstract: Large language models can produce confident but protocol-invalid answers in domains where procedural compliance is critical. This paper presents Answer Engineering, a deterministic runtime and authoring layer that applies localized rule-guided interventions to the visible reasoning trajectory during standard autoregressive generation, without retraining, modifying model weights, or performing global search. The method is evaluated on a controlled clinical benchmark for sudden sensorineural hearing loss (SSNHL), where correct management depends on protocol-consistent interpretation of symptom timing, Weber/Rinne tuning-fork findings, and otoscopic findings. In the benchmark, step-by-step reasoning shifted rather than eliminated errors: compliant outcomes for SSNHL decreased from 54.5% under unguided generation to 25.1%, while acceptance on the conductive contrast condition increased from 1.6% to 58.9%. Local trajectory editing increased SSNHL compliance to 83.5% and conductive-case adherence to 77.9%, raising balanced accuracy from 42.0% under reasoning-only generation to 80.7%. The results support a systems-level view in which protocol adherence can be improved through auditable runtime control of reasoning trajectories, while also identifying limitations caused by rule coverage, trigger reliability, and persistent diagnosis-first generation dynamics.

</details>


### [442] [PulseCX: Breaking the Closed-World Assumption in Real-Time CX](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21124)
*Rajat Agarwal,Suvidha Tripathi,Shubham Sharma*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PulseCX is a framework that decouples knowledge acquisition from consumption using a Decay-Aware Temporal Knowledge Graph and hierarchical intent gating to achieve <10ms overhead and improve Intent Resolution and Customer Satisfaction in dynamic environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conversational AI agents in Customer Experience suffer from a Closed-World Constraint, ignoring high-velocity external shifts like viral trends or outages. Ad-hoc web search introduces prohibitive latency and context poisoning.

Method: PulseCX employs (1) an asynchronous agent to linearize signals into a Decay-Aware Temporal Knowledge Graph (DA-TKG) governed by reinforcement-decay dynamics, and (2) hierarchical intent gating to decouple knowledge acquisition from consumption, removing synchronous search bottlenecks.

Result: Achieves <10ms overhead, significant gains in Intent Resolution (IRR) and Customer Satisfaction (s-CSAT) in dynamic environments.

Conclusion: Decoupling knowledge acquisition from consumption via a structure-first paradigm with temporal knowledge graphs and hierarchical gating effectively addresses the Closed-World Constraint in conversational AI without prohibitive latency.

Abstract: Conversational AI agents in Customer Experience (CX) typically suffer from a Closed-World Constraint, ignoring high-velocity external shifts like viral trends or outages. Ad-hoc web search attempts to bridge this gap but often introduce prohibitive latency and context poisoning. We introduce PulseCX, a framework that decouples knowledge acquisition from consumption. Adopting a structure-first paradigm, PulseCX employs an asynchronous agent to linearize signals into a Decay-Aware Temporal Knowledge Graph (DA-TKG) governed by reinforcement--decay dynamics to actively manage information lifecycles. By coupling this self-evolving memory with hierarchical intent gating, PulseCX removes synchronous search bottlenecks (<10ms overhead) and drives significant gains in Intent Resolution (IRR) and Customer Satisfaction (s-CSAT) in dynamic environments.

</details>


### [443] [Learning Burst-Aware Early Warning Models for Capacity Stress under AI Workload Surges in Hyperscale Data Centers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21130)
*Zihan Yu,Xianling Zeng,Zhiming Xue,Yalun Qi,Sichen Zhao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes a burst-aware early warning framework using XGBoost to proactively predict capacity stress from AI workload surges in hyperscale data centers, achieving high recall (0.914) for enabling preemptive operational interventions like workload throttling and resource scaling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional reactive threshold-based mechanisms fail to handle the bursty, high-intensity, and rapidly shifting resource demands of large-scale AI workloads (LLM training/inference), leading to sudden capacity stress in hyperscale data centers.

Method: Formulates high-recall forecasting over multivariate telemetry windows; integrates workload intensity, temporal variation, and system pressure signals; uses lightweight XGBoost to capture nonlinear interactions in imbalanced data; introduces AI workload surge injection methodology for realistic evaluation.

Result: XGBoost achieves ROC AUC of 0.697, AP of 0.670; under deployment-oriented threshold selection, Recall of 0.914, significantly outperforming baselines.

Conclusion: High-recall, learning-based early warning systems can be integrated into operational control loops for proactive actions (workload throttling, resource scaling), enabling resilient and adaptive data center operations for AI-driven workloads.

Abstract: The rapid growth of large-scale AI workloads, particularly Large Language Model (LLM) training and inference, is fundamentally reshaping the operational dynamics of hyperscale data centers. Unlike traditional cloud workloads, AI-driven jobs exhibit bursty, high-intensity, and rapidly shifting resource demands, often leading to sudden capacity stress that cannot be effectively handled by reactive threshold-based mechanisms. In this paper, we propose a deployment-oriented, burst-aware early warning framework for proactive capacity stress prediction under AI workload surges. We formulate the problem as a high-recall forecasting task over multivariate telemetry windows, with the explicit goal of enabling operational intervention before system degradation occurs. The proposed framework integrates workload intensity, temporal variation, and system pressure signals, and employs a lightweight tree-based learning model to capture nonlinear interactions in highly imbalanced environments. To evaluate the system under realistic conditions, we introduce an AI workload surge injection methodology that simulates burst-driven demand patterns observed in large-scale AI systems. Our XGBoost-based model achieves an ROC AUC of 0.697 and an AP of 0.670, significantly outperforming baseline methods. Under deployment-oriented threshold selection, the framework achieves a Recall of 0.914, enabling the detection of the majority of stress-prone periods with acceptable false-alarm cost. Beyond predictive performance, we show how the proposed framework can be integrated into operational control loops to support proactive actions such as workload throttling and resource scaling. Our results highlight the practical value of high-recall, learning-based early warning systems in enabling resilient and adaptive data center operations in the era of AI-driven workloads.

</details>


### [444] [AgentRiskBOM: A Risk-Scoping Security Bill of Materials for Agentic AI Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21877)
*Srimonti Dutta,Akshata Kishore Moharir*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces AgentRiskBOM, a security Bill of Materials artifact that fills the agentic transparency gap by providing a structured account of what deployed AI agents can access, remember, change, delegate, and prove. It adds runtime authority fields (autonomy, tool permissions, memory, credential scope, approval gates, audit signals, inter-agent communication, external action capability) over existing BOM standards (SBOM, AIBOM, MLBOM). Evaluated on 13 open-source agents and 52 risk scenarios, AgentRiskBOM achieves perfect validation, 14/16 native-equivalent capability coverage vs. 1-2 for existing BOMs, 100% risk-category visibility vs. 10.5-20.9%, and correctly identifies all 33 injected deployment mutations. A secondary scorer shows Spearman correlation of 0.73, confirming rank consistency but highlighting need for human-calibrated thresholds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing BOM artifacts (SBOM, AIBOM, MLBOM) improve transparency for dependencies, model metadata, and training provenance, but leave an agentic transparency gap: capability opacity, meaning there is no structured account of what a deployed agent can access, remember, change, delegate, or prove after deployment. This lack of runtime authority visibility creates security risks as agentic AI systems autonomously retrieve context, invoke tools, write files, call external services, and coordinate with other agents.

Method: The paper proposes AgentRiskBOM, a JSON-schema security BOM artifact built as an additive layer over existing BOMs (SBOM, AIBOM, MLBOM). It adds fields for runtime authority: autonomy, tool permissions, memory, credential scope, approval gates, audit signals, inter-agent communication, and external action capability. The implementation includes a reproducible corpus, risk scenarios, a scorer, a diff detector, a control mapper, and reports. Evaluation covers 13 open-source agents (coding, RAG, multi-agent archetypes) and 52 risk scenarios across 14 categories, plus 33 structured deployment mutations for authority drift testing.

Result: AgentRiskBOM validates all 13 corpus artifacts. It achieves a native-equivalent score of 14/16 capability dimensions vs. 1 (SBOM), 1.5 (AIBOM), and 2 (MLBOM). It provides 100% risk-category visibility vs. 10.5% (SBOM-like) and 20.9% (AIBOM-like). The diff detector correctly identifies change types for all 33 injected deployment mutations. A secondary penalty-based scorer yields Spearman correlation of 0.73 with the primary scorer.

Conclusion: Agentic AI security requires a machine-readable authority-and-risk artifact deployed before incidents occur. AgentRiskBOM addresses the capability opacity gap that existing BOMs fail to cover, and the results demonstrate that comprehensive runtime authority transparency is both feasible and necessary for risk-scoping tool-using AI agents.

Abstract: Agentic AI systems retrieve private context, invoke tools, write files, call external services, coordinate with other agents, and may act without human approval. Existing bill of materials artifacts improve transparency for dependencies, model metadata, and training provenance, but leave an agentic transparency gap: capability opacity, the absence of a structured account of what a deployed agent can access, remember, change, delegate, and prove afterward. This paper introduces AgentRiskBOM, a security BOM for risk-scoping tool-using AI agents. It is an additive layer over SBOM, AIBOM, and MLBOM artifacts, referencing them where authoritative while adding fields for runtime authority: autonomy, tool permissions, memory, credential scope, approval gates, audit signals, inter-agent communication, and external action capability. We implement AgentRiskBOM as a JSON-schema artifact with a reproducible corpus, risk scenarios, scorer, diff detector, control mapper, and reports. We evaluate AgentRiskBOM on 13 open-source agents spanning coding, RAG, and multi-agent archetypes, plus 52 risk scenarios across 14 categories. The schema validates all 13 corpus artifacts. Coverage analysis gives AgentRiskBOM a native-equivalent score of 14 across 16 capability dimensions, vs. 1 for SBOM, 1.5 for AIBOM and 2 for MLBOM. Across modeled risk categories, AgentRiskBOM exposes 100% risk-category visibility vs. 10.5% for SBOM-like and 20.9% for AIBOM-like views. To test agentic authority drift, we inject 33 structured deployment mutations; the diff detector identifies the correct change type for all mutations. A secondary penalty-based scorer yields a Spearman correlation of 0.73 with the primary scorer, supporting rank-level consistency while showing that thresholds require human calibration. The results show that agentic AI security needs a machine-readable authority-and-risk artifact before incidents occur.

</details>


### [445] [Trip+: Benchmarking Agents in Personalized Interactive Travel Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21169)
*Junle Chen,Wei Chen,Yehong Xu,Zhengjun Huang,Yuqian Wu,Zhoujin Tian,Kai Wang,Lei Wang,Xiaofang Zhou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Trip+ is a new benchmark for evaluating language models on holistic, interactive travel planning. It tests agents on generating and revising minute-level itineraries based on traveler profiles and dynamic interactions, using an LLM-based simulator to assess experiential quality. Evaluation of 18 LMs reveals a gap: models produce technically feasible but exhausting itineraries that ignore traveler preferences.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks evaluate feasibility, personalization, or interaction in relatively isolated settings, failing to capture the holistic, multi-turn nature of real-world travel planning where agents must handle evolving preferences and unexpected disruptions.

Method: Trip+ introduces scenarios requiring agents to generate and revise minute-level itineraries based on traveler profiles and dynamic interactions. An LLM-based simulator evaluates end-to-end traveler experiences, including subjective metrics like fatigue.

Result: Evaluation of 18 language models shows a consistent gap in experiential quality—models produce technically feasible but exhausting itineraries that diverge sharply from profiled traveler preferences.

Conclusion: Current LMs struggle to balance feasibility with personalization and comfort in interactive travel planning, highlighting the need for better holistic reasoning in such settings.

Abstract: Interactive travel planning has become a popular use case for language models. Agents are deployed to manage evolving preferences and unexpected disruptions over multiple turns. Such settings require models to make complex, profile-conditioned planning decisions. However, existing benchmarks often evaluate feasibility, personalization, or interaction in relatively isolated settings. We therefore introduce Trip+ to measure the ability of agents to plan travel holistically. In Trip+, given traveler profiles and dynamic interactions, agents must generate and revise minute-level itineraries. End-to-end traveler experiences are evaluated via an LLM-based simulator, enabling the assessment of subjective metrics like fatigue. Our scenarios range from simple request resolutions to complex environment-driven replanning. We evaluate 18 LMs and find a consistent gap in experiential quality. Models favor technically feasible but exhausting itineraries that diverge sharply from profiled traveler preferences.

</details>


### [446] [Holmes: Multimodal Agentic Diagnosis for Mixed-Language Mobile Crashes at Industrial Scale](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21963)
*Jia Li,Wenyuan Ma,Ting Peng,Haibin Zheng,Yuetang Deng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Holmes is a multi-agent system that achieves 87.6% accuracy in root cause analysis of mobile crashes in ultra-large-scale (70-million-line) codebases by synthesizing multimodal runtime signals (stack traces, logs, thread states, registers, assembly) without requiring crash reproduction, reducing investigation time by over 98% to ~77 seconds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diagnosing mobile crashes in ultra-large-scale industrial applications is extremely challenging due to massive code volume, mixed-language environments, and inability to reproduce failures locally. Traditional static analysis struggles with scalability, and existing LLM-based agents depend on reproducible environments unavailable in post-mortem scenarios.

Method: Holmes uses a multi-agent system with a hierarchical Retrieve-Explore-Reason architecture. It synthesizes multimodal runtime signals (stack traces, logs, thread states) to reconstruct failure contexts without reproduction. It leverages low-level artifacts (registers, assembly) to bridge the semantic gap between open-source business logic and closed-source system frameworks, dynamically compressing the search space using runtime clues to navigate 70-million-line codebases.

Result: Evaluated on real-world crashes from WeChat, Holmes achieves 87.6% accuracy in function-level fault localization and reduces average investigation time by over 98% (to approximately 77 seconds).

Conclusion: Holmes demonstrates effectiveness in transforming labor-intensive debugging into an efficient verification workflow for ultra-large-scale industrial mobile applications, overcoming scalability and reproducibility limitations of prior approaches.

Abstract: Diagnosing mobile crashes in ultra-large-scale industrial applications is a formidable challenge due to the sheer volume of code, the complexity of mixed-language environments, and the inability to reproduce failures locally. Traditional static analysis struggles with scalability, while existing LLM-based agents often rely on reproducible environments unavailable in post-mortem scenarios. We present Holmes, a multi-agent system that automates root cause analysis by synthesizing multimodal runtime signals--stack traces, logs, and thread states--to reconstruct failure contexts without reproduction. Holmes introduces a hierarchical Retrieve-Explore-Reason architecture that leverages low-level artifacts (e.g., registers, assembly) to bridge the semantic gap between open-source business logic and closed-source system frameworks. By dynamically compressing the search space using runtime clues, Holmes precisely navigates 70-million-line codebases to identify non-local defects. Evaluated on real-world crashes from WeChat, Holmes achieves 87.6% accuracy in function-level fault localization and reduces average investigation time by over 98% (to ~77 seconds), demonstrating its effectiveness in transforming labor-intensive debugging into an efficient verification workflow.

</details>


### [447] [Whistleblowing and the machine -- towards a considered position](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21201)
*Marija Slavkovik,Liuwen Yu,Leon van der Torre,Reka Markovich*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper argues that AI agents should be capable of normative, principled machine whistleblowing, grounded in existing societal understandings, and calls for government regulation to define what machines should whistleblow on and legally protect developers of such systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI agents generate and keep secrets as commercial products and personal tools; prior work shows agents can whistleblow and multi-agent environments should allow it, motivating the need to formalize machine whistleblowing as a principled mechanism.

Method: Conceptual argumentation building on existing whistleblowing theory, prior demonstrations of AI whistleblowing capability, and analysis of regulatory needs for machine whistleblowing.

Result: The authors establish that machine whistleblowing must be normative and principled, rooted in whistleblowing as a rule-breaking societal mechanism, and identify gaps in legal protection for developers and lack of regulatory stance.

Conclusion: Government regulators must formulate an informed stance on permissible machine whistleblowing topics and provide legal protection for developers of whistleblowing AI systems.

Abstract: Artificial intelligent agents and autonomous systems are embedded in our environments. They are both a commercial product and a personal tool that generates a lot of data and can draw conclusions from it: machines generate and keep secrets. But should machines protect all secrets? It has been shown that artificial agents are able to whistleblow and it has been argued that digital multi-agent environments should allow for agents in them to whistleblow. We argue that machine whistleblowing must be normative and principled and routed in the existing understanding of whistleblowing as an important rule-breaking mechanism in society. We also argue that there is a need for government regulators to formulate an informed stance on both what machines should be allowed to whistleblow on and how to legally protect those who develop whistleblowing machines

</details>


### [448] [CFAgentBench: A Reproducible Environment and Benchmark for Autonomous Construction-Finance Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22000)
*Rishi Srivastava*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CFAgentBench is a reproducible benchmark for autonomous construction-finance agents, featuring 1,014 tasks across 8 domains, 77 families, 35 mock applications, and a money-movement guard principle. The strongest open-weight agent achieves pass^1=0.67 but drops to pass^5=0.38 under temperature-0 decoding, showing single-attempt accuracy overstates deployable competence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is no existing benchmark for autonomous agents operating in the construction-finance domain, which involves complex workflows across ERP, project management, payroll, lien waivers, and treasury portals with critical money-movement safeguards. Existing benchmarks like WebArena lack domain-specific guardrails for financial transactions.

Method: The benchmark uses a reproducible, self-hostable executable environment with 35 mock applications (31 reconciled to one company book + 4 PM platforms) over 9 archetypes. Tasks are graded by functional correctness via state diff, forbidden-side-effect checks, and required-output regexes. A key methodological innovation is the money-movement guard: 278 tasks embed payment/payroll/e-signature/e-filing steps where the correct behavior is to stop and stage for human approval.

Result: In a three-model open-weight sweep (k=5), the strongest agent achieves pass^1=0.67 but only pass^5=0.38 — a 43% loss in successes when required to repeat under temperature-0 decoding. The public split (n=711) is sized for 95% Wilson half-width ±4.1%; a private contamination-protected split (n=303) is reserved for remote scoring.

Conclusion: The within-model pass^1 to pass^5 collapse and sharp per-domain heterogeneity demonstrate that single-attempt accuracy significantly overstates deployable construction-finance competence. The benchmark reveals that current agents lack the reliability needed for production financial workflows.

Abstract: We introduce CFAgentBench, a reproducible, self-hostable environment and benchmark for autonomous construction-finance agents: a CFO/controller-class agent operating across the real software stack a US construction finance team runs - ERP, project management, email, documents, pay applications, payroll, certified payroll, lien waivers, and bank/treasury portals. It contains 1,014 machine-gradeable task specifications across 8 domains and 77 families, every family grounded in a real source; a self-validated subset of 40 tasks (54 with a project-management extension) is compiled into oracle-validated executable evaluators, the runnable suite reported here. Following WebArena, the benchmark runs on an executable environment rather than static traces: 35 mock applications (31 reconciled to one company book, plus 4 PM platforms) over 9 archetypes, each implementing a uniform self-hostable app contract, so every task is graded by functional correctness - a state diff plus forbidden-side-effect checks plus required-output regexes - with an LLM judge used only for reply quality, never as reward. A distinguishing principle is a money-movement guard: 278 instances embed a payment, payroll, e-signature, or e-filing step where the correct behavior is to stop and stage for human approval, and executing even the correct transaction fails the task. The public split (n=711) is sized for a 95% Wilson half-width of +/-4.1%; a private, contamination-protected split (n=303) is reserved for remote scoring. In a first three-model open-weight sweep (k=5), the strongest agent reaches pass^1 = 0.67 but only pass^5 = 0.38 - losing 43% of its successes when required to repeat them under temperature-0 decoding. The within-model pass^1 to pass^5 collapse and sharp per-domain heterogeneity are clear evidence that single-attempt accuracy overstates deployable construction-finance competence.

</details>


### [449] [ARCO: Adaptive Rubric with Co-Evolution for Multi-Step LLM-Based Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21262)
*Zihang Tian,Jingsen Zhang,Rui Li,Xiaohe Bo,Yuanzi Li,Xu Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ARCO proposes a rubric-based reinforcement learning framework where a same-scale model shares a backbone with two heads (generation and scoring) to produce per-step criteria and step-level rewards, with a trajectory decomposition constraint and co-evolving rubric/policy, achieving state-of-the-art results on multi-hop QA benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scalar rewards in multi-step LLM agent RL lack interpretability and cannot explain why a trajectory is good or bad. Existing rubric-based methods score at the trajectory level and rely on frozen closed-source judges, leaving step-level credit assignment unresolved.

Method: A same-scale model μ shares a backbone with two heads: a generation head producing per-step criteria and a score head predicting rubric-conditioned step-level rewards. A trajectory decomposition constraint ties sum of step rewards to terminal outcome. μ and policy π are jointly updated on on-policy data for co-evolving rubric and scoring.

Result: Across HotpotQA, 2WikiMultiHopQA, and MuSiQue with two open-source backbones, ARCO improves best EM in every setting over outcome-, rubric-, and process-reward baselines. Rubrics are step-specific, robust to design choices, and useful for diagnosing agent behavior.

Conclusion: ARCO enables interpretable, step-level credit assignment without step-level labels, and its co-evolution design yields superior performance and diagnostic utility over existing methods.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning for multi-step LLM agents often relies on scalar rewards that indicate success but cannot explain why a trajectory is good or bad. Rubric-based rewards improve interpretability through natural-language criteria, but existing methods score at the trajectory level and freeze the scorer behind a closed-source judge, leaving step-level credit assignment unresolved and the judge itself static. We propose ARCO (Adaptive Rubric CO-evolution), a rubric framework in which a same-scale model $μ$ shares a backbone with two heads: a generation head that produces per-step criteria, and a score head that predicts rubric-conditioned step-level rewards. A trajectory decomposition constraint ties the sum of step rewards to the terminal outcome, enabling credit assignment without step-level labels, while $μ$ and the policy $π$ are jointly updated on on-policy data so that the rubric content and the scoring function co-evolve at the parameter level. Across HotpotQA, 2WikiMultiHopQA, and MuSiQue with two open-source backbones, ARCO improves the best EM in every setting over strong outcome-, rubric-, and process-reward baselines, and analyses show that its rubrics are step-specific, robust to design choices, and useful for diagnosing agent behavior. Codes and data are available at https://github.com/zihangtian/ARCO.

</details>


### [450] [Hypothesis-Driven Skill Optimization for LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22330)
*Fangxin Shang,Yehui Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HDSO is a train-free framework for safely updating LLM agent skills via hypothesis-driven validation, improving ALFWorld performance by +4–7 points and showing robustness to noisy feedback.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: External skill injection can improve LLM agents without weight changes, but skills distilled from sparse or noisy trajectories may encode spurious shortcuts or unexecutable rules, creating risks in persistent updates.

Method: HDSO uses frozen inference endpoints for both curator and executor. The curator observes traces, proposes falsifiable hypotheses with validation plans, instantiates candidate skill packages, validates via paired control/treatment executions, reviews behavioral differences, and consolidates only supported candidates. Executor consumes approved skills via progressive disclosure, preserving the executor-only fallback path.

Result: On ALFWorld: +6.9 Avg. SR for Qwen3-8B, +4.0 for Qwen3.6-27B over executor-only baselines. Under 20% random feedback noise, +7.1-point gain preserved for Qwen3-8B. Transfer and heterogeneous-pair diagnostics show validated repositories transfer across runs but cross-model curation succeeds only when curator diagnosis, executor capability, and validation evidence align.

Conclusion: HDSO provides an auditable, risk-mitigated skill lifecycle for frozen action agents, replacing unconstrained memory accumulation with hypothesis-driven validation and progressive disclosure.

Abstract: External skills can improve action-oriented LLM agents without changing model weights, but persistent skill updates are risky when they are distilled from sparse or noisy trajectories. A plausible reflection may encode a useful procedure, a spurious shortcut, or a rule that the target executor cannot reliably follow. We propose Hypothesis-Driven Skill Optimization (HDSO), a train-free framework in which both the skill curator and the agent executor are frozen inference endpoints. The curator observes executor traces, proposes a falsifiable hypothesis with an explicit validation plan, instantiates the hypothesis as a candidate skill package, validates the package through paired control/treatment executions, reviews behavior differences, and consolidates only supported candidates into an approved repository. The executor consumes approved skills through progressive disclosure, preserving the executor-only path when no skill is selected. On ALFWorld, HDSO improves executor-only baselines by +6.9 Avg. SR points for Qwen3-8B and +4.0 points for Qwen3.6-27B. Under 20% randomly flipped success/failure feedback during skill discovery and validation, HDSO preserves a +7.1-point gain for Qwen3-8B. Transfer and heterogeneous-pair diagnostics further show that validated repositories can be useful beyond the run that produced them, but cross-model curation succeeds only when curator diagnosis, executor capability, and validation evidence align. HDSO provides an auditable skill lifecycle for frozen action agents rather than an unconstrained memory accumulation procedure.

</details>


### [451] [Towards Dys-XAI: Influence-Based Explanations for Dysarthria Severity Assessment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21306)
*Xiaoliang Wu,Qiyang Sun,Yupei Li,Erfan Loweimi,Jennifer Williams,Zhengjun Yue*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes an influence-based explainability framework for dysarthria severity assessment, using gradient-based influence approximations to identify supportive and competing training samples for each prediction, validated through controlled deletion experiments showing systematic prediction shifts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manual perceptual rating of dysarthria severity is time-consuming and variable across clinicians, and existing deep learning models lack interpretability, while current speech explainability methods provide acoustic feature importance scores that are difficult for end-users to interpret.

Method: An influence-based, instance-level explainability framework using gradient-based influence approximations to compute per-utterance influence scores, identifying supportive and competing training samples for each prediction.

Result: Controlled deletion experiments from 5% to 20% validate the explanations, demonstrating that removing highly influential samples systematically shifts predictions.

Conclusion: The approach provides auditable explanations by linking decisions to perceptible reference cases, enhancing interpretability for clinical adoption.

Abstract: Dysarthria severity assessment is essential for therapy planning and longitudinal monitoring, yet manual perceptual rating is time-consuming and variable across clinicians. Although deep learning models achieve strong performance, their black-box nature limits clinical adoption. Existing speech explainability methods typically provide acoustic feature importance scores that are difficult for end-users to interpret. We propose an influence-based, instance-level explainability framework that explains each decision through supportive and competing training samples. Using gradient-based influence approximations, we compute per-utterance influence scores to identify supportive and competing training samples for each prediction. Controlled deletion experiments from 5 to 20 percent validate the explanations, showing that removing highly influential samples systematically shifts predictions. This approach provides auditable explanations by linking decisions to perceptible reference cases.

</details>


### [452] [Text2DSL: LLM-Based Code Generation for Domain-Specific Languages](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22586)
*Alexander V. Kozachok,Alexander M. Nazimov,Shamil G. Magomedov*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper formalizes Text2DSL (automatic DSL code generation from natural language) as a distinct problem, introduces the PolkitBench dataset with 4,204 verified NL-to-Polkit-rule pairs, and shows that providing structured context (BNF grammar, API spec, permitted identifiers) boosts syntactic validity to 98.6-99.4%, structural validity by +9.7-35.5 pp, and CodeBLEU by +60-95% across two MoE models without fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manually authoring security policy rules in DSLs requires high expertise and is error-prone; existing Text-to-SQL and general code generation approaches do not adequately address the unique challenges of DSL code generation.

Method: Formalizes Text2DSL as a distinct problem class; constructs PolkitBench with 4,204 NL-to-Polkit-rule pairs validated via a three-level AST-based pipeline; conducts controlled prompt experiments on two MoE models (GigaChat-10B-A1.8B and Nemotron-3-Nano-30B-A3B) with and without structured context (BNF grammar, API specification, permitted identifier vocabulary).

Result: Supplying structured context raises syntactic validity to 98.6-99.4%, structural validity by +9.7 to +35.5 pp, and CodeBLEU by +60% to +95% across both models.

Conclusion: Injecting formal target-language specification into the prompt context is a robust enabling factor for high-quality Text2DSL generation without model fine-tuning, with consistent effects across models of different scale and provenance.

Abstract: Domain-specific languages (DSLs) are widely used for managing operating system security policies, yet manually authoring rules in such languages demands high expertise and is error-prone. This paper formalises the task of automatic DSL code generation from natural language descriptions - Text2DSL - as a distinct problem class, separate from Text-to-SQL and general-purpose code generation. We introduce the PolkitBench dataset comprising 4,204 verified natural-language-to-Polkit-rule pairs, each validated through a three-level AST-based pipeline. Controlled prompt experiments on two MoE models of different scale and provenance - GigaChat-10B-A1.8B (1.8B active parameters) and Nemotron-3-Nano-30B-A3B (3B active) - demonstrate the critical role of structured context (BNF grammar, API specification, permitted identifier vocabulary) for LLM-based DSL code generation. Across both models, supplying context raises syntactic validity to 98.6-99.4%, structural validity by +9.7 to +35.5 pp, and the CodeBLEU score by +60% to +95%. The consistency of the effect across models of different scale and provenance indicates that, for the Text2DSL class of problems, injecting a formal target-language specification into the prompt context is a robust enabling factor for high-quality generation without model fine-tuning.

</details>


### [453] [Social World Model for Lifelong Social Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21315)
*Yu Luo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces the Social World Model, a closed-loop learning framework that transforms social interaction into structured, iterable training signals. Using a five-dimensional decomposition (scene, observation, mental state, action, dialogue), it enables sustainable capability acquisition with zero forgetting. Validated on ASCENT-Bench, a 7B model matches/exceeds Gemini 3 Flash in key metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current research evaluates social intelligence statically rather than studying how these skills are continuously shaped and accumulated. Two pain points exist: social interaction trajectories lack unified structured representations for iterable learning, and capability improvement vs. retention are studied in isolation, hindering assessment of continuous evolution.

Method: Proposes the Social World Model, decomposing social interaction into five dimensions (scene setting, observation, mental state, action, dialogue) to form a closed-loop learning framework. Agents collect experiences, convert them into preference signals for model updating, and redeploy updated policies. Includes a reusable data synthesis mechanism and lifelong learning benchmark.

Result: On ASCENT-Bench, interactively trained Qwen2.5-7B outperforms baseline across all five metrics, matches Gemini 3 Flash in completion rate, exceeds it in pass rate, and achieves zero forgetting across three difficulty levels.

Conclusion: The end-to-end approach provides a trainable, verifiable, and retainable pathway for sustainable social capability acquisition, demonstrating that small open-source models can competitively acquire social coordination skills without capability decay.

Abstract: Social intelligence is a core competency for language agents, yet current research primarily focuses on static capability evaluation rather than how these skills are continuously shaped and accumulated. This gap calls for a shift toward sustainable learning paradigms. Currently, two methodological pain points exist: social interaction trajectories lack unified structured representations to form iterable learning signals, and capability improvement and retention are typically studied in isolation, hindering the assessment of continuous evolution.
  To bridge this gap, we propose the Social World Model. We decompose social interaction into five dimensions (scene setting, observation, mental state, action, and dialogue) to build a closed-loop learning framework. In this setup, agents collect interaction experiences, convert them into preference signals for model updating, and redeploy the updated policy for continued learning. Additionally, we provide a reusable data synthesis mechanism and a lifelong learning benchmark, transforming social capabilities from an "object of evaluation" into an "object of sustainable training".
  Validating our framework on the ASCENT-Bench, the interactively trained Qwen2.5-7B model outperforms its baseline across all five core metrics. Notably, it matches the closed-source Gemini 3 Flash in completion rate, exceeds it in pass rate, and achieves zero forgetting across three difficulty levels. Unlike prior works that merely report static comparisons or capability decay, this end-to-end approach provides a trainable, verifiable, and retainable pathway, demonstrating that small open-source models can sustainably acquire competitive social coordination capabilities.

</details>


### [454] [AgentLens: Interpretable Safety Steering via Mechanistic Subspaces for Multi-Turn Coding Agent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22673)
*Weidi Luo,Qiming Zhang,Yihao Quan,Mingyu Jin,Jie Cai,Chaowei Xiao,Jingcheng Niu,Zhen Xiang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AgentLens is a white-box defense framework that performs runtime safety detection and representation-level mitigation for multi-turn coding agents by analyzing hidden representations in a 10-dimensional subspace within a single layer, supported by the Mechanistic Agent Safety (MAS) benchmark across 194 tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing safety mechanisms for LLM-based coding agents rely on external guardrails with limited fine-grained behavioral control, while mechanistic interpretability methods are mostly confined to single-turn or jailbreak-style QA settings, failing to capture evolving risk dynamics in multi-turn agent execution.

Method: AgentLens performs runtime safety detection by analyzing step-level hidden representations and mitigates unsafe behavior by intervening in a 10-dimensional subspace within a single layer. The authors introduce the MAS benchmark with annotated multi-turn execution trajectories across 194 tasks using LLaMA-3.1-8B, Qwen-2.5-7B, and Gemma-2-9B.

Result: AgentLens achieves strong safety detection performance, provides preliminary evidence for lookahead risk anticipation, and substantially reduces harmful actions of coding agents.

Conclusion: AgentLens establishes a foundation for applying mechanistic interpretability to dynamic LLM agent safety, offering a white-box alternative to conventional external guardrails.

Abstract: Coding agents based on large language models (LLMs) demonstrate remarkable autonomous capabilities, but they also introduce significant safety and misuse risks during multi-turn interactions with external environments. Existing safety mechanisms mainly rely on external guardrails, which have a limited ability to perform fine-grained behavioral control during execution. Meanwhile, recent mechanistic interpretability methods for LLM safety are mostly confined to single-turn or jailbreak-style QA settings, limiting their ability to capture the evolving risk dynamics of multi-turn agent execution. In this paper, we investigate the safety of multi-turn coding agents from an internal perspective. We propose AgentLens (Mechanistic Subspace Intervention and Steering), a white-box defense framework that performs runtime safety detection and representation-level mitigation for coding agents. Unlike conventional agent guardrails, AgentLens detect harmful execution states from step-level hidden representations and mitigate unsafe behavior by intervening in a 10-dimensional subspace within a single layer. To support this research, we introduce the Mechanistic Agent Safety (MAS) benchmark, comprising comprehensively annotated multi-turn execution trajectories across 194 tasks using LLaMA-3.1-8B, Qwen-2.5-7B, and Gemma-2-9B. Extensive experiments show that AgentLens achieves strong safety detection performance, provides preliminary evidence for lookahead risk anticipation, and substantially reduces harmful actions of the coding agent, establishing a foundation for applying mechanistic interpretability to dynamic LLM agent safety. The code is available at: https://github.com/EddyLuo1232/AgentLens

</details>


### [455] [Mind the Noise: Sensitivity of Transformer-based Interaction-Aware Trajectory Prediction Models to Noisy Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21344)
*Shahab Salehi,Luca Lusvarghi,Miguel Sepulcre,Javier Gozalvez*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper analyzes how noisy object state information (position, speed, heading) degrades the trajectory prediction accuracy of Transformer-based interaction-aware models, finding accuracy reductions of 1.3x under small noise and up to 3.9x under realistic high-noise conditions, highlighting the need for noise-aware training and evaluation datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world trajectory prediction systems face noisy object state estimates due to perception uncertainties, localization errors, and V2X communication noise, yet state-of-the-art models are trained and evaluated on clean, noise-free datasets, creating a gap between idealized performance and real-world deployment conditions.

Method: The study introduces controlled noise into object state inputs (position, speed, heading) of a state-of-the-art Transformer-based interaction-aware trajectory prediction model and measures the resulting degradation in prediction accuracy under varying noise intensities.

Result: Prediction accuracy degrades by a factor of 1.3x under small noise levels and up to 3.9x under the highest realistic noise conditions, demonstrating strong sensitivity to input noise.

Conclusion: Current Transformer-based trajectory prediction models are highly sensitive to noisy object state information, underscoring the urgent need for realistic noise-inclusive training/evaluation datasets and robust noise mitigation strategies for reliable autonomous driving deployment.

Abstract: Trajectory prediction allows autonomous vehicles to anticipate the future behavior of surrounding objects (or agents) and, accordingly, maximize the safety and efficiency of their driving. State-of-the-art Transformed-based interaction-aware trajectory prediction models, which rely on attention mechanisms to capture multi-agent interactions and maximize prediction accuracy, are commonly trained and evaluated on long-range high-quality datasets. These datasets are typically obtained by aggregating data from multiple vehicles or drones and removing any object detection or tracking noise offline. Yet, information about a surrounding object's state (its position, speed, heading) is far from being noiseless in real-world deployments. Object state estimation is affected by perception uncertainties and localization errors that can be particularly large for objects received via Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) communications. In this paper, we analyze the impact of noisy object state information on the trajectory prediction accuracy of a state-of-the-art Transformer-based interaction-aware trajectory prediction model. Our study demonstrates that trajectory prediction accuracy can rapidly deteriorate as the noise intensity increases. Numerical results show that the prediction accuracy can reduce by a 1.3x factor under small noise levels and by as much as a 3.9x factor under the highest (yet realistic) noise conditions. These findings reveal the strong sensitivity of trajectory prediction models to noisy data, underscoring the need for more realistic training and evaluation datasets as well as noise mitigation strategies.

</details>


### [456] [GroundEval: A Deterministic Replacement for LLM-as-Judge in Stateful Agent Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22737)
*Jeffrey Flynt*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: GroundEval is a judge-free framework that evaluates agents by comparing their trajectories against grounded, time-bounded, and access-controlled evidence, revealing that plausible-sounding agent answers can score 0.000 when the evidence path is invalid.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-as-judge and final-answer evaluation methods fail to detect when agents use invalid evidence paths (e.g., not retrieving the artifact their answer depends on, reasoning from time-inaccessible information, or using wrong causal mechanisms).

Method: GroundEval uses domain configurations to generate questions, records the agent's trajectory (searches, fetches, citations, access permissions), and scores both the final answer and the evidence path against three tracks: Silence (did agent check before claiming absence), Perspective (did agent use only time-appropriate evidence), and Counterfactual (did agent use the correct causal mechanism).

Result: In a case study, two frontier LLM judges scored a plausible agent response >0.85, but GroundEval detected the agent never retrieved the required artifact, yielding a score of 0.000. The paper demonstrates this gap is common, not a rare corner case.

Conclusion: GroundEval provides structured, per-question diagnostics pairing tool activity with turn-level narration, making scores inspectable, and reveals that final-answer and judge-based scoring have a systematic blind spot that GroundEval addresses.

Abstract: Before letting an agent operate over real context, can you prove it used the right evidence? GroundEval turns that question into a deterministic test of what the agent searched, fetched, cited, and was permitted to access. In one case study, two frontier LLM judges scored a plausible agent response above 0.85. But the trace told a different story: the agent had never retrieved the artifact its answer depended on, yielding a GroundEval score of 0.000.
  We introduce GroundEval, a judge-free framework for evaluating agents against grounded, time-bounded, and access-controlled evidence. GroundEval uses a domain configuration to generate questions, lets the agent choose how to answer, and then scores both the final answer and the recorded trajectory that produced it. The benchmark targets three failures that LLM-as-judge evaluation struggles to detect: whether an agent checked before claiming absence, reasoned only from evidence available to the actor at the relevant time, and used the correct causal mechanism rather than a plausible one. These correspond to three tracks: Silence, Perspective, and Counterfactual. GroundEval exposes when plausible answers rest on invalid evidence paths, and produces structured per-question diagnostics that pair tool activity with the agent's turn-level narration, making each score inspectable rather than merely reported. What our case studies turned up is that this gap isn't some rare corner case. It's exactly the blind spot that final-answer and judge-based scoring were never built to catch.

</details>


### [457] [Calibration Is Not Control: Why LLM-Agent Oversight Needs Intervention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21399)
*Chubin Zhang,Zhenglin Wan,Xingrui Yu,Jingxuan Wu,Qi Wen,Pengfei Zhou,Wangbo Zhao,Ivor Tsang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLM agent oversight should shift from scalar risk prediction (estimating failure likelihood) to action-conditioned value estimation (measuring whether an intervention would improve outcomes), as risk estimates alone fail to distinguish recoverable from unrecoverable states.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current oversight methods frame the problem as scalar risk prediction—estimating failure likelihood, confidence, or uncertainty—then intervening when a threshold is crossed. The authors argue this targets the wrong object because two trajectory prefixes with identical risk estimates may require different actions (one recoverable, one not), leading to suboptimal control.

Method: Formalizes "target error" (the mismatch between risk estimation and optimal intervention) and proposes "intervention advantage" (expected utility gain from intervening vs. continuing) as the correct decision object. Introduces "prefix branching," a same-prefix counterfactual protocol that executes candidate actions from identical trajectory states. Evaluates action-conditioned control against scalar routing across four benchmarks, with a calibration decomposition analysis.

Result: Action-conditioned control yields regime-dependent gains over scalar routing. Recalibrating scalar scores improves prediction metrics but leaves control regret unchanged. A prefix-only action-conditioned controller reduces regret substantially in the strongest interactive regime (0.506 to 0.110 on ALFWorld). Gains diminish when interventions are weak or scalar routing already preserves intervention-relevant information.

Conclusion: LLM-agent oversight should move from calibrated risk scoring toward action-conditioned value estimation.

Abstract: Runtime oversight for LLM agents is commonly framed as scalar risk prediction: estimate failure likelihood, confidence, or uncertainty, then intervene once the score crosses a threshold. We argue that this framing targets the wrong object for control. The relevant question is not how likely the agent is to fail if it continues, but whether an available intervention would improve the outcome. Two trajectory prefixes can have the same risk estimate while requiring different actions, because one remains recoverable and the other does not. We formalize this mismatch as target error and identify intervention advantage, the expected utility gain from intervening rather than continuing, as the decision object for oversight. To measure this mismatch, we introduce prefix branching, a same-prefix counterfactual protocol that executes candidate actions from identical trajectory states. Across four benchmarks, action-conditioned control yields regime-dependent gains over scalar routing. In a calibration decomposition, recalibrating the same scalar score improves prediction metrics but leaves control regret unchanged, showing that calibration alone does not repair target error. A simple prefix-only action-conditioned controller substantially reduces regret in the strongest interactive regime, from 0.506 to 0.110 on ALFWorld. Gains shrink when interventions are weak or when scalar routing already preserves intervention-relevant information. These results suggest that LLM-agent oversight should move from calibrated risk scoring toward action-conditioned value estimation.

</details>


### [458] [Managing Procedural Memory in LLM Agents: Control, Adaptation, and Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23127)
*Julia Belikova,Rauf Parchiev,Evgeny Egorov,Grigorii Davydenko,Gleb Gusev,Andrey Savchenko,Maksim Makarenko*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AFTER is a benchmark of 382 enterprise tasks across 6 roles and 22 procedural skills for evaluating how procedural memory transfers across tasks, roles, and models. Experiments show local improvement of 3.7-6.7 points and 73.1% cross-model accuracy with diverse multi-model traces, revealing that some skills generalize broadly while others specialize to role-specific workflows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Procedural memory is increasingly used to improve LLM agents on recurring workplace tasks, yet its ability to produce reusable skills remains poorly understood.

Method: The authors introduce AFTER, a benchmark of 382 realistic enterprise tasks spanning six professional roles and 22 procedural skills, with controlled evaluation settings for local improvement, cross-task transfer, cross-role transfer, and cross-model generalization.

Result: Procedural memory delivers consistent gains: a single refinement round improves aggregate performance by 3.7-6.7 points, and skills evolved from diverse multi-model execution traces achieve 73.1% cross-model test accuracy, outperforming all single-model trace sources.

Conclusion: Some skills generalize broadly across tasks and models, whereas others become specialized to role-specific workflows and lose effectiveness under transfer. These results provide practical guidance for building, evaluating, and deploying procedural memory systems in production agent platforms.

Abstract: Procedural memory is increasingly used to improve LLM agents on recurring workplace tasks, yet its ability to produce reusable skills remains poorly understood. We introduce AFTER, a benchmark of 382 realistic enterprise tasks spanning six professional roles and 22 procedural skills, designed to evaluate how skills transfer across tasks, roles, and model backbones. The benchmark includes controlled evaluation settings for local improvement, cross-task transfer, cross-role transfer, and cross-model generalization. Experiments show that procedural memory delivers consistent gains in industrial workflows: a single refinement round improves aggregate performance by 3.7-6.7 points, while skills evolved from diverse multi-model execution traces achieve 73.1% cross-model test accuracy, outperforming all single-model trace sources. We further find that some skills generalize broadly across tasks and models, whereas others become specialized to role-specific workflows and lose effectiveness under transfer. These results provide practical guidance for building, evaluating, and deploying procedural memory systems in production agent platforms.

</details>


### [459] [Don't Blindly Trust It: How Unreliable Feedback Breaks Tool-Using LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21409)
*Chubin Zhang,Zhenglin Wan,Xingrui Yu,Pengfei Zhou,Wangbo Zhao,Jingxuan Wu,Yaxin Zhou,Ivor Tsang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Tool-augmented agents can suffer a value inversion under misleading feedback, performing worse than a no-feedback fallback, highlighting the need for matched fallback controls in evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To study the counterfactual of whether a tool-augmented agent would be better off receiving no task evidence when external feedback is unreliable, rather than relying on potentially misleading tool outputs.

Method: Controlled matched-loop comparison fixing agent loop, prompt, action space, and decoding while varying only the returned observation (faithful, misleading, or absent) across question answering and fact verification tasks.

Result: Persistent misleading feedback produces a value inversion (e.g., Qwen2.5-7B drops from 44.8 F1 with clean retrieval to 4.7 F1 under shuffled retrieval, worse than 22.3 F1 with no feedback). The inversion persists under stronger clean retrieval and plausible distractors but weakens with later clean evidence repair.

Conclusion: Clean-tool gains can overstate tool value; matched no-fallback fallback controls are necessary for properly evaluating tool-augmented agents.

Abstract: Tool-augmented agents are typically evaluated by their gains under reliable external feedback. Yet these gains leave open a key counterfactual: when feedback is unreliable, would the agent be better off receiving no task evidence? We study this question with a controlled matched-loop comparison that fixes the agent loop, prompt, action space, and decoding, while varying only the returned observation: faithful, misleading, or absent. Across question answering and fact verification, persistent misleading feedback produces a value inversion: agents that benefit from clean tools can perform worse than the matched no-feedback fallback. On HotpotQA, Qwen2.5-7B reaches 44.8 F1 with clean retrieval and 22.3 F1 with no feedback, but drops to 4.7 F1 under shuffled retrieval. The inversion persists under stronger clean retrieval and locally plausible distractors, but weakens when later clean evidence can repair the trajectory. Early trajectory signals predict many failures, yet simple repairs remain fallback-limited: rejecting bad evidence helps only when the exposed fallback is reliable. These results show that clean-tool gains can overstate tool value, and that matched no-feedback fallback controls are necessary for evaluating tool-augmented agents.

</details>


### [460] [AutoRAS: Learning Robust Agentic Systems with Primitive Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21445)
*Yang Yue,Xuancheng Zhu,Yuyang Ma,Guoshun Nan,Zihan Dou,Jingru Shan,Congyu Guo,Ji Zhang,Hua Wang,Jingfeng Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AutoRAS is a framework for automatically designing robust multi-agent systems that jointly optimize structural connectivity and behavioral actions using safety signals and flow-based sequence-level objectives, achieving strong performance in both vanilla and adversarial settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing automated multi-agent workflows focus on task performance but treat robustness as an afterthought, leaving systems vulnerable to adversarial attacks and internal failures.

Method: AutoRAS formulates system design as generating a sequence of symbolic primitives encoding structural connectivity and behavioral actions, optimized using execution-derived safety signals and flow-based sequence-level objectives.

Result: Best performance in both vanilla and adversarial settings with minimal performance degradation under attacks; strong transferability, stable optimization, stability across primitive sets, and favorable cost trade-offs.

Conclusion: AutoRAS demonstrates that robustness can be explicitly optimized during automated agentic system design, achieving superior adversarial resilience without sacrificing vanilla performance.

Abstract: The automated design of agentic systems offers a promising pathway for scaling large language models (LLMs) beyond single-agent reasoning. While prior work has advanced task performance through handcrafted or automatically generated multi-agent workflows, robustness is often treated as an afterthought, leaving systems vulnerable to external adversaries and internal failures. We propose AutoRAS, a framework for the Automated design of Robust Agentic Systems. AutoRAS formulates system design as generating a sequence of symbolic primitives that jointly encode structural connectivity and behavioral actions, and learns to optimize this sequence using execution-derived safety signals and flow-based sequence-level objectives. Extensive experiments show that AutoRAS achieves the best performance in both vanilla and adversarial settings, with the smallest performance degradation under attacks. Further analyses demonstrate strong transferability, stable optimization behavior, stability across primitive sets, and favorable cost trade-offs. Our code is available at $\href{https://github.com/guohezuy/AutoRAS}{\text{this https URL}}$.

</details>


### [461] [Towards Transparent Mental Health Insights: An Explainable AI Model for Career-Related Depression and Anxiety Among University Students Using Structured Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21474)
*Arsham Azam,Rasikh Ali,Tayyaba Farhat,Sheeraz Akram*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A privacy-preserving Explainable AI framework using multimodal data and Federated Learning to detect career-related mental health issues (depression/anxiety) in university students, achieving 92.08% accuracy with interpretable behavioral markers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Career anxiety and depression among university students pose growing challenges to mental health and academic achievement, necessitating early detection systems that are both privacy-preserving and culturally responsive.

Method: Proposes an XAI framework combining structured behavioral data and facial emotion features from interview videos via an intermediate fusion neural network with attention mechanisms. Label smoothing improves generalizability, and Federated Learning enables cross-institutional training without sharing raw data. Explainability via Integrated Gradients and SHAP.

Result: 91.88% precision, 86.54% recall, 89.12% F1-score, 92.08% accuracy on the Student Mental Health Survey dataset from Pakistan. Identified key behavioral markers: avoidance of direct gaze, lower facial expressiveness, and social withdrawal.

Conclusion: Presents an interpretable, scalable, and context-sensitive AI system for mental health pre-diagnosis, suitable for integration into student support services globally.

Abstract: Career anxiety and depression among university students present a growing challenge to mental health and academic achievement. This study proposes an Explainable AI (XAI) framework using multimodal data and Federated Learning (FL) to identify early indicators of career-related mental health problems in a privacy-preserving and culturally responsive manner. The framework combines structured behavioral data and facial emotion features from interview videos via an intermediate fusion neural network with attention mechanisms. Label smoothing was applied to improve model generalizability. FL was used across institutions to enable collaborative training without raw data sharing. Evaluation was conducted using the Student Mental Health Survey dataset from university students across Pakistan. Our model attained an F1-score of 89.12%, recall of 86.54%, accuracy of 92.08%, and precision of 91.88%. Using Integrated Gradients and SHAP, the model identified key behavioral markers of depression including avoidance of direct gaze, lower facial expressiveness, and social withdrawal, consistent with psychological theory. This research presents an interpretable, scalable, and context-sensitive AI system for mental health pre-diagnosis with potential integration into student support services globally.

</details>


### [462] [Causal Discovery in the Era of Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23608)
*Yujia Zheng,Vishal Verma,Mantej Gill,Haoyue Dai,Peter Spirtes,Kun Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes that LLM agents in causal discovery should assist the workflow (inspect data, retrieve context, explain assumptions, clarify outputs) but never supply edges, orientations, priors, or causal conclusions, ensuring causal claims remain grounded in data, assumptions, and formal algorithms. This is instantiated in causal-learn+, an online platform demonstrated on Big Five personality data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing approaches that combine LLMs with causal discovery rely on models to infer directions, propose graphs, or inject priors, which risks confounding causal evidence with textual associations, prompt artifacts, and hallucinated mechanisms.

Method: Proposes the principle that agents assist the workflow (data inspection, context retrieval, method explanation, graph clarification) but do not supply causal content. Implements this in causal-learn+, an online coordinating platform around the causal-learn ecosystem.

Result: A case study on Big Five personality data demonstrates an agent-assisted pipeline of causal discovery without turning language-model unreliability into causal evidence.

Conclusion: LLM agents should play a supportive role in causal discovery rather than a generative one, preserving the evidential integrity of causal claims.

Abstract: Recent attempts to combine large language models (LLMs) with causal discovery ask models to infer pairwise directions, propose graph structures, or inject language-model outputs as priors and constraints. These approaches promise faster analysis, but they also obscure whether a causal evidence is supported by data and assumptions or by textual associations, prompt artifacts and hallucinated mechanisms. We argue for a different role for agents in causal discovery. Agents should inspect data, retrieve context, explain method assumptions and clarify graph outputs, but they should not supply edges, orientations, priors, constraints or causal conclusions. We propose the principle that agents assist the workflow, while causal claims remain grounded in data, explicit assumptions, formal algorithms, diagnostics and user or domain-expert decisions. We instantiate this principle in causal-learn+, an online platform that coordinates data analysis, preprocessing, method recommendation, expert-knowledge incorporation, formal discovery and interpretation around the algorithmic ecosystem of causal-learn. A case study on Big Five personality data illustrates agent-assisted pipeline of causal discovery without turning language-model unreliability into causal evidence. The platform is available at causallearn.com.

</details>


### [463] [Balancing Performance and Diversity in GRPO Autoregressive Text-to-Image Post-Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21498)
*Yuanhao Chiang,Hongbo Duan,Chunru Yang,Jiahua Pei,Yi Liu,Xueqian Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes using JS divergence instead of fixed KL divergence for GRPO-style online reinforcement learning in autoregressive text-to-image generation, achieving better alignment with human preferences while maintaining diversity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing GRPO-style methods treat reference-policy divergence as fixed, which directly impacts policy optimization. The authors aim to systematically study different divergence choices (forward KL, reverse KL, JS) within a unified f-divergence framework for better T2I alignment.
