Method: The authors formulate a unified f-divergence framework for GRPO-style autoregressive T2I alignment, analyzing how forward KL, reverse KL, and JS divergence reshape token-level updates differently. They identify that JS regularization achieves a favorable trade-off by mitigating uniform bias while discouraging large deviations.

Result: Experiments on LlamaGen and Janus-7B show that JS divergence achieves the strongest or highly competitive optimization performance on most evaluation metrics while maintaining favorable generation diversity.

Conclusion: JS divergence is a more effective regularization choice than fixed KL divergence for GRPO-style autoregressive T2I alignment, balancing performance and diversity.

Abstract: Autoregressive text-to-image (T2I) generation has recently advanced rapidly, yet aligning generated images with human preferences remains challenging. GRPO-style online reinforcement learning provides an effective framework; however, existing methods typically treat reference-policy divergence as fixed, despite its direct impact on policy optimization. We study this overlooked factor within a unified f-divergence framework, encompassing forward KL, reverse KL, and JS divergence, for GRPO-style autoregressive T2I alignment. Our systematic theoretical analysis reveals that different divergences reshape token-level updates in distinct ways. In particular, under the sampled-token shaping form used, JS regularization achieves a favorable trade-off by mitigating uniform bias relative to the reference policy while still discouraging large deviations. Extensive experiments on LlamaGen and Janus-7B show that JS divergence achieves the strongest or highly competitive optimization performance on most evaluation metrics while maintaining favorable generation diversity. The code is available at https://github.com/tuoyou-hao/BPD-GRPO.

</details>


### [464] [AI Alignment From Social Choice Perspectives](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21550)
*Daniel Halpern,Evi Micha,Ariel D. Procaccia,Benjamin Schiffer,Itai Shapira,Shirley Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper surveys how social choice theory informs the aggregation of human feedback for aligning language models, identifying failure modes and design opportunities when judgments conflict.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: When human feedback reflects conflicting views on desirable model behavior, the aggregation of these judgments into a single objective becomes problematic. The authors aim to study this aggregation problem through social choice theory to understand and address these conflicts.

Method: The authors survey recent work at the intersection of alignment from human feedback and social choice theory, illustrating how social choice perspectives can be applied to the feedback aggregation layer.

Result: The social choice perspective helps identify failure modes in the feedback aggregation layer and reveals a broader design space for handling disagreement in explicit and principled ways.

Conclusion: Social choice theory provides a principled lens for analyzing and improving how conflicting human judgments are aggregated during alignment, expanding the design space for handling disagreement.

Abstract: Alignment from human feedback uses human judgments about model outputs to steer the behavior of language models after pretraining. When those judgments reflect conflicting views of desirable behavior, the learned objective becomes an aggregate determination of what the model should prefer. We survey recent work that has studied this aggregation problem through the lens of social choice theory. We illustrate how the social choice perspective helps identify failure modes in the feedback aggregation layer and reveals a broader design space for handling disagreement in explicit and principled ways.

</details>


### [465] [Composing Verifiable Conceptual Models via Building Blocks: Towards Design-Time Verification of Agentic AI Workflows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21565)
*Noe Y. Flandre,Alexander C. Nwala,Philippe J. Giabbanelli*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a design-time verification approach for agentic AI workflows that models them as compositions of reusable building blocks and checks compatibility through 12 structural rules. Implemented in a software prototype and evaluated on 48 flawed workflows and 168 structural variants, the verifier reliably detects violations even when flaws are obscured by structural transformations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current agentic AI platforms emphasize runtime safeguards but offer little support for verifying workflows during system design. This gap is analogous to composing conceptual models without verifying whether building blocks interact coherently.

Method: The authors model agentic workflows as compositions of reusable building blocks and define twelve structural rules to check their compatibility. Rules are implemented in a software prototype and evaluated on 48 workflows with known design flaws and 168 structurally transformed variants.

Result: The verifier reliably detects violations even when flawed designs are obscured through structural transformations such as splitting tasks between agents.

Conclusion: The verification approach can help compose safe agentic workflows, and future work could combine it with community repositories of building blocks.

Abstract: Agentic AI systems orchestrate multiple LLM-based agents through workflow architectures that coordinate decisions, tools, and external actions. While current platforms emphasize runtime safeguards, little support exists for verifying workflows during system design. From a Modeling \& Simulation perspective, this gap is analogous to composing conceptual models without verifying whether their building blocks interact coherently. We propose a design-time verification approach that models agentic workflows as compositions of reusable building blocks and checks their compatibility through twelve structural rules. We implemented these rules in a software prototype and evaluated them using two openly released datasets: 48 workflows with known design flaws and 168 variants that preserve workflow logic but alter graph structure. Results show that our verifier reliably detects violations even when flawed designs are obscured through structural transformations such as splitting tasks between agents. Future works could combine our verification with community repositories of building blocks to compose safe agentic workflows.

</details>


### [466] [Counsel: A Meta-Evaluation Dataset for Agentic Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21627)
*Sashank Pisupati,Henry Broomfield,Eujeong Choi,Antonia Calvi,Charlie Wang,Roman Engeler,Max Bartolo,Patrick Lewis*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Counsel is the first public meta-evaluation dataset for agentic tasks, containing process-level LLMJ critiques with human annotations on error location and reasoning quality. The best open-weight judge achieves ~88% agreement on location and ~65% on reasoning, highlighting significant room for improvement in automated evaluation alignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluating multi-step agent trajectories is a major bottleneck because human annotation can take hours per trajectory, yet automated LLM-as-a-judge (LLMJ) critiques often go unmeasured for soundness. There is a need for a standardized meta-evaluation dataset to assess and improve LLMJ alignment with human judgment.

Method: The authors collected process-level critiques from open-weight LLMJs on two agent benchmarks (tau-bench for customer support and DA-Code for coding agents). Human annotators labeled each flagged error as "spot on", "correct location but poor reasoning", or "should not have flagged", achieving high inter-annotator agreement (Krippendorff's alpha = 0.78). The dataset stratifies critiques by human alignment on both error location and reasoning quality.

Result: The best open-weight judge achieved ~88% agreement with humans on error location and ~65% on reasoning quality. More capable judge models and increased reasoning effort both improved human agreement. The dataset is permissively licensed and built entirely from open-weight models.

Conclusion: Counsel enables rigorous study and calibration of LLM-based evaluators for agentic systems, providing a standardized resource to improve, calibrate, or train LLMJs. The gap between location accuracy (~88%) and reasoning accuracy (~65%) highlights a critical area for future improvement in automated agent evaluation.

Abstract: As agentic systems tackle increasingly complex multi-step tasks, evaluating their trajectories presents a major bottleneck - human annotation of a single trajectory on popular agentic benchmarks can take hours, making it difficult to scale evaluations for measuring performance or curating training data. This has driven widespread reliance on automated approaches such as LLM-as-a-judge (LLMJ) to critique agents at the process and outcome-levels at scale, however, the soundness of LLMJ critiques often goes unmeasured. Here, we introduce Counsel, the first public dataset of meta-evaluations for agentic tasks. Counsel consists of process-level critiques from open-weight LLMJs on two agent benchmarks: tau-bench (customer support agents) and DA-Code (coding agents), and human meta-evaluations of these critiques. Human annotators label critiques on each flagged error as "spot on", "correct location but poor reasoning", or "should not have flagged", achieving reliable inter-annotator agreement (Krippendorff's alpha of 0.78). The resulting dataset stratifies LLMJ critiques by human alignment across both error location within a trajectory and reasoning quality, serving as valuable data to calibrate, improve, or train LLMJs for agents. Comparing open-weight judges, we find that more capable judge models and more reasoning effort both enabled improved human agreement, with the strongest judge reaching ~88% agreement on location and ~65% on reasoning. Counsel is generated using open-weight models and is permissively licensed for broad community use, which we hope will enable rigorous study and improved alignment of LLM-based evaluators for agentic systems.

</details>


### [467] [ChainWorld: Composing Long-Horizon Desktop Workloads from Atomic OSWorld Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21654)
*Vincent Siu,Manasi Sharma,Dawn Song,Daniel Yue Zhang,Chenguang Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ChainWorld extends OSWorld atomic tasks into long-horizon desktop workloads (347 chains, length 2-4), evaluating agents in single-turn (all tasks in one prompt) vs. multi-turn (revealed sequentially). Maximum chain completion across four agents is 31%. Multi-turn improves completion for 3 models but both protocols remain challenging, with single-turn failing on artifact precision and multi-turn on session management.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing computer use agents are evaluated on atomic desktop tasks, ignoring the need to sustain state across multiple objectives as in realistic desktop work.

Method: ChainWorld composes OSWorld atomic tasks into long-horizon chains via directional compatibility search, preserving original evaluators. Two protocols: single-turn (all tasks in one prompt) and multi-turn (tasks revealed sequentially).

Result: Max 31% chain completion across four agents. Multi-turn improves completion for 3 models. Different failure profiles: single-turn fails on artifact precision; multi-turn fails on session management (fragmented progress, later turn disengagement).

Conclusion: Current agents struggle with sustained desktop workflows. Multi-turn evaluation offers partial benefits but reveals distinct session management failures, highlighting the need for better long-horizon agent capabilities.

Abstract: Computer use agents are evaluated almost exclusively on atomic desktop tasks, but realistic desktop work requires sustaining state across multiple objectives. We study this gap with ChainWorld, which composes atomic OSWorld tasks into long horizon desktop workloads through directional compatibility search while preserving the source evaluators. The resulting workload contains 347 chains of length two to four and compares two renderings of the same task sequence. In single turn evaluation, all tasks are presented together in one prompt. In multi turn evaluation, tasks are revealed one at a time. Across four current computer use agents, maximum chain completion is 31%. Multi turn evaluation improves completion for three models, but both protocols remain challenging. The two protocols also expose different failure profiles. Single turn failures concentrate on artifact precision, while multi turn failures more often reflect session management problems such as fragmented progress and later turn disengagement.

</details>


### [468] [Hallucination as Context Drift: Synchronization Protocols for Multi-Agent LLM Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21666)
*Carson Rodrigues*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Multi-agent LLM hallucinations can arise from context drift (knowledge-state divergence between agents) rather than model deficiency alone. The authors propose CDS (a metric for measuring divergence) and SSVP (a protocol for periodic state synchronization). They find that naive full-broadcast synchronization can worsen hallucinations (+34%) by propagating erroneous states, while SSVP reduces hallucinations modestly (HR: 0.463) using 58% fewer API calls, reframing hallucination mitigation as a distributed systems problem.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hallucinations in multi-agent LLM systems cannot be fully explained by individual model limitations. The authors hypothesize that a significant class of failures stems from context drift — mismatched or stale internal world state representations between concurrent agents — making this a distributed systems issue rather than a purely modeling one.

Method: The authors define Context Divergence Score (CDS), a scalar metric for quantifying knowledge-state discrepancies across spatial, temporal, and task dimensions. They propose Shared State Verification Protocol (SSVP), where agents periodically exchange compressed state summaries and flag high-divergence conditions prior to joint reasoning. Experiments are conducted with Claude Haiku in two domains (multi-agent travel and software project planning) with controlled conditions (n=30 travel, n=10 software) across 8 scenarios.

Result: Naive full-broadcast synchronization increases hallucination rate by 34% over no-sync baseline (HR: 0.658 vs. 0.492, p=0.0022, d=1.18) — a contamination effect from propagating erroneous states. SSVP shows modest reduction (HR: 0.463, d=0.30) and significantly outperforms full-broadcast (p=0.0005, d=1.47) using 58% fewer API calls. The contamination effect does not replicate in the software domain (all conditions HR <0.2), indicating task-specificity.

Conclusion: Hallucination mitigation in multi-agent systems should be reframed as a distributed systems problem. Context synchronization is established as a first-class primitive in multi-agent LLM design, with SSVP demonstrating that selective, efficient synchronization avoids contamination while reducing hallucinations and API costs.

Abstract: Multi-agent LLM systems routinely produce hallucinated outputs that cannot be explained by model deficiencies alone. A significant class of these failures arises not from model incapacity but from context drift: the divergence of internal knowledge states between concurrent agents. When agents enter a collaborative task with mismatched or stale representations of shared world state, their joint reasoning produces contradictions that manifest as hallucination. We define the Context Divergence Score (CDS), a lightweight scalar metric quantifying knowledge-state discrepancy between agent pairs across spatial, temporal, and task dimensions, and propose the Shared State Verification Protocol (SSVP), which lets agents periodically exchange compressed state summaries and flag high-divergence conditions before joint reasoning. We evaluate SSVP across two domains (multi-agent travel and software project planning) using Claude Haiku. In controlled experiments (n=30 per condition, travel; n=10, software) across 8 scenarios, naive full-broadcast synchronization increases hallucination rate by 34% above the no-sync baseline (HR: 0.658 vs. 0.492, p=0.0022, d=1.18), a contamination effect from propagating erroneous agent states. SSVP avoids this failure mode while showing modest, consistent reduction (HR: 0.463, d=0.30) and achieves significantly lower hallucination than full-broadcast (p=0.0005, d=1.47) using 58% fewer API calls. The contamination effect does not replicate in the software domain, where all conditions converge to low HR (<0.2), confirming it is specific to tasks where one erroneous shared belief cascades across evaluation dimensions. Our results reframe hallucination mitigation as a distributed systems problem and establish context synchronization as a first-class primitive in multi-agent LLM design.

</details>


### [469] [Entropy Objectives in Markov Decision Processes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21726)
*S. Akshay,Raghav Goyal,Aditya Neeraje,Piyush Srivastava*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies synthesizing control policies for stochastic systems to enforce concentration properties on state distributions via entropy-based objectives in MDPs. It proves hardness, proposes a sound and conditionally complete verification/synthesis method using convex duality and invariant synthesis, examines memory/randomization roles, and provides empirical evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To formally address the problem of enforcing concentration properties (e.g., keeping state distributions concentrated) in stochastic systems, which has practical relevance for safety, reliability, and performance guarantees.

Method: Formalizes the problem as synthesizing strategies for entropy-based objectives in MDPs; proves complexity hardness; develops a verification and synthesis method leveraging convex duality and invariant synthesis; analyzes the necessity of memory and randomization.

Result: The problem is complexity-theoretically hard even in relaxed forms. The proposed method is sound and conditionally relatively complete. Memory and randomization play critical roles in achieving entropy objectives.

Conclusion: Entropy-based concentration objectives for MDPs are tractable via the proposed duality-invariant synthesis framework, despite inherent hardness, and the approach is validated empirically.

Abstract: We consider the problem of synthesizing control policies that enforce a concentration property on the state distributions of a stochastic system. We present a formalization of this problem in terms of synthesizing strategies for maintaining an entropy-based objective in Markov Decision Processes (MDPs). We first show that even relaxed versions of this problem are complexity-theoretically hard. We then present a sound and (conditionally) relatively complete method to verify and synthesize strategies for such entropy objectives. The main challenge is the non-linear nature of such objectives, and our approach addresses this by exploiting and combining ideas from convex duality and invariant synthesis. We also investigate the role of memory and randomization in ensuring entropy objectives. Finally, we implement our ideas to evaluate our approach empirically on a few illustrative benchmarks.

</details>


### [470] [Training the Orchestrator: A Supervised Approach to End-to-End PDDL Planning with LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21740)
*Rajesh Mangannavar,Zachary Coalson,Pranay Dugar,Prasad Tadepalli*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HALO trains a small QLoRA-tuned policy with three hardcoded rules to replace a frontier LLM orchestrator in a multi-agent refinement loop for translating natural language planning intent into verified PDDL plans, achieving comparable success rates at dramatically lower cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Translating natural-language planning intent into verified plans is a longstanding challenge. Existing approaches use frontier LLM orchestrators in verifier-checked refinement loops, which are expensive due to per-step API calls. The key insight is that the verifier's certified trajectories provide strong supervision signals that can be exploited to learn a cheaper orchestrator.

Method: HALO pairs a small QLoRA-tuned policy with three hardcoded rules for trivially decidable selections, operating over a 21-agent action space. It is trained on refinement trajectories that an external verifier has certified as ending in valid plans, across 11 PDDL domains, using the verifier's accepted trajectories as direct supervision.

Result: Across PlanBench, Natural Plan, and classical planning benchmarks, HALO matches/exceeds GPT-5-mini on success rate, sits within 3% of Gemini-3-Flash, reduces orchestration cost from $0.18 to $0.004 per task (~45× cheaper than GPT-5-mini, ~15× cheaper than Gemini-3-Flash), and cuts total LLM calls per episode by 40-50%.

Conclusion: HALO demonstrates that a small learned policy can effectively replace expensive frontier LLM orchestrators in verified planning pipelines by leveraging verifier-provided supervision, achieving comparable performance at drastically reduced cost and latency.

Abstract: Translating natural-language planning intent into verified plans is a longstanding challenge: people communicate goals in language, while classical planners require formal PDDL specifications. Recent agentic frameworks bridge this gap by orchestrating a pool of specialized repair agents inside a verifier-checked refinement loop, but the orchestrator at the centre is itself a prompted frontier LLM, paying a frontier-LLM API call at every refinement step. We present HALO (Hybrid Agent-Learned Orchestrator), which trains the orchestrator from refinement trajectories that an external verifier has certified as ending in valid plans, across 11 PDDL domains. HALO pairs a small QLoRA-tuned policy with three hardcoded rules for trivially decidable selections, and operates over an expanded 21-agent action space. Unlike approaches that prompt a frontier LLM at every step or learn an orchestrator from sparse end-of-episode rewards, our key observation is that the verifier already provides strong guidance: every accepted trajectory is a sequence of demonstrably correct (state, agent) decisions, directly usable as supervision. Across PlanBench, Natural Plan, and classical planning benchmarks, HALO matches or exceeds the GPT-5-mini prompted baseline on success rate, sits within three percentage points of the stronger Gemini-3-Flash prompted baseline, reduces orchestration cost by more than an order of magnitude (\$0.18 to \$0.004 per task against GPT-5-mini, roughly 45$\times$ cheaper; roughly 15$\times$ cheaper than Gemini-3-Flash), and cuts total LLM calls per episode by 40 to 50 percent.

</details>


### [471] [AgentCAT: Simulating Computerized Adaptive Testing via Multi-Agent Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21832)
*Weiyuan Zhou,Haiping Ma,Xiaoshan Yu,Changqian Wang,Shangshang Yang,Xingyi Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AgentCAT is an LLM-based multi-agent simulation system for Computerized Adaptive Testing that overcomes the limitations of static offline data and isolated component optimization by simulating the complete dynamic testing process with three modules: examinee agent, selection agent, and supervisor agent.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing CAT research is constrained by static offline data and isolated component optimization, degrading dynamic assessment into static sequence prediction and focusing on isolated perspectives (e.g., selection or diagnosis) while neglecting the overall CAT interaction process.

Method: AgentCAT consists of three modules: (1) an examinee agent with memory retrieval and Chain-of-Thought reasoning for response simulation; (2) a selection agent using coarse-to-fine bucketing and knowledge graph exploration for balancing local difficulty and global coverage; (3) a supervisor agent using dual-auditing and robust update for convergence and validity.

Result: Evaluated on two real-world datasets across three dimensions (macro-level ability convergence, micro-level interaction logic, data sparsity resilience), AgentCAT achieves effective ability estimation with a selection strategy that balances difficulty adaptation and instructional coherence, aligning with human pedagogical intuition.

Conclusion: AgentCAT provides a high-fidelity benchmarking environment for dynamic testing, overcoming the limitations of static offline data and isolated optimization in traditional CAT approaches.

Abstract: Computerized Adaptive Testing (CAT), as a key technology for personalized education, aims to accurately assess examinee proficiency by retrieving exercises dynamically matching current ability estimates. However, existing CAT research is constrained by limitations of static offline data and isolated component optimization. Restricted by partial labels in offline logs, researchers degrade the dynamic assessment process into static sequence prediction. Current research focuses on isolated perspectives, e.g., selection or diagnosis, neglecting the overall CAT interaction process. To address this, we propose AgentCAT, a Large Language Model-based multi-agent simulation system, to construct a high-fidelity benchmarking environment for dynamic testing. This framework comprises three modules: (1) The examinee agent with memory retrieval and Chain-of-Thought reasoning simulates responses based on cognitive profiles; (2) The selection agent uses coarse-to-fine bucketing and knowledge graph exploration to balance local difficulty and global coverage; (3) The supervisor uses dual-auditing and robust update to ensure convergence and validity. To validate the framework, we evaluated on two real-world datasets across three dimensions: macro-level ability convergence, micro-level interaction logic, and data sparsity resilience. Results show AgentCAT achieves effective ability estimation, and its selection strategy balances difficulty adaptation and instructional coherence, aligning with human pedagogical intuition.

</details>


### [472] [Measuring What Persists: Conditioning Mechanisms and a Geometric Framework for AI Agent Identity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21843)
*Andrew Tanner*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A geometric framework using √JSD metric spaces and magnitude homology detects AI agent identity drift as relaxation from non-geodesic structure toward geodesic; validated on persistent agents, revealing identity-vacuum and safety-basin clusters, but drift experiments showed padding artifacts rather than genuine drift, and the full diagnostic promise remains unconfirmed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current methods detect identity drift in long-context AI agents only after qualitative degradation is visible; earlier detection requires a formal geometric measure of identity structure.

Method: Construction of √JSD metric spaces and magnitude homology from enriched category theory; equilateral probe baselines with perturbation theory; drift experiments with diverse vs. repetitive padding.

Result: Two-mechanism conditioning (identity-vacuum and safety-basin clusters); 55 vs. 1 unique response patterns at max probe separation; drift findings were padding artifacts, not genuine context-length drift; no measurable deformation through 150K tokens with diverse padding.

Conclusion: The framework's diagnostic promise (anisotropic contraction, structural collapse via homological simplification) is architecturally grounded but empirically unconfirmed; genuine drift detection requires further study.

Abstract: AI agents in long-context applications drift from their specified identity. Current methods detect this only after qualitative degradation is visible. We present a geometric framework for measuring identity structure using $\sqrt{\mathrm{JSD}}$ metric spaces and magnitude homology from enriched category theory, where identity is non-geodesic structure and drift is its relaxation toward the geodesic.
  Validated on a persistent AI agent, the framework's strongest empirical finding is a two-mechanism conditioning structure: cross-condition distances reveal an identity-vacuum cluster where the identity specification fills a behavioral void, and a safety-basin cluster where it displaces from post-training attractors. An equilateral probe baseline confirms that the identity specification creates measurable behavioral richness (55 unique response patterns vs. 1 for the base model) at maximum probe separation. A first-order perturbation theory for equilateral configurations predicts magnitude changes from perimeter changes alone, with shape perturbations first-order cancelled by the $S_n$ symmetry; the formula is self-consistent at the observed perturbation amplitudes.
  A drift experiment measuring magnitude decrease under context pressure was subsequently found to reflect repetitive-padding artifacts rather than genuine context-length drift; diverse padding produces no measurable deformation through 150K tokens. The magnitude homology framework's full diagnostic promise -- detecting anisotropic contraction and structural collapse via homological simplification -- is architecturally grounded in the perturbation theory and selection rules but remains empirically unconfirmed.

</details>


### [473] [ForEx: A Formal Verification Framework for Explainable Reasoning in Logical Fallacy Detection and Annotation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21867)
*Pei-Cing Huang,Chienyu Liu,Chan Hsu,Ci-Siang Chen,Pei-Ju Lee,Yihuang Kang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ForEx translates LLM-generated explanations for logical fallacy detection into Lean4 formal proofs and verifies their derivability, revealing a systematic gap between formal reasoning validity (90%+ verifiable) and label agreement with human annotations (~20%).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current evaluations of LLMs on logical fallacy detection only assess predicted labels without checking whether the models' reasoning actually supports those labels.

Method: ForEx translates LLM-generated explanations into Lean4 and formally verifies whether the translated rationale is derivable under encoded premises. Introduces the LLM Argument Verification Matrix to separate label consistency from formal verification status.

Result: Over 90% of LLM outputs can be translated into formal reasoning chains that pass verification, but agreement with human annotations remains around 20%.

Conclusion: There is a systematic gap between formal derivability and label agreement that prediction-based metrics cannot capture; ForEx moves evaluation beyond label correctness toward machine-checkable analysis of formalized reasoning chains.

Abstract: Current evaluations of Large Language Models (LLMs) on logical fallacy detection focus on predicted labels, but do not establish whether those labels are supported by the reasoning the models provide. We propose ForEx (Formal Verification for Explainable Reasoning), a framework that translates LLM-generated explanations into Lean4 and verifies whether the translated rationale is derivable under encoded premises, not the logical validity of the original natural language argument. To distinguish prediction outcomes from the formal status of the supporting reasoning, we introduce the LLM Argument Verification Matrix, which separates label consistency from formal verification status. Experiments on LOGIC-Climate show that over 90% of LLM outputs can be translated into formal reasoning chains that pass verification, while agreement with human annotations remains around 20%. These results expose a systematic gap between formal derivability and label agreement, a distinction invisible to prediction-based metrics. ForEx moves LLM evaluation beyond label correctness toward machine-checkable analysis of formalized reasoning chains.

</details>


### [474] [Learning the ARTS of Search for Automated Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21891)
*Gurusha Juneja,Arnav Kumar Jain,Deepak Nathani,William Yang Wang,Xin Eric Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ARTS enhances scientific discovery by using a reasoning language model to navigate hypothesis-experiment spaces, decoupling hypothesis merit from execution quality, and employing test-time training to overcome context length limitations, achieving over 15.3% relative improvement across 22 tasks and enabling a 4B model to match frontier models at 5x lower cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing tree search methods like MCTS conflate hypothesis merit with experimental execution quality, and accumulate search logs that exceed context windows, leading to premature pruning of promising yet preliminary hypotheses.

Method: ARTS employs a reasoning language model to inspect prior execution logs, diagnose whether failures stem from faulty implementations or bad hypotheses, and select which hypothesis to build on next. It uses test-time training to encode the search tree's knowledge into model weights, mitigating context window limitations.

Result: ARTS outperforms leading algorithms across 22 MLGym and MLEBench tasks with over 15.3% relative improvement. A Qwen3-4B agent with test-time training matches Gemini-3 Pro and GPT o3-reasoning at up to 5x lower inference cost. On partially observable RL tasks, the trained 4B model surpasses ARTS with o3 by rediscovering the human-best recurrent-memory solution that heuristic methods prune.

Conclusion: Decoupling hypothesis quality from execution quality and using test-time training for search tree knowledge retention enables smaller models to outperform larger ones and discover solutions missed by heuristic pruning, suggesting a scalable path for automated scientific discovery.

Abstract: Scientific discovery can be formulated as an iterative search process over the space of hypotheses and experiments. Contemporary methods navigate this space using heuristics such as MCTS. These algorithms conflate the merit of a hypothesis with the quality of its experimental execution. A promising hypothesis with preliminary execution is therefore ranked below a modest hypothesis whose execution is refined. Moreover, prior methods prune the search logs as the search progresses because the accumulated history outgrows the context window. We propose Agentic Reasoning for Tree Search (ARTS), where we deploy a reasoning language model to navigate this space. The model inspects prior execution logs, diagnoses whether earlier failures arose from faulty implementations or bad hypotheses, and selects the hypothesis to build on next. To mitigate challenges with context length, ARTS uses test-time training to instill the knowledge of search tree in the model weights. Across 22 tasks from MLGym and MLEBench, we show that ARTS outperforms leading algorithms, with over 15.3% relative improvement in the normalized score. With test-time training we show that a Qwen3-4B agent can match performance with closed-source frontier models like Gemini-3 Pro and GPT o3-reasoning with upto 5x lower inference cost. We further observe that on partially observable RL tasks, the test-time trained Qwen3-4B scientist surpasses ARTS with the o3 scientist by rediscovering the human-best recurrent-memory solution that heuristic methods prune away.

</details>


### [475] [REBA: A Revealed Belief Automaton Framework for Online Planning in Continuous POMDPs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21971)
*Xiangwei Chen,Lingling Fang,Andreas Holzinger,Liming Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: REBA (Revealed Belief Automaton) is an event-driven framework for online planning in continuous POMDPs with ω-regular specifications. It dynamically constructs a Belief Automaton from continuous beliefs by certifying "revelation events" via information-theoretic gates, enabling formal parity policy synthesis without predefined abstractions, and guiding Monte Carlo Tree Search. It achieves +17.0% to +47.4% improvement over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for online continuous POMDP planning with ω-regular specifications either lack symbolic memory for long-horizon logical progress (direct belief-space search) or struggle to certify from noisy online beliefs (predefined discrete abstractions). Obtaining reliable symbolic states online from continuous observations remains a challenge.

Method: The paper introduces (1) an online revelation method using information-theoretic gates to dynamically discover reliable anchors among noisy continuous beliefs, (2) an incremental topology adaptation mechanism that builds a finite Belief Automaton over certified anchors, (3) integration with ω-regular specifications for formal parity policy synthesis without predefined discrete abstraction, which guides Monte Carlo Tree Search beyond local horizon, and (4) an error decomposition analysis to assess the discrete guidance's reliability.

Result: Empirical evaluations in patrolling and navigation scenarios show REBA matches or exceeds all baselines, with primary metric gains of +17.0% to +47.4% over state-of-the-art approaches.

Conclusion: REBA advances from global belief-space discretization to online certification of revelation events, providing a principled way to obtain reliable symbolic states from continuous observations for formal policy synthesis in continuous POMDPs.

Abstract: Online planning in continuous partially observable Markov decision processes (POMDPs) using $ω$-regular specifications requires handling continuous belief dynamics within the finite symbolic memory in order to track temporal progress. Existing methods based on either direct search in belief space or predefined discrete abstractions suffer from drawbacks, e.g., lack of symbolic memory for long-horizon logical progress or difficult to certify from noisy online beliefs. As such, obtaining reliable symbolic states online from continuous observations remains a challenge. To address this issue, we introduce the Revealed Belief Automaton (REBA), an event-driven framework that advances the research from global belief-space discretization to a fundamental new way of thinking, namely online certification of revelation events. Specifically, we propose an online revelation method that, through information-theoretic gates, can dynamically analyse and establish belief abstraction from the continuous belief space by discovering reliable anchors among noisy beliefs. We then develop an incremental topology adaptation mechanism over the certified anchors to realise the online finite Belief Automaton. By combining with the $ω$-regular specification, REBA is able to support formal parity policy synthesis without a predefined discrete abstraction, which in turn can guide the Monte Carlo Tree Search process to perform online search beyond its local horizon. In addition, we design an error decomposition analysis which can assess the effectiveness and reliability of this discrete guidance for the underlying continuous POMDP. Empirical evaluations in patrolling and navigation scenarios show that REBA matches or exceeds all evaluated baselines, with primary metric gains of +17.0\% to +47.4\% over state-of-the-art approaches.

</details>


### [476] [Human vs Machine Mathematical Difficulty on Project Euler: An Experimental Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21972)
*David Holmes,Johannes Schmitt*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper tests how AI performance scales with human difficulty on Project Euler problems, finding that machine cost scales sublinearly with human time (b < 1 for most models), success probability follows exponential decay with moderate support, and the 50% task-length horizon for SOTA models doubled approximately every 75 days, reaching 2.5–4.3 hours by April 2026.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether frontier AI systems scale worse than humans with increasing problem difficulty, as previously predicted, using a controlled benchmark (Project Euler via MathArena) with human solve times as a difficulty metric.

Method: Collected 3840 attempts across 50 problems and 26 model configurations; measured human difficulty via public solve times; fit power-law (t_machine = a * t_human^b) and exponential decay (p_success = e^{c * t_human}) models; used binning for data aggregation; fit logistic success curves to extract 50% task-length horizons (h_50).

Result: Found b < 1 for 20 of 25 models, contradicting the prediction of worse scaling; exponential decay model showed moderate support (median R² = 0.92); strongest models had h_50 of 2.5–4.3 hours; SOTA h_50 doubling time ~75 days.

Conclusion: The operationalization does not support the earlier prediction that machines scale worse than humans with difficulty; instead, AI systems show sublinear cost scaling. The observed exponential growth in capability horizon suggests rapid progress in frontier AI problem-solving ability.

Abstract: We study how the effort and success probability of frontier AI systems scale with human difficulty on problems from Project Euler, an online platform of computational mathematics problems. Our dataset, from the MathArena benchmark, consists of 3840 attempts across 50 problems and 26 model configurations, with problem difficulty measured by the site's public human solve times. Motivated by a proposal of Timothy Gowers, we test a power-law relation $t_{\text{machine}} = a \cdot t_{\text{human}}^b$ between generated-token cost per successful answer and human time, and find $b < 1$ for 20 of the 25 models with usable fits, including the strongest base models; this operationalization therefore does not support an earlier prediction that machines scale worse than humans with difficulty. We also investigate whether success probability on the tested problems can be modeled by a simple exponential decay $p_{\text{success}} = e^{c t_{\text{human}}}$, predicting a linear relation between $\log p_{\text{success}}$ and $t_{\text{human}}$. Using a binning approach for data aggregation we find moderate empirical support (median bin-level $R^2 = 0.92$ across the 22 best-covered configurations) for this model. Following METR, we also fit logistic success curves and extract 50\% task-length horizons $h_{50}$; the strongest configurations in our 20 April 2026 snapshot reach roughly $2.5$--$4.3$ hours on our fastest-five human baseline, with a log-linear fit through the state-of-the-art frontier giving a descriptive doubling time of about $75$~days for the SOTA $h_{50}$.

</details>


### [477] [IRumAI: Reinforcement Learning for Indian Rummy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21975)
*Vignesh Mohan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: IRumAI is the first RL agent for Indian Rummy, using PPO with meld-aware encoding, deadwood-driven rewards, and a dual-branch CNN. Trained only against weak heuristics with a behavior-cloning warm-start, it achieves a 53.9% win rate against the strongest search-based opponent while being 7,000x faster per action (0.33 ms). Ablations confirm design choices, and probing shows the network implicitly models opponent hidden hands.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Indian Rummy, despite its massive popularity and complex hidden-information dynamics, has received no reinforcement learning attention; existing agents rely on slow combinatorial search.

Method: Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) with meld-aware observation encoding, deadwood-driven reward shaping, and a dual-branch convolutional architecture; behavior-cloning warm-start on stronger demonstration data, then RL training solely against weak heuristics.

Result: 53.9% win rate against the strongest search-based opponent, 0.33 ms per action (over 7,000x faster than the state-of-the-art heuristic).

Conclusion: IRumAI demonstrates that RL can achieve strong, fast inference in Indian Rummy without explicit search, and that the network learns to implicitly model opponent hidden hands from public interactions.

Abstract: Despite its massive player base and complex hidden-information dynamics, Indian Rummy has received no reinforcement learning attention. Existing agents rely on combinatorial search, which is tactically strong but slow at inference. We present IRumAI, the first RL agent for the domain. IRumAI integrates Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), meld-aware observation encoding, deadwood-driven reward shaping, and a dual-branch convolutional architecture. IRumAI is RL-trained solely against weak heuristics, after a one-time behaviour-cloning warm-start on stronger demonstration data. It generalises to defeat the entire baseline hierarchy, including a 53.9% win rate against the strongest search-based opponent unseen during RL training. Bypassing explicit search, IRumAI requires just 0.33 ms per action, which is over 7,000x faster than the state-of-the-art heuristic. Ablations validate our architectural choices, and linear probing reveals that the network implicitly models the opponent's hidden hand from public interactions.

</details>


### [478] [Nous: A Predictive World Model for Long-Term Agent Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22030)
*Pranav Singh*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Nous is a novel agent memory architecture that treats knowledge as prediction, storing Bayesian belief updates (deltas) rather than facts. It uses predictive world models with categorical distributions, natural entropy-based forgetting, and achieves strong results on the LoCoMo benchmark (F1: 63.50 single-hop, 55.32 multi-hop, 58.57 temporal, 62.50 open-domain) without external vector databases or graph engines, though reproducibility issues with baselines are acknowledged.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing agent memory systems treat knowledge as static storage (database records, embeddings, or graph triples), which is fundamentally misaligned with how intelligent agents should handle information—as probabilistic predictions subject to revision, forgetting, and uncertainty. The authors argue that knowledge is prediction, not storage.

Method: Nous maintains a predictive world model using categorical probability distributions (dimensions) over entity-attribute pairs. Incoming observations are scored by information-theoretic surprise S=-log₂P(obs|D). Distributions update via closed-form Bayesian posterior. The stored artifact is a "delta" (shift from prior to posterior belief), not the fact itself. Forgetting occurs via entropy decay toward uniform distribution; identity resolution uses mutual information between entity dimension sets. No external vector DB or graph engine is needed.

Result: On LoCoMo benchmark (10 conversations, 1,540 questions) with GPT-4o-mini backbone: F1 scores of 63.50 (single-hop), 55.32 (multi-hop), 58.57 (temporal), 62.50 (open-domain). Shows substantial gains over A-MEM in 3 of 4 categories (though reproducibility issues noted with A-MEM's reported results). Exceeds self-reported BeliefMem numbers on all 4 categories (though uncontrolled evaluation differences flagged).

Conclusion: Belief-based (predictive) memory architectures offer a principled alternative to deterministic storage, with natural forgetting, uncertainty-awareness, and competitive performance. The authors stress the importance of transparent reproducibility reporting, openly discussing discrepancies in baseline citations rather than resolving them unilaterally.

Abstract: We present Nous, a novel agent memory architecture grounded in the principle that knowledge is prediction, not storage. Rather than persisting facts as database records, vector embeddings, or knowledge-graph triples, Nous maintains a predictive world model: a collection of categorical probability distributions, called dimensions, one per entity-attribute pair observed in conversation. Each incoming observation is scored by its information-theoretic surprise S = -log2 P(obs | D), and the distribution is updated via a closed-form Bayesian posterior. The primary stored artifact is the delta, a record of the shift from prior to posterior belief, rather than the fact itself. Forgetting emerges naturally as entropy decay toward the uniform distribution, and identity resolution is handled through mutual information between entity dimension sets. Evaluated on the LoCoMo long-term conversational memory benchmark across ten conversations (1,540 questions) using GPT-4o-mini as backbone, Nous achieves F1 of 63.50 (single-hop), 55.32 (multi-hop), 58.57 (temporal), and 62.50 (open-domain). Against A-MEM's self-reported GPT-4o-mini numbers, Nous shows substantial gains in three of four categories, though we note that independent citations of A-MEM's results disagree with each other on category assignment, a reproducibility issue we discuss openly rather than resolve unilaterally. We additionally compare against BeliefMem, a concurrently developed system built on the same core premise of belief-based rather than deterministic memory; on the same benchmark and backbone, Nous's self-reported numbers exceed BeliefMem's self-reported numbers on all four categories, though we flag several uncontrolled differences between the two evaluation pipelines that prevent this from being a fully controlled comparison. Nous requires no external vector database or graph engine.

</details>


### [479] [Attractor Domain Theory: A Mathematical Framework for Cardiovascular Attractor Analysis with Wearable Photoplethysmography (PPG) Validation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22039)
*Timothy Oladunni,Farouk Ganiyu Adewumi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces Attractor Domain Theory (ADT), which proves that reconstructed cardiac attractor information partitions into three non-redundant domains (Geometry, Ergodic, Variational). A Domain Sufficiency Theorem establishes these three domains as necessary and sufficient. Validation on 176,742 PPG segments shows Geometry Domain achieves AUC=0.757 and NPV=0.966 for artifact rejection, with C_NL as the dominant component.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Three decades of nonlinear cardiac dynamics have extracted various attractor properties (Lyapunov exponents, recurrence statistics, sample entropy) but no principled account exists of which attractor properties capture which cardiovascular quantities or why, leaving feature selection as a search problem and negative results uninterpretable.

Method: Attractor Domain Theory (ADT) is introduced to partition reconstructed attractor information into three mutually non-redundant domains: Geometry Domain G (delay embedding), Ergodic Domain S (asymptotic statistical invariants), and Variational Domain V (finite-time Lyapunov exponent field). A Domain Sufficiency Theorem is proved. Geometry Domain validated via SCSI framework across 176,742 PPG segments from four datasets.

Result: Geometry Domain validation yields AUC=0.757 [0.686-0.828] and NPV=0.966 after correcting three systematic evaluation artifacts (+0.179 net inflation). Ablation confirms C_NL as the dominant Geometry Domain component (Delta AUC=-0.413) and intra-domain redundancy across five components.

Conclusion: Three domains (Geometry, Ergodic, Variational) are necessary and sufficient for capturing cardiac attractor information, providing a principled theoretical foundation for nonlinear cardiac dynamics feature selection.

Abstract: The cardiovascular system evolves along a bounded trajectory in physiological state space that converges to a compact geometric object: the cardiac attractor. A wearable photoplethysmograph (PPG) or electrocardiograph (ECG) observes a one-dimensional projection of this attractor; by Takens' embedding theorem, delay coordinates reconstruct its full geometry. Three decades of nonlinear cardiac dynamics have extracted Lyapunov exponents, recurrence statistics, and sample entropy from reconstructed attractors, yet no principled account exists of which attractor properties capture which cardiovascular quantities, or why, leaving feature selection as a search problem and negative results uninterpretable. We introduce Attractor Domain Theory (ADT), which proves that the reconstructed attractor's information partitions into three mutually non-redundant domains: the Geometry Domain G (delay embedding; native capability: artifact rejection), the Ergodic Domain S (asymptotic statistical invariants; native capability: stability estimation), and the Variational Domain V (finite-time Lyapunov exponent field; native capability: hemodynamic inference). We prove a Domain Sufficiency Theorem (the Parseval analog for attractor information) and establish that three domains are necessary and sufficient. Geometry Domain validation via the SCSI framework across 176,742 PPG segments from four datasets yields AUC = 0.757 [0.686-0.828] and NPV = 0.966 after correcting three systematic evaluation artifacts (+0.179 net inflation). Ablation confirms C_NL as the dominant Geometry Domain component (Delta AUC = -0.413) and intra-domain redundancy across five components.

</details>


### [480] [When Does a Video-Language Model Stop Watching? Reward Strength Controls the Formation and Reversal of Visual Shortcuts in Multimodal RLVR](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22043)
*Zekun Xu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper characterizes the formation, reversal, and intervention dynamics of visual shortcuts in RLVR-trained LVLMs, showing that shortcut reliance emerges abruptly, follows a hysteresis-like asymmetry between acquisition and removal, and has a critical window for effective intervention.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While perception bypass in RLVR for LVLMs is documented, how it forms, whether it can be undone, and when intervention still helps remain open questions.

Method: The authors treat the strength of a grounding penalty (λ) as a control knob and characterize formation-reversal dynamics of visual shortcuts along the training time axis using a held-out, out-of-distribution diagnostic set.

Result: (i) Sharp onset — shortcut reliance emerges abruptly over a narrow optimization window and is robust across random seeds; (ii) Monotone dose-response — increasing λ progressively suppresses shortcuts, with hysteresis-like asymmetry between acquiring and removing them; (iii) Critical intervention window — penalty before onset arrests formation, same penalty after consolidation is markedly less effective.

Conclusion: Visual-shortcut collapse is not a binary defect but a controllable, time-dependent, and asymmetric process, with direct implications for when and how strongly to regularize multimodal RLVR.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) is increasingly applied to large vision-language models (LVLMs), yet outcome-only optimization can drive a model to stop attending to the video and instead exploit linguistic priors -- a failure we call a visual shortcut. While the existence of such perception bypass is by now documented, how it forms, whether it can be undone, and when intervention still helps remain open. We treat the strength of a grounding penalty, lambda, as a control knob and characterize the formation-reversal dynamics of visual shortcuts along the training time axis. On a held-out, out-of-distribution diagnostic set, we find: (i) a sharp onset -- shortcut reliance emerges abruptly over a narrow window of optimization steps and is robust across random seeds; (ii) a monotone dose-response -- increasing lambda progressively suppresses the shortcut, and at an intermediate dose the trajectory first forms and then reverses the shortcut, exposing a hysteresis-like asymmetry between acquiring and removing it; and (iii) a critical intervention window -- applying the penalty before onset arrests shortcut formation, whereas the same penalty applied after consolidation is markedly less effective. Together these results recast visual-shortcut collapse not as a binary defect but as a controllable, time-dependent, and asymmetric process, with direct implications for when and how strongly to regularize multimodal RLVR.

</details>


### [481] [Can Reasoning Models Detect Changes to their Chains of Thought?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22085)
*Sathvik Napa,Utkarsh Singh,Chengyuan Xue,Miriam Wanner,William Walden*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Recent reasoning models show only modest ability to detect interventions (prefilling, modifications) on their chain-of-thought, struggle to identify how their CoT was changed, and are equally poor at detecting modifications to their own vs. other models' CoTs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There are practical reasons to edit a model's chain of thought (e.g., prefilling with stronger reasoning or removing unsafe steps), but the success of such interventions depends on whether the model can detect tampering and alter its behavior accordingly.

Method: The authors systematically studied whether recent reasoning models can detect CoT interventions under various conditions: during vs. after reasoning, and when prefilled with their own CoTs vs. those of other models.

Result: (i) Models exhibit only very modest detection accuracy; (ii) models struggle to identify *how* their CoT was modified; (iii) models are about as good at detecting changes to their own CoTs as to those of other models.

Conclusion: Current reasoning models have limited ability to detect CoT tampering, suggesting that interventions like prefilling or removing steps may go unnoticed by the model, which has implications for safety and model steering techniques.

Abstract: There are many reasons one may want to edit a model's chain of thought (CoT) -- e.g., to prefill it with reasoning from a stronger model or to remove steps that may yield unsafe outputs. The success of these interventions plausibly depends on a model's inability to notice them, as the model may alter its behavior if it suspects tampering. In this work, we study whether recent reasoning models are able to detect such interventions on their CoTs under a variety of conditions: both during reasoning and after it, and when prefilled both with their own CoTs and with those of other models. Broadly, we find that (i) models exhibit only very modest detection accuracy; (ii) models struggle to identify *how* their CoT was modified; and (iii) models are about as good at detecting changes to their own CoTs as to those of other models.

</details>


### [482] [Geometry-Aware Online Scheduling for LLM Serving: From Theoretical Bound to System Practice](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22327)
*Li Kong,Qi Qi,Yinyu Ye,Zijie Zhou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces geometry-aware online scheduling for LLM inference, proposing the Smallest Volume First (SVF) algorithm and its efficient 1-bit variant, which significantly improve Key-Value cache memory management. The authors tighten the competitive ratio from 48 to 5 and demonstrate strong latency and throughput improvements on vLLM with Llama-3.1 models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing inference engines rely on time-centric scheduling heuristics (e.g., Shortest Job First) that fail to capture the 2D spatio-temporal geometric growth patterns specific to LLM inference, leading to suboptimal Key-Value cache memory management under dynamic workloads.

Method: Proposes the Smallest Volume First (SVF) algorithm and its 1-bit variant, a geometry-aware scheduling approach that accounts for the spatio-temporal dynamics of KV cache growth. Provides a novel proof methodology to tighten the worst-case competitive ratio (CR ≤ 48 → CR ≤ 5). Integrates as a plug-and-play layer in vLLM.

Result: SVF achieves strong reductions in both average and tail latency; 1-bit SVF, using only a single bit of information, delivers competitive throughput and latency. Comprehensive theoretical taxonomy across traffic scenarios and information availability is provided.

Conclusion: Geometry-aware scheduling is both theoretically sound and empirically effective for memory-constrained LLM inference, establishing a new paradigm for KV cache management in modern LLM deployments.

Abstract: The explosive demand for interactive Large Language Model serving has highlighted the management of the Key-Value cache's dynamic memory footprint as a critical area for performance optimization in inference engines. Modern inference systems overwhelmingly rely on time-centric scheduling heuristics, such as Shortest Job First. However, their theoretical optimality is rooted in traditional schedule modeling, failing to capture the highly dynamic, 2D spatio-temporal geometric growth specific to LLM inference mechanisms. To resolve this, we propose the geometry-aware online scheduling by introducing the Smallest Volume First (SVF) algorithm and its highly efficient variant, 1-bit SVF. Theoretically, we provide a rigorous mathematical foundation for our approach. Utilizing a novel proof methodology, we tighten the worst-case competitive ratio ($\text{CR} \le 48 \rightarrow \text{CR} \le 5$) for SVF with known output lengths. Building upon this core breakthrough, we complete a comprehensive theoretical taxonomy analyzing our algorithms across different traffic scenarios and information availability. Practically, we seamlessly integrate our approach as a plug-and-play layer in vLLM. Extensive evaluations on Llama-3.1 models demonstrate comprehensive performance gains: SVF delivers strong reductions in both average and tail latency, while 1-bit SVF, with merely a single bit information, achieves competitive throughput and latency. This work establishes a theoretically sound and empirically proven approach for resolving memory-constrained scheduling in modern LLM deployments. To facilitate future research, our code is available at https://github.com/Aurora-Kl/Geometry-Aware-Online-Scheduling.git.

</details>


### [483] [Reference-Free Assessment of Physical Consistency in World Model-based Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22363)
*Yun Oh,Sukmin Yun*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces reference-free metrics for evaluating physical consistency in generated videos, using DROID-SLAM and SEA-RAFT to quantify inconsistencies. Their relative consistency filtering improves VLA task success rates by over 8%, and their absolute approach enables spatio-temporal localization of physical artifacts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing video evaluation methods (Elo, FVD) require costly human voting or unavailable ground-truth references. Physical fidelity gaps in video generation environments prevent accurate reproduction of real-world VLA task success rates, creating a simulation-to-reality gap that needs addressing.

Method: The authors propose reference-free physical consistency measures combining relative and absolute approaches. They use DROID-SLAM and SEA-RAFT to quantify physical inconsistencies in generated videos, motivated by WorldScore.

Result: Videos filtered using the relative consistency assessment improve task success rates by over 8%, effectively narrowing the simulation-to-reality gap.

Conclusion: The absolute assessment enables spatio-temporal localization of artifacts, providing visualization of when and where physical inconsistencies occur in generated videos.

Abstract: We introduce reference-free measures for evaluating the physical consistency of generated videos, combining relative and absolute approaches to assess fidelity. Although tools like WorldGym or WorldEval enable robotic simulation via video generation, physical fidelity gaps often prevent these environments from accurately reproducing real-world task success rates of VLA models. Unlike existing evaluation methods, which require costly human voting (Elo) or unavailable ground-truth references (FVD), our approach utilizes DROID-SLAM and SEA-RAFT to quantify physical inconsistencies, motivated by WorldScore. Videos filtered using our relative consistency assessment show an improvement in task success rates of over 8%, effectively narrowing the simulation-to-reality gap. Furthermore, our absolute assessment enables spatio-temporal localization, providing visualization of when and where physical artifacts occur.

</details>


### [484] [ARIA: A Causal-Aware Framework for Rescuing LLM Reasoning in Trustworthy Materials Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22375)
*Yi Cao,Liaoyaqi Wang,Jieneng Chen,Benjamin Van Durme,Alan Yuille,Paulette Clancy*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ARIA is a causal-aware framework that mitigates contextual tunneling in LLMs by conditioning knowledge use on mechanistic completeness, using a three-tier cascade (direct causal reasoning, physics-informed analogical transfer, parametric fallback) to improve materials discovery with auditable causal traces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generative models for materials discovery often fail to satisfy underlying physical causality, and LLMs augmented with knowledge graphs exhibit "contextual tunneling"—over-anchoring on narrow, retrieved evidence while suppressing global physical reasoning.

Method: ARIA routes each query through a three-tier cascade: (i) direct causal reasoning using complete Process-Structure-Property (PSP) evidence chains, (ii) physics-informed analogical transfer for sparse or novel systems, and (iii) explicit parametric fallback when external evidence is incomplete. A KG of 2,839 PSP relations from peer-reviewed articles was constructed for evaluation.

Result: ARIA mitigates contextual tunneling, improves over unaugmented and naive KG-augmented baselines on forward prediction and inverse design for 2D materials, with further gains from online literature search enrichment.

Conclusion: ARIA produces auditable causal traces, enabling physically grounded and trustworthy AI-assisted materials discovery.

Abstract: Generative models have revolutionized the process of materials discovery, yet they often fail to satisfy underlying physical causality. Through an analysis of Large Language Models (LLMs) augmented with knowledge graphs derived from current literature, we uncover a phenomenon termed contextual tunneling, where models "over-anchor" on narrow, retrieved evidence while suppressing global physical reasoning. To address this problem, we introduce ARIA, a causal-aware framework that conditions knowledge use on mechanistic completeness. ARIA routes each query through a three-tier cascade: (i) direct causal reasoning when complete evidence chains of Process-Structure-Property (PSP) are available, (ii) physics-informed analogical transfer for sparse or novel material systems, and (iii) explicit parametric fallback when external evidence is incomplete. As a proof of concept, we construct a Knowledge Graph (KG) containing 2,839 extracted PSP relations from peer-reviewed articles in the materials literature and evaluate ARIA on forward prediction and inverse design tasks for two-dimensional (2D) materials. ARIA mitigates contextual tunneling, improves over unaugmented and naive KG-augmented baselines, and provides further gains when an online literature search is used for evidence enrichment. Crucially, ARIA produces auditable causal traces, enabling physically grounded and trustworthy AI-assisted materials discovery.

</details>


### [485] [MetaPS: Adaptive Programmatic Strategy Selection for Market Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22385)
*Jiaxiang Chen,Aotian Luo,Zhouyi Zheng,Weiyi Huang,Chi Zhang,Zenglin Xu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MetaPS is a framework that uses market simulations to guide LLMs in selecting among predefined programmatic trading strategies (momentum, mean reversion, etc.) rather than generating actions directly. It fine-tunes models on simulated rollouts showing which strategies perform best in which market states. Tested on multi-stock trading and a goods-exchange sandbox, compact fine-tuned models (0.8B-9B) outperform fixed-strategy baselines, direct decision-making agents, and even stronger API-based LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: No single market strategy always wins; performance depends on changing market conditions. Directly generating market actions with LLMs is unreliable. The authors aim to learn an adaptive, interpretable, and executable decision paradigm that selects among proven programmatic strategies based on market state.

Method: MetaPS simulates/backtests candidate strategies in market environments, identifies states where specific strategies yield better future outcomes, and converts these state-strategy pairs into supervised fine-tuning data. During inference, the pre-fine-tuned model observes the current market state and selects a strategy program without querying the simulator, and the chosen program produces the final action.

Result: Experiments on multi-stock trading and a goods-exchange sandbox show consistent improvement across model scales from 0.8B to 9B parameters. MetaPS outperforms fixed-strategy baselines, direct decision-making agents, and prompted API-based LLM agents. Compact fine-tuned models in several settings surpass stronger API models.

Conclusion: Market simulations can provide scalable and targeted supervision for learning adaptive, interpretable, and executable strategy selection in LLM-based agents.

Abstract: No single market strategy always wins: momentum, mean reversion, risk control,and event-driven rules can each succeed or fail as market conditions change.Rather than asking large language models to directly generate market actions,we study an executable decision paradigm where an agent selects from a library of programmatic strategies, each implemented as a code module mapping market observations to actions.We propose \textbf{MetaPS}, a simulation-guided framework for adaptive programmatic strategy selection. MetaPS rolls out candidate strategies in simulated or backtested markets, identifies states where particular strategies lead to better future outcomes, and converts these state--strategy pairs into supervised fine-tuning data. During inference, the simulator is no longer queried: MetaPS observes only the current market state and candidate strategy context, selects a suitable strategy program, and the selected program produces the final action. Experiments on multi-stock trading and a controlled goods-exchange sandbox show that MetaPS consistently improves across model scales from 0.8B to 9B parameters. It outperforms fixed-strategy baselines, direct decision-making agents, and prompted API-based LLM agents; in several settings, compact fine-tuned models even surpass stronger API models. These results demonstrate that market simulations can provide scalable and targeted supervision for learning adaptive, interpretable, and executable strategy selection.

</details>


### [486] [PlanBench-XL: Evaluating Long-Horizon Planning of LLM Tool-Use Agents in Large-Scale Tool Ecosystems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22388)
*Jiayu Liu,Qihan Lin,Cheng Qian,Rui Wang,Emre Can Acikgoz,Xiaocheng Yang,Jiateng Liu,Zhenhailong Wang,Xiusi Chen,Heng Ji,Dilek Hakkani-Tür*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PlanBench-XL is a benchmark of 327 retail tasks over 1,665 tools that evaluates LLM agents' ability to iteratively retrieve and use tools under retrieval-limited visibility and dynamic disruptions. Experiments show GPT-5.4 achieves 51.90% accuracy in block-free settings but drops to 11.36% under severe blocking, revealing agents' vulnerability to runtime failures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks rarely evaluate planning under retrieval-limited tool visibility and dynamic tool failures in large tool ecosystems, motivating the need for a benchmark that tests iterative tool discovery and robust adaptive planning in long-horizon tasks.

Method: PlanBench-XL comprises 327 retail tasks over 1,665 tools with an optional blocking mechanism that simulates missing, failing, or distracting tool functions. Agents must iteratively retrieve usable tools, invoke them to uncover intermediate evidence, and detect disrupted paths to adapt at runtime.

Result: GPT-5.4 achieves 51.90% accuracy in block-free settings but collapses to 11.36% under the most severe blocking condition. Agents are especially vulnerable when failures lack explicit error signals or when recovery requires longer alternative tool-use paths.

Conclusion: PlanBench-XL serves as a testbed for diagnosing agentic planning failures, highlighting the need for robust adaptive planning in long-horizon tasks with large, imperfect tool environments.

Abstract: LLM agents increasingly operate in large tool ecosystems, where real-world tasks require discovering relevant tools, inferring implicit sub-goals, and adapting to dynamic environments over long horizons. However, existing benchmarks rarely evaluate planning under retrieval-limited tool visibility. To address this gap, we introduce PlanBench-XL, an interactive benchmark of 327 retail tasks over 1,665 tools that tests whether agents can iteratively retrieve usable tools, invoke them to uncover intermediate evidence for subsequent calls toward the final goal. PlanBench-XL further features an optional blocking mechanism that simulates real-world unpredictability through missing, failing, or distracting tool functions, forcing agents to detect disrupted paths and adapt at runtime. Experiments on ten leading LLMs show that massive-tool planning remains challenging: while GPT-5.4 achieves 51.90% accuracy in block-free settings, it collapses to 11.36% under the most severe blocking condition. Further analysis shows that agents are especially vulnerable when failures lack explicit error signals or when recovery requires longer alternative tool-use paths. These results establish PlanBench-XL as a testbed for diagnosing agentic planning failures and highlight the need for robust adaptive planning in long-horizon tasks with large, imperfect tool environments.

</details>


### [487] [Code Isn't Memory: A Structural Codebase Index Inside a Coding Agent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22417)
*Ishaan Bhola,Adithyan Krishnan,Sravanth Kurmala,Mukunda NS*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Ablation study on a fixed coding-agent harness (Claude Opus 4.7, SWE-PolyBench/SWE-bench) shows that adding a structural codebase index improves localization and resolve rates with no cost penalty and lower cost per solved instance versus both the index-free harness and an agentic-grep baseline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Coding agents now widely use retrieval over working repositories, but retrieval implementations vary greatly. The question is whether adding a structural codebase index actually changes cost or resolve within a fixed agent harness.

Method: Three arms were run on SWE-PolyBench Verified and SWE-bench Pro with Claude Opus 4.7 held fixed: (1) harness with structural index, (2) same harness without index, (3) agentic-grep comparator. Three seeds per condition, leak-audited per-task sandbox.

Result: Within-harness: large localization gain + statistically separated resolve gain, no cost penalty per cell, lower cost per solve. Cross-harness: index does not regress vs agentic-grep on resolve or localization, no cost penalty.

Conclusion: The deployment question is not whether a structural codebase index is too expensive (it lands at lower $/solved than agentic grep), but whether the workload includes multi-file changes where structural ranking pays off.

Abstract: Coding agents now interleave LLMs with retrieval over the working repository, and retrieval implementations vary widely across deployed harnesses. Inside a fixed coding-agent harness on a fixed model, does adding a structural codebase index actually change cost or resolve? We ran three arms (the harness with the index, the same harness without it, and an agentic-grep comparator) on SWE-PolyBench Verified and SWE-bench Pro with Claude Opus 4.7 held fixed throughout, across three seeds, inside a leak-audited per-task sandbox. The within-harness ablation produces a large localization gain and a statistically separated resolve gain, with no cost penalty per cell and lower cost per solve. The cross-harness check shows that the index does not regress against an agentic-grep baseline on resolve or localization, again at no cost penalty. We release the per-cell exclusion ledger, the leak-audit script, the localization extractor, and the results database. The deployment question for a structural codebase index is thus not whether it is too expensive to run (across seeds, the index lands at a lower $/solved than agentic grep) but whether the workload includes multi-file changes where structural ranking pays off.

</details>


### [488] [SVGym (SciVerseGym): An Environment for Reinforcement Learning and Bayesian Optimization in Crystal Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22425)
*Bin Cao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SciVerseGym is a Gymnasium-compatible framework for sequential crystal discovery that frames crystal design as a Markov decision process, supporting local/global editing actions, configurable evaluators, and multiple search paradigms (RL, Bayesian optimization, evolutionary search, language agents) in a decoupled, reproducible testbed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing closed-loop crystal search methods are fragmented across bespoke pipelines for editing, relaxation, scoring, constraints, and bookkeeping, lacking a unified, standardized environment for sequential decision-making.

Method: SciVerseGym frames crystal design as a Markov decision process where agents observe atomistic structures, apply chemically meaningful edits (elemental substitution, lattice perturbation, atomic displacement, vacancy creation, atom insertion), and receive feedback from a configurable evaluator using ML interatomic potentials or ASE-compatible calculators, returning standard Gymnasium (obs, reward, terminated, truncated, info) tuples.

Result: The framework enables efficient atomistic evaluation for interactive materials discovery by supporting configurable chemical spaces, structure pools, atomistic/graph-based observations, custom rewards, optional relaxation, and stability/phonon diagnostics across local and global actions.

Conclusion: SciVerseGym provides an open, reproducible, and extensible testbed that decouples agent logic from materials infrastructure, supporting reinforcement learning, Bayesian optimization, evolutionary search, and language-agent workflows in closed-loop crystal discovery.

Abstract: Machine-learned interatomic potentials now enable efficient atomistic evaluation for interactive materials discovery, yet closed-loop crystal search methods remain fragmented across bespoke pipelines for editing, relaxation, scoring, constraints, and bookkeeping. We introduce SciVerseGym, a Gymnasium-compatible environment for sequential crystal discovery that frames crystal design as a Markov decision process. Agents observe an atomistic structure, apply chemically meaningful edits, and receive feedback from a configurable evaluator.
