SciVerseGym supports local and global actions, including elemental substitution, lattice perturbation, atomic displacement, vacancy creation, and atom insertion, along with configurable chemical spaces, structure pools, atomistic and graph-based observations, custom rewards, optional relaxation, and stability or phonon-related diagnostics. Each step applies an edit, evaluates the candidate using a machine-learned interatomic potential or any ASE-compatible calculator, and returns the standard (obs, reward, terminated, truncated, info) tuple. By decoupling agent logic from materials infrastructure, SciVerseGym provides an open, reproducible, and extensible testbed for reinforcement learning, Bayesian optimization, evolutionary search, and language-agent workflows in closed-loop crystal discovery. Code is available at: https://github.com/Bin-Cao/SciVerseGym.

</details>


### [489] [Efficient Multimodal Clinical Question Answering for Pulmonary Embolism Risk Assessment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22442)
*Xiangyuan Xue,Yang Yu,Yan Gao,Junyan Wang,Bin Chen,Lingyan Ruan,Ting Dang,Hong Jia*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper evaluates compact multimodal large language models (MLLMs) on pulmonary embolism (PE) diagnosis and prognosis using CTPA and EHR data from 23,248 studies, finding that Gemma4 models perform best with combined CTPA+EHR inputs, and diagnostic tasks outperform prognostic ones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: PE management requires timely diagnosis and risk assessment, making it a clinically meaningful setting for evaluating compact multimodal LLMs that combine CTPA imaging, radiology reports, and longitudinal EHR data.

Method: The authors built a benchmark on the INSPECT dataset (23,248 CTPA studies from 19,402 patients), formulated 8 diagnostic/prognostic tasks as structured QA problems, and evaluated efficient MLLMs under CTPA-Only, EHR-Only, and CTPA+EHR settings using zero-shot and few-shot prompting.

Result: Gemma4 E4B and Gemma4 E2B performed best with EHR evidence, especially under CTPA+EHR input. PE diagnosis achieved higher performance than prognostic tasks, with readmission prediction being the most challenging.

Conclusion: Compact multimodal models show strong potential for early-stage PE risk detection and explanation, particularly when combining imaging and EHR data.

Abstract: Pulmonary embolism (PE) is a high risk cardiopulmonary condition whose management requires both timely diagnosis and reliable assessment of future clinical risk. Because PE care routinely combines computed tomography pulmonary angiography (CTPA), radiology interpretation, and longitudinal electronic health record (EHR) evidence, it provides a clinically meaningful setting for evaluating compact multimodal language models. In this work, we build a benchmark using efficient multimodal large language models (MLLMs) on INSPECT, a multimodal PE dataset containing 23,248 CTPA studies from 19,402 patients. We formulate eight diagnostic and prognostic tasks as structured clinical question answering problems and evaluate on typical efficient MLLMs under CTPA-Only, EHR-Only, and CTPA+EHR settings with zero-shot and few-shot prompting. Results show that Gemma4 E4B and Gemma4 E2B perform more strongly when EHR evidence is available, especially under CTPA+EHR input. Task level analysis further shows that PE diagnosis achieves higher performance than prognostic tasks, particularly readmission prediction. These observations suggest that compact multimodal models have the great potential in early stage PE risk detection and explanation.

</details>


### [490] [A Differentiable Atari VCS:A Complex, Fully Known Ground Truth for Explainable AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22447)
*Andreas Maier,Siming Bayer,Patrick Krauss*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper solves the XAI ground-truth problem by building two fully differentiable, bit-accurate emulators of the Atari 2600 VCS (jutari in Julia, jaxtari in JAX), validated against xitari on all 64 ALE games. This creates a complex yet fully inspectable system, enabling gradient-based XAI methods with known ground truth internal functioning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: XAI lacks ground-truth mechanisms for complex systems (e.g., deep networks), while simple interpretable models have trivial mechanisms that test nothing. The dichotomy prevents rigorous validation of explanations. This work removes that barrier by creating a study object that is both genuinely complex and fully specified internally.

Method: Two independent differentiable Atari 2600 VCS emulators were built (jutari in Julia, jaxtari in JAX), validated bit-for-bit against xitari on all 64 ALE games (byte-identical RAM, pixel-identical screens). Cartridge ROM is treated as a weight tensor, RAM as a soft tape, control flow as gates—proving differentiable (soft) execution equals original (hard) execution in the forward pass at any finite temperature, while exposing surrogate gradients where bit logic has none.

Result: Both emulators reproduce xitari on all 64 ALE games (64/64 byte-identical RAM, 64/64 pixel-identical screens). The JAX port achieves batched differentiable rollouts at millions of environment-steps/s on one commodity GPU. The system was built in ~137 active hours over 29 days, largely written autonomously by coding agents.

Conclusion: The paper validates theoretically and through a qualitative gradient study that gradient-based XAI on this fully differentiable, inspectable-by-construction system is feasible, providing a foundation for rigorous explanation evaluation.

Abstract: Explanation requires ground truth: to verify an account of a system we must know its inner functioning-just what is missing where explainable AI (XAI) is most needed. Systems we can study fall into two camps. Simple, procedural one-decision trees, rule lists, sparse linear models-have a known but trivial mechanism, so explaining them tests nothing; genuinely complex ones-deep networks, real-world tasks-need XAI but have no ground-truth inner functioning, so an explanation can be plausible, confident, and wrong with no way to tell. We remove this dichotomy with a study object both genuinely complex and fully specified-inspectable by construction-and, so gradient methods apply, fully differentiable. We reimplement the Atari 2600 Video Computer System (VCS)-a real computer architecture, and the cradle of deep reinforcement learning-as two independent end-to-end differentiable emulators in Julia (jutari) and JAX (jaxtari), each validated bit-for-bit against xitari. Both reproduce xitari on all 64 supported Arcade Learning Environment (ALE) games: 64/64 byte-identical RAM and 64/64 pixel-identical screens. Treating the cartridge ROM as a weight tensor, RAM as a soft tape, and control flow as gates, we prove the differentiable (soft) execution equals the original (hard) one bit-for-bit in the forward pass at any finite temperature, while exposing surrogate gradients where the bit logic has none. The JAX port also opens a GPU path: batched differentiable rollouts reach millions of environment-steps/s on one commodity GPU. The system was built in roughly 137 active hours over 29 calendar days, much of it written autonomously by coding agents. This paper builds and validates the foundation, showing-theoretically and in a qualitative gradient study-that gradient-based XAI on it is feasible. Both ports' full code is available under the MIT license at https://github.com/akmaier/UnderstandingVCS.

</details>


### [491] [Self-Evolving Cognitive Framework via Causal World Modeling for Embodied Scientific Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22449)
*Yi Yu,Tetsunari Inamura*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a self-evolving cognitive framework via causal world modeling for embodied scientific intelligence, integrating causal world modeling, intervention-driven causal reasoning, and continual cognitive refinement. It reinterprets embodied interaction as an epistemic process for causal hypothesis generation and knowledge acquisition, advocating a shift from predictive intelligence to epistemic intelligence and suggesting a new causal-epistemic benchmarking paradigm.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current embodied world models are optimized for predictive objectives, which limits their ability to generalize under distribution shifts and reason systematically about unseen situations and hypothetical interventions. The authors argue for moving beyond predictive world modeling toward self-evolving cognitive systems.

Method: The framework integrates three components: (1) causal world modeling for constructing internal causal representations, (2) intervention-driven causal reasoning for generating and testing causal hypotheses, and (3) continual cognitive refinement via causal discovery, intervention-driven feedback, and counterfactual reasoning.

Result: The paper provides a conceptual and theoretical foundation for transitioning from predictive intelligence to epistemic intelligence, where intelligence emerges through continual construction, revision, and refinement of causal world models via environmental interaction.

Conclusion: An intervention-driven causal-epistemic benchmarking paradigm is suggested for evaluating self-evolving embodied scientific intelligence, reinterpreting embodied interaction as an epistemic process rather than mere trajectory optimization.

Abstract: Current embodied world models are primarily optimized for predictive objectives, limiting their ability to generalize under distribution shifts and reason systematically about unseen situations and hypothetical interventions. We argue that embodied intelligence should move beyond predictive world modeling toward self-evolving cognitive systems that continually construct and refine internal causal representations through interaction with the environment. To this end, we propose a self-evolving cognitive framework via causal world modeling for embodied scientific intelligence, which integrates three complementary components: causal world modeling, intervention-driven causal reasoning, and continual cognitive refinement. The proposed framework continuously revises and expands its internal causal world model through causal discovery, intervention-driven feedback, and counterfactual reasoning, supporting continual cognitive refinement and enabling cognition itself to evolve over time. Furthermore, we reinterpret embodied interaction not merely as a means of trajectory optimization, but as an epistemic process for causal hypothesis generation, intervention-driven experimentation, and continual knowledge acquisition. This work provides a conceptual and theoretical foundation for a transition from predictive intelligence toward epistemic intelligence, in which intelligence emerges through the continual construction, revision, and refinement of causal world models via interaction with the environment. Accordingly, an intervention-driven causal-epistemic benchmarking paradigm is suggested for evaluating self-evolving embodied scientific intelligence.

</details>


### [492] [PRIME: Evaluating Prompt Resolution Under Incompatible Instructions in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22470)
*Tehreem Javed,Shumaim Fatimah,Masooma Bakhtiari,Gibrail Islam,Mehwish Fatima*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PRIME evaluates LLM behavior under conflicting instructions, finding conflict type affects behavior more than model scale, with various failure modes across conflict categories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing instruction-following benchmarks assess meta-instructions in isolation, limiting understanding of how LLMs process conflicting instructions. This work introduces a framework to systematically analyze LLM behavior under incompatible prompts.

Method: PRIME generates calibrated conflicts across response length, output format, and reasoning. Five instruction-tuned open-weight LLMs are evaluated in balanced and naturally distributed settings, with responses classified using a deterministic behavioral taxonomy.

Result: Conflict type has a greater impact on model behavior than model scale. Different failure modes emerge across conflict categories.

Conclusion: Developing conflict awareness is valuable; LLM instruction-following ability cannot be adequately assessed through isolated constraints alone.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) often encounter conflicting prompts, although current instruction following benchmarks assess those meta-instructions in isolation, limiting the insights about how models process conflicting instructions. We introduce a framework \textit{PRIME}(\textit{Prompt Resolution under Incompatible Meta-Instructions Evaluation}) to analyze behavior of LLMs when provided with conflicting instructions. \textit{PRIME} purposefully produces calibrated conflicts across response length, output format, and reasoning; classifying model responses with a deterministic behavioral taxonomy. We are evaluating five instruction tuned open weight LLMs in two distinct settings, balanced and naturally distributed. The conclusion we reach upon analysis is that conflict type is more significant in affecting behavior than model scale, and various failure modes across different categories of conflict. Our findings emphasize the value of developing conflict awareness and suggest ability of LLM to follow instructions cannot be assessed through isolated constraints alone.

</details>


### [493] [VADAOrchestra: Neurosymbolic Orchestration of Adaptive Reasoning Workflows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22485)
*Teodoro Baldazzi,Luigi Bellomarini,Andrea Coletta,Michela Iezzi,Carsten Maple,Alessandro Pesare,Emanuel Sallinger*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: VADAOrchestra is a neurosymbolic framework combining LLM-based orchestration with Datalog+/- symbolic reasoning to model complex workflows as evolving reasoning processes, achieving verifiability, scalability, and explainability in decision-making, demonstrated on financial use cases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional BPM systems are rigid and cannot adapt at runtime, while LLM-based agentic systems are flexible but opaque, unreliable, and struggle with scalability over large datasets. The paper aims to combine the strengths of both paradigms.

Method: A hybrid neurosymbolic approach where an LLM-based orchestrator incrementally plans workflows encoded as logic programs in Datalog+/-, with predicates as tool invocations and rules as domain dependencies. Symbolic inference is executed by a Datalog+/- engine, decoupling orchestration from reasoning.

Result: Evaluated on real-world financial use cases, demonstrating faithfulness, scalability, and explainability compared to standard agentic architectures.

Conclusion: The neurosymbolic decoupling provides verifiable reasoning traces for auditability, addresses scalability via targeted data querying, and offers explainability superior to pure LLM-based agents.

Abstract: Decision-making in real-world settings rarely follows a fixed script. Instead, it unfolds as a dynamic reasoning process in which the appropriate course of action evolves as new context and data become available. Traditional Business Process Management systems provide rigor, determinism, and auditability, yet they generally struggle to adapt their execution at runtime. Conversely, agentic systems based on Large Language Models (LLMs) bring flexibility to decision-making, but they are inherently opaque, often unreliable, and suffer from significant scalability constraints when operating over large datasets. To combine these complementary paradigms, we introduce VADAOrchestra, a neurosymbolic framework that models complex workflows as evolving reasoning processes. The framework adopts a hybrid approach: given a user query and a collection of data sources, an LLM-based orchestrator incrementally plans and adapts the workflow. This is encoded as a logic program in a fragment of Datalog+/- where predicates correspond to tool invocations and rules represent both predefined domain dependencies and logic constructs synthesized on demand to manipulate intermediate results. All logical inference tasks are then executed by a state-of-the-art Datalog+/- symbolic engine. This approach provides a verifiable reasoning trace, supporting the auditability and reproducibility of the entire process. Furthermore, by decoupling high-level orchestration from symbolic inference, it addresses scalability concerns, enabling complex reasoning over large datasets through targeted data querying. We evaluate VADAOrchestra on real-world financial use cases, demonstrating faithfulness, scalability, and explainability compared to standard agentic architectures.

</details>


### [494] [SCOPE: Evolving Symbolic World for Planning in Open-Ended Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22488)
*Yundaichuan Zhan,Minghe Gao,Zhongqi Yue,Wendong Bu,Wenqiao Zhang,Guoming Wang,Jisheng Dang,Juncheng Li,Siliang Tang,Yueting Zhuang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SCOPE is a self-adaptive symbolic planning framework that refines action plans and evolves symbolic world representations through a Symbolic Execution Simulator and Self-Adaptive Symbolic Memory, improving completeness, robustness, and cross-task adaptability in open-ended embodied environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Symbolic representations obtained from perception in open-ended environments are often incomplete, leading to suboptimal performance when integrating VLMs with classical planners for long-horizon embodied tasks.

Method: SCOPE consists of two modules: (1) Symbolic Execution Simulator (SESim) for symbolic validation and real execution of action plans with feedback-driven refinement; (2) Self-Adaptive Symbolic Memory (SASMem) that distills feedback into evolving symbolic knowledge for improved long-horizon planning and world modeling.

Result: SCOPE significantly improves symbolic world completeness, plan success rates under environment perturbations, and cross-task grounding and adaptability across diverse embodied scenarios.

Conclusion: Self-adaptive symbolic evolution via execution feedback effectively addresses the incompleteness of symbolic representations in open-ended environments for VLM-based planning.

Abstract: Recent works have explored integrating Vision-Language Models (VLMs) with classical planners that rely on symbolic representations of planning problems to generate long-horizon plans for complex embodied tasks. However, in open-ended environments, these symbolic representations obtained from perception are often incomplete, leading to suboptimal performance. To address this, we introduce SCOPE, a self-adaptive symbolic planning framework that supports refining action plans and evolving the symbolic world, i.e., the symbolic representations of open-ended environments. SCOPE comprises two synergistic modules: a Symbolic Execution Simulator (SESim) that conducts symbolic validation and real execution of action plans, leveraging the feedback to refine the plans and evolve the symbolic world; and a Self-Adaptive Symbolic Memory (SASMem) that further distills feedback into evolving symbolic knowledge to enhance long-horizon planning and modeling of the symbolic world. Experiments in open-ended environments show that SCOPE significantly improves the completeness of the symbolic world, the success rate of plans under environment perturbations, and cross-task grounding and adaptability across diverse embodied scenarios.

</details>


### [495] [Deep Learning-Based Sign Language Recognition from Videos and Cross-Lingual Translation to Indian Vernaculars](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22494)
*Chandranath Adak,Ramesh Nandipalli*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper presents a two-stage deep learning pipeline: (1) fine-tuning VideoMAE video transformer to classify Indian Sign Language video clips into English word labels (13 classes, 78% validation accuracy), and (2) translating predicted English labels into Hindi, Telugu, and Bengali using Meta's NLLB-200 model. A Streamlit demo showcases the workflow from user-uploaded video to multilingual output.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Automated sign language recognition and translation tools remain limited, especially for low-resource Indian languages. The deaf and hard-of-hearing community lacks accessible systems that can recognize sign gestures from video and produce natural language text in multiple Indian languages.

Method: Two-stage pipeline: Stage (i) uses a fine-tuned VideoMAE video transformer to classify 16-frame clips (224×224 resolution, uniformly sampled) from an Indian Sign Language dataset into 13 English word labels. Stage (ii) uses Meta AI's NLLB-200 model to translate the predicted English label into Hindi, Telugu, and Bengali. A Streamlit-based inference demo provides user-facing video upload and multilingual output.

Result: On a 13-class, 197-clip dataset with 80-20 split, the fine-tuned VideoMAE model achieves 99% training accuracy and 78% validation accuracy after 15 epochs. Confusion matrix analysis reveals dominant failure modes among confusable adjective pairs (ugly, deaf, blind, hat, dress).

Conclusion: The pipeline demonstrates feasibility of sign-to-multilingual-text translation for Indian languages, but has limitations: small label set (13 classes), isolated-word recognition (no continuous signing), sensitivity to single-signer style, and ambiguity in single-word machine translation. Future work should expand to sentence-level generation and larger vocabulary.

Abstract: Sign language is a primary mode of communication for the global deaf and hard-of-hearing community, yet automated tools that recognize sign gestures from video and translate them into natural language text remain limited, particularly for low-resource Indian languages. We present a two-stage deep learning pipeline that (i) classifies short sign language video clips into English word labels using a fine-tuned VideoMAE video transformer, and (ii) translates the predicted English label into Hindi, Telugu, and Bengali using Meta AI's No Language Left Behind (NLLB-200) multilingual translation model. The classification model is fine-tuned on a 13-class subset of the AI4Bharat Indian Sign Language video corpus from IIT Madras, processing 16-frame clips sampled uniformly from each video at 224 x 224 resolution. Under a small-scale academic setting (13 classes, 197 clips, 80-20 split), the fine-tuned model reaches 99% training accuracy and 78% validation accuracy after 15 epochs. We provide a per-class breakdown via a confusion matrix and classification report, identify the dominant failure modes (confusable adjective pairs such as ugly, deaf, blind, hat, and dress), and describe a Streamlit-based inference demo that takes a user-uploaded video and returns the predicted English label alongside its Hindi, Telugu, and Bengali translations. We discuss the scope, limitations (small label set, isolated-word rather than continuous signing, single-signer style sensitivity, ambiguity of single-word machine translation), and directions for future work, including expanding to sentence-level generation and a larger vocabulary. Code is released to support reproducibility.

</details>


### [496] [Grounded Scaling: Why Agentic AI Needs Deterministic Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22495)
*Liang Ding,Xintong Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper argues that environment determinism (per-step success probability δ) is a fundamental bottleneck for long-chain agentic AI tasks, showing that success degrades exponentially (δ^k). It introduces a Determinism-Efficiency Bound, Verifier-Goodharting Floor, and skill evolution convergence condition, operationalized via a Supply Certainty Index (SCI) and Determinism Maturity Model (DMM), while engaging competing positions like sim-to-real and alignment sufficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To challenge the prevailing AGI-to-ASI scaling debate that focuses on compute growth versus frictions (data wall, abstraction barrier, embodied bottleneck, multi-agent trust), by showing that environment determinism is a complementary, cross-cutting binding axis that fundamentally limits agentic AI task success in real-world settings.

Method: Three formal results: (1) Determinism-Efficiency Bound on chain-task success; (2) Verifier-Goodharting Floor on flywheel ceilings under imperfect rewards; (3) convergence condition for environment-side skill evolution. Operationalized via Supply Certainty Index (SCI) over five measurable properties and a five-level Determinism Maturity Model (DMM). A falsifiable open-question programme (OQ1-OQ5) with explicit null results is proposed.

Result: Formally demonstrates that long-chain agent execution fails exponentially (δ^k) with per-step determinism δ<1, establishes bounds on task success and flywheel ceilings under imperfect verification, and provides convergence conditions for environment-side skill evolution. The framework is platform-agnostic and engages three competing positions.

Conclusion: Environment determinism is a fundamental, underappreciated bottleneck for agentic AI that cuts across existing friction categories. The proposed SCI and DMM provide operational tools for assessing and improving determinism, and the open-question programme offers a falsifiable research agenda for the field.

Abstract: Long-chain agent execution fails exponentially in environments designed for human tolerance: with per-step determinism $δ< 1$, $k$-step chain success degrades as $δ^k$. The AGI-to-ASI scaling debate (Genewein et al., 2026) has so far framed progress as a race between compute growth and a list of frictions (data wall, abstraction barrier, embodied bottleneck, multi-agent trust); we argue that environment determinism is a complementary binding axis cutting across all four, for the broad class of agentic AI tasks whose outcomes are verifiable economically, physically, or through multi-party settlement. Three formal results pin down the regime: a Determinism-Efficiency Bound on chain-task success, a Verifier-Goodharting Floor on flywheel ceilings under imperfect rewards, and a convergence condition for environment-side skill evolution. We operationalise the framework as a Supply Certainty Index (SCI) over five measurable properties, a five-level Determinism Maturity Model (DMM) as adoption ladder, and a falsifiable open-question programme (OQ1-OQ5) with explicit null results that would force retraction. The position is platform-agnostic. We engage three competing positions: sim-to-real sufficiency, alignment sufficiency, and AI-as-normal-technology.

</details>


### [497] [Imagine to Ensure Safety in Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22509)
*Gregory Gorbov,Artem Latyshev,Aleksandr I. Panov*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes a hierarchical safe reinforcement learning method combining a learnable world model with high-level and low-level policies to address safe exploration in long-horizon tasks, outperforming existing baselines in success rate and constraint satisfaction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Safe exploration in RL is challenging, especially in long-horizon tasks where compounding estimation errors and restricted exploration capabilities fundamentally limit existing safe methods.

Method: The method combines a learnable world model with two complementary policies: a high-level policy generates intermediate subgoals to bias exploration toward safe regions, while a low-level policy uses imagined rollouts in the learned world model to reduce unsafe behaviors when reaching these subgoals.

Result: The method significantly outperforms existing Safe RL baselines in both success rate and constraint satisfaction on challenging long-horizon navigation and manipulation tasks with high-dimensional action spaces, consistently meeting the prescribed safety budget across seeds.

Conclusion: The proposed hierarchical safe exploration approach effectively solves complex long-horizon scenarios where prior Safe RL methods fail.

Abstract: This work investigates the safe exploration problem in reinforcement learning, where an agent must maximize cumulative performance while simultaneously satisfying safety constraints. This challenge becomes even more pronounced in long-horizon tasks, where existing safe methods face fundamental limitations due to compounding estimation errors and restricted exploration capabilities. To address this problem, we propose a method that combines a learnable world model with two complementary policies a high-level policy and a low-level policy to promote safety at both hierarchical levels. The high-level policy generates intermediate subgoals that bias exploration toward safe regions, while the low-level policy uses imagined rollouts in the learned world model to reduce unsafe behaviors when reaching these subgoals. The proposed method was evaluated on challenging long-horizon navigation and manipulation tasks with high-dimensional action spaces, where it significantly outperforms existing Safe RL baselines in both success rate and strong empirical constraint satisfaction, consistently meeting the prescribed safety budget across seeds, while prior approaches fail to effectively solve these complex long-horizon scenarios.

</details>


### [498] [Governance Decay: How Context Compaction Silently Erases Safety Constraints in Long-Horizon LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22528)
*Shiyang Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Context compaction (summarization/eviction) silently removes safety constraints that LLM agents otherwise obey, causing a failure mode called Governance Decay. The paper introduces ConstraintRot benchmark, demonstrates that violations jump from 0% to 30-59% after compaction, presents a Compaction-Eviction Attack, and proposes Constraint Pinning as a mitigation that restores 0% violation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Context-management layers (summarization, compaction, eviction) are essential for long-running LLM agent sessions, but their impact on safety-critical governance constraints has been overlooked. The authors aim to identify and characterize this vulnerability.

Method: The authors introduce ConstraintRot, a benchmark of long-horizon agent scenarios with deterministic tool-call grading. They measure violation rates across 1,323 episodes and seven model families before and after context compaction, and design a Compaction-Eviction Attack using adversarial in-context content. A mitigation called Constraint Pinning is proposed.

Result: Violation rises from 0% (full policy context) to 30% after compaction, reaching 59% for some models. When constraint survives summary, violation stays at 0%; when dropped, violation reaches 38%. The Compaction-Eviction Attack defeats all evaluated models. Constraint Pinning restores violation to 0%.

Conclusion: Context management is a first-class governance surface for deployed LLM agents. The paper identifies Governance Decay as a critical failure mode and demonstrates that simple mitigation (Constraint Pinning) can effectively address it.

Abstract: Modern LLM agents increasingly rely on context compaction, summarization, or eviction to keep long-running sessions within a token budget. We show that this context-management layer is a safety-critical failure surface: in-context governance constraints that agents reliably obey while visible can be silently removed by compaction, causing the same agent to perform prohibited tool actions later in the session. We call this failure mode Governance Decay. We introduce ConstraintRot, a benchmark of long-horizon agent scenarios with deterministic tool-call grading, and measure compaction-induced violations across seven model families. Across 1,323 episodes, violation rises from 0% with the policy in full context to 30% after compaction, reaching 59% for some models; when the constraint survives the summary, violation remains 0%, but when it is dropped, violation reaches 38%. We further study a Compaction-Eviction Attack, in which adversarial in-context content biases the summarizer to omit a legitimate policy, and show that optimized injections defeat every evaluated model. Finally, we propose Constraint Pinning, a simple training-free mitigation that quarantines governance constraints from lossy compaction and restores violation to 0% in our benchmark. These results identify context management as a first-class governance surface for deployed LLM agents.

</details>


### [499] [MacAgentBench: Benchmarking AI Agents on Real-World macOS Desktop](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22557)
*Yikun Fu,Bowen Fu,Zhenyu Wu,Shuang Cheng,Xiaowei Sun,Bowen Yang,Zehao Li,Yibo Zhao,Zichen Ding,Zhoumianze Liu,Shijie Wang,Biqing Qi,Bowen Zhou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MacAgentBench is a new macOS agent benchmark with 676 tasks across 25 apps, using rule-based evaluation and multi-checkpoint scoring. The best system (Claude Opus 4.6 on OpenClaw) achieves 73.7% Pass@1, with advantages driven mainly by the skill library rather than framework design. Fine-grained metrics reveal models with similar Pass@1 differ substantially in sub-goal completion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing macOS benchmarks evaluate agents without framework augmentation and rely on binary evaluation, failing to capture modern CUA framework capabilities and partial progress on long-horizon, multi-application tasks.

Method: MacAgentBench comprises 676 tasks across 25 applications (~60% involving both GUI and CLI interaction), uses deterministic rule-based evaluation, and introduces fine-grained multi-checkpoint scoring with capability annotations for multi-application tasks.

Result: Best configuration (Claude Opus 4.6 on OpenClaw) achieves 73.7% Pass@1; advantage primarily driven by skill library rather than framework design; models with similar Pass@1 differ substantially in sub-goal completion.

Conclusion: Current benchmarks are insufficient for evaluating modern CUAs; fine-grained multi-checkpoint scoring reveals meaningful differences in sub-task completion not captured by binary Pass@1 metrics; skill libraries are a key differentiator.

Abstract: Computer use agents (CUAs) have advanced rapidly in desktop automation, and a growing number of users deploy CUAs such as OpenClaw on Mac Mini for always-on automation. However, existing benchmarks, including those for macOS, evaluate agents without framework augmentation and rely on binary evaluation. As a result, they fail to capture both the framework capabilities leveraged by modern CUAs and the partial progress on long-horizon, multi-application tasks. We present MacAgentBench, a comprehensive macOS agent benchmark comprising 676 tasks across 25 applications, with nearly 60% involving both GUI and CLI interaction. The benchmark adopts deterministic rule-based evaluation and introduces fine-grained multi-checkpoint scoring with capability annotations for multi-application tasks. Experiments across three frameworks and 16 models show that the best configuration, Claude Opus 4.6 on OpenClaw, attains 73.7% Pass@1, while this advantage is primarily driven by the skill library rather than by framework design. Fine-grained metrics further reveal that models with similar Pass@1 can differ substantially in sub-goal completion. Our code and data are publicly available at https://github.com/JetAstra/MacAgentBench.

</details>


### [500] [PaperClaw: Harnessing Agents for Autonomous Research and Human-in-the-Loop Refinement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22610)
*Weiwei Ye,Hangchen Liu,Dongyuan Li,Renhe Jiang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PAPERCLAW is a multi-agent system that autonomously carries out the full research lifecycle—from curating a domain's literature, data, and code, to brainstorming an idea, iteratively testing hypotheses, and finally writing a venue-compliant paper—while allowing human-in-the-loop refinement at any stage, with memory persistence and grounded citations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To automate the entire research process (not just individual tasks like writing code or searching literature) by leveraging LLMs' reasoning and tool-use capabilities, enabling fully autonomous or human-in-the-loop scientific discovery and paper generation.

Method: A multi-agent system with: (1) domain curation from live literature, datasets, and code; (2) brainstorming into an idea with a pre-registered main-result contract; (3) a stoppable hypothesis map driven by an iterative propose-test-reflect loop that only grows from measured verdicts and halts when evidence supports the idea; (4) a full-lifecycle memory for pausing/inspection/resumption; (5) an in-cycle research assistant that can run the pipeline autonomously or allow human intervention; (6) grounded output with validated citations and genuinely executed results.

Result: Evaluation with an LLM judge shows PAPERCLAW produces strong papers both fully autonomously and with human-in-the-loop refinement.

Conclusion: PAPERCLAW demonstrates that end-to-end automated research is feasible, and its human-in-the-loop design enables refinement from a first autonomous draft to a stronger paper.

Abstract: Large language models have become capable reasoners and tool users that write and run code and search the literature, which makes automating the research process itself a realistic goal. We present PAPERCLAW, a harnessed multi-agent system that carries a project autonomously, from a field of study to a finished paper. PAPERCLAW curates a domain from a field's live literature, datasets, and code; brainstorms it into an idea with a pre-registered main-result contract; and drives a stoppable hypothesis map through an iterative propose, test, reflect loop that grows only from measured verdicts and halts once the evidence supports the idea, at which point it writes a venue-compliant paper. A full-lifecycle memory keeps each stage in a single living record, so a long run can be paused, inspected, and resumed without losing context. At the centre is an in-cycle research assistant with research tools and skills: it can drive the whole pipeline on its own, while the same interface lets a person step in at any stage, turning a first autonomous draft into a stronger paper through human-in-the-loop refinement. Throughout, PAPERCLAW keeps its output grounded and checkable, citing only references validated against open scholarly indexes and reporting results that genuinely ran. An evaluation with an LLM judge finds that PAPERCLAW produces strong papers both fully autonomously and with human-in-the-loop refinement.

</details>


### [501] [SkillAudit: From Fixed-Suite Benchmarking to Skill-Centered Assessment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22613)
*Dexu Yu,Youhua Li,Zhaoyang Guan,Xianhao Lin,Jining Luan,Zihao Rao,Xuanqi Lan,Yang Ran,Bo Lan,Nai-Xin Zhai,Hanwen Du,Junchen Fu,Wenhao Deng,Yongxin Ni,Chunxiao Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SkillAudit is an automated framework that evaluates arbitrary LLM agent skills across utility, efficiency/cost, and safety dimensions by generating capability-aligned tasks directly from skill packages, running them in sandboxed environments, and using baseline comparison and two-stage risk detection. In a real-world scan of top-ranked skills across 23 occupational categories, over 7% were found risky.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluation methods rely on fixed task suites that conflate a skill's marginal contribution with backbone model strength and fail to assess value for tasks outside the skill's intended scope, making them inadequate for growing skill marketplaces.

Method: SkillAudit is an end-to-end framework that: (1) takes an arbitrary agent skill as input, (2) constructs capability-aligned evaluation tasks directly from the skill package, (3) runs tasks in isolated sandbox environments for execution evidence, (4) uses LLM-based judging for automated checks, (5) employs a baseline comparison principle to measure utility and efficiency/cost, and (6) introduces a two-stage detection paradigm combining static semantic analysis with dynamic runtime verification for safety assessment.

Result: After scanning top-ranked real-world skill packages spanning 23 occupational categories, over 7% of skills were found to be at risky status.

Conclusion: The fixed-task evaluation paradigm is inadequate for skill marketplaces; SkillAudit provides a reliable, scalable, multi-dimensional alternative that isolates the skill's own contribution and surfaces safety risks missed by conventional benchmarks.

Abstract: Agent skills have become a practical way to extend large language model agents, but the growing skill ecosystem still lacks a reliable way to judge whether a skill is worth deploying. Existing evaluation methods remain largely anchored to fixed task suites, assessing skills through performance on predefined tasks and environments. As skill marketplaces expand, this paradigm becomes inadequate: fixed suites can conflate a skill's marginal contribution with backbone strength and miss its value when tasks fall outside the skill's intended scope. We introduce SkillAudit, an end-to-end framework for skill-centered assessment that takes an arbitrary agent skill as input and automatically generates a comprehensive, multi-dimensional evaluation report spanning utility, efficiency/cost, and safety. SkillAudit focuses on the skill artifact itself and constructs capability-aligned evaluation tasks directly from the skill package. The generated tasks are conducted in isolated sandbox environments to collect execution evidence, followed by automated checks with LLM-based judging to produce auditable results. To dissect the agent skills, we propose the baseline comparison principle to measure utility and efficiency/cost, and introduce a two-stage detection paradigm combining static semantic analysis with dynamic runtime verification to assess safety risks. After scanning top-ranked real-world skill packages spanning 23 occupational categories, we found that over 7% of skills are at risky status.

</details>


### [502] [Confident but Conflicted: Internal Uncertainty and Cognitive Dissonance Resolution in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22633)
*Weihong Qi,Kristina Lerman*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMs resolve cognitive dissonance from conflicting inputs via persuasion, backfire, or immunity. Trust Elasticity (TE) measures model persuadability, varies across models, and correlates with internal uncertainty indicators (e.g., Confidence Miscalibration in Qwen, Internal Uncertainty Change in Llama).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To systematically study how LLMs resolve conflicts between their prior outputs and contradictory evidence (cognitive dissonance), and to connect this behavioral phenomenon to internal model uncertainty, which is not well understood.

Method: Varied persuasion attempts along source authority and evidence quality across 12 health-science claims. Introduced Trust Elasticity (TE), an econometrics-inspired measure of persuadability. Analyzed four LLMs, and for two open-weight models, examined internal uncertainty indicators (Confidence Miscalibration in Qwen; Internal Uncertainty Change in Llama).

Result: TE varies substantially across models; clearly false claims elicit near-zero TE across all models. Cross-model behavioral variation is associated with internal uncertainty indicators: Confidence Miscalibration in Qwen and Internal Uncertainty Change in Llama.

Conclusion: Links cross-model behavioral variation to measurable internal properties, suggesting future work on interventions targeting internal uncertainty.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) frequently encounter inputs that disagree with their prior outputs, through user pushback, retrieved documents, or web search results. While the way they resolve such conflicts -- a process we frame as cognitive dissonance resolution -- has been characterized behaviorally, its connection to internal model uncertainty is not well understood. To study this systematically, we vary persuasion attempts along two dimensions, source authority and evidence quality, across 12 health-science claims of stratified epistemic status. Dissonance can be resolved through persuasion, backfire, or immunity. We introduce Trust Elasticity (TE), an econometrics-inspired measure of how readily a model is persuaded toward conflicting evidence. Across four LLMs, TE varies substantially, while clearly false claims elicit near-zero TE across all models. On two open-weight models, we further find that this variation is associated with two complementary internal uncertainty indicators, Confidence Miscalibration in Qwen and Internal Uncertainty Change in Llama. These results link cross-model behavioral variation to a measurable internal property and point to interventions targeting internal uncertainty as future work.

</details>


### [503] [Skin-Deep: A Geometric Diagnostic for Alignment Fragility in Large Language Model Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22676)
*Dongyub Jude Lee,Jungseob Lee,Seungyoon Lee,Seongtae Hong,Suhyune Son,Sugyeong Eo,Jaehyung Seo,Heuiseok Lim*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Skin-Deep introduces the Geometric Fragility Score (GFS), a diagnostic that detects alignment fragility in LLMs by analyzing hidden-state activations before any fine-tuning. GFS predicts which models will lose refusal behavior after low-cost fine-tuning, serving as a pre-deployment safety check.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Open-weight LLMs can pass refusal safety tests at release but lose their harmful-request refusal capability after benign low-cost fine-tuning. Prior work documents this failure but provides no way to detect this fragility before an attack or fine-tuning occurs.

Method: Skin-Deep compresses layer-wise safety geometry from hidden-state activations into a single scalar called the Geometric Fragility Score (GFS). It identifies a recurring low-rank safety subspace across model families. Direction ablations verify that removing directions in this subspace weakens refusal.

Result: Applied to 21 instruction-tuned models (6 alignment recipes, 3B–32B parameters). GFS identifies, before any fine-tuning, which initially safe model retains the most refusal after small-scale LoRA fine-tuning.

Conclusion: GFS is a practical pre-deployment diagnostic for flagging fragile refusal behavior without needing to run an attack, enabling detection of alignment fragility before it is exploited.

Abstract: Alignment tuning is meant to make harmful-request refusal robust, yet this safety behavior can be erased by a small set of benign fine-tuning examples. This is a deployment risk for open-weight models because a checkpoint can pass refusal tests at release time and later lose refusal under low-cost downstream fine-tuning. Prior work has established these refusal failures, but existing studies do not show how to detect this fragility in the aligned model itself before an attack or fine-tuning intervention is run. We introduce Skin-Deep, a geometric diagnostic that detects alignment fragility directly from the aligned model's hidden-state activations before such an intervention is run and compresses the layer-wise safety geometry into a single scalar, the Geometric Fragility Score (GFS). Applied to twenty-one instruction-tuned models spanning six alignment recipes and 3B--32B parameters, Skin-Deep reveals a recurring low-rank safety subspace across model families. Direction ablations show that removing directions in this subspace weakens harmful-request refusal, providing causal evidence that the recovered geometry underlies refusal behavior. Crucially, GFS identifies, before any fine-tuning, the initially safe model that retains the most refusal after small-scale LoRA fine-tuning. These results establish GFS as a practical pre-deployment diagnostic for flagging fragile refusal behavior without running an attack.

</details>


### [504] [VISTA Architect: A graph database-oriented health AI system demonstrated in multidisciplinary tumor boards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22692)
*Tuomo Kiiskinen,Jason Fries,Philip Adamson,David Wu,Timothy John Ellis-Caleo,Aaron Fanous,Balasubramanian Narasimhan,Joel Neal,Sylvia Plevritis,Manuel A. Rivas*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: VISTA Architect is a database-oriented AI architecture that precomputes clinically abstracted timelines from EHRs using a two-layer knowledge graph (MEDS Graph + TOA), eliminating repeated raw-text processing. It achieved 96.4% accuracy on thoracic oncology tumor board evaluations and reduced preparation time to ~2.2 minutes for 30 patients.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Direct long-context prompting and RAG methods for clinical LLM applications miss temporal relationships, incur high cost and latency from repeated raw-text reprocessing, and cannot efficiently reconstruct longitudinal patient histories for complex multidisciplinary decision-making like tumor boards.

Method: Two-layer architecture: (1) MEDS Graph — source-faithful knowledge graph preserving granular EHR structure with full provenance; (2) Timeline Object Architecture (TOA) — graph-guided LLM extraction that synthesizes deduplicated, temporally coherent clinical events. Precomputation of clinical summaries eliminates query-time reprocessing with traversal to source documentation only when verification is needed.

Result: 96.4% accuracy (mean 9.75/10) on 15 tumor board-salient variables across 1,180 patients (17,700 evaluations; 95% CI 96.1-96.7%), surpassing BM25 RAG baseline and recent LLM clinical extraction benchmarks. Agentic interface reduced preparation for 30-patient cohort to ~2.2 minutes without accuracy loss.

Conclusion: VISTA Architect demonstrates that database-oriented precomputation of clinical timelines can overcome limitations of RAG and long-context LLM approaches for longitudinal EHR analysis. The modular design supports adaptation to other specialties, though validation beyond thoracic oncology remains future work.

Abstract: We introduce VISTA Architect, a database-oriented AI architecture for integrating large language models (LLMs) with longitudinal electronic health records (EHRs). At ingestion, it transforms complex clinical documentation into a persistent, provenance-linked knowledge graph, eliminating repeated reprocessing of raw records at query time. The architecture has two layers: a source-faithful MEDS Graph preserving granular EHR structure with full provenance, and a clinically abstracted Timeline Object Architecture (TOA) that uses graph-guided LLM extraction to synthesize a concise timeline of deduplicated, temporally coherent clinical events. This addresses key limitations of direct long-context prompting and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), which often miss temporal relationships and incur high cost and latency from repeated raw-text processing. By precomputing clinical synthesis once, downstream queries access an organized patient state and traverse to source documentation only when detailed verification is needed.
  We demonstrate the system in multidisciplinary thoracic oncology tumor boards at Stanford Medicine, where precise reconstruction of patient histories is critical. Across 1,180 patients, VISTA Architect achieved 96.4% accuracy (mean 9.75/10) on 15 tumor board-salient variables (17,700 evaluations; 95% CI 96.1-96.7%), surpassing a matched BM25 RAG baseline and recent benchmarks for LLM-based clinical extraction. An agentic interface reduced preparation for a 30-patient held-out cohort to about 2.2 minutes without sacrificing accuracy. While configured here for thoracic oncology, the modular design adapts to other specialties through customizable event definitions, episode structures, and agentic tools; validation beyond thoracic oncology remains future work.

</details>


### [505] [Safety-Aware Evaluation of LLM-Generated Driver Intervention Messages through Multi-Task Risk Fusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22706)
*Keito Inoshita*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes DSAIS, a domain-specific metric for evaluating driver intervention systems, and an end-to-end LLM-based framework integrating multi-task recognition outputs. Achieves high reliability (ICC 0.798-0.840) and significant effect sizes (Cohen's d > 1.5), with multi-task integration improving contextual relevance by 9.1%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing driver intervention systems rely on auditory alerts and fixed templates, failing to leverage multi-task recognition outputs. General-purpose metrics like BLEU and BERTScore cannot capture intervention-specific quality dimensions such as risk-urgency alignment, cognitive load, and driver acceptability.

Method: Proposes DSAIS (Driver Safety-Aware Intervention Score) evaluating five dimensions via hybrid rule-based computation and LLM Judge evaluation. An end-to-end framework integrates four-task recognition outputs into an LLM through risk fusion, state history management, and dynamic prompt construction.

Result: DSAIS achieves ICC 0.798-0.840 across three architecturally distinct judges and Cohen's d > 1.5 across all control conditions. Multi-task integration improves contextual relevance by 9.1% over rule-based baselines. Driver emotion recognition is the most critical upstream factor. Compact local LLMs (7B-9B) achieve quality superior to API-based models.

Conclusion: The proposed framework and metric provide practical design guidelines for in-vehicle deployment, with sub-score decomposition revealing gains that aggregate scoring masks and each framework component contributing to contextual relevance.

Abstract: Existing driver intervention systems rely on auditory alerts and fixed templates, failing to leverage multi-task recognition outputs. General-purpose metrics such as BLEU and BERTScore cannot capture intervention-specific quality dimensions including risk-urgency alignment, cognitive load, and driver acceptability. In this paper, we propose the Driver Safety-Aware Intervention Score (DSAIS), a domain-specific metric evaluating five dimensions through a hybrid architecture combining lightweight rule-based computation with LLM Judge evaluation, together with an end-to-end framework integrating four-task recognition outputs into an LLM through risk fusion, state history management, and dynamic prompt construction. Experiments on the AIDE dataset with five models and seven conditions demonstrate that DSAIS achieves ICC 0.798-0.840 across three architecturally distinct judges and Cohen's d > 1.5 across all control conditions. Multi-dimensional sub-score analysis quantifies the contextual adaptability gap between rule-based and LLM-based systems, revealing that multi-task integration improves contextual relevance by 9.1% over rule-based baselines. Ablation experiments demonstrate that each framework component contributes to contextual relevance, with sub-score decomposition revealing gains that aggregate scoring masks. Driver emotion recognition is identified as the most critical upstream factor, and compact local LLMs (7B--9B parameters) achieve quality superior to API-based models, providing practical design guidelines for in-vehicle deployment.

</details>


### [506] [Beyond Penalizing Mistakes: Stabilizing Efficiency Training in Large Reasoning Models via Adaptive Correct-Only Rewards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22716)
*Jungseob Lee,Seungyoon Lee,Seongtae Hong,Minhyuk Kim,Chanjun Park,Heuiseok Lim*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ACOER (Adaptive Correct-Only Efficiency Reward) stabilizes efficiency-aware optimization for LLM reasoning by applying brevity bonuses only to correct answers, preventing reward collapse and over-compression, achieving 60%+ token reduction while maintaining accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Training large language models to reason efficiently is critical, but integrating length-penalizing rewards into GRPO often triggers reward collapse, degrading reasoning capabilities.

Method: ACOER isolates brevity bonuses to correct completions, and prevents stochastic compression via dynamic budget normalization and control-loop penalty adjustments.

Result: Improves overall accuracy compared to the base model while reducing token generation by over 60% across diverse mathematical reasoning benchmarks.

Conclusion: ACOER establishes a fundamentally stable approach for efficiency-aware optimization by robustly preventing both deterministic reward collapse and stochastic compression collapse.

Abstract: Training large language models to reason efficiently is a critical challenge. While integrating length-penalizing rewards into Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) aims to reduce verbosity, it frequently triggers reward collapse, severely degrading reasoning capabilities. Through a systematic evaluation of various reward configurations, we identify the root mechanism: GRPO's group normalization creates divergent advantages when incorrect answers receive continuous length penalties. Consequently, methods penalizing the length of incorrect answers are structurally prone to collapse under sustained optimization. Furthermore, restricting penalties exclusively to correct answers avoids this primary failure, but leaves the model susceptible to a stochastic collapse driven by response over-compression. To robustly prevent both failure modes, we propose ACOER (Adaptive Correct-Only Efficiency Reward). ACOER eliminates the structural penalty loop by isolating brevity bonuses to correct completions and prevents stochastic compression via dynamic budget normalization and control-loop penalty adjustments. Evaluated across diverse mathematical reasoning benchmarks, ACOER improves overall accuracy compared to the base model while reducing token generation by over 60%, establishing a fundamentally stable approach for efficiency-aware optimization.

</details>


### [507] [Text Dictates, Music Decorates: Energy-based Attention for Editable Dance Motion Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22726)
*Seong Jong Yoo,Siyuan Peng,Felix Gu,Stratis Aloimonos,Cornelia Fermüller*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: STREAM is a modality-decoupled diffusion transformer for choreographic motion generation that separates text (semantic structure) and music (rhythmic timing) conditioning pathways to prevent modality collapse, achieving state-of-the-art alignment while preserving editable choreographic semantics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing dance generation models are black boxes that suffer from modality collapse—dense acoustic rhythms overwhelm sparse text prompts—destroying user controllability. There is a need for a controllable, interpretable system that respects both semantic intent and musical timing.

Method: STREAM uses a modality-decoupled diffusion transformer with: (1) Adaptive Layer Normalization (AdaLN) for global text semantics dictating kinematic structure, and (2) a Bimodal Energy-Based Attention Module (BEAM) for routing features to musical beats without overwriting semantics. Also introduces Motorica++ dataset and Exchange Evaluation Protocol with Editable Dance Score (EDS).

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art alignment between motion and music while perfectly preserving choreographic semantics, with proposed EDS rigorously quantifying zero-shot editability.

Conclusion: Positioning AI as a controllable, collaborative partner for artistic direction rather than merely a reactive motion synthesizer.

Abstract: Choreographic motion generation poses unique challenges for AI, demanding precise semantic control over complex, temporally structured, and expressive full-body dynamics. While existing models can synthesize motion from music, they remain largely black boxes. Conversely, attempting to condition generation on both text and music frequently leads to modality collapse, where dense acoustic rhythms overwhelm sparse semantic text prompts, destroying user controllability. To resolve this spatial-temporal conflict, we propose STREAM (Structural-Temporal Rhythmic Energy-based Attention for Motion), a modality-decoupled diffusion transformer. STREAM strictly separates conditioning pathways: global text semantics dictate the kinematic structure via Adaptive Layer Normalization (AdaLN), while a novel Bimodal Energy-Based Attention Module (BEAM) routes these features to the musical beat without overwriting the semantics. We further introduce Motorica++, a newly curated dataset enriched with domain-specific dance vocabulary and frame-level semantic annotations from existing Motorica dataset. Additionally, to rigorously quantify zero-shot editability, we propose the Exchange Evaluation Protocol and Editable Dance Score (EDS). Through extensive experiments, STREAM achieves state-of-the-art alignment between motion and music while perfectly preserving choreographic semantics, positioning AI not merely as a reactive synthesizer, but as a controllable, collaborative partner for artistic direction. The source code and datasets are available at https://github.com/SeongJong-Yoo/STREAM.

</details>


### [508] [Closed-loop Auto Research for Molecular Property Prediction: Discovering and Certifying Generalizable Improvements](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22731)
*Jingjie Ning,Xiaochuan Li,Ji Zeng,Chenyan Xiong,Guolin Ke*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper investigates whether automated research improvements in molecular property prediction (via feature engineering, model architecture changes, and external evidence) generalize beyond validation signal. Across 36 endpoints, gains are often non-transferable: model search gains drop from 0.041 (validation) to 0.003 (test), curated data shows negative transfer (-0.019 on test), while a routed pipeline achieves positive held-out gains (up to 0.042). Contamination filtering is necessary but insufficient for transfer. A matched-trial AutoML control fails to reproduce agent-level model interventions, and the pipeline remains competitive with a large pretrained 3D model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether closed-loop Auto Research systems that edit representations, model code, and acquire external evidence produce improvements that generalize beyond the validation signal used to select them, rather than overfitting to the validation proxy.

Method: Isolates three Auto Research axes (features, models, external evidence) under file-level ablation lock. Evaluates across 36 molecular property prediction endpoints (TDC, Polaris, MoleculeNet). Uses a routed pipeline selecting best validation axis per endpoint, with contamination filtering and matched-trial AutoML control.

Result: Routed pipeline achieves positive held-out gains of 0.013, 0.011, and 0.042 across suites. Model search shows severe drop (0.041 val → 0.003 test). Curated data shows negative transfer (-0.019 test). Contamination filter necessary but insufficient. AutoML control fails to reproduce agent-level model interventions (0.006 vs 0.042). Pipeline competitive with 84M-parameter pretrained 3D model.

Conclusion: Separating discovery from held-out certification is a domain-agnostic lesson for closed-loop systems optimizing a proxy for a held-out quantity. Non-transfer signatures (validation-test divergence) must be explicitly monitored, and contamination filtering alone does not guarantee generalizable gains.

Abstract: Closed-loop Auto Research extends automated machine learning from fixed-dataset fitting to changing the research workflow, with language-model agents editing representations and model code and acquiring external evidence. Molecular property prediction spans many small endpoints. We ask whether this action space yields improvements generalizing beyond the validation signal selecting them.
  We isolate three Auto Research axes, features, models, and external evidence, under a file-level ablation lock attributing each gain to one axis over a strong baseline. Across 36 endpoints in three benchmark suites we score each selected configuration once on a held-out test whose labels the search never read. A routed pipeline taking each endpoint's best validation axis reaches positive held-out gains of 0.013, 0.011, and 0.042, the transferable axis differing by suite, data on TDC, model on Polaris, feature and model on MoleculeNet. The largest model-search gain falls from 0.041 on validation to 0.003 on test, while curated data reaches 0.022 but negative 0.019 on test, two non-transfer signatures. Curated external data raises held-out CYP2C9-substrate performance by 0.17 and half-life by 0.08, admitted through a contamination filter rejecting same-source files overlapping 64 to 89 percent of test structures, necessary but not sufficient for transfer. A matched-trial automated machine learning control did not reproduce the agent's code-level model intervention, reaching 0.006 against 0.042, and the pipeline stays competitive with an 84M-parameter pretrained 3D model on the shared training split.
  The experiments stay within molecular property prediction, but separating discovery from held-out certification is a domain-agnostic lesson for any closed-loop system optimising a proxy for a held-out quantity.

</details>


### [509] [Learning Filters with Certainty](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22786)
*Yuval Banoun,Daniel Sadoc Menasche,Ori Rottenstreich*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes using the certainty information from Counting Bloom Filter counters (beyond binary membership indications) to improve the performance of Bloom Filter-ML hybrid architectures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hash collisions in Bloom filters cause uncertainty in positive membership indications, but this certainty signal is typically discarded. The authors argue that certainty information can be useful, particularly in systems combining Bloom filters with machine learning models.

Method: The work focuses on Counting Bloom Filters (CBFs), which maintain counters instead of bits. The counters provide additional information beyond binary membership indications, enabling estimation of the certainty of positive membership indications. This certainty signal is then exploited in hybrid architectures that integrate Bloom filters with ML models.

Result: The paper demonstrates how the certainty signal from CBF counters can be effectively utilized in Bloom Filter-ML combined architectures, though specific quantitative results are not detailed in the abstract.

Conclusion: Revealing and leveraging the certainty information inherent in Counting Bloom Filter counters can enhance the performance of systems that combine Bloom filters with machine learning, suggesting a novel direction for improving hash-based data structure-ML hybrid systems.

Abstract: Hash-based data structures such as Bloom filters are widely used in network systems for tasks including caching, anomaly detection, and machine learning pipelines. They typically provide binary indications of whether an element belongs to a set of interest, e.g., the contents of a cache. When uncertainty arises due to hash collisions, a positive indication is returned to avoid false negatives. We argue that the certainty associated with such indications can itself be useful information. This work focuses on Counting Bloom Filters (CBFs), a Bloom-filter variant that maintains counters rather than bits. Besides supporting insertions and deletions, these counters provide additional information that can be used to estimate the certainty of positive membership indications. We show how this certainty signal can be exploited in architectures that combine Bloom Filters with machine learning (ML) models.

</details>


### [510] [The Origins of Stochasticity: Comprehensive Investigations on Uncertainty Quantification for Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22792)
*Xiang-Jun Ou,Shuang Liang,Xin-Yu Hu,Rong-Hao Huang,Jing Wang,Shao-Qun Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes a granular uncertainty taxonomy for LLMs (input-level, parameter-level, token-level, decoding-process sources), categorizes UQ methods into Bayesian, ensemble, consensus-based, and single-pass approaches, and empirically evaluates 21 methods across Qwen3, Llama 3.2, and DeepSeek-V3 on TriviaQA, GSM8K, and HumanEval. Key findings: UQ effectiveness depends on task type; consensus-based methods (Deg, EigV) perform best; larger models show lower uncertainty (scaling law).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing uncertainty taxonomies (e.g., aleatoric vs. epistemic dichotomy) fail to capture the multi-component, multi-stage nature of LLM generation and cannot effectively evaluate various UQ methods.

Method: Proposes a granular four-level uncertainty taxonomy (input, parameter, token, decoding) and a corresponding four-category UQ method classification (Bayesian, ensemble, consensus-based, single-pass). Empirically evaluates 21 UQ methods across three LLM families on multiple benchmarks.

Result: (i) UQ method effectiveness is task- and setting-sensitive; (ii) consensus-based methods (Deg, EigV) consistently outperform others; (iii) larger models exhibit lower uncertainty, suggesting an empirical scaling law.

Conclusion: This work bridges the gap between theoretical uncertainty origins and practical LLM deployment, offering a systematic diagnostic tool for uncertainty quantification.

Abstract: Recent advancements in Large Language Models (LLMs) have enabled sophisticated reasoning and content generation, yet their inherent stochasticity poses significant challenges for ensuring predictive credibility. While traditional uncertainty taxonomy paradigms, such as the dichotomy of aleatoric and epistemic uncertainties, provide conceptual foundations, they often fail to capture the multi-component and multi-stage nature of LLM generation and struggle to evaluate the effectiveness of various Uncertainty Quantification (UQ) methods. In this paper, we propose a granular uncertainty taxonomy that systematically attributes LLM uncertainty into input-level, parameter-level, token-level, and decoding-process sources. Correspondingly, we categorize existing UQ methods into Bayesian, ensemble, consensus-based, and single-pass approaches. Furthermore, we introduce a comprehensive evaluation framework covering diverse generation settings and metrics. We empirically evaluate 21 typical UQ methods across three prominent LLM families, including Qwen3, Llama 3.2, and DeepSeek-V3, on benchmarks such as TriviaQA, GSM8K, and HumanEval. Our experimental results demonstrate that (i) the effectiveness of UQ methods is sensitive to task types and generation settings; (ii) consensus-based methods, typed Deg and EigV, consistently outperform other UQ approaches; and (iii) larger model scales correlate with lower uncertainty estimates, suggesting an empirical scaling law for LLM uncertainty. This work bridges the gap between theoretical origins and practical deployment, providing a versatile diagnostic tool for systematically quantifying uncertainty in LLM applications.

</details>


### [511] [A Formula-Driven Survey and Research Agenda for On-Policy Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22793)
*Bowen Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This survey studies On-Policy Distillation (OPD) for LLMs as a feedback-to-update problem, developing a taxonomy from direct distributional losses and policy-gradient-style log-ratio updates. It distinguishes temporal credit assignment from vocabulary-level probability routing, proposes GAE-OPD and Counterfactual Routed OPD (CR-OPD), and maps diagnostics, failure modes, and open problems onto the same framework.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing work conflates multiple mechanisms under OPD. The authors aim to reframe OPD as a feedback-to-update problem rather than a single loss family, and to disentangle temporal credit assignment from vocabulary routing—two often conflated mechanisms—to better understand stability and effectiveness.

Method: A formula-driven taxonomy based on two routes: direct distributional losses (e.g., KL divergence) and policy-gradient-style log-ratio updates. The taxonomy organizes methods by state compatibility, support construction, temporal credit, vocabulary-level probability routing, gates/weights, and regularization. The paper further separates temporal credit (how log-ratio returns weight actions across a rollout) from vocabulary routing (where probability mass moves when negative feedback suppresses a sampled token), and proposes GAE-OPD (value-based log-ratio returns) and CR-OPD (counterfactual routing toward teacher-supported, student-reachable alternatives).

Result: The taxonomy reveals that OPD effectiveness depends on factors beyond KL direction or teacher access. The temporal-credit/vocabulary-routing distinction yields bias boundaries for immediate, return-to-go, discounted, and baseline-corrected estimators. GAE-OPD and CR-OPD are proposed as principled solutions. The paper also provides a reporting checklist and maps actionability diagnostics, failure mechanisms, and case studies onto the feedback-to-update variables.

Conclusion: OPD should be understood via a structured taxonomy of feedback-to-update design choices. Separating temporal credit from vocabulary routing clarifies stability issues and motivates new algorithms (GAE-OPD, CR-OPD). The framework provides actionable guidance for practitioners and opens concrete research directions.

Abstract: On-policy distillation (OPD) trains an LLM on states induced by the current or recent student policy: the student generates complete or partial rollouts, a teacher or self-teacher scores the resulting tokens under their generated contexts, and dense log-probability, logit, or distributional signals are converted into post-training updates. This survey studies OPD as a feedback-to-update problem rather than a single loss family. We develop a formula-driven taxonomy from two routes -- direct distributional losses and policy-gradient-style log-ratio updates -- and use it to organize core methods, verifier- or outcome-guided hybrids, industrial reports, framework implementations, failure modes, and stabilization recipes under explicit evidence boundaries. The taxonomy shows that OPD effectiveness depends not only on KL direction or teacher access, but also on state compatibility, support construction, temporal credit, vocabulary-level probability routing, gates and weights, and regularization. We further separate two mechanisms often conflated in sampled-token OPD stability discussions. Temporal credit asks how teacher-student log-ratio returns should weight sampled actions across a rollout; vocabulary routing asks where probability mass should move when negative feedback suppresses a sampled token. This distinction yields bias boundaries for immediate, return-to-go, discounted, and baseline-corrected estimators, motivates GAE-OPD as a value-based hypothesis for log-ratio returns, and motivates Counterfactual Routed OPD (CR-OPD) for routing probability mass toward teacher-supported, student-reachable alternatives. We close by mapping actionability diagnostics, failure mechanisms, case studies, open problems, and a reporting checklist onto the same feedback-to-update variables.

</details>


### [512] [Measuring Behavior Portability in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22797)
*Tianjia Dong,Nadav Kunievsky,James A. Evans*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMs exhibit behavioral portability failures: their decision-making mappings learned in one payoff-equivalent environment do not reliably transfer to structurally identical but superficially different environments, quantified via a formal framework with worst-case bounds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs show sensitivity to surface-level changes across payoff-equivalent decision environments, making suite-based evaluation fragile and raising the question of behavioral portability—whether a behavioral mapping learned in one environment is informative for another with the same incentive structure.

Method: A formal framework that fits an interpretable behavioral model on pooled data from source environments, evaluates out-of-sample prediction on held-out target environments against an oracle, and quantifies portability via a loss-agnostic measure with worst-case bounds.

Result: Controlled experiments across seven canonical economic decision problems reveal substantial and systematic portability losses.

Conclusion: Behavioral characterizations of LLMs obtained in one decision environment cannot be assumed to transfer reliably to structurally equivalent alternatives.

Abstract: Large language models are increasingly deployed as autonomous decision makers, yet the behavioral mapping they exhibit can vary substantially across decision environments that are payoff-equivalent by construction-environments that share identical payoff-relevant structure but differ in surface presentation. This sensitivity renders suite-based evaluation fragile and raises a fundamental question of behavioral portability: how well does a behavioral mapping learned in one decision environment informative on another that preserves the same underlying incentive structure? We introduce a formal framework to measure this property. Our protocol fits an interpretable behavioral model on data pooled from a set of source environments and evaluates its out-of-sample predictive performance in a held-out target environment, benchmarking against an oracle trained directly on target data. Portability is quantified via a loss-agnostic measure that delivers worst-case bounds on the performance of the induced prediction-action mapping in the target environment. In controlled experiments spanning seven canonical economic decision problems, we document substantial and systematic portability losses, suggesting that behavioral characterizations of LLMs obtained in one decision environment cannot be assumed to transfer reliably to structurally equivalent alternatives.

</details>


### [513] [AI-Assisted Help-Seeking Trajectories in Programming Education from an SRL-Informed Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22809)
*Boxuan Ma,Huiyong Li,Gen Li,Li Chen,Atsushi Shimada,Shin'ichi Konomi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This study analyzes AI-assisted help-seeking trajectories of 71 university students in introductory Python programming using 1,290 prompts and 17,190 code submissions. It finds that most students used AI reactively for troubleshooting rather than for planned, self-regulated problem-solving. While trajectory patterns did not significantly relate to task scores, they differed substantially in the number of code submissions required, suggesting the importance of how help-seeking trajectories develop over correctness alone.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing work on generative AI in programming education has largely focused on correctness, usability, or overall usage frequency, with insufficient attention to how student-AI help-seeking interactions unfold over time. This study addresses the gap by analyzing the structure and developmental patterns of AI-assisted help-seeking trajectories.
