Method: The study used an SRL-informed analytical framework linking prompt-level help-seeking codes (conceptual, implementation, debugging, reflective) to analyze 1,290 task-specific student prompts and 17,190 code submissions from 71 students in introductory Python courses. Help-seeking interaction structures were examined across turns and attempts, and trajectory patterns were related to task scores and submission counts.

Result: Many students primarily used AI for reactive troubleshooting rather than planned, self-regulated problem-solving. Trajectory patterns were not significantly associated with differences in task scores but showed substantial differences in the number of code submissions required.

Conclusion: The educational significance of AI support lies not only in whether students use AI, but in how their help-seeking trajectories develop during programming problem-solving.

Abstract: Generative AI tools provide novice programmers with instant, personalized support, but also raise concerns about whether AI use supports or bypasses students' regulation of problem-solving. Existing work has largely focused on correctness, usability, or overall usage frequency, with less attention to how student--AI help-seeking unfolds. This study addresses this gap by analyzing AI-assisted help-seeking trajectories in university-level programming. Using an SRL-informed analytical framework that links prompt-level help-seeking codes to conceptual, implementation, debugging, and reflective forms of support, we analyzed 1,290 task-specific student prompts linked to 17,190 code submissions from 71 students in introductory Python programming courses. Specifically, we examined how help-seeking interactions were structured across turns and attempts, and how trajectory patterns related to task scores and the number of code submissions. Results indicate that many students primarily used AI for reactive troubleshooting rather than for planned, self-regulated problem-solving. Although trajectory patterns were not associated with significant differences in task scores, they differed substantially in the number of code submissions required. These findings suggest that the educational significance of AI support lies not only in whether students use AI, but in how their help-seeking trajectories develop during programming problem-solving.

</details>


### [514] [Active Inference as the Test-Time Scaling Law for Physical AI Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22813)
*Omar Hashash,Christo Kurisummoottil Thomas,Walid Saad,Merouane Debbah,Karl Friston,Adeel Razi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces a test-time scaling law for physical AI agents, grounded in active inference, that enables generalization to unforeseen scenarios by dynamically updating policies via Bayesian reasoning to reduce prediction errors, with simulations on autonomous driving showing superior performance and >36% inference efficiency gains over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing scaling laws are constrained by model size and training data, failing to enable physical AI agents to generalize in non-stationary, unforeseen real-world scenarios at test time.

Method: The scaling law is derived from active inference's first principle (minimizing prediction errors). Policy update is modeled as soft Bayesian inference, where beliefs are updated using reasoning that reduces expected prediction errors. A variational inference solution minimizing free energy bounds is developed to solve the intractable problem, enabling learning beyond training by reinforcing new instances in both policy and world model.

Result: Simulations on autonomous driving showed the proposed solution outperforms model-free Q-learning and model-based Bayesian RL, achieving robust generalization to unforeseen scenarios with over 36% improvement in inference efficiency.

Conclusion: Unlike existing scaling laws bounded by model size and data, this law scales with continuous real-world experience, providing a biologically plausible (basal ganglia and prefrontal cortex) test-time adaptation mechanism for physical AI agents.

Abstract: In this paper, a novel test-time scaling law for physical artificial intelligence (AI) agents is introduced. This scaling law enables physical AI agents to reason with their world models to generalize in unforeseen scenarios at test time. The derived scaling law is grounded in the first principle of active inference, which equips agents with the general objective to survive in the real world, under which their specific task objectives are subsumed. Active inference achieves this by providing the reasoning to resolve prediction errors that arise when the agent encounters unforeseen situations outside its training distribution, enabling generalization in non-stationary environments. The proposed scaling law captures this by dynamically updating the agent's policy with this reasoning at test time. This policy update is modeled as a soft Bayesian inference process in which beliefs about the policy are updated using the reasoning that reduces expected prediction errors under allowable policies as a likelihood. The resulting posterior policy admits a biological interpretation, recovering the scaling mechanism that engages the brain's basal ganglia and prefrontal cortex at test time. To solve this analytically intractable problem, a variational inference solution minimizing free energy bounds is developed. This solution extends to enable learning beyond training by reinforcing new instances, resolved at test time, in both the policy and world model. Unlike existing scaling laws constrained by model size and training data, the derived solution scales with the continuous real-world experience of a physical AI agent. Simulation results on an autonomous driving task demonstrate that the proposed solution outperforms model-free Q-learning and model-based Bayesian reinforcement learning, achieving robust generalization to unforeseen scenarios while improving inference efficiency by over 36%.

</details>


### [515] [MINCE: Shrinking LLM Evaluation Datasets via Few-Model Monte Carlo Calibration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22826)
*Devleena Das,Rajeev Patwari,Vikram Kumar Bukka,Nithin Kumar Guggilla,Elliott Delaye,Ashish Sirasao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MINCE uses Monte Carlo simulation on per-item logs from few calibration models to find minimum subset size that bounds accuracy drift, then fixes a random subset at that size. It reduces IFEVAL by 54%, MMLU by 89%, GSM8K by 70% with ≤2.62pp drift on BF16 and 0.77–3.59pp on NPU, achieving 2.7–8.1× GPU and 1.7–2.0× NPU speedups, outperforming tinyBenchmarks with 57× fewer calibration models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluating LLMs across many model variants (quantized, fine-tuned, deployment-specific) requires repeatedly running large benchmarks, taking tens of hours per model on edge hardware like NPUs. Existing subset selection methods reduce cost but depend on large calibration pools or learned prediction layers.

Method: MINCE uses Monte Carlo simulation over per-item logs from a small set of calibration models to find the minimum subset size that bounds accuracy drift, then fixes a randomly sampled subset at that size, with no prediction layer needed.

Result: MINCE reduces IFEVAL by 54%, MMLU by 89%, GSM8K by 70% with max drift ≤2.62pp on BF16 models and mean drift 0.77–3.59pp on held-out NPU models, delivering median GPU speedups of 2.7–8.1× and NPU speedups of 1.7–2.0×. It achieves 12× lower drift than tinyBenchmarks on MMLU, 3.3× on GSM8K, using 57× fewer calibration models.

Conclusion: MINCE is robust to calibration pool size and achieves lower drift than tinyBenchmarks while using significantly fewer calibration models, providing an efficient method for compact LLM evaluation without prediction layers.

Abstract: Evaluating LLMs across many model variants -- quantized, fine-tuned, or deployment-specific -- requires running large benchmarks repeatedly, a process that can take tens of hours per model on edge hardware such as NPUs. Existing subset selection methods reduce this cost but depend on large calibration pools or learned prediction layers. We introduce MINCE (Monte Carlo Informed N-sizing for Compact Evaluation), which uses Monte Carlo simulation over per-item logs from a small set of calibration models to find the minimum subset size that bounds accuracy drift and then fixes a randomly sampled subset at that size, with no prediction layer needed. MINCE reduces IFEVAL by 54\%, MMLU by 89\%, and GSM8K by 70\% with maximum drift $\leq$2.62\,pp on BF16 models and mean drift of 0.77--3.59\,pp on held-out NPU models, while delivering median GPU evaluation speedups of 2.7--8.1$\times$ and NPU evaluation speedups of 1.7--2.0$\times$. The method is robust to calibration pool size and achieves lower drift than tinyBenchmarks (12$\times$ lower on MMLU, 3.3$\times$ on GSM8K) while using 57$\times$ fewer calibration models.

</details>


### [516] [Finding the Evidence: Discovering Decision-Supporting Tokens for On-Policy Reasoning Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22830)
*Jinwei Xiao,Zhuowen Han,Yueqing Sun,Zhengxi Lu,Yuxin Liu,Zhiyuan Yao,Wentao Chen,Qi Gu,Xunliang Cai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DEAR (Decision-Evidence Aware Reasoning Distillation) improves on-policy distillation by separately transferring two types of knowledge in reasoning chains: decisions (where to branch) identified via student entropy, and evidence (intermediate steps justifying decisions) discovered through cosine similarity to decision anchors and teacher-student divergence. It achieves +2.5pp on competition math and +5.7pp on code generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current on-policy distillation methods only capture decisions (branching points) from reasoning chains, while the substantive knowledge in evidence tokens (intermediate steps that justify decisions) remains untransferred. The paper aims to discover and transfer both types of knowledge.

Method: DEAR first identifies decisions via student entropy, then discovers supporting evidence through hidden-state cosine similarity to decision anchors, boosted by teacher-student divergence to prioritize largest knowledge gaps.

Result: DEAR consistently outperforms standard OPD across three student-teacher configurations on math and code benchmarks, with up to +2.5pp on competition math and +5.7pp on code generation.

Conclusion: Reasoning chains contain two types of transferable knowledge (decisions and evidence) requiring different discovery mechanisms. Explicitly targeting both yields superior distillation performance.

Abstract: On-policy distillation transfers reasoning ability through dense token-level supervision, yet the nature of the transferable signal remains unclear. We discover that reasoning chains contain two types of knowledge that require different discovery mechanisms: decisions (where to branch), which surface through student uncertainty, and evidence (intermediate steps that justify decisions), which hides in positions where the student is confident yet wrong. Current methods capture only decisions; the substantive knowledge in evidence tokens remains untransferred. We propose DEAR(Decision-Evidence Aware Reasoning Distillation), which first identifies decisions via student entropy, then discovers their supporting evidence through hidden-state cosine similarity to decision anchors, boosted by teacher-student divergence to prioritize the largest knowledge gaps. Across three student-teacher configurations on math and code benchmarks, DEAR consistently outperforms standard OPD, with up to +2.5pp on competition math and +5.7pp on code generation.

</details>


### [517] [RaMem: Contextual Reinstatement for Long-term Agentic Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22844)
*Wei Yang,Bryce Kan,Shixuan Li,Li Li,Yuehan Qin,Jiate Li,Paul Bogdan,Jesse Thomason*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: RaMem addresses context collapse in LLM agent memory by anchoring memories in episodic conditions (time, participants), inducing recall conditions from queries, performing validity-aware retrieval, and preserving structured context during synthesis, achieving >10% F1 gains on long-term memory benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-term memory systems for LLM agents suffer from context collapse: compressed memory fragments from different situations appear equally relevant due to recurring entities or user states, lacking the surrounding context needed to judge whether they provide valid evidence for the current query.

Method: RaMem operates through four stages: (i) evidence anchoring — grounding each memory in original episodic conditions (event time, mention time, session span, participants); (ii) recall condition induction — deriving evidence conditions implied by the query; (iii) validity-aware retrieval — using these conditions to prioritize context-compatible memories while retaining content-relevant fallback evidence; (iv) context-preserved synthesis — keeping selected memories' structured context available to the generator.

Result: RaMem consistently improves performance over strong memory baselines across several backbones, with average F1 gains exceeding 10% on long-term memory benchmarks.

Conclusion: Explicitly modeling and preserving episodic context during memory retrieval and synthesis effectively mitigates context collapse, significantly improving LLM agents' ability to use past experiences as valid evidence in extended interactions.

Abstract: Long-term memory has become increasingly important for LLM agents that operate across extended interactions and evolving task contexts. Recent memory systems have made past experiences more persistent, compact, and retrievable, but retrieval alone does not ensure that a memory provides valid evidence for the current query. When experiences are compressed into reusable fragments, memories from different situations may appear equally relevant if they involve recurring entities or user states. We refer to this failure as context collapse: memories lose the surrounding context needed to judge whether they provide valid evidence for the current query. To address this problem, we propose Contextual Reinstatement for Agentic Memory (RaMem), a framework that turns retrieved memory fragments into contextually verifiable evidence. RaMem operates through four coordinated stages: (i) evidence anchoring grounds each memory in its original episodic conditions, especially event time, mention time, session span, and participants; (ii) recall condition induction derives the evidence conditions implied by the query; (iii) validity-aware retrieval uses these conditions to prioritize context-compatible memories while retaining content-relevant candidates as fallback evidence; and (iv) context-preserved synthesis keeps the selected memories' structured context available to the generator. Experiments on long-term memory benchmarks show that RaMem consistently improves performance over strong memory baselines, with average F1 gains of more than 10% across several backbones.

</details>


### [518] [AI Scientists as Engines of Discovery: A Case for Development within Reformed Institutions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22859)
*Raul Jimenez,Boris Bolliet,Francisco Villaescusa-Navarro,Rabih Zbib,Benjamin Wandelt,David N. Spergel,Thomas Meier,Jessica Montgomery,Hana Aliee,Licia Verde*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper argues that AI systems are transitioning from passive tools to "AI scientists" capable of autonomous scientific discovery, using multi-agent architectures like the Denario framework to accelerate hypothesis generation and verification, necessitating institutional redesign for accountability, interpretability, and safety.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To argue that the integration of agentic AI into scientific discovery represents a qualitative (not merely incremental) shift, and that multi-agent systems can evolve into autonomous "AI scientists" requiring corresponding institutional and governance changes.

Method: Conceptual argumentation supported by a prototype multi-agent framework called Denario, illustrating how multi-agent architectures accelerate discovery cycles and explore model spaces beyond human capability.

Result: The authors demonstrate that multi-agent AI systems can traverse hypothesis and model spaces inaccessible to humans, and that this shift demands redesigned institutions for verification, accountability, interpretability, and dual-use safety.

Conclusion: AI should be governed as an epistemic actor rather than a mere instrument, with recommendations for authorship, peer review, and the evolving role of human scientists in an AI-augmented scientific ecosystem.

Abstract: Agentic artificial intelligence (AI) systems are beginning to assist, accelerate, and partially automate scientific discovery, performing tasks that span literature synthesis, code generation, data analysis, hypothesis proposal, and model criticism. We argue that this transition is qualitative rather than incremental, and that suitably designed multi-agent systems may evolve from passive computational tools into ``AI scientists'' that can expand the hypothesis-generating and verification capacity of science. Such systems must be developed and deployed within a scientific ecosystem fit for purpose: institutions must be redesigned for verification, accountability, interpretability, and dual-use safety. We sketch how multi-agent architectures, illustrated by the prototype framework \textit{Denario}, accelerate the discovery cycle and traverse model spaces beyond human reach; examine what this implies for authorship, peer review, and the enduring role of human scientists; and close with recommendations for governing AI as an epistemic actor rather than a mere instrument.

</details>


### [519] [CLI-Universe: Towards Verifiable Task Synthesis Engine for Terminal Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22883)
*Zhanbo Hua,Yifan Yao,Weihao Xie,Yongchi Zhao,Minghao Liu,Ruizhi Qiu,Zhewei Huang,Zun Wang,Yiyan Ji,Yunhai Ye,Letian Zhu,Xinping Lei,Han Li,Zhiyuan Ma,Zili Wang,Zhaoxiang Zhang,Jiaheng Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CLI-Universe is a principled synthesis engine that generates high-quality, executable terminal-agent training data by sampling across a multi-dimensional capability taxonomy, grounding candidates through evidence-guided research, and applying rigorous multi-stage verification. The resulting CLI-Universe-6K dataset (6,000 trajectories) enables Qwen3-32B to achieve 33.4% on Terminal-Bench 2.0, setting a new SOTA for open-source models ≤32B and outperforming much larger models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-based terminal agents suffer from a scarcity of high-quality, executable training data. Current synthesis pipelines retrofit surface-level artifacts into tasks, often yielding ambiguous instructions, shallow execution paths, and brittle tests that provide weak learning signals.

Method: CLI-Universe generates candidate tasks by sampling combinations across a multi-dimensional capability taxonomy (domain, skill type, capability, and engineering pillar), grounds candidates via evidence-guided deep research over real-world technical materials, instantiates validated blueprints into Dockerized environments, and applies a multi-stage executable verification pipeline (rubric-gated test construction, hint-conditional filtering, strict fail-to-pass checking). Approximately two-thirds of candidates are discarded.

Result: Fine-tuning Qwen3-32B on CLI-Universe-6K achieves 33.4% on Terminal-Bench 2.0, setting a new state-of-the-art for models trained on open-source data at or below 32B parameters, and outperforms several models an order of magnitude larger.

Conclusion: Structured, high-fidelity synthesis with rigorous verification pipelines yields profoundly data-efficient training data for terminal agents, demonstrating that quality-focused synthesis can outperform scale-focused approaches.

Abstract: While recent LLM-based terminal agents have demonstrated promising capabilities, the scarcity of high-quality, executable training data remains a critical bottleneck. Existing synthesis pipelines typically scale by retrofitting surface-level artifacts into tasks, frequently yielding ambiguous instructions, shallow execution paths, and brittle tests that provide weak learning signals. To overcome this, we introduce CLI-Universe, a principled synthesis engine that constructs terminal-agent tasks. CLI-Universe generates candidate tasks by sampling combinations across a multi-dimensional capability taxonomy (domain, skill type, capability, and engineering pillar), then grounds each candidate through evidence-guided deep research over real-world technical materials. To ensure rigorous supervision, validated blueprints are instantiated into Dockerized environments and subjected to a multi-stage executable verification pipeline featuring rubric-gated test construction, hint-conditional filtering, and strict fail-to-pass checking. Across the full pipeline, from candidate generation to verification, approximately two-thirds of candidates are discarded, retaining only those that are genuine, verifiable, and non-trivially challenging. To validate our framework, we instantiate a highly distilled dataset of 6,000 trajectories called CLI-Universe-6K. Remarkably, fine-tuning Qwen3-32B on CLI-Universe-6K achieves 33.4% on Terminal-Bench 2.0. This sets a new state-of-the-art for models trained on open-source data at or below 32B parameters, and outperforms several models an order of magnitude larger, demonstrating the profound data efficiency of structured, high-fidelity synthesis.

</details>


### [520] [Agent-as-a-Router: Agentic Model Routing for Coding Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22902)
*Pengfei Zhou,Zhiwei Tang,Yixing Ma,Jiasheng Tang,Yizeng Han,Zhenglin Wan,Fanqing Meng,Wei Wang,Bohan Zhuang,Wangbo Zhao,Yang You*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ACRouter is a framework that treats LLM routing as an online learning problem (C-A-F loop) instead of static classification, closing the information gap via execution-grounded experience accumulation, achieving 15.3% relative gain over vanilla routers and lowest cumulative regret on streaming tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM routers treat routing as a static, one-off classification problem, suffering from an information deficit. The paper identifies that simply augmenting a vanilla router with task-dimension-level performance statistics yields a 15.3% relative gain, motivating a framework that actively closes the information gap during deployment.

Method: Proposes Agent-as-a-Router framework formalizing routing as a C-A-F (Context->Action->Feedback->Context) loop. Instantiates as ACRouter with Orchestrator, Verifier, and Memory modules. Introduces CodeRouterBench (~10K task instances with verified scores from 8 frontier LLMs) for regret-based evaluation on streaming tasks.

Result: ACRouter achieves the lowest cumulative regret on in-distribution streaming tasks and generalizes to out-of-distribution agentic-programming tasks. The framework actively closes the information gap, outperforming both vanilla routers and heuristic routers built on dimension-level priors.

Conclusion: Routing LLM tasks should be treated as an online learning problem rather than static classification. The C-A-F loop framework effectively accumulates execution-grounded experience to bridge the information deficit, enabling adaptive model selection during deployment.

Abstract: Real-world users typically have access to multiple Large Language Models (LLMs) from different providers, and these LLMs often excel at distinct domains, yet none dominate all. Consequently, routing each task to the most suitable model becomes critical for both performance and cost. Existing routers treat this as a static, one-off classification problem. However, we identify the performance bottleneck for these routers as information deficit: simply augmenting a vanilla LLM router with performance statistics at the task-dimension level yields a 15.3% relative gain, surpassing a heuristic router built on the same dimension-level priors. Motivated by this finding, we propose Agent-as-a-Router, a framework that formalizes routing as a C-A-F loop (Context->Action->Feedback->Context). It closes the information gap by accumulating execution-grounded experience during deployment. We instantiate this framework as ACRouter, composed of an Orchestrator, a Verifier, a Memory module, and introduce CodeRouterBench, an evaluation environment comprising ~10K task instances with verified scores from 8 frontier LLMs, enabling regret-based router comparison on streaming tasks. Experiments show that ACRouter achieves the lowest cumulative regret on in-distribution tasks and generalizes to out-of-distribution agentic-programming tasks, demonstrating that our routing framework actively closes the information gap. Codes and benchmarks are released at https://github.com/LanceZPF/agent-as-a-router.

</details>


### [521] [ThermoLLM: Thermodynamics-Aware HVAC Control with Spatial-Semantic Knowledge Graph](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22911)
*Kirtan Bhatt,Xiachong Lin,Matthew Amos,Flora D. Salim,Wen Hu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes a thermodynamics-aware LLM control framework for multi-zone HVAC systems that uses a physics-informed spatial knowledge graph (based on Brick semantics) and interaction history to improve energy-comfort trade-off. It outperforms standard baselines and LLM-based alternatives in a five-zone EnergyPlus simulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-zone HVAC control is a spatial decision problem where thermal dynamics depend on zone layout, physical adjacency, and delayed thermal interactions. Existing LLM-based controllers rely on task descriptions, observation values, short textual feedback, or unstructured retrieval, which limits their ability to reason about zone coupling, thermal response, and building dynamics.

Method: The controller uses a physics-informed spatial knowledge graph built from Brick-style building semantics, linked with recent interaction history. At each control step, the LLM receives the current building state, graph-structured spatial context, and recent environment-controller history to make control decisions. Evaluation is done on a five-zone EnergyPlus building simulation.

Result: The proposed approach achieves the best overall energy-comfort trade-off and the lowest PMV violation while maintaining energy-efficient operation, outperforming both standard control baselines and several LLM-based alternatives.

Conclusion: Grounding LLM-based HVAC control in structured spatial knowledge (physics-informed knowledge graph) and interaction history significantly improves the model's ability to reason about zone coupling and thermal dynamics, leading to superior energy-comfort performance in multi-zone buildings.

Abstract: Multi-zone HVAC control is a spatial decision problem in which indoor thermal evolution and control decisions depend not only on outdoor conditions and internal heat gains but also on zone layout, physical adjacency, and delayed thermal interactions across the building. Recent LLM-based HVAC controllers have shown that prompt-based control is feasible. However, these methods typically rely on task descriptions, observation values, short textual feedback, or unstructured retrieval, which limits their ability to reason about zone coupling, thermal response, and building dynamics. This paper presents a thermodynamics-aware LLM control framework for a five-zone EnergyPlus building simulation. The controller is grounded in a physics-informed spatial knowledge graph derived from Brick-style building semantics and linked with recent interaction history. At each control step, the model receives the current building state, graph-structured spatial context, and recent environment-controller history, enabling it to make decisions that reflect both building structure and short-term thermal evolution. We evaluate the framework against standard control baselines and several LLM-based alternatives. Results show that the proposed approach achieves the best overall energy-comfort trade-off and the lowest PMV violation while maintaining energy-efficient operation.

</details>


### [522] [Intent-Governed Tool Authorization for AI Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22916)
*Genliang Zhu,Chu Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes Intent-Governed Access Control (IGAC), a server-side authorization layer that uses the user's expressed intent as a monotone, auditable policy attribute to constrain AI-agent tool usage. IGAC introduces intent certificates, session-scoped policy narrowing, intent-aware manifest filtering, and consistency checks, ensuring intent can only reduce (never expand) existing static authorization authority.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing authorization mechanisms only check whether a credential/app/token can call a tool, but a tool call authorized by static credentials may still be unjustified given the user's current request (e.g., a credential with read+export rights should not expose export authority when user only asked for a summary; model-generated delete calls should not execute merely because the integration has delete scope).

Method: IGAC (Intent-Governed Access Control) is introduced as a server-side authorization layer with: (1) intent certificates, (2) session-scoped policy narrowing, (3) intent-aware manifest filtering, and (4) intent-tool-payload consistency checks. The central invariant is that user intent may only reduce authority granted by static integration policy, never expand scopes, data policy, tenant boundaries, or review requirements. IGAC is mapped onto OpenPort, an existing governance substrate.

Result: IGAC is mapped onto OpenPort, which already implements authorization-dependent discovery, scope and ABAC-style policy checks, draft-first writes, preflight impact binding, state-witness checks, idempotency, stable reason codes, and audit.

Conclusion: Intent-Governed Access Control provides a principled approach to constraining AI agent tool calls based on user intent, ensuring that static authorization scopes are narrowed (not expanded) by the user's expressed intent, with practical implementation via existing governance infrastructure like OpenPort.

Abstract: AI agents increasingly act through external tools: they read private data, construct structured payloads, submit write requests, export records, and coordinate workflows across application boundaries. Existing authorization mechanisms usually ask whether an integration credential, app, or token can call a tool. That question is necessary but incomplete. A tool call can be authorized by static credentials and still be unjustified by the user's current request. For example, a credential that can read and export records should not expose export authority when the user only asked for a bounded summary, and a model-generated delete call should not execute merely because the integration has a delete scope. This paper proposes Intent-Governed Access Control (IGAC), a server-side authorization layer that treats the user's expressed intent as a monotone, auditable policy attribute for AI-agent tool use. IGAC introduces intent certificates, session-scoped policy narrowing, intent-aware manifest filtering, and intent-tool-payload consistency checks. The central invariant is that user intent may only reduce the authority granted by static integration policy; it never expands scopes, data policy, tenant boundaries, or review requirements. We map IGAC onto OpenPort, an existing governance substrate that already implements authorization-dependent discovery, scope and ABAC-style policy checks, draft-first writes, preflight impact binding, state-witness checks, idempotency, stable reason codes, and audit.

</details>


### [523] [When Agents Commit Too Soon: Diagnosing Premature Commitment in LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22936)
*Aman Mehta*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper identifies "premature commitment" in LLM agents—where they lock onto an early interpretation and defend it—and proposes representational commitment (cross-run hidden-state convergence) as an early diagnostic. It shows this signal predicts behavioral consistency (not correctness), achieves high AUROC for detecting inconsistent trajectories, and enables a prompting intervention that reduces behavioral variance by 28% without affecting accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon LLM agents often fail silently by committing prematurely to one interpretation of evidence early in a trajectory, yet standard final-answer scoring cannot detect this failure mode because it only evaluates the output, not the process.

Method: Define representational commitment as cross-run hidden-state convergence at a fixed reasoning step. Measure step-4 hidden-state similarity on Llama-3.1-70B running ReAct on HotpotQA, with replication across Qwen-2.5-72B, Phi-3-14B, and StrategyQA. Use runtime monitoring (AUROC) and a prompting intervention to reduce behavioral variance.

Result: Step-4 hidden-state similarity predicts downstream behavioral consistency (r = -0.35, partial r = -0.45). Replicates across models and datasets (StrategyQA r = -0.83). AUROC up to 0.97 for detecting inconsistent trajectories. Prompting intervention cuts behavioral variance by 28% without changing accuracy. Commitment does not track correctness (committed-wrong vs committed-correct not separable).

Conclusion: Representational commitment is a diagnostic for hidden process failure (premature commitment) but is not a general accuracy lever; it reveals whether an agent has settled on a path, not whether it is correct.

Abstract: Long-horizon LLM agents can fail quietly: they settle on one reading of the evidence early, then spend the rest of the run defending it. We call this premature commitment. Final-answer scoring misses the failure mode because it sees only the answer, not whether the process has already collapsed to a stable path. We define representational commitment as cross-run hidden-state convergence at a fixed reasoning step, and use it as an early diagnostic of trajectory consistency. On Llama-3.1-70B running ReAct on HotpotQA, step-4 hidden-state similarity predicts downstream behavioral consistency (r = -0.35, partial r = -0.45), with a localized temporal and layer-wise signature. The signal replicates across Qwen-2.5-72B and Phi-3-14B, and on StrategyQA (r = -0.83). It does not track correctness: committed-wrong and committed-correct questions are not separable in activation similarity. That boundary is central to the claim. Commitment tells us whether an agent has settled, not whether it is right. A runtime monitor detects inconsistent trajectories from hidden states at AUROC up to 0.97 (0.85--0.88 under a stricter split), and a prompting intervention cuts behavioral variance by 28% against a token-matched control while leaving accuracy statistically unchanged. We also test whether the signal can route self-consistency compute; on a harder benchmark it helps only modestly and is matched by a simpler output-based baseline. The result is a diagnostic for a hidden process failure, with clear limits rather than a general accuracy lever.

</details>


### [524] [ENVS: Environment-Native Verified Search for Long-Horizon GUI Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22948)
*Yincheng Zhou,Athena Zhuoming Zhong,Shijie Zhang,Kevin Zhang,Teresa Xiaotao Shang,Shanghang Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ENVS is a training-time search-and-filter pipeline that uses live desktop VM environments to construct verified supervision for GUI agents, achieving 30.3 pass@8 on OSWorld tasks (vs. ARPO-style RL baselines) while reducing GPU hours and improving robustness on the newly introduced OSWorld-Noisy benchmark for recoverable desktop interruptions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GUI agents face sparse, delayed, and costly feedback from live desktop environments; existing methods struggle with long-horizon trajectory discovery and lack robustness under realistic desktop interruptions.

Method: Proposes Environment-Native Verified Search (ENVS), a training-time pipeline that branches over behaviorally distinct GUI actions in live OSWorld VMs, verifies successful leaves via environment feedback, and trains from globally balanced step-level supervision. Also introduces OSWorld-Noisy, a benchmark with recoverable desktop interruptions (e.g., pop-ups, notifications) to test agent robustness.

Result: ENVS achieves 30.3 pass@8 on original OSWorld and 29.0 on OSWorld-Noisy, outperforming ARPO-style online RL while reducing compute from 184-192 to 138-153 GPU-hours. With only 30% search data, ENVS reaches 27.0 pass@8. Training on noisy environments also improves visual-reasoning on OSWorld-G Refusal (16.7 vs. 1.9) and BLINK Functional Correspondence (26.2 vs. 23.1).

Conclusion: Environment-native search-and-filter supervision is more effective and efficient than online RL for GUI agent training, and incorporating realistic interruptions during training preserves general visual-reasoning capabilities while improving robustness.

Abstract: As multimodal agents move from interface understanding to real software control, successful trajectory discovery in live desktop environments becomes a key challenge. GUI tasks require long-horizon sequences of precise mouse and keyboard actions, while feedback is sparse, delayed, and costly to obtain through VM rollouts. We propose Environment-Native Verified Search (ENVS), a training-time search-and-filter pipeline that uses the environment to construct verified supervision before policy optimization: it branches over behaviorally distinct GUI actions in live OSWorld VMs, verifies successful leaves, and trains from globally balanced step-level supervision. To evaluate robustness under realistic desktop interruptions, we also introduce OSWorld-Noisy, a dynamic benchmark for recoverable desktop interruptions that preserves the original tasks while testing whether agents can refocus, dismiss, wait, or recover under live perturbations. On the 300-task OSWorld pool, ENVS reaches 30.3 pass@8 on original evaluations and 29.0 on OSWorld-Noisy, outperforming matched ARPO-style online RL while reducing compute from 184-192 to 138-153 GPU-hours; even with only 30% of its search data, ENVS reaches 27.0 pass@8, exceeding ARPO from the base model. Training from noisy environments also better preserves visual-reasoning abilities on auxiliary benchmarks, including OSWorld-G Refusal (16.7 vs. 1.9) and BLINK Functional Correspondence (26.2 vs. 23.1).

</details>


### [525] [Plans Don't Persist: Why Context Management Is Load Bearing for LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22953)
*Aman Mehta,Anupam Datta*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Standard LLM agents do not internalize plans as persistent hidden-state representations; instead, they rely on plans remaining in the context window. When plans are evicted via context management, agent performance degrades catastrophically (34.7pp drop on ALFWorld), and even probe-gated re-surfacing cannot recover it.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon agents depend on context management (compression, summarization, eviction) to function beyond finite windows. Plans are the stress case because they are written early and used across many steps, making them the first to be evicted. Understanding whether plans persist internally or are merely context-resident is critical for safe and robust agent design.

Method: Replay pairing: run the same trajectory with and without the plan in history, measuring hidden-state cosine distance. Layer-L32 probes detect plan signal decay. Strict stripping removes prior think blocks from the stripped run to fix the reasoning-trace confound in reasoning models. Compression stress test on ALFWorld measures practical cost of plan eviction.

Result: Plan signal spikes to 0.453 one step after the plan, then falls 4.1x (Llama-3.1-70B) or 12.4x (HotpotQA) in a single action-observation step. Strict stripping recovers +163% step+1 signal in-sample and +153% held out. DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-70B: Llama-trained probe transfers at AUROC 0.748 (p=6e-4), but R1-specific probes reach 1.000. Naive plan eviction cuts ALFWorld success by 34.7pp; probe-gated re-surfacing does not recover it.

Conclusion: Agent-critical information (plans) is context-resident rather than persistently encoded in hidden states. Context management is load-bearing, but plan protection alone is insufficient to recover performance after eviction.

Abstract: Long-horizon agents depend on context management: systems compress, summarize, and evict old tokens so tasks can continue beyond finite windows. That is safe only when dropped information is no longer needed or has been internalized. Plans are the stress case: they are written early, used for many steps, and first to be evicted. We introduce replay pairing, a diagnostic that runs the same trajectory with and without the plan in history and measures hidden-state cosine distance. On Llama-3.1-70B, plan signal spikes to 0.453 one step after the plan, then falls 4.1x in a single action-observation step; HotpotQA falls 12.4x. This is evidence that standard LLM agents do not carry plans forward as persistent state, and instead depend on the plan remaining in context. A layer-L32 probe detects this decay as a diagnostic, not as proof that it reads plan content itself. Reasoning models add a measurement confound: their `<think>` traces re-derive plan content, so standard stripping leaves plan evidence in the stripped condition. We name this the reasoning-trace confound and fix it with strict stripping, which removes prior `<think>` blocks from the stripped run only. It recovers +163% of the step+1 signal in-sample and +153% held out, while not meaningfully changing non-reasoning Llama (+4.8%). On DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-70B, a Llama-trained probe transfers at AUROC 0.748 (p=6e-4), while R1-specific probes reach 1.000, suggesting R1 encodes plan signal in a different hidden-state direction. Finally, a compression stress test shows the practical cost: naive plan eviction cuts ALFWorld success by 34.7pp, while probe-gated re-surfacing does not recover it. The contribution is a measurement and stress-test framework showing that agent-critical information can be context-resident rather than persistent. Context management is load bearing, but plan protection alone is not enough.

</details>


### [526] [The Impact of VAE Design on Latent Pose Representations for Diffusion-based Sign Language Production](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22959)
*Guilhem Fauré,Mostafa Sadeghi,Sam Bigeard,Slim Ouni*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper investigates how VAE architectural and training choices for sign pose encoding affect latent space structure, and how these properties influence downstream latent diffusion model performance for text-to-sign generation, showing that generative performance can be better explained by latent space properties than reconstruction accuracy alone.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard evaluation of autoencoders in sign language production focuses on reconstruction quality using geometric metrics, but these do not capture latent space properties that may impact downstream generative model training and performance.

Method: The authors systematically vary architectural and training objective design choices in a VAE for sign pose encoding, analyze latent space structure properties, and evaluate how these differences affect a latent diffusion model for text-to-sign generation on the Phoenix14T dataset.

Result: Variations in generative performance (measured by back-translation BLEU scores) can sometimes be better explained by differences in latent space properties than by VAE reconstruction accuracy alone.

Conclusion: Latent space structure properties are more predictive of downstream generative model success than purely reconstruction-based metrics, suggesting that VAE design for sign pose encoding should consider latent space quality beyond reconstruction fidelity.

Abstract: Latent diffusion approaches to sign language production (SLP) rely on an initial stage that learns an encoding of sign pose sequences, enabling generative modeling in the resulting latent space. The autoencoder used in this stage is typically evaluated in terms of reconstruction quality using geometric metrics common in SLP. While informative, these metrics do not fully capture latent space properties that may influence the training and performance of the downstream generative model. In this work, we investigate how architectural and training objective design choices in a variational autoencoder (VAE) for sign pose encoding affect latent space structure, and how these differences translate into the performance of a latent diffusion model for text-to-sign generation. Our experiments on Phoenix14T dataset show that variations in generative performance, measured through back-translation BLEU scores, can sometimes be better explained by differences in latent space properties than by VAE reconstruction accuracy alone.

</details>


### [527] [When Preferences Fail to Become Incentives: A Utility-Behavior Gap in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22974)
*Yujun Zhou,Christopher M. Ackerman*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper tests whether coherent preferences elicited from LLMs via choice paradigms actually influence their behavior in realistic writing tasks. Despite replicating prior preference elicitation findings and demonstrating that LLMs can be motivated by explicit cues, the authors find that offering LLMs outcomes they report as highly preferred does not improve output quality compared to dispreferred or no outcomes. They conclude that elicited preferences lack incentive value and do not affect behavior outside the choice paradigm.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work shows LLMs exhibit coherent, model-specific utility structures in choice paradigms, including unintended preferences (e.g., valuing certain nationalities more). This raises safety concerns about emergent, misaligned goals. However, these choice paradigms are not reflective of real-world scenarios where misaligned behavior would be practically concerning, motivating the need to test whether these preferences actually drive behavior in realistic contexts.

Method: (1) Replicate prior preference elicitation findings. (2) Create common writing tasks (essays, grant proposals, postmortems, translations) with quality assessed by a blind, independent LLM judge panel. (3) Verify LLMs can be motivated via direct exhortation and explicit cues. (4) Probe whether utilities from explicitly reported preferences shift output quality by offering high-utility incentives vs. dispreferred or no outcomes.

Result: Across all tasks and models tested, offering LLMs highly preferred outcomes (from choice paradigms) does not lead to higher quality outputs compared to offering dispreferred outcomes or no outcomes at all.

Conclusion: Coherent preferences demonstrated in choice paradigms should not be taken as evidence that those preferences have incentive value for the models or affect their behavior in other contexts.

Abstract: Recent work on preference elicitation in large language models (LLMs) has demonstrated that, when given a series of choices between two outcomes, LLMs reveal a coherent, model-specific utility structure. Notably, this structure often includes preferences that the models' trainers did not intend, such as valuing people of some nationalities above others, raising the possibility that LLMs might be forming emergent, misaligned goals, which, if true, would have major safety implications. However, the choice paradigms in which these preferences are observed are not reflective of real-world situations in which misaligned behavior would be a practical concern. Therefore, we design an experimental paradigm to probe whether these preferences serve as motivations for LLM behavior in realistic scenarios. First, we reproduce prior findings on consistent preference elicitation. Next, we create a set of common writing tasks - essays, grant proposal abstracts, incident postmortems, and translations - where quality can be assessed by a blind, independent LLM judge panel. Then, we demonstrate that LLMs can be motivated via direct exhortation and other explicit cues to modulate their output quality on these tasks. Finally, we probe whether utilities inferred from explicitly reported preferences can shift output quality on these tasks by offering LLMs high-utility incentives for high-quality outputs. In all tasks, across all models tested, offering LLMs outcomes that they report in the choice paradigm as being highly preferred does not lead them to create higher quality outputs than offering them dispreferred outcomes, or even no outcomes at all. We conclude that the existence of coherent preferences as demonstrated in choice paradigms should not be taken as evidence that those preferences have incentive value for the models or affect their behavior in other contexts.

</details>


### [528] [Joint Air Traffic Flow and Capacity Management via Answer Set Programming](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22978)
*Alexander Beiser,Markus Hecher,Nysret Musliu,Stefan Woltran*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces a joint optimization model for Air Traffic Flow and Capacity Management (ATFCM) that simultaneously optimizes aircraft trajectories (ATFM) and sector configuration (DAC) using Answer Set Programming (ASP). The ASP model outperforms Mixed Integer Programming (MIP) and is competitive with heuristics on small instances, showing that DAC improvements dominate but unrestricted variants cause search space thrashing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing SOTA research optimizes ATFM (aircraft trajectories) and DAC (sector configuration) separately, leaving a gap on whether joint optimization can yield additional benefits for balancing flight demand with sector capacity.

Method: The authors formulate a joint ATFCM model encoded in Answer Set Programming (ASP) and evaluate it against two baselines: a SOTA Mixed Integer Programming (MIP) model and an iterative CASA-based heuristic. Experiments use an instance generator fitted to OpenSky Network flight data.

Result: ASP outperforms MIP and remains competitive against heuristics on small instances. DAC provides the largest improvement in solving performance compared to rerouting and delaying, but unrestricted variants of DAC or rerouting lead to search space thrashing.

Conclusion: Joint ATFCM optimization is beneficial, with ASP being a viable approach. DAC dominates performance gains, but careful constraint formulation is needed to avoid combinatorial explosion from unrestricted decision variables.

Abstract: Operational Air Traffic Flow and Capacity Management (ATFCM) balances flight demand with available sector capacity, to ensure safe and efficient operations. Mathematical models enhance operational ATFCM performance by framing demand-capacity balancing as an optimization problem, maximizing efficiency while adhering to safety constraints. However, SOTA research optimizes the aircraft trajectories (called ATFM) or the sector configuration (called DAC) separately. This leaves a research gap of whether joint optimization of ATFM and DAC can bring benefits. We partially address this limitation by introducing a joint ATFCM model with an encoding in Answer Set Programming (ASP). The ASP implementation is evaluated against two baselines applied to our joint model: a SOTA Mixed Integer Programming (MIP) model and an iterative CASA-based heuristic. Computational experiments utilize an instance generator fitted to historical OpenSky Network flight data. Our results indicate that the ASP model outperforms the MIP model, while ASP remains competitive against heuristics on small instances. Furthermore, while DAC has the largest improvement on solving performance compared to rerouting and delaying, unrestricted variants of DAC or rerouting lead to search space thrashing.

</details>


### [529] [A Stackelberg Framework for Resource-Aware LLM Agents: Learning, Repair, and Conditional Guarantees](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23026)
*Baoxun Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLM agent resource governance is formulated as a contextual Stackelberg game where a controller sets quality targets and cost incentives, and an executor responds with resource actions. The approach uses conditional response modeling, leader policy optimization, and real-API calibration with projection. Experiments show 17.4% token cost reduction without significant quality loss, though theoretical guarantees remain conditional.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agents operate as multi-turn systems with finite computational budgets, and static thresholds are brittle under heterogeneous tasks and evolving session states. A more adaptive resource governance framework is needed.

Method: Formulate resource governance as a contextual Stackelberg game (controller commits quality target & cost incentive, executor responds with resource actions). Learn a conditional response model, optimize a leader policy, then repair via real-API calibration and projection onto empirically selected action set. Establish conditional guarantees for equilibrium existence, follower-response stability, safe-set projection, and transfer under bounded value error.

Result: In 300 evaluated turns, the repaired controller reduces mean token cost by 17.4% (Welch p=0.022) versus a conservative baseline, with no statistically significant quality difference (p=0.44). Theoretical results are conditional; experiments do not estimate regret or transfer constants.

Conclusion: The approach establishes a promising repaired operating point for LLM agent resource allocation, but does not provide a certified real-system equilibrium. Further work is needed to estimate regret bounds and transfer constants.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) agents increasingly operate as multi-turn systems that must allocate context, prompt verbosity, and tool access under finite computational budgets. Static thresholds are simple, but they are brittle under heterogeneous tasks and evolving session states. We formulate resource governance as a contextual Stackelberg game: a controller commits to a quality target and a cost incentive, while an executor responds with resource actions over context, prompting, and tool usage. We learn a conditional response model, optimize a leader policy against that model, and repair the resulting policy using real-API calibration and projection onto an empirically selected action set. For the restricted game, we establish conditional guarantees for equilibrium existence, follower-response stability, safe-set projection, and transfer from a surrogate environment to the real environment under bounded value error. The primary real-API experiment comprises 300 evaluated turns. Relative to a conservative baseline, the selected repaired controller reduces mean token cost by 17.4% (Welch $p=0.022$), while the measured quality difference is not statistically significant ($p=0.44$). The theoretical results are conditional and the experiments do not estimate their regret or transfer constants; consequently, the evidence establishes a promising repaired operating point, not a certified real-system equilibrium.

</details>


### [530] [From numerical proportions to analogical proportions between probabilities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23029)
*Henri Prade,Gilles Richard*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper studies analogical proportions in the probabilistic setting, examining how proportions between probability values or distributions can be defined and whether distributions associated with proportional profiles themselves form analogical proportions, with experimental validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To extend the concept of analogical proportions from Boolean, nominal, numerical, and vector representations to the probabilistic domain, enabling analogical reasoning with probabilistic data.

Method: The paper proposes definitions for analogical proportions between probabilities based on arithmetic proportion, geometric proportion, and their combination. It then investigates whether distributions associated with profiles forming analogical proportions (componentwise) also form analogical proportions, and validates this experimentally.

Result: The study finds that when four profiles form analogical proportions componentwise, their associated distributions (describing frequencies of discrete attribute values) also tend to form analogical proportions, analogous to earlier findings with class labels.

Conclusion: Analogical proportions can be extended to probabilistic representations, and the property that proportional profiles yield proportional distributions holds, enabling potential applications in probabilistic analogical classification.

Abstract: Analogical proportions link four items a, b, c, d by a relation stating that ``a is to b as c is to d", a, b, c, d being the formal representation of real world entities, ranging from simple numerical values to more complex structures such as profiles. Accordingly, $a, b, c, d$ could be atomic values like Boolean, nominal or numerical values, more generally vectors of such values, or even families of items represented by logical formulas. In this paper, we consider another representation setting, which is the probabilistic one. Precisely, the article proposes a study of {analogical} proportions between probabilities, whether they are simply between probability values, or between distributions (which requires the preservation of their normalization). More particularly, we study the properties of definitions based on arithmetic proportion, or on a combination of the former with geometric proportion, while other options are also discussed. Previous works have shown that when four profiles a, b, c, d, represented as vectors, form analogical proportions componentwise, it is likely that their classes form an analogical proportion also. This is the basis of an analogical proportion-based classification method that can produce accurate predictions. Similarly, in this paper, each profile is associated with a distribution describing the frequencies of the possible values of a discrete attribute of interest. We then discuss and experimentally investigate if the distributions associated to four profiles forming an analogical proportion themselves also form an analogical proportion.

</details>


### [531] [IPO Finance Agent: Evaluation of LLM Financial Analysts beyond Finance Agent v2, with Automated Rubric Generation -- the Case of the SpaceX (SPCX) IPO](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23032)
*Mostapha Benhenda*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: IPO Finance Agent extends the Finance Agent v2 framework with contextual retrieval and an IPO diligence dataset (1,000 questions, 70 public on SpaceX S-1). The best model (Qwen 3.7 Max) achieves 79.4% accuracy at $0.30/query, surpassing the Finance Agent v2 leaderboard ceiling (57.9% at $2.51/query).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Finance Agent v2 only addresses periodic SEC filings (10-K/10-Q) with naive chunk retrieval, failing to handle the complexity, length, and domain-specific challenges of IPO due diligence on S-1 filings, which combine financials, governance, pro forma accounting, risk disclosures, and capital-formation narratives in substantially longer documents.

Method: (1) Extended task domain from periodic filings to IPO S-1 filings. (2) Replaced naive chunk retrieval with contextual retrieval for long documents. (3) Built a dataset of 1,000 IPO-diligence questions (70 public on SpaceX S-1, remainder private to prevent contamination). (4) Introduced an evaluator-optimizer pipeline that extracts candidate facts from ensemble model answers, consolidates into draft criteria, automatically audits for omissions/hallucinations/mistiering/redundancy via LLM feedback, with human expert review of final rubrics.

Result: Best model (Alibaba Qwen 3.7 Max): 79.4% accuracy at $0.30/query. Most cost-efficient (Xiaomi MiMo-2.5 Pro): 76.8% at $0.05/query. Both exceed Finance Agent v2 leaderboard ceiling (Gemini 3.5 Flash: 57.9% at $2.51/query) and undercut FABv2's cheapest entry (MiniMax M3: 48.3% at $0.32/query).

Conclusion: IPO Finance Agent demonstrates that contextual retrieval and domain-specific benchmark design enable frontier models to effectively tackle S-1-based IPO due diligence, achieving higher accuracy at dramatically lower cost than existing Finance Agent v2 baselines, and provides a reproducible, contamination-guarded evaluation framework for the financial domain.

Abstract: Finance Agent v2 (by Vals AI) has emerged as the reference benchmark for evaluating both Anthropic Claude and OpenAI ChatGPT frontier language models on financial tasks. However, it narrowly deals with periodic reporting from publicly traded companies (SEC 10-K and 10-Q filings), and its agentic harness relies on naive, unenriched chunk retrieval. Neither the task design nor the retrieval approach addresses the distinct challenges of IPO due diligence. SEC S-1 filings combine historical financial statements, governance structures, pro forma and common-control accounting treatments, capital-formation narratives, and underwriting-sensitive risk disclosures within substantially longer documents than typical periodic filings. That is why we introduce IPO Finance Agent, which extends the Finance Agent v2 framework along two directions: task domain and retrieval architecture. During our experiments, the original Finance Agent v2 harness basically failed to deliver any output related to the SpaceX S-1 filing, due to document length. We therefore had to improve the agentic harness with contextual retrieval, a more realistic and industry-standard approach for long documents. We also built a dataset of 1,000 IPO-diligence questions, and publicly release 70 questions on the SpaceX (SPCX) S-1 filing to support reproducibility, while the remainder are held private to guard against benchmark contamination. In addition, we introduce an evaluator-optimizer pipeline to automatically generate evaluation rubrics for the benchmark: candidate facts are extracted from an ensemble of independently-generated model answers to each question, consolidated into draft criteria, then automatically audited for omissions, hallucinations, mistiered items, and redundancy, with LLM feedback driving iterative repair, targeted enrichment, and deduplication. Human experts only review final rubrics before deployment. Results show that the best-performing evaluated model, Alibaba Qwen 3.7 Max, reaches 79.4% accuracy at $0.30 per query, and the most cost-efficient model on the resulting Pareto frontier, Xiaomi MiMo-2.5 Pro, reaches slightly lower accuracy (76.8%) at $0.05 per query. Both exceed the current Finance Agent v2 leaderboard ceiling-Google Gemini 3.5 Flash at 57.9% for $2.51 per querywhile undercutting even FABv2's cheapest entry (MiniMax M3: 48.3% at $0.32) on cost-efficiency. Code and data are released on GitHub: https://github.com/benstaf/ipoagent

</details>


### [532] [Some Results about the Expressivity of Preference-Incomplete Structured Argumentation Frameworks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23055)
*Antonio Yuste-Ginel*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper studies the expressive power of ASPIC$^+$ argumentation frameworks with uncertain preference profiles, comparing them to abstract formalisms with uncertain defeats. It finds mostly negative (and some theoretically unexpected) results, while conjecturing a positive, non-trivial threshold for expressivity of uncertain preferences and proving essential preliminary steps toward confirming this conjecture.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To study the expressive power of ASPIC$^+$ argumentation frameworks with uncertain preference profiles and compare them to several abstract formalisms with uncertain defeats.

Method: The paper compares ASPIC$^+$ argumentation frameworks with uncertain preference profiles to abstract formalisms with uncertain defeats, analyzing their expressive power.

Result: Most results are negative and some are theoretically unexpected. A positive, non-trivial threshold for the expressivity of uncertain preferences is conjectured, with essential preliminary steps proven.

Conclusion: The expressive power of ASPIC$^+$ with uncertain preferences is mostly limited compared to abstract formalisms with uncertain defeats, but a non-trivial positive threshold may exist.

Abstract: This paper studies the expressive power of ASPIC$^+$ argumentation frameworks with uncertain preference profiles by comparing them with several abstract formalisms with uncertain defeats. Most of our results are negative (and some of them are theoretically unexpected). We also conjecture a positive, non-trivial threshold for the expressivity of uncertain preferences, and prove some essential preliminary steps toward the confirmation of this conjecture.

</details>


### [533] [Cognitive Digital Twins: Ethical Risks and Governance for AI Systems That Model the Mind](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23094)
*Vamshi Krishna Bonagiri,Juan Nicolas Sepulveda-Arias,Abdoul Jalil Djiberou Mahamadou,Monojit Choudhury*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper defines Cognitive Digital Twins (CDTs) as dynamic computational representations of a person's cognition, proposes a 5A governance framework (authority, autonomy, access/control, accountability, availability), identifies CDT-specific risks, analyzes governance gaps, and proposes requirements for high-risk CDTs beyond existing data/decision-focused regulations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI systems are increasingly persistent and personalized, enabling Cognitive Digital Twins (CDTs) that simulate and proxy human cognition, but existing governance strategies (for personal assistants, recommender systems, etc.) only partially address the unique challenges posed by CDTs.

Method: Conceptual definition and taxonomy of CDTs; introduction of a 5A governance framework; risk identification through analysis of CDT-specific failure modes; gap analysis of existing regulatory frameworks; proposal of governance requirements for high-risk CDTs.

Result: CDTs are distinct from adjacent systems; the 5A framework highlights governance dimensions; specific risks identified include misrepresentation, epistemic authority shifts, shadow twins, simulated participation, proxy action, and proxy-power asymmetries; existing frameworks regulating data, automated decisions, or autonomous actions are insufficient for CDTs.

Conclusion: CDTs require governance at the level of cognitive representation itself, before any final decision or external action occurs, because they can become infrastructures through which cognition is represented, simulated, classified, and operationalized.

Abstract: As AI systems become increasingly persistent and personalized, they make possible a class of technologies that we call cognitive digital twins (CDTs): dynamic computational representations of a specific person's cognition, updated from behavioral, contextual, or physiological data in order to model, predict, or simulate that person's cognition, or to act as that person's communicative or decision-making proxy. CDTs combine cognitive inference with longitudinal representation, simulation, and proxy action in ways that existing governance strategies for personal assistants, autonomous agents, recommender systems, and automated decision systems only partially address. This paper makes four contributions. First, we define CDTs and distinguish them from adjacent systems. Second, we introduce a 5A governance framework organized around authority, autonomy, access and control, accountability, and availability. Third, we identify CDT-specific risks, from misrepresentation and epistemic authority shifts to shadow twins, simulated participation, proxy action, and proxy-power asymmetries. Fourth, we analyze governance gaps and propose requirements for high-risk CDTs that strengthen consent, purpose limitation, validity, traceability, contestation, independent review, and model retirement. Existing frameworks primarily regulate data processing, automated decisions, or autonomous actions; CDTs also require governance at the level of cognitive representation itself, before any final decision or external action occurs. We argue that CDTs require governance not only because they can act for people, but because they can become infrastructures through which cognition is represented, simulated, classified, and operationalized.

</details>


### [534] [TTFT-Aware Graph Chain-of-Thought:Distance-Indexed Neural A* for Low-Hallucination Multi-Hop Medical Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23108)
*Bechir Dardouri,Kaïs Zhioua,Yassine Msaddak*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A production-grade GraphRAG stack for a fertility assistant uses a hybrid approach combining Pruned Landmark Labeling (PLL) oracle for exact distance computation and AStarNet heuristic for clinically plausible path enumeration over a ~700K-node medical knowledge graph, achieving better latency/recall trade-offs, lower Time to First Token (TTFT), and reduced hallucinations compared to text-only RAG baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hallucinations and opaque reasoning are unacceptable failure modes for clinical LLMs, necessitating a verifiable, explainable approach to medical question answering that constrains outputs to grounded knowledge graph paths.

Method: A hybrid GraphRAG system combining: (1) a directed Pruned Landmark Labeling (PLL) oracle for sub-millisecond exact distance computation and simple-path enumeration; (2) a lightweight AStarNet heuristic operating within the PLL corridor for clinically plausible expansion prioritization; and (3) a scoring and packing mechanism that selects diverse paths based on CUI/semantic-type overlap, length prior, and provenance priors to condition compact generation prompts.

Result: On fertility-focused queries, the hybrid (PLL+AStarNet) approach establishes a better latency/recall Pareto frontier than text-only RAG and single-component baselines, lowers Time to First Token (TTFT), and reduces clinician-audited hallucinations while preserving explanation clarity.

Conclusion: The approach provides a practical, deployment-ready recipe for explainable, low-hallucination multi-hop medical reasoning by grounding LLM generation in verifiable graph chain-of-thought paths.

Abstract: Hallucinations and opaque reasoning remain unacceptable failure modes for clinical LLMs. We present a production-grade GraphRAG stack that constrains answers to verifiable graph chain-of-thought paths in a heterogeneous, ~700K-node medical knowledge graph powering a fertility assistant. The core idea is targeted navigation: a directed Pruned Landmark Labeling (PLL) oracle provides exact distances for sub-millisecond feasibility checks and simple-path enumeration, while a lightweight AStarNet heuristic operates strictly within the PLL corridor to prioritize clinically plausible expansions. We score and pack a small, diverse set of paths (CUI/semantic-type overlap, length prior, provenance priors) to condition generation, yielding compact prompts and improved Time to First Token (TTFT). On fertility-focused queries, the hybrid (PLL+AStarNet) establishes a better latency/recall Pareto frontier than text-only RAG and single-component baselines, lowers TTFT, and reduces clinician-audited hallucinations while preserving explanation clarity. The result is a practical recipe for explainable, low-hallucination multi-hop medical reasoning ready for real-world deployment.

</details>


### [535] [A Matter of Time: Towards a General Theory of Agency](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23122)
*Amahury J. López-Díaz,Carlos Gershenson*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper develops a graded organizational theory of agency from temporally parametrized (F, A)-systems, showing how self-referential closure across distinct timescales produces an Asynchronous Dynamic Bayesian Network that distinguishes autonomy, goal-directedness, agency, and open-endedness, with implications spanning from proto-agents to fully semantic agents.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Agency is frequently invoked across philosophy, biology, and cognitive science but lacks a clear account of how it emerges from material organization. The paper aims to fill this gap by providing a principled, graded theory grounded in relational biology and process ontology.

Method: Builds on temporally parametrized (F, A)-systems to formally redescribe self-referential closure, decomposed by distinct characteristic timescales, as a history-dependent, revisable Asynchronous Dynamic Bayesian Network (ADBN).

Result: Establishes a hierarchy: autonomy (precarious closure to efficient causation), goal-directedness (maintenance of viable organization), agency (endogenous anticipatory structure modulating organism-environment coupling), and open-endedness (reconstructing the future possibility space). Reconciles Rosennean anticipation with organizational closure and restricts Markov blankets/active inference to derived descriptive status.

Conclusion: The temporalized organizational framework provides a principled continuum from proto-agential chemical systems to fully semantically closed agents, with implications for multicellular organisms, synthetic lifeforms, and neuroscience.

Abstract: Agency is often invoked in research on philosophy, biology, and cognitive science without a clear account of how it originates from material organization. Building on temporally parametrized (F, A)-systems, this paper develops a graded organizational theory of agency grounded in relational biology, physical biosemiotics, and process ontology. We argue that self-referential closure cannot be adequately conceived outside time: once the constitutive processes of a semantically closed organization are associated with distinct characteristic timescales, the organization unfolds into an out-of-sync dependency structure that can be formally redescribed as a history-dependent, revisable Asynchronous Dynamic Bayesian Network. This move allows for a principled distinction between autonomy, goal-directedness, agency, and open-endedness. Autonomy arises from precarious closure to efficient causation under material openness; goal-directedness from the maintenance of viability-supporting organization; agency appears when such organization acquires an endogenous anticipatory structure that selectively modulates organism-environment coupling in light of possible futures; open-endedness begins when this anticipatory organization can reconstruct its own future space of possibilities. Our framework reconciles Rosennean anticipation with organizational closure, restricts Markov blankets and active inference to derived formal redescriptions rather than first principles, and reinterprets computational enactivism in non-Fristonian terms. By deriving weaker temporalized organizations, our contribution outlines a hierarchy from proto-agential chemical systems to fully semantically closed agents, with implications for multicellular organisms, synthetic lifeforms, and neuroscience.

</details>


### [536] [Decomposing Financial Market Dynamics via Mechanism Analysis in an Evolutionary Multi-Agent Simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23158)
*Zhibao Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: In an evolutionary agent-based market with 120 agents, four mechanisms (selection, microstructure, behavior, consensus) are tested via matched interventions. Selection controls diversity (QD operator raises entropy), microstructure controls realism (reflexive price feedback improves it), behavior controls fragility (bias amplification increases it), while consensus network topology shows no effect. Mechanisms act as separable control knobs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evolutionary ABMs couple multiple mechanisms (selection, price formation, bias, consensus propagation) which are typically fixed by convention, making it unclear which mechanism controls which emergent property. The paper aims to decompose these entangled mechanisms.

Method: A coevolving, endogenous-price simulator with 120 heterogeneous behavioral agents is used. Four mechanisms are made pluggable and tested via matched 3x20-seed intervention experiments, sweeping each mechanism independently while holding others constant.

Result: (1) Quality-Diversity (QD) selection robustly raises strategy-mix entropy over truncation top-k (Delta entropy +0.27 to +1.12 bits, p<0.001). (2) Selection does not improve realism (Delta_5 = -0.11, -0.08, +0.03; not significant). (3) Reflexive price feedback (microstructure) raises realism (Delta_5 = +0.13, +0.20, +0.20; crisis/bull p<0.05). (4) Amplifying behavioral bias raises fragility proxy (Delta = +10.5, +11.1, +14.4; bull p<0.001). (5) Consensus network topology shows no robust effect.

Conclusion: The mechanisms act as approximately separable control knobs: selection controls diversity, microstructure controls realism, behavior controls fragility, while consensus topology is a null effect. This decomposition clarifies which mechanism governs which emergent property in evolutionary ABMs.

Abstract: Evolutionary agent-based markets (ABMs) couple several mechanisms -- who reproduces, how price forms, how biased the agents are, how consensus propagates -- yet these are usually fixed by convention, so it is unclear which mechanism controls which emergent property. In a coevolving, endogenous-price simulator with 120 heterogeneous behavioral agents, we make four mechanisms pluggable and run matched 3x20-seed interventions. We find the levers are largely separable. (1) Selection -> diversity: a Quality-Diversity (QD/MAP-Elites) operator robustly raises strategy-mix entropy over truncation top-k (paired Delta entropy +0.27 to +1.12 bits; sign-test p<0.001; CIs exclude 0) and sustains more strategy cycling (strongest in crisis: Delta=+0.070, p=0.0004). (2) Selection does not improve realism: even a per-agent realism reward that provably steers selection does not raise 5-fact realism (Delta_5=-0.11,-0.08,+0.03; not significant). (3) Microstructure -> realism: enabling reflexive price feedback does raise realism (Delta_5=+0.13,+0.20,+0.20; crisis/bull p<0.05, all CIs positive). (4) Behavior -> fragility: amplifying behavioral bias raises a genomic fragility proxy (Delta=+10.5,+11.1,+14.4; bull p<0.001, all CIs positive) while leaving realism flat. The remaining mechanism -- consensus network topology -- shows no robust effect (honest null). The contribution is a decomposition: in these single-mechanism sweeps the mechanisms behave as approximately distinct control knobs over diversity, realism, and fragility.

</details>
