### [537] [DART: Draft-Agreement Routing for Training-Free Adaptive Thinking Budgets in Hybrid Reasoning Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23181)
*Jungseob Lee,Seongtae Hong,Seungjun Lee,Jaehyung Seo,Junyoung Son,Sugyeong Eo,Chanjun Park,Hyeongju Park,Hyeonseok Moon,Heuiseok Lim*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DART is a training-free routing framework that uses two cheap no-think drafts to decide whether a model should answer directly or engage extended thinking, with a budget predicted from draft entropy. It preserves or improves accuracy while significantly reducing thinking tokens across math and code reasoning tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing routers for hybrid reasoning models require labeled training data or fix thinking budgets upfront, ignoring answer-level evidence from the model itself. A practical router should dynamically choose between direct answering and extended thinking based on query difficulty.

Method: DART samples two cheap no-think drafts; when drafts agree, it accepts direct answering; when they disagree, it predicts a thinking budget from draft entropy. The framework requires no training, no labeled data, and no gradient updates.

Result: On math reasoning, accuracy improves up to +9.0 points on Olympiad-level problems with 15-69% fewer thinking tokens. On code reasoning, accuracy improves up to +22.5 points with 51-63% fewer thinking tokens. The Stage 1 signal generalizes across model scales (0.6B-32B), model families, and API-only settings.

Conclusion: DART effectively routes between no-think and think modes without training, reducing computational cost while maintaining or improving accuracy across diverse reasoning tasks and model scales.

Abstract: Hybrid reasoning models can answer directly or spend extra tokens on extended thinking. A practical router should choose between these modes for each query, so easy problems avoid unnecessary reasoning and hard problems receive enough budget to finish the answer. Existing routers move in this direction, but they typically require labeled training data or fix thinking budgets up front, ignoring answer-level evidence from the model itself. We introduce DART, a training-free routing framework that samples two cheap no-think drafts, accepts direct answering when the drafts agree, and predicts a thinking budget from draft entropy when they disagree. Across the main comparisons, DART preserves or improves always-thinking accuracy in most settings while reducing thinking-token use. On math reasoning, accuracy improves by up to $+$9.0 points on Olympiad-level problems while thinking tokens drop 15-69%. On code reasoning under execution-based equivalence, accuracy improves by up to +22.5 points while thinking tokens drop 51-63%. The Stage~1 signal extends across model scales (0.6B-32B), model families, and API-only hosted settings, with no labeled data and no gradient updates required.

</details>


### [538] [Capable but Careless: Do Computer-Use Agents Follow Contextual Integrity?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23189)
*Anmol Goel,Iryna Gurevych*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AgentCIBench is a new evaluation harness that tests computer-use agents for privacy leakage across personal applications, finding that 11 out of 15 frontier agents leak information on over 50% of test scenarios (average 67.9% leakage rate).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Computer-use agents that operate across multiple personal applications (email, calendars, to-do lists) pose a privacy risk: when acting in one context, they can inappropriately pull in information from another context.

Method: The paper introduces AgentCIBench, an evaluation harness that converts the contextual privacy risk into executable, deterministically scored test scenarios targeting three failure modes: visual co-location, task-ambiguity overshare, and recipient misalignment.

Result: Evaluation of 15 frontier agents reveals a high failure rate: 11 out of 15 agents leak on more than 50% of scenarios, with an average leakage of 67.9%. Failures persist even when agents act end-to-end in the environment.

Conclusion: The paper releases AgentCIBench to encourage development of safer computer-use agents and proposes contextual disclosure testing as a pre-deployment safety check.

Abstract: Computer-use agents (CUAs) now act on a user's behalf across personal applications such as email, calendars, and to-do lists. This cross-application access is useful, but it also creates a privacy risk that has been largely overlooked: when an agent works in one context, it can pull in information from another that is inappropriate in that context. Hence, we introduce AgentCIBench, an evaluation harness that turns this risk into executable, deterministically scored scenarios. We target three common failure modes in CUAs: visual co-location, where the agent pulls in prohibited items that sit next to the task target in the UI; task-ambiguity overshare, where the agent dumps dense personal state in response to an under-specified prompt; and recipient misalignment, where the agent sends content to an addressee for whom it is inappropriate. We evaluate 15 frontier agents and find a surprisingly high failure rate: 11 of 15 leak on more than 50% of scenarios, with an average leakage of 67.9%, and the same failures persist when agents act end-to-end in the environment to complete the task. We release AgentCIBench to encourage the development of safer computer-use agents and position contextual disclosure testing as a pre-deployment safety check.

</details>


### [539] [SPADE: Structure-Prior Adaptive Decision Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23219)
*Yifan Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SPADE is a closed-form framework that uses one specification test and Stein-unbiased shrinkage to decide whether and how strongly to enforce physical-structure priors in scientific machine learning, achieving oracle-tracking performance with minimal computational cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Physical-structure priors (conservation laws, Hamiltonian forms, symmetries) can improve scientific ML when correct but degrade predictions when misspecified. Existing methods lack a calibrated rule for deciding whether to impose a prior, how strongly, which prior to use, or which subset of candidate laws holds.

Method: SPADE treats prior enforcement as shrinkage of the structure-violating block of an unconstrained estimator. It uses: (1) one exact specification test to decide if the prior is data-supported, (2) Stein-unbiased James-Stein shrinkage to set enforcement strength with O(σ²/n) oracle guarantee, and (3) a gate that commits to the hard prior only when the test certifies it. The test also enables consistent nested structure selection and Benjamini-Hochberg control for non-nested constraint families.

Result: Across linear-subspace prior, reservoir conservation law, and nonlinear Hamiltonian prior on Duffing dynamics, SPADE tracks the oracle, beats neural-network baseline, reduces correct-prior regret from 10.3% to 2.6%, matches cross-validation with 1/71 of the solves, selects correct structure with 100% accuracy, and recovers partial laws with controlled false relaxation.

Conclusion: SPADE provides a principled, computationally efficient framework for adaptive structure-prior estimation, eliminating the need for costly tuning while ensuring robust performance even under prior misspecification.

Abstract: Physical-structure priors such as conservation laws, Hamiltonian forms, and symmetries can improve scientific machine learning when correct, but can degrade predictions when misspecified. Existing methods usually enforce a chosen structure or tune a soft penalty, without a calibrated rule for deciding whether to impose a prior, how strongly to impose it, which prior to use, or which subset of candidate laws holds. We introduce SPADE, Structure-Prior Adaptive Decision Estimation, a closed-form framework that treats this problem as shrinkage of the structure-violating block of an unconstrained estimator. SPADE uses one exact specification test and one estimand: the test decides whether the prior is supported by data, Stein-unbiased James-Stein shrinkage sets the enforcement strength with an $O(σ^2/n)$ oracle guarantee, and a gate commits to the hard prior only when the test certifies it. The same test yields consistent nested structure selection and Benjamini-Hochberg control for subset discovery in non-nested constraint families. Across a linear-subspace prior, a reservoir conservation law, and a nonlinear Hamiltonian prior on Duffing dynamics, SPADE tracks the oracle, beats a neural-network baseline, reduces correct-prior regret from $10.3\%$ to $2.6\%$, matches cross-validation with $1/71$ of the solves, selects the correct structure with $100\%$ accuracy, and recovers partial laws with controlled false relaxation.

</details>


### [540] [HOLMES: Evaluating Higher-Order Logical Reasoning in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23238)
*Yucheng Wu,Jundong Xu,Mingzhen Ju,Yue Yu,Chenpeng Wang,Haoxuan Li,Liangming Pan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HOLMES is the first real-world benchmark for higher-order symbolic reasoning in LLMs, containing 1379 instances across law and finance. Current LLMs achieve only 50.64% average accuracy, with the best at 59.54%, revealing higher-order reasoning as a key bottleneck for reliable AI.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing reasoning benchmarks are first-order-logic-centric and miss realistic scenarios requiring reasoning over rules, predicates, functions, constraints, and decision procedures. Higher-order symbolic reasoning is identified as an underexplored but critical capability for reliable AI.

Method: HOLMES is built on higher-order logic, pairing natural-language problems with HOL formalizations, ground-truth answers, verifiable reasoning traces, and fine-grained controllable reasoning factors across law and finance domains.

Result: Current LLMs average 50.64% accuracy (best 59.54%). High final-answer accuracy can mask shortcut reasoning in conflict-resolution settings; performance drops sharply under scope-conditioned and compositional reasoning.

Conclusion: Higher-order symbolic reasoning is a key bottleneck for building reliable and verifiable LLMs, and current models still struggle significantly on this benchmark.

Abstract: Logical reasoning is essential for reliable AI, yet existing benchmarks are largely first-order-logic-centric, focusing on object-level deduction over fixed predicates. This misses many realistic scenarios where models must reason over rules, predicates, functions, constraints, and decision procedures themselves. We introduce HOLMES (Higher-Order Logic Meets real-world Explainable Symbolic reasoning), the first real-world benchmark for higher-order symbolic reasoning in LLMs, containing 1379 instances. Built on higher-order logic, HOLMES pairs natural-language problems with HOL formalizations, ground-truth answers, verifiable reasoning traces, and fine-grained controllable reasoning factors across law and finance. Experiments show that current LLMs still struggle on HOLMES, with an average accuracy of only 50.64% and the best model reaching 59.54%. Our analyses further reveal that high final-answer accuracy can mask shortcut reasoning in conflict-resolution settings, while performance drops sharply under scope-conditioned and compositional reasoning. These findings identify higher-order symbolic reasoning as a key bottleneck for building reliable and verifiable LLMs. The project code and dataset are publicly available at https://github.com/wuyucheng2002/HOLMES.

</details>


### [541] [GIF: Locally Sound Geometric Information Flow Control for LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23277)
*Adam Storek,Nikolaus Holzer,Zhuo Zhang,Suman Jana*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces Geometric Information Flow (GIF), a semantic framework that uses the LLM Jacobian and local output geometry to upper-bound the Shannon mutual information between input tokens and outputs, providing a principled, provably sound measure of information flow through autoregressive models. GIF achieves near-perfect recall on prompt-injection and privacy-leakage benchmarks at up to 81x lower token cost than LLM-as-judge baselines, and transfers across model sizes via surrogates up to 200x smaller.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing IFC-based defenses for LLM agentic systems lack a principled semantic foundation for reasoning about information flow through the model itself, and suffer from severe taint explosion because any input token can influence any output token in autoregressive models.

Method: GIF uses the LLM Jacobian and local output geometry to upper-bound Shannon mutual information between perturbed input spans and model outputs, computed via automatic differentiation and low-rank approximation. It includes a fully mechanized Lean 4 proof of local geometric soundness.

Result: GIF achieves near-perfect recall on integrity and confidentiality tasks across multiple benchmarks even without a downstream declassifier, outperforming attention-based baselines. With lightweight LLM-based declassifiers, it matches/exceeds GPT-5.5 xhigh reasoning F1 while using up to 81x lower token cost. GIF flows from small surrogate models (up to 200x smaller) transfer to larger state-of-the-art models.

Conclusion: GIF provides a scalable, provably sound information flow measure for LLMs that enables efficient detection of prompt injections and privacy leaks without requiring gradient access on the target model, supporting black-box deployment.

Abstract: Large language models increasingly mediate interactions between sensitive data, untrusted inputs, and privileged actions in agentic systems, creating security and privacy risks. These range from prompt injections that manipulate downstream tool use to leakage of confidential information through model outputs. Recent Information Flow Control (IFC)-based defenses show promise but lack a principled semantic foundation for reasoning about information flow through the model itself. Since any input token may influence any output token in an autoregressive LLM, existing approaches suffer from severe taint explosion.
  We present Geometric Information Flow (GIF), a semantic framework for tracking information flow from input tokens to outputs. GIF uses the LLM Jacobian and local output geometry to upper-bound the Shannon mutual information between perturbed input spans and model outputs, yielding a scalable measure computable on large models via automatic differentiation and low-rank approximation. Unlike attention-based or correlational attribution heuristics, GIF satisfies local geometric soundness, and we provide a fully mechanized Lean 4 proof that it upper-bounds the true information flow induced by a given prompt under local regularity assumptions.
  We evaluate GIF on integrity and confidentiality tasks across multiple prompt-injection and privacy-leakage benchmarks. GIF achieves near-perfect recall even without a downstream declassifier, outperforming attention-based baselines. Combined with lightweight LLM-based declassifiers, it matches or exceeds the F1 of direct LLM-as-judge baselines such as GPT-5.5 xhigh reasoning while using up to 81x lower token cost. GIF flows detected with small surrogate models transfer to larger state-of-the-art models and other model families, even when the surrogate is up to 200x smaller, suggesting black-box deployment without gradient access.

</details>


### [542] [EHR-Complex: Benchmarking Medical Agents for Complex Clinical Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23301)
*Yitong Qiao,Lei Liu,Yue Shen,Jian Wang,Jinjie Gu,Zhixuan Chu,Kui Ren*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: EHR-Complex is a large-scale benchmark (52K tasks, 365K patients, 31 tables, 500M+ records) for interactive clinical database reasoning, requiring agents to execute SQL/Python in a sandboxed environment. Top models achieve only 62.3% exact-match accuracy, with pass@k consistency below 50% for k=4, exposing SQL logic errors, medical-code lookup failures, and semantic misunderstandings as dominant failure modes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks for clinical EHR analysis rely on idealized, clean EHRs with static SQL generation, failing to reflect the complexity of practical, interactive, and longitudinal clinical reasoning tasks.

Method: Built on MIMIC-IV (365K patients, 31 tables, 500M+ records), EHR-Complex contains ~52K tasks across six clinical intents, supporting patient-level and population-level queries. Each task requires agents to interact with a sandboxed environment via SQL queries or Python code, with queries averaging 31.93 SQL structural components for multi-table aggregation and compositional reasoning.

Result: Top-performing model achieves 62.3% exact-match accuracy. Pass@k consistency drops below 50% for nearly all models at k=4. Analysis of 3,800+ failed trajectories reveals three dominant failure modes: SQL logic errors, medical-code lookup failures, and semantic misunderstandings.

Conclusion: EHR-Complex provides a rigorous testbed exposing significant gaps in robust reasoning for clinical agents, highlighting the need for improved reliability in large-scale EHR analysis.

Abstract: Clinical agents promise to democratize access to electronic health records (EHRs), yet existing benchmarks fail to reflect the complexity of practical EHR analysis, e.g., often operating on idealized, clean EHRs via static SQL generation rather than interactive execution. In this work, we introduce EHR-Complex, a large-scale benchmark designed for interactive clinical database reasoning. Built on the large MIMIC-IV substrate (365K patients, 31 tables, 500M+ records), EHR-Complex comprises about 52K tasks spanning six clinical intents, supporting both patient-level and population-level queries, where each task requires an agent to interact with a sandboxed environment by executing SQL queries or Python code. Notably, EHR-Complex considers the real-world SQL task complexity for longitudinal multi-table aggregation and compositional reasoning, resulting in 31.93 SQL structural components per query on average. Evaluation results on EHR-Complex reveal the clinical difficulty of these EHR reasoning scenarios, with the top-performing model achieving only 62.3% exact-match accuracy. Pass^k consistency drops below 50% for nearly all evaluated models at k=4, exposing broad stochastic fragility. A fine-grained analysis of more than 3,800 failed trajectories for representative LLMs reveals three dominant failure modes: SQL logic errors, medical-code lookup failures, and semantic misunderstandings. EHR-Complex provides a rigorous testbed for clinical agents and highlights remaining gaps in robust reasoning for large-scale EHR analysis.

</details>


### [543] [Abstract representational geometry supports inference in large language models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23345)
*Yunan Zeng,Yuwang Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMs form abstract, hippocampus-like geometric representations (low-dimensional, orthogonal manifolds) in higher layers that support generalizable inference, with geometry mechanistically causally implicated in reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether LLMs, like humans, construct abstract representational geometries (low-dimensional orthogonal manifolds) for flexible inference in changing environments, or instead rely on task-specific statistical regularities.

Method: Adapted a contextual reversal-learning paradigm to text, comparing humans and LLMs at behavioral and representational levels; used representational geometry analysis across model depth, and conducted intervention experiments (task-sequence language modeling and geometric regularization).

Result: LLMs show generalizable inference less frequently than humans; when inference occurs, internal states exhibit hippocampal-like abstract geometric structures organized hierarchically across layers (lower layers encode stimulus identity, higher layers form functional band enriched for abstract context geometry); geometric regularization increases emergence of generalizable inference.

Conclusion: Abstract representational geometry is a mechanistic principle supporting inference in large language models.

Abstract: A defining feature of human intelligence is the ability to adapt to changing environments by inferring latent task structure from sparse observations. Neuroscientific research indicates that this capability relies on the hippocampus constructing abstract representations, expressed as low-dimensional, approximately orthogonal manifolds in neural state space. However, the internal mechanisms of large language models (LLMs) remain largely opaque, making it unclear whether they form comparable abstract representations or instead rely on task-specific statistical regularities when performing comparable reasoning tasks. Here we adapt a contextual reversal-learning paradigm to a text-based setting and compare humans and LLMs at both the Behavioural and representational levels. We report that although LLMs exhibit generalizable reasoning less frequently than humans, when such inference occurs, their internal states exhibit abstract geometric structures that resemble those reported in the hippocampus. Notably, this representational geometry is not uniformly distributed but is organized hierarchically across model depth: whereas lower layers show early, stable encoding of stimulus identity, higher layers form a hippocampal-like functional band enriched for abstract context geometry associated with inference. Furthermore, complementary intervention experiments mechanistically implicate geometry in reasoning: task-sequence language modelling induces geometric disentanglement, whereas geometric regularization of higher layers increases the emergence of generalizable inference. Together, these findings establish abstract representational geometry as a mechanistic principle supporting inference in large language models.

</details>


### [544] [Litmus: Zero-Label, Code-Driven Metric Specification for Evaluating AI Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23403)
*Prajjwal Gupta,Prasang Gupta,Vishal Bhutani,Apoorva Sharma,Sumanth Chundru,Waqar Sarguroh,Kevin Paul*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Litmus is a zero-label system that automatically designs evaluation and monitoring metrics for AI pipelines by first eliciting evaluation intent from source code and targeted interrogation, then constructing a justified, per-stage metric portfolio. It outperforms baselines across three real-world pipelines in coverage, redundancy, and validity, achieving up to Spearman ρ=0.72 vs. <0.47 on scientific QA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluating agentic LLM systems is increasingly important yet difficult; evaluation goals are often left implicit, making metric choices hard to justify, interpret, or validate. Current approaches focus on automatic metric implementation rather than first clarifying what must be measured and why.

Method: Litmus is a zero-label system that elicits evaluation intent from source code and targeted interrogation, identifies what must be measured and why, then converts those answers into constraints for constructing a justified, per-stage metric portfolio. It is evaluated on three code-defined AI pipelines (financial account grouping, scientific QA, inherent risk assessment) against AutoMetrics and DynamicRubric baselines.

Result: Litmus achieves the broadest or tied-broadest concern coverage, spans more pipeline stages, produces near-zero-redundancy portfolios, and ranks first in validity against per-row quality labels on all three pipelines. On scientific QA, Spearman ρ=0.72 vs. less than 0.47 for all baselines; results on other pipelines are within overlapping confidence intervals relative to two audit framework components.

Conclusion: The results support a shift from automatic metric implementation to automatic metric specification: evaluation systems should first ask what must be measured and why before deciding which metric to compute.

Abstract: As agentic LLM systems move from prototypes to deployment across increasingly diverse domains, evaluating them has become both more important and more difficult. The challenge is not only that individual metrics may be unreliable, but that evaluation goals are often left implicit. Without a clear account of what a system is expected to do, how it can fail, and which failures matter, metric choices become difficult to justify, interpret, or validate. We present Litmus, a zero-label system that designs evaluation and monitoring metrics for AI pipelines by eliciting evaluation intent from source code and targeted interrogation. Instead of assuming that the evaluation target is already known, Litmus first identifies what must be measured and why, then converts those answers into constraints for constructing a justified, per-stage metric portfolio. We evaluate Litmus on three real, code-defined AI pipelines - financial account grouping, scientific QA, and inherent risk assessment - against AutoMetrics and three DynamicRubric baselines. Litmus achieves the broadest or tied-broadest concern coverage, spans more pipeline stages, produces a near-zero-redundancy portfolio, and ranks first in validity against per-row quality labels on all three pipelines - decisively on scientific QA (Spearman $ρ=0.72$ vs. less than $0.47$ for every baseline), and within overlapping confidence intervals in relation to two components of the audit framework despite using no labels during metric design. Our results support a shift from automatic metric implementation to automatic metric specification: before asking which metric to compute, evaluation systems should ask what must be measured and why.

</details>


### [545] [Digital Humanism and Evolutionary Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23417)
*Wolfgang Höhl*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper explores the relationship between digital humanism and evolutionary design, identifying common structures, synergies, and differences, particularly in autonomy, decision-making, and subjectivity. It argues that functional specialization driven by market forces and even energy optimization negatively impacts open technology development.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To better understand digital humanism and evolutionary design within the context of human-centered technological development, especially amid the current AI debate, and to identify potential synergies and challenges between these two concepts.

Method: Conceptual analysis and graphical visualization of digital humanism and evolutionary design; discussion of freedom, responsibility, conviviality, subjectivity; illustrative examples (Turing Test, Chinese Room); introduction of evolutionary design variants (co-evolutionary/sustainable software, clean code, green IT) and Simondon's "open machine" concept.

Result: Both concepts share similar structures and can produce positive synergies through cooperation. Key differences exist in autonomy vs. determination in decision-making and genuine vs. simulated subjectivity. Market-driven functional specialization and even energy efficiency optimizations in sustainable software harm open, quality-oriented technology development.

Conclusion: Digital humanism and evolutionary design have overlapping structures that can benefit from mutual synergy. However, challenges remain in reconciling autonomy and subjectivity, and current market/consumer-oriented specialization—including sustainability optimizations—undermines open technology development.

Abstract: This paper examines the two concepts of digital humanism and evolutionary design. The aim is to identify and highlight potential common structures, synergies, and challenges. How should and can technical systems be designed, and what implications does this have for the design of our environment? In light of the current debate surrounding artificial intelligence, this paper aims to serve as a preliminary study to help better understand the two concepts of digital humanism and evolutionary design within the context of human-centered technological development. Following a brief introduction, the two concepts of Digital Humanism and Evolutionary Design are presented and graphically visualized. The terms of freedom and responsibility in human decision-making, conviviality, and subjectivity are discussed, along with examples illustrating the distinction between human and artificial intelligence (Turing Test and Chinese Room). The various concepts of evolutionary design (e.g., co-evolutionary or sustainable software development, clean code, or green IT) and Gilbert Simondon's concept of the "open machine" are introduced. The interdependencies between functional specialization and open technology development are highlighted. Both concepts share similar structures. In joint cooperation, they can lead to positive effects and mutual synergies. Significant differences lie in the areas of autonomy and determination in decision-making, as well as in genuine and simulated subjectivity. Open technology development is also currently suffering from the functional specialization of software and AI applications due to a purely market- and consumer-oriented approach. Even optimizations for energy efficiency in sustainable software development lead to greater specialization and thus also have a detrimental effect on open and quality-oriented technology development.

</details>


### [546] [Cross-Architectural Mixture-of-Experts with Adaptive Soft Routing for Plant Leaf Disease Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23441)
*Phi-Hung Hoang,Thi-Thu-Hong Phan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes an adaptive soft Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) framework with cross-architectural routing (EfficientNet-B0, DenseNet-121, Swin-Tiny) for plant leaf disease classification under complex backgrounds, illumination variations, and class imbalance. Achieves 91.68% recall and 92.62% F1-score on potato dataset, with strong cross-dataset generalization to durian (94.03%) and sesame (97.04%) datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Plant leaf disease classification is challenging under complex backgrounds, illumination variations, and severe class imbalance. Single-architecture models fail to capture both local and global representations effectively.

Method: An adaptive soft Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) framework with cross-architectural routing integrating EfficientNet-B0, DenseNet-121, and Swin-Tiny. Uses a soft gating mechanism for input-dependent expert weighting and a two-stage refinement training strategy for optimization stability.

Result: On a highly imbalanced potato leaf disease dataset: 91.68% recall and 92.62% F1-score, outperforming strongest individual expert by 5.91% and 5.03%. On durian and sesame datasets: F1-scores of 94.03% and 97.04%.

Conclusion: The proposed MoE framework demonstrates robust cross-dataset generalization and potential for reliable real-world crop health monitoring.

Abstract: Plant leaf disease classification is crucial for crop protection and precision agriculture but remains challenging under complex backgrounds, illumination variations, and severe class imbalance. Moreover, single-architecture models often fail to effectively capture both local and global representations. To address these challenges, this study proposes an adaptive soft Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) framework with cross-architectural routing that integrates EfficientNet-B0, DenseNet-121, and Swin-Tiny to exploit complementary multi-scale, local, and global features. A soft gating mechanism dynamically assigns input-dependent expert weights, while a two-stage refinement training strategy improves optimization stability and generalization. Experiments on a highly imbalanced potato leaf disease dataset achieve 91.68% recall and 92.62% F1-score, surpassing the strongest individual expert by 5.91% and 5.03%, respectively. Additional evaluations on durian and sesame leaf disease datasets yield F1-scores of 94.03% and 97.04%, demonstrating robust cross-dataset generalization and the potential of the proposed framework for reliable real-world crop health monitoring

</details>


### [547] [AOHP: An Open-Source OS-Level Agent Harness for Personalized, Efficient and Secure Interaction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23449)
*Shanhui Zhao,Jiacheng Liu,Guohong Liu,Jichao Yan,Jialei Ye,Yuhao Yang,Hao Wen,Shizuo Tian,Yizhen Yuan,Yuxuan Chen,Yunxin Liu,Ju Ren,Ya-Qin Zhang,Chao Huang,Yao Guo,Yuanchun Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AOHP is an OS-level agent harness on Android that treats AI agents as first-class OS actors, introducing personalized service composition, efficient agent interfaces, and secure information flow, achieving +21.12% completion rate, -51.55% token cost, and improved security compliance over conventional systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing end-user operating systems are designed for application-centric workflows with little native support for AI agents, leading to execution overhead, safety risks, and limited adoption of agents. The research community lacks an open testbed to explore architectural primitives for agent-mediated interaction.

Method: AOHP is built on AOSP (Android Open Source Project) with agents as first-class OS actors, introducing three mechanisms: personalized service composition (adaptive UI and service orchestration), efficient agent interfaces (streamlined agent-system communication), and secure information flow (policy-based access control).

Result: In preliminary experiments on challenging OS agent tasks, AOHP achieves +21.12% higher task completion rate, -51.55% reduction in token cost, and improved security-policy compliance compared to conventional systems.

Conclusion: Treating agents as first-class OS actors with native system support significantly improves agent effectiveness, efficiency, and safety, suggesting OS-level architectural changes are crucial for the agent-native computing paradigm.

Abstract: AI agents are driving a new software paradigm, with the ability to autonomously call tools, extract information, manage memory, and complete tasks that span applications and data sources. Most existing end-user operating systems, however, are designed for application-centric workflows and offer little native support for AI agents. This mismatch limits the wider adoption of agents and leads to execution overhead and safety risks when running agents on conventional systems. While the concept of agent-native operating systems is emerging, the research community lacks an open testbed to explore the architectural primitives desired for agent-mediated interaction. We present AOHP (Android Open Harness Project), an OS-level agent harness built on the Android Open Source Project (AOSP). The core design principle of AOHP is to treat agents as first-class OS actors, enabling adaptive user interfaces and agent-friendly runtime environments. AOHP preserves the mature Android software and hardware ecosystem while introducing three agent-oriented system mechanisms: personalized service composition, efficient agent interfaces, and secure information flow. Based on preliminary experiments on challenging tasks covering key capabilities of OS agents, AOHP shows clear advantages in task completion (+21.12% completion rate), execution cost (-51.55% token cost), and security-policy compliance.

</details>


### [548] [CADRE: Stable, Parameter Efficient Adaptation of Medical Vision Language Models with Bounded Forgetting and Prior Drift](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23487)
*Amrita Singh,Rishabh Jha*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CADRE is a parameter-efficient continual adaptation framework for medical VLMs that uses LoRA with similarity-aware elastic weight consolidation and anchor-to-prior penalties to prevent catastrophic forgetting and embedding drift, achieving high accuracy and low forgetting across three dissimilar breast cancer imaging modalities while training only ~0.23% of parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Adapting medical VLMs to new clinical services is a safety problem, not just an accuracy one; deployed models can silently fail via catastrophic forgetting (losing previously handled modalities) and drifting from trustworthy pretrained priors toward modality-specific shortcuts. Existing continual learning methods lack guarantees for these stability properties.

Method: CADRE uses a frozen backbone with LoRA for parameter-efficient adaptation, combined with: (1) an online, self-scaling, similarity-aware elastic weight consolidation (EWC) term to bound retained-competence loss, with guarantees on total consolidation mass and scale invariance to fix vanilla EWC's order fragility; and (2) an anchor-to-prior penalty to bound embedding drift from the frozen prior. Training only ~0.23% of parameters.

Result: Under a multi-seed, multi-order protocol with paired significance testing across three maximally dissimilar breast cancer modalities (histopathology, ultrasound, chest radiography), CADRE achieved the highest accuracy, SPQ, and backward transfer, and the lowest forgetting among adapting methods. It reduced forgetting roughly sevenfold vs the strongest regularized baseline (0.075 to 0.011; paired p=0.023) and achieved positive backward transfer where all baselines were negative.

Conclusion: CADRE demonstrates that framing continual adaptation through clinical-safety aligned stability properties (forgetting and drift) rather than leaderboard accuracy is effective. However, the authors note these are stability properties aligned with safety desiderata, not a deployment guarantee, and robustness to distribution shift and adversarial inputs remains out of scope.

Abstract: Medical vision-language models (VLMs) such as BiomedCLIP generalize broadly, but adapting them to a clinical service is as much a safety problem as an accuracy one. Updating a deployed model for a new imaging modality can fail silently in two ways that harm patients: it can forget modalities it already handled (catastrophic forgetting), and it can drift from its trustworthy pretrained prior toward modality-specific shortcuts. We study parameter-efficient continual adaptation through these two properties rather than leaderboard accuracy, presenting CADRE: a frozen-backbone framework combining low-rank adaptation (LoRA) with an online, self-scaling, similarity-aware elastic weight consolidation term that bounds retained-competence loss, and an anchor-to-prior penalty bounding embedding drift from the frozen prior. Two short guarantees, a bound on total consolidation mass and a scale-invariance property, remove the scale-related sources of vanilla EWC's order fragility. Using breast cancer across three maximally dissimilar modalities (histopathology, ultrasound, chest radiography) as a controlled cross-modality stress test, under a multi-seed, multi-order protocol with paired significance testing and training approximately 0.23% of parameters, CADRE attains the highest accuracy, SPQ, and backward transfer and the lowest forgetting among adapting methods, reducing forgetting roughly sevenfold versus the strongest regularized baseline (0.075 to 0.011; paired p=0.023) and achieving positive backward transfer where every baseline is negative. We frame these as stability properties aligned with clinical-safety desiderata, not a deployment guarantee; robustness to distribution shift and adversarial inputs is out of scope.

</details>


### [549] [POTracker: Optimizing Large Language Models for Standard-Compliant Power Outage Report Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23533)
*Hung Phan,Aniroop Naladala,Dubey Avanindra,Supryia Chinthavali,Lunga Dalton,Ali Jannesari*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: POTracker fine-tunes Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct with a novel loss function (POTrackerLoss) combining textual and structural (tag) similarity, achieving 86.47% structural accuracy for generating regulatory-compliant power outage reports, outperforming baselines by up to 51%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs struggle with domain-specific data generation that requires strict formatting and structural compliance (e.g., JSON/XML) to meet regulatory standards, as open-ended generation objectives are insufficient for such constrained tasks. The specific problem is generating machine-readable U.S. power outage reports that must follow energy-sector regulatory guidelines.

Method: POTracker fine-tunes Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct using POTrackerLoss, a custom loss function that jointly optimizes textual similarity and structural (tag) similarity between generated and ground-truth reports. Evaluation is on a 1,000-report dataset against five fine-tuning baselines and one rule-based XML method.

Result: POTracker achieves 86.47% structural accuracy, improves overall accuracy by up to 51% over other fine-tuning methods, and receives an average domain-expert score of 4.03/5 for generated labels.

Conclusion: The proposed loss function effectively bridges the gap between general LLM text generation and structured, regulatory-compliant domain-specific data generation, with strong quantitative and human-evaluated results.

Abstract: Recent large language models (LLMs) are good at general text generation, but it is still hard to use them for domain-specific data generation because the output must follow strict formatting and structural rules. Unlike open-ended tasks such as question answering or translation, domain-specific generation must be both semantically correct and compliant with existing guidelines and standards. In this work, we study the nationwide interoperability problem of utility power outage reports in the United States. In practice, outage reports need to be machine-readable (e.g., JSON or XML) and must strictly follow requirements from energy-sector regulatory bodies. To address this problem, we propose POTracker, an optimized LLM for power outage report generation. We fine-tune Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct using our proposed objective. The key contribution is a new loss function, POTrackerLoss, that considers both textual similarity and structural (tag) similarity between the generated report and the ground-truth report. We evaluate POTracker on a dataset of 1,000 power outage reports and compare it with five well-known fine-tuning methods and one rule-based XML conversion method. Results show that POTracker outperforms other fine-tuning approaches, improving overall accuracy by up to 51% and reaching 86.47% structural accuracy for generated power outage reports. In addition, we conduct a human study to assess the quality of the ground-truth standard reports, where domain experts assign the generated labels an average score of 4.03 on a 0--5 scale.

</details>


### [550] [VeriEvol: Scaling Multimodal Mathematical Reasoning via Verifiable Evol-Instruct](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23543)
*Haoling Li,Kai Zheng,Jie Wu,Can Xu,Qingfeng Sun,Han Hu,Yujiu Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: VeriEvol is a verifiable data-construction framework for scaling visual mathematical reasoning in RL, decoupling prompt difficulty evolution from answer reliability via offline hypothesis-test falsification, achieving +3.88 improvement over an un-evolved RL baseline on a five-benchmark visual-math suite.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing data pipelines scale supervision by trusting the labeller, and policy-side methods assume answers are already correct. As data volume grows for visual mathematical reasoning RL, reward labels must remain reliable, but current approaches lack mechanisms to ensure answer correctness at scale.

Method: VeriEvol has two components: (1) Type-aware evolution module that rewrites low-difficulty image-question seeds into harder, image-grounded prompts via route-specific evolution operators; (2) HTV-Agent verifier that accepts an answer only after multi-source counter-evidence fails to refute it (offline hypothesis-test falsification). The framework decouples prompt difficulty scaling from answer reliability enforcement before policy updates, and integrates with GRPO-style RL recipes.

Result: Scaling evolved SFT data from 10K to 250K samples improves mean accuracy from 35.42 to 54.73 on a five-benchmark visual-math suite. With backbone, SFT initialization, and GRPO recipe fixed, VeriEvol adds +3.88 over an un-evolved RL baseline: +1.82 from evolved prompts and +2.06 from the HTV-Agent verifier.

Conclusion: VeriEvol demonstrates that scaling verifiable data through decoupled evolution and falsification-based verification outperforms simply scaling unverified data, and the framework is extensible by adding evolution routes or verifier channels. All artifacts are released for auditing and downstream scaling.

Abstract: Scaling reinforcement learning for visual mathematical reasoning requires more than generating harder questions: as data volume grows, the reward labels themselves must remain reliable. Yet existing data pipelines scale supervision while trusting the labeller, and policy-side methods assume the underlying answers are already correct. We instead treat scaling as a verifiable data-construction problem and decouple two axes before any policy update: prompt difficulty, expanded by route-specific evolution operators, and answer reliability, enforced by offline hypothesis-test falsification. We instantiate this as VeriEvol, an iterative framework with two extensible components: a type-aware evolution module that rewrites low-difficulty image-question seeds into harder, image-grounded prompts; and HTV-Agent, a verifier that accepts an answer only after multi-source counter-evidence has failed to refute it. The resulting verified data scales in volume, extends by adding evolution routes or verifier channels, and plugs directly into existing GRPO-style RL recipes. On a five-benchmark visual-math suite, scaling evolved SFT data from 10K to 250K samples raises the mean accuracy from 35.42 to 54.73; then, with backbone, SFT initialization, and GRPO recipe held fixed, VeriEvol adds a cumulative +3.88 over an un-evolved RL baseline, of which +1.82 comes from evolved prompts and +2.06 from the HTV-Agent verifier. We release the prompts, data, models, code, and the full verifier trace of every sample, so that downstream work can scale and audit the pipeline rather than only inspect its outputs.

</details>


### [551] [The Topology of Ill-Posed Questions: Persistent Homology for Detection and Steering in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23590)
*Guangyu Jiang,Sizhe Tang,Mahdi Imani,Tian Lan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes using persistent homology to analyze LLM internal states (hidden states as point clouds) to detect and steer responses to ill-posed questions, outperforming baselines in classification and improving response quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ill-posed questions (ambiguous, underspecified, contradictory) are challenging for LLMs, and existing approaches largely analyze ill-posedness through model outputs or focus on specific subclasses, lacking a unified representation.

Method: Models contextual hidden states of prompt tokens as point clouds per transformer layer, applies finite zero-dimensional persistent homology to extract three descriptors (mean finite lifetime, normalized lifetime entropy, largest-lifetime concentration), concatenates them across layers to form a topology representation, and introduces topology-conditioned activation steering to retrieve similar examples and construct query-specific interventions.

Result: Topology features improve accuracy from 67.4% to 78.9% (AmbigQA), 79.9% to 88.5% (SituatedQA), 57.6% to 69.6% (CLAMBER 9-way). Topology-conditioned steering increases total acceptable response rate from 61.4% to 70.6% and grounded acceptable responses from 11.9% to 16.4%.

Conclusion: Persistent homology provides an interpretable representation of ill-posedness and an effective mechanism for targeted response steering in LLMs.

Abstract: Ill-posed questions, including ambiguous, underspecified, or contradictory queries, may admit no valid answer or multiple plausible answers, posing a challenge for large language models (LLMs). Existing approaches largely analyze ill-posedness through model outputs and often focus on specific subclasses. We investigate whether diverse sources of ill-posedness can be represented within a unified topology of LLM internal states and whether this structure can be used to steer response behavior. We model the contextual hidden states of prompt tokens at each transformer layer as a point cloud and characterize its geometry using finite zero-dimensional persistent homology. Each layer is summarized by three compact descriptors: mean finite lifetime, normalized lifetime entropy, and largest-lifetime concentration. Concatenating these descriptors across layers yields a topology representation of the question. We further introduce topology-conditioned activation steering, which retrieves topologically similar examples and constructs query-specific activation interventions that encourage source-aware clarification or abstention. Across three open-weight LLMs, topology features consistently outperform prompt-based and pooled-hidden-state baselines for ill-posedness classification, improving average accuracy from \(67.4\%\) to \(78.9\%\) on AmbigQA, from \(79.9\%\) to \(88.5\%\) on SituatedQA, and from \(57.6\%\) to \(69.6\%\) on CLAMBER 9-way classification. Topology-conditioned steering increases the average total acceptable response rate from \(61.4\%\) to \(70.6\%\) and grounded acceptable responses from \(11.9\%\) to \(16.4\%\). These results show that persistent homology provides both an interpretable representation of ill-posedness and an effective mechanism for targeted response steering.

</details>


### [552] [SPIRAL: Learning to Search and Aggregate](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23595)
*Jubayer Ibn Hamid,Ifdita Hasan Orney,Michael Y. Li,Omar Shaikh,Yoonho Lee,Dorsa Sadigh,Chelsea Finn,Noah Goodman*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SPIRAL is a reinforcement learning framework that trains language models to jointly optimize three inference compute primitives—parallel sampling, sequential reasoning, and aggregation—outperforming GRPO by up to 11× in scaling efficiency and 15% in accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Language models are post-trained only for sequential reasoning, yet test-time scaling benefits from also using parallel traces and aggregation. This mismatch leaves performance on the table.

Method: SPIRAL trains a unified pipeline where the model (1) samples independent parallel traces via sequential CoT, (2) generates a final aggregation trace conditioned on those traces, and (3) optimizes end-to-end with set RL (for parallel trace diversity/utility) and standard RL (for aggregation quality).

Result: SPIRAL scales effectively with inference compute, outperforming GRPO by up to 11× scaling efficiency and 15% higher performance when all three compute primitives are scaled.

Conclusion: Training LMs to use parallel sampling and aggregation together with sequential reasoning during post-training yields substantially better test-time scaling than optimizing sequential reasoning alone.

Abstract: Language model reasoning can be substantially improved at test time via scaffolds that scale inference compute across different primitives -- sequential reasoning within a trace, independently sampled parallel traces, and aggregation of multiple reasoning traces into a final response. During post-training, however, language models are optimized only for sequential reasoning within a single trace. We introduce Sequential-Parallel-Aggregative Reinforcement Learning (SPIRAL), a framework in which a language model is trained to use all three primitives, as part of a unified inference compute pipeline. Concretely, the language model first samples a set of independent traces in parallel, each produced through sequential chain-of-thought reasoning, and then generates a final aggregation trace conditioned on those traces; all components are optimized end-to-end against the reward of the final aggregated response. To train this system, SPIRAL uses set reinforcement learning to teach models to produce a set of traces that are collectively useful for an aggregator and standard reinforcement learning to teach models to aggregate the set into improved final responses. Our experiments on reasoning tasks show that SPIRAL effectively scales with inference compute, outperforming GRPO by up to 11$\times$ scaling efficiency and 15% higher performance when all three compute primitives are scaled.

</details>


### [553] [Against Proxy Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23597)
*Sven Neth*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Maximizing a proxy utility function can be harmful, posing problems for decision theory.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To identify when optimizing proxy utility functions leads to negative outcomes, and to highlight challenges for decision theory.

Method: Theoretical analysis of conditions under which proxy utility maximization results in harmful behavior.

Result: Conditions for harm from proxy utility maximization are identified, indicating fundamental issues for decision theory application.

Conclusion: Proxy utility maximization can be problematic, suggesting limitations in current decision-theoretic frameworks.

Abstract: I discuss conditions under which maximizing a proxy utility function is harmful and suggest this poses problems for applying decision theory.

</details>


### [554] [AI-driven Optimisation of Quality of Recovery (QoR) in Remote Patient Monitoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23631)
*Yansong Liu,Li-Hsi,Lin,Pramit Khetrapal,Ronnie Stafford,John Kelly,Ivana Drobnjak*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The authors develop QoR-compact, a 5-item daily survey derived from the 15-item QoR-15, achieving comparable predictive performance (AUC-ROC ~0.968 vs 0.964) for postoperative recovery severity while reducing daily patient burden in remote monitoring settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Only 55% of post-surgical patients submitted the full QoR-15 survey beyond 14 of 30 monitoring days, indicating poor compliance with daily administration of a tool originally designed for occasional in-hospital use.

Method: Exhaustive evaluation of all 3,003 possible 5-question subsets of the QoR-15, selecting the best performing set (Q3, Q9, Q10, Q12, Q14) to predict near-term postoperative recovery severity, targeting one-third of the original items.

Result: QoR-compact achieves mean AUC-ROC of 0.968 (95% CI 0.915-0.988), statistically comparable to the 0.964 baseline; patient-level backtesting shows equivalent tracking of readmission events.

Conclusion: QoR-compact matches the full QoR-15's predictive performance for recovery severity with one-third the items; external validation is needed before clinical use, and a prospective study of improved compliance is warranted.

Abstract: Remote patient monitoring depends on patient-reported data to capture the subjective dimension of recovery that devices cannot measure. The Quality of Recovery (QoR-15) survey is the gold-standard instrument for this purpose. It was designed and validated for occasional in-hospital assessment, yet remote monitoring now administers it to patients daily. In our own post-surgical deployment, only 55% of patients submitted the survey more than 14 days of 30 monitoring days. We developed QoR-compact, a five-item daily input for the RPM prediction pathway. Setting a deployment-driven target of one-third of the daily items, we exhaustively evaluated all 3,003 five-question subsets of the QoR-15 and tested whether the best of them matches the full instrument in predicting near-term postoperative recovery severity. QoR-compact achieves a mean AUC-ROC of 0.968 (95% CI 0.915-0.988), statistically comparable to the 0.964 baseline obtained with one-third of the items. Patient-level backtesting indicates that it tracks readmission events as faithfully as the full form. Its five items span the physical and psychological axes of recovery: Q3 (feeling rested), Q9 (feeling comfortable and in control), Q10 (general well-being), Q12 (severe pain), and Q14 (feeling worried or anxious). The QoR-15 remains the gold-standard measure of recovery; QoR-compact complements it as a shorter daily input designed for prediction. This parity provides the basis for a prospective study of whether a lighter daily input is, in turn, completed more consistently. External validation on larger cohorts is required before clinical use.

</details>


### [555] [AI Exposure Scores: what they measure, what they miss, and what comes next](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23633)
*Campbell Lund,Thomas Euyang,Zanele Munyikwa,Marzieh Fadaee*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper critiques the widespread use of static GPT exposure scores (Eloundou et al., 2023) in future-of-work policy debates, identifying two gaps: (1) structural limitations of static scores vs. dynamic policy needs, and (2) insufficient researcher-policymaker coordination. It surveys methodological responses and calls for shared efforts to bridge the research-policy gap.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The GPTs are GPTs exposure scores have become central empirical inputs to policy debates about AI's impact on work, but their methodological limitations (temporal, geographic, ontological) are often overlooked as they travel beyond their original context. The authors aim to surface these gaps and argue that better coordination between researchers and policymakers is needed.

Method: The paper uses the diffusion of Eloundou et al.'s exposure scores as a case study, analyzing how static measures are applied in policy-facing analyses. It surveys five families of methodological responses (dynamic measures, ensemble methods, task-framework extensions, worker-centered metrics, adoption/usage data) and identifies a second gap: lack of engagement between researchers and policymakers.

Result: Two gaps identified: (1) structural - static exposure scores cannot answer dynamic policy questions about who is harmed, who benefits, how, and when; (2) coordination gap - policy work continues to cite static scores without engaging methodological updates. The paper calls for ex-post frameworks, participatory methods, data infrastructure, and a shift from prediction to preparedness.

Conclusion: Better measurement alone cannot close the research-policy gap. Policymakers must widen their evidence base, engage workers as epistemic partners, and shift to preparedness; researchers must build data infrastructure, adopt participatory methods, and write with policymakers in mind. Closing the gap is a shared, political task requiring deliberate work on what futures are worth building.

Abstract: A set of exposure scores calculated in 2023 has become a central empirical input to the future of work debate. Produced by Eloundou et al. (2023) and referred to here as the GPTs are GPTs scores, they define exposure as the share of occupational tasks a large language model can assist with. This work is a genuine methodological contribution, but as the scores travel from the time and place they were produced, the limitations the authors named do not always travel with them. Two gaps have widened as a result. The first is structural, between what static exposure scores measure and what policy questions actually require. Taking the diffusion of these scores as a case study, we show how their temporal, geographic, and ontological limitations compound in policy-facing analyses, and we survey five families of research responding to these limits: dynamic and benchmark-based measures, ensemble methods, task-framework extensions, worker-centered metrics, and adoption and usage data. The second gap is the one we argue needs more attention: the coordination between researchers and policymakers. The policy-relevant work which ask who is harmed, who benefits, how, and when, continues to reference the static GPTs are GPTs scores without engagement with the methodological updates that would let these questions be answered more reliably. We then ask what additional steps towards navigating uncertainty remain: ex-post frameworks and the deliberate, political work of reimagining what futures are worthy of building towards are. Closing the research-policy gap is a shared task: policymakers must widen their evidence base, engage workers as epistemic partners, and shift from prediction to preparedness; researchers must build data infrastructure, adopt participatory methods, and write with policymakers in mind. Better measurement matters, but it will not close the second gap alone.

</details>


### [556] [TailorMind: Towards Preference-Aligned Multimodal Content Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23643)
*Hengji Zhou,Ye Liu,Yufeng Liu,Si Wu,Lianghao Xia,Liqiang Nie*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TailorMind is a framework for personalized multimodal content generation that combines collaborative preference modeling (via hypergraph collaborative filtering and textual gradient descent) with controllable multimodal generation, outperforming retrieval-based baselines and ground-truth UGC across coherence, novelty, aesthetic quality, and hallucination metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Personalized content systems rely on available UGC and fail when suitable content is absent, delayed, or costly to create. Multimodal generators can synthesize content on demand, but translating behavioral traces into generation-ready preferences is underexplored.

Method: TailorMind uses hypergraph collaborative filtering to enrich sparse user histories, optimizes textual profiles via ranking-error feedback and textual gradient descent, employs retrieval-augmented style control for authentic UGC grounding, and applies cross-modal cohesion reflection to reduce semantic drift. Evaluated on TailorBench across five dimensions.

Result: Achieves competitive/stronger coherence, improved novelty and aesthetic quality over generation baselines and ground-truth UGC; up to 29% Recall gains in reranking.

Conclusion: Demonstrates advantages over retrieving available content or comparable UGC for personalized multimodal generation.

Abstract: Personalized content systems depend on available UGC and struggle when suitable content is absent, delayed, or costly to create. Although multimodal generators can synthesize content on demand, how to translate behavioral traces into generation-ready preferences remains underexplored. We study personalized multimodal content generation: creating user-tailored multimodal content without existing item pools or waiting for matching UGC. We propose TailorMind, linking collaborative preference modeling with controllable multimodal generation. TailorMind enriches sparse user histories via hypergraph collaborative filtering and optimizes textual profiles with ranking-error feedback and textual gradient descent. Retrieval-augmented style control grounds outputs in authentic UGC patterns, while cross-modal cohesion reflection reduces semantic drift. We construct TailorBench, a benchmark from three mainstream platforms evaluated along five dimensions: coherence, novelty, aesthetic, hallucination, profiling. Experiments show that TailorMind achieves competitive or stronger coherence, improves novelty and aesthetic quality over representative generation baselines and ground-truth UGC, demonstrating advantages over retrieving available content or comparable UGC, while achieving up to 29% Recall gains in reranking. Our code is released at: https://github.com/iLearn-Lab/TailorMind.

</details>


### [557] [Teaching LLMs String Matching, Backtracking, and Error Recovery to Deduce Bases and Truth Tables for the Combinatorially Exploding Bit Manipulation Puzzles](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23672)
*Prateek Agnihotri,Sanchit Jain,Prabhat Agnihotri,Aditya Prasad,Shubham Jain*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces a novel approach for the NVIDIA Nemotron Model Reasoning Challenge on Bit Manipulation Puzzles, replacing traditional arithmetic logic with string similarity, structured search, and autonomous error recovery. Key innovations include Bases and Truth Table Formulation (reframing logic deduction as base-selection using minimal bit flips), Backtracking DFS with error recovery, and Bit Tokenization with Interactive Reasoning SFT. The method achieves over 96% validation accuracy, securing 7th place overall.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs struggle with bit manipulation puzzles due to the complexity of simulating boolean logic and arithmetic, which leads to hallucinations, and the combinatorial explosion in the search space of bitwise operations. Traditional methods are computationally intractable for this task.

Method: 1) Bases and Truth Table Formulation: reframes logic-gate deduction as a base-selection task using string similarity (minimal bit flips) to isolate primitive transformations and deduce truth tables. 2) Backtracking DFS and Error Recovery: tests candidate bases, detects logical collisions, and backtracks on failure. 3) Bit Tokenization and Interactive Reasoning SFT: forces tokenizer to encode binary strings as single-bit tokens, uses dynamic masking to simulate oracle feedback, training the model to hypothesize, self-evaluate, and backtrack natively.

Result: Over 96% validation accuracy on bit manipulation puzzles, achieving the highest performance in this category and 7th place overall in the contest.

Conclusion: Abandoning complex arithmetic logic in favor of string similarity, structured search, and autonomous error recovery can effectively solve bit manipulation puzzles where traditional LLM reasoning fails, as evidenced by state-of-the-art performance in the competition.

Abstract: This paper presents our algorithmic innovations for the NVIDIA Nemotron Model Reasoning Challenge, focusing on Bit Manipulation Puzzles. In this task, the objective is to discover a hidden logical rule transforming input binary strings to outputs, then apply it to unseen inputs. Large Language Models (LLMs) notoriously struggle here; traditional methods force them to simulate complex boolean logic and arithmetic, leading to hallucinations. Furthermore, the search space of bitwise operations (combinations of shifts, rotations, and logic gates) suffers from a severe combinatorial explosion. To overcome this computational intractability, we present a novel approach that abandons arithmetic logic entirely in favor of string similarity, structured search, and autonomous error recovery.
  Our core contributions are: 1. Bases and Truth Table Formulation: We reframe logic-gate deduction into a base-selection task, leveraging string similarity (minimal bit flips) to isolate primitive transformations ("bases") and deduce truth tables without complex arithmetic. 2. Backtracking DFS and Error Recovery: We formalize a search process that tests candidate bases, detects logical collisions across examples, and backtracks upon failure to perform robust error recovery. 3. Bit Tokenization and Interactive Reasoning SFT: We force the tokenizer to encode binary strings as individual single-bit tokens. We use dynamic masking to simulate external oracle feedback, training the model to hypothesize, self-evaluate, and backtrack natively.
  Evaluated on bit manipulation puzzles, our approach achieved over 96% validation accuracy. This represents the highest performance in this category, driving our 7th Place overall finish in the contest.

</details>


### [558] [PsyBridge: A Hybrid Intelligent Framework for Multi-Dimensional Mental Health Assessment and Decision Support](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23673)
*Sunil Wanjari,Manish Thakre,Aayushi Asole,Sharwari Raut,Kwabena Adu-Duodu,Yinhao Li,Stanly Wilson*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PsyBridge is a hybrid intelligent framework integrating PHQ-9, GAD-7, cognitive evaluation, and personality profiling via modular design and weighted aggregation to provide interpretable multi-dimensional mental health assessment, achieving 0.84 accuracy on a semi-synthetic dataset of 500 profiles and outperforming standalone screening tools.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing mental health assessments rely on isolated screening instruments or black-box data-driven models that lack interpretability and fail to integrate multiple dimensions (e.g., depression, anxiety, cognition, personality) for comprehensive decision support.

Method: PsyBridge combines PHQ-9 and GAD-7 assessments with cognitive and behavioral indicators using a modular architecture and a weighted aggregation mechanism to produce interpretable risk classifications and recommendations.

Result: Overall accuracy of 0.84, outperforming standalone PHQ-9 and GAD-7 assessments with improved precision, recall, and F1-score; sensitivity and ablation studies confirm that cognitive and personality components enhance classification stability and reduce inconsistencies in moderate-risk prediction.

Conclusion: PsyBridge offers a scalable and interpretable AI-assisted decision-support framework for multi-dimensional mental health assessment, particularly suitable for digital healthcare and telehealth environments.

Abstract: Mental health assessment commonly relies on isolated screening instruments or data-driven models that often lack interpretability and multi-dimensional integration. Existing approaches frequently focus on individual indicators such as depression or anxiety while providing limited support for comprehensive and explainable decision-making. To address this limitation, this study proposes PsyBridge, a hybrid intelligent decision-support framework designed for multi-dimensional mental health assessment through the integration of clinically validated screening tools, cognitive evaluation, and personality profiling within a unified architecture. The proposed framework incorporates PHQ-9 and GAD-7 assessments alongside cognitive and behavioural indicators using a modular design and a weighted aggregation mechanism to generate interpretable mental health risk classifications and recommendations. To evaluate the framework, a semi-synthetic dataset consisting of 500 patient profiles representing varying severity levels was constructed based on clinically grounded score distributions. Experimental results demonstrate that PsyBridge achieves an overall accuracy of 0.84, outperforming standalone PHQ-9 and GAD-7 assessments while improving precision, recall, and F1-score. Sensitivity analysis and ablation studies further indicate that integrating cognitive and personality components contributes to more stable classification performance and reduces inconsistencies in moderate-risk prediction. The findings suggest that PsyBridge provides a scalable and interpretable approach for AI-assisted mental health decision support, particularly within digital healthcare and telehealth environments.

</details>


<div id='cs.MM'></div>

# cs.MM [[Back]](#toc)

### [559] [Composition: Building Community with Arts, Math, and Code (Experience Report)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23526)
*Isidore Mohr,Claire Wang*

Main category: cs.MM

TL;DR: This experience report describes Composition, a free event series on art, mathematics, and code, covering its event structure, artist selection, outreach efforts, and community response.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To share insights and best practices from organizing Composition, a free event series bridging art, mathematics, and code.

Method: The report details Composition's event structure, artist selection process, outreach for submissions and promotion, and community feedback.

Result: An overview of the operational aspects and community reception of the Composition event series.

Conclusion: The experience report provides a model for organizing similar interdisciplinary events and highlights the positive community response.

Abstract: Composition (https://composition.codes) is a free event series on art, mathematics, and code. This experience report covers Composition's event structure, artist selection process, outreach efforts for submissions and event promotion, and the community response.

</details>


### [560] [UI-LIC: A Unified Framework for Evaluating Learned Image Compression Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23545)
*Nicholas J. Nolen,Luc Trudeau,Andrew C. Freeman*

Main category: cs.MM

TL;DR: UI-LIC is an open-source framework that standardizes evaluation of Learned Image Compression models by integrating six high-performance models with shared configuration, GUI-based comparison with traditional codecs, unified quality metrics (PSNR, SSIM, VMAF, LPIPS), and interactive analysis tools.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The evaluation and comparison of LIC systems is complicated by heterogeneous software stacks, varying training conditions, and divergent evaluation methodologies, creating a need for standardized benchmarking.

Method: The authors built an open-source framework integrating six high-performance LIC models with a centralized controller for training, inference, and analysis using shared configs. A GUI enables side-by-side comparison with traditional video intra-frame encoders at equalized bitrates, computing PSNR, SSIM, VMAF, and LPIPS. An interactive image analyzer provides configurable quality heatmap overlays.

Result: The framework lowers barriers to LIC research by enabling comparative metrics and subjective analysis with a single setup command, released as open-source under MIT license.

Conclusion: UI-LIC provides a standardized, accessible evaluation platform for the LIC community, unlocking reproducible comparisons and subjective visual analysis.

Abstract: The evaluation and comparison of Learned Image Compression (LIC) systems is complicated by heterogeneous software stacks, varying training conditions, and divergent evaluation methodologies. To address these challenges, we introduce UI-LIC, an open-source software framework for evaluating LIC models. We integrate six high-performance LIC models, and provide a centralized controller for performing training, inference, and analysis with shared configuration parameters. Our GUI program offers a streamlined interface to evaluate these models alongside traditional video intra-frame encoders, equalizing the compressed bitrates and calculating quality metrics such as PSNR, SSIM, VMAF, and LPIPS. Finally, we provide an interactive image analyzer with configurable quality heatmap overlays. Our framework lowers barriers to further LIC research, unlocking comparative metrics and subjective analysis with a single setup command. The open-source software is released under the MIT license and is available at github.com/BaylorMultimediaLab/UI-LIC.

</details>


<div id='cs.GR'></div>

# cs.GR [[Back]](#toc)

### [561] [Line Drawings using LightBenders: Authoring and Illuminating](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22499)
*Hamed Alimohammadzadeh,Shahram Ghandeharizadeh*

Main category: cs.GR

TL;DR: A system using drone swarms (LightBenders) with servo-actuated LED strips to create mid-air line drawings and letterforms, with software tools for authoring, optimization, and deployment, validated by a human study showing acceptable perceptual quality at 10.1mm misalignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable transformative mid-air illumination of line drawings and letterforms using drones, addressing challenges in hardware design, swarm optimization, and perceptual quality.

Method: Hardware: drone with servo-actuated rod joints and dense addressable LED strips. Software: algorithms for minimum LightBender estimation, downwash-mitigating swarm formations, SFL file generation, Blender add-on for authoring/deployment, SVG import via Blender or standalone LB-Author tool. Evaluation: IRB-approved human subject study (n=21) measuring perceptual impact of misalignment.

Result: System achieves 10.1mm maximum misalignment, which is perceptually acceptable with median quality rating of 8/10 across tested illuminations.

Conclusion: The proposed LightBender swarm system can effectively render line drawings and letterforms in mid-air with perceptually acceptable alignment quality, supported by both technical implementation and human subject validation.

Abstract: This study presents the hardware and software architecture of a transformative system for illuminating line drawings and letterforms. These mid-air illuminations are indoors and might be animated. The hardware contribution is a drone equipped with servo-actuated rod joints and a dense, addressable LED strip that enables arbitrary orientation, a LightBender. The software contributions are threefold. First, the system implements algorithms and heuristics to estimate the minimum number of LightBenders required to render a line drawing or letterform, stagger swarm formations to mitigate LightBender downwash, generate Swarm Flight and Lighting (SFL) files, and execute these files using a swarm of LightBenders to illuminate line drawings and letterforms. Second, a Blender add-on enables users to register LightBenders, author graphics and animations represented by swarms of LightBenders, and deploy the swarm for illumination through one-click functions. Third, users may import SVG files into either the Blender add-on or a standalone LB-Author tool to illuminate line drawings directly from vector graphics.
  We present results from an IRB-approved human subject study (n=21) to evaluate the impact of LightBender misalignment on the perceived illuminations. Obtained results demonstrate that the system's 10.1 mm maximum misalignment is perceptually acceptable across tested illuminations, with a median quality rating of 8 on a 0-10 scale.

</details>


### [562] [Multimodal Image Colorization: Quantifying the Impact of Text-Conditioned Guidance on Grayscale-to-Color Translation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20722)
*Colten Reissmann,Hugo Garrido-Lestache Belinchon*

Main category: cs.GR

TL;DR: This paper quantifies how CLIP text conditioning improves grayscale-to-color image generation across U-Net and Stable Diffusion 1.5 architectures, showing consistent gains in PSNR, SSIM, LPIPS, and colorfulness metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Grayscale image colorization is an ill-posed problem with many plausible solutions; the authors aim to quantify whether text conditioning can reduce ambiguity and improve colorization quality in a controlled, apples-to-apples comparison.

Method: Two architectures (U-Net and Stable Diffusion 1.5) are each tested with and without CLIP text conditioning, holding all other variables constant. Metrics include PSNR, SSIM, LPIPS (perceptual), and colorfulness.

Result: Text conditioning improved PSNR by 5.6% (U-Net) and 5.8% (SD), SSIM by 1.2% and 1.5%, colorfulness by 36.6% and 0.6%, and reduced LPIPS by 7.6% and 11.3% respectively.

Conclusion: Text conditioning provides consistent, measurable improvements to colorization quality across both architecture scales, demonstrating its effectiveness as a conditioning signal for the ill-posed colorization task.
