Abstract: Grayscale images are commonly found in historical photography restoration, medical imaging, and artistic media. However, automatically applying color to these images remains a significant challenge in computer vision because many plausible colorizations can correspond to the same grayscale input.
  In this work, we quantify the effect of text conditioning on pixel-level and perceptual metrics for grayscale-to-color image models. Specifically, we compare two architectures, a U-Net and Stable Diffusion 1.5, each tested with and without CLIP text conditioning while holding all other variables constant. Our results show that text conditioning improves PSNR by 5.6%, SSIM by 1.2%, and colorfulness by 36.6%, while reducing LPIPS by 7.6% in the U-Net tier. In the Stable Diffusion tier, text conditioning improves PSNR by 5.8%, SSIM by 1.5%, and colorfulness by 0.6%, while reducing LPIPS by 11.3%. These results indicate that text conditioning provides consistent, measurable improvements to colorization quality across both architecture scales.

</details>


### [563] [PIAvatar: Physically Interactive Avatars via Deformation Gradient Decoupling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21162)
*Sang-Hun Han,Min-Gyu Park,Jisu Shin,Seunghyun Shin,Jin-Hwi Park,Hae-Gon Jeon*

Main category: cs.GR

TL;DR: PIAvatar is a framework for physically aware 3D human avatars that supports non-rigid deformable body simulation during avatar-avatar and avatar-environment interactions by decoupling kinematic velocity from deformation gradient and integrating a skeletal framework for pose estimation and real-time tracking within a Material Point Method framework.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing pose-conditioned 3D avatars lack physical ability for interactions, showing only limited physical deformations and constrained interaction behaviors.

Method: Decouples kinematic velocity from deformation gradient to handle stress induced by external forces; integrates a skeletal framework for closed-form pose estimation and real-time tracking during non-rigid interactions; implemented within a conventional Material Point Method (MPM) framework.

Result: Evaluated on both human-object and human-human interaction scenarios, demonstrating diverse interaction behaviors with physically consistent dynamics.

Conclusion: PIAvatar enables physically aware, non-rigid deformable interactions between avatars and with environments, overcoming limitations of prior physically-based avatar models.

Abstract: 3D human avatars have shown impressive visual fidelity driven by pose-conditioned models, yet they still lack the physical ability required for interactions with each other and environments. Although recent studies have made various attempts to incorporate physical characteristics into 3D avatars, they only exhibit limited physical deformations, often leading to constrained interaction behaviors. To resolve this issue, we present PIAvatar, a framework to simultaneously enable physically aware interactions between avatar-avatar and avatar-environment, and a non-rigid deformable human body simulation. In this work, our key insight is to decouple kinematic velocity from deformation gradient. When external forces act on avatars, the kinematic velocity induces stress which hinders the avatar's ability to achieve a desired pose. In addition, we integrate a skeletal framework within the avatar. It allows estimating its poses and real-time tracking in a closed form, even during non-rigid physical interactions. Our approach is implemented within a conventional Material Point Method framework to ensure physically consistent dynamics. We lastly evaluate the method on both human-object and human-human interaction scenarios to assess its behavior under diverse interaction settings.

</details>


### [564] [Illuminating English Letters Using a Flying Light Speck](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22592)
*Hamed Alimohammadzadeh,Shahram Ghandeharizadeh*

Main category: cs.GR

TL;DR: This paper presents the design and implementation of a Flying Light Speck (FLS) to illuminate English letters, using onboard camera and computing for localization and trajectory following. It reports 42-56mm illumination errors affecting letter detection, and finds that presentation order significantly impacts detection duration based on a human subject study with 20 participants.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To design and implement a Flying Light Speck capable of illuminating English letters through autonomous localization and trajectory following, with quantitative and qualitative evaluation of the illumination performance.

Method: The FLS uses onboard camera and computing for self-localization and trajectory following to illuminate letters. Evaluation includes quantitative error measurement and qualitative IRB-approved human subject study with 20 participants assessing detection performance.

Result: 42-56 millimeter illumination error that impacts letter detection. Presentation order of letter illumination significantly affects detection duration in human subjects.

Conclusion: The FLS system achieves autonomous letter illumination with measurable error, and human perception of illuminated letters is influenced by presentation order, suggesting the need for sequence-aware illumination strategies.

Abstract: This paper presents the design and implementation of a Flying Light Speck (FLS) to illuminate English letters. The FLS uses its onboard camera and computing to localize and follow a trajectory to illuminate a letter. We evaluate the illuminations quantitatively and qualitatively. The latter is based on an IRB approved human subject study with 20 participants. The obtained results show a 42 to 56 millimeter error that impacts the detection of letters. A key finding is that the order in which the illumination of letters is presented to subjects has a significant effect on detection duration.

</details>


### [565] [Scene-Level Heterogeneous Physics Simulation with 3D Gaussian Splats](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21753)
*Xiaoyang Liu,Shangzhe Wu,Kai Han*

Main category: cs.GR

TL;DR: This paper proposes a Representation Abstraction Framework that bridges the gap between 3D Gaussian Splatting assets and production-grade physics engines by translating diverse assets (3DGS, meshes, fluids) into a unified physical particle set, enabling scene-level, heterogeneous, multi-solver physical simulations with complex two-way interactions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 3DGS achieves photorealistic rendering but cannot be physically interacted with due to representation incompatibility with physics engines, and prior physics-for-3DGS methods are monolithic silos limited to object-centric, isolated environments incapable of handling complex static collision geometry or heterogeneous assets.

Method: A Representation Abstraction Framework that translates all diverse assets (3DGS, virtual meshes, fluids) into a unified physical particle set, processes them in a solver-agnostic physics kernel, and maps results back to drive each asset's visual reconstruction.

Result: Demonstrates complex two-way interactions between deformable 3DGS assets, standard CG assets (fluids, meshes), and large-scale captured static environments, enabling realistic coupled phenomena previously unattainable.

Conclusion: The framework bridges the representation gap for the first time, enabling 3DGS assets to participate in scene-level, heterogeneous, multi-solver physical simulations with capabilities impossible with prior art.

Abstract: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has achieved state-of-the-art photorealistic rendering, but the representation gap prevents these assets from being physically interactive. Production-grade physics engines do not understand the 3DGS representation, while prior physics-for-3DGS methods are monolithic silos. These prior works are fundamentally limited, demonstrating only object-centric physics in isolated environments, such as on an ideal plane. They are incapable of interacting with complex static collision geometry or heterogeneous assets. We propose a novel framework that, for the first time, bridges this gap by enabling 3DGS assets to participate in scene-level, heterogeneous, multi-solver physical simulations. Our core contribution is a Representation Abstraction Framework that translates all diverse assets, including 3DGS, virtual meshes, and fluids, into a unified physical particle set. This abstraction is key to enabling complex behaviors, such as the non-rigid deformation of 3DGS assets, within a unified physics pipeline. This particle set, along with the static scene collision boundaries derived from scene capture, is processed within a solver-agnostic physics kernel. The physical results are then mapped back to drive each asset's specific visual reconstruction. This architecture unlocks capabilities impossible with prior art. We demonstrate complex, two-way interactions between deformable 3DGS assets, standard CG assets such as fluids and meshes, and large-scale captured static environments, showcasing realistic coupled phenomena that were previously unattainable.

</details>


### [566] [Single-Event Upsets in 3D Gaussian Splatting Rendering: Bit-Level Criticality, Spatial Extent, and a Parallel Support Guard](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21791)
*Faruk Alpay,Baris Basaran*

Main category: cs.GR

TL;DR: This paper proposes a lightweight "support guard" defense for 3D Gaussian splatting against single-bit upsets (silent data corruption). By clamping parameters to training-observed coordinate boxes, the guard prevents catastrophic frame-covering corruption while adding only 76 μs per frame. Over 768K guarded upsets, worst-case corruption is limited to 11.68% of the frame, and under 20,000 simultaneous upsets, PSNR improves from 10.6 dB (unguarded) to 21.8 dB (guarded).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 3D Gaussian splatting is increasingly deployed on hardware exposed to transient faults (space, robotics), where single-bit flips cause silent data corruption. No prior work has characterized fault effects on this representation or built a defense.

Method: (1) A GPU-parallel fault-injection engine applies 3.8M controlled single-bit upsets across 4 scenes, 6 parameter fields, all bit positions, and 3 formats (fp32, fp16, bf16). (2) A closed-form perturbation bound from IEEE-754 layout + pipeline activations predicts per-bit importance. (3) A "support guard" clamps each primitive's parameters to its training-observed coordinate box at render time.

Result: Most upsets are benign due to redundancy; high-order bits (especially sign bit) can enlarge a single primitive to cover up to 75.7% of the frame. The guard costs 76 μs/frame, limits worst-case corruption to 11.68% of the frame, and under 20K simultaneous upsets improves PSNR from 10.6 dB to 21.8 dB.

Conclusion: The support guard is provably harmless to clean models, prevents frame-covering corruption, contains corruption in distributed renderers, and is lightweight enough for real-time deployment on fault-prone hardware.

Abstract: Three-dimensional Gaussian splatting is a standard real-time scene representation increasingly deployed on hardware exposed to transient faults, such as spaceborne processors and robotic edge devices where silent data corruption occurs. A trained model is a large array of floating-point parameters in GPU memory, where a single-event upset corresponds to a single flipped bit. This paper measures these effects and constructs a defense. A GPU-resident parallel fault-injection engine applies over 3.8 million controlled single-bit upsets across four scenes, six fields, all bit positions, and three numeric formats (fp32, fp16, bf16), using 5.3 GPU-hours. The effect is highly concentrated: most upsets leave the image perceptually unchanged due to high redundancy, but a small set of high-order bits principally the logarithmic scale's sign bit enlarge a single primitive to cover up to 75.7% of the frame. A closed-form perturbation bound derived from the IEEE-754 layout and pipeline activations predicts this per-bit ordering. This concentration motivates a support guard: a per-primitive clamp of each parameter to the coordinate box observed during training, costing 76 us per frame. Over 768,000 guarded upsets, the worst corruption footprint is restricted to 11.68% of the frame. We prove the guard leaves clean models unchanged and prevents frame-covering corruption. Under an accumulated dose of 20,000 simultaneous upsets, the unguarded renderer degrades to 10.6 dB, whereas the guarded renderer remains at 21.8 dB. The corruption footprint also dictates the number of tile/compositing nodes contaminated in distributed renderers, where the per-node guard contains it.

</details>


### [567] [Mesh2GS: White-Box 3DGS Construction via Plenoptic Sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21898)
*Haoran Zhu,Youcheng Cai,Huangsheng Du,Jingyang Meng,Ligang Liu*

Main category: cs.GR

TL;DR: Mesh2GS converts mesh geometry into 3D Gaussian Splatting representations using plenoptic sampling theory, achieving Nyquist-level performance for high-quality global illumination rendering with albedo-shading decomposition and neural illumination enhancement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods focus on 3DGS-to-mesh conversion, while the inverse problem of converting mesh to 3DGS has received less attention and relies on heuristic strategies rather than principled approaches.

Method: 1) Plenoptic sampling guided 3DGS construction with theoretically derived minimum sampling rates; 2) Albedo-shading decomposition for efficient global illumination capture; 3) Neural illumination enhancement module for non-Lambertian effects.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art baselines; effective for real-time shared rendering and non-Lambertian effects such as specular highlights.

Conclusion: Mesh2GS provides a white-box, theoretically grounded framework for mesh-to-3DGS conversion that achieves high-quality global illumination rendering with practical effectiveness.

Abstract: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has emerged as a promising method for high-quality, real-time 3D reconstruction. To associate 3DGS with mesh representations, existing methods primarily focus on 3DGS-to-mesh reconstruction from multi-view images. In contrast, the problem of converting a mesh into 3DGS has received comparatively less attention. Instead of relying on heuristic strategies that bind 3D Gaussians to the mesh, we propose a novel white-box 3DGS construction framework, termed Mesh2GS, which generates 3DGS directly from mesh geometry based on plenoptic sampling theory, achieving Nyquist-level performance for high-quality global illumination rendering. Firstly, we propose a plenoptic sampling guided 3DGS construction strategy that theoretically derives the minimum sampling rate of the sampled views and the distribution of 3D Gaussians. Second, we propose a novel 3DGS update procedure with albedo--shading decomposition for efficient global-illumination capture. Finally, we introduce a neural illumination enhancement module to handle non-Lambertian effects. Experimental results demonstrate that our method surpasses state-of-the-art baselines and is practically effective for both real-time shared rendering and non-Lambertian effects capturing specular highlights. The project code will be released upon acceptance.

</details>


### [568] [Lighting-Consistent Object Transfer Across Radiance Fields](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22481)
*Nicolás Violante,George Kopanas,Linus Franke,Julien Philip,George Drettakis*

Main category: cs.GR

TL;DR: This paper introduces a diffusion model for harmonizing lighting-inconsistent composited objects when transferring them between 3D Gaussian Splatting scenes, producing visually compelling results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Naively compositing objects extracted from one 3DGS scene into another yields unrealistic results due to mismatched lighting conditions between source and target scenes.

Method: A diffusion model trained on a heterogeneous dataset (synthetic, generated, and real image pairs) harmonizes inconsistent composites; the pipeline includes extracting an object, rendering inconsistent views, harmonizing each view with the diffusion model, and post-optimizing into a consistent 3DGS representation.

Result: The method significantly improves visual quality compared to previous methods for object transfer between 3DGS scenes.

Conclusion: The approach makes object transfer between 3DGS easy to use, producing visually compelling results through learned lighting harmonization.

Abstract: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) is widely used to capture and render real scenes. Compositing objects from one capture into another has applications in many domains, such as VFX, architecture and interior design, or marketing. However, extracting an object from a source scene and naively pasting it into a target scene will fail to produce realistic results due to the different lighting conditions between the two scenes. To address this problem, we introduce a diffusion model that harmonizes naively composited images with inconsistent lighting. The model is trained with a heterogeneous dataset of image pairs (inconsistent composite input, consistent output), combining synthetic, generated, and real data. Our complete 3D solution allows a user to extract an object from the source scene and composite it into the target scene. From this, the (inconsistent) views of the target scene with the composite object are rendered. Our diffusion model harmonizes each one of these views, which are finally consolidated in a 3DGS representation with a post-optimization step. Our method provides visually compelling results, making object transfer between 3DGS easy to use and significantly improving quality compared to previous methods.

</details>


### [569] [DJM: Compact Base Meshes for Displacement Mapping using Triangle Jacobians](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22880)
*Congyi Zhang,Nicholas Vining,Yanhong Lin,Alireza Khatami,Ziyu Sun,Xiaohu Guo,Wenping Wang,Alla Sheffer*

Main category: cs.GR

TL;DR: This paper introduces DJM (Displacement Jacobian Metric), a novel base-mesh construction method that uses the Jacobian of displacement functions to guide simplification, producing compact base meshes with bijective, low-distortion displacement mappings for accurate mesh reconstruction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The core challenge in converting detailed triangle meshes into displacement-based representations is computing base meshes with minimal triangles while supporting displacement functions that accurately approximate the input, requiring bijective mappings with low parametric distortion.

Method: DJM measures distortion via the pointwise Jacobian of displacement functions, proposes a QEM-based simplification variant constrained to produce bijective, low-distortion displacement mappings (satisfying a lower bound on the Jacobian), and uses explicit mapping storage with a robust inverse barycentric displacement solver to avoid unreliable ray-mesh intersections.

Result: DJM outperforms prior approaches in reconstruction accuracy-to-size trade-off, and demonstrates robustness for micromesh-based rendering and neural encoding applications.

Conclusion: The Jacobian-guided base-mesh construction enables superior accuracy-to-compactness trade-offs, with practical utility in modern rendering and neural representation pipelines.

Abstract: Representing complex geometry as a displacement function defined over a coarse base mesh enables compact storage and accelerated rendering. The core challenge in converting detailed triangle meshes into this representation is computing base meshes that have as few triangles as possible, while also supporting displacement functions that accurately approximate the input. Accurate approximation requires the supported displacement functions to bijectively map the input surface onto the base with low parametric distortion. We observe that this distortion can be measured by evaluating the pointwise Jacobian of the displacement functions. Our new DJM (Displacement Jacobian Metric)-based base-mesh construction method uses the Jacobian of the displacement functions to guide base mesh computation, enabling us to outperform prior approaches in terms of accuracy to size trade-off. We achieve this goal by proposing a variant of the QEM-based simplification scheme that constrains the displacement mapping between the input and the base to be bijective and low distortion (defined as satisfying a lower bound on the mapping Jacobian). When evaluating and encoding the displacement maps, we avoid unreliable ray-mesh intersections by explicitly storing the mapping between the input mesh and the base throughout the construction process, and use this mapping within a robust inverse barycentric displacement solver to obtain dense base-to-mesh correspondences to assist all computations. We demonstrate DJM to outperform alternative schemes in terms of reconstruction accuracy to size trade-off, and demonstrate its robustness and usability for micromesh-based rendering and neural encoding.

</details>


### [570] [Controllable Texture Tiling with Transformed RoPE-Enhanced Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22945)
*Junrong Huang,Zhiyuan Zhang,Rui Tang,Hongbo Fu,Jnig Liao*

Main category: cs.GR

TL;DR: A novel Diffusion Transformer-based framework for controllable texture tiling that uses Coordinate-Transformed Rotary Embedding for precise spatial control and Disjoint Attention Mask to preserve structural integrity and scene coherence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing material transfer and reference-guided inpainting methods fail at texture tiling due to destructive pixel-level resampling, lack of fine-grained spatial information, and inability to preserve scene lighting and geometry.

Method: Two innovations: (1) Coordinate-Transformed Rotary Embedding applies 2D affine transformations to relative positional embeddings between target latent and image condition, enabling precise control over tiling patterns without explicit pixel warping; (2) Disjoint Attention Mask shields reference features from semantic leakage, preserving structural integrity while blending textures with original scene lighting/geometry.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in both control accuracy and texture fidelity.

Conclusion: The proposed Diffusion Transformer framework achieves high-fidelity, controllable texture tiling by decoupling spatial manipulation from content generation, effectively addressing limitations of prior methods.

Abstract: Realistic integration of user-specified textures into scene images is a fundamental task in computer graphics and image editing. While existing material transfer and reference-guided inpainting methods can edit surface appearances, they often fail to address the specific requirements of texture tiling. This task necessitates precisely repeating a reference pattern according to user-defined parameters such as frequency, orientation, and scale. Furthermore, current generative approaches often struggle to maintain the structural fidelity of the reference texture, limited by either destructive pixel-level resampling or the lack of fine-grained spatial information in semantic image encoders, and they frequently fail to preserve the coherent lighting and geometry of the original scene. In this paper, we propose a novel framework for controllable and high-fidelity texture tiling based on Diffusion Transformers. Our approach introduces two key technical innovations to decouple spatial manipulation from content generation. First, we propose a Coordinate-Transformed Rotary Embedding mechanism. By applying 2D affine transformations directly to the relative positional embeddings between the target latent and the image condition, we achieve precise control over tiling patterns without explicit pixel warping, thereby utilizing the full information of the reference condition without degradation. Second, a Disjoint Attention Mask is employed to shield reference features from semantic leakage. This preserves structural integrity while seamlessly blending the synthesized texture with the scene's original lighting and geometry. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in both control accuracy and texture fidelity.

</details>


### [571] [VolHuMe: a High-Resolution Large Scale Dataset of Volumetric Human Meshes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23062)
*Giulia Martinelli,Niccolò Bisagno,Nicola Garau,Esa Rahtu,Nicola Conci*

Main category: cs.GR

TL;DR: VolHuMe is a high-quality 4D human scan dataset of 104 subjects captured with 64 RGB and 32 depth cameras, offering extensive ground truth (SMPL-X, meshes, segmentation, etc.) via a close-range setup that preserves fine details. Benchmarks reveal limitations in current human reconstruction methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing human datasets primarily rely on full-body imagery and lack fine-grained body-part details, limiting geometric fidelity and texture resolution for 4D human reconstruction.

Method: A volumetric studio with 64 RGB and 32 depth cameras captures 104 subjects in close-range, high-resolution 4D scans, producing SMPL-X, high-res meshes, multi-view RGB/depth, rigged meshes, point clouds, garment segmentation, and detailed hand/face geometry.

Result: The dataset provides superior geometric fidelity and texture resolution compared to prior datasets; benchmarking on state-of-the-art 3D/4D reconstruction methods reveals limitations in current evaluation testbeds.

Conclusion: VolHuMe advances 4D human reconstruction research by offering high-quality ground truth with fine-grained detail, exposing shortcomings of existing methods and evaluation benchmarks.

Abstract: We introduce VolHuMe, a dataset of high-quality 4D human scans captured with a state-of-the-art volumetric studio using 64 RGB and 32 depth cameras. VolHuMe contains individual captures of 104 subjects and provides extensive ground truth, including SMPL-X, high-resolution meshes, multi-view RGB/depth images, rigged meshes, point clouds, garment segmentation, and detailed hand and facial geometry. Unlike prior datasets that primarily rely on full-body imagery, VolHuMe uses a close-range, high-resolution capture setup that preserves fine-grained body-part details, improving geometric fidelity and texture resolution. We benchmark VolHuMe on state-of-the-art methods across 3D and 4D human reconstruction tasks, showcasing the dataset's quality and exposing the limitations of current evaluation testbeds.

</details>


### [572] [MeshFlow: Mesh Generation with Equivariant Flow Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23489)
*Qi Sun,Kiyohiro Nakayama,Jing Nathan Yan,Qixing Huang,Alexander Rush,Leonidas Guibas,Gordon Wetzstein,Jing Liao,Guandao Yang*

Main category: cs.GR

TL;DR: MeshFlow is a generative model that directly produces triangle meshes via equivariant optimal-transport flow matching, respecting permutation symmetries of faces and vertices, achieving comparable quality to autoregressive methods with ~18× inference speedup.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Meshes are common 3D representations but are difficult to generate directly due to inherent symmetries (permutation invariance of faces and vertices). Existing methods often serialize meshes into long autoregressive sequences, which is inefficient and fails to respect these symmetries.

Method: MeshFlow uses equivariant optimal-transport flow matching to generate triangle meshes as triangle soups. It modifies the Diffusion Transformer architecture to maintain desired equivariance (permutation of faces and vertices within each face) and employs an optimal-transport-based training objective that removes symmetry-violating supervision signals for better convergence.

Result: MeshFlow achieves mesh quality comparable to state-of-the-art autoregressive mesh generators while providing approximately an 18× speedup during inference.

Conclusion: Directly generating meshes with symmetry-aware equivariant flow matching is both effective and efficient, outperforming autoregressive approaches in speed while maintaining quality.

Abstract: Meshes are among the most common 3D scene representations, but directly generating meshes is challenging because the representation contains important symmetries, including permutation invariance of faces and vertices. MeshFlow learns to generate triangle meshes directly as triangle soups, avoiding the need to serialize meshes into long autoregressive sequences. We adopt equivariant optimal-transport flow matching models that respect the key symmetries of triangle soups: arbitrary permutations of faces and permutations of the vertices within each face.
  Toward this goal, we propose a simple yet effective modification to the Diffusion Transformer architecture, resulting in a scalable network capable of modeling a velocity field while maintaining the desired equivariance. We further introduce an optimal-transport-based training objective that improves convergence by eliminating supervision signals that violate these symmetries. MeshFlow achieves mesh quality comparable to state-of-the-art autoregressive mesh generators while providing about an 18$\times$ speedup during inference. Project page is at https://qiisun.github.io/MeshFlow/.

</details>


<div id='cs.LG'></div>

# cs.LG [[Back]](#toc)

### [573] [Towards CSI-Native Foundation Models: A Channel-Adaptive Roadmap for 6G](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20670)
*Chenyu Zhang,Xinchen Lyu,Chenshan Ren,Shuhan Liu,Qimei Cui*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a channel-adaptive framework for CSI-native foundation models in 6G, aligning pretraining, positional modeling, and attention control with physical propagation conditions. It achieves superior zero-shot generalization (NMSE reduction >4 dB), scale extrapolation (up to 5.4 dB gain), and inference efficiency (up to 18.8% acceleration). System-level evaluation shows only 7.01% dense-pilot overhead with 36.6% spectral efficiency improvement over LMMSE.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing generic-backbone adaptation and CSI pretraining methods treat CSI as task tensors rather than propagation-conditioned channel responses, failing to capture the intrinsic time-frequency-spatial geometry of wireless environments.

Method: Proposes a unified framework that aligns pretraining, positional modeling, and attention control with three channel requirements: scale-aware heterogeneous exposure, physical time-frequency-antenna coordinates, and correlation-bounded token interaction.

Result: Zero-shot generalization reduces NMSE by >4 dB across spatial-temporal-frequency tasks; scale extrapolation yields up to 5.4 dB gain under 8x unseen antenna scaling; inference acceleration up to 18.8%. System-level: 7.01% dense-pilot overhead, -18.64 dB average NMSE, 36.6% net spectral efficiency improvement over LMMSE and 15.5% over WiFo.

Conclusion: CSI-native representation learning can support pilot-efficient radio access, providing a path toward reusable CSI intelligence for 6G systems.

Abstract: Wireless foundation models offer a path toward reusable channel state information (CSI) intelligence for sixth-generation (6G) systems. However, existing generic-backbone adaptation and CSI pretraining methods often treat CSI as task tensors rather than propagation-conditioned channel responses, thereby failing to capture the intrinsic time-frequency-spatial geometry of wireless environments. This paper presents a channel-adaptive roadmap toward CSI-native foundation models, proposing a unified framework that aligns pretraining, positional modeling, and attention control with three channel requirements: scale-aware heterogeneous exposure, physical time-frequency-antenna coordinates, and correlation-bounded token interaction. Extensive experiments demonstrate the superiority of the proposed framework across three dimensions: zero-shot generalization, reducing NMSE by more than 4 dB across spatial-temporal-frequency tasks; scale extrapolation, yielding up to a 5.4 dB gain under 8 times unseen antenna scaling; and inference efficiency, accelerating mobility-aware processing by up to 18.8%. A system-level evaluation with Sionna SYS further shows that the proposed framework uses only 7.01% of dense-pilot overhead, reaches -18.64 dB average NMSE, and improves average net spectral efficiency by 36.6% over dense LMMSE and 15.5% over WiFo, indicating that CSI-native representation learning can support pilot-efficient radio access.

</details>


### [574] [NeuroShield: A Device-Agnostic Foundation Model for EEG Authentication](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20673)
*Matin Fallahi,Patricia Arias-Cabarcos,Thorsten Strufe*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: NeuroShield is a reusable foundation model for EEG authentication that uses a dual-stage transformer architecture to learn identity-discriminative embeddings from variable-channel and variable-length recordings, pretrained on 15,762 subjects across 3 datasets, and achieves 0.44–8.06 percentage point EER reduction over state-of-the-art while generalizing to unseen channel layouts and longer segments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: EEG authentication models are tied to specific acquisition settings (hardware, channel layout, signal duration), causing fragmentation where each new headset/dataset requires separate model development, limiting multi-dataset learning, knowledge transfer, and model reusability.

Method: A dual-stage transformer architecture pretrained on 3 public EEG datasets (15,762 subjects, 28,116 sessions) that learns identity-discriminative embeddings from variable-channel and variable-length EEG recordings, then fine-tuned on unseen downstream datasets.

Result: After fine-tuning, NeuroShield reduces equal error rate by 0.44–8.06 percentage points relative to state-of-the-art, and generalizes to longer segments and channel layouts not encountered during pretraining.

Conclusion: NeuroShield is established as a reusable and adaptable EEG identity encoder across heterogeneous recording settings, released as open source for reproducibility and community adoption.

Abstract: A central challenge in EEG authentication is that models are typically tied to the acquisition settings in which they are trained. In particular, variations in headset hardware, channel layout, and signal duration create heterogeneous recordings that existing models are not designed to handle, causing each new headset or dataset to be treated as a separate model-development problem. This fragmentation limits multi-dataset learning, hinders knowledge transfer, and reduces model reusability. To address this limitation, we present NeuroShield, a reusable foundation model for EEG authentication that learns identity-discriminative embeddings from variable-channel and variable-length EEG recordings through a dual-stage transformer architecture. We pretrain NeuroShield on three public EEG datasets comprising 15{,}762 subjects and 28{,}116 sessions, and evaluate transfer on two unseen downstream datasets. Our evaluations show that, after fine-tuning, NeuroShield reduces equal error rate by 0.44--8.06 percentage points relative to the state of the art. NeuroShield further generalizes to segments longer than those seen during training and operates across channel layouts not encountered during pretraining. These results establish NeuroShield as a reusable and adaptable EEG identity encoder across heterogeneous recording settings. We release NeuroShield as open source to support reproducibility and community adoption.

</details>


### [575] [Massive Activations Are Architecturally Robust: A Controlled Scratch/Commitment Residual Stream Test](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20743)
*Maruthi Vemula*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Transformers develop massive activations (outlier dimensions on the start token). The paper tests whether these are artifacts of the residual stream's dual role as scratchpad and answer channel. Using a Ledger Residual architecture that separates these roles, they find outliers persist in the protected decode channel, suggesting they are functionally necessary, not incidental artifacts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether massive activations in transformers are removable artifacts caused by the residual stream being overloaded with both read/write (scratchpad) and decode (answer) functions, or if they serve a functional role.

Method: Proposes Ledger Residuals, an architecture that splits the residual stream into: (1) a mutable scratch stream (Deliberation) for intermediate computation, and (2) a protected, decode-only accumulator (Commitment) for the final readout representation. Trains matched-loss language models at 160M and 290M scales and analyzes outlier formation in the protected channel.

Result: Outliers re-emerge in the protected Commitment channel even though it is not used for intermediate computation. The rebuilt outliers are smaller in magnitude but more sharply concentrated on the start token. Adding a stronger sparsity penalty makes outliers more persistent and concentrated, not less.

Conclusion: Massive activations are architecturally robust—they reappear in whatever representation the model decodes from—supporting the hypothesis that they are functional rather than incidental artifacts.

Abstract: Trained transformers reliably develop massive activations, a small number of hidden dimensions whose magnitude is far above the median and which concentrate on the sequence-start token. Whether these outliers are a removable artifact of the residual stream's overloaded read and write role, or instead a functional necessity, is actively debated. We test the artifact hypothesis directly, with an architectural intervention. Our architecture, Ledger Residuals, splits the residual stream into a mutable scratch stream (Deliberation) that intermediate computation may freely overwrite and a protected, decode-only accumulator (Commitment) that holds the representation the model reads out. If massive activations exist only because one stream is forced to be both scratchpad and answer, then a dedicated answer channel should remove the need for them. We find that it does not. In matched-loss language models at the 160M and 290M scales, the model rebuilds the canonical fixed-dimension, start-token outlier inside the protected channel. The rebuilt feature is smaller in magnitude than in a standard transformer but more sharply concentrated on the start token, and a stronger sparsity penalty makes it more persistent and more concentrated still, rather than removing it. Massive activations therefore look architecturally robust: they re-emerge in whichever representation the model decodes from, which is what we would expect if they are functional rather than incidental. We release our architecture and measurement code.

</details>


### [576] [CIExplainer++: Generating Causal and Interpretable Explanations for Graph Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20747)
*Francisco Caldas,Sahil Satish Kumar,Ruben Belo,Cláudia Soares*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces CIExplainer, a perturbation-based causal inference method for explaining GNN predictions by identifying causally influential subgraphs, and G2TeXplainer, which converts those subgraphs into natural language explanations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To make black-box GNN models more trustworthy by identifying components with genuine causal influence on outputs and translating them into human-understandable representations.

Method: CIExplainer uses the Potential Outcome Framework to identify subgraphs with the highest causal effects on GNN predictions. G2TeXplainer transforms causal subgraphs into natural language explanations capturing both feature-level and relational information.

Result: CIExplainer is evaluated on multiple GNN architectures (GCN, GraphSAGE, GAT, GIN) and datasets.

Conclusion: The combination of CIExplainer and G2TeXplainer provides a complete pipeline from causal subgraph identification to human-interpretable natural language explanations for GNN predictions.

Abstract: Explainable Artificial Intelligence aims to make black-box models more trustworthy by presenting, in a human-understandable manner, the elements that lead to the model's output. This involves both (i) identifying components and connections with genuine causal influence on outputs and (ii) translating such structures into an interpretable representation. For the former, we introduce CIExplainer, a novel perturbation-based method grounded in causal inference for explaining Graph Neural Networks (GNNs). CIExplainer identifies the subgraph with the highest causal effects on GNN predictions using the Potential Outcome Framework. We evaluate and compare CIExplainer on various GNN architectures (GCN, GraphSAGE, GAT, GIN) and datasets.
  To bridge subgraph explanations with human interpretability, we further propose G2TeXplainer, a method that transforms causal subgraphs into natural language explanations that capture both feature-level and relational information.

</details>


### [577] [Evidential Fusion Network for Multimodal Survival Prediction under Missing Modalities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20757)
*Yucheng Xing,Hailan Mo,Zi Wang,Ling Huang,Mengling Feng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EMMS is a multimodal survival prediction model that handles missing modalities using Dempster-Shafer theory and Gaussian Random Fuzzy Numbers, achieving state-of-the-art performance with calibrated uncertainty estimates without requiring generative imputation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multimodal survival prediction models assume data completeness and lack robustness toward missing modalities, which are common in real-world clinical settings.

Method: EMMS employs Dempster-Shafer theory and Gaussian Random Fuzzy Numbers for multimodal decision fusion, considering both aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty alongside modality reliability. Missing modalities are treated as vacuous evidence to prevent interference.

Result: State-of-the-art performance on four cancer datasets with calibrated and interpretable uncertainty estimates under incomplete multimodal observations, without additional computational overhead.

Conclusion: EMMS provides a straightforward, computationally effective approach to multimodal survival prediction under missing modalities that does not require a generative phase for missing data.

Abstract: Recent multimodal survival prediction models have demonstrated strong predictive performance by leveraging complementary information across modalities. However, such models generally assume data completeness and exhibit limited robustness toward missing modalities, which are frequently encountered in real-world clinical settings. We propose the Evidential Missing Modality Survival Fusion (EMMS) model for multimodal survival prediction under missing modalities. EMMS offers a straightforward, computationally effective approach to survival analysis without requiring a generative phase for missing data. By employing Dempster-Shafer theory and Gaussian Random Fuzzy Numbers for multimodal decision fusion, it considers both aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty alongside modality reliability for fusion. Moreover, the model treats missing modalities as vacuous evidence, preventing interference with available inputs and naturally reflecting increased uncertainty and calibrated predictions. Extensive experiments on four cancer datasets demonstrate state-of-the-art performance while providing calibrated and interpretable uncertainty estimates under incomplete multimodal observations, without introducing additional computational overhead.

</details>


### [578] [ELADO: Elliptic PDE Assessment Datasets for Operator Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20771)
*Frank Ehebrecht,Toni Scharle,Martin Atzmueller*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ELADO is a benchmark suite of elliptic PDE datasets designed to expose specific failure modes of neural operators, including heavy-tailed solution distributions, spectral distribution shift, frequency-domain heavy tails, input sensitivity (local Lipschitz), and input signal complexity effects, revealing that standard metrics like mean relative L² error obscure substantial accuracy degradation under these challenges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks focus on average-case performance and fail to highlight systematic failure modes of neural operators when learning solution operators for elliptic PDEs, motivating the construction of datasets that isolate specific sources of difficulty.

Method: The authors construct multiple datasets based on Poisson's and Helmholtz equations with non-constant coefficients, using a controllable data-generating process. They isolate five failure sources: heavy-tailed solution distributions, spectral shift of inputs, heavy-tailed frequency-domain distributions, input sensitivity via local Lipschitz analysis, and input signal complexity under controlled amplitude normalization.

Result: Heavy-tailed targets, spectral shift, and input sensitivity each cause substantial degradation of prediction accuracy that is obscured by standard metrics like mean relative L² error across several neural operator architectures.

Conclusion: Standard datasets and metrics can mask critical failure modes in neural operators; ELADO provides a more diagnostic benchmark to reveal these weaknesses systematically.

Abstract: We introduce ELADO (Elliptic PDE Assessment Datasets for Operator Learning), a systematic benchmark suite constructed to show and quantify failure modes of neural operator architectures when learning solution operators of elliptic PDEs. While the benchmarks of existing datasets focus on average case performance, the ELADO datasets are constructed to highlight challenges that arise naturally in elliptic PDE problems. In particular, we construct several datasets built around Poisson's equation and the Helmholtz equation, each with non-constant coefficients. We define a controllable data-generating process to create datasets, that are designed to isolate a distinct source of difficulty. Specifically, these are (1) heavy-tailed solution distributions arising from light-tailed coefficient field distributions, (2) spectral distribution shift of the input data, (3) heavy-tailed distributions in the frequency domain of solutions, arising from light-tailed coefficient field distributions, (4) input sensitivity of learned operators, quantified by an empirical local Lipschitz analysis, and (5) the effect of input signal complexity on prediction accuracy under controlled amplitude normalization. We evaluate several neural operator architectures across all datasets and show that heavy-tailed targets, spectral shift, and input sensitivity each cause substantial degradation of the prediction accuracy that standard datasets and metrics (e.g., the mean relative $L^2$ error) may obscure.

</details>


### [579] [B[FM]$^2$: Brain Foundation Model via Flow Matching with SplitUNet](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20812)
*Jaedong Hwang,Kathleen Zhang,Wei Dai,Konstantinos Kontras,Maarten Vanmarcke,Maarten De Vos,Ila Fiete,Paul Pu Liang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: B[FM]² is an EEG foundation model pretrained directly on raw multi-channel signals using continuous-time flow matching (no patches/tokenization/masking). It introduces SplitUNet to handle time-electrode asymmetry and achieves SOTA on 7/9 downstream tasks with 30x less pretraining data than existing models, and generates synthetic EEG indistinguishable from real data by neurologists.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing EEG foundation models discretize continuous multi-channel waveforms into patches or codebook tokens, which fragments continuous brain rhythms and obscures fine-grained temporal dynamics. The authors aim to preserve the continuous nature of EEG signals during pretraining.

Method: Uses continuous-time flow matching pretraining directly on raw EEG signals (no patches, tokenization, or masking). Proposes SplitUNet, a velocity network that factorizes each block into separate 1D temporal and 1D electrode convolutions, downsampling only along the time axis to preserve electrode topology and address time-electrode asymmetry (dense timepoints vs. few channels).

Result: Achieves SOTA on 7 out of 9 standard downstream EEG classification tasks. Requires only 36,895 segments (~307h) for pretraining, which is 1-2 orders of magnitude (~30x) less than existing EEG foundation models. Generates synthetic EEGs that two board-certified neurologists cannot distinguish from real data (Cohen's κ = -0.096).

Conclusion: B[FM]² demonstrates that flow matching with a properly designed architecture (SplitUNet) can outperform existing EEG foundation models with far less pretraining data, while also enabling high-fidelity EEG generation that fools human experts.

Abstract: EEG foundation models can learn generalizable representations from large-scale EEG corpora to enable single-backbone transfer across diverse clinical and brain-computer interface tasks. Existing models typically discretize the continuous multi-channel EEG waveform into patches or codebook tokens and train a transformer with masked self-supervision. Recognizing that this discretization fragments continuous brain rhythms and obscures fine-grained temporal dynamics, we present B[FM]$^2$(Brain Foundation Model via Flow Matching), whose inductive bias aligns with the data by pretraining directly on the raw signal using continuous-time flow matching without patches, tokenization, or masking. However, multi-channel EEG signals pose an architectural challenge for flow matching: time is densely sampled and highly autocorrelated (thousands of timepoints), while the electrode axis is short (tens of channels) at distinct scalp positions. To address this time-electrode asymmetry, we introduce SplitUNet, a velocity network that factorizes each block into separate 1D temporal and 1D electrode convolutions and downsamples only along time, preserving electrode topology throughout the hierarchy. B[FM]$^2$ sets a new state of the art on 7 of 9 standard downstream EEG classification tasks, using a pretraining budget of only 36,895 segments ($\approx$ 307h), 1-2 orders of magnitude ($\approx$ 30x) less than required by existing EEG foundation models. Further, it generates synthetic EEGs that two board-certified neurologists cannot distinguish from brain data (Cohen's $κ=$ -0.096). https://jd730.github.io/projects/BFM2

</details>


### [580] [CELEUS: Certifiable and Efficient LLM Evaluation via E-Processes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20820)
*Zhijian Zhou,Zesheng Ye,Zhaorun Chen,Bo Li,Feng Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes Celeus, a certifiable framework for LLM evaluation that uses E-processes to construct anytime-valid confidence intervals, combining uncertainty-guided sampling and surrogate-assisted approximations to reduce evaluation samples by 54-62% while maintaining statistical guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM evaluation methods that sequentially update confidence intervals fail to maintain claimed coverage (e.g., 95%) when CIs are repeatedly updated and used to decide when to stop, creating a gap between theoretical rigor and practical evaluation.

Method: Celeus leverages E-processes to build anytime-valid CIs, combining (i) uncertainty-guided sampling to select informative evaluation samples, and (ii) surrogate-assisted approximations for unevaluated samples. The signals remain unbiased conditional on the past, enabling statistically-grounded and anytime-valid e-process CIs. The framework also analyzes the oracle variance-optimal sampling rule.

Result: Celeus reaches target precision using 54-62% fewer evaluated samples than baselines while preserving anytime-valid coverage. The CIs can shrink at a near-parametric rate up to logarithmic factors.

Conclusion: The proposed framework bridges the gap between theoretical rigor and practical LLM evaluation by providing certifiable, anytime-valid guarantees while significantly improving sample efficiency through uncertainty-guided sampling and surrogate approximations.

Abstract: Can we trust evaluation scores to capture an LLM's true real-world performance? Certifiable evaluation answers this question by providing guarantee for LLM evaluation. In particular, existing methods sequentially curate evaluation samples and keep updating confidence intervals (CIs) that cover the true performance with high probability (e.g., 95%) until some conditions are satisfied, e.g., the CI width reaches a target precision. However, existing methods are not generally anytime-valid: the claimed coverage (e.g., 95%) may fail when CIs are repeatedly updated and used to decide when to stop, leaving a gap between theoretical rigor and practice. This paper bridges this gap by proposing Celeus, a Certifiable framework for Efficient LLM evaluation, which leverages E-processes to build anytime-valid CIs. Concretely, we propose signals that combine two ingredients: (i) Uncertainty-guided sampling to select informative samples for evaluation, and (ii) Surrogate-assisted approximations for unevaluated samples. We prove that such signals remain unbiased for the evaluation score conditional on the past, enabling statistically-grounded and anytime-valid $e$-process CIs. More importantly, the two ingredients reduce estimation variance and help reach the target precision with fewer evaluated samples. We also prove that CIs obtained by Celeus can shrink at a near-parametric rate up to logarithmic factors and analyze the oracle variance-optimal sampling rule that motivates the empirical uncertainty-guided one. Experiments show that Celeus reaches the target precision using 54-62% fewer evaluated samples than baselines, while preserving anytime-valid coverage.

</details>


### [581] [Evolutionary Discovery of Developmental Reward Schedules in Deep Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20858)
*Alan Nadelsticher Ruvalcaba*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes an evolutionary framework to discover developmental reward schedules in RL, combining agency, novelty, and reactivity with time-varying weights. Evaluated on MiniGrid tasks, L-SHADE and CMA-ES outperform baselines, with novelty emerging as the dominant early signal. Results show evolutionary optimization is promising for reward schedule discovery, though optimal schedules differ from biological expectations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The temporal structure of reward composition in RL is typically hand-designed and fixed throughout training, leaving the progression of motivational priorities largely unexplored. The authors aim to automate the discovery of developmental reward schedules.

Method: An evolutionary framework that combines three biologically inspired motivational components (agency, novelty, reactivity) through time-varying weights, optimized using four evolutionary algorithms (CMA-ES, xNES, DE, L-SHADE) on two sparse-reward MiniGrid tasks (DoorKey-6x6, KeyCorridorS3R1).

Result: On DoorKey-6x6, all evolved methods outperform non-evolved baselines; L-SHADE achieves best performance (~11.4% relative mean improvement over extrinsic baseline). On KeyCorridorS3R1, CMA-ES achieves best performance; other evolved methods show weaker generalization. Novelty consistently emerged as the dominant early signal across both tasks.

Conclusion: Evolutionary optimization is a promising approach for developmental reward schedule discovery in deep RL. Optimal schedules found computationally may differ from what is optimal in biology.

Abstract: The temporal structure of reward composition in reinforcement learning (RL) is typically hand-designed and held fixed throughout training, leaving the progression of motivational priorities largely unexplored. In this work, we propose an evolutionary framework for discovering developmental reward schedules, in which three distinct biologically inspired motivational components -- agency, novelty, and reactivity -- are combined through time-varying weights that dynamically shift over the course of training. Evaluated on two sparse-reward MiniGrid tasks: DoorKey-6x6 and KeyCorridorS3R1, our framework compares the generalizability of four evolutionary algorithms: CMA-ES, xNES, DE, and L-SHADE against an extrinsically motivated baseline (our main comparison point), and three additional hand-designed methods. On DoorKey-6x6, all evolved methods outperform the non-evolved baselines, with L-SHADE achieving the best performance -- an approximate relative mean improvement of 11.4% over the extrinsic only baseline. On KeyCorridorS3R1, CMA-ES achieves the best overall performance, with the remaining evolved methods showing weaker and less reliable generalization capability compared to the extrinsic only baseline. Interestingly, the discovered schedules diverge from our defined developmental ordering, with novelty consistently emerging as the dominant early signal during training, across both tasks. Collectively, our results position evolutionary optimization as a promising approach for developmental reward schedule discovery in deep reinforcement learning, and suggest that what evolution finds to be optimal in computational settings may differ from what it finds to be optimal in biology. The code for this project can be found at: https://github.com/alannadels/Evolutionary_RL.git.

</details>


### [582] [Machine Learning Classification of Cryopathy Syndromes: A Comprehensive Comparative Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20874)
*Nataliya Shakhovska,Valentyna Chopyak,Ivan Izonin,Vira Haievska*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This study evaluated 12 machine learning approaches for automated classification of 14 cryopathy syndromes from laboratory data (2,686 patients). Tree-based methods outperformed neural networks, with a soft-voting ensemble of Random Forest and Gradient Boosted Trees achieving the best multiclass performance. Cryoglobulin-based interaction features were the most informative predictors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cryopathy syndromes are difficult to classify due to overlapping laboratory patterns across diagnostic categories, rarity of some diagnoses, and dependence on expert judgment. The goal was to develop automated classification methods and identify a practical clinical decision support strategy.

Method: Analyzed lab records from 2,686 patients across 14 diagnostic categories. Data preprocessing included cleaning, encoding, imputation, normalization, and clinically informed interaction feature construction. Evaluated 12 modeling strategies (Random Forest, Gradient Boosted Trees, Multi-Layer Perceptron, soft-voting ensembles, SMOTE, hierarchical classification, period-aware models, binary classifiers, probability calibration) with stratified train-test and 5-fold cross-validation using macro F1, accuracy, Top-3 accuracy, and expected calibration error.

Result: Best multiclass performance from a soft-voting ensemble of Random Forest and Gradient Boosted Trees. Cross-validation confirmed stable performance for balanced Random Forest. Tree-based methods consistently outperformed MLP. Cryoglobulin-derived interaction features were the most informative predictors.

Conclusion: Machine learning, particularly tree-based ensemble methods with clinically informed feature engineering, can provide practical decision support for cryopathy classification despite class imbalance and diagnostic overlap.

Abstract: Cryopathy syndromes are difficult to classify because laboratory patterns often overlap across diagnostic categories, while some diagnoses are rare. This makes routine interpretation of cryoglobulin-related tests challenging and increases dependence on expert judgment. The aim of this study was to develop and compare machine learning approaches for automated classification of cryopathy syndromes from laboratory data and to identify a practical strategy for clinical decision support. Methods: We analysed laboratory records from 2,686 patients assigned to 14 diagnostic categories. The dataset included demographic variables, cryoglobulin measurements, precipitation tests, and hemagglutinin and hemolysin titers. Data preprocessing included cleaning, encoding, imputation, normalization, and construction of clinically informed interaction features. We evaluated 12 modelling strategies, including Random Forest, Gradient Boosted Trees, Multi-Layer Perceptron, soft-voting ensembles, class balancing with Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique, hierarchical classification, period-aware models, targeted binary classifiers, and probability calibration. Performance was assessed using stratified train-test evaluation and stratified 5-fold cross-validation. The main metrics were macro-averaged F1 score, accuracy, Top-3 accuracy, and expected calibration error. The overall task proved difficult because of marked class imbalance and clinical overlap between diagnoses. The best multiclass performance was achieved by a soft-voting ensemble of Random Forest and Gradient Boosted Trees. Cross-validation confirmed stable performance for the balanced Random Forest model. Tree-based methods consistently outperformed the neural network model. Feature engineering improved discrimination, and the most informative predictors were derived cryoglobulin-based interaction features.

</details>


### [583] [Understanding Latent Flow Models for Tabular Data Synthesis: Targets, Paths, and Sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20878)
*Bahrul Ilmi Nasution*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An empirical study of tabular latent flow models comparing learning targets (velocity, score, noise, posterior matching), probability paths (OT vs VP), and sampling dynamics (ODE vs SDE) across seven datasets, providing actionable configuration guidance for utility-risk-compute trade-offs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Synthetic tabular data generation in regulated domains requires balancing analytical utility, disclosure risk, and computational cost, yet the theoretical equivalences across latent flow model configurations break down under finite-step integration and explicit compute budgets, making empirical guidance necessary.

Method: Empirical evaluation on seven tabular datasets using latent flow models with velocity, score, noise, and posterior matching objectives under OT and VP probability paths, with ODE and SDE sampling at varying integration budgets.

Result: (1) Learning target largely determines utility-risk regime: velocity/posterior matching yield higher utility, score/noise matching yield lower disclosure risk. (2) Midpoint integration improves distributional fidelity; OT paths tolerate earlier stopping than VP, enabling compute savings. (3) Actionable defaults and practical configuration guidance for pre-release model selection.

Conclusion: Choice of learning target, probability path, and sampling configuration systematically shifts the utility-disclosure risk-compute trade-off, and the findings are distilled into practical guidance for model selection under resource and risk constraints.

Abstract: Synthetic tabular data enables microdata sharing in regulated domains, yet deploying continuous-time generative models requires balancing analytical utility, disclosure risk, and computational cost. Latent-space flow models are flexible, but theoretical equivalences across learning targets, probability paths, and sampling dynamics can translate into different behaviour under finite-step integration and explicit compute budgets. We present an empirical study of tabular latent flow models across seven datasets, evaluating velocity, score, noise, and posterior matching objectives under optimal transport (OT) and variance-preserving (VP) paths, ODE and SDE sampling, and varying integration budgets. Our contributions are threefold: (1) we show that the learning target largely determines the utility-risk operating regime, with velocity and posterior matching tending to yield higher utility, while score and noise matching tend to achieve lower disclosure risk; (2) we demonstrate that configuration and sampling choices shift performance, with midpoint often improving distributional fidelity and OT paths often tolerating earlier stopping than VP, enabling compute savings under fixed budgets or risk thresholds; and (3) we distil these findings into actionable defaults and practical configuration guidance to support pre-release model selection under disclosure risk and resource constraints. The code implementation and supplementary materials can be accessed in https://github.com/rulnasution/tabular-latent-flow/.

</details>


### [584] [Temporal Causal Prior-Data Fitted Networks for Panel Data with Learned Reliability Signals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20889)
*Shravan Talupula,Saurabh Sharma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TCPFN is a foundation model for zero-shot temporal causal discovery that jointly estimates CATE and reliability signals (null-effect probability, confounding strength, etc.) using a mixed training prior over six causal regimes, a discrete-token panel architecture with cross-attention masking, and industrial-scale FAISS-based inference. It achieves AUROC 0.93–0.98 on five-domain benchmarks and scales to V=1,275 on a proprietary dataset in 6 hours (vs. ~12.5 days for PCMCI).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing causal foundation models only handle static cross-sectional data; neural temporal methods require per-dataset training; and no existing method outputs per-pair reliability signals alongside CATE estimates for industrial time series with time-varying treatments and unobserved confounders.

Method: (1) Causal Judgment Head for joint prediction of null-effect probability, confounding strength, identifiability, mediation fraction, and causal regime; (2) mixed training prior over six causal regimes (independent, direct, confounded, mediated, time-varying confounded, feedback) plus front-door and instrumental-variable priors; (3) discrete-token panel-data architecture with cross-attention masking to prevent inter-horizon leakage; (4) zero-shot industrial inference using FAISS context selection and one-step posterior correction.

Result: Zero-shot AUROC: 0.96 on Tennessee Eastman, 0.93 on SWaT, 0.98 on Causal Rivers, 0.97 on CAUSRCA. Null detector NullF1=0.94, AUROC=0.99. Scales to V=1,275 in 6 hours on a single GPU vs. ~12.5 days for PCMCI. TCPFN identifies cross-subsystem causal edges while PCMCI captures within-instrument controller-measurement coupling.

Conclusion: TCPFN is the first foundation model for zero-shot temporal causal discovery with explicit reliability signals, achieving competitive accuracy and dramatic scalability improvements over traditional methods like PCMCI on industrial-scale datasets.

Abstract: Estimating causal effects in industrial time series requires handling temporal dynamics, time-varying treatments, and unobserved confounders. Existing causal foundation models (CausalPFN, CausalFM) operate only on static cross-sectional data; neural temporal methods (CRN, G-Net) require per-dataset training; and concurrent temporal-PFN proposals have not been demonstrated at industrial scale. None output explicit per-pair reliability signals alongside their CATE estimates.
  We introduce Temporal Causal Prior-Data Fitted Networks (TCPFN), a foundation model for zero-shot temporal causal discovery with learned reliability signals. TCPFN makes four contributions: (1) a Causal Judgment Head that jointly predicts null-effect probability, confounding strength, identifiability, mediation fraction, and causal regime; (2) a mixed training prior covering six causal regimes (independent, direct, confounded, mediated, time-varying confounded, feedback) plus CausalFM-style front-door and instrumental-variable priors; (3) a discrete-token panel-data architecture with cross-attention masking that prevents inter-horizon leakage; (4) zero-shot inference at industrial scale via FAISS-based context selection and one-step posterior correction.
  On 19 benchmark datasets across five domains, TCPFN achieves competitive zero-shot causal discovery: AUROC 0.96 on Tennessee Eastman, 0.93 on SWaT, 0.98 on Causal Rivers, 0.97 on CAUSRCA. The null detector reaches NullF1 0.94, AUROC 0.99. TCPFN scales to V=1,275 on a proprietary Kraft pulp-and-paper dataset in 6 hours on a single GPU; PCMCI, a CPU-only library, on a V=666 sub-panel of the same data took 81.5 hours, extrapolating by O(V^2) to ~12.5 days at V=1,275. TCPFN's top edges identify cross-subsystem causal relationships while PCMCI's surface within-instrument controller-measurement coupling -- a scalability case study.

</details>


### [585] [MMGNN: Multi-level, multi-color graph neural networks for molecular property prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20906)
*Trung Nguyen,Duc Duy Nguyen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MMGNN decomposes molecular graphs into atom-type-pair-specific subgraphs (2D covalent or 3D geometric) and uses a shared message-passing backbone to improve molecular property prediction, achieving strong results on MoleculeNet benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard molecular message-passing GNNs propagate diverse chemical interactions through a single graph, mixing interaction-specific signals and requiring deep propagation to capture long-range effects, which is suboptimal.

Method: MMGNN is a hierarchical framework that decomposes a molecular graph into overlapping atom-type-pair-specific subgraphs. MMGNN-2D uses covalent-connectivity-based chemical-colored subgraphs; MMGNN-3D uses spatial-proximity-based geometric-colored subgraphs with distance, angular, and torsional edge descriptors. A shared communicative message-passing backbone processes each subgraph, followed by atom-wise aggregation and molecular readout.

Result: On MoleculeNet benchmarks (5 classification, 3 regression tasks, scaffold splits, 5 runs): MMGNN-2D achieved macro-average AUC-ROC of 0.838 (classification) and lowest RMSE on ESOL (0.803); MMGNN-3D achieved highest mean AUC-ROC on BBBP (0.956) and lowest RMSE on FreeSolv (1.793).

Conclusion: Overlapping interaction-specific graph decomposition is a competitive strategy for molecular property prediction, with topological and geometric representations offering complementary strengths.

Abstract: Molecular message-passing neural networks commonly propagate chemically diverse interactions through a single graph, which may mix interaction-specific signals and require deep propagation to capture long-range effects. We introduce the Multi-level, Multi-color Graph Neural Network (MMGNN), a hierarchical framework that decomposes a molecular graph into overlapping atom-type-pair-specific subgraphs while preserving atom-level resolution. MMGNN-2D constructs chemical-colored subgraphs from covalent connectivity, whereas MMGNN-3D constructs geometric-colored subgraphs from spatial proximity and augments their edges with distance, angular, and torsional descriptors. Both variants apply a shared communicative message-passing backbone to each subgraph and combine the resulting representations through atom-wise aggregation and molecular readout. We evaluated MMGNN on five classification and three regression benchmarks from MoleculeNet using common scaffold splits and five independent runs. MMGNN-2D achieved the highest macro-average AUC-ROC of 0.838 across the classification datasets and the lowest RMSE on ESOL (0.803). MMGNN-3D obtained the highest mean AUC-ROC on BBBP (0.956) and the lowest RMSE on FreeSolv (1.793), indicating complementary strengths of topological and geometric representations. Structural and leave-one-out analyses further illustrate how the subgraph decomposition affects learned representations and atom-type-pair sensitivities. These results support overlapping interaction-specific graph decomposition as a competitive strategy for molecular property prediction.

</details>


### [586] [Physics-Guided Dual-Stream Heterogeneous Graph Neural Network for Predicting Full-Field Structural Response of Stiffened Panels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20916)
*Yuecheng Cai,Jasmin Jelovica*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DS-HGNN is a novel graph neural network surrogate for fast full-field stress and displacement prediction in thin-walled structures, achieving superior accuracy with fewer training samples compared to six benchmark models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Finite element analysis is computationally expensive for iterative design optimization of large structures, and existing neural network surrogates struggle with varying topologies and complex boundary conditions.

Method: DS-HGNN uses panel-level heterogeneous graph representations with physics-guided edge states, dual-stream message passing (longitudinal/transverse with cross-stream exchange), FiLM-conditioned 1-D spectral convolutions, and a spectral-bypass low-rank readout for field reconstruction.

Result: DS-HGNN achieves the lowest stress and displacement RMSE against six benchmarks, reaches comparable accuracy using 19%-38% fewer training samples, and captures yield/post-yield stress features.

Conclusion: DS-HGNN provides an efficient and accurate surrogate for predicting mechanical fields in thin-walled structures, reducing computational cost for iterative design optimization.

Abstract: Iterative design and optimization of large, complex structures require fast and accurate prediction of stress, displacement, and other fields. Finite element analysis (FEA) is computationally expensive for this task. Existing neural network surrogates often struggle with varying topologies and complex boundary conditions. This study proposes the novel Dual-Stream Heterogeneous Graph Neural Network (DS-HGNN) for full-field stress and displacement prediction in thin-walled structures, demonstrated on box beams made of stiffened panels. DS-HGNN operates on panel-level heterogeneous graph representations and introduces physics-guided edge states initialized from edge types, spatial information, and boundary kinematics. These states are updated through dual-stream message passing that separates longitudinal and transverse structural information while allowing cross-stream exchange. Geometry and loading effects are incorporated through Feature-wise Linear Modulation (FiLM)-conditioned 1-D spectral convolutions, and physical fields are reconstructed using a spectral-bypass low-rank readout. The model is evaluated on stiffened panel datasets with different geometries, boundary kinematics, loading conditions, and material nonlinear responses. DS-HGNN achieves the lowest stress and displacement RMSE compared with six benchmark heterogeneous graph neural network models. It also reaches comparable accuracy to the strongest benchmark models using 19%-38% fewer training samples. A targeted evaluation further shows that DS-HGNN captures yield and post-yield stress features.

</details>


### [587] [Short-Term Electricity Demand Forecasting for New England Using a Hybrid Transformer-XGBoost Framework with Weather, Calendar, and COVID-19 Indicators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20918)
*Reza Ghanavati,Behrooz Mosallaei*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A hybrid Transformer-XGBoost framework for short-term electricity demand forecasting in New England, incorporating weather, calendar, autoregressive, and COVID-19 features. The hybrid model (RMSE 8,876 MWh, MAPE 2.05%, R²=0.906) slightly outperforms a pure XGBoost baseline, but the Diebold-Mariano test shows no statistically significant improvement. Ablation reveals COVID-19 features cause temporal validity decay: they helped during the pandemic but degraded performance post-acute phase due to overfitting to stale behavioral patterns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate short-term electricity demand forecasting is critical for power system operation, energy market planning, and infrastructure optimization. The paper specifically investigates whether incorporating COVID-19 epidemiological variables can improve forecasting and whether the hybrid Transformer+XGBoost approach outperforms a purely tabular baseline.

Method: A hybrid framework combining a Transformer encoder for temporal feature extraction with XGBoost for daily demand forecasting across New England. Inputs include meteorological data from six cities (all six states), calendar/holiday effects, autoregressive demand lags, and COVID-19 epidemiological variables. Hyperparameter tuning uses Optuna with multivariate TPE over 500 trials. A leakage-free chronological 70/15/15 split is used. Evaluation includes Diebold-Mariano test and SHAP temporal analysis.

Result: Hybrid model: RMSE 8,876 MWh, MAPE 2.05%, R²=0.906. XGBoost baseline: RMSE 9,304 MWh, MAPE 2.21%, R²=0.896. The 427.7 MWh difference is not statistically significant (DM=-1.126, p=0.262). COVID-19 ablation: removal degraded hybrid RMSE by 3.2% but improved XGBoost-only by 1.2%. SHAP shows 5/8 COVID features rank higher in post-acute test set than during pandemic training.

Conclusion: Temporal validity decay is identified as a central mechanism: COVID-19 behavioral disruptions drove a strong demand signal during 2020-2021, but adaptation was complete by mid-2022, leaving epidemiological features as noise that amplifies overfitting to stale pandemic patterns.

Abstract: Accurate short-term electricity demand forecasting is critical for reliable power system operation, energy market planning, and infrastructure optimization. This paper presents a hybrid framework combining a Transformer encoder for temporal feature extraction with gradient-boosted decision trees (XGBoost) for daily electricity demand forecasting across New England. The framework integrates meteorological observations from six cities spanning all six New England states, calendar and holiday effects, autoregressive demand lags, and COVID-19 epidemiological variables. Hyperparameter optimization uses Optuna with a multivariate Tree-structured Parzen Estimator over 500 trials, with a leakage-free 70/15/15 chronological train-validation-test split. The hybrid model achieves a test RMSE of 8,876 MWh, MAPE of 2.05%, and R-squared of 0.906. A tabular-only XGBoost baseline achieves RMSE of 9,304 MWh, MAPE of 2.21%, and R-squared of 0.896. A Diebold-Mariano test (Harvey-Leybourne-Newbold correction) confirms the 427.7 MWh difference is statistically indistinguishable from noise (DM = -1.126, p = 0.262). An ablation study reveals COVID-19 features improved training accuracy but had asymmetric test effects: removal degraded hybrid RMSE by 3.2% while marginally improving XGBoost-only by 1.2%. A SHAP temporal analysis shows 5 of 8 COVID features rank higher on the post-acute test set than during pandemic-active training, indicating the model over-applies learned pandemic patterns.
