We propose Federated Self-Contextualization (FSC), a multimodal language model framework for in-context clinical audio diagnosis across federated hospital clients. FSC constructs pseudo-label episodes via unsupervised clustering of audio representations, bypassing scarce real diagnostic labels, and enables contextual reasoning from support-query pairs. Our progressive three-stage pipeline first aligns audio embeddings with the language model via caption-based pretraining, then adapts it for episodic in-context inference through federated optimization. At test time, given a small labeled support set, the model diagnoses an unseen query through multimodal reasoning. On held-out respiratory and cardiac conditions, FSC achieves 71.6% accuracy in 2-way 2-shot evaluation, outperforming audio-language baselines by over 9%.

</details>


### [614] [Rejections Based on Predictive Uncertainty Enable Reliable Routine Soil Spectroscopy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21179)
*Jonas Schmidinger,Robin Gebbers,Marc-Olivier Gasser,Viacheslav Barkov,G. Mick Wu,Viacheslav I. Adamchuk*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces a "reject-to-remeasure" framework combining probabilistic machine learning with optical spectroscopy, where unreliable predictions are rejected and sent for conventional lab testing. Using foundation models on a soil spectral library, this approach balances cost and accuracy by meeting user-defined quality constraints while reducing measurement costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional soil property measurement methods are accurate but costly and time-consuming. Optical spectroscopy with ML offers a faster alternative but suffers from unreliable predictive accuracy that varies across soil properties and samples. The authors aim to balance the trade-off between cost efficiency and measurement reliability.

Method: A reject-to-remeasure framework combining probabilistic modeling with uncertainty-guided rejection. Soil samples are first analyzed via spectroscopy; predictions exceeding a predefined uncertainty threshold are rejected and remeasured using conventional laboratory methods. The approach leverages modern foundation models (TabPFNv2.5 and TabICLv2) and was validated on a regional visible-near-infrared spectral soil library from Québec.

Result: The reject-to-remeasure framework, particularly when paired with modern foundation models, successfully integrates optical spectroscopy into routine soil analysis workflows, meeting user-defined accuracy requirements while reducing measurement costs compared to full reliance on conventional lab methods.

Conclusion: Uncertainty-guided rejection provides a practical pathway to operationalize spectroscopic modeling in soil analysis by dynamically routing uncertain samples to conventional testing, thus balancing the cost-reliability trade-off.

Abstract: Soil properties relevant to agricultural and environmental applications are conventionally measured using elaborate laboratory methods involving physical and chemical processing. While highly accurate, these conventional methods are costly and time-consuming. In contrast, optical spectroscopy paired with machine learning enables rapid and cost-effective predictions of multiple soil properties. However, spectroscopic modelling is often considered unreliable, as the predictive accuracy varies between soil properties and individual samples. To balance this trade-off between cost and reliability, we introduce reject-to-remeasure: an AI-based measurement framework that combines probabilistic modelling with uncertainty-guided rejection. In this framework, soil samples are first analysed using spectroscopy, after which predictions are rejected if their predictive uncertainty exceeds predefined quality constraints. Rejected samples are subsequently remeasured using conventional laboratory procedures. On a regional visible-near-infrared spectral soil library from Québec, we demonstrate that reject-to-remeasure with modern foundation models (TabPFNv2.5 and TabICLv2) can facilitate the integration of optical spectroscopy into routine laboratory workflows while meeting user-defined accuracy requirements and reducing measurement costs.

</details>


### [615] [TF-SNO: Time-Frequency Gated Spectral Neural Operators for Learning Non-Stationary Partial Differential Equations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21189)
*Yitian Zhou,Chaoning Zhang,Zhenzhen Huang,Haoxuan Yu,Jiaquan Zhang,Yiran Li,Fan Mo,Kuien Liu,Jie Zou,Caiyan Qin,Yang Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TF-SNO is a state-adaptive spectral neural operator that uses learnable time-frequency gating to adjust spectral responses dynamically, improving accuracy and stability on non-stationary PDE benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Non-stationary PDEs have time-varying frequency content and energy distributions. Standard spectral neural operators use a fixed spectral response across rollout steps, causing mismatch with evolving spectra.

Method: TF-SNO introduces learnable time-frequency gating inside spectral blocks. It extracts compact frequency-domain and physical-space statistics from the current state to generate modulation coefficients, enabling adaptive spectral response without explicit time embeddings or increased complexity.

Result: On six 1D/2D non-stationary PDE benchmarks, TF-SNO significantly reduces prediction errors and improves robustness over strong baselines, with particularly large gains in long rollout scenarios.

Conclusion: State-dependent spectral adaptation via time-frequency gating effectively handles non-stationary dynamics, improving long-horizon stability and accuracy in neural operator learning.

Abstract: Non-stationary partial differential equations (PDEs) arise throughout scientific computing, where the dominant frequency content and energy distribution can drift over time. While efficient in PDE solving, many spectral neural operators apply a shared spectral response across rollout stages, leading to mismatch with time-varying spectra in non-stationary systems. To address this issue, we propose Time-Frequency Gated Spectral Neural Operator (TF-SNO), a state-adaptive framework with learnable time-frequency gating inside spectral blocks. TF-SNO extracts compact frequency-domain and physical-space statistics from the current state to generate modulation coefficients, enabling the spectral response to evolve with the dynamics. TF-SNO learns temporal variation implicitly from the evolving state without introducing an explicit time dimension or time embedding, keeping the modeling complexity low. We further embed the adaptive operator blocks to accurately capture the multi-scale features, thereby improving long-horizon stability. Experiments on six non-stationary PDE benchmarks in 1D and 2D demonstrate that TF-SNO significantly reduces prediction errors and improves robustness compared to strong baselines, with particularly clear gains in long rollout, suggesting the effectiveness of state-dependent spectral adaptation in modeling non-stationary physical systems.

</details>


### [616] [DCD-PFN: A Decoupling-Aware Foundation Model for Causal Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21212)
*Zhengkang Guan,Yikang Chen,Yi He,Yunze Tong,Zijing Hu,Haoyuan Qian,Fei Wu,Kun Kuang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DCD-PFN is a foundation model for causal discovery that uses decoupling-aware pre-training on synthetic SCMs to perform local causal discovery via Markov boundary identification, enabling efficient and robust zero-shot global graph reconstruction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional causal discovery algorithms struggle with highly non-linear and noisy systems or suffer from computational bottlenecks. Tabular foundation models (PFNs) have shown zero-shot inference capabilities but remain underexplored for explicit structural causal discovery.

Method: DCD-PFN pre-trains on diverse synthetic Structural Causal Models (SCMs) to learn sample-wise decoupling weights for Markov boundary (MB) identification. It then reconstructs global causal graphs through parallelized local discovery, grounded in decoupling-based causal discovery theory.

Result: The model achieves robust zero-shot generalization on causal discovery tasks.

Conclusion: DCD-PFN bridges the gap between tabular foundation models and explicit structural causal discovery, offering a scalable and theoretically grounded solution that generalizes well in zero-shot settings.

Abstract: Causal discovery is critical for understanding complex data-generating mechanisms, yet traditional algorithms often struggle with highly non-linear and noisy systems, or suffer from severe computational bottlenecks. Recent tabular foundation models based on Prior-Data Fitted Networks (PFNs) have demonstrated remarkable zero-shot inference capabilities, but their potential for explicit structural causal discovery remains underexplored. To bridge this gap, we propose DCD-PFN, a decoupling-aware foundation model for causal discovery. Instead of directly amortizing global graph reconstruction, DCD-PFN focuses on local causal discovery through a decoupling-based paradigm. Through pre-training on diverse synthetic Structural Causal Models (SCMs), the model learns sample-wise decoupling weights that enable Markov boundary (MB) identification. Furthermore, by leveraging parallelized local discovery, DCD-PFN efficiently reconstructs global causal graphs while remaining grounded in the theoretical foundations of decoupling-based causal discovery. Experiments demonstrate that our foundation model achieves robust zero-shot generalization.

</details>


### [617] [Sakana Fugu Technical Report](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21228)
*Yujin Tang,Edoardo Cetin,Jinglue Xu,Qi Sun,Stefan Nielsen,Vincent Richard,Haruto Goda,Iaroslav Tymchenko,Nhan Nguyen,Hyunin Lee,Mari Ashiga,Shashank Kotyan,So Kuroki,Tarin Clanuwat*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Sakana Fugu is a family of orchestrator language models that dynamically build agentic scaffolds to combine specialized LLMs into a collective intelligent system, achieving state-of-the-art results across multiple benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To combine the individual specializations of various frontier LLMs into a collectively intelligent system that surpasses the capabilities of any single model.

Method: Training orchestrator models (Fugu and Fugu-Ultra) that understand user queries and dynamically devise adaptive agentic scaffolds. The training paradigm includes large-scale fine-tuning, evolutionary algorithms, and reinforcement learning.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art results compared to other publicly accessible models on SWE-Bench Pro, Terminal Bench, LiveCodeBench, GPQA-Diamond, Humanity's Last Exam, and CharXiv Reasoning.

Conclusion: Multi-agent systems and dynamic, query-adaptive agentic scaffolds represent a promising path toward the next frontier of AI capabilities via collective intelligence.

Abstract: The capabilities of frontier Large Language Models (LLMs) continue to advance, with different providers increasingly specializing in distinct domains. This raises a natural next objective: how to combine the individual specializations of various LLMs into a collectively intelligent system. To this end, we report the development of Sakana Fugu, a family of orchestrator models that harness and amplify the capabilities of an LLM agent team. Fugu models are themselves language models trained to understand user queries and dynamically devise agentic scaffolds to solve them. Through these adaptive scaffolds, Fugu accesses performance beyond any individual LLM agent, achieving state-of-the-art results compared to other publicly accessible models across a range of challenging tasks, including SWE-Bench Pro, Terminal Bench, LiveCodeBench, GPQA-Diamond, Humanity's Last Exam, and CharXiv Reasoning. We release two models: Fugu, which balances performance with latency for everyday use, and Fugu-Ultra, which prioritizes answer quality on the hardest problems. We describe our training paradigm, which encompasses large-scale fine-tuning, evolutionary algorithms, and reinforcement learning approaches, along with the infrastructure and core design principles that turn these methods into a production system. We hope this report encourages further research into multi-agent systems and dynamic, query-adaptive agentic scaffolds as a path toward the next frontier of AI capabilities, accessed through collective intelligence.

</details>


### [618] [Comparative Evaluation of Machine Learning and Deep Learning Models for Wound-Rotor Synchronous Motor Performance Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21230)
*Kıvanç Doğan,Ahmet Orhan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This study systematically benchmarks 8 ML/DL models (including FT-Transformer) for predicting torque and efficiency of Wound Rotor Synchronous Motors, using 3351 Latin Hypercube samples, Optuna tuning, and 10-seed reproducibility. FT-Transformer achieves R²=0.9928 with 0.33ms inference, vastly outperforming FEA speed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: WRSM design requires optimizing many geometric/electromagnetic parameters, but FEA is computationally prohibitive. Existing surrogate modeling studies lack comprehensive benchmarks, exclude deep learning, and do not address WRSM specifically.

Method: Eight models from four algorithmic families (tree-based and neural-network-based) were compared. Data: 3351 samples via Latin Hypercube Sampling in Motor-CAD. Each model trained with 10 random seeds and tuned via Optuna. Evaluation used R², MAE, RMSE, MAPE. Pareto analysis of cost-accuracy trade-off included.

Result: Neural-network models systematically outperformed tree-based models. FT-Transformer achieved best single-model accuracy (R²=0.9928) with 0.33 ms inference, offering orders-of-magnitude speedup over FEA.

Conclusion: FT-Transformer is the most accurate and efficient surrogate model for WRSM design; the multi-seed reproducibility protocol and Pareto analysis provide robust benchmarking guidelines for future work.

Abstract: Wound rotor synchronous motors have emerged as a strong alternative that eliminates dependence on REEs. However, WRSM design requires the simultaneous optimization of numerous geometric and electromagnetic parameters, and the high computational cost of conventional finite element analysis severely limits the rapid exploration of the large parameter space. Although there are machine-learning-based surrogate modeling studies in the literature, they generally compare only a limited number of models, exclude deep learning architectures, and do not provide a comprehensive benchmark specific to WRSM. In this study, the performance of a total of eight machine learning and deep learning models from four different algorithmic families was systematically compared for the prediction of WRSM torque and motor efficiency. On a dataset of 3351 samples generated using Latin Hypercube Sampling in the Motor-CAD simulation environment, each model was trained with 10 different random seed values and tuned via Optuna hyperparameter optimization. Different from the existing literature, this study jointly offers a broad model spectrum including recent deep learning architectures such as FT Transformer, a multi-seed reproducibility protocol, and a Pareto analysis of the computational cost-accuracy trade-off. The results revealed that neural-network-based models systematically outperform tree-based models. The FT-Transformer model achieved the highest single-model accuracy with R^2 = 0.9928, producing predictions in 0.33 milliseconds and thus obtaining several orders of magnitude speedup compared to FEA. Model performances were evaluated in a multidimensional manner using R^2, MAE, RMSE, and MAPE metrics.

</details>


### [619] [Does RoPE Prevent or Degrade Retrieval Heads? A Mechanistic Analysis Across Model Families](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21249)
*Cengizhan Bayram*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Retrieval heads are causally necessary for long-context recall; higher RoPE base theta does not prevent their formation; the causal mechanism is RoPE frequency (especially low-frequency dimensions), not norm-utility; findings replicate across five models and two scales.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To test whether rotary position embeddings (RoPE) prevent or degrade retrieval heads—attention heads that copy information from earlier context—and to identify the actual causal variable underlying their function.

Method: Experiments across four open-weight 7-8B models (OLMo-2, Qwen2.5, LLaMA-2, LLaMA-3.1) and five models total (including Gemma-2, Mistral) using paired-seed needle-in-a-haystack tests, layer-clustered permutation, causal head-masking, and controlled RoPE dimension patching.

Result: (i) Retrieval heads are causally necessary: masking 87 heads in OLMo-2 collapses recall (1.00→0.00). (ii) Higher theta does not reduce retrieval-head count (LLaMA-3.1 θ=500K: 47 heads vs LLaMA-2 θ=10K: 42). (iii) Norm-utility relation is family-specific and not theta-driven. (iv) Zeroing low-frequency RoPE dimensions of retrieval heads degrades recall dose-dependently (1.00→0.18 for 32/128 dims, vs 0.98 for random dims); effect is head-specific, task-specific, and replicates across all five models.

Conclusion: RoPE frequency, not norm-utility, is the causal mechanism for retrieval head function. Higher RoPE base theta does not hinder retrieval head formation. The findings are robust across model families and scales.

Abstract: Retrieval heads, attention heads that copy information from earlier context to the current position, have been proposed as the mechanistic substrate for long-context recall. Rotary position embeddings (RoPE) rotate queries and keys by frequencies decaying with a base hyperparameter theta, and a natural hypothesis is that this rotation either prevents retrieval heads from forming or degrades their function. We test both across four open-weight 7-8B models spanning multi-head and grouped-query attention and a 100x range of theta, using paired-seed needle-in-a-haystack tests, layer-clustered permutation, and causal head-masking. (i) Retrieval heads are causally necessary: masking the 87 detected heads in OLMo-2 collapses recall from 1.00 to 0.00, while masking matched random heads has no effect; this replicates in Qwen. (ii) Higher theta does not reduce retrieval-head count (LLaMA-3.1 at theta=500K has 47 heads vs LLaMA-2 at theta=10K with 42), refuting the prevention hypothesis. (iii) The norm-utility relation is family-specific and significant in opposite directions (Qwen d=-0.49, OLMo d=+0.50, both significant; LLaMA null); since OLMo and LLaMA-3.1 share theta=500K yet differ, the effect is not theta-driven. (iv) Building on Chiang and Yogatama (2025), a controlled patch shows that zeroing the lowest-frequency RoPE dimensions of retrieval heads degrades recall dose-dependently (1.00 to 0.18 when 32 of 128 dimensions are zeroed, vs 0.98 for random dimensions); the effect is head-specific and task-specific. The causal variable is RoPE frequency, not norm-utility. The direction holds in all five models patched (OLMo-2, Qwen2.5-7B/14B, Gemma-2, Mistral) across four lineages and two scales. We do not claim cross-model magnitude. Code and a paired-seed harness are released.

</details>


### [620] [Gradient-Free Warm-Start Library Recovery: an Amortized-Regret Separation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21253)
*Jianwei Lou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper provides a provable analysis of gradient-free, local, online, and append-only continual learning under recurring regimes. It shows that a warm-start library learner achieves amortized recovery cost O(KD/ε² + (R−K) logK/Δ²) compared to a memoryless re-estimator's Θ(RD/ε²), with the advantage growing with dimension D and recurrence density. The key decoupling is that recognizing which of K seen regimes is active costs O(log K/Δ²), independent of D, while estimating a regime costs Θ(D/ε²). These bounds are shown to be minimax-tight, with matching lower bounds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a provable (rather than informal) account of the value of continual learning in gradient-free, local, online, and append-only settings, particularly for edge and streaming deployment where memory and computation are constrained.

Method: The authors formalize a recurring-regime stream model, design a warm-start library learner that decouples regime recognition (O(log K/Δ²), D-independent) from regime estimation (Θ(D/ε²)), prove matching minimax lower bounds, compare against a spawn-capable Bayesian baseline, and introduce a count-calibrated variant. They also analyze the fundamental limits via simplex packing (cap on recognizable regimes) and the impossibility of autonomous segmentation at the packing wall.

Result: The warm-start library learner attains amortized recovery cost O(KD/ε² + (R−K) logK/Δ²), versus a memoryless re-estimator's Θ(RD/ε²). The recognition cost is D-independent. Matching lower bounds prove minimax tightness. The advantage is born-immune (memoryless learner's advantage is zero). A count-calibrated variant ties the Bayesian baseline's constant up to a bounded per-recurrence overshoot, hyperparameter-free. Recognizable regimes are capped by simplex packing (e^{Θ(D)} walls); autonomous segmentation is impossible at the packing wall.

Conclusion: The paper provides a rigorous theoretical foundation for the benefits of continual learning under recurring regimes, showing a dimension-dependent separation that is paradigm-level rather than incremental. The approach matches (but does not beat) a fair Bayesian baseline, contributes zero forgetting by construction, and avoids end-to-end backprop. The scope is bounded by fundamental geometric and detection-theoretic limits.

Abstract: Continual learning that is gradient-free, local, online, and append-only is attractive for edge and streaming deployment, but its value is usually argued informally. We give a provable account on recurring-regime streams. Given segmentation, a warm-start library learner attains amortized recovery cost $O\!\big(KD/\varepsilon^2+(R-K)\logK/Δ^2\big)$ versus a memoryless re-estimator's $Θ(RD/\varepsilon^2)$, an advantage $(R-K)\,Θ(D/\varepsilon^2)$ growing with dimension $D$ and recurrence density. The mechanism is a decoupling: recognizing which of $K$ seen regimes is active costs $O(\log K/Δ^2)$, independent of $D$, whereas estimating a regime costs $Θ(D/\varepsilon^2)$. We prove this is tight: matching lower bounds give recognition $Θ(\log K/Δ^2)$ and a memoryless-class bound $Ω(RD/\varepsilon^2)$, so each term is individually minimax-tight (the joint statement is conditional). The separation is born-immune (a memoryless learner's advantage is identically zero) and paradigm-level: it matches, and does not beat, a fair spawn-capable Bayesian baseline; the contribution is attaining this cost structure without end-to-end backprop and with zero forgetting by construction. A count-calibrated variant ties the baseline's leading constant up to a bounded, never-negative per-recurrence overshoot, hyperparameter-free and with no per-step transcendentals. We bound the scope: recognizable regimes are capped by simplex packing (walls $e^{Θ(D)}$); autonomous segmentation is impossible at the packing wall (no detector escapes the false-alarm/delay frontier as regimes overlap); the advantage vanishes under overlap. The dimension-dependent separation is corroborated on synthetic streams and real $k$-mer genome distributions (memoryless cost $\propto D^{1.04}$, recognition $D$-independent); the one real sequential stream sits in the $D{=}1$ near-null corner.

</details>


### [621] [Intrinsic Flow Matching on Quantum Pure-State Manifolds with Phase-Aligned Transport](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21256)
*Jian Xu,Delu Zeng,John Paisley,Qibin Zhao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Intrinsic Flow Matching (IFM) is a deterministic transport framework on complex projective space $\mathbb{CP}^{d-1}$ for quantum pure-state ensembles, using Pancharatnam phase-aligned conditional paths and horizontal parameterization. It outperforms ambient Euclidean flow matching on most quantum benchmarks, particularly in high-dimensional and coherence-sensitive tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantum pure-state ensembles naturally reside on complex projective space, but existing generative models use flat Euclidean geometry, creating a geometric mismatch that degrades performance.

Method: IFM learns tangent velocity fields on $\mathbb{CP}^{d-1}$ via Pancharatnam phase-aligned conditional paths, uses horizontal parameterization to remove redundant ambient directions, and replaces local score teachers and reverse-time stochastic sampling with manifold probability flow.

Result: IFM improves over ambient Euclidean flow matching across higher-qubit, multimodal, spin-coherent, physics-inspired, and amplitude-encoded MNIST benchmarks, with strongest gains on high-dimensional and coherence-sensitive tasks, though not uniformly across every metric.

Conclusion: The IFM objective recovers the induced marginal transport field, enables deterministic projective ensemble flows, and provides endpoint and stability guarantees, demonstrating that geometric alignment matters for quantum generative modeling.

Abstract: Quantum pure-state ensembles live on complex projective space, making flat Euclidean generative modeling geometrically mismatched. We introduce Intrinsic Flow Matching (IFM), a deterministic transport framework on $\mathbb{CP}^{d-1}$ that learns tangent velocity fields using Pancharatnam phase-aligned conditional paths. IFM replaces local score teachers and reverse-time stochastic sampling with manifold probability flow, while horizontal parameterization removes redundant ambient directions. We show that the IFM objective recovers the induced marginal transport field, represents deterministic projective ensemble flows, and yields endpoint and stability guarantees. Empirically, IFM often improves over ambient Euclidean flow matching across higher-qubit, multimodal, spin-coherent, physics-inspired, and amplitude-encoded MNIST image-vector benchmarks, with strongest gains on high-dimensional and coherence-sensitive tasks but not uniformly across every metric.

</details>


### [622] [An Empirical Study of OpenPangu Quantization on Ascend NPUs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21257)
*Tong Shi,Jiacheng Wang,Hui Xie,Ying Li,Aishan Liu,Jinyang Guo,Xianglong Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper systematically evaluates post-training quantization methods (RTN, GPTQ, AWQ, SmoothQuant, GPTAQ, BiLLM, SliM-LLM) for OpenPangu 1B and 7B models on Huawei Ascend 910B1 NPUs. 8-bit weight-only quantization is lossless; 4-bit works for 7B but harms 1B on reasoning/math/code; ultra-low precision (2-bit, binary, W4A4) largely fails.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: OpenPangu models are attractive for private/domestic LLM deployment, but their robustness under aggressive post-training quantization on Ascend NPUs had not been systematically characterized.

Method: Controlled empirical study on Huawei Ascend 910B1 NPUs evaluating 7 weight-only and weight-activation PTQ methods (RTN, GPTQ, AWQ, SmoothQuant, GPTAQ, BiLLM, SliM-LLM) under a unified calibration and evaluation protocol across 18 tasks.

Result: 8-bit weight-only quantization is lossless for both models. 4-bit works for 7B but harms 1B on reasoning, math, and code tasks. 2-bit, binary, and W4A4 SmoothQuant collapse to near-random or non-finite perplexity.

Conclusion: Provides an NPU-oriented accuracy map for selecting OpenPangu quantization settings; highlights the persistent difficulty of extreme low-bit compression.

Abstract: OpenPangu models are attractive targets for private and domestic large-language-model deployment, yet their robustness under aggressive post-training quantization on Ascend NPUs has not been systematically characterized. This paper conducts a controlled empirical study of OpenPangu 1B and 7B models on Huawei Ascend 910B1 NPUs. We evaluate representative weight-only and weight-activation post-training quantization methods, including RTN, GPTQ, AWQ, SmoothQuant, GPTAQ, BiLLM, and SliM-LLM, under a unified calibration and evaluation protocol. Across 18 evaluation tasks, we find that 8-bit weight-only quantization is effectively lossless for both models, while 4-bit quantization remains practical for the 7B model but is visibly more harmful for the 1B model on reasoning, math, and code tasks. Ultra-low precision remains challenging: most 2-bit and binary settings collapse to near-random behavior, and W4A4 SmoothQuant produces non-finite perplexity in our evaluation. These results provide an NPU-oriented accuracy map for selecting OpenPangu quantization settings and highlight the persistent difficulty of extreme low-bit compression.

</details>


### [623] [Reward-free Pretraining for Reinforcement Learning via Occupancy Coverage Maximization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21271)
*Marco Pratticò,Pietro Novelli,Massimiliano Pontil,Carlo Ciliberto*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ROVER pre-trains transferable exploration policies for RL by maximizing state-space coverage via entropy maximization, using a learned resolvent world model to estimate occupancy without requiring extrinsic reward evaluation, enabling rapid adaptation to sparse rewards in downstream tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sparse rewards hinder RL as agents receive no signal until reaching the goal. Existing intrinsic-reward methods require extrinsic reward evaluation (online or via relabeling), which fails in reward-free settings common to multi-task, meta-, and continual RL.

Method: ROVER frames exploration as occupancy measure maximization via entropy maximization. It uses a learned resolvent world model (operatorial RL) to estimate occupancy without density estimation. A virtual "sink" state handles unexplored regions, balancing coverage of known vs. new states and preventing cyclic collapse.

Result: In tabular and pixel-based sparse navigation tasks, ROVER achieves more uniform aggregate coverage and stronger downstream task initializations compared to standard reward-free baselines.

Conclusion: Pre-training exploration policies via occupancy maximization with a resolvent world model enables effective transfer to sparse-reward downstream tasks without requiring reward evaluation during pre-training.

Abstract: Sparse rewards pose a central challenge in reinforcement learning, since agents receive no informative signal until they reach their goal. Intrinsic-reward methods address this issue by optimizing non-stationary objectives such as novelty, prediction error, or skill diversity, thereby injecting a supervision signal into the problem. While effective, these methods often require that the extrinsic (sparse) reward can be evaluated -- either online or during offline relabeling of the stored transitions. This limitation is particularly vexing for multi-task, meta-, and continual reinforcement learning, where agents' interactions with the environment are usually reward-free. In this work, we present a method to pre-train transferable exploration policies that rapidly adapt to sparse rewards at downstream task time. Our objective maximizes state-space covering for the occupancy measure, and can be framed in terms of entropy maximization. Its algorithmic implementation, ROVER, leverages recent advances on the operatorial formulation of RL to estimate occupancy with a learned resolvent world model, bypassing common hurdles associated with density and entropy estimation. ROVER further introduces a virtual "sink" state for unexplored regions, balancing coverage of known states with expansion into unseen ones and preventing cyclic expansion-collapse behavior during learning. In tabular and pixel-based sparse navigation tasks, ROVER produces more uniform aggregate coverage and stronger initializations for downstream tasks than standard reward-free baselines.

</details>


### [624] [Reconstructing Randomly Masked Spectra Helps DNNs Identify Discriminant Wavenumbers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21289)
*Yingying Wu,Jinchao Liu,Yan Wang,Stuart Gibson,Margarita Osadchy,Yongchun Fang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TeaNet is a task-enhanced augmentation network for vibrational spectroscopy that uses a reconstruction module with random masking to generate augmented samples from limited spectral data, outperforming CNN by 17% in difficult scenarios and offering better interpretability via discriminant wavenumber identification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vibrational spectroscopy data is very limited compared to images or text, making traditional deep learning approaches (including transfer and meta learning) insufficient. There is a need for novel methods tailored to small spectral datasets.

Method: TeaNet consists of a reconstruction module that takes randomly masked spectra and reconstructs them with additional domain-learned variations, which are then used to train the classification model. Both reconstruction and prediction parts are trained simultaneously end-to-end with back-propagation.

Result: TeaNet outperformed CNN by 17% in the most difficult synthetic scenarios. Neuron response analysis showed TeaNet's superior ability to identify discriminant wavenumbers compared to CNN. Superiority was verified on both synthetic and real-world datasets.

Conclusion: TeaNet is a general approach that can be easily adapted to other domains, offering a solution for more accurate and interpretable few-shot learning beyond vibrational spectroscopy.

Abstract: Nondestructive detection methods, based on vibrational spectroscopy, are vitally important in a wide range of applications including industrial chemistry, pharmacy and national defense. Recently, deep learning has been introduced into vibrational spectroscopy showing great potential. Different from images, text, etc. that offer large labeled data sets, vibrational spectroscopic data is very limited, which requires novel concepts beyond transfer and meta learning. To tackle this, we propose a task-enhanced augmentation network (TeaNet). The key component of TeaNet is a reconstruction module that inputs randomly masked spectra and outputs reconstructed samples that are similar to the original ones, but include additional variations learned from the domain. These augmented samples are used to train the classification model. The reconstruction and prediction parts are trained simultaneously, end-to-end with back-propagation. Results on both synthetic and real-world datasets verified the superiority of the proposed method. In the most difficult synthetic scenarios TeaNet outperformed CNN by 17%. We visualized and analysed the neuron responses of TeaNet and CNN, and found that TeaNet's ability to identify discriminant wavenumbers was excellent compared to CNN. Our approach is general and can be easily adapted to other domains, offering a solution to more accurate and interpretable few-shot learning.

</details>


### [625] [Topological Neural Dynamics: A Neuron-wise Framework for Sequence Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21295)
*Borui Cai,Yao Zhao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TND shifts sequence modeling from layer-wise to neuron-wise dynamics using a directed neuron graph, achieving 3x better performance than baselines on a Pong behavior cloning task.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing sequence models (RNNs, LSTMs, Transformers) force all neurons in a layer to co-evolve through shared parameters, lacking the independent local evolution seen in complex dynamical systems where global behavior emerges from locally evolving units with structured connectivity.

Method: TND represents a neural system as a directed neuron graph, an interaction operator, and a local dynamics function. Each neuron evolves independently, and collective computation emerges from interactions through explicit graph topology, instantiated as a discrete-time graph-coupled dynamical system.

Result: On a single-player Pong behavior cloning task, TND achieves the best catch rate and a mean of 17.47 consecutive catches per round, more than three times that of the strongest baseline (Vanilla RNN, Sparse RNN, LSTM, CfC, Transformer).

Conclusion: Shifting from layer-wise to neuron-wise dynamics provides an effective inductive bias for sequence modeling.

Abstract: Existing sequence models, including RNNs, LSTMs, continuous-time networks, and Transformers, share a common structural principle: layer-wise dynamics, where all neurons in the same layer co-evolve through a shared parameterized operator, leaving individual neurons no freedom to evolve independently. Yet in many complex dynamical systems, rich global behavior emerges precisely from locally evolving units interacting through structured connectivity. Inspired by this principle, we introduce Topological Neural Dynamics (TND), a sequence modeling framework that shifts computation from layer-wise to neuron-wise dynamics. TND represents a neural system as a directed neuron graph, an interaction operator, and a local dynamics function, where each neuron evolves independently and collective computation emerges from interactions through the explicit graph topology. We instantiate TND as a discrete-time graph-coupled dynamical system and evaluate it as a case study on a behavior cloning task in single-player Pong. Compared with Vanilla RNN, Sparse RNN, LSTM, Closed-form continuous-time neural network (CfC), and Transformer baselines, TND achieves the best catch rate and a mean of 17.47 consecutive catches per round, more than three times that of the strongest baseline. These results suggest that shifting from layer-wise to neuron-wise dynamics provides an effective inductive bias for sequence modeling.

</details>


### [626] [NASDAQ: Normalized Observation Space Dynamics-Augmented Q-Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21297)
*Xinwei Liu,Junyuan Liang,Zicong Hong,Jianting Zhang,Wuhui Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: NASDAQ normalizes observations in dynamics-predictive RL to balance reconstruction losses, achieving SOTA performance across diverse domains with less training time.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Observation-predictive RL struggles in challenging tasks with low-dimensional observations due to unbalanced reconstruction losses across observation dimensions, where dimensions with larger value ranges dominate the loss and cause neglect of smaller-range dimensions.

Method: Proposes a normalization method for online RL that balances losses/gradients across observation dimensions, enabling unified treatment of low- and high-dimensional inputs. Introduces NASDAQ framework coupling value learning with short-term value prediction and next normalized observation prediction as auxiliary tasks.

Result: NASDAQ achieves competitive or superior performance compared to state-of-the-art model-based and self-predictive RL methods, while requiring significantly less training wall-time.

Conclusion: Balancing reconstruction losses via observation normalization is key to improving observation-predictive RL in low-dimensional tasks, and NASDAQ offers an efficient, unified framework across diverse domains.

Abstract: Augmenting model-free reinforcement learning (RL) with representations learned through observation dynamics prediction (observation-predictive RL) can improve sample efficiency and performance, with minor modifications and limited additional computation. However, this approach still struggles in challenging tasks with low-dimensional observations. In this paper, we identify a key factor behind this problem: unbalanced reconstruction losses across observation dimensions, where dimensions with larger value ranges dominate the loss. This encourages the agent to neglect dimensions with relatively small ranges, leading to degraded performance. To address this issue, we propose a novel normalization method tailored to online RL, which normalizes low-dimensional observations and balances the resulting losses and gradients. Beyond balancing reconstruction losses, observation normalization enables dynamics prediction to be performed in a normalized observation space, thereby providing a unified treatment of low- and high-dimensional inputs (e.g., physical states and images). Building on this idea, we further introduce Normalized Observation Space Dynamics-Augmented Q-learning (NASDAQ), a framework for observation-predictive RL applicable across diverse domains. NASDAQ learns state-action representations by coupling value learning with two auxiliary tasks: short-term value prediction and next normalized observation prediction. Extensive experiments demonstrate that NASDAQ achieves competitive or superior performance compared with state-of-the-art model-based and self-predictive RL methods, while requiring significantly less training wall-time.

</details>


### [627] [Task-Differentiated Atomic Skill Expansion and Routing for Continual Learning Across Highly Heterogeneous Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21307)
*Jiacheng Wang,Xinjia He,Qi Ding,Yutao Yang,Jie Zhou,Liyang Yu,Liang Dou,Qin Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TASER is a continual learning framework that dynamically expands and routes atomic skills for heterogeneous tasks, outperforming baselines on a new 19-task benchmark (HeteroCLBench).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing CL methods assume tasks are semantically related or structurally similar, struggling with highly heterogeneous settings where tasks differ substantially in reasoning patterns and input-output formats, leading to catastrophic forgetting and inefficient capacity allocation.

Method: TASER consists of three components: (1) atomic skill incremental learning to dynamically expand capacity based on task divergence and model uncertainty; (2) orthogonality-enhanced skill detection to keep skills semantically distinct; (3) skill dynamic routing via lightweight task-conditioned gating to compose task-relevant skills.

Result: On HeteroCLBench (19 tasks across 9 cognitive dimensions), TASER consistently outperforms strong baselines in plasticity and catastrophic forgetting reduction.

Conclusion: Task-differentiated atomic skill expansion and routing effectively addresses heterogeneous continual learning by jointly determining skill quantity per task and skill activation, improving both plasticity and memory retention.

Abstract: Continual learning (CL) is commonly studied under the assumption that sequential tasks are semantically related or structurally similar. However, in highly heterogeneous settings, where tasks differ substantially in reasoning patterns and input-output formats, existing methods often suffer from catastrophic forgetting and inefficient capacity allocation. To address this challenge, we propose Task-differentiated Atomic Skill Expansion and Routing (\texttt{TASER}), a CL framework that jointly determines how many new atomic skills to introduce for each task and which skills to activate. The framework first uses atomic skill incremental learning to dynamically expand capacity based on task divergence and model uncertainty. It then applies orthogonality-enhanced skill detection to ensure these skills remain semantically distinct and independently reusable. Finally, a skill dynamic routing mechanism composes task-relevant skills through lightweight task-conditioned gating. We further introduce \texttt{HeteroCLBench}, a highly heterogeneous benchmark for CL, comprising 19 diverse tasks across 9 cognitive dimensions under a standardized sequential protocol. Experiments on \texttt{HeteroCLBench} show that \texttt{TASER} consistently outperforms strong baselines by improving plasticity and reducing catastrophic forgetting.

</details>


### [628] [Objective-Behavior Alignment: Diagnostics for MORL Policy Selection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21321)
*Antonio Mone,Zuzanna Osika,Florian Felten,Pradeep K. Murukannaiah,Mark Fuge,Frans A. Oliehoek,Luciano Cavalcante Siebert*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a diagnostic workflow for multi-objective RL that highlights behavioral variation along the Pareto front beyond what expected returns reveal, using quantitative and visual tools validated on grid and continuous control tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard MORL presents policies only through their value vectors, which can obscure substantial behavioral variation — policies with distinct trajectories may appear indistinguishable when evaluated solely by expected returns.

Method: The authors develop an exploratory diagnostic workflow with quantitative and visual tools that automatically highlights behavioral variation along the Pareto front that objective values alone do not reveal.

Result: The approach is validated on simple grid examples and scales to continuous control benchmarks, remaining effective as problem complexity increases.

Conclusion: Behavioral variation along the Pareto front can be surfaced beyond objective values, supporting better policy inspection in MORL.

Abstract: Real-world decision-making often requires optimizing multiple competing objectives simultaneously. In reinforcement learning (RL), this is typically addressed by combining reward signals into a single scalar objective via a scalarization function, which can be fragile: small changes in the weights can induce drastically different policies. Multi-objective reinforcement learning (MORL) instead produces sets of policies that explicitly represent trade-offs between objectives. However, these policies are typically presented to the decision maker only through their value vectors, which can obscure substantial behavioral variation: policies that induce distinct trajectories may appear indistinguishable when evaluated solely by expected returns. We propose an exploratory diagnostic workflow that automatically highlights behavioral variation along the Pareto front that objective values alone do not reveal, providing both quantitative and visual tools to support policy inspection. We validate our approach on simple grid examples and scale it to continuous control benchmarks, demonstrating that it remains effective as problem complexity increases.

</details>


### [629] [Distinguishing indistinguishable attractors: Unsupervised anomaly detection with reservoir computers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21322)
*Davide Prosperino,Haochun Ma,Christoph Räth*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A simple Kolmogorov–Smirnov test on reservoir computer output weights forms an unsupervised, online detector that is highly sensitive to regime changes in nonlinear dynamical systems, outperforming classical measures and deep-learning baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Detecting when a nonlinear dynamical system departs from its normal regime is a recurring problem across many scientific fields (cardiology, climate, energy systems). Existing methods, including classical nonlinear measures and modern deep-learning detectors, can fail to detect certain regime changes.

Method: The algorithm uses a reservoir computer with random, fixed input mapping and reservoir. The trained output weights (readout layer) are treated as a representation (fingerprint) of the input dynamics. The empirical cumulative distribution function (ECDF) of these weights is compared to a reference band from training data using a Kolmogorov–Smirnov test, enabling unsupervised, online detection.

Result: The method distinguishes two visually indistinguishable butterfly-shaped attractors, resolves parameter drifts 7x smaller than the strongest deep-learning baseline, flags noise 4 orders of magnitude below the signal, and identifies ventricular flutter in clinical ECG recordings.

Conclusion: The trained output weights of reservoir computers should be treated as a representation of the learned system in their own right, not merely as a means to forecasting, establishing a new perspective for anomaly detection in dynamical systems.

Abstract: Detecting when a nonlinear dynamical system departs from its normal regime is a recurring problem across the sciences, from cardiology to climate and energy systems. We show that a very simple Kolmogorov--Smirnov test on the output weights of a reservoir computer is highly sensitive to regime changes in nonlinear dynamical systems, including those invisible to both classical nonlinear measures and modern deep-learning detectors. The core idea of our algorithm is to treat the readout layer of a reservoir computer as a representation of the input dynamics. Since the input mapping and the reservoir itself are random and fixed, the trained output weights are the only object encoding the system at hand. We summarize this fingerprint by the empirical cumulative distribution function of the readout weights and compare it to a reference band built from the training data. This unsupervised, online detector distinguishes two visually indistinguishable butterfly-shaped attractors, resolves parameter drifts seven times smaller than the strongest deep-learning baseline, flags noise four orders of magnitude below the signal, and identifies ventricular flutter in a clinical ECG recording. More broadly, we aim to establish a perspective on reservoir computers in which the trained output weights are treated as a representation of the learned system in their own right, rather than merely as a means to forecasting.

</details>


### [630] [MedTS-TTT: Test-Time Training for Medical Time Series Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21329)
*Mingzhi Chen,Yiyu Gui,Guibo Luo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MedTS-TTT is a test-time training framework for medical time series that uses Closed-Loop Self-Alignment (CLSA-TTT) with single-step fast-weight updates and a Gated Convolutional Backbone (GCB) to handle subject-level distribution shifts. It achieves 11 top-1 rankings out of 12 evaluations across 4 public datasets and 9 baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Medical time series signals (EEG/ECG) suffer from subject-level heterogeneity causing distribution shifts that make fixed parameters generalize poorly. Existing solutions like domain adaptation depend on extra components or target-batch statistics, while TTT methods often require iterative inner-loop optimization that increases test-time overhead.

Method: MedTS-TTT consists of two key components: (1) CLSA-TTT, which constructs a token-level self-supervised target and performs a single-step fast-weight update for intra-layer closed-loop alignment, avoiding iterative inner-loop optimization; (2) GCB, a gated convolutional backbone that combines CLSA-TTT-based fast adaptation with token-level fusion and a gated convolutional branch to balance local dynamic modeling and information-flow control.

Result: On 4 public datasets (2 EEG, 2 ECG) with subject-independent splits, MedTS-TTT achieves 11 top-1 rankings out of 12 evaluations across 9 baselines and 3 metrics.

Conclusion: MedTS-TTT provides a practical, efficient solution for online adaptation in medical time series, outperforming existing methods while avoiding the overhead of iterative inner-loop optimization.

Abstract: Medical time series (MedTS) signals such as electroencephalography (EEG) and electrocardiography (ECG) support many clinical applications. However, substantial subject-level heterogeneity often induces subject-level distribution shift, causing a fixed parameter set to generalize poorly to unseen individuals. Compared with domain adaptation methods that often depend on extra adaptation components or target-batch statistics, Test-Time Training (TTT) provides a more practical solution for sequential clinical data by enabling online adaptation from unlabeled test samples. However, many representative TTT methods require iterative inner-loop optimization, increasing test-time overhead. In this paper, we propose MedTS-TTT, a test-time training framework for medical time series modeling. MedTS-TTT is built upon Closed-Loop Self-Alignment Test-Time Training (CLSA-TTT) and a Gated Convolutional Backbone (GCB). CLSA-TTT constructs a token-level self-supervised target and performs a single-step fast-weight update for intra-layer closed-loop alignment, enabling rapid sample-wise adaptation without iterative inner-loop optimization. GCB combines CLSA-TTT-based fast adaptation and token-level fusion with a gated convolutional branch to balance local dynamic modeling and information-flow control. On 4 public datasets (2 EEG and 2 ECG) with subject-independent splits, MedTS-TTT achieves 11 top-1 rankings out of 12 evaluations across 9 baselines and 3 metrics. The code is publicly available at https://github.com/mingzhi-c/MedTS-TTT.

</details>


### [631] [Ramanujan Graph Rewiring with Non Negative Resistance Curvature](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21333)
*Hugo Attali,Rachid El Jouhri*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces Ramanujan Propagation, a graph rewiring strategy using Ramanujan graphs to mitigate over-squashing in GNNs by ensuring non-negative resistance curvature, outperforming nine state-of-the-art rewiring techniques.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional message passing in GNNs suffers from over-squashing, where exponentially large neighborhoods are compressed into fixed-dimensional embeddings, hindering long-range dependency learning.

Method: The method leverages Ramanujan graphs as a graph rewiring strategy, proving they guarantee non-negative resistance curvature to mitigate over-squashing, and proposes an algorithmic framework to construct a Ramanujan rewired graph that preserves local connectivity of the original graph.

Result: The proposed method outperforms nine state-of-the-art rewiring techniques in experiments.

Conclusion: Ramanujan graphs establish a rigorous structural prior for scalable, topology-aware message passing in GNNs.

Abstract: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have emerged as a powerful paradigm for learning on graph-structured data by iteratively propagating and aggregating information across edges. However, conventional message passing schemes often suffer from over-squashing, whereby exponentially large neighborhoods are compressed into fixed-dimensional embeddings, impeding effective long-range dependency learning. In this work, we introduce Ramanujan Propagation, a graph rewiring strategy that leverages Ramanujan graphs to alleviate topological bottlenecks in GNNs. We first establish that suitably chosen Ramanujan graphs guarantee non-negative resistance curvature, which mitigates over-squashing and facilitates efficient information flow. We then propose an algorithmic framework to construct a Ramanujan rewired graph that preserves the local connectivity of the original graph. Our experiments demonstrate that our method outperforms nine state-of-the-art rewiring techniques. These results establish Ramanujan graphs as a rigorous structural prior for scalable, topology-aware message passing in GNNs.

</details>


### [632] [DataClaw0: Agentic Tailoring Multimodal Data from Raw Streams](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21337)
*Cong Wan,Zeyu Guo,Zijian Cai,Jiangyang Li,SongLin Dong,Lin Peng,Xiangyang Luo,Zhiheng Ma,Yihong Gong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces DataClaw₀, a 9B parameter model that shifts data processing from passive heuristic-based annotation to active, learnable "Agentic Data Tailoring." It uses a two-stage pipeline grounded in factual anchors to generate training data across five domains, and combines SFT with GRPO for alignment. A new benchmark (DataClaw₀-val) is introduced, and evaluations on video generation, VQA, and GUI navigation show improved data tailoring for downstream tasks under limited data regimes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Massive unstructured multimodal data suffers from high entropy, making human knowledge acquisition and AI post-training inefficient. Existing passive annotation methods (heuristic rules or general VLMs) are costly, monotonous, and fail to extract deep procedural logic from raw data.

Method: The authors propose Agentic Data Tailoring, a learnable paradigm that actively refines and structures data. They design a two-stage pipeline using generative semantic synthesis grounded in deterministic Factual Anchors to create a large-scale dataset across five domains. The DataClaw₀-9B model is trained via SFT + GRPO to align with complex refinement intents. A benchmark (DataClaw₀-val) is built for evaluation.

Result: Evaluations on video generation, real-world VQA, and GUI navigation tasks confirm that DataClaw₀ produces high-information-density tailored data, enabling efficient model adaptation under limited training data conditions.

Conclusion: The proposed paradigm shift from passive annotation to active data tailoring, embodied by DataClaw₀, effectively addresses data entropy and data scarcity bottlenecks, improving downstream task performance with limited data.

Abstract: Massive unstructured multimodal streams suffer from high "data entropy," impeding both efficient human knowledge acquisition and high-quality AI post-training. Existing passive annotation paradigms, heavily reliant on heuristic rules or general VLMs, are costly, monotonous, and fail to unlock the deep procedural logic embedded in raw data. We elevate data processing to a learnable capability, proposing a paradigm shift towards Agentic Data Tailoring, which actively refining and structuring data to align with diverse user and downstream intents. To overcome the data scarcity bottleneck in training such high-order capabilities, we design a two-stage pipeline grounding generative semantic synthesis in deterministic Factual Anchors, yielding a large-scale dataset spanning five core physical and digital domains. Building upon this, $\text{DataClaw}_0$-9B model synergizes Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), achieving robust alignment with complex refinement and tailoring intents. To systematically quantify this capability, we construct $\text{DataClaw}_0$-val, the first benchmark dedicated to data refinement. Crucially, we adopt downstream post-training as the ultimate validation touchstone. Evaluations on video generation, real-world VQA, and GUI navigation confirm that $\text{DataClaw}_0$ delivers high-information-density tailored data, facilitating efficient model adaptation to new tasks under limited training data regimes. Project page: https://czjdsg.github.io/MakeAnyData

</details>


### [633] [A Reward-Petri-Net Interpretation of Temporal Behavior Trees](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21350)
*Till Schmeil,Günther Waxenegger-Wilfing,Sebastian Schirmer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper translates Temporal Behavior Trees (TBTs) into Reward-Petri-Nets (RPNs) to automatically generate reward functions for reinforcement learning in robotic tasks with hierarchical structure and temporal constraints, demonstrating improved learning over vanilla RL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Designing reward functions for complex, long-horizon robotic tasks is notoriously difficult, especially when tasks have hierarchical structure and temporal constraints.

Method: The authors translate Temporal Behavior Trees (TBTs), which extend BTs with Linear Temporal Logic (LTL) constraints in leaf nodes, into Petri Nets (PNs) and automatically assign rewards based on TBT structure, forming Reward-Petri-Nets (RPNs).

Result: TBT-based rewards enable learning where vanilla RL fails, improve sample efficiency, and offer flexible, intuitive control over learning progress across increasingly challenging environments.

Conclusion: TBT-derived RPN rewards are effective for complex robotic RL tasks, outperforming vanilla RL and providing intuitive control via different reward distribution schemes and TBT structures.

Abstract: This paper introduces an interpretation of Temporal Behavior Trees (TBTs) as Reward-Petri-Nets (RPNs) for reinforcement learning (RL). Designing reward functions for complex, long-horizon robotic tasks is notoriously difficult, especially when tasks have hierarchical structure and temporal constraints. TBTs extend conventional behavior trees (BTs) used in robotic applications by incorporating temporal properties into their leaf nodes. This allows TBTs to represents not only the behavioral task structure defined by BT operators such as Sequence, Fallback, and Parallel, but also the task's temporal constraints. In this work, the constraints are specified in the leaf nodes using Linear Temporal Logic. In order to inform RL rewards using TBTs, we provide a translation from TBT into a Petri Net (PN) and show how rewards can be automatically assigned based on the TBT's structure, resulting in a RPN. In a series of increasingly challenging environments, we demonstrate how TBT-based rewards enable learning where vanilla RL fails, improve sample efficiency, and offer flexible, intuitive control over the learning progress. We showcase the learning impact by using different reward distribution schemes and TBT structures.

</details>


### [634] [Urban Power Grid Topology and Hierarchy Identification from Open Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21352)
*Shiliang Zhang,Sabita Maharjan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper develops an open-data-driven framework that fuses public infrastructure data and geospatial machine learning to reconstruct the complete urban power grid topology from high-voltage transmission down to individual building connections, demonstrated on the Alna district in Oslo, Norway with 7,330 buildings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Detailed urban power grid topology is largely proprietary, creating significant barriers for research and innovation in energy prognosis, power flow management, and system resilience analysis, especially for last-mile distribution networks connecting individual buildings.

Method: The framework fuses public infrastructure data (power-lines, substations, transformers, poles) to map the high and medium-voltage skeleton using graph-based algorithms, then leverages geospatial machine learning on OpenStreetMap building data to group power demand clusters and infer the physical topology of final distribution lines linking clustered buildings.

Result: Applied to the Alna district in Oslo, Norway, the framework successfully reconstructed the complete grid topology connecting 7,330 buildings and all major electricity infrastructure assets.

Conclusion: The framework provides a critical open-data-driven tool that facilitates power system analysis, including power flow optimization, cascading failure simulation, and grid resilience analysis against the penetration of distributed renewable generation.

Abstract: Understanding the complex topology and hierarchy of urban power grid is crucial for energy prognosis, power flow management, and system resilience analysis. However, detailed grid information remains largely proprietary. This creates significant barriers for research and innovation, especially when analyzing the last-mile distribution networks connecting individual buildings. This paper addresses this challenge by developing an open-data-driven framework for the complete identification of urban power grid topology, from high-voltage transmission down to individual building connections. Particularly, we fuse public infrastructure data (power-lines, substations, transformers, poles) to map the high and medium-voltage skeleton using graph-based algorithms. We then leverage geospatial machine learning on OpenStreetMap building data to group power demand clusters, and infer the physical topology of the final distribution lines linking the clustered buildings. We apply the developed framework to the district of Alna in Oslo, Norway, and we reconstruct the complete grid topology that connects 7,330 buildings and all major electricity infrastructure assets. With the research in this work, we provide a critical tool that facilitates power system analysis, e.g., power flow optimization, cascading failure simulation, and grid resilience against the penetration of distributed renewable generation.

</details>


### [635] [SOHET: Sequence Of Heterogeneous Events Transformer with Self-Supervised Pre-Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21356)
*Kees Jan de Vries,Mustafa Radha,Mathijs de Jong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SOHET is a hierarchical transformer architecture for heterogeneous event sequences that combines tabular encoders with temporal/type embeddings, achieving state-of-the-art results on fraud detection and benchmark tasks through self-supervised pre-training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many ML applications need to handle heterogeneous event streams for prediction, and existing methods lack effective architectures for combining multiple event types with both causal and bidirectional processing.

Method: SOHET uses a hierarchical architecture with event-type-specific tabular encoders, temporal and type embeddings, processed by a causal or bidirectional transformer, plus three self-supervised pre-training objectives for the causal setting.

Result: Outperforms FlexTPP, NAPPT, and CIPPT by 5.8% on Booking.com fraud detection (17 event types); pre-training adds 2.6% gain with 2.4% faster convergence; matches/exceeds SOTA on 6/8 EBES benchmark tasks.

Conclusion: SOHET effectively models heterogeneous event sequences with strong performance gains from both architecture and self-supervised pre-training.

Abstract: Many machine learning applications rely on heterogeneous event streams to make predictions, either causally as events arrive or bidirectionally over complete sequences. We propose SOHET (Sequence Of Heterogeneous Events Transformer), a hierarchical architecture combining event-type-specific tabular encoders with temporal and type embeddings, processed by a causal or bidirectional transformer. We introduce three self-supervised pre-training objectives for the causal setting. On a proprietary large-scale real-world Booking.com fraud detection task with 17 event types, SOHET outperforms FlexTPP, NAPPT, and CIPPT by 5.8%. Pre-training yields an additional 2.6% gain and 2.4% faster convergence. On the EBES benchmark, bidirectional SOHET matches or exceeds the published best on 6 out of 8 tasks.

</details>


### [636] [Predictive Repair Management Using a Multi-Head Attention Transformer and Online Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21364)
*Xinyao Zhang,Willie Cade,Karl R. Haapala,Arun Natarajan,Sara Behdad*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This study develops a deep learning framework using multi-head attention networks and online learning to predict repair duration from tabular data, achieving 78% accuracy on real-world automobile repair data (2013-2020).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate prediction of repair duration is critical for resource allocation, customer satisfaction, and operational performance in product maintenance.

Method: The framework uses a multi-head attention network designed for tabular data, combining categorical embeddings with numerical historical data, a weighted loss function for class imbalance, and an online learning strategy for continuous model updates.

Result: Achieved 78% accuracy on real-world repair data (2013-2020); multi-head attention outperformed feed-forward neural networks and random forests; online learning improved predictive performance on new data.

Conclusion: The multi-head attention mechanism effectively extracts meaningful interactions between vehicle identifiers and repair types, and the online learning strategy enables real-time adaptation to changing operational patterns.

Abstract: Accurate prediction of repair duration is an important challenge in product maintenance due to its implications for resource allocation, customer satisfaction, and operational performance. This study aims to develop a deep learning framework to help fleet repair shops accurately categorize repair time given product historical data. The study uses an automobile repair and maintenance dataset and creates an end-to-end predictive framework by employing a multi-head attention network designed for tabular data. The developed framework combines categorical information, transformed through embeddings and attention mechanisms, with numerical historical data to facilitate integration and learning from diverse data features. A weighted loss function is introduced to overcome class imbalance issues in large datasets. Moreover, an online learning strategy is used for continuous incremental model updates to maintain predictive accuracy in evolving operational environments. Our empirical findings demonstrate that the multi-head attention mechanism extracts meaningful interactions between vehicle identifiers and repair types compared to a feed-forward neural network and a random forest model. Also, combining historical maintenance data with an online learning strategy facilitates real-time adjustments to changing patterns and increases the model's predictive performance on new data. The model is tested on real-world repair data spanning 2013 to 2020 and achieves an accuracy of 78%, with attention weight analyses illustrating feature interactions.

</details>


### [637] [Enhancing Creativity in 3D Generative Design via a TRIZ-Inspired Text-to-CAD Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21378)
*Dongeon Lee,Leekyo Jeong,Soyoung Yoo,Sunwoong Yang,Namwoo Kang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper presents a TRIZ-inspired text-to-CAD framework that integrates the Theory of Inventive Problem Solving (TRIZ) into LLM prompting strategies to generate creative, editable 3D CAD models. The framework uses a three-stage pipeline (design generation, enhancement, optimization) to produce structurally diverse CAD variants that address technical contradictions. A chair design case study demonstrates 4.0-14.7% mass reduction across enhanced designs while maintaining structural integrity, bridging the gap between precision-focused synthesis and creativity-focused exploration in AI-driven engineering design.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-based 3D CAD generation approaches primarily focus on geometric precision and instruction-following performance, overlooking the fundamental aspect of creative design exploration. This work aims to bridge the gap between precision-focused synthesis and creativity-focused exploration in AI-driven CAD.

Method: The framework integrates TRIZ (Theory of Inventive Problem Solving) embedding deep human insights from patent records into LLM prompting strategies. It employs a comprehensive three-stage pipeline: design generation, enhancement, and optimization. The first two stages are implemented and evaluated, with design optimization positioned as future work. TRIZ inventive principles such as segmentation, anti-weight, dynamics, and composite materials are systematically applied.

Result: A product design case study (chair) demonstrates that the framework generates multiple creative design alternatives via systematic application of TRIZ principles, achieving 4.0-14.7% mass reduction across all enhanced designs while maintaining structural integrity.

Conclusion: Integrating systematic innovation methodologies (TRIZ) with LLM-based 3D CAD generation bridges the gap between precision-focused synthesis and creativity-focused exploration, advancing toward autonomous design systems where AI makes independent design decisions while supporting human decision-making in human-AI collaborative engineering design.

Abstract: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated significant potential in supporting engineering design tasks, including computer-aided design (CAD) automation. However, most existing LLM-based 3D CAD generation approaches primarily focus on geometric precision and instruction-following performance, often overlooking the fundamental aspect of creative design exploration. This study presents a TRIZ-inspired text-to-CAD framework that leverages LLMs to generate high-quality, editable CAD models while systematically exploring creative design alternatives. The framework integrates the Theory of Inventive Problem Solving (TRIZ)-embedding deep human insights from extensive patent records-into LLM prompting strategies, enabling autonomous generation of innovative CAD variants that address technical contradictions. Through a comprehensive three-stage pipeline of design generation, enhancement, and optimization, the framework produces structurally diverse CAD models from well-crafted prompts. The present study implements and evaluates the first two stages, while positioning the design optimization stage as future work. A product design case study (chair) demonstrates that the TRIZ-inspired text-to-CAD framework generates multiple creative design alternatives by systematically applying TRIZ inventive principles such as segmentation, anti-weight, dynamics, and composite materials, achieving 4.0-14.7% mass reduction across all enhanced designs while maintaining structural integrity. The key findings suggest that integrating systematic innovation methodologies with LLM-based 3D CAD generation bridges the gap between precision-focused synthesis and creativity-focused exploration, advancing toward autonomous design systems where AI makes design decisions independently, supporting human decision-making in human-AI collaborative design for engineering applications.

</details>


### [638] [Unsupervised Disentanglement Without Compromises : How Functional Orthogonality Enforces Identifiability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21385)
*Mathieu Cyrille Simon,Pascal Frossard,Christophe De Vleeschouwer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes that unsupervised disentangled representation learning is achievable by enforcing orthogonality constraints on the Jacobian of the generative mapping, proving identifiability for nonlinear generative models without requiring statistical independence or causal assumptions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To challenge prevailing impossibility claims for unsupervised disentanglement and provide a principled alternative foundation by defining latent concepts through locally orthogonal functional directions rather than statistical independence.

Method: Defines latent concepts as factors influencing observations through locally orthogonal Jacobian directions; proves identifiability of general nonlinear generative models under an orthogonality constraint and realizability condition; validates with orthogonality-regularized normalizing flows.

Result: Orthogonality-regularized normalizing flows empirically confirm the theory, demonstrating reliable recovery of ground-truth factors and shedding light on the success of VAEs.

Conclusion: Unsupervised disentanglement is achievable via functional orthogonality constraints, challenging prior impossibility results and offering a new foundation for identifiable representation learning.

Abstract: This paper explores unsupervised disentangled representation learning from a functional perspective. We define latent concepts as factors that influence observations through locally orthogonal directions, formalized as an orthogonality constraint on the Jacobian of the generative mapping. We prove that this condition yields identifiability of general nonlinear generative models, without requiring statistical independence or causal assumptions, provided the latent domain admits all combinations of factor values. Experiments with orthogonality-regularized normalizing flows empirically confirm the theory, demonstrate reliable recovery of ground-truth factors, and shed light on the success of VAEs. These findings challenge the prevailing impossibility claims for unsupervised disentanglement and provide a principled alternative foundation.

</details>


### [639] [VLA-FAIL: Efficient Task Failure Detection for Finetuned Vision-Language-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21386)
*Florian Seligmann,Emiliyan Gospodinov,Enes Ulas Dincer,Gerhard Neumann*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: VLA-FAIL is a lightweight failure detection framework for vision-language-action models that combines last-layer Mahalanobis distance (LLMD) for OOD detection and action chunk consistency (ACC) for temporal inconsistency detection, requiring no failure data or expensive sampling, and achieving reliable early detection across diverse robotic tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vision-language-action models (VLAs) achieve SOTA on robotic manipulation but behave unpredictably in out-of-distribution scenarios, necessitating runtime failure detection. Existing methods are either computationally expensive (requiring action sampling), architecturally restrictive, or require failure rollouts, motivating a lightweight, broadly applicable detector without failure data.

Method: Two complementary detectors: (1) Last-layer Mahalanobis distance (LLMD) measures token-wise deviations in last-layer features from training data to detect OOD states. (2) Action chunk consistency (ACC) exploits receding-horizon control to detect failures when consecutive action chunks become inconsistent. Also proposes AUCPDT, a threshold-independent metric jointly evaluating precision, recall, and detection latency.

Result: Real-world and simulation experiments show LLMD and ACC capture complementary failure modes; their combination enables reliable and early failure detection across diverse tasks, frequently outperforming significantly more expensive baseline methods.

Conclusion: VLA-FAIL provides a lightweight, broadly applicable, and failure-data-free framework for runtime failure detection in VLAs, with complementary detectors that together achieve superior detection accuracy and latency compared to costly alternatives.

Abstract: Vision-language-action models (VLAs) achieve state-of-the-art performance on many robotic manipulation tasks, yet they can still behave unpredictably in out-of-distribution scenarios. Runtime failure detection is therefore essential for the safe real-world deployment of VLAs. However, existing task failure detectors require computationally expensive action sampling, are based on architectural assumptions that limit their applicability to VLAs, or need access to failure rollouts. We propose VLA-FAIL, a lightweight and broadly applicable failure detection framework for VLAs that combines two novel failure detectors with minimal overhead, without requiring failure data. The first, last-layer Mahalanobis distance (LLMD), detects out-of-distribution states by measuring token-wise deviations in last-layer features relative to the training data. The second, action chunk consistency (ACC), exploits the temporal overlap induced by receding-horizon control and detects failures when consecutive action chunks become inconsistent. To capture the trade-off between detection accuracy and detection latency, we introduce AUCPDT, a threshold-independent metric that jointly evaluates precision, recall, and detection time.
