Through extensive real-world and simulation experiments, we demonstrate that LLMD and ACC capture complementary failure modes whose combination enables reliable and early failure detection across diverse tasks, frequently outperforming significantly more expensive baseline methods.

</details>


### [640] [Atomistic Language Models Understand and Generate Materials](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21395)
*Sathya Edamadaka,Krithik Ramesh,Ju Li,Rafael Gómez-Bombarelli*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ALMs integrate an atomistic encoder, LLM, and diffusion model via continuous projectors to enable native multimodal understanding and generation of crystal structures from natural language, achieving SOTA on crystal structure prediction and de novo generation, with a new benchmark (ALM Bench) for text-conditioned crystal tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Atomistic structures and natural language have historically been modeled separately; prior approaches either use language models as mere tools calling atomistic models or fine-tune on lossy textual encodings that discard atomistic information. The goal is to pursue native multimodality where a single language backbone can understand, generate, and optimize crystal structures directly from natural language.

Method: ALMs unify a pretrained atomistic encoder, a large language model, and a denoising diffusion model via purely continuous projectors with staged training. A continuous bridge maps LLM embeddings into the steering space of atomistic diffusion. T2C-FK (Text-to-Crystal Feynman-Kac) is a particle-based sampler that scores partial denoising trajectories to enforce stoichiometric targets at inference time. ALM Bench is introduced for evaluation.

Result: State-of-the-art results on crystal structure prediction and de novo generation tasks. ALM Bench is established as the first benchmark for text-conditioned crystal generation and optimization.

Conclusion: The unified continuous-bridge approach enables effective native multimodality for atomistic language modeling, outperforming prior methods. Code, data, and weights will be released.

Abstract: Atomistic structure and natural language have long been modeled separately, with language models either calling atomistic models as tools or being fine-tuned on lossy textual encodings that discard atomistic information. We introduce Atomistic Language Models (ALMs) to pursue native multimodality, in which a single language backbone understands atomistic structures, generates materials from natural language, and optimizes crystal structures as instructed by text. By unifying a pretrained atomistic encoder, large language model, and denoising diffusion model through purely continuous projectors and staged training, ALMs achieve state-of-the-art results on crystal structure prediction and de novo generation. ALMs are enabled by a continuous bridge that maps language model embeddings directly into the steering space of atomistic diffusion, and are assisted by Text-to-Crystal Feynman-Kac (T2C-FK), a particle-based sampler that scores partial denoising trajectories to enforce stoichiometric targets at inference time. To evaluate the ability of ALMs to optimize and generate materials from natural-language prompts and 3D atom-coordinate inputs, we introduce ALM Bench, the first benchmark for text-conditioned crystal generation and optimization. Code, training data, and model weights will be released soon.

</details>


### [641] [One Size does not Fit All: Heterogeneous Latent Space Alignment for Unsupervised Domain Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21415)
*Evangelia Koskinioti,Yi Shen,Georgios Stamou,Michael M. Zavlanos*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ADualVUOT integrates dual-encoder VAE with Continuous Normalizing Flows and Unbalanced Optimal Transport via Gaussian-Gromov-Wasserstein distance, plus adversarial augmentation, for unsupervised domain adaptation in medical image segmentation, outperforming prior OT-based methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Domain shift hinders reliable deployment of ML models in healthcare; existing domain adaptation methods suffer from limited expressiveness of latent representations and reliance on handcrafted static augmentations.

Method: Proposes ADualVUOT framework: (1) dual-encoder VAE with Continuous Normalizing Flows (CNFs) for flexible posterior modeling; (2) Unbalanced Optimal Transport (UOT) via Gaussian-Gromov-Wasserstein (GGW) distance for domain alignment handling structural/topological discrepancies; (3) adversarial augmentation for worst-case composition synthesis to enhance robustness.

Result: Significant gains over prior OT-based approaches on medical imaging benchmarks.

Conclusion: The proposed dual-encoder VAE with CNFs and UOT-based alignment, combined with adversarial augmentation, effectively addresses domain shift in medical image segmentation.

Abstract: Domain shift remains a major obstacle to the reliable deployment of machine learning models in high-stakes environments such as healthcare. While Domain adaptation aims to mitigate these effects, existing approaches suffer from limited expressiveness of latent representations and a reliance on handcrafted, static augmentations. In this work, we address these limitations by proposing a novel deep learning architecture for Unsupervised Domain Adaptation (UDA), specifically optimized for medical image segmentation. Our framework, ADualVUOT, integrates a dual-encoder Variational Autoencoder (VAE) with Continuous Normalizing Flows (CNFs) to increase modeling flexibility and posterior expressiveness. To achieve domain alignment, we leverage Unbalanced Optimal Transport (UOT) through the Gaussian-Gromov-Wasserstein (GGW) distance, which handles structural and topological discrepancies between domains. Furthermore, we incorporate an adversarial augmentation scheme to synthesize worst-case compositions, thus enhancing model robustness. Extensive experiments on medical imaging benchmarks show significant gains over prior OT-based approaches.

</details>


### [642] [Federated Temporal Attention Intelligence for Cyber-Resilient IoMT: Lightweight Digital Twins and PPO-Driven Honeypot Deception](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21422)
*Syed Zeeshan Haider,Anwar Shah,Muneeb Arif,Hamza Iftikhar,Waqas Ali*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LDT-FRL integrates Temporal Attention Encoder, LSTM-based Digital Twins, Federated PPO, and honeypot layers to achieve 99.66%–99.95% accuracy with 81% faster convergence and perfect detection on imbalanced MITM attacks in IoMT environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: IoMT devices face cybersecurity vulnerabilities due to resource constraints, strict latency requirements, and data-privacy regulations; existing solutions lack lightweight, privacy-preserving defense mechanisms tailored for such environments.

Method: Four-component framework: (1) GRU-based Temporal Attention Encoder for flow-level threat classification; (2) LSTM-based Digital Twins generating per-device anomaly scores with sigmoid coupling to gate TAE; (3) Federated PPO agent selecting ALLOW/ISOLATE/HONEYPOT_REDIRECT actions from a 7D state space; (4) honeypot layer converting redirected traffic into threat intelligence. Federated aggregation uses EMA-smoothed validation losses as inverse-weighted FedAvg coefficients.

Result: 99.66% and 99.95% test accuracy on CICDDoS 2019 and TON-IoT benchmarks; macro-F1 of 0.9913 and 0.9995; 81% faster convergence than DTFL-CD baseline; perfect F1=1.000 on imbalanced MITM class.

Conclusion: LDT-FRL provides an interpretable, privacy-preserving, and efficient defense architecture for IoMT security, validated through SHAP/LIME/Grad-CAM/counterfactual explainability.

Abstract: The rapid proliferation of Internet of Medical Things (IoMT) devices introduces critical cybersecurity vulnerabilities in healthcare environments where resource-constrained medical devices operate under strict latency requirements and stringent data-privacy regulations. To address these challenges, this paper presents the Lightweight Digital Twin and Federated Reinforcement Learning (LDT-FRL) framework, a privacy-preserving defense architecture integrating four complementary mechanisms: a Temporal Attention Encoder (TAE) built on a GRU backbone with learned temporal self-attention for flow-level threat classification; lightweight LSTM-based Digital Twins trained on normal-class traffic to generate per-device anomaly scores that gate the TAE classifier through a learned sigmoid coupling; a Federated Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) agent selecting among ALLOW, ISOLATE, and HONEYPOT_REDIRECT actions based on a seven-dimensional state; and an intelligent honeypot layer that converts redirected suspicious traffic into actionable threat intelligence. A federated aggregation strategy employing EMA-smoothed per-client validation losses as inverse-weighted FedAvg coefficients stabilizes global model updates under non-IID client distributions. Evaluated on CICDDoS 2019 and TON-IoT benchmarks, LDT-FRL achieves 99.66% and 99.95% test accuracy respectively, with macro-F1 scores of 0.9913 and 0.9995, converging 81% faster than the DTFL-CD baseline while attaining perfect F1=1.000 on the severely imbalanced MITM class. Explainability analysis via SHAP, LIME, Grad-CAM, and counterfactual methods confirms that the TAE focuses on semantically meaningful flow features, providing interpretable evidence for each defense decision.

</details>


### [643] [Dual-Attention Convolution Experts for Sparse Tensor Completion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21427)
*Yanlei Liu,Zhenyu Liao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DCGC is a neural tensor factorization approach combining multi-channel convolution networks, gated dual-attention mechanisms, and group-level contrastive learning to improve sparse tensor completion, outperforming state-of-the-art methods on traffic and recommendation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing neural tensor factorization methods struggle to capture complex cross-mode interactions and are vulnerable to extreme data sparsity.

Method: DCGC uses (1) multi-channel convolution networks to generate diverse non-linear alignment patterns of latent factors, (2) gated dual-attention mechanism to focus on important output channels and aligned features, and (3) group-level contrastive learning that aggregates positive samples with identical feedback levels while separating negative samples across different levels to inject self-supervised signals.

Result: DCGC outperforms state-of-the-art methods in sparse tensor completion across five datasets in traffic and recommendation applications.

Conclusion: The proposed dual-attention convolution expert network with group-level contrastive learning effectively addresses cross-mode interaction modeling and data sparsity challenges in tensor factorization.

Abstract: Tensor factorization (TF) has been widely adopted for high-dimensional sparse data completion tasks. Despite significant progress, neural TF methods often struggle to capture complex cross-mode interactions and remain vulnerable to (extreme) data sparsity. To address these challenges, we propose a novel neural tensor factorization approach, termed Dual-Attention Convolution Expert Networks with Group-Level Contrastive Learning (DCGC). For the first problem, DCGC generates diverse non-linear alignment patterns of latent factors via a multi-channel convolution network, and leverages the gated dual-attention mechanism to drive the model to focus on more important output channels (i.e., convolution experts) and the aligned features. Furthermore, DCGC introduces a group-level contrastive learning strategy that aggregates positive samples with identical feedback levels while separating negative samples across different levels. This strategy injects high-quality self-supervised signals to mitigate data sparsity. Extensive experiments conducted on five datasets demonstrate that our DCGC outperforms the state-of-the-art methods in sparse tensor completion for traffic and recommendation applications. Code to reproduce the experimental results in the paper is available at https://github.com/ku1z/DCGC.

</details>


### [644] [Universal Encoders for Modular Relational Deep Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21434)
*Jakub Peleška,Gustav Šír*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper identifies four core pillars (semantic granularity, structural topology, temporal causality, unified optimization) for foundational relational models, and proposes a Universal Row Encoder that decouples row encoding from graph message-passing using a transformer-based module integrating raw cell data with schema metadata to produce invariant row embeddings, enabling cross-database knowledge transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Relational Deep Learning (RDL) approaches face generalization obstacles: they are either schema-specific (requiring training from scratch per database) or rely on monolithic architectures that entangle feature encoding with graph message-passing, limiting cross-database transferability.

Method: Proposes a modular approach decoupling row encoding from graph message-passing via a Universal Row Encoder — a transformer-based module that integrates raw cell data with schema metadata (column semantics, table names, global distribution statistics) and uses intra-row self-attention to produce table-width invariant row embeddings, serving as a pretrained backend for downstream graph architectures.

Result: The pretrained encoder enhances cross-database knowledge transfer on RelBench benchmarks while improving learning convergence and memory footprint.

Conclusion: Decoupling row encoding from graph message-passing and focusing on four core pillars (semantic granularity, structural topology, temporal causality, unified optimization) enables more generalizable foundational relational models.

Abstract: Relational Deep Learning (RDL) models multi-tabular databases as temporal heterogeneous graphs for end-to-end representation learning. While RDL is evolving rapidly, existing approaches face significant generalization obstacles. They are either schema-specific, requiring training from scratch for every new database, or they rely on monolithic architectures that entangle feature encoding with graph message-passing. Analyzing these limitations, we establish four core pillars for building foundational relational models: semantic granularity, structural topology, temporal causality, and unified optimization.
  Addressing these pillars, we propose a modular approach that decouples row encoding from graph message-passing. We introduce the Universal Row Encoder, a transformer-based module that integrates raw cell data with schema metadata$-$including column semantics, table names, and global distribution statistics$-$to produce table-width invariant row embeddings. By explicitly feeding global statistics to an intra-row self-attention mechanism, the encoder natively contextualizes unseen features and handles sparse data. Serving as a flexible "backend" for any downstream graph architecture, our pretrained encoder enhances cross-database knowledge transfer on the established RelBench benchmarks while improving learning convergence and memory footprint.

</details>


### [645] [Fusing Backdoors, Machine Learning, and Optimization for Large-Scale Parametric Mixed-Integer Programs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21440)
*El Mehdi Er Raqabi,Pascal Van Hentenryck*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes BIPC, a Learning to Optimize framework that uses backdoor variables and supervised learning to significantly accelerate large-scale mixed-integer programming under parameter perturbations, with minimal loss in solution quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large-scale optimization problems are repeatedly solved under similar structural conditions with varying parameters, causing substantial computational overhead in applications like power systems, transportation, and supply chains.

Method: The BIPC framework has three phases: (I) identify backdoor variables from a distribution of instances, (II) use supervised learning to predict values/interval bounds for backdoor variables, creating a reduced problem, (III) solve the reduced problem with a correction step for feasibility/optimality guarantees.

Result: Experiments on real-world large-scale problems show substantial reductions in solution time with only limited loss in solution quality.

Conclusion: BIPC enables efficient solving of large-scale optimization problems under frequent parameter perturbations (e.g., demand fluctuations, operational changes), offering a practical ML-integration approach for existing optimization pipelines.

Abstract: Large-scale optimization problems are often solved repeatedly under similar structural conditions, leading to substantial computational overhead. This occurs in applications such as power systems, transportation, and supply chain networks, where the underlying structure is fixed while parameters frequently vary under perturbations.
  This paper proposes a Learning to Optimize (LTO) framework that accelerates the solution of large-scale general mixed-integer problems by leveraging the concept of a backdoor, i.e., a subset of variables that drive most of the computational complexity. The proposed BIPC framework consists of three phases. Phase I is an identification procedure that discovers a backdoor for a set of instances in the distribution. Phase II uses supervised learning to develop machine learning models that, given an instance, predict values for bounded-domain backdoor variables and intervals for wide-domain backdoor variables. These predictions define a reduced optimization problem where the predictions constrain the backdoor variables, while the other variables remain free. Phase III optimizes this reduced problem and, if necessary, applies a correction step to restore feasibility or the optimality guarantees.
  Experiments on real-world, large-scale problems show substantial reductions in solution time with only a limited loss in solution quality. The framework enables organizations to solve large-scale optimization problems efficiently in the presence of frequent perturbations, such as unexpected events, demand fluctuations, or operational changes. Because these changes affect parameters rather than the problem structure, BIPC can quickly provide high-quality, feasible solutions, offering a practical approach to integrating machine learning into existing optimization pipelines.

</details>


### [646] [Fast-TurboQuant: A Multiplier-Free Online Vector Quantization Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21448)
*Pedro M. R. Pereira,Felipe A. P. de Figueiredo,Rausley A. A. de Souza*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Fast-TurboQuant replaces dense random rotation matrices with a multiplier-free fast Johnson-Lindenstrauss transform (Rademacher phase inversion + FWHT) for 1-bit quantization of key-value caches and RAG embeddings, achieving 19.7x speedup on DBpedia OpenAI-3 Large embeddings while improving Recall@10 through dimension expansion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Memory bandwidth for key-value caches and RAG systems is a critical bottleneck in scaling large language models. While 1-bit quantization (TurboQuant) addresses this, its reliance on dense random rotation matrices requires millions of floating-point multiplications per embedding, making deployment difficult on constrained edge silicon.

Method: Replace dense random rotation matrices with a structured fast Johnson-Lindenstrauss transform consisting of: (1) Rademacher phase inversion, followed by (2) fast Walsh-Hadamard transform (FWHT). This leverages sub-Gaussian concentration to satisfy Lloyd-Max quantization prerequisites without Gaussian projections, reducing arithmetic complexity to only additions (eliminating hardware multipliers).

Result: 19.7x algorithmic speedup under sequential execution on DBpedia OpenAI-3 Large embeddings. Dimension expansion from FWHT zero-padding reduces mean squared error and improves Recall@10.

Conclusion: Fast-TurboQuant enables multiplier-free, hardware-efficient 1-bit quantization for edge deployment by replacing dense rotations with structured transforms, eliminating floating-point multiplications while improving both speed and retrieval quality.

Abstract: As large language models scale, memory bandwidth for key-value caches and retrieval-augmented generation systems becomes a critical bottleneck. While 1-bit quantization addresses this constraint, recent TurboQuant relies on dense random rotation matrices to condition the vector distribution before quantization. This projection demands millions of floating-point multiplications per embedding, making it difficult to deploy on constrained edge silicon. We introduce Fast-TurboQuant, a multiplier-free projection architecture that replaces the dense matrix with a structured fast Johnson-Lindenstrauss transform. By applying a Rademacher phase inversion followed by a fast Walsh-Hadamard transform (FWHT), the method leverages sub-Gaussian concentration to satisfy the prerequisites of scalar Lloyd-Max quantization without Gaussian projections. This substitution reduces the arithmetic complexity to only additions, eliminating hardware multipliers. Evaluation on DBpedia OpenAI-3 Large embeddings demonstrates a 19.7 times algorithmic speedup under sequential execution. Furthermore, the dimension expansion due to the FWHT zero-padding reduces the mean squared error and improves Recall@10.

</details>


### [647] [Post-Training Speech Enhancement Language Models with Perceptual Rewards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21458)
*Frédéric Berdoz,Luca A. Lanzendörfer,Antonis Asonitis,Roger Wattenhofer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a post-training stage for autoregressive speech enhancement language models using Group Sequence Policy Optimization (GSPO) with multi-metric perceptual rewards (DNSMOS, WER, UTMOS), achieving state-of-the-art results on DNS2020 without learned surrogates or offline preference pairs. Multi-reward optimization avoids reward hacking seen with single-metric training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Speech enhancement language models are trained with token-level cross-entropy loss, which does not align with the perceptual quality metrics (e.g., DNSMOS, WER, UTMOS) used during evaluation, creating a training-evaluation mismatch.

Method: The authors apply Group Sequence Policy Optimization (GSPO) as a post-training stage to directly optimize non-differentiable quality metrics as reward signals for autoregressive speech enhancement language models, using a composite multi-metric reward without learned surrogates or offline preference pairs.

Result: The approach achieves state-of-the-art results on the DNS2020 benchmark when applied to UniSE and GenSE base models. Human evaluation confirms the composite multi-metric reward is preferred over any single-metric variant.

Conclusion: Multi-reward optimization via GSPO effectively aligns speech enhancement language models with perceptual evaluation metrics and avoids reward hacking, outperforming single-metric training approaches.

Abstract: Speech enhancement language models achieve strong results when trained on discrete audio tokens, but their optimization relies on token-level cross-entropy rather than the perceptual metrics used for evaluation. We introduce a post-training stage for autoregressive speech enhancement language models using Group Sequence Policy Optimization (GSPO) with multi-metric perceptual rewards. Our method directly optimizes non-differentiable quality metrics (DNSMOS, WER, and UTMOS) as reward signals, without learned surrogates or offline preference pairs. Applied to two autoregressive base models, UniSE and GenSE, our approach achieves state-of-the-art results on the DNS2020 benchmark. A human evaluation ablation further shows that the composite multi-metric reward is preferred over any single-metric variant, confirming that multi-reward optimization avoids the reward hacking observed with single-metric training.

</details>


### [648] [Deep Learning for Soil Moisture Estimation: Fusing Satellite Data with Optimally-Lagged Meteorological Features](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21475)
*Adrian Canovas-Rodriguez,Aurora González Vidal,Antonio F. Skarmeta*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a Cross-Correlation Function (CCF) methodology to determine optimal temporal lags between meteorological variables and soil moisture, and inter-depth lags for vertical moisture propagation. Three deep learning architectures (CNN, LSTM, CNN-LSTM hybrid) were evaluated across five feature configurations on seven agricultural plots in Spain. The CNN-LSTM hybrid achieved the best overall performance (R²=0.930), demonstrating that modeling atmospheric and subsurface delays substantially improves soil moisture estimation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate soil moisture estimation in semi-arid agricultural regions requires integrating remote sensing and meteorological information while accounting for the delayed response of soil moisture to atmospheric forcing.

Method: A Cross-Correlation Function (CCF) methodology determines optimal temporal lags (0-30 days) between meteorological variables and soil moisture, and inter-depth lags (0-15 days) for vertical moisture propagation. Three deep learning architectures (per-pixel CNN, frame-level LSTM, CNN-LSTM hybrid with sliding windows) were tested under five feature configurations (satellite-only to full satellite-meteorology-depth fusion) across seven agricultural plots.

Result: The per-pixel CNN achieved the strongest single-patch result (R²=0.877, RMSE=2.28), with a seven-patch average R² of 0.535 (+1.00 over satellite-only baseline). The pooled CNN-LSTM hybrid obtained the highest overall performance (R²=0.930, CVRMSE=8.0%). Subsurface depth information proved decisive across all architectures.

Conclusion: Explicitly modelling atmospheric and vertical subsurface delays substantially improves soil moisture estimation for precision agriculture.

Abstract: Accurate soil moisture estimation in semi-arid agricultural regions requires integrating remote sensing and meteorological information while accounting for the delayed response of soil moisture to atmospheric forcing. This study introduces a Cross-Correlation Function (CCF) methodology to determine optimal temporal lags (0-30 days) between meteorological variables and soil moisture, as well as inter-depth lags (0-15 days) describing vertical moisture propagation from the surface (10 cm) to deeper layers (20-50 cm). The approach was validated across seven agricultural plots in southeastern Spain. Three deep learning architectures, each targeting a distinct prediction granularity, were evaluated under five feature configurations ranging from satellite-only to full satellite-meteorology-depth fusion: a CNN for per-pixel estimation within each plot, an LSTM for frame-level (daily plot-mean) prediction, and a CNN-LSTM hybrid operating on sliding windows with pooled multi-patch training. Models were assessed on held-out data to measure genuine generalisation. Meteorological variables improved performance over the satellite-only baseline, while subsurface depth information proved decisive across all architectures. The per-pixel CNN achieved the strongest single-patch result (R^2 = 0.877, RMSE = 2.28), with a seven-patch average R^2 of 0.535, representing an improvement of +1.00 over the satellite-only baseline. The pooled CNN-LSTM hybrid obtained the highest overall performance (R^2 = 0.930, CVRMSE = 8.0%). These results demonstrate that explicitly modelling atmospheric and vertical subsurface delays substantially improves soil moisture estimation for precision agriculture.

</details>


### [649] [Robustness Cannot be Reduced to Regularization: Studying Adversarial Training Beyond the Linear Case](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21488)
*David A. R. Robin,Rafael Pinot,Yann Chevaleyre*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proves that for two-layer neural networks, the adversarial risk cannot be reduced to any simple regularized risk (unlike linear models), and provides empirical evidence that this impossibility extends to deeper architectures like Wide-ResNets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Adversarial training is effective but computationally expensive; for linear models, adversarial risk is equivalent to a simpler regularized risk, enabling efficient training. The key question is whether this equivalence can extend beyond linear models to reduce computational costs in deeper networks.

Method: Formal proof via reduction to key properties showing fundamental separation between adversarial risk and simple regularized risk in two-layer networks, followed by empirical experiments on Wide-ResNets.

Result: No such equivalence is possible for two-layer networks; the adversarial risk is fundamentally different from any simple regularized risk that only exhibits weak data dependence. Empirical evidence on deeper architectures (Wide-ResNets) suggests the same impossibility persists.

Conclusion: The formal equivalence between adversarial risk and regularized risk that holds for linear models does not extend to deeper networks, suggesting that alternative approaches beyond simple regularization are needed for efficient adversarial training in non-linear models.

Abstract: The vulnerability of ML models to adversarial examples has recently emerged as a major concern. While adversarial training is one of the most effective countermeasures to this issue, its high computational cost remains an obstacle to practical deployment. Recent progress in reducing this cost has relied, in the case of linear models, on a formal equivalence between the adversarial risk and a simpler form of regularized risk. This enabled significantly more efficient training procedures, which naturally raises the question of whether such an equivalence can be extended beyond linear models. In this work, we formally show that no such equivalence is possible for two-layer networks. Our proofs proceed via a reduction to key properties that fundamentally separate the adversarial risk from any simple regularized risk which would only exhibit a weak form of data dependence. Beyond this setting, we provide empirical evidence on Wide-ResNets indicating that the same type of impossibility persists in deeper and more expressive architectures.

</details>


### [650] [Predicting High-Risk Colorectal Polyps in African Americans Using Pre-Colonoscopy Clinical Features: Machine Learning Model Development and Temporal Validation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21492)
*Basheer Qolomany,Mrinalini Deverapall,Adeyinka Laiyemo,Zaki Sherif,Mori Yuichi,Omer Ahmed,Hassan Brim,Hassan Ashktorab*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This study developed and validated machine learning models using only non-invasive, pre-colonoscopy features to predict high-risk colorectal polyps in a diverse, predominantly African American urban cohort, achieving risk stratification without reliance on colonoscopy or pathology findings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether non-invasive, pre-procedural features (demographic, clinical, behavioral) can effectively predict high-risk colorectal polyps, which could improve clinical decision-making, optimize colonoscopy resource allocation, and promote equitable access to risk stratification, especially in underserved settings.

Method: Retrospective cohort study using 4,681 patients (2015-2022) for internal validation and 1,562 patients (2023-2024) for external validation. Multiple machine learning models (neural networks, random forest, SVM, Naive Bayes, logistic regression, decision trees, KNN, XGBoost) were trained on pre-colonoscopy features. High-risk polyps were defined as villous/tubulovillous adenomas, high-grade dysplasia, polyps ≥10mm, or ≥3 polyps per procedure.

Result: Machine learning models using non-invasive pre-colonoscopy data were developed and externally validated for predicting high-risk colorectal polyps in a predominantly African American cohort (not specified in abstract, but models were compared across 8 algorithms).

Conclusion: Non-invasive, pre-procedural features can be leveraged for risk stratification of high-risk colorectal polyps, potentially reducing unnecessary colonoscopies in low-risk patients and prioritizing surveillance for high-risk individuals, especially in resource-limited settings.

Abstract: Risk stratification for advanced colorectal polyps typically relies on colonoscopy and/or pathology findings. However, there is growing interest in whether non-invasive features available prior to colonoscopy can help identify patients at higher risk. Such approaches may enhance clinical decision-making by prioritizing surveillance for individuals most likely to harbor high-risk polyps, when colonoscopy resources are limited while potentially reducing unnecessary procedures in lower-risk patients. Importantly, the use of non-invasive, pre-procedural information may also help promote more equitable access to risk stratification, particularly in settings where colonoscopy resources are limited or unevenly distributed. We aimed to develop and externally validate machine learning models to predict high-risk colorectal polyps using only non-invasive, pre-colonoscopy demographic, clinical, and behavioral features in a diverse, predominantly African American, urban cohort.
  We conducted a retrospective cohort study using demographic, lifestyle, and comorbidity data from patients who underwent colonoscopy at Howard University Hospital to develop and validate several machine learning models, including neural networks, random forest, support vector machines (SVM), Naive Bayes, logistic regression, decision trees, k-nearest neighbors (KNN), and XGBoost, for predicting high-risk colorectal polyps. High-risk polyps (HRP) were defined as villous or tubullovillous adenomas, high-grade dysplasia, polyps >= 10 mm in size, and/or the presence of >= 3 polyps per procedure; all other cases were classified as low-risk polyps (LRP). The dataset included 4,681 patients from 2015-2022 used for internal validation and 1,562 patients from 2023-2024 used for external validation.

</details>


### [651] [Breaking chains with trees: Deep learning with $\mathcal{O}(\log N)$ parallel time complexity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21497)
*Neeraj Mohan Sushma,Aditya Nagarsekar,Cabrel Teguemne Fokam,Robin Schiewer,Amit Kumar Pal,Anand Subramoney,David Kappel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HBLL is a training framework that decomposes deep neural networks into hierarchically linked blocks with local learning objectives, eliminating full backpropagation while achieving O(log N) parallel time complexity and competitive performance on vision/language tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Backpropagation suffers from locking (sequential layer-wise interdependence preventing parallelism) and the weight transport problem (requiring symmetric forward/backward pathways), which restrict parallelism, increase memory/communication overhead, and challenge scalable learning.

Method: HBLL decomposes deep neural networks into hierarchically linked blocks trained with local objectives derived from variational principles, eliminating end-to-end backpropagation. It enables O(log N) parallel training time and defines a family of subnetworks for flexible inference.

Result: HBLL achieves O(log N) parallel time complexity (first algorithm to do so), competitive performance on challenging vision and language modeling tasks, and extends to recurrent architectures (replacing backpropagation through time).

Conclusion: HBLL effectively removes the locking and weight transport constraints of backpropagation, enabling highly parallelized training while maintaining competitive performance across diverse tasks and architectures.

Abstract: Modern deep neural network architectures are trained via backpropagation, which requires errors to be sequentially propagated through all layers before parameters can be updated. This introduces two limitations: locking, where layer-wise updates are strictly interdependent and cannot proceed in parallel, and the weight transport problem, which requires symmetric forward and backward pathways for exact gradient computation. These constraints restrict parallelism, increase memory and communication overhead, and pose challenges for scalable learning. In this work, we propose Hierarchical Block-Local Learning (HBLL), a framework that decomposes deep neural networks into hierarchically linked blocks trained using local learning objectives derived from variational principles, eliminating the need for full end-to-end backpropagation while maintaining effective information propagation across the network. HBLL is the first algorithm that is able to train deep neural networks in $\mathcal{O}(\log N)$ parallel time complexity, where $N$ is the number of network layers. We show that HBLL implicitly defines a family of subnetworks corresponding to different hierarchical paths, enabling flexible inference with different effective numbers of layers. We evaluate HBLL on a set of challenging vision and language modeling tasks, achieving competitive performance. We also extend HBLL to recurrent sequence architectures, applying to settings that otherwise rely on backpropagation through time.

</details>


### [652] [Privacy-Preserving Federated Temporal Graph Learning with Digital Twin--Guided Adaptive Deception for Cyber-Resilient IoMT](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21513)
*Syed Zeeshan Haider,Anwar Shah,Muneeb Arif,Hamza Iftikhar,Waqas Ali*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes Federated TGCN-A2C, a privacy-preserving defense architecture combining Temporal GCN, LSTM-based Digital Twins, Federated A2C reinforcement learning, and enhanced honeypots for IoT/IoMT cybersecurity. It achieves 99.48%–99.61% accuracy on CICDDoS 2019 and TON-IoT benchmarks, outperforming baselines, with explainability via SHAP/LIME/Grad-CAM supporting regulatory compliance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: IoT and IoMT devices introduce critical cybersecurity vulnerabilities due to resource constraints, strict latency requirements, and data-privacy regulations, necessitating a privacy-preserving defense mechanism that can operate effectively under non-IID data distributions in healthcare and industrial environments.

Method: Four integrated mechanisms: (1) PyG-based Temporal GCN with GCNConv layers, global mean pooling, and learned anomaly gate for flow-level classification; (2) LSTM-based Digital Twins generating per-device anomaly scores that gate the classifier via learned sigmoid coupling; (3) Federated A2C agent selecting ALLOW/ISOLATE/HONEYPOT-REDIRECT actions based on a seven-dimensional state vector; (4) Enhanced honeypot layer with adaptive thresholds converting suspicious traffic into threat intelligence. Federated aggregation uses EMA-smoothed per-client validation losses as inverse-weighted FedAvg coefficients with cosine-annealed learning rates.

Result: 99.48% and 99.61% test accuracy with weighted-F1 scores of 0.9948 and 0.9961 on CICDDoS 2019 and TON-IoT; converges within 25 and 10 federated rounds; outperforms Fed-Inforce-Fusion by 0.21 percentage points; all 16 CICDDoS classes achieve F1 ≥ 0.9237, all 10 TON-IoT classes achieve F1 ≥ 0.9488 including severely imbalanced MITM category.

Conclusion: The framework achieves state-of-the-art performance with privacy preservation and explainability, supporting regulatory accountability in clinical deployments via SHAP, LIME, Grad-CAM, and counterfactual analysis confirming semantically meaningful flow features.

Abstract: The rapid proliferation of IoT and IoMT devices introduces critical cybersecurity vulnerabilities in healthcare and industrial environments where resource-constrained devices operate under strict latency and data-privacy regulations. This paper presents the Federated Temporal Graph Convolutional Network with Advantage Actor-Critic (Federated TGCN-A2C), a privacy-preserving defense architecture integrating four mechanisms: a PyG-based Temporal GCN using GCNConv layers with global mean pooling and a learned anomaly gate for flow-level threat classification; LSTM-based Digital Twins generating per-device anomaly scores gating the classifier via learned sigmoid coupling; a Federated A2C agent selecting among ALLOW, ISOLATE, and HONEYPOT-REDIRECT actions based on a seven-dimensional state capturing confidence, entropy, anomaly magnitude, and traffic composition; and an enhanced honeypot layer converting suspicious traffic into threat intelligence with adaptive thresholds. Federated aggregation employs EMA-smoothed per-client validation losses as inverse-weighted FedAvg coefficients to stabilize global model updates under non-IID distributions, with cosine-annealed learning rates per round. Evaluated on CICDDoS 2019 and TON-IoT benchmarks, the framework achieves 99.48% and 99.61% test accuracy with weighted-F1 scores of 0.9948 and 0.9961, converging within 25 and 10 federated rounds, outperforming Fed-Inforce-Fusion by 0.21 percentage points while covering three additional attack categories. All sixteen CICDDoS 2019 classes achieve F1 of at least 0.9237 and all ten TON-IoT classes achieve F1 of at least 0.9488, including the severely imbalanced MITM category. Post-hoc explainability via SHAP, LIME, Grad-CAM, and counterfactual analysis confirms decisions are grounded in semantically meaningful flow features, supporting regulatory accountability in clinical deployments.

</details>


### [653] [Towards Understanding the Power and Limits of the Muon Optimizer: A River-Valley Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21514)
*Tianqi Shen,Jinji Yang,Runze Shi,Jianhao Ma,Jiaye Teng,Ziye Ma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper develops a trajectory-level theory for Muon optimizer, showing it converges faster early in LLM training but slower near the optimum compared to gradient descent, motivating a two-stage approach switching to GD-like optimizers in the final phase.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a gap between Muon's positive theoretical narrative (spectral normalization, improved conditioning) and its mixed empirical performance in LLM training where gains over Adam/AdamW are schedule-sensitive and not uniformly superior.

Method: The authors develop a mixed-spiked matrix sensing model with signal/spike/bulk components and a river-valley perspective (river direction for desired solution, hill directions for nuisance info). They analyze Muon in momentum-free and momentum settings, studying convergence on the spectral river.

Result: In momentum-free setting: Muon moves faster along river direction early but converges slower near river bottom than GD. With momentum: Muon converges faster early but its orthogonalized update removes residual scale info, causing overshooting and oscillation near the target solution.

Conclusion: Muon's benefits are primarily early-phase; the authors recommend switching to GD-like refinement optimizers in the final phase rather than relying on fixed learning-rate schedules for Muon, with preliminary LLM training evidence supporting this two-stage approach.

Abstract: Recently, Muon has gained substantial attention as an appealing alternative to Adam-like optimizers, with many works highlighting its advantages through spectral normalization and improved conditioning. Yet this positive theoretical narrative contrasts with its empirical performance in large language model (LLM) training, where Muon's gains over Adam/AdamW are often mixed, schedule-sensitive, and not uniformly superior. To address this gap, we develop a trajectory-level theory characterizing both the strengths and limitations of Muon. We introduce a mixed-spiked matrix sensing model whose sensing operator decomposes into signal, spike, and bulk components, capturing a mixture of anisotropic structure and long-tail information reminiscent of LLM training. On top of it, we adopted a river-valley perspective in which we view the landscape as composed of a river direction flowing to the desired solution and hill directions encoding nuisance or task-irrelevant information. In the momentum-free setting, we show that Muon moves faster along the information-bearing river direction during early optimization, but can converge much more slowly near the river bottom than gradient descent. We then extend the river-valley perspective to general nonconvex objectives with momentum by studying points on the spectral river. There, while Muon converges faster early on, its orthogonalized update removes residual scale information, making it prone to overshooting and oscillation near the target solution. Together, these results suggest that our characterizations extend beyond spiked matrix sensing and motivate switching to GD-like refinement optimizers in the final phase, rather than relying only on a fixed learning-rate schedule for Muon. We also provide preliminary evidence supporting this two-stage approach in language model training experiments.

</details>


### [654] [Backpropagating Through Simulation: Analytic Policy Gradients for Sample and Learning Efficient Differentiable Continuous Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21525)
*Yueci Deng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces Analytic Policy Gradients (APG), which leverages differentiable environment dynamics to compute exact policy gradients via backpropagation through simulation, and evaluates it against PPO on four continuous control tasks with varying dynamical complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Model-free RL algorithms like PPO require millions of interactions and rely on high-variance advantage estimates due to treating the environment as a black box. When environment dynamics are differentiable, exact gradient computation via backpropagation through simulation becomes possible, potentially improving sample efficiency.

Method: The authors propose Analytic Policy Gradients (APG), which computes exact policy gradients by backpropagating through differentiable environment dynamics. They evaluate APG against PPO on four tasks of increasing complexity: 1D point-mass reaching, 2D point-mass navigation with obstacles, 2D rigid-body T-block pushing, and 7-DOF Franka FR3 end-effector reaching. Both algorithms share identical model architectures, observation normalization, and optimizer settings. A multi-axis evaluation protocol records performance against environment steps and gradient steps. A segmented backpropagation scheme with MC and critic-based bootstrap modes is introduced to mitigate gradient degradation on long horizons.

Result: APG is compared to PPO on four continuous control tasks with increasing dynamical complexity, with ablations over segment length and bootstrap strategy.

Conclusion: The segmented backpropagation scheme with MC and critic-based bootstrap modes mitigates gradient degradation on long-horizon tasks, enabling APG to potentially outperform PPO in sample efficiency on differentiable environments.

Abstract: Model-free reinforcement learning algorithms such as Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) treat the environment as a black box, estimating policy gradients from sampled rewards; this process demands millions of interactions and relies on high-variance advantage estimates. When environment dynamics are differentiable, the return is an end-to-end differentiable function of the policy parameters, enabling exact gradient computation via backpropagation through simulation. We term this approach Analytic Policy Gradients (APG) and evaluate it against PPO on four continuous control tasks of increasing dynamical complexity: a one-dimensional point-mass target-reaching task, a 2D point-mass navigation task with obstacle avoidance, a 2D rigid-body T-block pushing task, and a 7-DOF Franka FR3 end-effector reaching task. Both algorithms share identical model architectures, observation normalization, and optimizer settings. To decouple sample efficiency from compute efficiency, we design a multi-axis evaluation protocol that records performance against environment steps and gradient steps. We report a segmented backpropagation scheme with MC and critic-based bootstrap modes that mitigates gradient degradation on long-horizon tasks, and present ablations over segment length and bootstrap strategy.

</details>


### [655] [Memory Is No Longer a Bottleneck: Memory-Efficient Graph Filtering for Scalable Collaborative Filtering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21540)
*Jin-Duk Park,Won-Yong Shin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Mem-GF proposes a memory-efficient graph filtering approach for collaborative filtering that uses Krylov subspace approximation to avoid storing the full item similarity graph, achieving up to 5.74× lower memory usage and 4.38× speedup while outperforming existing GF and GCN methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing graph filtering (GF)-based CF methods suffer from a critical memory bottleneck because they need to store the full item similarity graph, making them impractical for large-scale datasets.

Method: Mem-GF leverages Krylov subspaces as a core mechanism to approximate polynomial graph filters without explicitly storing the item similarity graph, and provides theoretical analysis on the minimum Krylov subspace size for lossless approximation.

Result: Achieves up to 5.74× lower memory usage and 4.38× speedup in runtime, while consistently exceeding the recommendation accuracy of state-of-the-art GF and GCN-based methods. Scales robustly to datasets with tens of millions of interactions.

Conclusion: Mem-GF is a practically viable and theoretically grounded solution for efficient collaborative filtering, overcoming the memory bottleneck of prior GF methods while maintaining or improving accuracy.

Abstract: Graph convolutional networks (GCNs) have demonstrated significant success in capturing complex user-item relationships for collaborative filtering (CF). However, due to their reliance on extensive model training, training-free graph filtering (GF)-based CF methods have emerged as a promising alternative, offering computational efficiency by smoothing graph signals via matrix operations. In particular, polynomial GF-based approaches demonstrate improved accuracy through their ability to design more expressive and flexible filtering functions. Despite these advantages, existing GF methods suffer from a critical memory bottleneck: they necessitate storing the full item similarity graph, incurring prohibitive memory costs for large-scale datasets, which limits their practical applicability. To tackle this challenge, we propose Mem-GF (Memory-efficient GF), a new GF-based CF method that departs from conventional designs by principally leveraging the structure of Krylov subspaces as a core mechanism for approximating polynomial graph filters without explicitly storing the item similarity graph. We theoretically analyze the minimum Krylov subspace size that guarantees lossless approximation. Through extensive experiments, we demonstrate that Mem-GF achieves up to 5.74$\times$ lower memory usage and 4.38$\times$ speedup in runtime, while consistently exceeding the recommendation accuracy of state-of-the-art GF and GCN-based methods. Mem-GF robustly scales to datasets with tens of millions of interactions, establishing itself as a practically viable and theoretically grounded solution for efficient CF.

</details>


### [656] [LIG: Layer-wise Integrated Gradients for Within-Layer Flow Analysis in Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21564)
*Eight Suzuki,Hideitsu Hino,Noboru Murata*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LIG (Layer-wise Integrated Gradients) extends Integrated Gradients to set-to-set mappings via L2 scalarization, enabling token-to-token attribution across Transformer module boundaries (ATT and MLP) for interpretability without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Transformers are performant but opaque; existing XAI methods like IG are limited to scalar objectives, and LRP lacks completeness guarantees at module boundaries. The authors aim to provide a granular, conservation-principled attribution method at module-boundary level.

Method: Views each Transformer layer as a dynamic graph; applies LIG which uses set-to-set Integrated Gradients (via L2 scalarization) at nonlinear module boundaries (ATT and MLP). Composes within-layer contributions in LRP spirit, using IG completeness as conservation. Evaluated on BERT-base and PTB with various baseline configurations.

Result: Best configurations: target token embedding as ATT baseline; ATT output at a=0 or Zero as MLP baseline. LIG enables within-layer information flow tracing and module-wise vs. layer-whole attribution agreement analysis.

Conclusion: LIG provides a diagnostic XAI tool at module-boundary granularity for Transformers, without model-specific retraining or per-operation interpreter design.

Abstract: Transformers achieve strong performance, but their internal computations remain opaque. We view each Transformer layer as a dynamic graph whose nodes are token representations and per-head attention outputs, with Multi-Head Attention (ATT) and MLP as module boundaries. On this graph we use LIG (Layer-wise Integrated Gradients), which applies set-to-set Integrated Gradients (IG) at nonlinear module boundaries. Set-to-set IG applies IG to a map from a set of input token representations to a set of output representations, evaluating token-to-token contributions, which is not standard in prior IG applications. This extends IG from the usual scalar-objective setting to set-to-set maps via an L2 scalarization, and composes within-layer contributions in the spirit of Layer-wise Relevance Propagation (LRP), with IG completeness playing the role of LRP-style conservation at each boundary. We use LIG to analyze (i) the agreement between module-wise composition and layer-whole attribution under an L2 criterion, and (ii) within-layer information flow by tracing separated ATT and MLP contributions. On BERT-base and PTB, configurations that best preserved within-layer consistency used the target token's embedding as the ATT baseline and either the ATT output at a=0 or Zero as the MLP baseline. We therefore present LIG as a diagnostic XAI tool at module-boundary granularity, without model-specific retraining or per-operation interpreter design. Code is available at https://github.com/eightsuzuki/layer-wise-integrated-gradients.

</details>


### [657] [The Cost Geometry of Belief: finite-resource inference under noisy observation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21585)
*Laurent Caraffa*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a cost geometry on belief space using optimal transport in Wasserstein space, conformally reweighted by Fisher information. It establishes three key invariants: a wall (infinite distance to certainty), an honesty (eikonal cost proportional to Fisher information), and a rigidity (hyperbolic geometry with Gaussian as extremal). Results are scale-invariant and thermodynamic in nature.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To equip the space of beliefs with a meaningful cost geometry that captures both the cost of moving between beliefs (optimal transport) and the precision cost (Fisher information), distinct from the Fisher-Rao metric, motivated by the digital twin setting where a finite machine maintains probabilistic beliefs under noisy observations.

Method: Conformal modification of optimal transport Wasserstein cost by Fisher information; analysis of the resulting geometry on location-scale belief families; study of invariants under unit changes.

Result: Three geometric invariants: (1) a wall — infinite distance to certainty when cost dominates Fisher information; (2) honesty — eikonal cost yields geometries proportional to Fisher information; (3) rigidity — hyperbolic geometry with Gaussian as the most hyperbolic extremal, bounded by the Stam bound.

Conclusion: Only relative cost carries meaning; the geometry's absolute unit is fixed by thermodynamics. The framework reveals a geometric floor on precision cost diverging at certainty, with the value -1/4 as a geometric image of relative cost.

Abstract: We equip the space of beliefs with a cost geometry (what it costs to pass from one belief to another): optimal transport in Wasserstein space, reweighted conformally by Fisher information (the price of the precision at stake), distinct from the Fisher-Rao metric. In the setting we consider, a finite machine maintains a digital twin of a system; observing the territory through finite, noisy sensors, we model its coherent output as a belief: a probability density over states, the Bayes posterior. Certainty (the perfect twin) is denied twice, by observation and by physics, both read off the Fisher information. On the conformal class, essentially location-scale, three results emerge, all invariants of one change of cost unit. A wall: a well-posed inference rejects certainty to infinite distance as soon as the cost dominates the Fisher information (necessity conjectured beyond power laws). An honesty: an honest (eikonal) cost, each nat the same length everywhere, selects the geometries proportional to the Fisher information. A rigidity: these geometries are hyperbolic, and the Stam bound crowns the Gaussian, the most hyperbolic location-scale belief. Changing the unit dilates the geometry yet preserves the wall, the curvature ordering, and the extremality of the Gaussian: an absolute cost says nothing, only relative cost carries meaning, the value -1/4 being one of its images. The cost of reaching a given precision then has a geometric floor diverging at certainty. Thermodynamics fixes the cost unit and motivates this framework; the results are geometric, in nats.

</details>


### [658] [FAST: A Framework for Aligned Sampling and Training in Parallel Reinforcement Learning for Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21587)
*Bonan Wang,Letian Tao,Bin Shuai,Jiaxin Gao,Wenxin Zhao,Wei Xiong,Kehua Sheng,Bo Zhang,Yang Guan,Shengbo Eben Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FAST is a synchronous parallel simulation framework for closed-loop autonomous driving that uses Dynamic Parallel Sampling Alignment (DPSA) to eliminate the straggler effect by extending terminated episodes virtually, and Scaled Mask-Padding Optimization (SMPO) to maintain statistical unbiasedness, achieving ≥1.78× wall-clock speedup.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep reinforcement learning for closed-loop autonomous driving suffers from low sampling efficiency, and standard parallel sampling is hindered by the straggler effect—premature termination of a single environment forces synchronized batch re-initialization, causing poor sample utilization and high latency.

Method: FAST employs two key components: (1) Dynamic Parallel Sampling Alignment (DPSA), which maintains vectorization synchronization by virtually continuing terminated episodes instead of resetting immediately, and triggers global truncation dynamically based on the termination rate across parallel clips; (2) Scaled Mask-Padding Optimization (SMPO), which uses validity masking and adaptive loss normalization to eliminate bias from auxiliary padding data, preserving theoretical consistency.

Result: FAST achieves at least a 1.78× wall-clock speedup over the single-clip baseline while preserving statistical unbiasedness, as demonstrated through empirical evaluations.

Conclusion: FAST effectively eliminates the straggler effect bottleneck in parallel sampling for closed-loop autonomous driving, significantly improving sampling efficiency without sacrificing data diversity or statistical correctness.

Abstract: Deep reinforcement learning is pivotal for closed-loop autonomous driving yet remains constrained by severe bottlenecks in sampling efficiency. Standard parallel sampling mitigates this but suffers from the straggler effect, where the premature termination of a single environment necessitates a synchronized batch re-initialization, leading to suboptimal sample utilization and prohibitive re-initialization latency. To address this, we propose FAST, a synchronous parallel framework tailored for closed-loop simulation. Specifically, FAST employs Dynamic Parallel Sampling Alignment (DPSA) to maintain vectorization synchronization by extending terminated episodes via virtual continuation, thereby decoupling the sampling loop from individual terminations. By dynamically triggering global truncation based on the termination rate of parallel clips, FAST effectively eliminates the bottleneck of premature resets without sacrificing data diversity. Furthermore, to strictly preserve theoretical consistency, we incorporate a Scaled Mask-Padding Optimization (SMPO) that leverages validity masking and adaptive loss normalization to nullify the bias from auxiliary padding data. Empirical evaluations demonstrate that FAST achieves at least a 1.78 times wall-clock speedup over the single-clip baseline while preserving statistical unbiasedness.

</details>


### [659] [Geometric and Information Compression of Representations in Deep Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21593)
*Linara Adilova,Henning Petzka,Asja Fischer,Bernhard C. Geiger*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper investigates the relationship between mutual information (MI) and geometric compression in deep neural network representations, finding that low MI does not reliably correspond to geometric compression, and that the relationship is negative, nonlinear, and can reverse depending on training setup. Generalization may act as a confounder rather than a direct consequence of either.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand whether low mutual information between inputs and representations necessarily implies geometric compression of latent spaces and vice versa, as this relationship is poorly understood despite both being used to characterize representations.

Method: The authors used class-wise clustering as a measure of geometric compression and theoretically sound MI estimation in conditional entropy bottleneck (CEB) networks and continuous dropout networks, evaluating the interplay under controlled noise injection schemes on classification tasks.

Result: Low MI does not reliably correspond to geometric compression; the relationship is negative and nonlinear, and can reverse when varying training setup. Generalization appears to be a potential confounder rather than a direct consequence of either MI or geometric compression.

Conclusion: The connection between information-theoretic and geometric properties of representations is more nuanced than commonly assumed, and generalization may confound this relationship.

Abstract: Deep neural networks transform input data into latent representations that support a wide range of downstream tasks. These representations can be characterized along information-theoretic and geometric dimensions, but their relationship remains poorly understood. A central open question is whether low mutual information (MI) between inputs and representations necessarily implies geometrically compressed latent spaces and vice versa. We investigate this question using class-wise clustering as a measure of geometric compression and theoretically sound MI estimation in conditional entropy bottleneck (CEB) networks and continuous dropout networks. We evaluate the interplay between MI, geometric compression, and generalization on classification tasks under controlled noise injection schemes. Our findings show that low MI does not reliably correspond to geometric compression, and that the connection between the two is more nuanced than often assumed. Indeed, our experiments reveal a negative and nonlinear relationship that can reverse when varying training setup. Our results put forward a hypothesis that generalization acts as a potential confounder in this connection rather than being their direct consequence.

</details>


### [660] [Learning to Place Guards by Reinforcement: A Geo-Free Neural Policy for the Vertex-Guard Art Gallery Problem](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21604)
*Domagoj Ševerdija,Jurica Maltar,Nathan Chappel,Domagoj Matijević*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A reinforcement-trained NCO policy for the Art Gallery Problem learns sufficient geometric knowledge to solve the task, but its decoder fails to fully exploit this internalized representation. Probing the frozen encoder with a small classifier closes most of the feasibility gap, reducing under-covered polygons by ~10x at the cost of more guards.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand what a neural combinatorial optimization policy actually learns internally, versus what its decoder expresses, specifically investigating whether failures are due to missing knowledge or decoder miscalibration.

Method: Train a pointer-network policy with coverage-aware RL on the vertex-guard Art Gallery Problem under geo-free inference (no visibility/geometry at test time). Then freeze the encoder and probe its embeddings with a small single-shot classifier, still geo-free, to assess what the encoder has internalized.

Result: The classifier closes most of the feasibility gap, in and out of distribution and at up to ~5x the training range, cutting under-covered polygons by ~10x at an explicitly reported cost in guard count.

Conclusion: The RL-trained encoder already encodes the geometry required for feasibility; residual failures stem from decoder calibration, not missing knowledge. Probing frozen encoders is a practical method to inspect what neural combinatorial solvers have learned.

Abstract: Neural combinatorial optimization (NCO) has shown that policies trained by reinforcement can construct strong solutions to NP-hard problems directly from raw instances. What such a policy actually learns, as opposed to what its decoder expresses, remains much less clear. We study this distinction on the vertex-guard Art Gallery Problem, the NP-hard task of choosing polygon vertices from which to observe an entire region. A pointer-network policy is trained from a coverage-aware reward over its own rollouts under the constraint we call geo-free inference: at test time it sees only vertex coordinates, with no visibility computation and no geometric oracle. The policy places guards economically but leaves a tail of under-covered polygons that widens far beyond the training range. To locate the cause, we freeze the trained encoder and read its embeddings with a small single-shot classifier, still geo-free at inference. The classifier closes most of the feasibility gap, in and out of distribution and at up to roughly five times the training range, cutting under-covered polygons by about an order of magnitude at an explicitly reported cost in guard count. We read this as evidence that the reinforcement-trained representation already encodes the geometry required for feasibility, and that residual failures reflect decoder calibration rather than missing knowledge. Probing a frozen encoder thus offers a practical way to ask what a neural combinatorial solver has internalized.

</details>


### [661] [The Two-Hump Problem: Bridging the Difficulty Gap in Mathematical Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21611)
*Lucas Fagan,Michele Tarquini,Ali Shehper,Maksymilian Manko,Angus Gruen,Coco Huang,Giorgi Butbaia,Davide Passaro,Sergei Gukov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper identifies a "Two-Hump" distribution barrier in Reinforcement Learning for the Andrews-Curtis conjecture, addresses it through data generation and algorithmic enhancements (supermoves, Transformers), and releases large-scale benchmark datasets (AC-19 and AC-1M).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mathematical search problems (e.g., the AC conjecture) are challenging for RL due to vast search spaces and sparse rewards; the work identifies a "Two-Hump" distribution where instances are either trivially solvable or impossible, lacking the intermediate hard-but-solvable cases needed for effective learning.

Method: Novel data generation techniques to populate the difficulty gap, algorithmic enhancements including supermoves, and Transformer-based architectures.

Result: Substantial performance improvements over previous baselines; release of AC-19 (125,192 instances) and AC-1M (1,136,154 instances) benchmark datasets.

Conclusion: The proposed data generation and algorithmic improvements effectively overcome the structural "Two-Hump" barrier in the AC conjecture landscape, achieving significant performance gains and providing the first large-scale public benchmark datasets for this problem.

Abstract: Mathematical search problems present a unique challenge for Reinforcement Learning (RL) due to vast search spaces and sparse rewards. In previous works, the Andrews-Curtis (AC) conjecture was established as an illustrative example of such problems. In this work, we identify a critical structural barrier in the AC landscape: a "Two-Hump" distribution, where problem instances are either trivially solvable or effectively impossible, with a scarcity of intermediate "hard-but-solvable" instances required for effective learning. We tackle this challenge through two primary avenues: novel data generation techniques to populate the difficulty gap, and significant algorithmic enhancements including the introduction of supermoves and Transformer-based architectures. We demonstrate substantial performance improvements over previous baselines, and release new comprehensive benchmark datasets including AC-19 (125,192 AC-trivial presentations of varying difficulty with length at most 19) and AC-1M (1,136,154 hard AC-trivial presentations of length at most 30), the first large-scale, publicly available datasets of this kind.

</details>


### [662] [HERALD: High-Throughput Block Diffusion LLM Serving via CPU-GPU Cooperative KV Cache Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21633)
*Omin Kwon,Doyeon Kim,Jongseok Park,Seung Yul Lee,Ion Stoica,Jae W. Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HERALD is a KV cache offloading system for block diffusion LLMs that reduces memory pressure by selecting and reusing top-k KV entries across denoising steps, achieving near-lossless accuracy at 5-10% KV budget with up to 2.47x higher throughput over GPU-only inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Block dLLMs suffer from linear KV cache growth with context length, limiting throughput. Offloading to host DRAM is bottlenecked by limited PCIe bandwidth, and existing sparse selection methods are too expensive under offloading because they require exact attention over the full KV cache.

Method: HERALD exploits the observation that in block dLLMs, relevant KV entries remain consistent across denoising steps within a block. This enables: (1) amortizing selection overhead across the entire block (reducing compute by block size factor), and (2) overlapping selection with denoising computation.

Result: Across three block dLLMs and five long-context tasks, HERALD achieves near-lossless accuracy at 5-10% KV budget, up to 1.59x lower per-block latency, and up to 2.47x higher throughput compared to GPU-only inference, with speedups scaling with context length.

Conclusion: HERALD effectively resolves the memory-throughput trade-off in block dLLMs by leveraging the unique property of consistent KV relevance across denoising steps, enabling practical and efficient KV offloading for long-context inference.

Abstract: Diffusion LLMs (dLLMs) improve GPU utilization over autoregressive decoding by generating multiple tokens per forward pass, but their KV cache still grows linearly with context, limiting throughput at long contexts. KV cache offloading to host DRAM alleviates this memory pressure, but the limited PCIe bandwidth necessitates recalling only a sparse subset of KV entries. In block dLLMs, the relevant KV entries remain consistent across denoising steps within a block, enabling high-accuracy selection by identifying the top-k entries once and reusing them throughout all denoising steps. This property appears attractive for offloading as it amortizes the selection overhead across the entire block, but it requires exact attention over the full KV cache, which is too expensive under offloading. We present HERALD, a KV offloading system for block dLLMs that resolves this through two opportunities that reduce the required selection compute by a factor of the block size and enable selection to be overlapped with denoising. Across three block dLLMs and five long context tasks, HERALD achieves near-lossless accuracy at 5-10% KV budget and up to 1.59x lower per block latency and 2.47x higher throughput over GPU-only inference, with speedups growing with context length.

</details>


### [663] [A new classification method based on Minimum Spanning Trees](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21639)
*Julio González-Díaz,Beatriz Pateiro-López,Iria Rodríguez-Acevedo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a classification algorithm based on Minimum Spanning Trees (MSTs) for supervised learning, introduces a robust and computationally efficient version, and validates it through simulations and a real-world aircraft trajectory case study.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To extend the use of Minimum Spanning Trees from unsupervised learning (clustering) to supervised learning (classification), filling a gap in existing MST applications.

Method: A classification algorithm based on Minimum Spanning Trees is proposed, along with a robust and computationally more efficient variant of the method.

Result: Effectiveness validated through extensive simulation studies and a real-world application to aircraft trajectory classification.

Conclusion: Minimum Spanning Trees can be effectively adapted for supervised classification tasks, with the robust version offering improved performance and efficiency.

Abstract: Minimum Spanning Trees have been used in unsupervised learning, particularly in clustering tasks, due to their ability to recognize clusters by removing edges that are considered inconsistent in defining those clusters. This paper aims to study the use of Minimum Spanning Trees in supervised learning. Specifically, we propose a classification algorithm based on Minimum Spanning Trees. To improve its performance, we introduce a robust version of the method that is also computationally more efficient. We evaluate the effectiveness of our proposed method through an extensive simulation study. We also apply the proposed methodology to a real-world case study involving aircraft trajectories.

</details>


### [664] [When Is an LLM Worth It for Hyperparameter Optimization? A Budget-Matched Study on Tabular Data Finds the Warm-Start Is a Default Configuration, Not the Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21641)
*Carson Rodrigues,Oysturn Vas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LLM-based HPO advisors provide no measurable benefit over classical search methods when controlling for the default seed configuration; classical methods match or surpass LLM advisors within a small number of evaluations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To rigorously test the claim that LLMs can effectively warm-start hyperparameter optimization with strong prior knowledge under a budget-matched, multi-seed protocol.

Method: Compared LLM-OptFlow against random search, Optuna-TPE, GP-BO, and successive halving on 8 PMLB tabular benchmarks (40 task-seed units) with paired tests and bootstrap 95% CIs; controlled for the default seed configuration.

Result: 88.7% of the best CV performance comes from the fixed default seed (not LLM output); LLM proposals add only +0.40 pp CV accuracy and -0.01 pp test accuracy (p=0.92); classical search with the same seed ties the advisor by 5 evaluations and surpasses it by 12; without seed, classical search ties by 12 evaluations and beats by 40 (+0.6-0.8 pp, p≤1e-4).

Conclusion: On tabular HPO, the practical recommendation is to seed classical search with a sensible default; LLM advisors provide no measurable generalization benefit and are overtaken within a handful of evaluations.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have been proposed as hyperparameter-optimization (HPO) advisors that "warm-start" search from prior knowledge, proposing strong configurations in very few evaluations. We test that claim under a budget-matched, multi-seed protocol on eight PMLB tabular benchmarks, comparing an LLM advisor (LLM-OptFlow) against four classical baselines (random search, Optuna-TPE, Gaussian-process Bayesian optimization, and successive halving) over one shared search space, with paired tests and bootstrap 95% CIs across 8 x 5 = 40 (task, seed) units. The finding is cautionary. The advisor's strong first point is not an LLM output at all: like prior LLM-HPO systems the loop is seeded with a fixed default configuration, evaluated before any model call, which alone reaches 88.7% mean best-CV, identical to within 0.01 pp across all seven advisor models tested. The LLM's own proposals add only +0.40 pp of cross-validation accuracy over that seed and nothing on held-out test (LLM-Default = -0.01 pp, p = 0.92). When the same seed is granted to classical search, the apparent lead collapses: against seeded random search it leads by +0.20 pp at 2 evaluations, is tied by 5, and is behind by 12 (-0.37 pp). Without the seed, classical search ties the advisor by 12 evaluations and beats it by 40 (+0.6 to +0.8 pp, p <= 1e-4). Two LLM-specific behaviors survive: a single-task exploration failure (vehicle), and a rule-based confidence filter that removes ~33% of wasted compute without changing accuracy. The recommendation is deflationary: on tabular HPO, seed classical search with a sensible default; an LLM advisor adds no measurable generalization benefit and is overtaken within a handful of evaluations. We release the harness and a script that reproduces every statistic.

</details>


### [665] [A Framework for Directed Acyclic Hypergraph Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21668)
*Zhiyuan Dong,Carlos Mundo-Levano,Wei Qian,Daniel Lau,Gonzalo R. Arce*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper extends continuous DAG learning to Directed Acyclic Hypergraphs (DAHGs) by using a tensor-based framework with multiplicative interactions, acyclicity via t-product nilpotency, and differentiable optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing continuous optimization methods for DAG learning are limited to pairwise causal relationships represented by weighted adjacency matrices, failing to capture joint parental influences (e.g., interactions among multiple parents on a single child).

Method: Three components: (i) a generalized linear SEM with multiplicative interaction terms mapping non-zero weights to directed hyperedges; (ii) a weighted adjacency tensor with acyclicity enforced via nilpotency under the tensor t-product; (iii) a differentiable acyclicity constraint using Fourier decomposition of the t-product, reducing tensor nilpotency to slice-wise matrix nilpotency, optimized via augmented Lagrangian method.
