Result: The proposed framework enables differentiable least-squares learning of DAHGs, capturing higher-order joint causal effects that pairwise DAG models cannot.

Conclusion: The tensor-based framework successfully generalizes continuous DAG learning to hypergraph structures, allowing modeling of complex joint parental influences while maintaining differentiable acyclicity enforcement.

Abstract: Continuous optimization methods for learning Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs) operate on weighted adjacency matrices and are therefore limited to pairwise causal relationships. We propose a framework for learning Directed Acyclic Hypergraphs (DAHGs) from observational data, capturing joint parental influences that pairwise models cannot represent. Our approach rests on three components: (i) a generalized linear structural equation model (SEM) with multiplicative interaction terms whose non-zero weights correspond one-to-one with directed hyperedges; (ii) a weighted adjacency tensor representation whose acyclicity is characterized via nilpotency under the tensor t-product; and (iii) a differentiable acyclicity constraint derived through the Fourier decomposition of the t-product, which reduces tensor nilpotency to slice-wise matrix nilpotency and enables least-squares learning via the augmented Lagrangian method.

</details>


### [666] [Decodable but Not Faithful: Coupling Natural-Language Rationales to Programmatic Verifiers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21678)
*Vatsal Ananthula,Adarsh Kumarappan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Consistency training makes verifier information decodable from rationale representations but does not guarantee faithful generation—a decodability-faithfulness gap persists across LeanCheck, KataGo, and code settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Language models can generate plausible rationales for their predictions, but these explanations may not faithfully represent the model's internal reasoning. The paper aims to investigate whether making verifier information decodable from rationale representations (via consistency training) ensures faithful reasoning.

Method: Proposes verifier-coupled reasoning: inserts inline claims into reasoning traces and trains an auxiliary consistency head to predict programmatic verifier outputs from rationale-span hidden states. Evaluated on LeanCheck (formal theorem proving), KataGo (Go engine), and a code setting. Uses synthetic activation patching, FEVER, and per-claim analysis.

Result: Consistency training enables decodability (98.6% coupling in code setting, 81% win-rate accuracy in KataGo, perfect directional separation in LeanCheck), but generated explanations remain unfaithful (e.g., fluent prose describing unrelated algorithms). Synthetic activation patching confirms causal influence (73-89% vs. 31% baseline). FEVER shows evidence-only pooling sacrifices raw accuracy for sensitivity. Per-claim analysis shows consistency loss benefits fine-grained claims more than binary ones.

Conclusion: Consistency losses are effective diagnostics and representation-shaping tools, but not sufficient conditions for faithful reasoning. There is a fundamental gap between decodability and faithfulness.

Abstract: Language models can generate plausible rationales for their predictions, but these explanations may not faithfully represent the model's internal reasoning. We propose verifier-coupled reasoning, a framework that inserts inline claims into reasoning traces and trains an auxiliary consistency head to predict programmatic verifier outputs from rationale-span hidden states. The central finding is a gap between decodability and faithfulness: consistency training reliably makes verifier information decodable from rationale representations, but decodability does not guarantee faithful generation. In LeanCheck (formal theorem proving), rationale-only and proof-only pooling achieve perfect directional separation under counterfactual conflict. In KataGo (Go engine), commentary spans encode 10-way win-rate buckets at 81% accuracy. Yet in a code setting, the model achieves 98.6% coupling while its generated explanations remain unfaithful: fluent prose with correct structured claims, but describing unrelated algorithms; a controlled pretrained-vs-from-scratch comparison shows the gap is not capacity-driven. Synthetic activation patching confirms causal influence (73-89% vs. 31% baseline), FEVER reveals that evidence-only pooling isolates genuine evidence sensitivity at the cost of raw accuracy, and per-claim analysis shows that consistency loss disproportionately benefits fine-grained claims over binary ones. These results establish that consistency losses are effective diagnostics and representation-shaping tools, but not sufficient conditions for faithful reasoning.

</details>


### [667] [Expressivity Saturation: Reduced Affine Region Usage Under Increasing Task Complexity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21687)
*Xuan Qi,Yi Wei,Fanqi Yu,Manuel Lecha*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper studies the gap between theoretical region capacity and realized affine regions in piecewise-affine neural networks (ReLU/LeakyReLU). It provides a theoretical upper bound on affine pieces along line-segment probes and introduces the concept of "expressivity saturation"—where trained solutions use far fewer affine regions than worst-case capacity allows, especially under high task complexity. Training dynamics reveal consistent refinement of region partitions and decision boundaries.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand and quantify the discrepancy between the maximum possible number of affine regions a piecewise-affine neural network can represent (theoretical capacity) and the actual number of affine regions realized after training, which remains poorly understood.

Method: (1) A theoretical theorem bounding affine pieces along affine line-segment probes for MLPs with piecewise-affine activations, yielding a neuron-threshold lower bound. (2) Exact enumeration of affine regions in bounded 2D/higher-dimensional domains under controlled task complexity. (3) Empirical analysis of region usage, decision boundaries, and training dynamics.

Result: (i) A rigorous upper bound on affine pieces along line-segment probes expressed as product of layer-wise width and activation breakpoint factors. (ii) Discovery of "expressivity saturation": trained networks use markedly fewer affine regions than worst-case capacity, especially under high input-label complexity. (iii) Region-usage collapse often coincides with degraded decision boundaries in the most challenging regimes.

Conclusion: The gap between theoretical region capacity and realized regions is significant and systematic. Expressivity saturation suggests that complex tasks consume less representational budget in practice, providing a new perspective on neural network generalization and the effectiveness of overparameterization.

Abstract: Piecewise-affine neural networks (e.g., with ReLU or LeakyReLU activations) implement continuous piecewise-affine maps, and the number of affine regions provides a natural proxy for expressive capacity. However, the gap between theoretical region capacity and the affine regions realized after training remains insufficiently understood. We study this gap from two complementary perspectives. First, we give a rigorous, architecture-dependent theorem for affine line-segment probes: for multilayer perceptrons with piecewise-affine activations, the number of affine pieces realized along an affine line-segment probe is upper bounded by an explicit product of layer-wise width terms (and activation breakpoint factors). This yields a neuron-threshold lower bound for representing target functions with prescribed one-dimensional piece complexity, formalizing the minimal region budget required for complex signals. Second, we exactly enumerate affine regions realized within bounded 2D and higher-dimensional domains under controlled task complexity. Under fixed architectures and training protocols, increasing input--label complexity yields trained solutions with markedly fewer realized regions in the evaluation domain, even though worst-case architectural capacity is unchanged; we call this reduced region usage expressivity saturation. Moreover, in the most challenging regimes, 2D visualizations show that region-usage collapse often coincides with degraded decision boundaries. Finally, we visualize the training dynamics of affine-region partitions and decision boundaries, revealing a consistent refinement process during optimization.

</details>


### [668] [Embedding Linear Equality Constraints in Probabilistic Neural Networks for Dynamic Modelling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21728)
*Matthew Marsh,Benoit Chachuat,Antonio del Rio Chanona*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A probabilistic neural network framework that guarantees linear equality constraint satisfaction within a tolerance while capturing aleatoric uncertainty, showing improved accuracy, calibration, and constraint adherence on small data, and faster training on large data, validated on batch reactor mass balance case studies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Machine learning models for chemical process systems lack principled uncertainty quantification and mechanisms to enforce physical constraints.

Method: A probabilistic neural network framework that guarantees linear equality constraint satisfaction within a specified tolerance while capturing aleatoric (data) uncertainty.

Result: Improved predictive accuracy, uncertainty calibration, and constraint adherence on reduced data; competitive performance with significantly faster training times on large data regimes.

Conclusion: The proposed framework effectively combines probabilistic modeling with physics-constrained learning for chemical process systems, validated on two batch reactor case studies enforcing mass balances.

Abstract: Machine learning models are increasingly used to model chemical process systems, yet they often lack principled uncertainty quantification and mechanisms to enforce physical constraints. We propose a probabilistic neural network framework that guarantees satisfaction of linear equality constraints within a given tolerance, while capturing aleatoric uncertainty. Compared to state-of-the-art methods, our formulation demonstrates improved predictive accuracy, uncertainty calibration, and adherence to constraints on reduced data. It also demonstrates competitive performance, but with significantly faster training times when evaluated on large data regimes. We evaluated this on two batch reactor case studies, enforcing mass balances.

</details>


### [669] [AdaPrivate-TS: Private Thompson Sampling for Contextual Bandits with Privacy Amplification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21757)
*Mohammadreza Riyazat,Eranga Ukwatta*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AdaPrivate-TS is a differentially private contextual bandit algorithm using Thompson Sampling with batched zCDP composition, where privacy noise is interpreted as structured posterior uncertainty. It achieves 93-99% of non-private performance at ε∈[0.5,5], with privacy cost O(√d log T/√ρ), and outperforms UCB baselines on synthetic and real-world datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To design a differentially private contextual bandit algorithm that treats privacy noise as structured uncertainty rather than pure corruption, enabling better exploration-exploitation trade-offs under strict privacy budgets.

Method: Combines Thompson Sampling with batched zCDP composition; adds Gaussian noise N(0,σ²I) to b yielding sampling covariance v²A⁻¹ + σ²A⁻²; uses parallel composition across batches and optionally Poisson subsampling for privacy amplification.

Result: AdaPrivate-TS achieves 93-99% of non-private performance at ε∈[0.5,5]; outperforms UCB by 0.5-3.7% (up to 18% at extreme ε); privacy amplification yields 2-5% additional gains at low ε; TS advantage over UCB grows to +11% under DP-SVD features.

Conclusion: AdaPrivate-TS effectively treats differential privacy noise as a structured uncertainty signal, achieving near-optimal performance under event-level privacy with theoretical guarantees and strong empirical results across multiple benchmarks.

Abstract: We present AdaPrivate-TS, a differentially private contextual bandit algorithm that combines Thompson Sampling with batched zCDP composition. Our key insight is that differential privacy noise inflates the posterior covariance in a structured way: adding Gaussian noise $N(0,σ^2 I)$ to $b$ yields sampling covariance $v^2 A^{-1} + σ^2 A^{-2}$, which Thompson Sampling interprets as increased uncertainty rather than pure corruption. Under event-level privacy (protecting individual interactions) with stochastic contexts, we prove that the privacy cost is only $O(\sqrt{d}\,\log T/\sqrtρ)$, logarithmic in $T$, because parallel composition amortizes noise across batches. Additionally, we explore privacy amplification via Poisson subsampling, which can reduce effective noise at stringent privacy budgets. Experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate: (1) AdaPrivate-TS achieves 93-99% of non-private performance at $\varepsilon \in [0.5, 5]$, outperforming UCB by 0.5-3.7% and up to 18% with tuned adaptive exploration at extreme $\varepsilon$; (2) privacy amplification provides additional 2-5% gains at low $\varepsilon$; (3) on MovieLens and Jester, AdaPrivate-TS achieves the best overall performance among event-level baselines, dominating at $\varepsilon \geq 2$; (4) under DP-SVD private features, TS's advantage over UCB grows to +11%, confirming noise-as-uncertainty is not limited to reward privacy. We provide rigorous proofs for privacy guarantees under interactive zCDP composition and comprehensive evaluation including convergence curves, 12-seed CIs, and DP-SVD feature ablation.

</details>


### [670] [Decision-Focused Learning: When and Why Traditional Prediction Models Fail](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21773)
*Mo Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This tutorial reviews decision-focused learning (DFL) as an alternative to the predict-then-optimize paradigm, focusing on stochastic linear programming. It explains why traditional statistical learning tools (e.g., uncertainty-driven data collection, Wasserstein distance) are unsuitable for DFL and must be rethought, and highlights key distinctions and insights for developing new decision-focused methodologies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improved predictive accuracy does not generally translate into better decision quality in the predict-then-optimize paradigm, motivating the need for decision-focused learning that directly optimizes for downstream decision performance.

Method: The paper provides a tutorial-style review of recent DFL developments, focusing on stochastic linear programming as the downstream problem, and critically examines why traditional statistical learning tools (e.g., uncertainty-driven data collection, Wasserstein distance) are inappropriate for DFL.

Result: Identifies that common tools in traditional statistical learning—such as data collection based purely on predictive uncertainty and distributional distance measures like Wasserstein distance—are not directly suited to decision-focused settings and must be fundamentally rethought.

Conclusion: DFL possesses properties that distinguish it from conventional predictive modeling, and these insights can guide the development of new decision-focused tools.

Abstract: Plugging predictions of unknown parameters into downstream optimization problems, often referred to as the ``predict-then-optimize'' paradigm, has long been a standard approach in decision-making under uncertainty. However, improved predictive accuracy does not, in general, translate into improved decision quality. This disconnect has motivated growing interest in decision-focused learning (DFL) within the operations research community. This tutorial reviews recent developments in DFL and highlights key methodological insights, with a particular focus on stochastic linear programming as the downstream decision-making problem. We discuss why several widely used tools in traditional statistical learning are not directly suited to decision-focused settings and must be rethought, including (i) data collection strategies driven purely by predictive uncertainty and (ii) distributional distance measures such as the Wasserstein distance. We summarize properties of DFL that distinguish it from conventional predictive modeling and provide insights into the development of new decision-focused tools.

</details>


### [671] [Beyond the Next Step: Variable-Length Latent World Models for Long-Horizon Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21775)
*Tianqi Du,Qi Zhang,Yifei Wang,Yisen Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: VLWMs extend latent world models to predict future states conditioned on variable-length action sequences, using curriculum training to progressively expand prediction horizons, achieving 13% average improvement over SOTA on long-horizon control tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing JEPA-style latent world models rely on one-step prediction and recursive rollout for long-horizon planning, causing compounding errors and a mismatch between training objectives and downstream planning tasks.

Method: VLWMs learn to predict future latent states conditioned on action sequences of variable lengths; employs a curriculum training strategy that progressively expands the action horizon; designs planning methods tailored to exploit variable-length predictive capabilities at test time.

Result: Achieves 13% average improvement over the state-of-the-art LeWM across different datasets, with especially large gains on tasks requiring extended planning.

Conclusion: VLWM provides a simple yet effective paradigm for improving long-horizon prediction and planning in latent world models.

Abstract: Recently, world models have emerged as a promising paradigm for building intelligent agents by learning predictive models that estimate future environment states conditioned on observations and actions. In particular, JEPA-style latent world models provide an efficient alternative to pixel space prediction by learning action-conditioned dynamics in compact representation spaces. However, existing latent world models typically rely on one-step prediction and must be recursively rolled out for long-horizon planning, which leads to compounding errors and a mismatch between training objectives and downstream planning tasks. To address this limitation, we propose Variable-length Latent World Models (VLWMs), a framework that learns to predict future latent states conditioned on action sequences of variable lengths. Instead of training only on one-step transitions, VLWMs directly model temporally extended dynamics, allowing the same predictor to evaluate action plans over different horizons. We further introduce a curriculum training strategy that progressively expands the action horizon, stabilizing optimization from short-range dynamics to long-range prediction. At test time, we design planning methods tailored to VLWMs to better exploit their variable-length predictive capabilities. Experiments on long-horizon control tasks show that VLWMs significantly improve latent space world models, achieving 13\% average improvement over the state-of-the-art LeWM across different datasets, with especially large gains on tasks requiring extended planning. These results suggest that VLWM provides a simple yet effective paradigm for improving long-horizon prediction and planning in latent world models.

</details>


### [672] [A Causal DAG Prior for Synthetic Time-Series Classification Datasets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21776)
*Franco Martino O'Rourke,Ana Trisovic,Dimitris Bertsimas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a causal prior for generating synthetic multivariate time-series classification datasets with cross-modal temporal structure, enabling Prior-data Fitted Networks (like TabPFN) to be finetuned for time-series classification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior-data Fitted Networks learn the posterior predictive induced by their training prior, but extending this paradigm to multivariate time-series classification requires a synthetic generator that produces complete labeled datasets with temporal structure, which existing synthetic priors do not address.

Method: The authors introduce a causal prior that synthesizes each dataset from a randomly sampled DAG over typed nodes across two modalities (tabular attributes and time series), natively producing multivariate, multi-class TSC datasets with cross-modal causal structure across channels, timesteps and labels. They finetune TabPFN v2.5 with minimal adaptations and evaluate on 75 UCR/UEA datasets.

Result: Finetuning on the proposed generator significantly outperforms both the unmodified upstream model and a tabular-only ablation of the same prior (Wilcoxon signed-rank p=3.0×10⁻⁸ on ROC-AUC), isolating the contribution of the cross-modal temporal structure.

Conclusion: The cross-modal temporal structure in the synthetic prior is crucial for strong performance on multivariate time-series classification tasks, as demonstrated by the significant improvement over tabular-only ablation.

Abstract: A Prior-data fitted Network learns the posterior predictive induced by its training prior; bringing this paradigm to multivariate time-series classification therefore calls for a synthetic generator that produces complete labelled datasets with temporal structure. We introduce a causal prior that synthesizes each dataset from a randomly sampled DAG over typed nodes across two modalities (tabular attributes and time series), natively producing multivariate, multi-class TSC datasets with cross-modal causal structure across channels, timesteps and labels, a regime not addressed by existing synthetic priors. To validate the prior, we finetune TabPFN v2.5 with minimal adaptations and evaluate on 75 UCR/UEA datasets within TabPFN's operating regime. Finetuning on our generator significantly outperforms both the unmodified upstream model and a tabular-only ablation of the same prior (Wilcoxon signed-rank $p=3.0\times 10^{-8}$ on ROC-AUC), isolating the contribution of the cross-modal temporal structure.

</details>


### [673] [RocketPFN: Accurate Time Series Classification via In-Context Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21786)
*Franco Martino O'Rourke,Ana Trisovic,Dimitris Bertsimas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RocketPFN is a training-free pipeline for time series classification that combines Rocket feature extraction with TabPFN in-context classification, matching the top published method (HC2) on 92 UCR datasets (mean accuracy 0.900) with no training and 30s median inference time, while significantly outperforming TSC foundation models like MOMENT and Mantis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a training-free time series classification pipeline that can serve as a strong zero-shot reference point for evaluating TSC foundation models, and to demonstrate that simple feature extraction + in-context learning can match or exceed state-of-the-art ensemble methods and pretrained foundation models.

Method: Combines Rocket (random convolutional feature extraction) with TabPFN v2.5 (pretrained tabular foundation model for in-context classification) in a two-stage pipeline requiring no training on target data.

Result: Matches HC2 (mean accuracy 0.900, Wilcoxon p=0.50) on 92 UCR datasets with 30s median inference time; significantly outperforms MOMENT, Mantis, and MantisV2 (p<0.001) even when those encoders were pretrained on UCR training samples.

Conclusion: RocketPFN is a simple, training-free, and highly competitive zero-shot pipeline that should be used as a reference point when evaluating TSC foundation models, as it outperforms recent foundation models despite using no learned parameters and fewer features.

Abstract: We introduce RocketPFN, a training-free pipeline for time series classification that combines random convolutional feature extraction (Rocket) with in-context classification via a pretrained tabular foundation model (TabPFN v2.5). On 92 UCR datasets (30-resample protocol), RocketPFN matches HC2, the strongest published method on the archive, in mean accuracy (both 0.900, Wilcoxon p=0.50), with no training on the target data and a median inference time of 30 seconds per fold. It also significantly outperforms every individual classifier in the HC2 ensemble. On UEA (20 datasets) the difference is likewise not statistically significant. A separate comparison concerns TSC foundation models: when paired with the same downstream classifier, MOMENT, Mantis, and MantisV2 are all significantly outperformed by RocketPFN using fewer extracted features and no learned parameters (p<0.001 in each case). This holds even when the encoders were pretrained on corpora that include the UCR training samples. We propose this two-stage pipeline as a reference point for evaluating zero-shot TSC foundation models.

</details>


### [674] [What Do Lorentz-Equivariant Jet Taggers Learn?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21790)
*Jay Agarwal,Siddharth Khare,Dhruv Kumar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper investigates what Lorentz-equivariant jet taggers learn internally by analyzing five models (L-GATr, L-GATr-slim, LLoCa-T) through equivariance tests, linear probes, and grade ablations. Key findings: linear probes show equivariant models suppress frame-dependent pseudorapidity to zero while encoding jet mass and N-subjettiness strongly; grade ablations reveal bivector channels are negligible for top-quark tagging while vector-like channels are dominant but seed variable, indicating the network exploits multiple representational pathways.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand what physical features and algebraic grade structures carry discriminative information in Lorentz-equivariant jet taggers, which may inform future development of such models.

Method: Equivariance tests, linear probes, and grade ablations applied to five Lorentz-equivariant models including L-GATr, L-GATr-slim, and LLoCa-T.

Result: (1) Linear probes: equivariant models suppress frame-dependent pseudorapidity to zero while strongly encoding jet mass and N-subjettiness. (2) Grade ablations on L-GATr: bivector channels are negligible for top-quark tagging; vector-like channels are dominant but seed variable, indicating the network exploits multiple representational pathways.

Conclusion: The results characterize which physical features and algebraic grade structures carry discriminative information in equivariant taggers, providing insights for future model development.

Abstract: We study what Lorentz-equivariant jet taggers learn internally, using equivariance tests, linear probes and grade ablations across five models including L-GATr, L-GATr-slim and LLoCa-T. Linear probes show that equivariant models suppress frame-dependent pseudorapidity to zero while encoding jet mass and N-subjettiness strongly. Grade ablations on L-GATr reveal that bivector channels are negligible for top-quark tagging while vector-like channels are dominant but seed variable, consistent with the network exploiting multiple representational pathways. These results characterize which physical features and algebraic grade structures carry discriminative information in equivariant taggers and may inform future development of such models.

</details>


### [675] [Discretizing Reward Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21795)
*Vijay Viswanathan,Shiqi Wang,Devamanyu Hazarika,Chirag Nagpal,Tongshuang Wu,Graham Neubig,Yuning Mao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Reward models in RL are often oversensitive, assigning different scores to equally good responses. The paper proposes evaluating them via "discriminative ability" and "specificity," and introduces a training-free Monte Carlo dropout method to discretize rewards, reducing oversensitivity and reward hacking while improving policy quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The role of reward models in shaping reinforcement learning is poorly understood. While reward models promise fine-grained continuous scoring, their oversensitivity (assigning different scores to equally good responses) is a serious weakness that can lead to bad policies.

Method: The authors theoretically analyze oversensitivity in reward models, propose novel evaluation metrics (discriminative ability and specificity), and introduce a training-free Monte Carlo dropout algorithm to discretize neural reward models into clusters.

Result: Seemingly perfect reward models can be highly oversensitive; discretizing rewards reduces oversensitivity with minimal loss in discriminative ability; in both controlled and natural RL settings, discretized rewards lead to less reward hacking and better policies.

Conclusion: Existing reward model accuracy metrics are insufficient; evaluating specificity alongside discriminative ability is critical, and reward discretization via Monte Carlo dropout is an effective, training-free solution to mitigate oversensitivity in RL.

Abstract: Despite their widespread use, the role of reward models in shaping reinforcement learning is poorly understood. Reward models offer a tempting promise: they automatically estimate response quality in the absence of verifiers or human judges. Unlike "verifiable rewards" which typically produce binary scores, reward models typically produce continuous scores, allowing them to be sensitive to fine-grained differences in responses. However, we show this apparent strength is a serious weakness: many popular reward models are oversensitive, assigning different scores to equally good responses. Theoretically, we show that seemingly perfect reward models can be highly oversensitive; empirically, this oversensitivity can lead to bad policies. In place of existing notions of "reward model accuracy," we propose evaluating reward models using distinct measures of "discriminative ability" and "specificity" (the complement of oversensitivity). As a solution, we describe a training-free algorithm that uses Monte Carlo dropout on any neural reward model to produce discrete reward clusters. Theoretically, we prove there exist discretizations that reduce oversensitivity at minimal expense of discriminative ability; empirically we show, in both controlled and natural RL settings, that discretizing rewards leads to less reward hacking and better policies than training on the original rewards.

</details>


### [676] [Causal Variational Deep Embedding: A Family of Interventional Generators for Confounded Images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21806)
*Jingyuan Chen,Kangrui Ruan,Junzhe Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CauVaDE is a causal generative model that handles unobserved confounders by using a discrete latent cluster variable (canonical confounder) to represent a feasible region of interventional distributions, enabling diverse and high-quality counterfactual image generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep generative models inherit spurious associations from training data due to unobserved confounders that affect both controllable attributes and response attributes. Existing causal approaches impose overly strong structural assumptions to identify a single interventional distribution, which are rarely warranted in image domains. The goal is to handle causal ambiguity and produce a feasible set of interventional distributions consistent with observational data.

Method: CauVaDE builds on a canonical augmented SCM where the unobserved confounder is collapsed into a discrete latent cluster with bounded support, while continuous variations are absorbed into independent noises. The authors prove density of this canonical class in Wasserstein distance. It is instantiated as a mixture VAE where the cluster variable serves as the canonical confounder, with an entropy regularizer (weight γ) on the cluster posterior to trace candidate causal effects across the feasible region.

Result: On image benchmarks, CauVaDE produces diverse interventional samples and improves FID scores compared to an unconfounded reference model.

Conclusion: The canonical augmented SCM with discrete confounder provides a theoretically grounded, practical approach to generating diverse causal interventions from observational data without requiring strong structural assumptions, achieving both diversity and fidelity in image generation.

Abstract: Deep generative models reproduce the observational distribution of their training data, inheriting any spurious associations it contains. A common source is an unobserved confounder that shapes both an attribute the user wants to control at sampling time and an attribute expected to vary in response. Existing causal generative approaches resolve the resulting ambiguity by imposing structural assumptions strong enough to single out one interventional distribution; in image domains, such assumptions are rarely warranted, and the data is generally consistent with a set of distinct causal mechanisms -- a feasible region of interventional distributions. We propose CauVaDE (Causal Variational Deep Embedding), built on a canonical augmented SCM in which the unobserved confounder collapses, without loss of generality, into a discrete latent cluster of bounded support while continuous variation is absorbed into independent noises. We prove that this canonical class is dense, in both observational and interventional Wasserstein distance, in the class of augmented SCMs compatible with a given causal diagram, and instantiate it as a mixture variational autoencoder whose cluster variable plays the role of the canonical confounder. An entropy regularizer with weight $γ$ on the cluster posterior then traces a family of candidate causal effects that fit the observational data to comparable likelihood while spanning the feasible region. Experiments on image data benchmarks show that CauVaDE produces diverse interventional samples and improves FID against an unconfounded reference.

</details>


### [677] [Causal Gaussian Processes for Robust Treatment Effect Evaluation with Unobserved Confounding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21809)
*Junzhe Zhang,Jingyuan Chen,Elias Bareinboim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces Causal Gaussian Process (CGP) models that can evaluate causal effects from confounded observational data over continuous domains, requiring only temporal ordering between treatment and outcome, by leveraging a universal discretization of exogenous domains with finite latent states.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Confounding bias makes causal effect evaluation from observational data challenging, as effects are often not identifiable. Existing confounding-robust methods require detailed prior knowledge or only work for discrete treatments/outcomes, leaving a gap for continuous-domain causal evaluation with minimal assumptions.

Method: The authors propose a universal discretization of exogenous domains that approximates observational and interventional distributions of any causal model with arbitrary accuracy using finite latent states. Based on this universal approximation property, they develop Causal Gaussian Process (CGP) models to approximate both observational and interventional distributions under confounding.

Result: The proposed CGP models can effectively approximate observational and interventional distributions of any causal model with confounded observations, enabling causal effect evaluation over continuous domains.

Conclusion: Causal effect evaluation over continuous domains from confounded data is achievable with only temporal ordering information, using the proposed discretization framework and CGP models.

Abstract: The presence of confounding bias poses a key challenge in policy evaluation, as the target causal effects of actions are not identifiable (i.e., underdetermined) from observational data. On the other hand, existing confounding-robust evaluation strategies require detailed prior knowledge about the environment or apply only to discrete treatments and outcomes. This paper investigates causal effect evaluation over the continuous domain from confounded observations, while requiring only basic temporal ordering between the treatment and the outcome. We introduce a universal discretization of the exogenous domains that approximates the observational and interventional distributions of any causal model with arbitrary accuracy using a finite number of latent states. Building on this newfound universal approximation property, we develop a novel family of Causal Gaussian process (CGP) models that effectively approximate the observational and interventional distributions of any causal model with confounded observations.

</details>


### [678] [Local Causal Attribution of Chain-of-Thought Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21821)
*Dennis Wei,Yannis Belkhiter,Erik Miehling,Radu Marinescu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AttriCoT is a black-box method that constructs a structural causal model on chain-of-thought trace units to perform attribution using O(U) forward passes, producing more faithful attributions than alternatives across 5 datasets and 4 reasoning models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understanding the causal structure of a language model's thought process is important for both transparency and safety.

Method: Constructs a structural causal model on individual units (components) of a chain-of-thought trace, relating each unit to the log probability of generating subsequent output units. AttriCoT performs attribution by estimating importance parameters using O(U) forward passes.

Result: AttriCoT produces attributions more faithful to model behavior than alternative methods, evaluated via perturbation curves across 5 datasets and 4 reasoning models. Attribution results reveal notable differences in thought structure between models and domains.

Conclusion: AttriCoT provides a faithful black-box attribution method for analyzing causal structure in chain-of-thought reasoning, revealing model and domain-specific differences in thought structure.

Abstract: Understanding the causal structure of a language model's thought process is a problem of significant importance for both transparency and safety. In this work, we take a local approach toward this goal by analyzing the causal relationships among individual components, termed units, of a given, specific chain-of-thought trace. We construct a structural causal model on these units and relate each unit to the log probability of generating (subsequent) output units. Our algorithm, termed AttriCoT, is a black-box method that performs attribution by estimating importance parameters in the structural causal model using $O(U)$ forward passes through the model, where $U$ is the number of units. Evaluation of perturbation curves across 5 datasets and 4 reasoning models shows that AttriCoT produces attributions that are more faithful to the model's behavior than alternative methods. The attribution results also reveal notable differences in thought structure between models and domains.

</details>


### [679] [Mat-Pref: Verifiable-Reward Training Improves Compositional Reasoning in Inorganic Materials](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21830)
*Sarrah R. Mikhail Leung,Taehan Kim,Jeongbin Park*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Mat-Pref benchmark reveals frontier models (33-54%) struggle on compositional chemical reasoning; two-stage SFT+GRPO pipeline lifts Qwen3-8B to 65-72%, exceeding zero-shot Qwen3-235B by 20+ pp; GRPO reshapes answer distribution so correct answers become modal rather than merely reachable, formalized via distractor-permutation consistency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RLVR benchmarks do not decompose whether gains in scientific reasoning reflect structural transfer, property transfer, or memorization.

Method: (1) Mat-Pref benchmark: 10,837 ionic-substitution questions across 11 structure families with 3 evaluation splits (in-distribution, held-out families, cross-property transfer). (2) Two-stage pipeline: SFT + GRPO on Qwen3-8B. (3) Self-consistency sampling and logit lens analysis for mechanistic interpretation. (4) Distractor-permutation consistency metric.

Result: Zero-shot frontier models (70-671B) achieve only 33-54% across all splits. SFT+GRPO on Qwen3-8B reaches 65.2% in-distribution and 71.6% on held-out families, surpassing Qwen3-235B by 20+ pp. GRPO narrows the lenient-vs-strict scoring gap from 24.0 to 14.3 pp.

Conclusion: Scale alone does not resolve compositional chemical reasoning; GRPO's advantage comes from reshaping the output distribution to make correct answers modal rather than merely reachable, with measurable mechanistic signatures in answer crystallization at critical decision layers.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) has driven rapid progress in mathematical and code reasoning, but when extended to science, existing benchmarks do not decompose what generalizes: do gains reflect structural transfer, property transfer, or memorization? We introduce Mat-Pref, a benchmark of 10,837 ionic-substitution questions across 11 inorganic structure families, grounded in density functional theory calculations from the Materials Project, with three evaluation splits that isolate in-distribution performance, generalization to entirely held-out structure families, and cross-property transfer: applying band-gap reasoning to hosts seen during training only through formation-energy supervision. Four zero-shot frontier models (70-671B parameters) remain in the 33-54% range on every split, confirming that scale alone does not resolve the compositional chemical reasoning this task demands. A two-stage pipeline of supervised fine-tuning followed by Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) lifts Qwen3-8B to 65.2% in-distribution and 71.6% on held-out families, exceeding zero-shot Qwen3-235B by over 20 percentage points on both structural-generalization splits. Self-consistency sampling shows that the SFT policy can already produce correct answers but cannot reliably surface them as the modal response; GRPO reshapes the distribution so that correct answers become modal rather than merely reachable, and this sharper commitment is visible mechanistically: logit lens analysis reveals a ${\sim}$20pp advantage in answer crystallization at the critical decision layer. We formalize this observation as a distractor-permutation consistency metric under which GRPO narrows the gap between lenient scoring (at least one permutation correct) and strict scoring (all permutations correct) from 24.0 to 14.3 percentage points.

</details>


### [680] [UniRank: Unified Rank Allocation for Low-Rank LLM Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21847)
*Chao Han,Haozhe Hu,Fei Ma,Wei Zhang,Xiaoyu Shen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a global low-rank allocation method for LLM compression using sorting-and-truncation with dual scoring criteria (local singular energy ratio and global functional importance via cosine similarity), plus a rank-preserving fine-tuning strategy that outperforms uniform/heuristic baselines by up to 50% perplexity reduction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Low-rank decomposition is promising for LLM compression, but existing rank allocation methods suffer from either poor generalizability (manual rules) or excessive computational cost (learning-based approaches).

Method: (1) Formulates global low-rank allocation as a sorting-and-truncation pipeline. (2) Scores singular components via dual criteria: local singular energy ratio (intrinsic importance within the matrix) and global functional importance (input-output cosine similarity across the module). (3) Proposes rank-preserving fine-tuning that applies direct LoRA tuning on decomposed weights to avoid re-truncation information loss.

Result: In one-shot compression without fine-tuning, the method reduces perplexity by up to 50% compared to uniform and heuristic allocation baselines. Sustained performance gains are demonstrated across different decomposition schemes, model sizes, and architectures.

Conclusion: The dual-criteria scoring approach with rank-preserving fine-tuning offers an effective and generalizable solution for low-rank allocation in LLM compression.

Abstract: Low-rank decomposition serves as a promising compression paradigm for large language models, however, rank allocation remains challenging: manual rules lack generalizability, and learning-based approaches incur heavy computational overhead. To address these issues, we formulate global low-rank allocation as a sorting-and-truncation pipeline, and score each singular component via dual criteria: \textbf{Local} singular energy ratio that quantifies the intrinsic importance within the decomposed parameter matrix and \textbf{Global} functional importance (measured by input-output cosine similarity) that evaluates the functional significance of decomposed modules. We verify the strong correlation between high input-output cosine similarity and low effective rank through geometric interpretation and experimental validation. Furthermore, we propose rank-preserving fine-tuning, which performs direct LoRA tuning on decomposed weights and avoids extra information loss caused by re-truncation in conventional merging pipelines. Empirical results confirm that our method delivers sustained performance enhancements when combined with models featuring distinct decomposition schemes, model sizes and architectural designs, e.g. in one-shot compression without further fine-tuning, our method reduces perplexity by up to 50\% compared with uniform and heuristic allocation baselines. Code will be available at https://github.com/EIT-NLP/LLM-Pruning.

</details>


### [681] [WiSP: A Working-Set View of Mixture-of-Experts Serving on Extremely Low-Resource Hardware](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21868)
*Jiamu Zhang,Liang Wu,Mayank Darbari,Liangjie Hong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: WiSP is a routing-aware expert pager for MoE models that treats GPU VRAM as a working-set management problem between expert weights and KV cache, using MV-WSA allocation to dynamically balance memory for up to 1.95x decode throughput improvement over static offload.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing MoE serving on commodity GPUs relies on layer-level CPU offloading, which streams all experts across PCIe on every forward pass, losing the sparsity benefit of MoE. This creates a contention between routed expert weights and KV cache for limited VRAM.

Method: WiSP (Working-Set Paging) — a routing-aware expert pager that keeps only frequently reused experts resident on GPU; and MV-WSA (Marginal-Value Working-Set Allocation) — a policy that equalizes marginal latency benefit per byte between expert and KV cache memory pools, subject to a KV admission floor.

Result: Up to 1.95x decode throughput vs static offload at same memory budget; offline MV-WSA configurator stays within a few percent of per-workflow oracle while fixed splits are ~20% worse; online controller adds further 1.20x improvement.

Conclusion: The bottleneck in MoE serving is PCIe bandwidth, not routing prediction accuracy, shifting focus from prefetching to optimal memory allocation between experts and KV cache.

Abstract: Modern Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models place most of their parameters in expert layers, yet only a small fraction of those experts are used for any token. The unused weights must still be stored where the GPU can reach them. On commodity GPUs the common fix is layer-level CPU offloading, which keeps memory low but streams all of a layer's experts across PCIe on every forward pass, losing much of MoE's sparsity benefit. We cast low-resource MoE serving as a working-set management problem on the GPU: routed expert weights and the key-value (KV) cache are two streams of memory demand competing for limited VRAM. We realize this in WiSP (Working-Set Paging), a routing-aware expert pager that plugs into an unmodified serving engine with byte-identical outputs. Keeping resident only the experts a workload reuses, WiSP reaches up to 1.95x the decode throughput of static offload at the same memory budget when the model does not fit. We also find that prefetching experts from predicted routing helps little in single-stream decode: the bottleneck is PCIe bandwidth, not prediction accuracy. This shifts the question from prefetching to allocation: how should VRAM be split between experts and the KV cache? We answer with MV-WSA (Marginal-Value Working-Set Allocation), which equalizes marginal latency benefit per byte subject to a KV admission floor. MV-WSA runs either as an offline configurator or as an online controller that resizes both pools while serving. In real serving the offline configurator is the only policy we test that does well on both prefill and decode; in trace-driven simulation it stays within a few percent of a per-workflow oracle while fixed splits are about 20% worse. The online controller adds a further 1.20x without changing model outputs.

</details>


### [682] [Protein contacts are already in the attention: a single-forward-pass alternative to the Categorical Jacobian](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21876)
*Rome Thorstenson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The Categorical Jacobian (CJ) method for reading protein contacts from language models can be replaced by simply averaging the top-K contact-relevant attention heads, selected using few labeled proteins. This head readout matches or beats CJ with minimal labels, and both methods drop substantially (30-36 pp) when testing on leakage-clean data, indicating prior results were inflated by pretraining overlap.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work (Zhang et al., 2024) proposed the Categorical Jacobian (CJ) to extract protein contact predictions from a language model, but it requires ~19L forward passes. The authors hypothesized that the contact signal is concentrated in a small subset of attention heads, motivating a more efficient method requiring only a few labeled proteins and single forward pass.

Method: The authors select the top-K attention heads most relevant to contact prediction using as few as 10 labeled proteins, then average their attention maps to produce contacts in one forward pass. They compare this head readout against CJ on leakage-clean data (CAMEO split) and in-distribution settings, across multiple model sizes and architectures. They also introduce representation-CJ for architectures without masked-LM heads.

Result: The head readout beats CJ on leakage-clean data for every bidirectional model (e.g., +9 pp on ESM-2-650M, p<0.001). In-distribution it matches or beats CJ except for the smallest 8M model and a tie on ESM Cambrian. Both methods drop 30-36 percentage points from in-distribution to leakage-clean, implying substantial pretraining overlap inflation. Both methods fail on causal LMs (ProGen2).

Conclusion: The contact signal in bidirectional protein LMs is concentrated in a small subset of attention heads, enabling efficient extraction via head selection and averaging. Leakage from pretraining overlap significantly inflates prior contact prediction results. The failure on causal LMs suggests attention-encoded pair structure depends on bidirectional pretraining.

Abstract: The Categorical Jacobian (CJ) of Zhang et al. (2024) reads protein contacts from a language model by perturbing every residue with every alternative amino acid, about 19L forward passes. We show the signal it reconstructs is already concentrated in a small subset of attention heads: averaging the top-K contact-relevant heads, selected on as few as 10 labeled proteins, recovers contacts in one forward pass and beats CJ on leakage-clean data for every bidirectional model where CJ is defined, and matches or beats it in-distribution (the exceptions being the smallest 8M model and a statistical tie on ESM Cambrian). Ablations localize the gain to labeled head selection, not averaging: at a matched label budget the unweighted mean ties a supervised L1 logistic regression on the same heads, so the parameter-free mean is selection's minimal form, not the source of the advantage. Our primary test is leakage-clean: on a CAMEO split where neither selection nor evaluation touches data the models have plausibly memorized, the head readout beats CJ on ESM-2-650M by +9 pp (N=29, p<0.001), with the within-model margin reproducing across architectures on a wider pretraining-aware set. Both methods fall 30-36 percentage points from their in-distribution Zhang numbers to the leakage-clean numbers, consistent with substantial pretraining overlap inflating prior numbers (a CAMEO-vs-Zhang difficulty shift contributes too, so we read it as an upper bound on the leakage component). We additionally introduce representation-CJ, a hidden-state generalization of the Jacobian for architectures without a masked-LM head; show that the optimal K tracks how diffusely a model spreads its contact heads; and find that both methods lose the contact signal on both causal LMs we test (ProGen2), suggesting attention-encoded pair structure may depend on bidirectional pretraining.

</details>


### [683] [A Verifiable Search Is Not a Learnable Chain-of-Thought](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21884)
*Harsh Patel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Chain-of-thought fine-tuning fails to teach models to perform search-based reasoning (e.g., cryptarithm) even though models can execute the individual steps; the limitation persists across model sizes, fine-tuning methods, and prompting, and is only overcome by removing the search component and precomputing results into a recall/verification format.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper challenges the common assumption that any task solvable by a short program can be taught to a model via chain-of-thought fine-tuning, identifying an identifiable class of procedures where this assumption fails.

Method: Nine reasoning tasks from deterministic generators with public/hidden splits; reverse-engineered into Python solvers, rendered as chain-of-thought, distilled into LoRA over Nemotron (30B/3.5B active). Tested across 3B-671B backbones, multiple fine-tuning approaches (CoT, RL from verifiable rewards, self-training), and a controlled intervention (revealing cipher key).

Result: Forward-computable tasks (lookup/arithmetic, 8-bit boolean) transfer well (≥0.99, 0.68). Cryptarithm (backtracking search) fails at 0.01-0.07 across all designs, though the model performs arithmetic (97-100%) and ranks correct cipher in top eight (71%). Intervention: revealing cipher key raises accuracy from 0.03 to 0.57. Removing search by precomputing combinatorial core into a catalog achieves Private LB 0.92.

Conclusion: When a procedure's only solution requires search over information-free structure, no faithful forward chain-of-thought exists to imitate. What distills is memorization and verification, not search itself.

Abstract: It is tempting to assume any task solvable by a short program can be taught to a model as its chain-of-thought: write the steps out, fine-tune, and the model follows. This paper shows the assumption fails for an identifiable class of procedures. The testbed is nine reasoning tasks, each from a deterministic generator; public and hidden splits share generators, so held-out data proxies test accuracy. I reverse-engineer the generators into Python solvers, render them as chain-of-thought, and distill into a rank-<= 32 LoRA over a 30B (3.5B-active) Nemotron model. Forward-computable tasks install readily: lookup/arithmetic and an 8-bit boolean task transfer (>= 0.99 and 0.68). Cryptarithm does not: distilling its backtracking search holds at 0.01-0.07 across eleven chain-of-thought designs, RL from verifiable rewards, and self-training, even though a search solver answers 71% of instances. This is not a capability gap. The model does the arithmetic on 97-100% of lines and ranks the correct cipher in its top eight on 71%; it cannot carry the search forward as a left-to-right derivation. Fine-tuning learns the shape of a verifiable elimination step while its verdicts become unconditional templates, correct only 16-57% of the time ("verdict-as-token"). The ceiling holds across backbones from 3B to 671B and across fine-tuning and prompting; a controlled intervention isolates the cause: revealing the cipher key, which turns the derivation forward, lifts the same instances from 0.03 to 0.57. When a procedure's only solution is search over information-free structure, no faithful forward chain-of-thought exists to imitate. The task becomes learnable only by removing the search, precomputing its combinatorial core into a catalog and reducing the trace to recall plus verification; the 1st-place solution reaches Private LB 0.92 this way. What distills is memorization and verification, not search.

</details>


### [684] [Cohort-Anchored Foundation Models for Electronic Health Records: From Risk Scores to Auditable Peer Cohorts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21885)
*Kaiping Zheng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CAFM is a framework that elevates patient cohorts to first-class objects in foundation model learning, addressing limitations in interpretability, distribution shift, and clinician alignment through four stages: deviation-aware data curation, cohort-conditioned pretraining, multimodal cohort alignment, and clinician-in-the-loop refinement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing foundation models prioritize representation learning while treating patient comparison as an emergent property rather than primary clinical evidence, leading to limited interpretability, vulnerability to distribution shift, and weak alignment with clinician reasoning.

Method: CAFM proposes a four-stage framework: (1) deviation-aware data curation, (2) cohort-conditioned pretraining, (3) multimodal cohort alignment, and (4) clinician-in-the-loop refinement. It is compositional and can augment existing EHR foundation models without modifying underlying encoders.

Result: Demonstrated through four clinical case studies: acute kidney injury prediction, cardiovascular risk stratification from ECGs, optic neuropathy triage from orbital imaging, and electroretinogram-grounded report generation. Five empirically testable hypotheses are presented.

Conclusion: Explicitly anchoring foundation models to patient cohorts provides a principled path toward trustworthy clinical AI, though open challenges remain in data quality, irregular temporality, multimodal learning, distribution shift, and evaluation beyond predictive accuracy.

Abstract: Foundation models have achieved remarkable performance across medical question answering, imaging, and electronic health record (EHR) tasks, yet reliable clinical deployment remains challenging due to limited interpretability, vulnerability to distribution shift, and weak alignment with clinician reasoning. We argue that these limitations arise because existing approaches prioritize representation learning while treating patient comparison as an emergent property rather than a primary source of clinical evidence. To address this gap, we propose CAFM, a Cohort-Anchored Foundation Model framework that elevates patient cohorts to a first-class object throughout the learning pipeline. The framework consists of four stages: deviation-aware data curation, cohort-conditioned pretraining, multimodal cohort alignment, and clinician-in-the-loop refinement. Together, these stages improve data quality, organize representations around clinically meaningful cohort structure, preserve modality-specific relationships, and support auditable clinical decision-making. The framework is compositional and can augment existing EHR foundation models without modifying their underlying encoders. We illustrate CAFM through four clinical case studies spanning acute kidney injury prediction, cardiovascular risk stratification from electrocardiograms, optic neuropathy triage from orbital imaging, and electroretinogram-grounded report generation. We further present five empirically testable hypotheses and identify open challenges in data quality, irregular temporality, multimodal learning, distribution shift, and evaluation beyond predictive accuracy. We argue that explicitly anchoring foundation models to patient cohorts provides a principled path toward trustworthy clinical AI.

</details>


### [685] [Ranking-and-Selection with Multiple Correct Answers and Non-Answerable Estimates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21889)
*Qiaoqiao Wang,Wei You*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a unified framework (ENDS) for fixed-precision ranking-and-selection problems where answers may be non-unique or temporarily invalid, using answer-wise acceptance sets and generalized likelihood ratio stopping, with strong empirical performance across diverse pure-exploration tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address structured ranking-and-selection problems where the answer may be non-unique (e.g., multi-fidelity ranking-and-selection) or where noisy estimates may temporarily admit no valid answer (e.g., identifying a Condorcet winner from pairwise comparisons). Existing methods lack a unified theoretical and algorithmic framework for such settings.

Method: Proposes a unified framework with: (1) answer-wise acceptance sets, (2) restricted generalized likelihood ratio stopping, (3) answer-pitfall decomposition yielding a max-max-min characteristic value and common sampling principle. Introduces ENDS (Estimation, Nomination, pitfall Detection, and cost-aware information-directed Selection) procedure with explicit formulas for various problem instantiations.

Result: Extensive numerical experiments demonstrate strong performance across a broad range of pure-exploration problems, validating the effectiveness of the unified recipe.

Conclusion: Provides a practical framework and proof-of-concept algorithmic recipe for structured ranking-and-selection problems with non-unique or temporarily invalid answers.

Abstract: We study fixed-precision ranking-and-selection in structured settings where the answer may be non-unique and where noisy estimates may temporarily admit no valid answer at all. This phenomenon arises naturally in problems such as multi-fidelity ranking-and-selection and identifying a Condorcet winner from pairwise comparisons. To address this, we propose a unified framework based on answer-wise acceptance sets, restricted generalized likelihood ratio stopping, and an answer-pitfall decomposition that yields a max-max-min characteristic value and a common sampling principle. We introduce ENDS, a general procedure that combines estimation, nomination, pitfall detection, and cost-aware information-directed selection. We instantiate ENDS for various problems by deriving explicit formulas. Extensive numerical experiments show that this unified recipe performs well across a broad range of pure-exploration problems and offers a practical framework and proof-of-concept algorithmic recipe.

</details>


### [686] [AgroSense 2.0: Cross-Modal Transformer Fusion with Geospatial Raster Integration and Interpretable Multi-Task Learning for Precision Crop Recommendation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21892)
*Vishal Pandey,Rishav Tewari,Ruzina Haque Laskar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AgroSense~2.0 introduces a cross-modal Transformer fusion module integrating seven-band soil raster data with tabular nutrient features, and a multi-task objective combining soil classification and crop recommendation, achieving more principled fusion and interpretability via TreeSHAP analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior crop recommendation systems suffer from a modality gap where visual soil characterization and chemical nutrient profiling are treated independently, with fusion limited to late-stage feature concatenation.

Method: (1) Continental-scale geospatial integration of seven-band soil raster (Nitrogen, pH, SOC, Clay, Sand, Silt, Bulk Density) as 32x32 patches; (2) Cross-modal Transformer fusion where tabular features attend over image representations via multi-head attention; (3) Multi-task objective optimizing soil classification and crop recommendation jointly via a shared backbone; (4) TreeSHAP for interpretability.

Result: Humidity and rainfall are globally most influential; rainfall dominates rice, nitrogen/potassium dominate maize, humidity/nitrogen dominate coffee; crop-conditioned nutrient sensitivity profiles diverge meaningfully.

Conclusion: AgroSense~2.0 provides a more principled, interpretable, and geospatially grounded framework for precision agriculture, revealing both agronomically consistent patterns and dataset-specific divergences.

Abstract: Crop recommendation systems in precision agriculture have long suffered from a fundamental modality gap: visual soil characterization and chemical nutrient profiling are typically treated as independent inference problems, with fusion often reduced to late-stage feature concatenation. AgroSense~2.0 addresses this limitation through three architectural advances. First, we introduce continental-scale geospatial integration via a seven-band soil raster (\texttt{india\_soil\_7bands.tif}) spanning India, encoding Nitrogen, pH, SOC, Clay, Sand, Silt, and Bulk Density as $32\times32$ spatial patches, a modality entirely absent from prior work. Second, we replace naive feature concatenation with a cross-modal Transformer fusion module, where tabular nutrient features attend over image representations via multi-head attention, enabling richer inter-modal dependency modeling than shallow fusion. Third, we adopt a multi-task objective jointly optimizing soil classification and crop recommendation through a shared backbone, improving generalization via complementary cross-task signal. To enhance interpretability, we apply TreeSHAP to the tabular branch, revealing crop-conditioned nutrient sensitivity: humidity and rainfall emerge as the most influential features globally, while crop-specific profiles diverge meaningfully rainfall dominates rice, nitrogen and potassium dominate maize, and humidity and nitrogen dominate coffee. These explanations provide transparency into model decisions and surface both agronomically consistent patterns and dataset-specific divergences worth further study. Together, these contributions establish AgroSense~2.0 as a more principled, interpretable, and geospatially grounded framework for precision agriculture.

</details>


### [687] [Data Pruning: Redundant, Problematic, and Interdependent Samples](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21916)
*Leon Freese,Marthinus W. Theunissen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper investigates data pruning methods under noisy and noiseless conditions, finding that popular pruning techniques fail under significant label noise, and identifies three key factors affecting pruning success: dataset redundancy, problematic samples, and sample interdependence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep learning performance depends on both data quantity and quality. Data pruning aims to reduce dataset size while maintaining test performance by keeping only important samples. However, the effectiveness of pruning methods under noisy conditions is not well understood.

Method: The authors empirically investigate two popular data pruning methods under noisy and noiseless conditions, conducting experiments on benchmark classification datasets using neural network architectures. They compare pruning performance across data distributions and training protocols.

Result: Both data pruning methods fail under significant label noise. Three factors distinctly affect pruning success: redundancy in the dataset, presence of problematic samples, and interdependence between samples. These observations are consistent across data distributions and training protocols.

Conclusion: Current data pruning methods are unreliable under label noise, and practitioners must consider dataset redundancy, problematic samples, and sample interdependence when applying pruning in noisy settings.

Abstract: The performance of deep learning models is affected by not only data quantity but also data quality. Data pruning is a process by which practitioners can reduce the size of a dataset by only keeping the most important training data points, thereby achieving similar test set performance. We empirically investigate two popular data pruning methods under noisy and noiseless conditions and show that these methods fail in the presence of significant label noise. We highlight that the success of data pruning is distinctly affected by three factors: redundancy in the dataset, the presence of problematic samples, and interdependence between samples. We perform a detailed investigation on commonly used benchmark classification datasets and neural network architectures. We find that our observations are consistent across data distributions and training protocols.

</details>


### [688] [Selective Ensemble Based on Preference-Directed Multi-Objective Bandits](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21929)
*Lanjihong Ma,Zhen-Yu Zhang,Masashi Sugiyama,Zhi-Hua Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper formalizes selective ensemble learning under partial preferences as a multi-objective bandit problem (PDMOB), proposes Pareto C-optimality and PrefUCB algorithm with logarithmic regret guarantees, and validates on model ensemble and asset allocation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern machine learning systems require selecting promising models under limited evaluation budgets, while downstream tasks only provide partial preferences over capabilities (e.g., accuracy, robustness, reasoning). This creates a sequential decision problem under partially specified linear preferences that lacks formal treatment.

Method: The paper formalizes Preference-Directed Multi-Objective Bandits (PDMOB) with polyhedral preference cones, introduces Pareto C-optimality generalizing both standard Pareto optimality and single-weight scalarization, and proposes PrefUCB (preference-directed upper confidence bound) algorithm with directional confidence intervals for guided exploration.

Result: Establishes instance-dependent logarithmic regret bounds for both indicator-based and gap-weighted regret criteria, recovering optimal logarithmic dependence on horizon T in classical special cases. Experiments on large pre-trained model selective ensemble and online asset allocation validate efficacy.

Conclusion: The PDMOB framework and PrefUCB algorithm effectively address selective ensemble learning under partial preferences with theoretical guarantees and strong empirical performance.

Abstract: Selective ensemble for modern machine learning systems requires choosing promising model candidates under limited evaluation budgets, while downstream tasks often specify only partial preferences over capabilities such as accuracy, robustness, and reasoning. This setting naturally gives rise to a sequential decision problem under partially specified linear preferences. We formalize it as preference-directed multi-objective bandits (PDMOB), where admissible trade-offs are represented by a polyhedral preference cone. Based on this formulation, we introduce Pareto $C$-optimality, which recovers standard Pareto optimality and single-weight scalarization as special cases. We then propose the preference-directed upper confidence bound (PrefUCB) algorithm, which maintains directional confidence intervals to guide exploration. We analyze both indicator-based and gap-weighted regret, and establish instance-dependent logarithmic bounds for both criteria, recovering the optimal logarithmic dependence on the horizon $T$ in classical special cases. Experiments on large pre-trained model selective ensemble tasks and online asset allocation under institutional mandates validate the efficacy of our method.

</details>


### [689] [DevoTG: Temporal Graph Neural Networks for Modeling C. elegans Developmental Connectomics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21940)
*Jayadratha Gayen,Bradly Alicea*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DevoTG is a temporal graph framework using Temporal Graph Neural Networks to model C. elegans neural development from birth to adulthood, achieving 0.839 AUC on lineage prediction (outperforming static GNNs by 26 points) and identifying three connection stability classes across development.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understanding how a nervous system wires itself from birth to adulthood is a fundamental challenge in developmental neuroscience.

Method: DevoTG applies Temporal Graph Neural Networks (TGNs) to two complementary representations: a Continuous-Time Dynamic Graph (CTDG) of cell division events from lineage data, and a Discrete-Time Dynamic Graph (DTDG) of the developing synaptic connectome across eight reconstructed electron-microscopy datasets.

Result: Lineage prediction: mean test AUC 0.839 ± 0.007 (validation AUC 0.937 ± 0.001), outperforming static GNN by 26 AUC points. Connectome analysis identified three connection stability classes (stable, developmental, variable) across 225 neurons and 858-2,496 connections. Hub interneurons AVA, AVB, AVE showed persistent centrality reinforced across larval stages.

Conclusion: Temporal memory is the decisive factor for lineage prediction; DevoTG provides a temporal-graph-theoretic complement to individual-variability classification and enables biological hypothesis generation through interactive visualizations. Open-source and designed for extension to other developing nervous systems.

Abstract: Understanding how a nervous system wires itself from birth to adulthood is a fundamental challenge in developmental neuroscience. We present DevoTG, a temporal graph framework that applies Temporal Graph Neural Networks (TGNs) to two complementary representations of C. elegans neural development: a Continuous-Time Dynamic Graph (CTDG) of cell division events derived from cell lineage data, and a Discrete-Time Dynamic Graph (DTDG) of the developing synaptic connectome spanning eight reconstructed electron-microscopy datasets. On the lineage prediction task, our TGN achieves a mean test AUC of 0.839 +/- 0.007 (5 seeds; validation AUC 0.937 +/- 0.001), outperforming a static GNN with the identical architecture by 26 AUC points (0.577 +/- 0.080), demonstrating that temporal memory is the decisive factor. Applied to the connectome DTDG, DevoTG identifies three connection stability classes (stable, developmental, and variable) across 225 neurons and 858 to 2,496 connections over development (L1 birth to adult), providing a temporal-graph-theoretic complement to the individual-variability classification of Witvliet et al. Analysis of hub command interneurons AVA, AVB, and AVE reveals their persistent centrality and how their integration roles are progressively reinforced across larval stages. Accompanying interactive visualizations (3D animated networks, centrality heatmaps, and a spatiotemporal lineage graph) make developmental dynamics accessible for biological hypothesis generation. DevoTG is open-source and designed for extension to other developing nervous systems. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/DevoLearn/DevoGraph/tree/main/DevoTG.

</details>


### [690] [Modularized Reinforcement Learning on LLMs: From MDP Creation to Exploration and Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21943)
*Zhao Yang,Yuxuan Jiang,Ting-Chih Chen,Lincen Yang,Annie Wong,Chao Gao,Jacob E. Kooi,Zhong Li,Jiayang Shi,Kevin Qiu,Qi Huang,Xinrui Zu,Shiping Yang,Hengyuan Zhang,Ngai Wong,Filip Ilievski,Shujian Yu,Aske Plaat,Zhaochun Ren,Mark Hoogendoorn,Vincent François-Lavet*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This survey provides a structured examination of RL algorithm design decisions (MDP creation, exploration, learning paradigms) applied to LLM post-training, revealing that current LLM-RL research is heavily concentrated on critic-free policy gradients and Monte Carlo credit assignment, while value-based methods, off-policy actor-critic, and bootstrapping remain largely unexplored.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand why PPO and GRPO dominate LLM post-training and identify underexplored RL techniques that could be transferred to LLM training, given that current RL-for-LLM methods represent only a narrow slice of what RL offers.

Method: Organizes analysis around three stages of RL algorithm construction: (1) MDP creation (reward, state/action spaces, termination, discount), (2) exploration strategies (temperature sampling, entropy regularization, intrinsic motivation, tree search, curriculum learning), and (3) learning across four classical RL dimensions (model-free vs model-based, value-based vs policy-based vs actor-critic, on-policy vs off-policy, credit assignment via Monte Carlo vs bootstrapping).

Result: Maps the LLM literature onto the proposed taxonomy, showing a non-uniform distribution: critic-free policy gradients and Monte Carlo credit assignment are densely populated, while value-based methods, off-policy actor-critic training, and bootstrapping-based credit assignment are largely unexplored despite having well-established counterparts in classical RL.

Conclusion: There are concrete opportunities for transferring proven RL techniques (value-based methods, off-policy actor-critic, bootstrapping) to LLM training; the survey offers a shared framework for researchers in both RL and LLMs to understand current practice and identify promising future directions.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) has become central to LLM post-training, yet the methods that dominate current pipelines, PPO and GRPO, represent only a narrow slice of what RL offers. Understanding why these methods prevail, and what alternatives exist, requires a principled examination of the design decisions that underlie any RL algorithm.
  This survey organizes that examination around three stages of algorithm construction. We begin with MDP creation: how the reward function, state space, action space, termination condition, and discount factor are, or could be, defined for LLM training. We then turn to exploration, covering temperature sampling, entropy regularization, intrinsic motivation, tree search, and curriculum learning. Finally, we address learning along four classical RL dimensions: model-free versus model-based, value-based versus policy-based versus actor-critic, on-policy versus off-policy, and credit assignment, including both Monte Carlo methods, which rely on full return estimates, and bootstrapping methods, which update estimates using other learned predictions.
  Mapping the LLM literature onto this taxonomy reveals a strikingly non-uniform distribution of research effort. Critic-free policy gradients and Monte Carlo credit assignment are densely populated, while value-based methods, off-policy actor-critic training, and bootstrapping-based credit assignment remain largely unexplored despite well-established counterparts in classical RL. These gaps represent concrete opportunities for transferring proven RL techniques to LLM training.
