  By making these gaps explicit alongside the methods that have proven effective, this survey offers researchers in both RL and LLMs a shared framework for understanding current practice and identifying promising directions for future work.

</details>


### [691] [On the Curse of Dimensionality in Private Sparse Covariance Estimation and PCA](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21951)
*Syamantak Kumar,Shourya Pandey,Purnamrita Sarkar,Kevin Tian*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper studies differentially private covariance estimation and PCA under k-row-column sparsity, revealing an exponential gap between private and non-private sample complexity when k = polylog(d). The authors show poly(k, log d) sample complexity is possible for DP PCA under eigenvector sparsity, but prove poly(d) lower bounds for both sparse covariance estimation and PCA under DP, demonstrating a fundamental separation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether the curse of dimensionality is inherent for sparse covariance estimation tasks under differential privacy, given that non-private methods achieve poly(k, log d) sample complexity while existing DP results require Ω(d) samples.

Method: The paper provides upper bounds showing poly(k, log d) sample complexity for DP PCA when the leading eigenvector is also sparse, and establishes poly(d) lower bounds for both sparse covariance estimation and PCA under DP, using techniques that also imply stronger lower bounds for standard DP PCA without sparsity.

Result: An exponential gap (poly(k, log d) vs. poly(d)) between private and non-private sample complexity for sparse covariance estimation and PCA under k-RCS when k = polylog(d). This is the first demonstration of such a separation for any sparse estimation problem in private high-dimensional statistics.

Conclusion: The curse of dimensionality is inherent for sparse covariance estimation under DP, establishing a fundamental separation from the non-private setting. The techniques also yield stronger lower bounds for standard DP PCA.

Abstract: We study high-dimensional differentially private (DP) covariance estimation in the operator norm, and principal component analysis (PCA), under $k$-row-column sparsity ($k$-RCS) of the covariance matrix. In the non-private setting, it is known that $\mathsf{poly}(k, \log d)$ samples suffice to solve both of these problems. However, the only comparable result known under DP (Wang et al. 2021) requires $Ω(d)$ samples under standard parameterizations of the problem. We investigate when this curse of dimensionality is inherent for sparse covariance estimation tasks under DP.
  On the upper bound front, we show that a $\mathsf{poly}(k, \log d)$ sample complexity for PCA is possible under DP, if we also posit sparsity of the leading eigenvector. We complement this result with $\mathsf{poly}(d)$ lower bounds under DP for both sparse covariance estimation and PCA, establishing an exponential gap between the private and non-private variants of these problems when $k = \mathsf{polylog}(d)$. To our knowledge, no such separation has previously been demonstrated for any sparse estimation problems in private high-dimensional statistics. Our techniques are flexible enough that they imply stronger lower bounds even for the well-studied problem of standard DP PCA, without sparsity assumptions.

</details>


### [692] [Learning by Shifting: Temporal View Construction for Time Series Contrastive Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21957)
*Abdul-Kazeem Shamba,Kerstin Bach,Gavin Taylor*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes ShiFT (Shift Invariant Feature Training), a simple deterministic method that encodes temporal shift invariance for self-supervised contrastive learning on time series, achieving state-of-the-art results on multiple benchmarks while reducing training time.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Supervised learning requires expensive labeled data, and existing self-supervised contrastive learning methods for time series rely on hand-crafted augmentations and masking heuristics that embed strong domain assumptions, limit generalization, and may introduce spurious correlations.

Method: ShiFT encodes temporal shift invariance through a simple, deterministic view construction that exploits temporal structure, without relying on hand-crafted augmentations or masking heuristics.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on six diverse real-world time series benchmark datasets as well as the UCR and UEA archives, while also reducing training time.

Conclusion: Explicitly encoding temporal shift invariance via deterministic view construction is sufficient for strong time series representation learning, and the paper provides practical insights for designing efficient contrastive learning frameworks for time series.

Abstract: Supervised learning demands large quantities of labeled data, a bottleneck that is expensive and reliant on domain-specific expertise. Self-supervised learning, particularly contrastive learning, has emerged as a compelling alternative, enabling rich representation learning directly from unlabeled data. Yet its success hinges critically on the design of positive and negative sample pairs. Existing approaches for time series rely on hand-crafted augmentations and masking heuristics that embed strong domain assumptions, often limiting generalization across diverse temporal patterns and potentially introducing spurious correlations. In this work, we challenge this paradigm by demonstrating that explicitly encoding temporal shift invariance through a simple, deterministic view construction is sufficient to learn strong representations for time series classification. By exploiting temporal structure, our method, Shift Invariant Feature Training (ShiFT), achieves state-of-the-art performance on six diverse real-world time series benchmark datasets, as well as the UCR and UEA archives, while reducing training time. Beyond empirical performance, we present a systematic analysis of contrastive learning dynamics in time series settings, examining the effects of batch size and the number of negatives on downstream performance. Our findings provide practical insights for designing efficient contrastive learning frameworks for time series representation learning. The source code is publicly available at https://github.com/sfi-norwai/ShiFT.

</details>


### [693] [A Standard Processing Pipeline for High-accuracy Measurement of Few-shot Regression on Laser Induced Breakdown Spectroscopy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21960)
*Hao Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A standardized pipeline integrating diffusion-based denoising (3D UNet), attention-based autoencoder for dimensionality reduction, group shuffling data augmentation, and OLS regression achieves superior LIBS quantitative measurement under few-shot scenarios, with 37.7-37.6% improvement over baselines (mean RMAE=0.2847).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LIBS struggles with high-accuracy quantitative measurement in few-shot scenarios due to spectral noise and data scarcity; traditional preprocessing fails to preserve subtle spectral features or capture nonlinear correlations.

Method: Standardized processing pipeline: (1) Diffusion-based denoising using 3D UNet to remove noise while preserving emission features, (2) Attention-based autoencoder for dimensionality reduction capturing nonlinear spectral correlations, (3) Group shuffling data augmentation for model robustness via feature group permutation, (4) Ordinary least squares regression.

Result: Mean RMAE of 0.2847, with 37.7% improvement over baseline autoencoder and 37.6% improvement over traditional PCA-PLS regression.

Conclusion: The framework validates generalizability and establishes a new benchmark for few-shot LIBS regression.

Abstract: Laser-induced breakdown spectroscopy (LIBS) faces challenges in high-accuracy quantitative measurement under few-shot scenarios due to spectral noise and data scarcity. Traditional preprocessing methods often fail to preserve subtle spectral features or capture nonlinear correlations. This work proposes a standardized processing pipeline integrating diffusion-based denoising, attention-based autoencoder for dimensionality reduction, group shuffling data augmentation, and ordinary least squares regression. The diffusion module employs a 3D UNet architecture to remove spectral noise while preserving essential emission features. The attention-autoencoder captures nonlinear spectral correlations, effectively reducing high-dimensional spectral data to compact latent representations. Group shuffling data augmentation enhances model robustness by creating synthetic samples through feature group permutation. Experimental results on multiple elemental concentrations demonstrate that our Diffusion-DA-AE pipeline achieves superior performance with a mean RMAE of 0.2847, representing 37.7\% and 37.6\% improvements over baseline autoencoder and traditional PCA-PLS regression, respectively. The framework's effectiveness validates its generalizability and establishes a new benchmark for few-shot LIBS regression.

</details>


### [694] [VegSim: A Geospatial World Model for Scenario-Conditioned Vegetation Simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21961)
*Irene Iele,Elena Mulero Ayllón,Paolo Soda,Matteo Tortora*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: VegSim is a geospatial world model for scenario-conditioned vegetation simulation that infers latent vegetation states from NDVI histories and covariates, propagates them under controllable future weather forcing, and predicts NDVI quantiles. It achieves strong accuracy under distribution shifts and enables conditional simulation under user-defined meteorological scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing vegetation forecasting models are limited to predicting under observed/historical weather trajectories and cannot answer scenario-conditioned questions about how vegetation would respond to alternative meteorological conditions, which is critical for climate stress monitoring.

Method: VegSim uses a recurrent latent dynamics model that: (1) infers a latent vegetation state from sparse NDVI histories, past meteorological covariates, and static spatial context; (2) propagates the latent state forward under controllable future weather forcing; (3) decodes predictive NDVI quantiles at each lead time. Future forcing is a controllable input, enabling both forecasting and conditional simulation.

Result: Evaluated on GreenEarthNet, VegSim achieves strong point and probabilistic accuracy against baselines under in-distribution data and spatial, temporal, and joint spatial-temporal shifts. Simulations across Europe under four meteorological scenarios and a France summer 2022 case study show spatially coherent patterns consistent with known temperature and precipitation sensitivity.

Conclusion: VegSim is a compact architecture that supports both probabilistic forecasting under observed weather and scenario-conditioned simulation under user-defined meteorological forcing without requiring supervision on scenario responses, enabling robust vegetation monitoring under climate stress.

Abstract: Vegetation monitoring under climate stress requires answering not only how it will evolve given the expected weather, but how it would respond to alternative meteorological conditions. Forecasting models return the expected vegetation state for the observed weather and cannot answer these scenario-conditioned questions, because future weather is fixed to the recorded trajectory. We present VegSim, a geospatial world model for scenario-conditioned vegetation simulation. VegSim infers a latent vegetation state from sparse satellite-derived NDVI histories, past meteorological covariates, and static spatial context, propagates it forward under future weather forcing through recurrent latent dynamics, and decodes predictive NDVI quantiles at each lead time. Because future forcing enters as a controllable input, the same trained model supports probabilistic forecasting under observed weather and conditional simulation under user-defined meteorological forcing, without supervision on scenario responses. We evaluate VegSim on GreenEarthNet across in-distribution data and spatial, temporal, and joint spatial-temporal shift, where it achieves strong point and probabilistic accuracy against time series and Earth observation forecasting baselines while using a compact architecture. We then simulate vegetation responses across Europe under four meteorological scenarios, and in a France summer 2022 case study, obtaining spatially coherent patterns consistent with known sensitivity to temperature and precipitation. The code is available at https://github.com/arco-group/vegsim.

</details>


### [695] [SPOTR: Spatio-temporal Pooling One-Token Reconstruction for Universal Physiological Signal Self-supervised Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21973)
*Yiyu Gui,Mingzhi Chen,Yuesheng Zhu,Guibo Luo,Yuchao Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SPOTR is a compress-reconstruct SSL pretraining framework for physiological signals that compresses each waveform into a single-token representation via spatio-temporal pooling, enabling efficient reconstruction and strong linear probing performance across 20 datasets spanning EEG, iEEG, ECG, and PPG, with 78% lower latency and 52% less GPU memory than general time-series foundation models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current SSL methods for physiological signals struggle with heterogeneous datasets, distort clinically meaningful structures, learn shortcuts from temporal/cross-channel redundancy, perform poorly under linear probing (which better matches real medical scenarios), and suffer from high computation/memory costs due to flattened spatiotemporal token sequences in Transformer-based models.

Method: SPOTR introduces a single-token global bottleneck via a compress-reconstruct pretraining framework: each waveform is compressed into a single-token representation, and the signal is reconstructed conditioned only on that token. It also includes an efficient spatio-temporal compaction module to reduce computation and memory.

Result: Pretrained on 20 datasets (EEG, iEEG, ECG, PPG), SPOTR outperforms the strongest baseline under linear probing, improving average AUC by 18.49% (EEG), 21.71% (iEEG), 17.86% (ECG), and 4.64% (PPG). Compared to a general time-series foundation model, it achieves ~78% lower latency and ~52% lower peak GPU memory.

Conclusion: SPOTR effectively addresses key limitations of SSL for physiological signals by providing a computationally efficient, single-modality-agnostic framework that preserves clinically meaningful structure and achieves strong linear probing performance across diverse physiological signal modalities.

Abstract: Physiological signals such as EEG, ECG, and PPG are widely used in clinical monitoring. Recent self-supervised learning (SSL) methods offer an attractive way to leverage unlabeled recordings, yet they still fall short in practice. In particular, current SSL methods struggle across heterogeneous datasets, often distorting clinically meaningful structures or learning shortcuts from temporal and cross-channel redundancy. Consequently, existing SSL methods often deliver limited performance under linear probing, a lightweight adaptation setting that better matches real-world medical scenarios. Moreover, most Transformer-based SSL models encode a flattened spatiotemporal token sequence, incurring high computation and memory cost, and are typically developed within a single modality. To address these limitations, we present SPOTR (Spatio-temporal Pooling One-Token Reconstruction), a compress-reconstruct pretraining framework that introduces a single-token global bottleneck for physiological signals. SPOTR compresses each waveform into a single-token representation and reconstructs the signal conditioned only on this representation. Meanwhile, SPOTR introduces an efficient spatio-temporal compaction module to reduce computation and memory cost. Pretrained on 20 datasets spanning EEG, iEEG, ECG, and PPG, SPOTR consistently outperforms the strongest baseline under linear probing, improving average AUC by 18.49%, 21.71%, 17.86%, and 4.64%, respectively. Compared with a representative general-purpose time-series foundation model, SPOTR achieves around 78% lower latency and 52% lower peak GPU memory on average. The code can be found at https://github.com/5GYYYYY/SPOTR.

</details>


### [696] [Prefix-Guided On-Policy Distillation: Mining Golden Trajectories from Rollouts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21994)
*Qingfei Zhao,Huan Song,Shuyu Tian,Jiawei Shao,Xuelong Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PG-OPD improves on-policy distillation for long-horizon math reasoning by using prefix-level teacher-student compatibility (top-k overlap) to selectively allocate rollout budgets, cutting training time by up to 2.46x while boosting accuracy by up to 4.80 points.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard OPD wastes computation on long trajectories that become weakly aligned with the teacher, producing noisy supervisory signals. The authors identify that local teacher-student compatibility (measured by top-k overlap on prefixes) is a key prerequisite for successful OPD, motivating a mechanism to prune unproductive rollouts early.

Method: PG-OPD first decodes all sampled candidates to a fixed prefix length, computes teacher-student top-k overlap within an early probe window of that prefix, and then only continues generating the full long rollout for high-overlap candidates. Low-overlap candidates are truncated at the prefix, avoiding costly suffix generation and noisy supervision.

Result: On AMC, AIME, and HMMT benchmarks across diverse teacher-student pairs, PG-OPD improves average accuracy by up to 4.80 points and reduces training time by up to 2.46x compared to standard OPD.

Conclusion: Prefix-level compatibility is a practical and effective signal for routing OPD computation toward learnable trajectories, enabling both efficiency gains and accuracy improvements in long-horizon mathematical reasoning.

Abstract: On-policy distillation (OPD) improves reasoning models by applying dense teacher supervision on student-sampled trajectories. However, scaling OPD to long-horizon mathematical reasoning exposes a reliability and efficiency problem: standard OPD assigns every sampled candidate the same long rollout budget, even though some trajectories may quickly become weakly aligned with the teacher and provide less useful supervision. Prior analyses suggest that successful OPD depends on local teacher-student compatibility, which can be measured by top-k overlap on student-visited prefixes. When this overlap is low, continuing to generate or train on long suffixes may waste computation and introduce noisy learning signal. To address this, we introduce Prefix-Guided On-Policy Distillation (PG-OPD), a simple rollout-allocation framework that uses fixed-length prefixes to estimate trajectory value before expensive long-horizon generation. PG-OPD first decodes every sampled candidate to the same prefix length, computes teacher-student top-k overlap within an early probe window of that prefix, and selectively continues high-overlap candidates to a fixed long length. Low-overlap candidates stop at the fixed prefix, avoiding unnecessary suffix generation. Across diverse teacher-student combinations on AMC, AIME, and HMMT benchmarks, PG-OPD improves average accuracy by up to 4.80 points while reducing training time by up to 2.46x. These results suggest that prefix-level compatibility provides a practical signal for directing OPD computation toward trajectories that remain learnable from the teacher.

</details>


### [697] [Load Testing for Machine Learning Model Serving Systems at Scale](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22013)
*Amr S. Abdelfattah,Nakul Tirumalai,Indu Mohanan,Xiao Li,Pengchao Wang,Dinakar Dhurjati,Eric Sung*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper presents \sys, an industrial load testing framework for ML serving systems that uses adaptive, feedback-driven search to estimate serving capacity under SLO constraints, evaluated across 14 industrial case studies spanning recommendation, ranking, vision, and NLP models. Key findings show workload calibration reduces estimation error from ~30% to 2-6%, and proper warmup handling yields 22.2% improvement in accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ML model serving capacity planning remains largely ad hoc, leading to either under-provisioning (causing SLO violations and incidents) or over-provisioning (causing substantial GPU resource waste).

Method: The authors propose \sys, an adaptive feedback-driven load testing framework that uses real-time performance signals with dampening, spike tolerance, and convergence detection to systematically identify maximum sustainable throughput under SLO constraints.

Result: Workload calibration using recorded traffic reduces estimation error from ~30% to 2-6%; proper warmup handling yields 22.2% improvement in accuracy. Deployment substantially reduced under-provisioning incidents across 14 industrial case studies spanning four ML architecture classes.

Conclusion: Systematic load testing with ML-specific design decisions (workload calibration, warmup handling) leads to substantial improvements in GPU resource efficiency and operational reliability. The paper distills six lessons and architectural guidelines for ML load testing.

Abstract: Machine learning (ML) model serving has become a dominant consumer of GPU infrastructure, yet capacity planning in these systems remains largely ad hoc. Under-provisioning leads to service-level objective (SLO) violations and production incidents, while over-provisioning results in substantial resource waste. This paper presents \sys, an industrial load testing framework for ML serving systems that systematically estimates serving capacity through an adaptive, feedback-driven search strategy. The approach leverages real-time performance signals, incorporating dampening, spike tolerance, and convergence detection to efficiently identify maximum sustainable throughput under SLO constraints. We evaluate \sys through a longitudinal analysis of 14 industrial case studies spanning four ML architecture classes: recommendation, ranking, vision, and NLP. This study demonstrates that systematic load testing leads to substantial improvements in GPU resource efficiency and operational reliability. Prior to adopting \sys, a significant fraction of model launches were under-provisioned, resulting in recurring incidents; these issues were substantially reduced after deployment. Our results show that ML-specific design decisions are critical to accurate capacity estimation: workload calibration using recorded traffic reduces estimation error from approximately 30\% to 2--6\%, while proper warmup handling yields a 22.2\% improvement in accuracy. Further analysis reveals key factors influencing prediction error, including model size and co-location effects. This paper distills six lessons and derive architectural guidelines for ML load testing, offering actionable insights for building reliable and efficient ML serving systems.

</details>


### [698] [Channel Location Constrains the Auditability of Subliminal Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22019)
*Tamas Madl*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Subliminal learning transfers hidden teacher traits to students via specific channels (body, vocabulary geometry, conditional routing), and the location of these channels determines which pre-training audits are valid; misaligned audits give false assurance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine when subliminal learning (inheriting a teacher's hidden trait from distillation data that never names it) can be audited before training, and to identify the mechanism — channel location rather than model identity or scale — that governs audit validity.

Method: The authors identify three channel regimes: (1) body channel (initialization-dependent) where coverage (cosine between student's initial distillation update and teacher's fine-tuning displacement) predicts transfer (Spearman ρ≈0.95, AUROC 0.997); (2) vocabulary geometry channel (initialization-independent) in pretrained LMs for masked single-token traits, where initialization-alignment screens fail but post-hoc detection and targeted mitigation work; (3) conditional routing via body computation for sycophancy, which evades four audits across two model families.

Result: Coverage achieves high predictive performance (Spearman ρ≈0.95, AUROC 0.997) for body-channel transfer. For vocabulary channel, removing a named entity from the loss still yields ~0.40 held-out probability (~2500× increase), and orthogonalizing the trait's output row collapses leakage while random edits do not. For sycophancy, transfer reaches ~0.63 of teacher's effect, localizes to body, evades four audits.

Conclusion: Channel location is necessary for deciding which audits are sound; using an audit outside its carrier regime gives false assurance. No single pre-training screen is universally valid.

Abstract: Subliminal learning lets a student inherit a teacher's hidden trait from distillation data that never names it. We ask when such transfer can be audited before training. The answer is not model identity or scale alone, but channel location: the carrier through which the trait reaches the student. We find three regimes. In a controlled initialization-dependent body channel, a pre-training screen works. Coverage, the cosine between the student's initial distillation update and the teacher's fine-tuning displacement, predicts held-out transfer (Spearman $ρ\approx 0.95$; AUROC 0.997). In pretrained language models, masked single-token traits instead ride convergent vocabulary geometry. This channel is initialization-independent, so initialization-alignment screens, including coverage, are not mechanistic; the useful handles are post-hoc detection and targeted mitigation. Even when a single-token named entity is removed from the loss, the student's held-out probability for that entity rises to 0.40 on average ($\sim 2500\times$), and a related semantic class transfers. In an untied-head model, orthogonalizing the trait's output row against entangled neighbours collapses leakage, while equal-size random-subspace edits do not. Thus removing a target string from distillation labels does not remove the corresponding preference: neighbouring tokens can carry it. Finally, conditional behaviours can route through the network body. For sycophancy, with agreement and correction markers masked from the loss, transfer reaches about 0.63 of the teacher's effect, localizes to body computation, and evades four audits across two model families. We scope this as masked transfer of a condition-present policy. Channel location is necessary for deciding which audits can be sound. It is not a deployment-ready screen: an audit used outside its carrier regime can give false assurance.

</details>


### [699] [Cluster-Specific Localized Drift Detection for Efficient Batch Model Adaptation under Controlled Distribution Shift](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22026)
*Ignacio Cabrera Martin,Marcello Trovati,Almas Baimagambetov,Nikolaos Polatidis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a cluster-induced distribution shift simulation framework to convert static tabular datasets into evolving data streams, and systematically evaluates six drift adaptation strategies across multiple models and datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Machine learning systems in dynamic environments face performance degradation from nonstationary data, but tabular benchmarks lack temporal structure, hindering reproducible evaluation of drift adaptation methods.

Method: Develops a cluster-induced distribution shift simulation framework that applies structured perturbations across feature-space partitions. Evaluates six strategies: static learning, sliding-window retraining, global ADWIN retraining, cluster-local ADWIN retraining, random subspace drift detection, and feature-partitioned drift detection on five benchmark datasets with diverse model families.

Result: Experiments conducted on five benchmark datasets covering classification and regression using linear models, k-NN, tree ensembles, boosting methods, and adaptive online learners.

Conclusion: The framework enables controlled, reproducible evaluation of drift adaptation on static tabular data, with systematic comparison of six adaptation strategies across diverse tasks and model families.

Abstract: Machine learning systems deployed in dynamic environments frequently operate under nonstationary data distributions, where controlled distribution shift can progressively degrade predictive performance. However, many widely used tabular benchmark datasets lack explicit temporal structure, limiting reproducible evaluation of drift adaptation methods. This work proposes a cluster-induced distribution shift simulation framework that transforms static tabular datasets into controlled evolving data streams through structured perturbations across featurespace partitions. Using this framework, six adaptation strategies are systematically evaluated: static learning, sliding-window retraining, global ADWIN retraining, cluster-local ADWIN retraining, random subspace drift detection, and feature-partitioned drift detection. Experiments are conducted on five benchmark datasets covering both classification and regression tasks using diverse predictive model families, including linear models, k-Nearest Neighbours, tree ensembles, boosting methods, and adaptive online learners.

</details>


### [700] [A Completion-Aware Framework for Impactful Counterfactual Explainability in Graph Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22033)
*Maria Myrto Villia,Filippos Gouidis,Theodore Patkos,Panos Trahanias*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A novel pipeline for model-agnostic, local-level counterfactual explainability in GNNs that combines factual explainability with link prediction to generate high-quality counterfactual explanations by both adding and removing edges, validated on real-world and synthetic graph classification benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing counterfactual explainers for GNNs that can add/remove edges lack generic, efficient, and high-quality qualitative explanation generation.

Method: Couples progress in factual explainability with missing edge prediction models rooted in link prediction research to generate counterfactual explanations.

Result: Multi-faceted experimental analysis on real-world and synthetic graph classification benchmarks (binary and multi-label) demonstrates advancements over state-of-the-art baselines across diverse metrics.

Conclusion: The proposed pipeline improves quality, robustness, and intuitiveness of counterfactual explanations for GNNs.

Abstract: In this study, we propose a novel pipeline for generic, model-agnostic, local-level counterfactual explainability in graph neural networks (GNNs). Although counterfactual explainers capable of both adding and removing edges have emerged in recent years, the need for generic and efficient solutions remains unmet, particularly concerning qualitative explanation generation. Our approach couples progress in factual explainability with missing edge prediction models rooted in link prediction research, in order to enhance the quality, robustness and intuitiveness of explanations. A multi-faceted experimental analysis conducted on real-world and synthetic graph classification benchmarks, both binary and multi-label, demonstrates the advancements in comparison to state-of-the-art baselines across diverse metrics.

</details>


### [701] [Gradient-Descent Steps to Success over Mean Accuracy: A Paradigm Shift for ML](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22053)
*Riccardo Poli,Ahmet Yilmaz*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes shifting ML model evaluation from maximizing accuracy regardless of cost to minimizing computational effort (number of gradient descent steps to reach target accuracy with high probability). Across 11 models and 5 datasets, they find optimal hyperparameters favor unusually large learning rates, which promote generalization and minimize expected effort. They also identify phase transitions in search strategies—single runs suffice for low accuracy targets, but numerous independent short restarts are needed to reach model limits—and demonstrate this paradigm's utility for model selection under computational budgets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional ML evaluation focuses on maximizing accuracy without considering computational cost. The authors argue this overlooks practical efficiency and propose a paradigm that instead measures performance based on the computational effort required to reach acceptable accuracy.

Method: The authors extend Koza's computational effort metric (originally for Genetic Programming) to any ML model trained via gradient descent, defining it as the total number of gradient descent steps needed to achieve a target accuracy with high probability. They evaluate 11 diverse ML models on 5 standard classification datasets.

Result: Optimal hyperparameters consistently favor unusually large learning rates, which enable rapid aggressive landscape traversal that promotes generalization (like superconvergence) and statistically minimizes expected computational effort. A phase transition exists: single runs suffice for low accuracy targets, but reaching model performance limits requires many independent short restarts.

Conclusion: This effort-based paradigm provides a robust framework for model selection, enabling practitioners to choose optimal algorithms based on problem difficulty for a given target accuracy or to maximize achievable accuracy under a fixed budget of gradient descent steps.

Abstract: Traditional evaluation of machine learning (ML) models typically focuses on achieving the maximum possible accuracy irrespective of the computational cost. In this article, we propose a paradigm shift towards evaluating performance based on computational effort-explicitly defined here as the total number of gradient descent steps required to reach an acceptable level of accuracy with high probability. Building upon the concept of computational effort originally introduced by Koza for Genetic Programming, we extend this metric to any ML model trained via gradient descent. Furthermore, we demonstrate that minimising this effort acts as a novel form of Automatic Machine Learning (AutoML). By evaluating it across 11 diverse ML models and five standard classification datasets, we uncover significant insights into the dynamics of gradient-based learning. Our findings reveal that optimal hyper-parameters consistently favour unusually large learning rates. Crucially, we demonstrate that the rapid, aggressive landscape traversal enabled by these large rates not only promotes generalisation-as seen in phenomena like superconvergence-but also statistically minimises the expected computational effort for training. Furthermore, we identify distinct phase transitions in the optimal search strategy: while a single training run suffices for lower accuracy targets, reaching a model's performance limit requires a dramatic shift towards conducting numerous independent, short restarts. Finally, we illustrate how this effort-based paradigm provides a robust framework for model selection, allowing practitioners to choose optimal algorithms based on the difficulty of a problem as perceived by different models for a given target accuracy, or to maximise the achievable accuracy for a fixed budget of gradient descent steps.

</details>


### [702] [Provably Efficient Policy-Reward Co-Pretraining for Adversarial Imitation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22056)
*Tian Xu,Zexuan Chen,Zhilong Zhang,Yi-Chen Li,Chenyang Wang,Lei Yuan,Yang Yu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper provides a theoretical analysis of pretraining in adversarial imitation learning (AIL), identifies the overlooked issue of missing reward pretraining, and proposes CoPT-AIL—a policy-reward co-pretraining method that achieves provably better imitation guarantees and superior empirical performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While AIL outperforms behavioral cloning (BC), it requires extensive online environment interaction. Prior work uses BC pretrained policies to reduce this burden, but lacks rigorous theoretical understanding of why pretraining helps and what is missing.

Method: The authors first analyze AIL with policy pretraining alone and identify reward error as the dominant suboptimality source. They then develop a policy-reward co-pretraining approach (CoPT-AIL) based on reward shaping analysis, which jointly pretrains both policy and reward via a single BC procedure.

Result: CoPT-AIL achieves an improved imitation gap bound over standard AIL, providing the first theoretical guarantee for pretraining benefits in AIL. Experiments confirm its superior performance over existing methods.

Conclusion: Co-pretraining policy and reward is a principled and effective way to accelerate AIL, bridging a critical gap overlooked in prior work.

Abstract: Adversarial imitation learning (AIL) achieves high-quality imitation compared to behavioral cloning (BC), but demands substantial online environment interaction. Recent empirical work has explored initializing AIL algorithms with BC pretrained policies to address this limitation, yet a rigorous theoretical understanding of pretraining's role in AIL remains elusive. This paper provides a systematic theoretical analysis and introduces principled pretraining algorithms for accelerating AIL. We begin by analyzing AIL with policy pretraining alone, identifying reward error as the dominant source of suboptimality. This reveals a critical and previously overlooked gap: the absence of reward pretraining. Motivated by this finding, we develop a principled policy-reward co-pretraining approach grounded in a reward shaping analysis. Our analysis uncovers a fundamental connection between expert policies and shaping rewards, which naturally gives rise to CoPT-AIL, an approach that jointly pretrains both policy and reward through a single BC procedure. We prove that CoPT-AIL achieves an improved imitation gap bound over standard AIL, establishing the first theoretical guarantee for the benefits of pretraining in AIL. Experimental results confirm CoPT-AIL's superior performance over existing AIL methods.

</details>


### [703] [New Smooth Loss functions for Robust Regression that Closely Approximate Absolute Error and Provide Improved Performance on Datasets With Significant Outliers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22068)
*Mathew Mithra Noel,Arindam Banerjee,Yug D. Oswal,Geraldine Bessie Amali D,Venkataraman Muthiah-Nakarajan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes two new infinitely differentiable loss functions (Square Root Loss and Smooth Mean Absolute Error) that closely approximate MAE while being differentiable, showing superior performance in regression tasks with many outliers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard loss functions for regression (MSE, MAE, Huber, Log-Cosh) have limitations: MSE is sensitive to outliers, MAE is non-differentiable at origin and has large derivatives near minimum causing instability, while existing differentiable approximations don't closely enough match MAE's robustness.

Method: The authors introduce two new loss functions: Square Root Loss (SRL) which is strictly convex, and Smooth Mean Absolute Error (SMAE) which is strictly quasi-convex. Both are infinitely differentiable and better approximate MAE. They also present two robust linear regression models using these losses. Performance is evaluated across multiple benchmarks and datasets.

Result: The proposed SRL and SMAE loss functions demonstrate superior performance compared to MSE, MAE, Huber, and Log-Cosh losses on regression tasks with significant outliers across a wide variety of datasets and benchmarks.

Conclusion: SRL and SMAE are effective robust alternatives to existing loss functions for regression tasks with many outliers, offering differentiability with strong robustness properties.

Abstract: The performance of supervised machine learning models is directly related to the quality of the training dataset. In particular, the presence of significantly many outliers in the training data can lead to low accuracy because popular loss function like the Mean Squared Error (MSE) assign very high importance to large errors. The Mean Absolute Error (MAE) loss assigns equal importance to all errors and is most robust to outliers, but suffers from being non-differentiable at the origin. MAE also has large derivative values close to its minimum leading to instability and oscillations during training. Thus differentiable approximations to MAE namely Huber and Log-Cosh losses were introduced for robust regression tasks. This paper introduces two new infinitely differentiable loss functions that more closely approximate the MAE loss and provide improved performance on regression tasks with significantly many outliers in the training dataset. A comparison of the performance of regression models with different loss functions on a wide variety of benchmarks and datasets is presented to demonstrate the superior performance of the Square Root Loss (SRL) and Smooth Mean Absolute Error (SMAE) losses proposed in this paper. The SRL loss is shown to be strictly convex and the SMAE loss is shown to be strictly quasi-convex. Given the fundamental importance of linear regression, two new robust linear regression models are presented.

</details>


### [704] [Frequency-Domain Neural ODEs for Modeling Non-Linear Dynamical Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22075)
*Mohammed Ashraf,Ayman A. El-Badawy*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces the Frequency-domain Neural ODE (FNODE), which uses Fast Fourier Transform to project dynamics into the frequency domain, achieving better generalization and convergence stability than standard NODEs and discrete models (GRUs, LSTMs) across four dynamical systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard Neural ODEs struggle with highly nonlinear dynamics when applied to complex dynamical systems, motivating the need for an architecture that can better generalize to such systems.

Method: The FNODE architecture projects continuous temporal dynamics into the frequency domain using the Fast Fourier Transform (FFT), enabling frequency-domain learning. It is evaluated against GRUs, LSTMs, ANODE, and other continuous-depth variants on four dynamical systems using curriculum and ensemble learning for robustness assessment.

Result: FNODE achieves better generalization and remarkable convergence stability compared to discrete models (GRUs, LSTMs) and other continuous-depth variants (ANODE) across all four dynamical systems tested.

Conclusion: Operating in the frequency domain via FFT provides significant advantages for modeling complex nonlinear dynamical systems, with FNODE demonstrating superior generalization and convergence properties.

Abstract: Standard continuous-depth models, such as Neural Ordinary Differential Equations (NODEs), offer significant advantages in modeling physical systems by learning continuous vector fields rather than discrete temporal steps. However, when applied to complex dynamical systems, standard NODEs frequently struggle with highly nonlinear dynamics. This paper investigates the Frequency-domain Neural ODE (FNODE), an architecture that projects continuous temporal dynamics into the frequency domain using the Fast Fourier Transform (FFT). By operating in the frequency domain, the model provides better generalization to the dynamical system. The architecture is empirically evaluated against discrete models, specifically Gated Recurrent Units (GRUs) and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTMs), and other continuous-depth variants, including Augmented Neural ODE (ANODE), across four distinct dynamical systems: the Lotka-Volterra model, the forced Duffing oscillator, the Van der Pol oscillator, and the Lorenz system. To rigorously assess generalization and robustness, curriculum and ensemble learning are used to evaluate the model's convergence by estimating confidence intervals across different ensemble models. The empirical results demonstrate that the FNODE architecture achieves better generalization while exhibiting remarkable convergence stability.

</details>


### [705] [OphthaDT: Generative Digital Twins for Forecasting Visual Acuity Trajectories in Ophthalmology](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22101)
*Pietro Belligoli,Nikita Makarov,Sayedali Shetab Boushehri,Fabian Schmich,Raul Rodriguez-Esteban,Michael Menden*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OphthaDT is an LLM-based digital twin for ophthalmology that serializes longitudinal clinical data into structured narratives to forecast BCVA, outperforming baselines in nAMD (6% MAE reduction) and showing competitive results in DME, while handling irregular sampling without imputation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Precision medicine in ophthalmology requires accurate longitudinal predictions, but fragmented multimodal clinical data remains a barrier to forecasting.

Method: OphthaDT serializes longitudinal patient histories from 3,220 patients across four Phase III clinical trials into structured narratives, then uses LLM-based modeling to forecast best corrected visual acuity (BCVA).

Result: In nAMD, OphthaDT achieved average MAE reduction of 6.0% vs. all baselines. In DME, it outperformed Random Forest (2.6% MAE reduction) and XGBoost (6.9% MAE reduction) while being competitive overall. Predictive advantage scales with trajectory complexity.

Conclusion: LLM-based clinical trajectory modeling (OphthaDT) effectively handles irregular sampling without imputation, reduces patient burden, and could accelerate drug development in ophthalmology.

Abstract: Precision medicine in ophthalmology requires accurate longitudinal predictions, but the fragmented nature of multimodal clinical data remains a barrier to forecasting. We introduce OphthaDT, an LLM-based digital twin for ophthalmology that serializes longitudinal patient histories from 3,220 patients across four Phase III clinical trials into structured narratives to forecast best corrected visual acuity (BCVA). In benchmarks spanning up to 100 weeks, OphthaDT demonstrated the lowest prediction error in neovascular age-related macular degeneration (nAMD), achieving an average mean absolute error (MAE) reduction of 6.0% compared to all baselines. In diabetic macular edema (DME), OphthaDT demonstrated competitive performance against all baselines while outperforming Random Forest and XGBoost by an average MAE reduction of 2.6% and 6.9%, respectively. Results reveal that OphthaDT's predictive advantage scales with trajectory complexity: whereas linear models remain effective for the more stable treatment responses of DME, OphthaDT's capacity is better suited for capturing the high longitudinal variability of nAMD. Finally, OphthaDT handles irregular sampling without imputation, positioning LLM-based clinical trajectory modeling as a methodology that could reduce patient burden and accelerate drug development.

</details>


### [706] [Meta-Reinforcement Learning via Evolution for Multi-Objective Combinatorial Supply Chain Optimisation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22146)
*Rifny Rachman,Bahrul Ilmi Nasution,Josh Tingey,Richard Allmendinger,Pradyumn Shukla,Wei Pan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A population-based Meta-RL framework combining decomposition with evolutionary search in weight space produces more diverse Pareto front approximations and improves hypervolume by up to 32% over baseline methods in multi-objective supply chain optimisation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional few-shot meta-RL methods fine-tune from a single shared meta-policy, reducing solution diversity and limiting Pareto front exploration in high-dimensional combinatorial problems like supply chain optimisation.

Method: Maintains a population of weight vectors, each associated with a distinct meta-policy trained via gradient-based meta-learning, and iteratively refines the population through elitist selection, crossover, and mutation guided by hypervolume and entropy contributions.

Result: Yields more diverse and better distributed Pareto front approximations; improves cross-task adaptation; increases hypervolume by up to 32% over Meta-MORL; achieves the lowest average Hausdorff distance among all compared methods.

Conclusion: The proposed population-based approach effectively addresses diversity and exploration limitations of single-policy meta-RL for multi-objective optimisation, demonstrated in supply chain and standard RL benchmarks.

Abstract: Meta-reinforcement learning is a promising approach to multi-objective optimisation because it enables rapid policy adaptation across changing environments and preference settings. However, conventional few-shot methods usually fine-tune from a single shared meta-policy, which can reduce solution diversity and limit exploration of the Pareto front, especially in high-dimensional combinatorial problems such as supply chain optimisation. We propose a population-based Meta-reinforcement learning framework that combines decomposition with evolutionary search in scalarisation weight space. The framework maintains a population of weight vectors, each associated with a distinct meta-policy trained through gradient-based meta-learning, and iteratively refines this population through elitist selection, crossover, and mutation guided by hypervolume and entropy contributions. We evaluate the method in a multi-objective supply chain setting with conflicting economic, environmental, and social goals, and further test its generality on standard reinforcement learning problems. The results show that the proposed approach yields more diverse, better distributed Pareto front approximations, improves cross-task adaptation, increases hypervolume by up to 32\% over Meta-multi-objective reinforcement learning in the complex case, and attains the lowest average Hausdorff distance among all compared methods.

</details>


### [707] [Parameterized Representations via Implicit Stochastic Modulation for High-Dimensional and High-Order Neural PDE Solvers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22150)
*Zhangyong Liang,Huanhuan Gao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PRISM is a plug-and-play framework that decouples physical parameters from high-order AD graphs in stochastic neural PDE solvers, enabling unbiased zero-shot generalization across parameters with reduced memory and variance, scaling to 100,000 dimensions on a single GPU.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing stochastic derivative estimators for high-dimensional/high-order PDEs require retraining for each new physical parameter because direct conditional parameterization entangles parameters with the AD graph, causing memory growth and variance amplification.

Method: PRISM uses a hyper-generator that maps physical parameters to affine modulators (scale/shift) acting on a spatial latent manifold, keeping parameter branches value-connected but spatial-tangent-disconnected to remove the parameter encoder from high-order spatial AD. It provides a variance-aware Lipschitz envelope over parameter space.

Result: PRISM-STDE and PRISM-SDGD achieve stable zero-shot generalization, reduced memory usage, scalability to 100,000 dimensions on a single GPU, and efficient low-rank SVD adaptation for unseen parameters on nonlinear parameterized PDEs.

Conclusion: The framework proves parameterized unbiasedness, estimation-error bounds, and convergence under bounded stochastic variance, offering a scalable and efficient solution for parametric PDE families without per-parameter retraining.

Abstract: Solving high-dimensional and high-order PDEs is challenged by the coupled growth of spatial dimensionality and derivative order. Recent stochastic derivative estimators reduce this cost by replacing full derivative tensors with randomized dimension or Taylor estimators, but they are mostly designed for fixed physical parameters and require retraining for each new parameter. We show that direct conditional parameterization of such solvers entangles physical parameters with the high-order automatic differentiation graph, causing extra memory growth and parameter-induced variance amplification. We propose Parameterized Representations via Implicit Stochastic Modulation (PRISM), a plug-and-play framework for parameterized high-dimensional and high-order stochastic neural PDE solvers. PRISM uses a hyper-generator to map physical parameters to affine modulators that scale and shift a purely spatial latent manifold, while keeping parameter branches value-connected but spatial-tangent-disconnected. This design preserves unbiased stochastic dimension and Taylor estimators, removes the parameter encoder from high-order spatial AD, and provides a variance-aware Lipschitz envelope over the parameter space. We prove parameterized unbiasedness, estimation-error bounds, and convergence under bounded stochastic variance. Experiments with PRISM-STDE and PRISM-SDGD on nonlinear parameterized PDEs show stable zero-shot generalization, reduced memory usage, and scalability up to 100,000 dimensions on a single GPU, with efficient low-rank SVD adaptation for unseen parameters.

</details>


### [708] [Drowning in Routine: Signal Dilution in Multi-Turn Agent Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22164)
*Yann Pernot,Vi Retault*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The performance gap between turn-level and trajectory-level credit assignment in multi-turn agents is governed by decision density ρ (fraction of actions affecting return). Low decision density causes signal dilution for trajectory-level methods like GRPO, with SNR scaling as ρ^{-1/2} under controlled critic error. At high ρ, trajectory-level methods remain competitive without a critic. Empirical validation achieves R²=0.999 fit to predicted scaling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand why trajectory-level credit assignment in multi-turn agents is challenging—specifically to distinguish whether the difficulty stems from long horizons or from the density of decisions that actually affect the return, and to characterize the signal-to-noise ratio trade-off.

Method: The paper analyzes the signal-to-noise ratio of turn-level vs. trajectory-level estimators (e.g., GRPO) under different decision densities ρ. It derives theoretical scaling of SNR as ρ^{-1/2} assuming controlled critic error, and validates predictions in a controlled environment with tunable ρ.

Result: The predicted SNR scaling of ρ^{-1/2} is empirically recovered with R²=0.999 fit accuracy. The training-step gap between trajectory-level and turn-level methods significantly widens as ρ approaches zero, confirming low decision density as the primary source of difficulty.

Conclusion: Decision density ρ, not horizon length per se, governs the difficulty of trajectory-level credit assignment. Low ρ creates signal dilution (high variance, low expected signal) for trajectory-level estimators. At high ρ, trajectory-level methods remain viable without a critic. This reframes the credit assignment problem in multi-turn agent settings.

Abstract: Multi-turn agents interleave consequential decisions with routine execution: some actions change the downstream return distribution, while others are necessary but reward-equivalent. The cost of trajectory-level credit assignment, often attributed to long horizons, is in fact governed by decision density $ρ$: the fraction of turns whose actions affect the return. When decision density is low, routine turns create signal dilution: they add gradient variance to trajectory-level estimators such as GRPO without adding expected signal. Under explicit assumptions, the resulting turn-level to trajectory-level signal-to-noise ratio scales as $ρ^{-1/2}$, provided critic error remains controlled. The same analysis identifies the complementary regime: at high decision density, trajectory-level methods can remain competitive while avoiding the cost of a critic. In a controlled environment where $ρ$ is exactly tunable, the predicted scaling is recovered with $R^2 = 0.999$, and the training-step gap widens significantly as $ρ\to 0$.

</details>


### [709] [Early-Exit Graph Neural Networks for Link Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22167)
*Roman Knyazhitskiy,Andrea Giuseppe Di Francesco*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes an early-exiting strategy for Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) to improve inference speed for link prediction without sacrificing prediction quality, demonstrating improved Pareto frontiers on the HeaRT benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While GNNs are effective for link prediction, the speed/quality tradeoff has been barely studied; inference latency limits real-world large-scale applicability despite being negligible on small benchmarks.

Method: The authors apply early-exiting strategies to GNNs for link prediction without auxiliary losses, allowing early exiting to emerge as an implicit property of the architecture.

Result: The method enables early exiting in multiple setups and moves the Pareto frontier on the HeaRT benchmark for GCN and SAS-GNN backbones, improving inference speed with little or no loss in prediction quality.

Conclusion: Inference speed of GNNs on link-prediction problems can be improved while maintaining or even improving prediction quality using implicit early-exiting strategies.

Abstract: Graph Neural Networks are great for link prediction in various network-like structures; however, the question of their speed/quality tradeoff has been barely studied. While in practice the time it takes to do inference matters little for small benchmarks, the latency does limit applicability in large-scale domains. In this work, we explore early-exiting strategies that can be applied to Graph Neural Networks to solve the problem of link-prediction faster. We use no auxiliary losses to enforce early exiting, allowing it to emerge as an implicit property of the architecture. We show that our method enables early exiting in several setups, moving the Pareto frontier on the HeaRT benchmark for GCN and SAS-GNN backbones. Our findings show that inference speed of GNNs on many link-prediction problems can be improved, while losing little, or even winning in terms of prediction quality. The code is available in our repository: https://github.com/knyazer/link_prediction.

</details>


### [710] [Beyond Time Series: Spatial Reasoning for Epidemic Forecasting via Multimodal Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22171)
*Diana Guadalupe Gomez,Chenwei Wu,Zhiyi Wang,Liyue Shen,Alexander Rodríguez*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: M-SPICE is a structure-aware spatiotemporal forecasting framework that integrates region-level temporal surveillance data with misaligned spatial auxiliary signals via attention-based multimodal fusion, consistently outperforming state-of-the-art baselines on COVID-19, influenza, and ILI forecasting tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Epidemic forecasting models typically treat administrative regions as atomic units, ignoring sub-regional spatial structure that shapes disease dynamics; this work aims to incorporate spatially localized auxiliary signals despite their misalignment in resolution and structure with traditional surveillance data.

Method: M-SPICE (Multimodal SPatIal Context for Epidemic Forecasting) uses attention-based multimodal fusion to jointly reason over temporal disease dynamics and spatial context, allowing spatial signals to selectively condition temporal representations across forecast horizons.

Result: Consistently outperforms state-of-the-art multivariate time-series, multimodal, and epidemiological forecasting baselines on real-world COVID-19, influenza, and ILI forecasting tasks under realistic real-time evaluation protocols, with strong probabilistic forecasting performance.

Conclusion: Incorporating spatially structured auxiliary signals improves epidemic forecasting; interpretability analyses reveal when, where, and how spatial signals are leveraged, highlighting failure cases of purely temporal region-aggregated models.

Abstract: Epidemic forecasting models typically rely on surveillance data reported over administrative regions, treating them as atomic units, thereby obscuring sub-regional spatial structure that shapes disease dynamics. We introduce a spatially structured multimodal epidemic forecasting setting that integrates region-level temporal surveillance data with spatially localized auxiliary signals that are misaligned in resolution and structure, reflecting realistic public health reporting constraints. Building on this formulation, we propose M-SPICE (Multimodal SPatIal Context for Epidemic Forecasting), a structure-aware spatiotemporal forecasting framework that performs joint reasoning over temporal disease dynamics and spatial context via attention-based multimodal fusion, allowing spatial signals to selectively condition temporal representations across forecast horizons. We evaluate our approach on real-world COVID-19, influenza, and influenza-like illness (ILI) forecasting tasks under realistic real-time evaluation protocols. Across all forecasting settings, our method consistently outperforms state-of-the-art multivariate time-series, multimodal, and epidemiological forecasting baselines while maintaining strong probabilistic forecasting performance. Finally, interpretability analyses reveal when, where, and how spatial signals are leveraged, highlighting settings in which purely temporal, region-aggregated models are most likely to fail.

</details>


### [711] [Gated MLPs as Symmetry-Broken Rank-1 Bilinear Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22172)
*Nathan Breslow*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper reinterprets gated MLPs as a rank-1 approximation of bilinear attention, where moving nonlinearity onto one factor breaks exchange and inverse-scaling symmetries, potentially explaining their practical effectiveness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a theoretical understanding of why gated MLPs are empirically effective in modern architectures, and to bridge the conceptual gap between MLPs and attention mechanisms.

Method: The authors mathematically demonstrate that a conventional gated MLP corresponds to a rank-1 approximation of a bilinear attention mechanism, where the two factors act as query and key. They analyze the symmetry-breaking effects of placing nonlinearity on only one factor.

Result: Gated MLPs break exchange symmetry (between query and key factors) and, for non-homogeneous activations, also break inverse-scaling symmetry, which distinguishes them from full bilinear attention.

Conclusion: This theoretical framing offers a novel perspective on the effectiveness of gated MLPs and may guide future architecture design by clarifying the essential symmetry properties.

Abstract: We show that the conventional gated MLP can be viewed as a rank-1 approximation to a bilinear attention mechanism with two distinct factors corresponding to the query and the key. We further show that moving the nonlinearity onto one factor breaks the exchange symmetry between the two factors and, for non-homogeneous activations, the inverse-scaling symmetry as well. This perspective may help explain why gated MLPs are effective in practice and inform the design of future architectures.

</details>


### [712] [Residue-Level Attributions in Protein Language Models Do Not Recover Allergen Epitopes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22181)
*Jianzhou Yao,Anxiong Song,Katja Baerenfaller,Damir Zhakparov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a benchmark to evaluate attribution faithfulness in protein allergenicity models, finding that explanations from ESM-2 and DeepPlantAllergy align poorly with annotated epitopes and likely capture physicochemical features rather than immunological mechanisms, cautioning against their use in safety screening without quantitative validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep allergenicity classifiers are increasingly used in food safety screening, but whether their explanation signals capture biologically meaningful (epitope-level) information remains unclear, motivating the need for a quantitative evaluation of attribution faithfulness.

Method: The authors introduce an epitope-grounded residue-level benchmark to evaluate attribution faithfulness. They test frozen ESM-2, multi-task ESM-2, and DeepPlantAllergy, computing AUROC, AUPRC, and Precision@k for residue-level alignment with annotated epitopes, and also use Integrated Gradients and saturation mutagenesis analyses.

Result: Protein-level classification was robust across models, but classification-head explanation signals did not significantly exceed random in residue-level epitope alignment. Integrated Gradients identified functionally important residues that did not overlap annotated epitopes. Saturation mutagenesis suggested reliance on physicochemical/compositional features rather than epitope-specific mechanisms.

Conclusion: Residue-level importance signals from current allergenicity classifiers should not be interpreted as immunological explanations for safety screening or hypoallergen design without quantitative validation.

Abstract: Deep allergenicity classifiers are increasingly used in safety screening of novel foods, and recent protein language models have substantially improved protein-level allergenicity prediction. However, whether their explanations capture biologically meaningful information remains unclear. We introduce an epitope-grounded residue-level benchmark for quantitatively evaluating attribution faithfulness in protein allergenicity models. Across frozen ESM-2, multi-task ESM-2, and DeepPlantAllergy, protein-level classification was robust, yet classification-head explanation signals did not significantly exceed random in their residue-level alignment with annotated epitopes across AUROC, AUPRC, and Precision@k. Integrated Gradients identified residues that were functionally important to the model, but not overlapping annotated epitopes. Saturation mutagenesis further suggested classifiers may rely on physicochemical and compositional sequence features rather than epitope-specific mechanisms. Residue-level importance signals should therefore not be interpreted as immunological explanations for safety screening or hypoallergen design without quantitative validation. Code available: https://github.com/Jeffateth/XAllergen2.0-paper

</details>


### [713] [Bayesian Adaptation Gym: A Benchmark for the Bayesian Low-Rank Adaptation of Multi-Modal Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22188)
*Colin Samplawski,Ramneet Kaur,Manoj Acharya,Anirban Roy,Adam D. Cobb*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BAG is an open-source benchmark for evaluating Bayesian adaptation methods in large multi-modal language models, providing standardized datasets, tasks, and reference implementations to assess calibration, robustness, and decision-making under uncertainty.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bayesian adaptation methods for large multi-modal language models lack a standardized benchmark, making it unclear where these approaches provide meaningful benefits over alternatives.

Method: The benchmark (BAG) provides reference implementations of classic Bayesian baselines and state-of-the-art adaptation methods, along with a multi-modal dataset and task suite designed to probe calibration, robustness under distribution shift, and active learning for decision-making under uncertainty.

Result: Extensive experiments across model sizes, datasets, and tasks highlight both successes and failures of current Bayesian adaptation approaches.

Conclusion: BAG enables standardized evaluation and further research into Bayesian adaptation for multi-modal language models, with all code open-sourced.

Abstract: Large multi-modal language models are increasingly deployed in high-stakes domains, making well-calibrated uncertainty essential. Traditional Bayesian methods approximate posteriors over all model weights, which becomes intractable for modern large models. For this reason, recent work instead considers Bayesian low-rank adaptation to enable tractable posterior approximation. Due to a lack of a standardized benchmark to evaluate these approaches, it remains unclear where these methods provide meaningful benefits. To fill this gap, we introduce Bayesian Adaptation Gym (BAG), a benchmark for the Bayesian adaptation of multi-modal language models. BAG provides reference implementations of classic Bayesian baselines and state-of-the-art adaptation methods, along with a multi-modal dataset and task suite designed to probe calibration, robustness under distribution shift, and decision-making under uncertainty via active learning. Using BAG, we conduct and report extensive experiments across model sizes, datasets, and tasks to highlight the successes and failures of current Bayesian adaptation approaches. To enable further research, BAG is fully open source: https://github.com/SRI-CSL/BayesAdapt.

</details>


### [714] [L20-Edu-135M: An Auditable Single-GPU Study of Data-Efficient Small Language Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22189)
*Yin Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A resource-constrained 134.5M-parameter language model (L20-Edu-135M) trained on a single NVIDIA L20 GPU with ~13B tokens achieves 87.1% of SmolLM-135M's performance using only 2.17% of its training tokens, while also documenting a failure mode of GRPO-style RLVR on GSM8K.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore and document a sharply resource-constrained LLM training regime (single GPU, limited tokens) as an auditable case study, challenging the assumption that strong small models require hundreds of billions of tokens on large clusters.

Method: Trains a 134.5M-parameter model on one NVIDIA L20 GPU with 13B tokens (10B FineWeb-Edu + 3B edu/math/code/reasoning mix), employing cross-source MinHash/LSH deduplication, segment deduplication, benchmark-overlap removal, throughput optimization, SFT with weight interpolation, and GRPO-style RLVR on GSM8K.

Result: L20-Edu-135M achieves mean zero-shot score 0.4150 across six tasks, reaching 87.1% of SmolLM-135M's score (0.4767) with only 2.17% of its training tokens. RLVR on GSM8K reduced accuracy from 1.82% to 1.59% (192-token) and 1.21% (320-token completions), revealing a concrete failure mode.

Conclusion: The paper presents a reproducible, resource-constrained case study rather than a state-of-the-art claim, demonstrating feasible training under extreme hardware/data limits and identifying specific RLVR failure patterns without claiming general lower bounds.

Abstract: Small language models are cheap to serve and feasible on local hardware, but strong public 135M-class systems are commonly trained with hundreds of billions to trillions of tokens on large clusters. We study a sharply resource-constrained regime: a complete 134.5M-parameter language-model pipeline executed on one NVIDIA L20 GPU. The released checkpoint, L20-Edu-135M, receives approximately 13B pretraining tokens: 10B FineWeb-Edu tokens followed by a 3B-token educational, mathematics, code, and reasoning mixture. We document the architecture, data gates, cross-source MinHash/LSH near-deduplication, segment deduplication, benchmark-overlap removal, throughput optimization, supervised fine-tuning (SFT) with weight interpolation, and reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) on GSM8K. In a self-run zero-shot six-task harness, L20-Edu-135M obtains a mean score of 0.4150. It trails SmolLM-135M (0.4767) and SmolLM2-135M (0.4917), but its mean is 87.1% of SmolLM-135M's while its nominal token count is 2.17% as large. This ratio is descriptive, not evidence of statistical equivalence or a controlled scaling law. The model exceeds several older 100M-160M public baselines under the same harness. Direct GRPO-style RLVR decreases GSM8K exact-match accuracy from 1.82% to 1.59% (192-token completions) and 1.21% (320-token completions). These single-run results identify a concrete failure mode rather than establishing a general lower bound on RLVR. The contribution is an auditable resource-constrained case study, not a state-of-the-art claim.

</details>


### [715] [Neural Conjugate Aggregation: Identifiable Unsupervised Multi-Sensor Regression under Heterogeneous Sensor Bias](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22200)
*Muhammed Faruk Aytin,Zehra Demir,Alper Ünal,Julian Marshall,Gözde Ünal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces NCAM, a hierarchical Bayesian framework combining neural networks with conjugate Gaussian inference for unsupervised multi-source data fusion, resolving non-identifiability via sensor anchoring and providing calibrated uncertainty via Monte Carlo conformal prediction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Regression-based data fusion under uncertainty where multiple noisy/biased measurement sources are available but ground-truth labels are absent during training, which is common in sensor networks, simulation ensembles, and scientific monitoring systems where supervision is costly or infeasible.

Method: Neural Conjugate Aggregation Model (NCAM): a hierarchical Bayesian framework that combines neural networks with conjugate Gaussian inference for unsupervised multi-source fusion, using sensor anchoring and variance regularization to resolve structural non-identifiability, and integrating locally adaptive Monte Carlo conformal prediction for finite-sample coverage guarantees.

Result: Improved predictive accuracy and well-calibrated uncertainty compared to unsupervised baselines (mean aggregation, probabilistic PCA, and Kalman filtering) on synthetic and real-world air-quality datasets.

Conclusion: NCAM provides a principled approach to unsupervised multi-source fusion with tractable posterior inference, decomposed uncertainty (epistemic and aleatoric), and finite-sample coverage guarantees.

Abstract: We study regression-based data fusion under uncertainty, where multiple noisy and biased measurement sources are available but ground-truth labels are absent during training. This setting arises in sensor networks, simulation ensembles, and scientific monitoring systems where supervision is costly or infeasible. We propose the Neural Conjugate Aggregation Model (NCAM), a hierarchical Bayesian framework that combines neural networks with conjugate Gaussian inference for unsupervised multi-source fusion. NCAM learns source-specific bias and reliability conditioned on contextual covariates, yielding an analytically tractable posterior over a latent target variable with decomposed epistemic and aleatoric uncertainty. Structural non-identifiability is resolved through sensor anchoring and variance regularization, enabling stable and interpretable posterior aggregation. To complement Bayesian uncertainty with finite-sample guarantees, we integrate locally adaptive Monte Carlo conformal prediction, producing heteroscedastic prediction intervals with coverage guarantees under exchangeability assumptions. Experiments on synthetic and real-world air-quality datasets demonstrate improved predictive accuracy and well-calibrated uncertainty compared to unsupervised baselines, including mean aggregation, probabilistic PCA, and Kalman filtering.

</details>


### [716] [Sequential Minimal Optimization Algorithm for One-Class Support Vector Machines With Privileged Information](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22210)
*Andrey Lange,Dmitry Smolyakov,Evgeny Burnaev*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes an SMO algorithm for one-class SVM with privileged information (OC-SVM+), establishing finite-time convergence and demonstrating superiority over interior point methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The missing brick in LUPI research has been a one-class SVM with privileged information (OC-SVM+); no SMO algorithm existed for this setting despite SMO methods existing for supervised SVM, unsupervised one-class SVM, and SVM+.

Method: Develops a Sequential Minimal Optimization (SMO) algorithm for OC-SVM+ with finite-time convergence guarantees.

Result: The proposed SMO algorithm significantly outperforms non-sequential (interior point) algorithms for training OC-SVM+; privileged information effectively affects the descriptive domain in original feature space.

Conclusion: The SMO algorithm for OC-SVM+ fills the gap in LUPI research, achieving superior performance over interior point algorithms in benchmark tests.

Abstract: One of the powerful techniques in data modeling is accounting for features that are available at the training stage, but are not available when the trained model is used to classify or predict test data -- the Learning Using Privileged Information paradigm (LUPI). Sequential Minimal Optimization (SMO) methods have been developed for supervised Support Vector Machines (SVM), unsupervised one-class SVM, and SVM with privileged information (SVM+). The missing brick in this research has long been a one-class SVM with privileged information (OC-SVM+). In this paper, we propose an SMO algorithm for OC-SVM+ that significantly outperforms non-sequential algorithms for training the OC-SVM+ model. Its finite-time convergence is established. The experiments show how privileged information affects a descriptive domain in the space of original features. Comparative benchmark tests demonstrate that our algorithm is superior over interior point algorithms.

</details>


### [717] [SamatNext v0.2-B: An Exploratory Study of RMS-Normalized Hybrid Decoders for Curriculum Retention in Small Code Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22248)
*Samat Zharassov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SamatNext v0.2-B, a 356M-parameter hybrid sequence decoder with Differential-Attention and DeltaNet-style layers, achieves 100% pass rate on a staged Python coding holdout and 98.8% retention of prior-stage behavior under sequential fine-tuning, outperforming a Transformer baseline (97.6% pass, 6% retention), though long-horizon early retention remains limited.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard autoregressive Transformer decoders suffer from catastrophic forgetting under sequential fine-tuning on shifting curriculum distributions, motivating the need for architectures with better retention/plasticity tradeoffs.

Method: SamatNext v0.2-B is a 356M-parameter hybrid sequence decoder that alternates Differential-Attention-style layers with DeltaNet-inspired simplified linear-state mixer layers using RMS normalization and output scale calibration. It is evaluated under a controlled staged Python code curriculum against a parameter-matched Transformer baseline.

Result: SamatNext v0.2-B achieves 100.0% pass rate on Stage 5 holdout, retains 98.8% of Stage 3 semantic behavior, and reaches 12.0% on Stage 2E early syntax holdout.
