The Transformer baseline reaches 97.6% on Stage 5 but retains only 6.0% of Stage 3 behavior. Both architectures remain weak on long-horizon early-stage retention.

Conclusion: The results demonstrate an altered retention/plasticity tradeoff favoring the hybrid architecture in this controlled setting, but do not constitute a general solution to catastrophic forgetting, as long-horizon retention remains challenging for both models.

Abstract: Standard autoregressive Transformer decoders can often exhibit substantial forgetting under sequential fine-tuning on shifting curriculum distributions. This technical report evaluates SamatNext v0.2-B, an experimental 356M-parameter hybrid sequence decoder that alternates Differential-Attention-style layers with DeltaNet-inspired simplified linear-state mixer layers using RMS normalization and output scale calibration. We study the model under a controlled staged Python code curriculum and compare it with a parameter-matched Transformer baseline. In this setting, SamatNext v0.2-B achieves a 100.0% pass rate on the controlled Stage 5 holdout while retaining 98.8% of adjacent Stage 3 semantic behavior and reaching 12.0% on the Stage 2E early syntax holdout. The strongest Transformer baseline reaches 97.6% on Stage 5 but retains only 6.0% of Stage 3 behavior. Both architectures remain weak on long-horizon early-stage retention, so the result should be interpreted as evidence of an altered retention/plasticity tradeoff in this controlled setting, not as a general solution to catastrophic forgetting. Code, model specifications, evaluation scripts, and result tables are provided for independent verification.

</details>


### [718] [On the Expressive Power of Weight Quantization in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22249)
*Shao-Qun Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper theoretically investigates weight quantization in LLMs, establishing that 1.58-bit is the limiting precision through universal approximation and expressive collapse properties, and shows that expressive capacity degrades polynomially as quantization bits decrease.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While weight quantization techniques for LLM compression and acceleration have been practically developed, the theoretical understanding of approximation properties and expressive power degradation as quantization bits decrease remains unclear.

Method: The authors provide a theoretical analysis establishing universal approximation and expressive collapse properties for weight-quantized LLMs, analyzing the relationship between quantization bit-width and model expressiveness.

Result: 1.58-bit is identified as the limiting precision for weight quantization. Expressive capacity degrades polynomially as the number of quantization bits decreases.

Conclusion: These theoretical findings provide a foundation for advancing weight quantization in scaling laws and offer insights for future model compression and inference acceleration research.

Abstract: In recent years, weight quantization that encodes the learnable parameters of large language models in an $n$-bit format has garnered significant attention due to its potential for model compression and inference acceleration. Many practical techniques have been developed; however, the theoretical understanding of many aspects, especially the approximation and degradation of expressive power as the number of quantization bits decreases, remains unclear. In this paper, we provide a theoretical investigation into the expressive capability of large language models relative to the number of quantization bits. We argue that 1.58-bit is the limiting precision for weight quantization by establishing the universal approximation and expressive collapse properties of weight-quantized models with respect to the number of quantization bits. Additionally, we confirm that weight quantization leads to expressive degradation, in which the expressive capacity of weight-quantized models degrades polynomially as the number of quantization bits decreases. These theoretical findings provide a solid foundation for advancing weight quantization in the context of scaling laws and shed insights for future research in model compression and inference acceleration.

</details>


### [719] [Evolving Spatial Weights for Cartographic Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22252)
*Gesiel R. Lopes,Roberto F. da Silva,Mellina Yamamura,Sergio H. V. L. de Mattos,Antonio M. Saraiva,Alexandre C. B. Delbem,Eric K. Tokuda*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper presents GIS-moGA, a multi-objective evolutionary framework for cartographic synthesis that automatically estimates layer weights by maximizing global spatial autocorrelation (Global Moran's I) and minimizing local spatial heterogeneity (LISA variance). It exploits 97.7% sparsity in queen contiguity matrices to reduce computational complexity from O(N²) to O(Nk), enabling scalable analysis. Evaluated on a 523-unit spatial epidemiology dataset from Araraquara, Brazil, across 64 experimental scenarios, the framework outperforms expert-derived AHP baselines with significant hypervolume gains and spatial coherence improvements (p < 0.001, Cliff's delta = 0.87).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cartographic synthesis (integrating multiple thematic data layers into a composite map) traditionally relies on expert-driven weighting schemes that are subjective. There is a need for a systematic, data-driven, and scalable multi-objective approach that leverages spatial autocorrelation structure to determine optimal layer weights.

Method: A bi-objective evolutionary framework (GIS-moGA) is developed that simultaneously maximizes global spatial structure (Global Moran's I) and minimizes local spatial heterogeneity (variance of LISA). Computational efficiency is achieved by exploiting 97.7% sparsity of queen contiguity matrices, reducing complexity from O(N²) to O(Nk). A 64-scenario experimental design with varying parameter settings is conducted on a spatial epidemiology dataset with N=523 units from Araraquara, Brazil.

Result: Higher mutation rates are critical for maintaining population diversity and preventing premature convergence in spatially autocorrelated fitness landscapes; crossover operators can disrupt geographically coherent structures. Compared to expert-derived AHP baselines, Pareto fronts show substantial hypervolume gains and significant improvements in spatial coherence (p < 0.001, Cliff's delta = 0.87).

Conclusion: The framework provides a systematic, data-driven, and scalable solution for geographic multi-criteria decision analysis, replacing subjective expert weighting with optimization grounded in spatial autocorrelation structure.

Abstract: The integration of multiple thematic data layers into a single composite map, known as the cartographic synthesis problem, is typically addressed through expert-driven weighting schemes. This study presents a multi-objective formulation of cartographic synthesis grounded in spatial autocorrelation structure. We develop a bi-objective evolutionary framework, GIS-moGA, that estimates layer weights by simultaneously maximizing global spatial structure, measured by Global Moran's I, and minimizing local spatial heterogeneity, measured by the variance of Local Indicators of Spatial Association (LISA). Because naive evaluation of spatial relationships requires O(N^2) operations, direct computation becomes impractical for larger datasets. We address this challenge by exploiting the 97.7% sparsity of queen contiguity matrices, reducing effective complexity to O(N k) and enabling scalable municipal-level analysis. The framework is evaluated on a high-dimensional spatial epidemiology dataset with N = 523 units from Araraquara, Brazil. A 64-scenario experimental design is used to examine evolutionary behavior across parameter settings. Results show that higher mutation rates are important for maintaining population diversity and preventing premature convergence in spatially autocorrelated fitness landscapes, where crossover operators can disrupt geographically coherent structures. Compared with expert-derived Analytic Hierarchy Process baselines, the resulting Pareto fronts show substantial hypervolume gains and significant improvements in spatial coherence (p < 0.001, Cliff's delta = 0.87). These findings provide a systematic and scalable framework for data-driven geographic multi-criteria decision analysis.

</details>


### [720] [From Handcrafted Features to Functional Edge Learning: Evolution of EEG Seizure Detection Frameworks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22258)
*Sepideh Kheirollahi,Mohammad Rasoul Roshanshah*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper reviews limitations of standard DL models for EEG-based seizure detection (poor interpretability, high data/compute demands) and proposes Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) as a paradigm shift that offers parameter efficiency, inherent interpretability, and robust performance under data scarcity, enabling transparent clinical EEG monitoring.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard DL models for EEG-based seizure detection, despite high accuracy, suffer from opacity (black-box), heavy data and annotation requirements, and high computational costs, preventing their transition from controlled experiments to routine clinical deployment and resource-constrained neuromodulation devices.

Method: This is a comprehensive review that systematically analyzes KANs as an alternative paradigm. KANs replace fixed activation functions in traditional neurons with flexible, learnable functions along network connections, bridging predictive accuracy and mathematical transparency.

Result: KAN architectures resolve traditional DL limitations by offering exceptional parameter efficiency, inherent interpretability for physician trust, and robust performance under data scarcity, positioning them as a fundamental paradigm shift for clinical EEG monitoring.

Conclusion: KANs are not merely an incremental algorithmic update but a fundamental paradigm shift necessary to actualize next-generation, patient-specific, and thoroughly transparent clinical EEG monitoring systems.

Abstract: Electroencephalogram (EEG) analysis remains the clinical gold standard for epilepsy diagnosis and seizure detection. While Deep Learning (DL) has significantly advanced automated EEG interpretation, its transition from controlled experimental settings to routine clinical deployment is severely bottlenecked by fundamental architectural flaws. Standard DL models operate as opaque black-boxes lacking clinical interpretability, demand massive amounts of balanced annotated data, and incur steep computational costs incompatible with resource-constrained wearable or implantable neuromodulation devices. This paper presents a comprehensive review of these prevailing limitations and explores Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) as a emerging paradigm for EEG-based seizure detection. By replacing the fixed activation functions of traditional neurons with flexible, learnable functions along the network's connections, KANs bridge the critical gap between predictive accuracy and mathematical transparency. We systematically analyze how KAN architectures resolve the shortcomings of traditional DL-based models by offering exceptional parameter efficiency, inherent interpretability for physician trust, and robust performance under data scarcity. Ultimately, this review establishes KANs not merely as an incremental algorithmic update, but as a fundamental paradigm shift necessary to actualize next-generation, patient-specific, and thoroughly transparent clinical EEG monitoring systems.

</details>


### [721] [Learning a Normal World Model for Few-Shot Boundary-Calibrated Abnormality Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22261)
*Weizhi Nie,Weichao Liu,Weijie Wang,Yuting Su*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a Hypergraph Entropic Normal-World Model (HENWM) for abnormality detection in complex systems under label scarcity. It learns normal behavior from abundant normal events, uses hypergraphs to capture high-order variable relations, and defines a composite entropy-aware energy score for anomaly detection. The model achieves strong zero-shot and few-shot results on NASA C-MAPSS (AUROC 0.9983 on FD004), and mechanistic validation tests confirm it encodes meaningful normal-world structure rather than superficial mappings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Two practical barriers exist in abnormality detection for complex systems: abnormal labels are scarce, and binary labels do not quantify the degree of departure from normal behavior. The authors aim to model the "normal world" from abundant normal data while using few abnormal examples only for boundary calibration.

Method: The model (Hypergraph Entropic Normal-World Model) represents multivariate sensor windows as context-conditioned hypergraphs, where hyperedges capture high-order relations among variable groups. Abnormality is defined via an entropy-aware normal-world energy combining three components: temporal prediction surprise, hypergraph consistency surprise, and latent normal-manifold departure.

Result: On NASA C-MAPSS turbofan benchmark, the model achieves strong zero-shot and few-shot performance across all four subsets, reaching AUROC 0.9983 on FD004 (the most complex setting). Mechanistic validation tests show the learned energy accepts unseen healthy engines, increases along degradation trajectories, and sharply penalizes context-mismatched cross-variable coupling breaks.

Conclusion: Normal-world energy can serve as an anomaly score, a graded risk measure, and a testable representation of normal system behavior under severe abnormal-label scarcity.

Abstract: Abnormality detection in complex systems faces two practical barriers: abnormal labels are scarce, and binary labels do not quantify how far an event has departed from normal behavior. We study a normal-world modeling formulation for this setting. Instead of learning a large and incomplete space of abnormal classes, the model learns the normal world from abundant normal events and uses a few abnormal examples only to calibrate the boundary of normality. We instantiate this idea as a Hypergraph Entropic Normal-World Model. The model represents multivariate sensor windows as context-conditioned hypergraphs, where hyperedges capture high-order relations among groups of variables. It then defines abnormality by an entropy-aware normal-world energy that combines temporal prediction surprise, hypergraph consistency surprise, and latent normal-manifold departure. On the NASA C-MAPSS turbofan degradation benchmark, the proposed full energy achieves strong zero-shot and few-shot performance across all four subsets and reaches AUROC 0.9983 on FD004, the most complex setting with multiple operating conditions and fault modes. Beyond standard detection metrics, we introduce mechanistic validation tests to probe whether the energy encodes normal-world structure rather than a superficial input-output mapping. The learned energy accepts unseen healthy engines, increases along degradation trajectories, and sharply penalizes context-mismatched cross-variable coupling breaks. These results suggest that normal-world energy can serve as an anomaly score, a graded risk measure, and a testable representation of normal system behavior under severe abnormal-label scarcity.

</details>


### [722] [SCENIC: Semantic-Conditioned Edge-Aware Neural Framework for Structured IoT Command Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22296)
*Luke Ztz Hu,Hongbing Lang,Songping Mai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SCENIC is an end-to-end framework for edge IoT command generation that treats natural-language-to-command mapping as a many-to-one structured output task, using sub-0.2B-scale transformer backbones with triplet-loss contrastive fine-tuning, pruning, and quantization. The best dense decoder-only model achieves 99.0% EM@1, while a pruned INT8 encoder-decoder preserves 91.0% EM@1 with 25.38% size reduction and up to 1.8x speedup on sparse encoder exports.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Edge IoT agents face constraints in memory, privacy, latency, and inference cost. Current smart-home solutions rely on API-level interfaces or cloud-based language models that are hard to deploy on edge devices.

Method: SCENIC is an end-to-end framework covering model architecture selection, Smart Home Instruct data generation, triplet-loss contrastive supervised fine-tuning, pruning and quantization, and deployment-oriented export, using sub-0.2B-scale transformer backbones.

Result: Best dense decoder-only model reaches 99.0% EM@1. Pruned INT8 encoder-decoder preserves 91.0% EM@1 and 99.0% EM@5 with 25.38% size reduction. TensorRT profiling shows up to 1.8x encoder-component speedup on NVIDIA 2:4 sparse exports.

Conclusion: The selected encoder-decoder deployment path can retain structured command accuracy under edge-oriented compression, with component-level hardware acceleration evidence, and the framework is open-sourced for reproducibility.

Abstract: Edge Internet of Things (IoT) agents are often constrained by memory capacity, privacy requirements, communication latency, and recurring inference cost. Current smart-home assistants commonly rely on API-level command interfaces or cloud-based language models that remain difficult to deploy on edge devices. This paper addresses edge IoT command generation as a many-to-one structured output task, where multiple natural-language instructions map to the same canonical command string for deterministic smart-home parsing. To support this setting, we propose Semantic-Conditioned Edge-Aware Neural Framework for Structured IoT Command Generation (SCENIC), an end-to-end framework covering model architecture selection, Smart Home Instruct data generation, triplet-loss contrastive supervised fine-tuning, pruning and quantization, and deployment-oriented export. We evaluate sub-0.2B-scale transformer backbones, which are, to the best of our knowledge, among the smallest language-model backbones studied for edge IoT structured command generation. On Smart Home Instruct-Bench, the strongest dense decoder-only row reaches 99.0% EM@1, while the encoder-decoder model retains stronger high-sparsity behavior. A representative pruned INT8 encoder-decoder export preserves 91.0% EM@1 and 99.0% EM@5 while reducing exported model size by 25.38%. TensorRT profiling of the NVIDIA 2:4 sparse encoder export further shows up to 1.8x encoder-component speedup, indicating that the selected encoder-decoder deployment path can retain structured command accuracy under edge-oriented compression while hardware acceleration evidence remains component-level. The SCENIC code and experimental artifacts are open sourced to support reproducibility.

</details>


### [723] [Encoder-Decoder Manifold Alignment for Idempotent Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22304)
*Dareen Alharthi,Abdul Waheed,Bhiksha Raj*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper identifies geometric mismatch between encoder and decoder manifolds as the root cause of idempotency failure in generative models, and proposes a training framework that aligns these manifolds to achieve stable, fixed-point mappings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing idempotent generative models fail to achieve exact fixed points, causing instability and drift under repeated application. The authors identify that this stems from a geometric mismatch between the latent manifolds learned by the encoder and decoder.

Method: A new training framework that explicitly aligns the encoder and decoder to learn consistent representations of the same underlying data manifold, thereby closing the geometric gap between their respective latent spaces.

Result: The proposed approach achieves significantly lower idempotency error and consistently regenerates identical outputs under repeated application compared to existing methods. It also improves identity preservation and information stability in generative editing tasks.

Conclusion: Enforcing idempotency via encoder-decoder manifold alignment leads to more stable, realistic, and controllable generative models for both image generation and image editing.

Abstract: Recently, several learning paradigms have been introduced to enforce idempotency in generative models. The goal is to ensure that repeated application of a model leaves samples unchanged once they lie on the target data manifold. In practice, however, many of these approaches fail to achieve exact fixed points, leading to instability and drift under repeated application. In this work, we argue that a key reason for this failure is a geometric mismatch between the manifolds learned by the encoder and decoder. The encoder projects inputs onto one latent manifold, while the decoder implicitly learns to reconstruct data from a different manifold. This discrepancy prevents the model from learning truly idempotent mappings. To address this issue, we propose a new training framework that explicitly closes this gap by forcing the encoder and decoder to learn consistent representations of the same underlying data manifold. By aligning the geometry of these components, our method encourages stable projections. Empirically, we show that our approach achieves significantly lower idempotency error and consistently regenerates identical outputs under repeated application, compared to existing methods. We demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework on both image generation and image editing tasks. Finally, we show that enforcing idempotency in this manner improves identity preservation and information stability, leading to more realistic and controllable generative editing models.

</details>


### [724] [Enhancing Protein Representation Learning via Manifold Restore Mixing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22307)
*Yizhou Dang,Chuang Zhao,Lianbo Ma,Guibing Guo,Xingwei Wang,Zhu Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes Manifold Restore Mixing (MRM), a data augmentation method for protein representation learning that mixes hidden representations of original and augmented protein data to restore disrupted structural information while maintaining diversity, using a sample difficulty scheduler to progressively increase training difficulty.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing perturbation- and sampling-based DA methods disrupt protein structure and function, while homology modeling tools reduce structural diversity. The paper aims to restore disrupted structure caused by DA while providing data with both original structure and diverse variations.

Method: MRM mixes hidden representations of original and augmented protein data (inspired by manifold mixup) to generate new samples that restore structural information lost in DA, combined with a sample difficulty scheduler that adjusts beta distribution in mixup for progressively challenging training samples.

Result: Comprehensive experiments on various PRL backbones and downstream tasks demonstrate effectiveness and generalization of MRM.

Conclusion: MRM effectively addresses the structure-defect issue in protein data augmentation by restoring lost structural information while introducing diverse variations, outperforming existing DA methods across multiple PRL tasks.

Abstract: Data augmentation (DA) has been proven to be an effective means for improving protein representation learning (PRL) by generating additional training samples. Although mainstream perturbation- and sampling-based augmentation methods can produce data containing sufficient variations, they carry the risk of disrupting the protein structure and function. Some crafted protein homology modeling tools can generate conformations, but reduce structural diversity. The above dilemmas lead us to a question: Can we restore the disrupted structure caused by DA operations, providing data with both the original structure and diverse variations? In this work, we first analyze and empirically reveal the structure defect and performance degradation issues of existing DA methods. Based on the findings, we propose a simple yet effective DA method, Manifold Restore Mixing (MRM), for protein representation learning. Specifically, inspired by manifold mixup, we mix the hidden representations of original and augmented protein data to generate new samples that restore structural information lost in DA while introducing diverse variations. Furthermore, we develop a sample difficulty scheduler that adjusts the beta distribution in mixup to provide models with progressively challenging mixed samples during training, which improves the final performance. Comprehensive experiments on various PRL backbones and downstream tasks demonstrate the effectiveness and generalization of our method. The complete code and weights will be released upon acceptance. We provide a implementation at https://github.com/KingGugu/MRM.

</details>


### [725] [Diffusion Integrated Gradients: Controllable Path Generation for Flexible Feature Attribution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22314)
*Soyeon Kim,Kyowoon Lee,Jaesik Choi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Diffusion Integrated Gradients (DiffIG) reformulates path-based attribution for explainable AI by training a diffusion model to generate attribution paths, enabling guided sampling and producing perceptually aligned explanations that match or outperform existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing path-based attribution methods like Integrated Gradients rely on fixed or hand-crafted paths, which often produce noisy or distorted attributions. The choice of attribution path significantly impacts explanation quality, but no flexible, learnable approach existed for path generation.

Method: DiffIG first trains a diffusion model to learn a distribution over paths generated from a Stick-Breaking Process, then employs guided sampling to embed user guidance during inference, reformulating path generation as a conditional generative modeling problem.

Result: Quantitatively matches or outperforms existing path-based attribution methods, achieving perceptually aligned explanations.

Conclusion: This work introduces a new generative perspective for flexible, inference-time controllable Explainable AI (XAI) methods, demonstrating that learnable path distributions can replace fixed paths for improved attribution quality.

Abstract: Path-based attribution methods such as Integrated Gradients (IG) are widely adopted for their strong axiomatic properties and effectiveness in attributing model predictions to input features by integrating gradients along a path from a baseline to the input. However, the choice of the attribution path largely affects the quality of explanations, and existing approaches rely on fixed or hand-crafted paths that often produce noisy or distorted attributions. To address this limitation, we propose Diffusion Integrated Gradients (DiffIG), a novel method that reformulates path generation as a conditional generative modeling problem. DiffIG first trains a diffusion model to learn a distribution over paths generated from a Stick-Breaking Process, then employs guided sampling to embed user guidance during the sampling procedure. We demonstrate that DiffIG quantitatively matches or outperforms existing path-based methods, achieving perceptually aligned explanations. This work introduces a new generative perspective for flexible, inference-time controllable Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) methods.

</details>


### [726] [Curriculum Reinforcement Learning Can Incentivize Reasoning Capacity in LLMs Beyond the Base Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22317)
*Pengxiang Cai,Tianchen Fang,Xiaohan Li,Qingyuan Zeng,Guocong Li,Jintai Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a boundary-aware Curriculum RL approach that improves both pass@1 and pass@k (k=256) scores by first locating the model's reasoning capacity boundary via pass@k sampling, applying teacher guidance to near-boundary examples, and then using RL to consolidate new reasoning patterns, achieving +9.8 pp over base models and +10.3 pp over Vanilla RLVR in pass@256.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mainstream RLVR often reallocates sampling probabilities among existing trajectories, improving pass@1 but offering limited gains (or even decreases) in pass@k for large k, thus failing to expand the base model's reasoning capacity boundary.

Method: A three-stage boundary-aware Curriculum RL approach: (1) locate current reasoning capacity boundary using pass@k sampling, (2) apply targeted teacher guidance to examples near or beyond that boundary, (3) use RL to consolidate newly introduced reasoning patterns.

Result: Across Qwen, Llama, and DeepSeek base models, pass@256 improves by 9.8 pp over base models and 10.3 pp over Vanilla RLVR, with concurrent improvements in pass@1.

Conclusion: Boundary-aware Curriculum RL provides a scalable route for LLMs to continuously improve beyond the base model's empirical reasoning capacity boundary.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) is widely viewed as a promising path toward continuously improving large language models. Recent works, however, suggest that mainstream RLVR often reallocates sampling probabilities among trajectories already present in the base model: it can improve sampling efficiency, reflected by higher pass@1 scores, but yields limited gains, and can even decrease pass@k scores when k is large, and therefore may fail to expand the base model's reasoning capacity boundary. In this paper, we present a boundary-aware Curriculum RL approach to move beyond the base model's reasoning capacity boundary. Our approach first uses pass@k sampling to locate the current reasoning capacity boundary, then applies targeted teacher guidance to examples near or beyond that boundary, and finally uses RL to consolidate the newly introduced reasoning patterns. Across Qwen, Llama, and DeepSeek base models, boundary-aware Curriculum RL improves both pass@1 scores and pass@256 scores, with pass@1 reflecting one-attempt performance and pass@256 serving as an empirical proxy for the reasoning capacity boundary. In our experiments, average pass@256 improves by 9.8 percentage points over the base models and by 10.3 percentage points over Vanilla RLVR. These results suggest that boundary-aware Curriculum RL can provide a scalable route for LLMs to continuously improve beyond the base model's empirical reasoning capacity boundary.

</details>


### [727] [All Routes Lead to Collapse](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22325)
*K. R. Balasubramanian*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Attention sinks, representation collapse, and norm stratification are not transformer-specific but arise from any content-based router using a fixed similarity metric ill-matched to its representations. Softmax attention equals Boltzmann-weighted aggregation over Euclidean distances but is blind to key magnitude (missing a -||k||² term). This blindness forces routers to compensate by concentrating routing and collapsing representations. Verified across diverse architectures (transformers, graph attention, SSMs, recurrent mixers, learned residuals) and within-model ablations, the signature is caused by routing mechanism, not incidental dynamics. The form (onset) is contingent on positional brake strength, but the mechanism is not; it does not require norm stratification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To show that phenomena previously considered specific to transformer attention (attention sinks, representation collapse, norm stratification) are actually general properties of any content-based routing mechanism that uses a fixed similarity metric poorly matched to its representations.

Method: Identifies that softmax attention is Boltzmann-weighted aggregation over Euclidean distances with constant key norms, but its score omits the -||k||² term, making it blind to key magnitude. Tests this prediction across diverse routers: softmax attention over tokens (9 pretrained transformers), graph attention over nodes, selective state-space model, recurrent mixer over time, and learned residuals over depth. Uses two within-model ablations to isolate routing mechanism as the cause.

Result: All tested architectures develop the same signature (concentration of routing and collapsed representations). The onset can be shifted by changing the positional brake strength. Norm-normalized keys still produce the same effect, confirming the mechanism does not require norm stratification.

Conclusion: The phenomena are not transformer-specific pathologies but general consequences of content-based routing with a fixed, norm-blind similarity metric. The geometric view is a diagnostic tool revealing the inadequacy of the flat metric, not a claim that models implement Riemannian geometry.

Abstract: Attention sinks, representation collapse, and norm stratification are treated as transformer-specific pathologies. We show they are not specific to attention: they are what content-based routing does under a fixed similarity metric. We give a reframing identity: softmax attention is Boltzmann-weighted aggregation over Euclidean distances with constant key norms, so its score omits a $-\|k\|^2$ term and is blind to key magnitude. This predicts that any router whose metric is ill-matched to its representations should compensate, by concentrating its routing and collapsing the routed representations. We test it on routers that score and aggregate over different axes: softmax attention over tokens (nine pretrained transformers), graph attention over nodes, a selective state-space model and a recurrent mixer over time, and learned residuals over depth. All develop the same signature, and two within-model ablations show it is caused by the routing mechanism rather than by incidental dynamics. The form is contingent, set by the strength of the positional brake each router carries alongside its content score; we sweep that brake and move the onset across its whole range. The mechanism is not contingent, and it does not require norm stratification: a router with norm-normalized keys concentrates just the same. We do not claim these models implement Riemannian geometry; the geometric view is a diagnostic that names the inadequacy of the flat, norm-blind metric.

</details>


### [728] [Select-to-Act: Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning via Adaptive Language Guidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22350)
*Hanping Zhang,Adam Koziak,Yuhong Guo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HRLLI is a hierarchical RL framework that decomposes natural-language instructions into piecewise guidance elements and uses a Select-to-Act paradigm—a high-level policy selects relevant instruction pieces to guide a low-level policy—improving sample efficiency and outperforming instruction-conditioned RL baselines on the RTFM benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RL methods that leverage external knowledge via natural-language instructions treat the entire instruction as a single conditioning input, failing to account for the stage-dependent nature of language guidance in complex environments, leading to poor sample efficiency.

Method: HRLLI decomposes instructions into piecewise guidance elements and proposes a Select-to-Act hierarchical policy: a high-level semantic policy selects the most relevant instruction piece given the current state, and a low-level policy executes environment actions conditioned on that selected guidance; both levels are learned simultaneously to maximize augmented expected returns.

Result: HRLLI consistently outperforms strong instruction-conditioned RL baselines on the instruction-intensive RTFM benchmark.

Conclusion: Explicitly modeling adaptive instruction selection significantly improves the effectiveness of RL in complex, instruction-guided environments.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning (RL) has been widely applied to sequential decision-making, yet it often suffers from poor sample efficiency due to costly interactions with the environment. A limited line of recent work has started exploring improving RL efficiency by leveraging external knowledge expressed in natural-language instructions. However, the few existing approaches typically treat the entire instruction as a single conditioning input, failing to account for the stage-dependent nature of language guidance, especially in complex environments. In this paper, we propose \emph{Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning with Language Instructions (HRLLI)}, a hierarchical RL framework that explicitly models natural-language instructions as dynamically selectable semantic guidance during decision-making. HRLLI decomposes instructions into a set of piecewise guidance elements, where each instruction piece may become relevant at different stages of interaction with the environment. A novel hierarchical RL policy structure is then formulated in a \emph{Select-to-Act} paradigm: a high-level semantic policy acts as a guidance selector that selects the most relevant instruction piece to the current state to guide the low-level agent's decision, while a low-level policy executes environment actions conditioned on the selected guidance. The two-level policies are learned simultaneously to maximize augmented expected returns from interactions with the environment. This design enables the agent to adaptively ground language instructions into stage-specific decisions during interaction. Experiments on the instruction-intensive RTFM benchmark show that HRLLI consistently outperforms strong instruction-conditioned RL baselines, demonstrating that explicitly modeling adaptive instruction selection significantly improves the effectiveness of RL.

</details>


### [729] [Reliability-Guided Adaptive Ensembling for Robust Test-Time Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22351)
*Adam Koziak,Yuhong Guo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SAFER is a training-free reliability-guided augmentation wrapper that improves the robustness of test-time adaptation methods against adversarial attacks in continuous test streams by replacing single-view predictions with correlation-weighted pooled predictions from stochastic augmentations, while maintaining competitive clean performance on PACS, VLCS, and OfficeHome benchmarks under PGD attacks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Test-time adaptation (TTA) is brittle under adversarially contaminated test streams, where corrupted inputs destabilize online updates. Robust test-time adaptation (RTTA) in the adversarial-stream setting remains underexplored relative to standard TTA.

Method: SAFER (Stochastic Augmentation Framework for Enhanced Robustness) is a training-free reliability-guided augmentation wrapper that replaces brittle single-view predictions with a reliability-guided pooled predictor. For each test sample, it generates stochastic augmentations and aggregates their predictions via correlation-weighted pooling with outlier detection. An adaptive-mixing extension adjusts original-versus-augmentation weighting using feature disagreement signals to improve clean-performance retention.

Result: Evaluated on PACS, VLCS, and OfficeHome under PGD attacks at various attack rates. SAFER improves resilience of TTA methods to adversarial attacks while maintaining competitive clean performance across all benchmarks.

Conclusion: SAFER effectively enhances robustness of TTA methods against adversarial test streams without requiring additional training, offering a practical plug-and-play solution for RTTA.

Abstract: Test-time adaptation (TTA) can mitigate domain shift without source data, but it is highly brittle under adversarially contaminated test streams, where corrupted inputs also destabilize online updates. We study robust test-time adaptation (RTTA) in the adversarial-stream setting, which remains comparatively underexplored relative to standard TTA, and propose SAFER (Stochastic Augmentation Framework for Enhanced Robustness), a training-free reliability-guided augmentation wrapper for RTTA. SAFER preserves the wrapped TTA objective while replacing brittle single-view predictions with a reliability-guided pooled predictor. For each test sample, SAFER generates stochastic augmentations and aggregates their predictions through correlation-weighted pooling with outlier detection. We further study an adaptive-mixing extension that improves clean-performance retention by adjusting original-versus-augmentation weighting using feature disagreement signals. We evaluate on PACS, VLCS, and OfficeHome under PGD attacks at various attack rates. Across benchmarks, SAFER improves resilience of TTA methods to adversarial attacks while maintaining competitive clean performance.

</details>


### [730] [On the Sparsity-Storage-Accuracy Tradeoff in Parsimoniously Activated Dictionary Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22352)
*Zihui Zhao,Yuanbo Tang,Yang Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper provides a probabilistic reinterpretation of parsimoniously activated dictionary learning (PADL), derives generalization guarantees and analytical tradeoffs between sparsity, storage, and reconstruction accuracy, and develops an efficient hyperparameter-free algorithm that improves reconstruction performance and accelerates vision-language model inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many structured dictionary learning methods with global regularization lack a clear generative probabilistic interpretation, making theoretical analysis difficult. The authors aim to bridge this gap by showing PADL admits a MAP estimation formulation under a structured generative model.

Method: The authors reformulate PADL as maximum a posteriori estimation under a probabilistic model with auxiliary latent variables governing global activation patterns. This connection enables deriving generalization bounds and an analytical tradeoff characterization, leading to a hyperparameter-free algorithm.

Result: The proposed algorithm eliminates manual hyperparameter tuning, achieves improved reconstruction performance under comparable sparsity levels on visual benchmarks, and demonstrates practical utility in accelerating inference for vision-language models.

Conclusion: PADL's probabilistic reinterpretation not only provides theoretical insights (generalization guarantees, analytical tradeoffs) but also yields practical algorithmic improvements and broader applicability to vision-language model acceleration.

Abstract: Dictionary learning has long been studied from both optimization and probabilistic perspectives. While formulations with element-wise sparsity regularization (e.g., L1-based sparse coding) admit well-established probabilistic interpretations, many structured variants that impose global constraints lack a clear and tractable generative view. In this paper, we revisit a class of practically effective yet theoretically under-explored dictionary learning methods that impose a simple global regularization on the number of activated dictionary atoms, which we term parsimoniously activated dictionary learning (PADL). We show that PADL admits an equivalent formulation as maximum a posteriori estimation under a structured generative model, with auxiliary latent variables that govern global activation patterns. This formulation allows us to derive generalization guarantees that are difficult to obtain under the original formulation. More importantly, it yields an analytical characterization of the tradeoff between sparsity, storage cost, and reconstruction accuracy, enabling data-driven estimation of optimal hyperparameters. Based on this connection, we develop an efficient and interpretable PADL algorithm that eliminates manual hyperparameter tuning, achieving improved reconstruction performance under comparable sparsity levels on visual benchmarks. We further demonstrate its practical utility in accelerating inference for vision-language models.

</details>


### [731] [Multigrid Training for Molecular Generation using Graph Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22377)
*Zixuan Ling,Paula Mercurio,Di Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces a multigrid training strategy that transfers parameters from low-resolution to high-resolution models across discretizations, accelerating learning and improving generalization for molecular graph and 3D generation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Computational cost for modeling biochemical molecular systems scales with resolution, making full graph/grid computation of molecular densities expensive and often unstable.

Method: The approach uses (1) biased random walk upsampling for graph molecular representations to transfer parameters from coarse to fine graphs, and (2) voxelization at multiple resolutions with coarse-resolution CVAE pretraining and parameter transfer to initialize fine-resolution CVAE for 3D molecular generation.

Result: On receptor-conditioned 3D ligand generation, multigrid training accelerates convergence and improves generalization compared to training from scratch.

Conclusion: Multigrid training across discretizations is an effective strategy to reduce computational cost and improve performance in molecular modeling tasks.

Abstract: Deep learning has demonstrated significant success for modeling biochemical molecular systems, where inputs are commonly represented as graphs or 3D grids. A major challenge is that computational cost scales with resolution, making full graph/grid computation of molecular densities expensive and often unstable. We introduce a multigrid training strategy that leverages low-resolution optimization to accelerate learning at higher resolution through parameter transfer across discretizations. For graph molecular representations, we progressively transfer parameters learned from a coarse graph to a sequence of increasingly finer graphs via biased random walk upsampling. For 3D molecular generation, we voxelize the molecular structures at multiple resolutions, pretrain a coarse-resolution conditional Variational Autoencoder (CVAE), and initialize a fine-resolution CVAE by transferring shape compatible convolutional parameters from the coarse model. Numerical experiments on receptor-conditioned 3D Ligand generation show that multigrid training accelerates convergence and improves generalization compared to training from scratch.

</details>


### [732] [Bypassing Minimization Bias: A Shift-Invariant Variance Estimator for Off-Equilibrium Local Learning Coefficients](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22389)
*Yingjia Cai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes SIVE, a variance-based estimator that eliminates the additive loss baseline problem in Singular Learning Theory's LLC measurements, enabling robust geometric diagnostics during off-equilibrium training phases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mean-energy LLC estimators depend explicitly on an unknown additive loss baseline (local minimum). During transient training phases, substituting it with noisy mini-batch loss induces a systematic minimization bias that distorts geometric measurement.

Method: SIVE (Shift-Invariant Variance Estimator) uses the variance operator to structurally eliminate the unknown additive baseline, combined with a Law of Total Variance correction to separate geometric fluctuations from mini-batch evaluation noise.

Result: On analytically tractable toy models, SIVE recovers expected finite-temperature geometric signals where anchored mean estimators fail. Applied to deep neural networks, it provides robust localized online diagnostics for tracking structural phase transitions.

Conclusion: SIVE offers a bias-free approach to measuring the Local Learning Coefficient during off-equilibrium training, enabling reliable geometric phase transition tracking without requiring knowledge of the local loss minimum.

Abstract: Singular Learning Theory leverages the Local Learning Coefficient (LLC) to quantify the geometry of neural network loss landscapes. However, mean-energy LLC estimators depend explicitly on an additive loss baseline, typically an estimate of the local minimum. During transient, off-equilibrium training phases, this minimum is unknown; substituting it with the lowest noisy mini-batch loss induces a systematic minimization bias that distorts the geometric measurement. In this paper, we propose the Shift-Invariant Variance Estimator (SIVE), a variance-based local LLC probe that structurally eliminates the unknown additive baseline through the variance operator. Combining this shift-invariant observable with an explicit correction derived from the Law of Total Variance, SIVE separates geometric loss fluctuations from mini-batch evaluation noise. Controlled experiments on analytically tractable toy models show that SIVE recovers the expected finite-temperature geometric signal in regimes where anchored mean estimators fail. Applied to deep neural networks, SIVE provides a robust, localized online diagnostic for tracking structural phase transitions throughout training.

</details>


### [733] [Asymptotic Signal Subspace Recovery in Softmax Attention Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22406)
*Lan V. Truong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper provides a rigorous theoretical analysis of how softmax-attention mechanisms learn to extract relevant signals from noisy data using stochastic gradient ascent, proving that the learned query converges to the latent signal subspace under high-dimensional scaling assumptions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite the empirical success of attention mechanisms, the theoretical principles underlying their ability to identify relevant information from large collections of tokens remain poorly understood.

Method: The authors study a stylized softmax-attention model with a query vector learned via stochastic gradient ascent from informative and nuisance tokens. They derive a population objective, characterize the limiting ODE governing learning dynamics, and use stochastic approximation and dynamical systems theory to connect the stochastic algorithm to its deterministic limit.

Result: Under suitable high-dimensional scaling assumptions and standard step-size conditions, the learned query converges almost surely to the one-dimensional signal subspace spanned by the latent informative direction, asymptotically recovering the latent signal up to sign ambiguity.

Conclusion: This work provides a rigorous theoretical foundation for understanding attention mechanisms as signal extraction procedures in high-dimensional noisy environments, offering a dynamical-systems perspective on how attention discovers relevant information amidst substantial noise.

Abstract: Attention mechanisms have demonstrated remarkable empirical success in identifying relevant information from large collections of tokens, yet the theoretical principles underlying this behavior remain poorly understood. We study a stylized softmax-attention model in which a query vector is learned by stochastic gradient ascent from a collection of informative and nuisance tokens. Exploiting the symmetry of the model, we derive a population objective and characterize the limiting ordinary differential equation governing the learning dynamics. Using tools from stochastic approximation and dynamical systems theory, we establish a rigorous connection between the stochastic learning algorithm and its deterministic limit. Our main result shows that, under suitable high-dimensional scaling assumptions and standard step-size conditions, the learned query converges almost surely to the one-dimensional signal subspace spanned by the latent informative direction. Equivalently, the query asymptotically recovers the latent signal up to the intrinsic sign ambiguity. These results provide a rigorous theoretical foundation for understanding attention mechanisms as signal extraction procedures in high-dimensional noisy environments and offer a dynamical-systems perspective on how attention discovers relevant information in the presence of substantial noise.

</details>


### [734] [QeHDC: Hyperdimensional Computing based on Quantum-enhanced binding and SuperClass Construction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22421)
*Yangjie Xu,Hui Huang,Li Ning,Radu State*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a Quantum Hyperdimensional Computing framework with one-pass training, sinusoidal/quantum encoding, reference-state-based quantum binding, and density-matrix-based superclass generation using eigenvalue decomposition, achieving superior classification performance and noise robustness on benchmark datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To extend classical Hyperdimensional Computing (HDC) into the quantum domain by leveraging quantum mechanical properties to enhance computational efficiency, accuracy, and robustness for classification tasks.

Method: (1) One-pass training method; (2) Sinusoidal and quantum encoding to project classical data into quantum amplitude states; (3) Reference-state-based quantum binding operation realized via quantum circuits; (4) Density-matrix-based superclass generation using eigenvalue decomposition for feature extraction.

Result: Superior performance, robustness to noise, and computational feasibility compared to classical HDC and existing quantum-enhanced approaches on standard benchmark datasets.

Conclusion: Quantum HDC shows practical benefits for quantum-enhanced classification tasks and paves the way for future quantum-inspired computational paradigms.

Abstract: Hyperdimensional Computing (HDC) is a robust computational framework inspired by human cognition characterized by simple and efficient operations within high-dimensional vector spaces. Quantum-enhanced Hyperdimensional Computing (QeHDC) extends classical HDC by leveraging quantum mechanical properties to enhance computational efficiency. In this paper, we propose a novel Quantum HDC framework featuring a one-pass training method, leveraging sinusoidal and quantum encoding to project classical data into quantum amplitude states efficiently. Our framework introduces an innovative reference-state-based quantum binding operation realized via quantum circuits. Furthermore, we propose a density-matrix-based superclass generation strategy employing eigenvalue decomposition to extract critical quantum state features effectively, enabling a more accurate and robust class representation. Experimental evaluations conducted on standard benchmark datasets demonstrate our approach's superior performance, robustness to noise, and computational feasibility compared to traditional classical and existing quantum-enhanced approaches. The results highlight the practical benefits and potential of Quantum HDC for quantum-enhanced classification tasks and pave the way for future advancements in quantum-inspired computational paradigms.

</details>


### [735] [Enhancing LLMs for Graph Tasks via Graph-aware LoRA Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22429)
*Junshu Sun,Wanxing Chang,Qingming Huang,Shuhui Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GaRA injects whole-graph information into language models via graph-conditioned low-rank weight updates, improving zero-shot graph learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing GNNs are dataset-specific with limited transferability, while LM-based methods struggle to encode whole-graph information, leading to information loss and suboptimal graph understanding.

Method: A weight-level information injection paradigm that generates task-specific low-rank weight updates (GaRA) conditioned on original graph structures, using LoRA-style adaptation with norm constraints to avoid optimization bias.

Result: GaRA consistently outperforms baselines on zero-shot graph learning tasks.

Conclusion: The proposed weight-level injection paradigm effectively encodes whole-graph information for LM adaptation, achieving superior graph understanding and cross-dataset transferability.

Abstract: Graph neural networks (GNNs) tightly couple their input-output parameters to dataset-specific feature spaces and target sets, exhibiting limited transferability across different datasets. In contrast, language models (LMs) generalize flexibly via a unified input-output interface, motivating recent attempts to adapt LMs to graph tasks. However, existing methods struggle to encode whole-graph information, leading to potential information loss and suboptimal graph understanding. In this work, we propose a novel weight-level information injection paradigm for adapting LMs to graph tasks. This paradigm injects whole-graph information by generating task-specific weight updates that interact directly with hidden representations. Instantiating this paradigm following low-rank adaptation (LoRA), we introduce GaRA, a Graph-aware LoRA generation model. GaRA constructs low-rank weight updates conditioned on the original graph structures and constrains the norm of the generated updates, thus injecting whole-graph information and avoiding the optimization bias in the weight generation. Empirical studies demonstrate that GaRA consistently outperforms baselines on zero-shot graph learning tasks.

</details>


### [736] [Escaping the Variance Trap: Jacobian-Free Dynamics for Root-Finding Bilevel Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22433)
*Zhiyu Li,Xi Xuan,Davide Carbone*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper identifies a "Variance Trap" in forcing stochastic root-finding problems into loss minimization via squared residuals, and proposes Root-Finding Bilevel Optimization (RF-BO) solved with Two-Time-Scale Stochastic Approximation (TTSA) to avoid variance amplification, achieving significant gains across SimCLR, ODE control, RL, and generative modeling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many central ML tasks (e.g., entropy tuning in RL, equilibrating GANs) are inherently stochastic root-finding problems, not loss minimization. Forcing them into minimization via squared residuals introduces a "Variance Trap" — noise amplification from implicit Jacobians in hypergradient estimation destabilizes convergence.

Method: Formalizes Root-Finding Bilevel Optimization (RF-BO) as a distinct problem class. Proposes a Jacobian-free solution using Two-Time-Scale Stochastic Approximation (TTSA) that updates directly along the root error, structurally avoiding variance amplification. Provides first non-asymptotic convergence guarantees under Markovian noise.

Result: 2.6% top-1 accuracy gain in SimCLR, 17× faster convergence in non-linear ODE control (where baselines fail), improved entropy stability in RL, and 11.1% quality improvement in generative modeling.

Conclusion: The RF-BO paradigm with TTSA decisively outperforms squared-residual and implicit-gradient baselines, offering a principled alternative for stochastic root-finding problems that avoids the Variance Trap.

Abstract: Many central machine learning tasks, from entropy tuning in reinforcement learning to equilibrating generative adversarial networks, are fundamentally stochastic root-finding problems rather than loss minimization. Yet, they are frequently forced into a minimization framework via squared residuals, introducing a critical flaw we identify as the Variance Trap. Standard bilevel minimization algorithms require estimating hypergradients involving implicit Jacobians; in stochastic settings, these terms act as noise amplifiers, destabilizing convergence. We formalize Root-Finding Bilevel Optimization (RF-BO) as a distinct problem class that bypasses this pathology. We propose a Jacobian-free solution using Two-Time-Scale Stochastic Approximation (TTSA) that updates directly along the root error, structurally avoiding variance amplification. We provide the first non-asymptotic convergence guarantees for TTSA in this setting under Markovian noise. Extensive experiments demonstrate the decisive advantage of this paradigm: compared to squared-residual and implicit-gradient baselines, our framework achieves a 2.6\% top-1 accuracy gain in SimCLR, 17$\times$ faster convergence in non-linear ODE control where baselines fail, significantly improved entropy stability in reinforcement learning, and an 11.1\% quality improvement in generative modeling.

</details>


### [737] [Distribution-Aware Robust Bilevel Optimization: Quantile-Guided Huber Updates in Two-Timescale Stochastic Approximation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22436)
*Zhiyu Li,Xi Xuan,Davide Carbone*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RQ-TTSA uses rolling quantile estimation for adaptive Huber-style clipping to stabilize bilevel optimization under heavy-tailed noise, achieving O(T^{-(p-1)/(3p-2)}) convergence and outperforming baselines across six tasks with only ~2.7% computational overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bilevel optimization suffers from instability under heavy-tailed stochastic noise, and existing variance-reduction techniques rely on myopic magnitude checks that fail to distinguish geometric signals from impulsive outliers.

Method: Proposes RQ-TTSA (Robust Quantile-guided TTSA), which uses historical gradient buffers to estimate rolling quantiles for adaptive Huber-style clipping, preserving local optimization geometry while bounding effective variance.

Result: Convergence rate of O(T^{-(p-1)/(3p-2)}) under nonconvex-strongly convex assumptions with infinite-variance noise (p in (1,2]); outperforms SOTA baselines across six tasks including vision benchmarks, dynamic games, and offline RL; ~2.7% computational overhead increase.

Conclusion: Distribution-aware gradient control via quantile-guided clipping is a practical and necessary component for reliable bilevel learning under heavy-tailed noise.

Abstract: Bilevel optimization (BLO) is fundamental to hierarchical decision-making but suffers from critical instability under heavy-tailed stochastic noise. Existing variance-reduction techniques typically rely on myopic magnitude checks, which fail to distinguish informative geometric signals from impulsive outliers. To resolve this, we propose \textbf{RQ-TTSA} (Robust Quantile-guided TTSA), a distribution-aware framework that leverages historical gradient buffers to estimate rolling quantiles for adaptive Huber-style clipping, effectively preserving local optimization geometry while strictly bounding effective variance. Theoretically, we provide a convergence analysis for quantile-guided TTSA under nonconvex-strongly convex assumptions with infinite-variance noise ($p \in (1,2]$), deriving a rate of $\mathcal{O}(T^{-\frac{p-1}{3p-2}})$ that recovers optimal dependence on the heavy-tailed parameter. Empirically, across six diverse tasks, spanning heterogeneous vision benchmarks, dynamic games under momentum poisoning, and offline reinforcement learning, RQ-TTSA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines by eliminating divergence spikes and ensuring stable convergence. Our method demonstrates significant robustness to hyperparameter variations and incurs negligible computational overhead ($\approx 2.7\%$ increase), validating distribution-aware gradient control as a practical and necessary component for reliable bilevel learning.

</details>


### [738] [Adaptive Recurrent Message Passing for Test Time Computing on Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22462)
*Junshu Sun,Wanxing Chang,Qingming Huang,Shuhui Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AdaR is an adaptive recurrent graph model that overcomes the fixed architecture limitation of pre-trained foundation models on graph data by enabling flexible test-time computing through theoretically-grounded step-dependent recurrent updates with gradient-based convergence supervision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pre-trained foundation models have succeeded in many domains but extending them to graph learning is challenging due to the mismatch between graph data and fixed architectural designs.

Method: AdaR (Adaptive Recurrent graph model) uses recurrent graph models with step dependence as a necessary and sufficient condition for adaptive convergence, encodes normalized step information and representation-target relations into recurrent updates, and employs gradient-based supervision signals to guide representation updates throughout recurrence.

Result: AdaR consistently outperforms strong baselines in both inductive and transductive settings.

Conclusion: Recurrent graph models with adaptive test-time computing can overcome the limitations of fixed architectural designs in graph learning, and AdaR provides an effective framework for this paradigm.

Abstract: Pre-trained foundation models have demonstrated remarkable success in many domains, enabling a unified backbone to generalize across diverse downstream tasks. However, extending this paradigm to graph learning remains challenging due to the intrinsic mismatch between graph data and fixed architectural designs. In this work, we show that this limitation can be overcome via recurrent graph models. To achieve this, we conduct a systematic theoretical analysis, rigorously deriving step dependence as a necessary and sufficient condition for an adaptively convergent recurrent process. Building on this foundation, we propose AdaR, an Adaptive Recurrent graph model, empowering flexible test-time computing on various downstream tasks without changing model parameters. To enable adaptive inference, AdaR explicitly encodes normalized step information and representation-target relations into the recurrent updates. To ensure convergence of the recurrent process, AdaR employs gradient-based supervision signals that guide representation updates throughout the recurrence. Empirical results demonstrate that AdaR consistently outperforms strong baselines in both inductive and transductive settings.

</details>


### [739] [Federated learning with heavy-tailed gradient noise and communication noise: a variance-reduction based algorithm](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22466)
*Shengchao Zhao,Yongchao Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: VRA-FedSGD is a variance-reduction federated learning algorithm designed to handle heavy-tailed gradient and communication noises, achieving specific convergence rates in both mean and almost sure senses, validated on logistic regression tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing FL algorithms struggle with heavy-tailed gradient noise and communication noise prevalent in wireless networks and IoT deployments, hindering convergence and stability.

Method: VRA-FedSGD combines momentum variance reduction with nonlinear mapping to mitigate heavy-tailed gradient noise, and employs a variance-reduced aggregation mechanism to suppress heavy-tailed communication noise.

Result: Achieves mean convergence rate of O(K^{-(p-1)/(2p-1)}) for nonconvex objectives, and almost sure convergence rate of Õ(K^{-(1-1/(p-ε))}) for strongly convex objectives, where p is the tail index and ε a small constant.

Conclusion: VRA-FedSGD effectively addresses heavy-tailed noise in FL, with theoretical guarantees and empirical validation on logistic regression with real-world data.

Abstract: Federated learning (FL) is an emerging distributed machine learning paradigm that enables local devices to jointly train a global model while keeping data decentralized and private. We propose a variance-reduction based algorithm, VRA-FedSGD, for FL in the presence of heavy-tailed gradient noise and communication noise, where these noises are prevalent in large-scale machine learning over wireless networks and Internet of Things deployments. VRA-FedSGD employs a momentum variance reduction technique together with a nonlinear mapping to mitigate heavy-tailed gradient noise, and uses a variance-reduced aggregation mechanism to suppress heavy-tailed communication noise. In the mean sense, VRA-FedSGD achieves a convergence rate of {\small$\mathcal{O}\left(K^{-(p-1)/(2p-1)}\right)$} for nonconvex objective functions, where $p$ is the tail index of heavy-tailed noise. In the almost sure sense, VRA-FedSGD achieves a convergence rate of $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}\left(K^{-(1-1/(p-ε))}\right)$ for strongly convex objective functions, where $ε$ is an arbitrarily small constant. Simulated experiments on a logistic regression problem with real-world data verify the effectiveness of VRA-FedSGD.

</details>


### [740] [Fed-CausalDiff: Decoupled Synchronization for Federated Do-Simulation and Policy Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22510)
*Pengfei Li,Mohammad Khalil*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Fed-CausalDiff is a federated causal diffusion framework that decouples global causal mechanisms from local confounders via decoupled synchronisation, enabling accurate do-simulation and policy evaluation across heterogeneous decentralized data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard federated learning methods are purely observational and insufficient for interventional inference and policy evaluation because sequential actions dynamically alter future states.

Method: The architecture decomposes latent state evolution into a global causal score function and a local confounding score function, enabling decoupled synchronisation (DSS) where clients aggregate only the shared causal mechanism while retaining site-specific confounders locally.

Result: Experiments on four datasets demonstrate better ATE and policy-value estimation accuracy, with a favorable trade-off between communication cost and inference fidelity.

Conclusion: Fed-CausalDiff effectively addresses the limitation of purely observational federated learning for causal inference and policy evaluation in decentralized settings with heterogeneity.

Abstract: While federated learning enables collaborative modelling on decentralised data, standard methods merely fit historical observations. This purely observational approach is fundamentally insufficient for interventional inference and policy evaluation, as sequential actions dynamically alter future states. We propose \textbf{Fed-CausalDiff}, a federated causal diffusion framework for do-simulation. The architecture decomposes the evolution of the latent state into a global causal score function and a local confounding score function. This design enables \emph{decoupled synchronisation} (DSS), where clients aggregate only the shared causal mechanism while retaining site-specific confounders locally to handle heterogeneity. Experiments on four datasets demonstrate that Fed-CausalDiff achieves better ATE and policy-value estimation accuracy, offering a favorable trade-off between communication cost and inference fidelity.

</details>


### [741] [The Scissors Effect: When Resize-Based Input Diversity Helps or Hurts Transfer Attacks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22516)
*Yuhang Jiang,Xiaojing Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Input Diversity (DI) improves transferability for standard surrogates but harms it for robust ones—a pattern termed the Scissors Effect—caused by resize-induced gradient misalignment, with a training-free rule (CG-DI) that avoids the harm by disabling DI when local gradient consistency is high.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate the regime-dependence of Input Diversity (DI) in transfer-based adversarial attacks, specifically challenging the near-default assumption that DI always improves transferability, by examining its effect across different surrogate model types (standard vs. robust).

Method: Systematic evaluation of DI probability across standard and robust surrogates on ImageNet and CIFAR-10; controlled robustness-strength sweep; gradient geometry analysis via resize/translation decomposition and source-target gradient-alignment measurement; introduction of Local Gradient Consistency (LGC) as a probe; proof of a bias-variance crossover theorem; proposal of CG-DI (a training-free rule that disables DI when LGC is high).

Result: Increasing DI probability raises transfer success for standard surrogates but lowers it for robust ones (Scissors Effect). On ImageNet, blind DI costs robust surrogates 10.3% attack success across CNN, ViT, Swin, and ConvNeXt targets and ten attacks. Resize operations cause ~67% of the harm. The effect is graded, crossing from beneficial to harmful already in the little-robustness regime. CG-DI avoids the loss on robust surrogates while preserving DI's benefit on standard ones.

Conclusion: The Scissors Effect is a DI-specific manifestation of the broader robustness-transferability trade-off, and CG-DI provides a practical, training-free fix to avoid harming robust surrogates while retaining benefits for standard ones.

Abstract: Input Diversity (DI), which applies random resizing and padding at each attack iteration, is a near-default ingredient of transfer-based adversarial attacks, widely assumed to improve transferability. We show this assumption is regime-dependent and, for robustly trained surrogates, often reversed. Varying only the surrogate, increasing the DI probability raises transfer success for standard surrogates but lowers it for robust ones: the two response curves separate like a pair of scissors, a pattern we call the Scissors Effect. The effect is strong and consistent on ImageNet, where blind DI costs the robust source 10.3% attack success on average across CNN, ViT, Swin, and ConvNeXt targets and across ten attacks spanning 2018-2024; it is smaller on CIFAR-10 unless DI is made aggressive. A controlled robustness-strength sweep that varies only the training budget shows the harm is graded rather than binary, crossing from beneficial to harmful already in the little-robustness regime. We trace it to gradient geometry: a resize/translation decomposition attributes roughly 67% of the harm to resize, and a direct source-target gradient-alignment measurement confirms the same resize operation improves alignment for standard surrogates but degrades it for robust ones. We summarize the regime with Local Gradient Consistency (LGC), a single input-space probe that separates the two surrogate types, and prove a bias-variance crossover theorem isolating where DI helps from where its resize bias dominates. A training-free rule (CG-DI) that disables diversity when LGC is high avoids the loss on robust surrogates while keeping DI's benefit on standard ones, positioning the Scissors Effect as a DI-specific manifestation of the broader robustness-transferability trade-off.

</details>


### [742] [Generative Robust Optimisation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22536)
*Yuhui Yin,Vassilis M. Charitopoulos*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Generative Robust Optimisation (GRO) uses deep generative models to define uncertainty sets with complex nonlinear dependencies, evaluated via a five-point framework and instantiated with a Wasserstein Adversarial Autoencoder, validated on production planning and facility location problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical robust optimisation uncertainty sets use fixed geometric shapes that cannot represent complex dependencies (nonlinear correlations, asymmetry, multimodality) found in real-world data.

Method: Proposes GRO where a deep generative model (neural network decoder) defines the uncertainty set over a calibrated latent set. Introduces a five-point evaluation framework (reconstruction fidelity, distribution matching, latent regularity, robust relevance, computational tractability). Instantiates with a Wasserstein Adversarial Autoencoder using GMM-guided training and constraint-consistency regularisation, with ReLU activations for exact MIP-based verification.

Result: Validated on production planning (six uncertainty distributions, six architectures) and multi-period facility location, showing systematic attention to all five criteria yields expressive, well-calibrated, and optimisation-tractable uncertainty sets.

Conclusion: Deep generative models can effectively replace fixed geometric uncertainty sets in robust optimisation, and the five-point framework provides systematic model-agnostic criteria for evaluating neural-network-based uncertainty sets.

Abstract: Classical uncertainty sets for robust optimisation impose fixed geometric shapes that cannot represent the complex dependencies present in real-world data. We propose Generative Robust Optimisation (GRO), a framework in which a deep generative model defines the uncertainty set as the image of a neural network decoder over a calibrated latent set, naturally accommodating nonlinear correlations, asymmetry, and multimodality. A five-point evaluation framework (reconstruction fidelity, distribution matching, latent regularity, robust relevance, and computational tractability) provides systematic, model-agnostic criteria for assessing any neural network-based uncertainty set. We instantiate this framework with a Wasserstein Adversarial Autoencoder employing Gaussian mixture model-guided training for latent regularity and constraint-consistency regularisation for robust relevance. Restricting the decoder to ReLU activations enables exact worst-case verification through mixed-integer programming embedding. Extensive experiments on a production planning problem across six uncertainty distributions and six generative architectures, together with a multi-period facility location study, validate the framework and demonstrate that systematic attention to all five criteria yields uncertainty sets that are simultaneously expressive, well-calibrated, and optimisation-tractable.

</details>


### [743] [Mitigating Measurement-Induced Training Instability in Hybrid Quantum Neural Networks for Protein Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22551)
*Milton Mondal,Sushovan Chanda,Mohamad Mahdi Alawieh,Brijesh Sukhadiya,Donatus Krah,Clinton Gonsalves,Antonios Ntolkeras,Silvio O. Rizzoli,Ali H. Shaib*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper identifies measurement-induced logit contraction as a cause of trainability degradation in hybrid quantum neural networks, where bounded quantum measurement outputs [-1,1] suppress gradients when used with cross-entropy loss. The authors propose a learnable scaling parameter called Quantum Measurement Temperature (QMT) that rescales outputs before loss computation, improving gradient magnitude, variance, and classification accuracy without modifying the quantum architecture.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard Pauli measurements in hybrid QNNs produce outputs bounded to [-1,1], which when used directly with cross-entropy loss and softmax normalization, result in weak loss sensitivity to logit differences and suppressed gradients, causing unstable optimization in variational quantum classifiers.

Method: The authors introduce a learnable scaling parameter called Quantum Measurement Temperature (QMT) that rescales quantum measurement outputs prior to the loss function, compensating for physically imposed bounds on quantum measurements without altering the quantum ansatz, circuit depth, or measurement operators.

Result: Experiments on fluorescence microscopy images and six-class Fashion MNIST show that QMT consistently enhances logit separation, strengthens gradients, stabilizes training across random initializations, and improves classification accuracy compared to unscaled measurement readouts.
