TL;DR: PeLAP-A introduces a learnable channel-wise importance predictor for latent diffusion models and discovers a "sparsity collapse" phenomenon where all latent channels can be suppressed to near-zero yet the denoising UNet achieves lower diffusion loss and reconstruction MSE than the unpruned baseline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether all latent channels in a VAE-compressed latent space contribute equally to the diffusion process, or if significant redundancy exists.

Method: Augments a standard latent diffusion pipeline with a lightweight two-layer MLP that acts as a channel-wise importance predictor. It produces a soft mask via global pooled latent features to suppress unimportant channels before they enter the denoising UNet. The system is trained jointly on CIFAR-10 with a combined diffusion, reconstruction, and sparsity loss.

Result: Under aggressive sparsity regularization (lambda=0.01), the importance predictor drives all latent channels to near-zero, yet the denoising UNet achieves lower diffusion loss (0.0236 vs 0.0240) and lower VAE reconstruction MSE (22.59 vs 24.67) compared to the unpruned baseline — a phenomenon termed "sparsity collapse."

Conclusion: Denoising UNets are remarkably robust to latent channel suppression even under aggressive regularization; the findings reveal redundancy in latent diffusion representations and serve as an exploratory study of sparsity dynamics in latent diffusion training.

Abstract: Latent diffusion models achieve strong generative performance by operating in a compressed latent space produced by a variational autoencoder (VAE). However, it remains unclear whether all latent channels contribute equally to the diffusion process, or whether significant redundancy exists. We introduce PeLAP-A (Adaptive Latent Pruning for Diffusion), a lightweight framework that augments a standard latent diffusion pipeline with a learnable channel-wise importance predictor. A two-layer MLP operating on globally pooled latent features produces a soft mask that suppresses unimportant latent channels before they enter the denoising UNet. The entire system is trained jointly on CIFAR-10 under a combined diffusion, reconstruction, and sparsity loss. Experiments reveal a striking result: under aggressive sparsity regularization (lambda = 0.01), the importance predictor drives all latent channels to near-zero yet the denoising UNet achieves lower diffusion loss (0.0236 vs. 0.0240) and lower VAE reconstruction MSE (22.59 vs. 24.67) compared to the unpruned baseline. We term this the sparsity collapse phenomenon and provide an analysis of why it occurs and what it reveals about the information requirements of latent diffusion models. These findings constitute an exploratory study of sparsity dynamics in latent diffusion training, and demonstrate that denoising UNets can remain remarkably robust to latent channel suppression even under aggressive regularization. Code is available at: https://github.com/kissasium/PeLAP-A.git.

</details>


### [797] [FlowTrain: Flow-Based Decoupled Training for Industrial-Grade Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23087)
*Zhida Jiang,Zhaolong Xing,Yang Pei,Xiaolong Chen,Yuanhang Xiao,Chengzhi Huang,Xiyu Liu,Haopeng Liu,Qingyuan Sang,Lingfeng Zhou,Jiaxing Wang,Zicheng Zhang,Wenzhe Wang,Xinyu Liu,Yan Li,Zhen Chen,Ke Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FlowTrain is a flow-based decoupled training framework for vision-language models that reformulates training as a producer-consumer dataflow over a unified memory pool, achieving over 50% MFU and up to 1.7x throughput improvement by enabling independent progress of encoder and backbone with module-specific parallelism strategies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Industrial-grade distributed training of VLMs remains far less efficient than unimodal LLM training, and existing solutions (monolithic design with uniform parallelism or disaggregated batch-synchronized pipelines) are insufficient.

Method: FlowTrain introduces: (1) a flow-based decoupled training framework with producer-consumer dataflow over a unified memory pool; (2) a heterogeneous parallel allocator that assigns module-specific parallelism strategies via throughput matching; (3) a dynamic packing scheduler for balanced microbatches based on LLM-side computation cost.

Result: Achieves over 50% MFU and up to 1.7x throughput improvement on real-world workloads, narrowing the efficiency gap to LLM-only training.

Conclusion: VLM training can be further decoupled beyond existing solutions, and FlowTrain's flow-based decoupled approach significantly improves training efficiency.

Abstract: Industrial-grade distributed training of vision-language models (VLMs) remains far less efficient than that of unimodal LLMs. Existing solutions either follow a monolithic design that assigns uniform parallelism to heterogeneous modules or adopt a disaggregated deployment that separates modules while executing them as a batch-synchronized pipeline. In this paper, we highlight that the above solutions are still not sufficient, and VLM training can be further decoupled. To this end, we present FlowTrain, a flow-based decoupled training framework that reformulates VLM training as a producer-consumer dataflow coordinated through a unified memory pool. The encoder and backbone can progress independently over a global virtual address space. Since this execution decoupling fundamentally changes the optimization objective of allocation and scheduling, FlowTrain further introduces a heterogeneous parallel allocator that assigns module-specific parallelism strategies by solving a throughput matching problem. The dynamic packing scheduler is used to construct balanced microbatches at runtime according to the actual LLM-side computation cost. Extensive experiments on real-world workloads show that FlowTrain achieves over 50% MFU and up to 1.7x throughput improvement, narrowing the efficiency gap to LLM-only training.

</details>


### [798] [FLFL: Federated Latent Factor Learning for Private Recovery of Spatio-Temporal Signals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23091)
*Chengjun Yu,Di Wu,Yi He,Jia Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A federated latent factor learning (FLFL) model is proposed for privacy-preserving spatio-temporal signal recovery in WSNs, where sensors share only gradient information (not raw data) and spatio-temporal correlations are used as regularization to improve accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LFL models require centralized data storage, which raises privacy concerns for data owners. This paper aims to address the privacy issue while maintaining accurate missing data recovery in WSNs.

Method: FLFL combines two key designs: 1) a sensor-level federated learning framework based on LFL where sensors upload only gradient information instead of raw data, and 2) spatio-temporal correlation incorporated as regularization constraints within the federated framework to enhance recovery accuracy.

Result: Experiments on four real-world WSN datasets show FLFL significantly outperforms eight state-of-the-art federated and non-federated signal recovery models in recovery accuracy with privacy preservation.

Conclusion: FLFL effectively achieves both accurate missing data recovery and privacy preservation in WSNs, providing a viable solution for privacy-sensitive sensing applications.

Abstract: Wireless sensor network (WSNs) stands out as a burgeoning and promising domain in intelligent sensing. Owing to various factors such as sudden sensor malfunctions or deliberate shutdown of partial nodes to save energy, the collected sensing signals from WSNs commonly have massive missing data, leading to adverse effects on subsequent analysis or decision-making. Latent factor learning (LFL) has proven to be highly effective in recovering the missing data for WSNs. However, the existing LFL models require the collected sensing signals to be maintained in one central place like a central server, which is becoming unacceptable for data owners who are getting increasingly privacy-sensitive. To address this issue, this paper innovatively proposes a federated latent factor learning (FLFL) model for privacy-preserving spatio-temporal signal recovery. Its main idea is two-fold: 1) it designs a sensor-level federated learning framework based on LFL, where each sensor only needs to upload gradient information rather than raw data for training a privacy-preserving recovery model, and 2) it incorporates the spatio-temporal correlation into the designed federated learning framework as the regularization constraint to improve its recovery accuracy. With such designs, FLFL can not only accurately recover the missing data of WSNs but also ensure data owners' privacy-preserving of raw data. To evaluate the proposed FLFL model, extensive experiments have been conducted on four real-world WSN datasets. The results demonstrate that FLFL significantly outperforms eight state-of-the-art federated and non-federated signal recovery models in terms of recovery accuracy with privacy-preserving.

</details>


### [799] [Minimax Quantile Lower Bounds for Interactive Statistical Decision Making with Privacy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23096)
*Raghav Bongole,Amirreza Zamani,Tobias J. Oechtering,Mikael Skoglund*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper develops a δ-explicit minimax-quantile theory for interactive statistical decision making (ISDM) to capture rare but consequential failures that expectation-based minimax risk and regret criteria miss. It provides structural relations between minimax quantiles and risk, derives high-probability converse tools (interactive Fano's and Le Cam's methods), extends the framework to mutual-information privacy, and establishes explicit minimax quantile lower bounds for Gaussian mean estimation and multi-armed Gaussian bandits with privacy considerations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard minimax risk and regret criteria are expectation-based and do not capture rare but consequential failures (tail risks). The authors aim to develop a theory that addresses these rare-event concerns in interactive statistical decision making.

Method: The paper develops a δ-explicit minimax-quantile theory. It establishes structural relations between minimax quantiles, lower minimax quantiles, and minimax risk. It derives two high-probability converse tools: interactive Fano's method and interactive Le Cam's method. It extends the framework to MI-privacy by restricting the admissible decision class, and derives a two-point template for coordinatewise Gaussian privatization.

Result: The framework yields explicit lower bounds in confidence level δ and privacy budget: log(1/δ)/n for squared-error Gaussian mean estimation, √(T log(1/δ)) for two-armed bounded-mean Gaussian bandits, and √(KT log(1/δ))-type for K-armed bandits. Privacy manifests as a Gaussian variance-inflation factor.

Conclusion: The minimax-quantile framework effectively captures tail risks in ISDM, provides explicit high-probability lower bounds, and seamlessly incorporates privacy constraints. The interactive Fano method captures exploration costs over multiple possible best arms in bandit problems.

Abstract: Minimax risk and regret are expectation-based criteria and do not capture rare but consequential failures. To address this concern, we develop a $δ$-explicit minimax-quantile theory for interactive statistical decision making (ISDM). We first provide structural relations between minimax quantiles, lower minimax quantiles, and minimax risk. This includes a quantile-to-expectation conversion and an equivalence between strict and lower minimax quantiles outside a countable set of confidence levels. We then derive two converse tools for ISDM: a high-probability interactive Fano's method and a high-probability interactive Le Cam's method. Then, we show that mutual-information (MI) privacy can be handled in the same framework by restricting the admissible decision class. For coordinatewise Gaussian privatization, we derive a two-point template that isolates the privacy-induced variance inflation. We instantiate this template for Gaussian mean estimation, and use the same two-point strategy directly for two-armed Gaussian bandits. We then derive a minimax quantile lower bound for the $K$-armed Gaussian bandit problem, showing that the interactive Fano method captures the exploration cost over multiple possible best arms. The resulting lower bounds are explicit in the confidence level $δ$ and in the privacy budget for the private problems. They yield $\log(1/δ)/n$ scaling for squared-error Gaussian mean estimation, $\sqrt{T\log(1/δ)}$ scaling for two-armed bounded-mean Gaussian bandits, and $\sqrt{KT\log(1/δ)}$-type scaling for the $K$-armed bandits, with privacy appearing through a Gaussian variance-inflation factor for the private problems.

</details>


### [800] [ReNIO: Reweighting Negative Trajectory Importance for LLM On-Policy Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23104)
*Chen Lin,Kedi Chen,Wei Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ReNIO improves on-policy distillation for LLM reasoning by reweighting negative student-generated trajectories using student-to-teacher probability ratios, without requiring final answer correctness. It consistently outperforms standard OPD/OPSD by leveraging incorrect outputs which better preserve exploratory reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard on-policy distillation treats all student-generated outputs equally, but the authors observe asymmetric benefits: training on incorrect-only SGOs outperforms correct-only ones. This motivates a method to exploit negative trajectories without needing full answer-verification rollouts.

Method: ReNIO (Reweights Negative trajectory Importance for LLM On-policy distillation) uses the student-to-teacher probability ratio to identify pivotal tokens leading to wrong reasoning traces, then aggregates these into a normalized sample weight. This inherently assigns larger weights to likely negative trajectories without requiring final-answer correctness labels, preserving OPD's prefix training advantage.

Result: ReNIO improves both OPD and OPSD across mathematical reasoning and code generation tasks, with relative gains up to 8.90% for Qwen3-1.7B and 10.00% for R1-Distill-Qwen-7B on mathematical reasoning benchmarks.

Conclusion: Leveraging negative trajectories via token-level probability ratios is an effective and efficient alternative to full-answer verification for improving on-policy LLM distillation, and the approach generalizes across model scales and tasks.

Abstract: On-policy distillation (OPD) improves LLM reasoning by training a student model on its own generated outputs, but standard OPD treats all student-generated outputs (SGOs) equally regardless of their informativeness. We observe a consistent asymmetry in controlled filtering experiments: in both OPD and on-policy self distillation (OPSD), training only on incorrect SGOs outperforms training only on correct ones. Our further analysis suggests that models trained on correct-only SGOs tend to generate shorter reasoning traces and show weaker reflection behavior, while incorrect SGOs better preserve exploratory reasoning near the model's capability boundary. To exploit this signal without requiring full answer-containing rollouts, we introduce ReNIO, which Reweights Negative trajectory Importance for LLM On-policy distillation. By using the student-to-teacher probability ratio, ReNIO identifies pivotal tokens leading to wrong reasoning traces and aggregates their information into a normalized sample weight, inherently assigning larger weights to likely negative trajectories without observing the correctness of final-answer. Since Re-NIO only uses prefix-conditioned token probabilities, it preserves OPD's prefix training advantage over full-rollout reinforcement learning. Across both mathematical reasoning and code generation tasks, ReNIO improves both OPD and OPSD, with representative relative gains of up to 8.90% for Qwen3-1.7B and 10.00% for R1-Distill-Qwen-7B on mathematical reasoning benchmarks. Code repo: https://github.com/BDML-lab/ReNIO.

</details>


### [801] [Self-Evolution for Multi-Turn Tool-Calling Agents via Divergence-Point Preference Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23112)
*Jiaqiang Tang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ToolGraph combines schema-derived topology with DPO-trained preference optimization to improve multi-turn tool-using agents, achieving a 16.8% relative reward gain over baseline on 375 tau2-bench tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing approaches separate inference-time orchestration from parameter-level learning, leading to weakly structured tool selection and vulnerability to train-deployment prompt mismatch in multi-turn tool-using agents.

Method: ToolGraph constructs schema-derived topology with transition weights from successful rollouts and history-aware controls. It builds 161 preference pairs via state-based matching and prefix-based alignment with action-correctness annotations, then trains DPO under the same ToolGraph context used at inference.

Result: On 375 tau2-bench tasks, ToolGraph raises weighted average reward from 0.304 to 0.338 (+11.2%), and ToolGraph+DPO reaches 0.355 (+16.8% over baseline). DPO gains are concentrated in airline and retail domains.

Conclusion: Graph-structured tool orchestration combined with DPO preference optimization improves multi-turn agent performance. Diagnostics reveal step-budget exhaustion in telecom trajectories and chosen reward positivity as the most useful checkpoint signal across DPO configurations.

Abstract: Multi-turn tool-using agents must coordinate long-horizon tool sequences while tracking dialogue state and policy constraints. Existing approaches often separate inference-time orchestration from parameter-level learning, leaving tool selection weakly structured and preference updates vulnerable to train--deployment prompt mismatch. For within-benchmark self-improvement, ToolGraph combines schema-derived topology, transition weights estimated from successful rollouts, and history-aware controls for write prerequisites and repeated-search loops. We then construct 161 preference pairs by locating divergence points via state-based matching and prefix-based alignment, filtered through action-correctness annotations, and train DPO under the same ToolGraph context used at inference. Across 375 tau2-bench tasks, ToolGraph raises the weighted average reward from 0.304 to 0.338 (+11.2% relative), while ToolGraph+DPO reaches 0.355 (+16.8% over the baseline), with the DPO gain concentrated in airline and retail. Fine-grained diagnostics further show that roughly half of telecom trajectories exhaust the step budget before action execution and that chosen reward positivity is the most useful checkpoint signal across our 16 evaluated DPO configurations.

</details>


### [802] [Temporal-Spectral Alignment with Frequency Adaptation for Source-Free Time-Series Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23120)
*Shichang Meng,Linquan Wu,Xuan Ai,Linqi Song*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SAFA proposes a source-free domain adaptation method for time-series that aligns target domain data with source distribution by jointly modeling temporal dependencies, spectral characteristics, and modulating phase/amplitude in the frequency domain.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing source-free domain adaptation (SFDA) for time-series addresses temporal dynamics but overlooks spectral shifts in the frequency domain, which are critical for time-series signals.

Method: SAFA first models the source domain at multiple scales by capturing both temporal dependencies and spectral characteristics. For target adaptation, it introduces a trainable frequency adaptation module that modulates the phase and amplitude of target signals in the frequency domain to align with source distribution.

Result: Extensive experiments on multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate efficacy and robustness of SAFA.

Conclusion: Jointly modeling temporal and spectral information with frequency adaptation improves source-free domain adaptation for time-series data, addressing both feature shift and temporal drift.

Abstract: The goal of source-free domain adaptation (SFDA) for time-series data is to transfer knowledge from a pre-trained source model to an unlabeled target domain without requiring access to source data, while addressing feature shift and temporal drift inherent in the signals. Although existing approaches have explored temporal dynamics in unsupervised source-free adaptation, they largely overlook spectral shifts in time-series data. Towards this end, we propose a novel approach termed temporal-Spectral Alignment with Frequency Adaptation (SAFA) for source-free time-series domain adaptation. Specifically, we first model the source domain at multiple scales by jointly capturing temporal dependencies and spectral characteristics. To adapt time-series data in the target domain, we introduce a trainable frequency adaptation module that modulates the phase and amplitude of target signals in the frequency domain to align them with the source distribution. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate the efficacy and robustness of SAFA.

</details>


### [803] [The Fractal Neural Operator: Overcoming Spectral Bias in Chaotic Attractors via Prime-Harmonic Weierstrass Encodings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23123)
*Kanishk Awadhiya*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FNO uses a non-resonant prime number basis (Harmonic Weierstrass Encoder) to inject infinite spectral resolution, extending Lorenz-63 prediction to 347 Lyapunov times (2.3x over SOTA).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard deep learning models (Transformers, Neural Operators) suffer from spectral bias (low-pass filtering), which fails to capture the high-frequency, fractal nature of chaotic dynamical systems and their strange attractors.

Method: Fractal Neural Operator (FNO) with a non-resonant prime number basis and Harmonic Weierstrass Encoder, replacing geometric ($2^k$) encodings to avoid spectral gaps and resonance, enabling infinite spectral resolution in latent space.

Result: Valid prediction horizon of Lorenz-63 extended to 347 Lyapunov times, surpassing Reservoir Computing baselines by 2.3x.

Conclusion: Chaos is not inherently unpredictable to neural networks; non-differentiable, fractal embedding manifolds are required to overcome spectral bias.

Abstract: Deep learning models, particularly Transformers and Neural Operators, exhibit a well-documented "spectral bias," effectively acting as low-pass filters that smooth out high-frequency information. While benign in fluid dynamics, this bias is catastrophic for Chaotic Dynamical Systems, where the underlying strange attractor is characterized by fractal geometry and infinite spectral density. We introduce the Fractal Neural Operator (FNO), a novel architecture that utilizes a non-resonant prime number basis to approximate continuous dynamical systems. Unlike geometric encodings ($2^k$), which suffer from spectral gaps and resonance, our Harmonic Weierstrass Encoder injects infinite spectral resolution into the latent space. We demonstrate that FNO extends the valid prediction horizon of the Lorenz-63 system to 347 Lyapunov times, exceeding state-of-the-art Reservoir Computing baselines by a factor of 2.3x. These results suggest that "chaos" is not inherently unpredictable to neural networks, but rather requires non-differentiable, fractal embedding manifolds.

</details>


### [804] [Weighted Score-Oriented Losses for Temporally Localized Event Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23145)
*Edoardo Legnaro,Sabrina Guastavino,Francesco Marchetti*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a temporally localized weighted score-oriented loss (wSOL) that directly optimizes event-based metrics (like F1, critical success index) for time-series event detection, addressing the mismatch between pointwise training losses and event-based evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Operational event-detection systems are evaluated using event-based metrics (temporal windows, false-alarm costs) rather than pointwise accuracy, creating a score-loss mismatch when neural networks are trained with classical loss functions like cross-entropy.

Method: The method introduces temporal weights that discount near-event false positives and reduce false-negative penalties when an event is preceded by an admissible alarm, within the weighted SOL framework. The loss is differentiable and can be instantiated with balanced accuracy, true skill statistic, F1, critical success index, and related confusion-matrix scores.

Result: On three benchmark datasets for time-series event prediction/detection, wSOL improves performance compared to cross-entropy and unweighted score-oriented loss when evaluation utility is localized in time and not already encoded by pointwise labels.

Conclusion: Weighted score-oriented loss effectively bridges the gap between training objectives and event-based evaluation metrics, yielding better performance in temporally localized evaluation scenarios.

Abstract: Operational event-detection systems are rarely assessed by pointwise accuracy alone. In anomaly detection, changepoint detection, and warning systems, the utility of an alarm depends on its temporal position relative to an event. This produces a score-loss mismatch. Neural networks are commonly trained with classical loss functions, such as cross-entropy, whereas deployment decisions are obtained by thresholding network predictions, merging alarms through post-processing rules, and evaluating them with event-based metrics defined by detection windows and false-alarm costs. This paper studies a temporally localized specialization of weighted score-oriented loss (wSOL) for event prediction. Starting from score-oriented losses based on expected confusion matrices and from the weighted SOL framework of Marchetti et al., we consider temporal weights that discount near-event false positives and reduce false-negative penalties when an event is preceded by an admissible alarm. The resulting objective is differentiable with respect to the network predictions, and therefore can be optimized by back-propagation. It can be instantiated with balanced accuracy, true skill statistic, F1, critical success index, and related confusion-matrix scores. We evaluate the proposed approach by comparing cross-entropy, unweighted score-oriented loss, and wSOL on three benchmark datasets for time-series event prediction and detection. The results show that wSOL can improve performance when the evaluation utility is localized in time and is not already encoded by the pointwise labels.

</details>


### [805] [Substitution-Based Analysis of Structural Novelty for Generative Models of Materials](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23166)
*Masahiro Negishi,Aron Walsh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Analysis reveals 81-92% of AI-generated crystals are duplicates of training data or derived from elemental substitution, with high-symmetry structures showing memorization bias and low-symmetry structures enabling wider exploration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To assess whether generative AI models for inorganic crystal design genuinely expand the accessible materials search space beyond conventional strategies like elemental substitution within known structure types.

Method: Developed a workflow to classify AI-generated crystals as training duplicates, substitution-derived structures, or unmatched; applied the workflow to representative generative models and analyzed structural fingerprints.

Result: 81-92% of chemically valid and metastable generated crystals are either training duplicates or substitution-derived; this tendency is strong in high-symmetry systems; low-symmetry structures beyond duplication/substitution represent interpolation in data-rich regions; high-symmetry duplicates result from memorization in training-sparse regions.

Conclusion: Current generative AI models exhibit bias towards known structural prototypes in high-symmetry regions but enable wider exploration of low-symmetry structural space, highlighting a fundamental limitation in novelty generation.

Abstract: There has been rapid progress in generative artificial intelligence (AI) models for inorganic crystal design, which can efficiently generate large numbers of candidate compounds after being trained on databases of known crystals. However, it remains unclear whether they genuinely expand the accessible materials search space beyond conventional strategies such as elemental substitution within known structure types. We address this question by developing a workflow to assess whether AI-generated crystals are duplicates of training structures, reproducible by elemental substitution, or unmatched by either criterion. Applying this workflow to representative generative models reveals that 81-92% of chemically valid and metastable generated crystals are either training duplicates or substitution-derived structures. This tendency is particularly strong in high-symmetry crystal systems, even though many possible structural prototypes remain unexplored. Further analysis of the underlying structural fingerprints shows that low-symmetry structures beyond duplication or substitution can be interpreted as interpolation in training-data-rich regions, while high-symmetry duplicates appear to result from memorisation in training-sparse regions. Our findings highlight a limitation in the current generation of models that exhibit a bias towards known structural prototypes in the high symmetry regions, but enable wider exploration of the low-symmetry structural space.

</details>


### [806] [Position: Correct Answer, Wrong Mechanism -- When AI Scientists Defend General Claims Their Own Data Contradicts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23175)
*Steven Young Eulig*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper argues that current outcome-only evaluation of AI scientist systems (particularly coding agents) is insufficient, demonstrating through 28 simulation episodes that agents often produce correct answers through incorrect reasoning (CAWM: Correct Answer, Wrong Mechanism). The authors propose separate evaluation of task outcome, mechanism fidelity, and epistemic honesty, and introduce lightweight tests (regime-shift check and companion recomputation) to detect such failures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current evaluation of AI scientist systems focuses solely on final outcomes (correct answers), which is insufficient because agents can produce correct results through flawed reasoning that breaks under changed conditions. The authors argue that trust in scientific co-authors requires mechanism-fidelity verification that current systems do not reliably self-apply.

Method: Analysis of 28 episodes of a coding agent attempting to rediscover a known particle identification observable in Geant4 simulation, including 8 cross-model probe episodes. The study measures dissociation between task outcome, mechanism fidelity, and epistemic honesty, and proposes two lightweight tests: a one-step regime-shift check and a companion recomputation.

Result: In 4/20 primary-model and 3/8 cross-model episodes, agents exhibited CAWM (correct answer through wrong mechanism). Honesty and mechanism fidelity dissociate within single agent trajectories. When given partially misleading priors, all five agents rejected false components on evidence, yet one defended its chosen observable with physics inconsistent with its own data.

Conclusion: Coding agents prove reliable tools but unreliable scientific co-authors for open-ended claim-making. The proposed lightweight tests (regime-shift check and companion recomputation) successfully flagged every CAWM case in the study, suggesting detectable failures that can be addressed with simple verification procedures.

Abstract: AI scientist systems are described as tools, coauthors, or founders, but we evaluate them as if only the final answer matters. This position paper argues that outcome-only evaluation is insufficient, and that task outcome, mechanism fidelity, and epistemic honesty must be measured separately. Our evidence comes from 28 episodes of a coding agent attempting to rediscover a known particle identification observable in a Geant4 simulation, including an 8-episode probe across two additional frontier models. In 4/20 primary-model and 3/8 cross-model episodes, agents reach right-looking results through incorrect reasoning that breaks when conditions change, which we call Correct Answer, Wrong Mechanism (CAWM). Honesty and mechanism fidelity dissociate within a single agent trajectory. When given a partially misleading prior, all five agents reject the false component on evidence, yet one defends its chosen observable with physics inconsistent with its own data. In the simulation-based discovery setting studied here, coding agents prove reliable tools but unreliable scientific co-authors for open-ended claim-making, where co-author trust requires mechanism-fidelity verification they do not reliably self-apply. The failure is detectable, and we propose a lightweight test. A one-step regime-shift check needs only the agent's claim and flags the over-generalized cases. A companion recomputation flags the remaining cases when the correct observable is known. Together, these checks flag every CAWM case in this study.

</details>


### [807] [EML Trees Are Universal Approximators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23179)
*Joe Germany,Elie Abdo,Joseph Bakarji*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper studies EML (Exp-Minus-Log) function trees, proving they can universally approximate functions in Sobolev spaces W^{k,∞} and introducing a learning algorithm for practical optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To establish EML trees as a theoretically grounded function approximation framework by analyzing their expressive power and developing a practical learning algorithm.

Method: Leverages classical neural network approximation arguments and constructs EML trees that explicitly mimic polynomial representations to prove universal approximation for W^{k,∞} functions; proposes a learning algorithm with fitting parameters for EML-type trees.

Result: EML trees possess universal approximation property for functions in W^{k,∞} (k∈ℕ), and the proposed learning algorithm demonstrates feasibility in practical optimization problems.

Conclusion: EML trees are a theoretically grounded framework for function approximation, bridging continuous analogues of logic gates with practical learning.

Abstract: The recently introduced EML (Exp-Minus-Log) function acts as continuous analogue of NAND gates, providing a compositional building block capable of representing elementary functions. In this work, we study the expressive power of tree-structured compositions of EML functions. We show that such trees enjoy a universal approximation property for functions in $W^{k, \infty}$ for $k \in \mathbb N$, drawing on classical neural network approximation arguments while exploiting the ability to explicitly construct EML trees that mimic polynomial representations. We further propose a learning algorithm for EML-type trees equipped with fitting parameters, and demonstrate its feasibility in practical optimization problems. Our results establish EML trees as a theoretically grounded framework for function approximation.

</details>


### [808] [Stage-dependent integer-binary encoding in factorization-machine black-box optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23188)
*Ryo Ogawa,Mayumi Nakano,Yuya Seki,Shu Tanaka*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A stage-dependent FMQA framework is proposed that uses one-hot encoding for surrogate model learning and domain-wall encoding for Ising-machine search, achieving improved optimization performance especially under fine discretization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional FMQA uses a single encoding throughout the process, but the optimal encoding for surrogate learning may differ from that for Ising-machine solution search, motivating a stage-dependent approach.

Method: The authors propose a stage-dependent FMQA framework with conversion formulas between one-hot and domain-wall QUBO matrices that preserve the surrogate objective over feasible integer states up to an additive constant. The OhDw variant uses one-hot for learning and domain-wall for search.

Result: On the Rastrigin function (N=2,5; q=61,301), one-hot encoding in the learning stage consistently yields lower residual errors. For N=5, q=301, OhDw outperforms one-hot-only FMQA; for N=5, q=61, one-hot-only performs better.

Conclusion: One-hot encoding in the learning stage is the primary driver of performance, and stage-dependent encoding can provide additional gains under finer discretization.

Abstract: Black-box optimization (BBO) deals with problems where objective functions lack explicit analytical forms and are expensive to evaluate. Factorization machine with quadratic-optimization annealing (FMQA) constructs a surrogate model using a factorization machine (FM) and optimizes it with an Ising machine. Conventional FMQA applies a single integer-binary encoding throughout the optimization process, although the encoding best suited to surrogate learning may differ from the one best suited to Ising-machine solution search. We propose a stage-dependent FMQA framework and derive conversion formulas between one-hot and domain-wall QUBO matrices that preserve the surrogate objective over feasible integer states up to an additive constant. We evaluate the OhDw variant, which employs one-hot encoding for learning and domain-wall encoding for search, on the Rastrigin function with input dimensions N = 2 and 5 and discretization levels q = 61 and 301. Across all conditions, the dominant factor governing optimization performance is the encoding used in the learning stage, with one-hot encoding consistently yielding lower residual errors than domain-wall or binary encoding. The additional benefit of switching to domain-wall encoding for solution search is condition-dependent. For N = 5 and q = 301, OhDw achieves a lower residual error and solutions closer to the global optimum than one-hot-only FMQA, whereas for N = 5 and q = 61 the latter achieves a lower residual error. These results indicate that one-hot encoding in the learning stage is the primary performance driver and that stage-dependent encoding can provide further improvement under finer discretization.

</details>


### [809] [Memory Contagion: Cross-Temporal Propagation of Evaluator Bias via Agent Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23195)
*Zewen Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper identifies and formalizes Memory Contagion, a phenomenon where evaluator bias propagates through LLM agent memory systems over time. Even with perfect memory consolidation, biased input causes bias propagation across agents. The effect varies by bias type (length preference vs. authority bias), and no safe threshold exists—bias propagates at contamination rates as low as 20%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing work acknowledges that agent memories degrade during consolidation but assumes memories come from unbiased experiences. The authors identify a gap: no formal treatment exists for how evaluator bias propagates through agent memory systems across time.

Method: The authors formalize Memory Contagion and conduct experiments across two bias types (length preference, authority bias) and four experimental phases, including an oracle (perfect consolidation) condition to isolate the effect of biased input.

Result: (1) Memory Contagion occurs even with perfect consolidation. (2) Consolidation attenuates length bias but amplifies authority bias, showing bias-type-dependent interaction. (3) Bias propagation is detectable at contamination rates as low as p=0.2, with no safe threshold observed.

Conclusion: Memory Contagion exposes a critical vulnerability in current LLM agent memory designs, and the authors provide formal tools for measuring cross-temporal bias propagation.

Abstract: Large Language Model (LLM) agents increasingly rely on memory systems to maintain long-term coherence. Recent work shows that agent memories degrade during continuous consolidation. However, existing research assumes memories are derived from unbiased experiences. In this work, we identify and formalize a novel phenomenon: Memory Contagion -- the cross-temporal propagation of evaluator bias through agent memory. We show that when agents are trained or guided by biased evaluators, their experiences become biased; when these trajectories are stored and consolidated into memory, the bias propagates to future agents retrieving from the same memory store, even when consolidation is perfect (oracle). Across two bias types (length preference, authority bias) and four experimental phases, we demonstrate: (1) Memory Contagion occurs even with perfect consolidation (oracle condition), proving that biased input is a sufficient cause of contagion; (2) Consolidation has opposite effects depending on bias type -- robustly attenuating length bias while preliminarily amplifying authority bias (single-run estimate), suggesting a bias-type-dependent interaction; (3) No observed safe threshold: bias propagation is detected at contamination rates as low as p=0.2. Our findings expose a critical vulnerability in current agent memory designs and provide formal tools for measuring cross-temporal bias propagation.

</details>


### [810] [Bridge the Gaps: Heterogeneous Attributed Graph Clustering via Quaternion Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23199)
*Xinxi Chen,Junyang Chen,Yiqun Zhang,Chuangming Qiu,Xiang Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AGREE is an end-to-end framework for attributed graph clustering that handles heterogeneous attributes (numerical and categorical), mitigates over-smoothing via shallow architectures, alleviates over-dominating via quaternion-based graph convolution, and jointly optimizes graph reconstruction and clustering without predefined cluster numbers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Attributed graph clustering faces two key challenges: (1) heterogeneous attribute types (numerical + categorical) hinder unified representation learning, and (2) representation degradation due to over-smoothing (OS) from repeated graph propagation and over-dominating (OD) where topological influence overshadows discriminative attribute information.

Method: AGREE uses multi-level alignment and similarity-based graph construction to unify any-type attributes with graph topology; employs quaternion-based graph convolution to enhance attribute interaction (reducing OD); adopts shallow graph architectures to mitigate OS; and jointly optimizes graph reconstruction and clustering without requiring a predefined cluster count during training.

Result: Experiments on diverse benchmarks demonstrate that AGREE achieves strong overall performance in accuracy, robustness, and adaptability.

Conclusion: AGREE effectively bridges gaps in attributed graph clustering by handling heterogeneous attributes and mitigating both over-smoothing and over-dominating effects, offering a flexible and high-performing solution.

Abstract: Attributed graph clustering partitions nodes by jointly exploiting node attributes and graph topology. It remains challenging due to attribute heterogeneity and representation degradation during graph learning. Real-world datasets often contain heterogeneous attributes, i.e., numerical and categorical attributes, complicating unified representation learning. This challenge becomes more complex in attributed graphs, where constructing a clustering-friendly graph structure from attributes and topology remains difficult. Under deep graph architectures, repeated graph propagation causes node embeddings to become overly similar, leading to the over-smoothing (OS) effect. Meanwhile, graph representation learning amplifies topological influence, making discriminative attribute information harder to exploit for clustering, an effect we refer to as over-dominating (OD). To bridge these gaps, an end-to-end framework, Any-type attributed Graph REpresentation lEarning (AGREE), is proposed. It unifies attributed graphs and any-type attributed data through multi-level alignment and similarity-based graph construction. Quaternion-based graph convolution strengthens attribute interaction to alleviate OD, while shallow graph architectures help relieve OS. The learned embeddings are jointly optimized for graph reconstruction and clustering, without requiring a predefined number of clusters during training. Experiments on diverse benchmarks show that AGREE achieves strong overall performance in accuracy, robustness, and adaptability.

</details>


### [811] [Leveraging AutoML for Sustainable Deep Learning: A Multi-Objective HPO Approach on Deep Shift Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23208)
*Leona Hennig,Marius Lindauer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper applies multi-fidelity multi-objective hyperparameter optimization with AutoML to design Deep Shift Neural Networks (DSNNs) for image classification, achieving 20% performance improvements and 60% emission reductions, while uncovering counterintuitive quantization strategies (e.g., low-precision quantization of smaller subnetworks can be optimal) that balance accuracy and energy efficiency across multiple backbone architectures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep Learning's computational demands pose environmental and resource challenges. DSNNs reduce complexity via shift operations but remain poorly understood and optimized compared to standard DNNs. The paper aims to provide insights into designing better DSNNs, especially for low-resource environments with complementary objectives like accuracy and energy consumption.

Method: The authors combine state-of-the-art multi-fidelity (MF) hyperparameter optimization (HPO) with multi-objective optimization using AutoML techniques, focusing on image classification. They treat accuracy and energy consumption as complementary objectives to find Pareto optimal configurations for DSNN design, and investigate quantization strategies across multiple backbone architectures.

Result: The approach produced significantly better DSNN configurations regarding loss and emissions compared to defaults, simultaneously increasing performance by ~20% and reducing emissions by over 60% in some cases. Surprising model-specific trade-offs emerged: quantizing smaller network portions with low precision can be optimal for energy consumption while retaining/improving performance, contrary to common expectations.

Conclusion: The findings highlight important nuances in quantization strategies and offer an automated approach to balancing energy efficiency and model performance. The results are corroborated across multiple backbone architectures, demonstrating that automated multi-objective MF HPO can effectively optimize DSNNs for sustainable deep learning.

Abstract: Deep Learning (DL) has advanced various fields by extracting complex patterns from large datasets. However, the computational demands of DL models pose environmental and resource challenges. Deep Shift Neural Networks (DSNNs) present a solution by leveraging shift operations to reduce computational complexity at inference. Compared to common DNNs, DSNNs are still less well understood and less well optimized. By leveraging AutoML techniques, we provide valuable insights into the potential of DSNNs and how to design them in a better way. We focus on image classification, a core task in computer vision, especially in low-resource environments. Since we consider complementary objectives such as accuracy and energy consumption, we combine state-of-the-art multi-fidelity (MF) hyperparameter optimization (HPO) with multi-objective optimization to find a set of Pareto optimal trade-offs on how to design DSNNs. Our approach led to significantly better configurations of DSNNs regarding loss and emissions compared to default DSNNs. This includes simultaneously increasing performance by about 20% and reducing emissions, in some cases by more than 60%. Investigating the behavior of quantized networks in terms of both emissions and accuracy, our experiments reveal surprising model-specific trade-offs, yielding the greatest energy savings. For example, in contrast to common expectations, quantizing smaller portions of the network with low precision can be optimal with respect to energy consumption while retaining or improving performance. We corroborated these findings across multiple backbone architectures, highlighting important nuances in quantization strategies and offering an automated approach to balancing energy efficiency and model performance.

</details>


### [812] [Efficient Network Inference via Hardware-Aware Architecture Search, Model Pruning & Quantization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23210)
*Lucas Heublein,Mark Deutel,Axel Plinge,Felix Ott*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper investigates efficient DNN inference for embedded GNSS interference monitoring by combining iterative structured pruning, post-training static quantization, and hardware-aware zero-shot NAS. Experiments on GNSS interference classification and characterization tasks demonstrate that model compression and hardware-aware design reduce model size, computational complexity, and memory usage while maintaining performance across three embedded platforms (iMXRT1062 MCU, Raspberry Pi Zero 2W, Raspberry Pi 5).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Embedded GNSS interference monitoring requires fast, memory-efficient inference for real-time processing of large volumes of raw IQ samples. There is a fundamental tension between the need for increasingly expressive DNNs for robust interference classification across diverse signal conditions and the deployability constraints of resource-constrained hardware.

Method: The authors investigate iterative structured pruning, post-training static quantization, and hardware-aware zero-shot neural architecture search (NAS), starting from MCUNet as a compact baseline. They analyze how model compression and automated architecture optimization affect model size, computational complexity, and memory usage while maintaining task performance.

Result: Experiments on a GNSS interference dataset (covering both classification and generalized characterization) show the benefits of combining compression and hardware-aware design for embedded deployment. Results provide practical guidance across three platforms: iMXRT1062 MCU, Raspberry Pi Zero 2W, and Raspberry Pi 5.

Conclusion: Combining iterative structured pruning, post-training static quantization, and hardware-aware zero-shot NAS enables efficient DNN inference for real-time GNSS interference monitoring on embedded platforms, balancing predictive performance with deployability constraints.

Abstract: Embedded global navigation satellite system (GNSS) interference monitoring requires fast and memory-efficient inference to process large volumes of raw in-phase and quadrature (IQ) samples in real time. At the same time, increasingly expressive deep neural networks (DNNs) are needed for robust interference classification and characterization across diverse signal conditions. This creates a fundamental tension between predictive performance and deployability on resource-constrained hardware. In this paper, we investigate efficient network inference for GNSS interference characterization using iterative structured pruning, post-training static quantization, and hardware-aware zero-shot neural architecture search (NAS). Starting from MCUNet as a compact baseline, we analyze how model compression and automated architecture optimization affect model size, computational complexity, and memory usage while maintaining task performance. Experiments on a GNSS interference dataset, covering both classification and generalized characterization, show the benefits of combining compression and hardware-aware design for embedded deployment. Our results provide practical guidance for developing compact machine learning (ML) models for real-time GNSS interference monitoring on embedded platforms (iMXRT1062 MCU, Raspberry Pi Zero 2W, and Raspberry Pi 5).

</details>


### [813] [Deep learning-based detection of cessation of breathing in pre-term infants](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23213)
*Dineo Serame,Lionel Tarassenko,Mauricio Villarroel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Deep learning models, particularly ConvNeXt with IP+PPG fusion, achieve 88.7% balanced accuracy for detecting apnoea-related breathing cessation in preterm infants using standard NICU monitoring signals, with signal modality (especially IP) impacting performance more than architectural complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Apnoea of prematurity remains difficult to detect reliably using standard NICU monitoring due to high false-positive alarm rates and missed short/irregular events; improving automated detection from routinely acquired signals could enhance clinical monitoring without additional hardware.

Method: Evaluated deep learning detection of COBE events using IP, ECG, and PPG signals from ~430 hours of NICU recordings (24 preterm infants). Compared shallow CNN, ResNets, and ConvNeXt architectures. Three independent reviewers annotated 346 COBE and 608 non-COBE events.

Result: Signal modality influenced performance more than architecture. Unimodal IP models achieved 86.8-88.0% balanced accuracy, outperforming ECG (62.6-69.7%) and PPG (65.1-66.4%). Multimodal fusion gave modest gains; best ConvNeXt (IP+PPG) achieved 88.7% balanced accuracy and 0.75 F1.

Conclusion: Deep learning on routine NICU signals can reliably detect COBE events, with IP being the most informative modality. Signal modality choice outweighs architectural complexity in data-constrained neonatal settings.

Abstract: Apnoea of prematurity is characterised by recurrent episodes of cessation of breathing and remains difficult to detect reliably using routinely monitored physiological signals in the Neonatal Intensive Care Unit (NICU). Existing bedside monitors rely primarily on respiratory rate and oxygen saturation thresholds, often generating high false-positive alarm rates and missing short or irregular events. Improving automated detection using routinely acquired clinical signals could enhance identification of clinically meaningful events without additional sensing hardware.
  We evaluated deep learning-based detection of apnoea-related Cessation Of BrEathing (COBE) events using impedance pneumography (IP), electrocardiography (ECG), and photoplethysmography (PPG) signals from approximately 430 hours of NICU recordings collected from 24 pre-term infants. Three independent reviewers annotated COBE events, producing a dataset of 346 COBE and 608 non-COBE events. We compared a shallow convolutional neural network (CNN), residual networks (ResNets), and a ConvNeXt architecture using an independent held-out test set.
  Across all architectures, detection performance was influenced more strongly by signal modality than by architectural complexity. Unimodal IP-based models achieved balanced accuracies of 86.8-88.0%, outperforming ECG-derived (62.6-69.7%) and PPG-derived (65.1-66.4%) respiratory surrogates. Multimodal fusion yielded modest improvements over IP alone. The best-performing model, a ConvNeXt architecture combining IP and PPG inputs, achieved 88.7% balanced accuracy and an F1 score of 0.75 on the independent test set.
  These findings demonstrate that deep learning models applied to routinely monitored NICU signals can reliably detect COBE events and highlight the importance of signal modality in data-constrained neonatal monitoring settings.

</details>


### [814] [Dynamic multi-agent deep reinforcement learning-based pricing and incentivization approach in multimodal transportation networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23257)
*Khadidja Kadem,Mostafa Ameli,Carlos Lima Azevedo,Mahdi Zargayouna,Latifa Oukhellou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a multi-agent deep reinforcement learning framework where a public authority and an SMS provider use dynamic incentivization and pricing strategies to balance equity, emissions, efficiency, and revenue in multimodal transportation systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: SMS demand is concentrated in high-density areas, limiting accessibility and causing uneven integration. Conflicting objectives among authorities (sustainability/equity), SMS providers (revenue), and travelers (cost minimization) need coordinated resolution.

Method: Two RL agents: (i) public authority allocates spatio-temporal public transport incentives; (ii) SMS provider dynamically adjusts fares. Agents interact with evolving demand, congestion, and network conditions in a multi-agent deep RL framework.

Result: Dynamic incentivization reduces congestion peaks, lowers commuter costs by ~20% and emissions by ~10%, nearly doubles public transport profit, and improves benefit distribution equity. Combined dynamic pricing balances conflicting objectives.

Conclusion: The proposed multi-agent RL approach serves as a decision-support tool for sustainable and equitable multimodal mobility planning.

Abstract: In multimodal transportation systems, shared mobility services (SMSs) are promoted for their potential to enhance flexibility and reduce congestion. However, SMS demand is often concentrated in high-density areas, which can limit the effectiveness and accessibility for various commuter groups. This uneven integration challenges transportation system efficiency, especially in terms of emissions and spatial equity. Addressing these issues requires coordination among multiple stakeholders whose objectives frequently conflict. Whereas authorities aim to ensure sustainable and equitable mobility, SMS providers focus on revenue maximization, and travelers seek to minimize personal travel costs. This paper proposes a multi-agent deep reinforcement learning framework that captures these interactions through dynamic pricing and incentivization strategies for SMSs and public transport. The framework integrates two reinforcement learning (RL) agents: (i) a public authority that allocates spatio-temporal public transport incentives to improve equity, emissions, and efficiency, and (ii) an SMS provider that dynamically adjusts fares to optimize revenue. The agents interact with the transportation system and adapt strategies in response to evolving demand, congestion, and network conditions. Numerical experiments conducted over a three-hour morning peak period show that dynamic incentivization effectively reduces congestion peaks, lowers commuters' costs by around 20% and emissions by approximately 10%, while nearly doubling public transport profit and supporting a more equitable distribution of benefits. When combined with dynamic SMS pricing, the two RL agents demonstrate the ability to balance conflicting objectives between private providers and public authorities. The proposed approach provides a decision-support tool for sustainable and equitable multimodal mobility planning.

</details>


### [815] [Exposing the Illusion of Erasure in Knowledge Editing for LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23276)
*Advik Raj Basani,Anshuman Chhabra*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper reveals that Knowledge Editing (KE) methods do not fully erase or overwrite original facts in LLMs. Instead, they redistribute knowledge via low-rank updates, act as targeted suppression mechanisms, and leave edited knowledge in narrow, anisotropic regions vulnerable to adversarial elicitation, proving KE algorithms are inherently bypassable.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Knowledge Editing has emerged as a way to update facts in LLMs without costly retraining, but its reliability and underlying mechanisms remain poorly understood.

Method: The authors examine KE from an adversarial elicitation perspective, conduct mechanistic analysis of popular KE methods, analyze low-rank updates, and study the loss landscape of edited knowledge.

Result: Edited knowledge is often not fully erased and continues to surface. Low-rank updates redistribute rather than overwrite knowledge. KE methods act as targeted suppression mechanisms, not removal. Edited knowledge resides in narrow, anisotropic regions highly sensitive to perturbations, making them vulnerable to indirect prompting and attacks.

Conclusion: KE algorithms are inherently bypassable due to profound architectural vulnerabilities, motivating a fundamental reevaluation of how post-hoc updates are deployed in LLM applications.

Abstract: Knowledge Editing (KE) has emerged as a frontier for updating specific facts in LLMs without costly retraining, but its reliability and underlying mechanisms remain poorly understood. In this work, we examine KE from an adversarial elicitation perspective, revealing that edited knowledge is often not fully erased and continues to surface, with consistent failures observed across diverse model architectures. To explain this behavior, we conduct a mechanistic analysis of popular KE methods. We show that low-rank updates do not overwrite existing knowledge but instead redistribute it within the model's representation space. Furthermore, we find that these methods act as targeted suppression mechanisms that reduce the likelihood of expressing original facts, rather than removing them from the model. Analysis of the loss landscape reveals that edited knowledge lies in narrow, anisotropic regions that are highly sensitive to perturbations, making them highly vulnerable to indirect prompting and adversarial attacks. By exposing these profound architectural vulnerabilities, our work proves that KE algorithms are inherently bypassable and motivates a fundamental reevaluation of how we deploy post-hoc updates in several LLM applications.

</details>


### [816] [Non-asymptotic estimates of the minimal risk in statistical learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23295)
*Liming Wu,Sen Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proves concentration inequalities for error probabilities in Empirical Risk Principle (ERP), relaxing boundedness to Gaussian/exponential integrability. It provides non-asymptotic high-confidence lower and upper bounds for minimal risk, with the lower bound's confidence independent of training parameters/dimension, and the upper bound's confidence high when sample size exceeds the box dimension of the parameter set in Orlicz metric.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To derive concentration inequalities for error probabilities in ERP under relaxed conditions (Gaussian/exponential integrability instead of boundedness), enabling efficient detection of learning machine deficiency and establishing high-confidence bounds for minimal risk.

Method: Uses Talagrand's concentration inequalities (sharp versions by Bousquet and Klein-Rio), transport-entropy inequalities, and recent empirical process theory; relaxes boundedness to Gaussian or exponential integrability conditions; employs Orlicz metric d_{ψ_1} and box dimension of parameter set.

Result: Lower bound confidence is independent of number of training parameters and input dimension; upper bound confidence is high when sample size n significantly exceeds the box dimension of Θ in Orlicz metric d_{ψ_1}.

Conclusion: Provides non-asymptotic high-confidence bounds for minimal risk under relaxed integrability conditions, with dimension-free lower bound confidence and sample-size-dependent upper bound confidence tied to parameter set complexity.

Abstract: In this paper we prove some concentration inequalities for two types of error probabilities in the Empirical Risk Principle (ERP) in statistical learning, which provide a lower bound and an upper bound for the minimal risk (in terms of the minimal empirical risk) with non-asymptotic high confidence. The usual boundedness condition of the empirical risk function is relaxed to the Gaussian or exponential integrability condition. The confidence of the lower bound of the minimal risk is shown to be independent of the number of training parameters and the dimension of the input vectors, allowing one to detect the deficiency of a learning machine efficiently; and the confidence of the upper bound of the minimal risk is proved to be high provided that the sample size $n$ is much greater than the box dimension of the parameter set $Θ$ in the Orlicz metric $d_{ψ_1}$ associated with the risk functions. Our work is based on Talagrand's concentration inequalities (the sharp versions by Bousquet and Klein-Rio), transport-entropy inequalities and the recent progress in the theory of empirical processes and statistical learning.

</details>


### [817] [GRIMIP: A General Framework for Instance-Specific Configuration of MIP Solvers Using LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23299)
*Yidong Luo,Xuemin Chen,Chenguang Wang,Fangzhou Zhu,Tao Zhong,Tianshu Yu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GRIMIP is a hybrid intelligence framework that integrates Large Language Models with Bayesian Optimization to automatically configure MIP solver hyperparameters, achieving over 40% reduction in Primal-Dual Integral on hard instances and outperforming existing methods like SMAC.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Configuring MIP solver hyperparameters is a high-dimensional, instance-dependent optimization problem where default settings are often suboptimal, and traditional tuning methods suffer from cold-start problems, inefficient search, or heavy reliance on expert experience.

Method: GRIMIP synergistically integrates Large Language Models (LLMs) with Bayesian Optimization (BO), using the LLM as a complete probabilistic surrogate within the BO loop to enable instance-specific configuration with improved sample efficiency and reduced evaluation costs.

Result: On seven benchmarks including MIPLIB, GRIMIP achieves over 40% reduction in Primal-Dual Integral on hard instances, outperforming SMAC and other LLM-assisted BO methods.

Conclusion: By granting LLMs sufficient autonomy, GRIMIP combines expert-level reasoning of LLMs with efficient BO search, achieving state-of-the-art performance in MIP solver hyperparameter configuration.

Abstract: Configuring the hyperparameters of Mixed-integer programming (MIP) solvers is a high-dimensional, instance-dependent optimization problem where suboptimal settings can degrade solving time by orders of magnitude. Default configurations are often suboptimal, while traditional tuning methods either suffer from the ``cold-start'' problem and inefficient search or heavily rely on expert experience. This paper introduces \textbf{GRIMIP} (\textbf{\underline{G}}eneral \textbf{\underline{R}}easoning for \textbf{\underline{I}}nstance-specific \textbf{\underline{MIP}} configuration), a novel hybrid intelligence framework that synergistically integrates the semantic reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) with the sample-efficient search of Bayesian Optimization (BO). GRIMIP enables the LLM to function as a complete probabilistic surrogate within the BO loop, significantly improving performance and reducing sampling and evaluation costs. On seven benchmarks including MIPLIB, GRIMIP achieves over 40\% reduction in Primal-Dual Integral on hard instances, outperforming SMAC and other LLM-assisted BO methods. By granting LLMs sufficient autonomy, GRIMIP combines the expert-level reasoning of LLMs with the efficient search of BO, achieving state-of-the-art performance.

</details>


### [818] [Superhuman AI for Generals.io Using Self-Play Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23348)
*Matej Straka,Viliam Lisý,Martin Schmid*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A superhuman AI agent for Generals.io was developed using a JAX-native simulator achieving ~10,000x speedup, enabling a vision transformer policy trained via self-play to reach #1 on the public 1v1 leaderboard.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To create a superhuman AI agent for Generals.io, a real-time strategy game requiring long-horizon planning and short-term tactics under imperfect information, and to investigate what matters for performance when the data bottleneck is removed by a fast simulator.

Method: Built a JAX-native simulator achieving tens of millions of frames per second (~10,000x speedup over prior simulator). Trained a Vision Transformer policy end-to-end using self-play with policy-gradient loop, sparse win/loss reward, top-advantage sample filtering, and exponential moving average of policy parameters. Training lasted 4 days on 4x NVIDIA H200 GPUs.

Result: Agent reached #1 on the public 1v1 leaderboard of 5,000+ human players, leading second place by the same margin as second to 25th. Beat top two humans 199-70 across 269 ladder matches.

Conclusion: A fast simulator removes the data bottleneck, enabling superhuman performance. The findings highlight what matters and what does not in agent design when data is plentiful.

Abstract: We present a superhuman AI agent for Generals.io, a real-time strategy game that requires both long-horizon planning and short-term tactics under strong imperfect information. Trained for four days on 4x NVIDIA H200 GPUs, our agent reaches #1 on the public 1v1 leaderboard of over 5,000 human players, leading the second-ranked player by the same margin that separates second place from 25th, and beats the two top-ranked humans head-to-head with a combined 199-70 record across 269 ladder matches. A key enabler is a JAX-native simulator that reaches tens of millions of frames per second on a single GPU, roughly a 10,000x speedup over the prior simulator. On top of this, we train a vision transformer policy end-to-end by self-play with a policy-gradient loop and sparse win/loss reward, using top-advantage sample filtering and an exponential moving average of the policy parameters. Taken together, our findings highlight what matters, and what does not, once a fast simulator removes the data bottleneck.

</details>


### [819] [SOAP-Bubbles: Structured Weight Uncertainty for Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23357)
*Adrian Robert Minut,Nico Daheim,Marco Miani,Mohammad Emtiyaz Khan,Wu Lin,Thomas Möllenhoff*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces EVON, a new optimizer that combines the SOAP preconditioner with IVON's variational inference to produce structured (non-diagonal) weight uncertainty at the same cost as SOAP, outperforming diagonal-covariance methods in language model pretraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Structured weight-uncertainty can improve deep learning but remains costly to estimate and difficult to implement; the authors aim to make it more practical and scalable.

Method: The authors adapt the SOAP optimizer by running IVON (a diagonal-covariance variational method) in the eigenspace of SOAP's preconditioner, then use the preconditioner to transform the diagonal estimate into a non-diagonal covariance.

Result: EVON recovers the exact Gaussian covariance for logistic regression and yields significantly better results than existing diagonal-covariance methods for language model pretraining.

Conclusion: This work makes it easier to estimate more expressive posterior distributions for deep learning at scale, with costs similar to SOAP and requiring no drastic pipeline changes.

Abstract: Structured weight-uncertainty can improve many aspects of deep learning, but it remains costly to estimate and difficult to implement. Here, we show that these issues can be addressed by adapting the SOAP optimizer. Our key idea is to run IVON, an existing diagonal-covariance variational method, in the eigenspace of SOAP's preconditioner and then use the preconditioner to transform the diagonal estimate into a non-diagonal covariance. The resulting method has costs similar to those of SOAP and requires no drastic changes to training pipelines. We call the posteriors obtained in this way SOAP-Bubbles and our new optimizer Eigenspace-VON (EVON). We show that, for logistic regression, EVON recovers the exact Gaussian covariance and that, for language model pretraining, it yields significantly better results than existing diagonal-covariance methods. Our work makes it easier to estimate more expressive posterior distributions for deep learning at scale.

</details>


### [820] [Adaptive Hard-Soft Physics-Informed Neural Networks for Robust Boundary-Constrained PDE Solving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23359)
*Duc Tien Nguyen,Trinh Minh Tuan,Nguyen Duc Manh,Vu Linh Nguyen,Dinh Gia Ninh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes HSPINN, a unified hard-soft physics-informed neural network that enforces Dirichlet and periodic boundary conditions exactly (hard constraints) while treating PDE residuals, Neumann fluxes, and initial conditions as soft constraints, with adaptive loss weighting via inverse-share softmax, achieving faster convergence and higher accuracy than conventional PINNs on elliptic, parabolic, and hyperbolic problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional PINNs suffer from slow convergence, sensitivity to loss weight scaling, and inaccurate boundary enforcement due to poor conditioning of the optimization landscape caused by treating all constraints as soft penalty terms in a composite loss.

Method: A unified hard-soft PINN (HSPINN) framework where Dirichlet and periodic boundary conditions are exactly enforced via analytical/polynomial lifting, masking functions, and periodic feature mappings, while PDE residuals, Neumann fluxes, and initial conditions remain soft constraints. An inverse-share softmax strategy adaptively balances loss components without manual tuning.

Result: HSPINN consistently achieves faster convergence, higher accuracy, and greater stability than conventional PINNs across representative elliptic (Poisson), parabolic (Burgers), and hyperbolic (convection with periodic boundaries) problems.

Conclusion: The proposed HSPINN establishes a general and scalable foundation for physics-constrained deep learning by ensuring boundary admissibility throughout optimization and improving convergence efficiency and numerical robustness.

Abstract: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) provide an effective way to solve partial differential equations (PDEs) by embedding physical principles into the learning process. However, the conventional PINN formulation, in which all constraints are imposed as soft penalty terms within a composite loss, often exhibits slow convergence, sensitivity to loss weight scaling, and inaccurate boundary enforcement due to poor conditioning of the optimization landscape. To address these limitations, this study proposes a unified hard--soft physics--informed neural network (HSPINN) with adaptive loss weighting. In this framework, Dirichlet and periodic boundary conditions are enforced exactly by construction through analytical or polynomial lifting, masking functions, and periodic feature mappings, while the governing PDE residuals, Neumann fluxes, and initial conditions are treated as soft constraints. An inverse-share softmax strategy dynamically balances the relative importance of individual loss components during training, eliminating manual penalty tuning and improving gradient stability. This formulation ensures boundary admissibility throughout optimization and enhances convergence efficiency and numerical robustness. Applications to representative elliptic (Poisson), parabolic (Burgers), and hyperbolic (convection with periodic boundaries) problems demonstrate that HSPINN consistently achieves faster convergence, higher accuracy, and greater stability than conventional PINNs, establishing a general and scalable foundation for physics-constrained deep learning across science and technology.

</details>


### [821] [Rethinking Molecular Graph Backdoors under Chemistry-aware Admission](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23361)
*Thinh T. H. Nguyen,Sze Jue Yang,Khoa D. Doan,Chee Seng Chan,Kok-Seng Wong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper identifies that real molecular learning pipelines have an admission stage (ChemGuard) that filters out chemically invalid or representation-inconsistent molecules, which undermines many existing graph-based backdoor attacks. The authors then propose ChemBack, a chemically valid, admission-aware backdoor attack that achieves high success rates even after passing admission checks, revealing that while chemistry-aware admission helps, chemically valid molecular backdoors remain a practical threat.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing backdoor attacks on molecular GNNs are evaluated on abstract graph edits without considering real-world molecular learning pipelines, which enforce parsing, sanitization, canonicalization, and consistency checks. This discrepancy overestimates the threat of many backdoor attacks.

Method: The authors formalize ChemGuard, an operational admission protocol requiring that a molecular string is sanitizable and the reconstructed graph matches the submitted graph. They then propose ChemBack, an admission-aware attack that constructs chemically feasible motif-anchor attachments and ranks candidates by fingerprint-based Tanimoto similarity to clean target-class molecules, without any model access.

Result: Many existing graph-based backdoors lose efficacy under ChemGuard admission. ChemBack achieves high attack success rates with fully admitted poisons across multiple benchmarks, validators, architectures, and defenses, while preserving clean accuracy.

Conclusion: Chemistry-aware admission (ChemGuard) suppresses many graph-only backdoors, but chemically valid and target-aligned molecular backdoors (ChemBack) remain a practical threat, revealing a two-sided lesson for molecular security.

Abstract: Backdoor attacks on molecular graph neural networks (GNNs) are typically evaluated as abstract graph edits, but real molecular learning pipelines do not train on arbitrary graphs. Molecular records must first survive parsing, sanitization, canonicalization, and graph-string consistency checks. We formalize this overlooked admission stage as ChemGuard, an operational protocol for testing whether a submitted molecular record can enter a realistic learning pipeline, while complementing existing defenses. ChemGuard admits a record only when its molecular string is sanitizable and the graph reconstructed from that string matches the submitted molecular graph. Under this operational view, many existing graph-based backdoors lose much of their apparent efficacy because their poisons are chemically invalid or representation-inconsistent. We then show that admission checks alone are insufficient to rule out molecular backdoors. We propose ChemBack, an admission-aware molecular backdoor attack that constructs chemically feasible motif-anchor attachments and ranks admitted candidates by fingerprint-based Tanimoto similarity to clean target-class molecules.
