ChemBack is model-free during trigger selection, using molecular structures, target labels, fingerprints, and public validity checks, but no victim model, surrogate GNN, learned embedding, gradient, logit, or training-code access. Across molecular benchmarks, validators, architectures, and defenses, \textbf{ChemBack} achieves high attack success with fully admitted poisons while preserving clean accuracy. Our results reveal a two-sided lesson, chemistry-aware admission suppresses many graph-only backdoors, yet chemically valid and target-aligned molecular backdoors remain a practical threat.

</details>


### [822] [Convergence of Gradient Descent for General Neural Network Architectures Beyond the NTK Regime](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23364)
*Yuqing Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a convergence framework for analyzing gradient descent dynamics across a broad family of neural network architectures (including pre-normalized multi-layer transformers) beyond the NTK regime, proving convergence to stationary point neighborhoods under mild assumptions using PL-type inequalities and smoothness conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Training dynamics is central to understanding neural networks, yet its theoretical analysis remains difficult even for simple architectures and becomes substantially more challenging for general modern architectures.

Method: The framework is formulated at the level of network blocks, proving GD convergence via: (1) an iterate-dependent PL-type inequality through analyticity and measure-zero arguments, and (2) Lipschitz smoothness along the GD trajectory through polynomial generalized smoothness and a local relaxed dissipative condition.

Result: For almost all initializations, GD with regular learning rates converges to the neighborhood of a stationary point. The learning rate scale depends on depth and effective bottleneck dimensions rather than the largest width. Structural nondegeneracy implications for residual connections and function composition are derived, along with a generic characterization of global minimizers.

Conclusion: The framework provides a unified theoretical analysis of GD dynamics for modern architectures beyond NTK regime, with practical insights on learning rate scaling under Xavier initialization and architectural scaling.

Abstract: Training dynamics is central to understanding neural networks, yet its theoretical analysis remains difficult even for simple architectures and becomes substantially more challenging for general modern architectures. In this paper, we propose a convergence framework for analyzing gradient descent (GD) dynamics under a broad family of neural network architectures and datasets beyond the neural tangent kernel (NTK) regime. The framework is formulated at the level of network blocks and covers architectures including pre-normalized multi-layer transformers. More precisely, under mild assumptions, we prove that for almost all initializations, GD with regular learning rates converges to the neighbourhood of a stationary point. This is mainly proved by establishing an iterate-dependent PL-type inequality through analyticity and measure-zero arguments, and by proving Lipschitz smoothness along the GD trajectory through polynomial generalized smoothness and a local relaxed dissipative condition. We further interpret the theorem under Xavier initialization and practical architectural scaling, showing that the learning rate scale depends on the depth and effective bottleneck dimensions rather than the largest width. Finally, we derive structural nondegeneracy implications for residual connections and function composition, and provide a generic characterization of global minimizers within our framework.

</details>


### [823] [Distribution-Aware Diffusion-LLM for Robust Ultra-Long-Term Time Series Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23391)
*Falguni Ghosh,Vahid Hashemi,Bernhard Kainz*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Diffusion-LLM integrates a conditional diffusion model into an LLM-based forecasting pipeline to improve probabilistic modeling and semantic alignment for time series forecasting, outperforming existing LLM baselines on six benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs face challenges in multimodal time series forecasting due to a lack of calibrated probabilistic modeling for non-text data and difficulty aligning heterogeneous representations.

Method: A framework that integrates a conditional diffusion model into an LLM-based forecasting pipeline, jointly learning the conditional distribution of future data while improving semantic alignment in a shared latent space.

Result: Outperforms existing LLM-based baselines on six long-term forecasting benchmarks (ETT, Weather, ECL), with notable gains in ultra-long-term and few-shot forecasting.

Conclusion: Distribution-aware regularization enhances robustness and generalization in time series LLMs, demonstrating the value of diffusion-based probabilistic modeling for multimodal forecasting.

Abstract: Time series forecasting is a fundamental machine learning task. Recent work has explored Large Language Models (LLMs) for this purpose due to their strong generalization, pattern recognition, and zero-shot or few-shot capabilities. Despite their suitability for long-context learning, LLMs face challenges in multimodal settings: they lack calibrated probabilistic modeling for non-text data and struggle to align heterogeneous representations. To address these issues, we propose a new framework Diffusion-LLM that integrates a conditional diffusion model into an LLM-based forecasting pipeline. This joint design enables learning the conditional distribution of future data while improving semantic alignment in a shared latent space. We evaluate Diffusion-LLM on six long-term forecasting benchmarks, including ETT, Weather, and ECL. Our method consistently outperforms existing LLM-based baseline, achieving notable gains in ultra-long-term and few-shot forecasting and demonstrating the value of distribution-aware regularization for enhancing robustness and generalization in time series LLMs.

</details>


### [824] [Physics-Informed Modeling for Wood Thermal Analysis and Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23402)
*Jingren Xie,Alex John Buckthal,Ryan Anthony O'Connor,Isak Worre Foged,Dim P. Papadopoulos*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces physics-informed deep learning frameworks (PICNNs and PInteCNNs) that integrate partial differential equations to predict pixel-level thermal responses of spatially heterogeneous wood materials, outperforming data-driven approaches in accuracy, interpretability, and handling material heterogeneity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Wood materials have complex, spatially varying thermal properties that challenge traditional homogeneity assumptions. Existing data-driven approaches are uninterpretable black boxes that prioritize statistical correlation and may absorb experimental noise rather than thermodynamic plausibility.

Method: Two physics-informed frameworks are proposed: (1) Physics-Informed Convolutional Neural Networks (PICNNs) embedding physics as a soft penalty term in the loss function, and (2) Physics-Integrated Convolutional Neural Networks (PInteCNNs) hard-coding an analytical approximator-predictor-corrector solver into CNNs. Both enforce a normalized 2D steady-state heat transfer equation. Validation uses three multimodal datasets: Poplar, Grandis-CC, and Grandis-RC wood samples.

Result: The physics-informed frameworks outperform data-driven approaches in handling complex wood material heterogeneity, balancing predictive accuracy, physical interpretability, and intra-species diversity, while enabling extraction of interpretable physical parameters.

Conclusion: Embedding physical inductive biases successfully balances predictive accuracy, physical interpretability, and intra-species diversity for thermal response prediction in heterogeneous wood materials.

Abstract: Wood materials exhibit complex, spatially varying thermal properties that challenge traditional architectural assumptions of material homogeneity. Although data-driven approaches can directly map wood RGB images to their corresponding thermal responses, they operate as uninterpretable black boxes that prioritize statistical correlation and may absorb experimental noise rather than thermodynamic plausibility. To address these limitations, we present physics-informed deep learning frameworks that integrate partial differential equations (PDEs) to predict pixel-level thermal responses of spatially heterogeneous wood materials using wood RGB images and testbed temperature maps. Specifically, we investigate two distinct approaches to enforcing a normalized 2D steady-state heat transfer equation derived from the general heat transfer equation: Physics-Informed Convolutional Neural Networks (PICNNs), which embed physics as a soft penalty term in the loss function, and Physics-Integrated Convolutional Neural Networks (PInteCNNs), which hard-code an analytical approximator-predictor-corrector solver directly into convolutional neural networks. To validate our proposed approaches, we collect three real-world multimodal datasets of Poplar, Grandis Cross-Cut (Grandis-CC), and Grandis Radial-Cut (Grandis-RC) wood samples. We further demonstrate that embedding physical inductive biases successfully balances predictive accuracy, physical interpretability, and intra-species diversity, outperforming data-driven approaches in handling complex wood material heterogeneity and enabling the extraction of interpretable physical parameters. Project: https://zekifayes.github.io/pim

</details>


### [825] [HyperQuant: A Rate-Distortion-Optimal Quantization Pipeline for Large Language and Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23406)
*Yuval Domb,Hadar Sackstein,Tomer Solberg*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HyperQuant is a unified post-training quantization pipeline for weights and KV cache of large language and diffusion transformers, achieving near-lossless compression (e.g., ~3.9x on weights, ~3.79x on KV cache at 4 bps) by combining Randomized Hadamard Transform, optimal lattice quantization (E8, D4, A2, Z), entropy-optimized Rice coding, and bias-correction for KV cache.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a unified, high-performance post-training quantization method that compresses both weights and KV cache for large transformers (LLMs and diffusion models) with near-lossless quality, outperforming existing schemes like HIGGS, TurboQuant, and OCTOPUS across multiple bit-widths.

Method: Four components: (i) per-tile Randomized Hadamard Transform (RHT) to Gaussianize per-coordinate distributions; (ii) low-dimensional optimal lattice quantization (E8, D4, A2, or Z); (iii) lossless bit-stripping with near-entropy-optimal variable-length Rice coding for lattice indices; (iv) bias-correction for KV cache to preserve unbiased inner products. Integrates with 8-bit/4-bit Tensor-Core MMA paths (fp8-e4m3, int8, nvfp4, mxfp4).

Result: Outperforms HIGGS at every operating point (3–5 bps on weights); beats TurboQuant and OCTOPUS on KV quantization down to 1.7 bps. On H100 at 4 bps, compresses linear weights ~3.9x and KV cache ~3.79x near-losslessly. Quantizes the 19B LTX-2 DiT video model with no observable per-frame artifacts. int8 outperforms fp8 on post-RHT lattice output.

Conclusion: HyperQuant demonstrates that combining RHT, optimal lattice quantization, Rice coding, and bias correction yields a state-of-the-art unified quantization pipeline for both weights and KV cache across LLMs and diffusion transformers, with practical Tensor-Core integration.

Abstract: We present HyperQuant (Hadamard, optimallY Packing, Entropy Rice-coding), a unified post-training quantization pipeline for the weights and the KV cache of large language and diffusion transformers. Across a suite of self-contained experiments (Table 1), HyperQuant outperforms the recent HIGGS scheme at every operating point from 3 to 5 bits per scalar (bps) on weights, and beats both TurboQuant and OCTOPUS on KV quantization down to 1.7 bps. Beyond the LLM setting, HyperQuant quantizes the 19B-parameter LTX-2 DiT video model with no observable per-frame artifacts. End-to-end on an H100 at 4 bps, HyperQuant compresses the linear weights ~3.9x and the KV cache ~3.79x at near-lossless quality.
  HyperQuant combines four known ideas into a single construction: (i) a per-tile Randomized Hadamard Transform that makes the per-coordinate distribution of weights and activations approximately Gaussian; (ii) quantization to a low-dimensional optimal lattice (E8, D4, A2, or Z); (iii) lossless bit-stripping and near-entropy-optimal variable-length Rice coding of the lattice indices; and (iv) bias-correction methods for the KV cache that keep the reconstruction unbiased under inner products, preserving attention semantics. We further integrate the pipeline with 8-bit and 4-bit Tensor-Core MMA paths (fp8-e4m3, int8, nvfp4, mxfp4), and find that int8 beats fp8 on the post-RHT lattice output. Project page: https://moonmath.ai/hyperquant/

</details>


### [826] [Differential Spectral Damping Gap Adaptive Regularization for Ill-Conditioned Kernel Methods](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23407)
*Praveg Vashishtha*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Differential Spectral Damping (DSD), a reliability-aware regularization method that adapts damping based on eigengap structure, outperforming standard Tikhonov regularization for ill-conditioned kernel methods like LSTSVM, achieving +4.8 to +10.4 percentage point accuracy gains on high-dimensional classification benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Kernel methods requiring matrix inversion (e.g., LSTSVM) suffer from exponential eigenvalue decay leading to severe ill-conditioning. Standard Tikhonov regularization applies uniform damping regardless of eigenvector reliability, which is suboptimal.

Method: Differential Spectral Damping (DSD): a regularization formula that adapts penalty strength based on localized eigengap structure, derived from Davis-Kahan sin(Θ) theorem. Preserves eigenvectors with large spectral gaps (reliable) while suppressing those with small gaps (corrupted beyond recovery).

Result: DSD improves LSTSVM accuracy by +4.8 pp on GINA (d=970, Cohen's d=4.49, p<0.0001), +10.4 pp at d=200, +2.6 pp on Madelon (d=500). On pre-image reconstruction, matches Tikhonov at high noise (p=0.99), slightly underperforms at lower noise; both reduce naive inversion error by 66×.

Conclusion: DSD is effective for severely ill-conditioned regimes (d ≥ 100, condition number > 10³) where simpler methods suffice otherwise. Provides clear deployment guidance for practitioners.

Abstract: Kernel methods requiring matrix inversion -- particularly Least-Squares Twin Support Vector Machines (LSTSVM) -- suffer from exponential eigenvalue decay in their system matrices, producing severely ill-conditioned problems where standard Tikhonov regularization applies uniform damping regardless of eigenvector reliability. We propose Differential Spectral Damping (DSD), a regularization formula that adapts its penalty to localized eigengap structure: preserving eigenvectors with large spectral gaps (reliable per Davis-Kahan perturbation theory) while aggressively suppressing those with small gaps (directionally corrupted beyond recovery). We motivate DSD through a principled design procedure grounded in the Davis-Kahan $\sin(Θ)$ theorem, systematically deriving the requirements for a reliability-aware damping function and selecting the exponential form for its smoothness, differentiability, and natural saturation properties. Through rigorous paired testing with fairly optimized baselines (including gradient-optimized Tikhonov receiving equal optimization opportunity), we demonstrate that DSD improves LSTSVM classification accuracy by +4.8 percentage points on real-world GINA ($d=970$, Cohen's $d = 4.49$, $p < 0.0001$), +10.4 percentage points at $d=200$, and +2.6 percentage points on Madelon ($d=500$) -- all using only principled spectral initialization while Tikhonov receives grid search. For pre-image reconstruction on manifold data, DSD ties Tikhonov at high perturbation noise ($p=0.99$) but slightly underperforms at lower noise levels; both reduce naive inversion error by $66\times$. We characterize the precise operating regime ($d \geq 100$, condition number $> 10^3$) and document where simpler methods suffice, providing practitioners with clear deployment guidance.

</details>


### [827] [Leveraging Similarities in Multi-Armed Bandits](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23414)
*Khaled Eldowa,Thibaud Rahier,Augustin Cablant,Panayotis Mertikopoulos,Pierre Gaillard*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper studies online learning with similarity-structured action sets (encoded as a tree). It shows one-point bandit feedback cannot exploit similarity structure, but proposes algorithms for richer feedback models (semi-bandit to two-point) that achieve regret bounds replacing action count K with a similarity-aware effective count K_eff, with application to Lipschitz bandits.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Actions in online learning often share inherent similarities (latent traits, tags, hierarchical structure), but standard bandit algorithms treat them independently. The paper aims to understand when and how similarity structure can be exploited under different feedback models.

Method: The authors establish an impossibility result showing one-point bandit feedback cannot leverage tree-induced similarity. They then develop a unified algorithmic framework that adapts to richer feedback models (semi-bandit, multi-point, two-point), achieving regret bounds with K_eff replacing K.

Result: The proposed algorithms achieve best-of-both-worlds guarantees and provably exploit action similarities. Under two-point feedback, they achieve √T regret in Lipschitz bandits when d ≤ 2.

Conclusion: Similarity structure can be exploited under sufficient feedback richness (semi-bandit or multi-point), but not under minimal one-point bandit feedback. The work provides tight characterization of when similarity helps in online learning.

Abstract: In many online learning and bandit problems, the actions we consider possess inherent similarities--for instance because they share latent traits, tags, or hierarchical structure. We study online learning with a similarity-structured action set, encoded by a rooted tree whose leaves are the actions and whose levels quantify how closely two actions are related. The loss sequence is assumed tree-compatible: losses of similar actions are constrained to be close. We establish an impossibility result showing that usual one-point bandit feedback cannot, in general, leverage range or tree-induced similarity, even under very strong similarity constraints. We then provide a unified set of algorithms which adapt to a wide range of richer feedback models, from semi-bandit feedback down to multi-point bandit protocols, including the minimal two-point feedback setting. We show these algorithms exhibit best-of-both-worlds guarantees and provably exploit action similarities by replacing the number of actions $K$ by a similarity-aware effective number of actions $K_{\mathrm{eff}}$ in the regret bounds. As an application, we show that under two-point feedback, it is possible to achieve $\sqrt{T}$ regret in Lipschitz bandits when $d \leq 2$.

</details>


### [828] [GRINQH: Graded Input-based Quantization Hierarchy for Efficient LLM Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23419)
*Jette Oberländer,Jan Finkbeiner,Catherine M. Schöfmann,Emre Neftci*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GRINQH is a weight-only post-training quantization framework that unifies quantization and sparsification to accelerate LLM decoding by dynamically assigning weight channels to different precision levels based on activation magnitudes, achieving state-of-the-art Pareto frontier for generation quality vs. inference speed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM autoregressive decoding is bottlenecked by GPU memory bandwidth, especially in edge computing. Existing quantization methods treat inference uniformly, failing to exploit the asymmetry between the compute-bound prefill stage and the memory-bound decoding stage.

Method: GRINQH uses activation magnitudes as a proxy for computational importance to dynamically assign weight channels to different precision levels, unifying quantization and sparsification. It employs a hierarchical nested memory layout for multi-precision storage in a custom GPU kernel.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art fixed- and mixed-precision baselines at comparable 3- and 4-bit settings on Llama3 and Qwen3 models, and enables effective 2-bit generation. Theoretical speedups are experimentally verified.

Conclusion: GRINQH establishes a new state-of-the-art Pareto frontier for LLM generation, enabling a dynamic trade-off between generation quality and inference speed.

Abstract: Autoregressive decoding with LLMs is primarily bottlenecked by GPU memory bandwidth, especially in edge-computing settings. While quantization is essential for mitigating this bottleneck, most existing methods treat inference as a uniform process and fail to account for the asymmetry between the compute-bound prefill stage and the memory-bound decoding stage. We propose GRINQH (GRaded INput-based Quantization Hierarchy), a weight-only post-training quantization framework that accelerates decoding by unifying quantization and sparsification. GRINQH leverages activation magnitudes as a proxy for computational importance to dynamically assign weight channels to different precision levels, enabling flexible average bit widths during decoding. Evaluated on Llama3 and Qwen3 models, GRINQH outperforms state-of-the-art fixed- and mixed-precision baselines at comparable 3- and 4-bit settings, even enabling effective 2-bit generation. We experimentally verify theoretical speedups by leveraging a hierarchical nested memory layout for multi-precision storage in a custom GPU kernel. Ultimately, GRINQH establishes a new state-of-the-art Pareto frontier for LLM generation, enabling a dynamic trade-off between generation quality and inference speed.

</details>


### [829] [Interpretable Kolmogorov-Arnold Network with Feature-Isolated Temporal Attention Mechanism for Electricity Load Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23425)
*Jinhao Li,Hao Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LoadKAN is a hybrid interpretable framework for electricity load forecasting that combines a feature-isolated temporal attention mechanism with a Kolmogorov-Arnold network (KAN), achieving competitive performance against black-box deep learning models while enabling granular interpretability of non-linear relationships between mobility patterns and electricity load across three U.S. markets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing deep learning load forecasting models are black-box and lack interpretability; KAN offers promise due to learnable activation functions but faces challenges in modeling complex temporal patterns directly, and simple integration into existing architectures fails to leverage KAN's interpretability strengths.

Method: LoadKAN synergistically combines a feature-isolated temporal attention mechanism (which extracts temporal dynamics from each input feature independently, e.g., historical load and human mobility) with a KAN module for interpretable predictions.

Result: LoadKAN remains highly competitive compared to extensively-tuned, state-of-the-art black-box deep learning benchmarks on datasets from three representative U.S. electricity markets, and its interpretability enables granular analysis of learned non-linear relationships between six distinct mobility patterns and electricity load, revealing complex market-specific dependencies via KAN-learned activation functions.

Conclusion: LoadKAN demonstrates that interpretable forecasting frameworks can achieve competitive performance while generating insights often obscured by opaque black-box neural forecasting models.

Abstract: Accurate electricity load forecasting is a crucial prerequisite for stable power system operations. While prevalent deep learning models present competitive performance, they often operate as black boxes and lack interpretability. While the Kolmogorov-Arnold network (KAN) has emerged as a promising alternative because of its learnable activation function design, its direct application to time-series forecasting faces challenges in modeling complex temporal data patterns. Also, simple integration into existing architectures, such as serving as replacement of neural modules, cannot fully leverage KAN's interpretability strengths. To address these gaps, this study develops LoadKAN, a novel hybrid and interpretable framework for load forecasting that synergistically combines a specifically-designed feature-isolated temporal attention mechanism with a KAN module. The attention stage aims to extract temporal dynamics from each input feature independently, such as historical load and human mobility, providing distilled feature representations to the KAN module for interpretable predictions. When evaluated on datasets from three representative U.S. electricity markets, our LoadKAN remains highly competitive when compared to extensively-tuned, state-of-the-art, black-box deep learning benchmarks. More importantly, LoadKAN's interpretability enables a granular analysis of the learned non-linear relationships between six distinct mobility patterns and electricity load. Through KAN-learned activation functions, our quantitative sensitivity analyses on mobility features reveal complex and market-specific dependencies. These findings further demonstrate the ability of our LoadKAN to generate insights often obscured by opaque black-box neural forecasting models.

</details>


### [830] [What Does a Chemical Language Model Know About Molecules?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23443)
*Christian Kenneth,Etowah Adams,Liam Bai,Gerard JP van Westen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper applies sparse autoencoders (SAEs) to MolFormer (an encoder-only chemical language model) to study how molecular representations are built across layers. Early layers track token positions to parse molecular grammar, while later layers encode substructure and pharmacologically relevant features. Non-canonical SMILES cause more disruptive representation shifts than invalid SMILES due to position-latent disruption. The authors introduce InterMol, an interactive visualizer for SAE activations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To mechanistically understand whether chemical language models (cLMs) learn meaningful molecular semantics or merely surface-level syntactic patterns, by examining how molecular representations are constructed across model layers.

Method: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) applied to MolFormer, an encoder-only cLM, to dissect latent representations across layers; development of InterMol, an interactive visualization tool.

Result: Early layers use position-tracking latents for molecular grammar parsing; later layers encode atom-in-substructure and pharmacologically relevant features. Non-canonical SMILES cause stronger representation disruption than invalid SMILES due to position-latent disruption.

Conclusion: cLMs learn meaningful molecular semantics beyond surface syntax, with a layered architecture where early layers handle grammar via position tracking and later layers build chemically relevant features. InterMol supports further exploration of these representations.

Abstract: Chemical language models (cLMs) are widely assumed to learn surface-level syntactic patterns rather than learning meaningful molecular semantics. Here, we apply sparse autoencoders (SAEs) to MolFormer, an encoder-only cLM, to mechanistically examine how molecular representations are built across layers. We discover that early layers rely on position-tracking latents to parse molecular grammar, while later layers encode atom-in-substructure and pharmacologically relevant features. Additionally, we show that non-canonical SMILES produce more disruptive representation shifts than invalid SMILES, driven by position-latent disruption propagating across layers. To support further exploration, we develop InterMol, an interactive visualizer for SAE activations on molecular strings and structures.

</details>


### [831] [Selective Time Series Forecasting via Metalearning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23448)
*Ricardo Inácio,Vitor Cerqueira,Marília Barandas,Carlos Soares*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a selective forecasting framework with reject options, using metalearning to model empirical percentiles of forecasting errors based on structural features from recent lags, enabling abstention transfer across heterogeneous time series.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep learning forecasting accuracy varies across samples, yet existing reject option mechanisms are underexplored in forecasting. Current abstention strategies rely on domain-specific proxies (e.g., prediction intervals, learned confidence scores) that fail to generalize across domains.

Method: The framework models the empirical percentile of forecasting errors (a scale-invariant statistic) using structural characteristics from recent lags via metalearning. The rejection decision is decoupled from the forecast itself and grounded in domain-agnostic features, enabling transfer across heterogeneous time series.

Result: In both in-domain and transfer learning settings, rejecting samples predicted as challenging consistently improves forecasting accuracy across coverage levels.

Conclusion: The proposed selective forecasting framework effectively enables abstention transfer across heterogeneous time series, overcoming generalization limitations of existing proxy-based rejection methods.

Abstract: Deep learning methods have achieved state-of-the-art in time series forecasting, yet their accuracy varies considerably across samples, as some instances remain inherently difficult to predict. Reject option mechanisms, which allow models to abstain from high-risk predictions, are well established in classification and regression but underexplored in forecasting. Existing abstention strategies typically rely on proxies, such as the width of the prediction interval or learned confidence scores derived from forecasts. However, these approaches are inherently tied to the training domain, limiting their ability to generalize. We propose a selective forecasting framework that addresses this limitation by modeling the empirical percentile of forecasting errors, that is, a scale-invariant statistic, based on structural characteristics extracted from recent lags via metalearning. By decoupling the rejection decision from the forecast itself and grounding it in domain-agnostic features, the framework enables effective abstention transfer across heterogeneous time series. Experiments in both in-domain and transfer learning settings show that rejecting samples predicted as challenging consistently improves forecasting accuracy across coverage levels.

</details>


### [832] [Do Location Encoders Capture Spatial Effects? A GeoShapley Benchmark Across Scales](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23453)
*Daniel Kiv,Shaowen Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper benchmarks GeoShapley's ability to recover spatially varying coefficients from location-encoder embeddings across 11 encoders, 3 scales, and multiple conditions, finding high recovery for primary coefficients but scale-dependent recovery for secondary ones, with raw coordinates remaining competitive.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine how well location-encoder embeddings from models can capture interpretable spatial effects, specifically whether GeoShapley can recover known spatially varying coefficients.

Method: Benchmarked 11 location encoders from TorchSpatial across 3 scales (grid, county, global), with/without raw coordinates, under untrained and contrastively trained conditions, using GeoShapley to recover synthetic coefficients and measuring recovery via correlation.

Result: Primary coefficient recovery is consistently high across encoders; secondary coefficient recovery is scale-dependent, with most variation at global scale; raw-coordinate baseline remains competitive throughout.

Conclusion: GeoShapley can recover spatially varying coefficients from location-encoder embeddings, but performance varies with scale and coefficient importance; simpler raw-coordinate baselines are still competitive.

Abstract: Location encoders transform geographic coordinates into high dimensional embeddings for downstream machine learning, but it is unclear how well these representations capture interpretable spatial effects. We benchmark whether GeoShapley, a game-theoretic explainer that treats all location features as a single joint player, can recover spatially varying coefficients from models built on location-encoder embeddings. Eleven encoders from the TorchSpatial framework are evaluated against a synthetic process with known coefficients, across three scales (grid, county, global), with and without raw coordinates alongside the embedding, and under untrained and contrastively trained conditions. Measuring recovery as the correlation between estimated and true coefficients, we report how it varies with scale and encoder architecture and compare the embeddings against a raw-coordinate baseline. Recovery of the primary coefficient is consistently high across encoders, whereas recovery of a secondary coefficient is more scale-dependent, differing most at the global scale; the raw-coordinate baseline remains competitive throughout.

</details>


### [833] [TROPT: An Open Framework for Unifying and Advancing Discrete Text Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23496)
*Matan Ben-Tov,Mahmood Sharif*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TROPT is the first open-source framework unifying discrete text-trigger optimization, featuring 30+ recipes, 15+ optimizers, and 15+ losses across domains like jailbreaking and model probing, lowering the barrier for adoption and advancement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing discrete text-trigger optimizers are scattered across research codebases tied to specific models, objectives, and domains; variants proliferate with high engineering overhead, making them hard to use, extend, or compare head-to-head.

Method: TROPT provides a unified open-source framework with a single interface that standardizes execution and development, allowing customizable end-to-end optimization recipes by swapping models, objectives, and optimizers; it ships with 30+ recipes built from 15+ optimizers and 15+ losses.

Result: Demonstrates utility via controlled large-scale experiments comparing LLM jailbreak strategies (revealing potent underadopted techniques) and porting optimizers across domains (e.g., from LLM jailbreaking to corpus-poisoning embedding models).

Conclusion: TROPT significantly lowers the barrier to adopting and advancing discrete text optimization across diverse domains and applications.

Abstract: Discrete text-trigger optimization -- searching for text sequences that, when ingested by a model, steer it toward a specified objective -- underpins model red-teaming (e.g., LLM jailbreaks), as well as auditing and interpretability. However, the current state of discrete optimizers hinders their adoption and progress. First, existing optimizers, when open-sourced at all, are scattered across research codebases tied to specific models, objectives, and problem domains. Second, optimizer variants proliferate, each requiring engineering overhead to use or extend, and remaining hard to compare head-to-head. Together, these raise the bar for adopting optimizers in existing or new domains, and for advancing them via new strategies. We address these gaps with TROPT, the first open-source framework that unifies discrete optimizers' execution and standardizes their development under a single interface. TROPT makes it easy to customize end-to-end optimization recipes by swapping any component -- models, objectives, and optimizers -- extending its reach across domains and new applications. TROPT currently ships with 30+ optimization recipes -- covering applications such as jailbreaking and probing model internals -- built from 15+ optimizers (spanning white-box to black-box access) and 15+ losses, from foundational to state-of-the-art methods. Demonstrating its utility, we leverage TROPT in several studies: (i) controlled, large-scale experiments comparing and enhancing optimization strategies for LLM jailbreaks, revealing potent-yet-underadopted techniques; and (ii) porting optimizers from one domain (e.g., LLM jailbreak) to new domains (e.g., corpus-poisoning embedding model). In all, TROPT significantly lowers the barrier to adopting and advancing discrete text optimization.

</details>


### [834] [Collapsed Effective Operators for Higher-order Structures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23517)
*Maximilian Krahn,Lennart Bastian,Vikas Garg,Björn Schuller,Tolga Birdal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces Collapsed Effective Operators, a method that condenses higher-order topological structures into a single vertex-level operator via Schur complementation of a graded Laplacian, enabling improved spectral clustering, signal smoothing, and topological positional encoding for neural networks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing spectral operators for higher-order structures decompose topology into separate ranks, forcing practitioners to use ad hoc methods to fuse information back to vertices. The authors aim to provide a principled, unified vertex-level operator that encodes higher-order interactions.

Method: The authors collapse higher-order degrees of freedom into a single vertex-level operator by performing Schur complementation on a graded Laplacian, producing a (generally dense) operator that encodes long-range topological interactions while preserving positive semi-definiteness and maintaining a spectral upper bound relative to the rank-0 Hodge Laplacian.

Result: The operator preserves positive semi-definiteness, has a spectral upper bound relative to the rank-0 Hodge Laplacian, effectively lowers system energy under higher-order connectivity, and empirically improves spectral clustering, signal smoothing, and enables topological positional encoding in neural networks.

Conclusion: Collapsed Effective Operators provide a principled way to fuse higher-order topological information into a vertex-level operator, offering practical improvements in spectral clustering, signal smoothing, and neural network architectures through topological positional encoding.

Abstract: Higher-order structures are powerful relational modeling tools, yet existing spectral operators decompose the topology into separate ranks, leaving practitioners to fuse the information back to vertices through ad hoc choices. We introduce Collapsed Effective Operators, which condense higher-order degrees of freedom into a single vertex-level operator via Schur complementation of a graded Laplacian. This yields a (generally dense) operator that encodes long-range interactions mediated by topology and is applicable to arbitrary higher-order constructs. We show it preserves positive semi-definiteness with a spectral upper bound relative to the rank-0 Hodge Laplacian, effectively lowering system energy under higher-order connectivity. Empirically, our operator improves spectral clustering, signal smoothing, and enables the inclusion of topological features in neural network architectures via positional encoding. The project page can be found http://circle-group.github.io/research/CollapsedEffectiveOperators

</details>


### [835] [Simulation-Free Estimation of Traffic Flows from Sparse Count Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23536)
*Davide Guastella,Gianluca Bontempi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A method to estimate time-varying traffic flows from sparse aggregated vehicle counts using spatial partitioning, route enumeration, and weighted least-squares optimization, achieving accurate daily profiles at low computational cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To estimate time-varying traffic flow patterns from sparse aggregated vehicle counts, where direct full observation is unavailable.

Method: Partitions the study area into spatial regions, constructs feasible region-to-region routes, solves a weighted least-squares optimization to allocate vehicle counts per route, uses a weighted contribution matrix for sensor coverage, and derives edge-level trajectories by scoring routes against temporal and volumetric sensor profiles.

Result: Reproduces daily traffic profiles from input data and outperforms baseline methods at a fraction of the computational cost, evaluated on the Brussels road network with real and synthetic data.

Conclusion: The proposed approach is computationally efficient and accurate for estimating traffic flows from sparse aggregated counts.

Abstract: We propose a method for estimating time-varying traffic flow patterns from sparse aggregated vehicle counts. The method partitions the study area into spatial regions, constructs a set of feasible region-to-region routes, and solves a weighted least-squares optimization problem to determine the number of vehicles to allocate on each route. A weighted contribution matrix encodes sensor coverage, steering the optimizer toward flow configurations that are directly observable by sensors. Edge-level trajectories are then derived by scoring candidate routes against the temporal and volumetric profiles of aggregated regional sensor counts.
  The method is evaluated on the Brussels road network using real and synthetic traffic data. Results show that the proposed approach reproduces the daily traffic profile in the input data and outperforms the baseline methods at a fraction of the computational cost.

</details>


### [836] [The Energy Consumption of Transformer Fine-Tuning: A Roofline-Inspired Scaling Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23546)
*Mansour Zoubeirou a Mayaki*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A framework for modeling and predicting energy consumption of Transformer training across multiple GPUs, using lightweight proxies and a scaling law that accounts for parallelism strategies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As Transformer training scales in model size and parallelism, computational and energy costs grow rapidly. Accurately predicting energy consumption is critical for sustainable and cost-aware system design.

Method: The authors conduct controlled architectural sweeps of BERT models, relating measured energy to proxies for compute, memory traffic, and hardware efficiency. They incorporate a roofline-inspired speedup-based hardware-efficiency factor that captures tensor parallelism and fully sharded data parallelism, then derive a scaling law model.

Result: The proposed scaling law model accurately predicts training energy across heterogeneous configurations.

Conclusion: Energy consumption of Transformer training can be effectively modeled and predicted using lightweight proxies and a scaling law that accounts for parallelism strategies, enabling sustainable and cost-aware system design.

Abstract: Transformer-based models underpin modern natural language processing but incur rapidly growing computational and energy costs. As training scales in both model size and parallelism, accurately predicting energy consumption has become critical for sustainable and cost-aware system design. We present a framework for modeling the energy consumption of Transformer training on multiple GPUs. Using controlled architectural sweeps of BERT models, we relate measured energy to lightweight proxies for compute, memory traffic, and hardware efficiency. Inspired by roofline models, our approach incorporates a speedup-based hardware-efficiency factor that captures the effects of tensor parallelism and fully sharded data parallelism. We derive a scaling law model that accurately predicts training energy across heterogeneous configurations.

</details>


### [837] [Scheduling Thoughts: Learning the Order of Thought in Diffusion Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23567)
*Jiawei Xu,Minghui Liu,Aakriti Agrawal,Yifan Chen,Furong Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SAS (Self-Aware Scheduling) learns an optimal unmasking order policy for masked diffusion language models via a tractable KL divergence bound, improving decoding quality beyond heuristic schedules.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The unmasking order in masked diffusion models critically affects generation quality but is typically chosen heuristically; the authors aim to formalize and learn optimal order selection.

Method: Derives a tractable upper bound on the sequential decoding mismatch (KL divergence) in terms of pathwise log-likelihood, which enables order selection as a policy optimization problem. SAS instantiates this by learning a lightweight order policy via Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with a frozen denoiser, applicable to any-order and semi-autoregressive decoding.

Result: On Sudoku with 1B MDM: accuracy improved from 82.0% (best heuristic) to 91.8%, and 97.5% with fine-tuning. On GSM8K with LLaDA-8B: pass@1 from 64% to 76%; MBPP: from 39.5% to 41%.

Conclusion: SAS provides a principled framework for learning unmasking schedules that consistently matches or exceeds heuristic schedules across diverse tasks, generation lengths, and block sizes.

Abstract: Masked diffusion language models decode by iteratively unmasking tokens, where the unmasking order defines an "order of thought" that strongly influences generation quality yet is typically chosen heuristically. We derive a tractable upper bound on the sequential decoding mismatch, measured by the Kullback-Leibler divergence and expressed in terms of the model's pathwise log-likelihood, with tightness under sufficient model expressivity. This bound induces a dense self-aware reward over ordered trajectories, casting order selection as a principled policy optimization problem with a frozen denoiser. We instantiate this idea as Self-Aware Scheduling (SAS), which learns a lightweight order policy using Group Relative Policy Optimization and applies seamlessly to both any-order and semi-autoregressive decoding. On Sudoku with 1B MDM, SAS improves puzzle accuracy from 82.0% (best heuristic schedule) to 91.8%, and reaches 97.5% with second-stage fine-tuning along learned trajectories. On mathematical reasoning with LLaDA-8B, SAS improves pass@1 on GSM8K from 64% to 76% and on MBPP from 39.5% to 41%, consistently matching or exceeding heuristic schedules across generation lengths and block sizes. Project page: https://jimmyxu123.github.io/SAS

</details>


### [838] [SVD-Surgeon: Optimal Singular-Value Surgery for Large Language Model Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23568)
*Mahmoud Safari,Frank Hutter*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SVD-Surgeon is a training-free method that applies the Optimal Brain Surgeon framework to singular-value decomposition for LLM compression, improving perplexity-compression trade-offs by computing closed-form updates to retained singular values when pruning others.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs achieve strong performance but are limited by memory and compute requirements during deployment. Existing low-rank compression via SVD focuses on how to factorize and which components to keep, but does not optimally adjust the retained singular values after truncation.

Method: The authors introduce SVD-Surgeon, which treats each singular value as a parameter and applies the Optimal Brain Surgeon (OBS) framework to compute a closed-form update of retained singular values that compensates (to second order in model loss) for those removed by truncation. It also derives a saliency metric for choosing which singular values to prune.

Result: Applied on top of SVD-LLM, SVD-Surgeon improves the perplexity-compression trade-off on OPT family models and LLaMA 2-7B without any retraining.

Conclusion: SVD-Surgeon is a lightweight, plug-and-play enhancement for existing SVD-based LLM compression methods that optimally adjusts retained singular values to compensate for pruning, yielding better compression quality without additional training.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) achieve remarkable performance across a wide range of tasks, but their deployment is constrained by substantial memory and compute requirements. Low-rank compression via singular value decomposition (SVD) is an effective remedy, but existing methods focus on how to factorize and which components to keep. We introduce SVD-Surgeon, a training-free method that brings the Optimal Brain Surgeon (OBS) framework to the singular-value basis. Treating each singular value as a parameter, it computes a closed-form update of the retained singular values that compensates, to second order in the model loss, for those removed by truncation. The same analysis yields a saliency for choosing which values to prune. As it operates directly on the singular-value factorization, SVD-Surgeon can be layered on top of existing SVD compressors. Applied to SVD-LLM, a leading SVD-based method, it improves the perplexity-compression trade-off on the OPT family and LLaMA 2-7B without any retraining.

</details>


### [839] [Patient-Aware Contrastive Learning Preserves Per-Patient Structure in RR-Interval Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23570)
*Yasantha Niroshana,Weijith Wimalasiri,Chathuranga Hettiarachchi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a patient-aware contrastive learning objective for Paroxysmal Atrial Fibrillation detection from RR-interval sequences, which preserves per-patient sinus rhythm structure by forming positive pairs from same-patient, same-class segments. This achieves superior cross-patient generalization (AUROC 0.989) compared to supervised contrastive learning and BCE, demonstrating that per-subject geometric consistency is more important than global class separability for robust generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard contrastive representation learning struggles on physiological signals due to distinct per-patient baseline patterns; class-level objectives merge per-subject structure into per-class clusters, removing individual variation needed for generalization to unseen patients.

Method: A patient-aware contrastive objective that forms positive pairs only from same-patient, same-class segments, preserving each patient's own sinus rhythm baseline while still separating classes.

Result: Achieves per-patient SR cohesion of 0.850 (vs. 0.800 SupCon, 0.772 BCE); patient-independent AUROC of 0.989 ± 0.003 with 2.6× lower seed variance than supervised contrastive baselines on IRIDIA-AF dataset.

Conclusion: Per-subject geometric consistency, rather than global class separability, is key to robust cross-patient generalization.

Abstract: Contrastive representation learning struggles on physiological signals when each subject contributes a distinct baseline pattern. If class differences overlap with subject differences,class-level objectives such as supervised contrastive learning tend to merge per-subject structure into a single per-class cluster,removing the individual variation that a model needs to generalize to unseen patients. We study this problem in the setting of Paroxysmal Atrial Fibrillation(PAF) detection from RR-interval(RRI) sequences and propose a patient-aware contrastive objective that forms positive pairs only from same-patient, same-class segments, preserving each patient's own sinus rhythm(SR) baseline while still pushing the two classes apart. Examining the learned embeddings directly, our objective achieves the most consistent per-patient SR structure (cohesion $0.850$ vs. $0.800$ for supervised contrastive loss (SupCon) and $0.772$ for binary cross-entropy (BCE)). We also identify that BCE produces the cleanest global class separation yet the most disordered per-patient structure. This is precisely why a linear probe trained on its features breaks down on unseen patients. On the IRIDIA-AF dataset, the resulting representation reaches a patient-independent Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUROC) of $0.989 \pm 0.003$ with $2.6\times$ lower seed variance than supervised contrastive baselines.These results highlight that per-subject geometric consistency, rather than global class separability, is key to robust cross-patient generalization.

</details>


### [840] [A Spectral Theory of Normalized Corrected GNN Propagation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23572)
*Qihan Chen,Wei Li,Meng Qin,Jianfeng Hou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper develops a spectral theory for normalized corrected GNN propagation, proving that the corrected operator preserves class-discriminative signal after many layers. The main result is a high-probability exact-recovery theorem for binary CSBM after O(log n) propagation steps under dense polylogarithmic regimes, along with a multi-class partial recovery theorem.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a theoretical understanding of whether normalized corrected GNN propagation (with degree-stationary component removed) can preserve class-discriminative signal across many propagation layers, addressing the oversmoothing problem in GCN-style models.

Method: Spectral theory analysis of the symmetric normalized adjacency matrix with its degree-stationary component removed, analyzed under the Contextual Stochastic Block Model (CSBM) with high-probability recovery guarantees.

Result: Exact-recovery theorem for binary CSBM after k=O(log n) propagation steps in the dense polylogarithmic regime (p ≥ C log^B n / n, B>4); multi-class partial recovery theorem showing contraction toward class centers for most nodes; empirical validation on synthetic and real data.

Conclusion: The corrected normalized propagation operator preserves discriminative signal under explicit graph-signal and feature-SNR conditions, enabling deep GNN propagation without catastrophic oversmoothing.

Abstract: We develop a spectral theory for \emph{normalized corrected GNN propagation}. The object of study is the symmetric normalized adjacency with its degree-stationary component removed, matching the normalization used by standard GCN-style models while isolating the stationary direction most directly tied to oversmoothing. The central theoretical question is whether this corrected normalized operator preserves class-discriminative signal after many propagation layers. Our main result is a high-probability exact-recovery theorem for the binary Contextual Stochastic Block Model after \(k=O(\log n)\) propagation steps in the dense polylogarithmic regime \(p\ge C\log^B n/n\), for any fixed \(B>4\), under explicit graph-signal and feature-SNR conditions. We also establish a multi-class partial recovery theorem showing contraction toward class centers for most nodes. Synthetic and real node-classification experiments are included as empirical checks of the theory's predicted dependence on depth, graph signal, and feature noise.

</details>


### [841] [Solve for the Hyperparameter, Skip the Search: Kolmogorov-Optimal Scaling Laws for Spline Regression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23575)
*Yong Yi Bay,Kathleen A. Yearick*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces KORE, a method that replaces exhaustive grid search for hyperparameter tuning in spline regression with a closed-form analytical solution for optimal resolution, using classical approximation theory and ANOVA decomposition to achieve comparable accuracy with ~8x fewer model fits across low-dimensional interaction structures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hyperparameter tuning (specifically resolution selection in spline regression) is traditionally done via expensive grid search with cross-validation. The authors aim to eliminate this computational overhead by deriving a closed-form solution that achieves the same accuracy.

Method: Three key ingredients: (1) classical approximation theory links squared bias to a known power of resolution G via Kolmogorov n-width; (2) basis dimension is an explicit polynomial in G; (3) leave-one-out error from a single fit via PRESS identity. Extends to multi-dimensions via ANOVA decomposition where interaction order replaces ambient dimension. KORE algorithm: fits two pilot resolutions, solves a leverage-calibrated 2x2 system for bias/noise scales, evaluates closed-form plug-in resolution with a leave-one-out certificate.

Result: KORE matches exhaustive 3-fold CV and classical criteria (GCV, Cp, AIC, BIC) while fitting ~8x fewer models. On 36 real tabular datasets, ranks first among 21 methods in accuracy per compute, ahead of tuned boosters and kernel machines.

Conclusion: When complexity resides in low interaction order, solving analytically for resolution is superior to searching for it, both computationally and in accuracy.

Abstract: Hyperparameter tuning almost always means search: fit the model at every value on a grid, score each by cross-validation, and keep the winner. For spline regression that search is unnecessary. The optimal resolution can be solved for in closed form, to the accuracy an exhaustive search reaches, at a fraction of the compute. Three ingredients make this possible: classical approximation theory pins the squared bias to a known power of the resolution G, exactly the Kolmogorov n-width of the smoothness class; the basis dimension is an explicit polynomial in G; and leave-one-out error follows from a single fit via the PRESS identity. Balancing the two known curves gives the minimizer analytically. We extend this calculus to many coordinates by replacing ambient input dimension with interaction order, the number of active low-order components in an ANOVA decomposition, yielding a scaling law in which the optimal resolution and error are power functions of the effective density (sample size per active component), with input dimension absent from the exponent. The law becomes an algorithm. KORE (Kolmogorov-optimal Order-aware Resolution Estimation) fits two pilot resolutions, solves a leverage-calibrated 2x2 system for the bias and noise scales, and evaluates the closed-form plug-in resolution with a tiny leave-one-out certificate: about a dozen fits instead of a full grid sweep, with a consistency guarantee as the sample grows. Across additive and sparse pairwise targets up to 80 input dimensions, KORE matches exhaustive 3-fold cross-validation and the full classical ladder (GCV, Mallows' Cp, AIC, BIC) while fitting roughly 8x fewer models; on 36 real tabular datasets it ranks first among 21 methods in accuracy per unit of compute, ahead of tuned boosters and kernel machines. When complexity lives in low interaction order, solving for the resolution beats searching for it.

</details>


### [842] [It's Much Easier for Neural Networks to learn Game of Life Dynamics with the Right Activation Function: Polynomial Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23587)
*Tashin Ahmed,Q. Tyrell Davis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper shows that minimal neural networks can learn Conway's Game of Life by using appropriate activation functions (especially 2nd degree polynomial), challenging the assumption that large networks are necessary. The authors argue that matching inductive biases to the task is more important than scaling up network size.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a known gap between the large networks that reliably learn Conway's Game of Life and minimal networks that can represent it with hard-coded parameters. The authors aim to bridge this gap by reframing learning as a learning problem rather than a search problem, hypothesizing that proper inductive biases enable minimal networks to succeed.

Method: The authors experiment with different activation functions (including ReLU and 2nd degree polynomial) in minimal network variants, testing whether they can learn Game of Life dynamics with and without learning neural weights.

Result: Alternative activation functions, particularly 2nd degree polynomial, meaningfully outperform ReLU in learning Life dynamics consistently, even without weight learning.

Conclusion: Matching inductive biases to the task is more effective than defaulting to large networks. Cellular automata are advocated as simple testbeds for developing strategies beneficial to physics-based deep learning and interpretable machine learning.

Abstract: Previous work has found a gap between the scale of neural networks that reliably learn Conway's Game of Life, and minimal networks capable of representing the classic cellular automaton with hard-coded parameter values. Viewing neural network learning as a search process suggests a dependence on networks large enough to contain sub-networks with lucky initializations (sometimes known as 'winning tickets') that actually learn the task. In this work, we reorient our perspective from discovering Life rules as a search problem back to a learning problem, and reason that with fitting inductive biases, the problem should be much more amenable to minimal networks. We find that network variants with several alternative activation functions meaningfully outperform the default choice of Rectified Linear Units, and in particular, that a 2nd degree polynomial activation function consistently learns Life dynamics with or without the benefit of learning neural weights. Our results provide an informative demonstration of the benefits of matching learning to the task at hand and challenge the easy default choice of scale for all problems. In particular, we advocate for the use of cellular automata as simple test domains for developing strategies that can benefit machine learning for science, physics-based deep learning, and interpretable machine learning.

</details>


### [843] [Quantifying the Agreement Between Data-Influence and Data-Similarity to Understand LLM Behavior](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23591)
*Christopher J. Anders,Henrique Da Silva Gameiro,Nico Daheim,Mohammad Emtiyaz Khan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper compares data-similarity and data-influence measures for tracing LLM outputs back to training data, finding significant ranking agreement but an asymmetry: data-similarity's top documents are ranked more consistently by data-influence than vice versa. This asymmetry is exploited for a cost-accuracy trade-off.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite existing comparisons between data-similarity and data-influence for ground-truth tasks, no systematic comparison exists for the specific downstream task of output tracing (linking model outputs back to training data). The paper aims to fill this gap.

Method: The authors rank training documents by each measure (data-similarity and data-influence), then compute the overlap between the two rankings. Experiments are conducted across five models: OLMo2-1B, Qwen3-1.7B, LlaMa3.2-1B, Gemma3-1B, and GPT2.

Result: The two rankings agree significantly, but an asymmetry exists: data-similarity's top documents receive more consistent ranks from data-influence than the reverse. This asymmetry allows using costly data-influence to refine cheaper data-similarity results, achieving a favorable cost-accuracy trade-off.

Conclusion: The asymmetry between the two measures can be exploited to improve efficiency in output tracing, combining the low cost of data-similarity with the higher accuracy of data-influence.

Abstract: One way to understand LLM behavior is to trace its output back to the training data. Two types of measures are commonly used for output tracing: data-similarity and data-influence. The former is cheaper while the latter is believed to be more accurate. Even though many works have compared them for ground-truth tasks, no such comparisons exist for output tracing. Here, we fill this gap and precisely quantify the commonalities and differences between the two measures. We do this by first ranking the training documents according to each measure and then computing the overlap between the two rankings. Our main finding is that the two rankings agree significantly, but there is an asymmetry between them: The top documents of data-similarity are assigned more consistent ranks by data-influence than the other way around. This result is valid across a range of experiments involving OLMo2-1B, Qwen3-1.7B, LlaMa3.2-1B, Gemma3-1B, and GPT2. We exploit the asymmetry to obtain a favorable cost-accuracy trade-off by using the costly data-influence to refine the results of data-similarity.

</details>


### [844] [MORL-A2C: Multi-Objective Reinforcement Learning Reranker for Optimizing Healthiness in MOPI-HFRS](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23603)
*Aarya Vasantlal,Joshua Zolla*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MORL-A2C extends the MOPI-HFRS food recommendation system by introducing sequential decision-making via Advantage Actor-Critic to optimize the health-preference trade-off, achieving substantial health alignment gains (H-Score@20 from 46.05% to 69.57%) with modest ranking quality degradation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing food recommendation systems prioritize user preference over nutritional health, and prior multi-objective approaches (like MOPI-HFRS) use static per-step tradeoffs that fail to capture the sequential nature of dietary decision-making.

Method: MORL-A2C formulates recommendation as a K-step reranking problem using an Advantage Actor-Critic (A2C) algorithm with a scalarized relevance/health reward, leveraging frozen GNN embeddings and warm-starting via behavior cloning against a dot-product ranker. A bug in the MOPI-HFRS evaluation pipeline was also identified and corrected.

Result: On macro-nutrient benchmark: Recall@20 dropped from 25.64% to 23.61%, NDCG@20 from 23.52% to 20.64%, while H-Score@20 improved from 46.05% to 69.57%. Similar trends on full-nutrient benchmark.

Conclusion: Policy-driven sequential optimization effectively navigates the health-preference trade-off in multi-objective food recommendation, validating the approach's effectiveness.

Abstract: Unhealthy dietary behavior continues to be a persistent public health issue in the United States, exacerbated by recommendation systems that prioritize user preference without considering nutritional health. The Multi-Objective Personalized Interpretable Health-aware Food Recommendation System (MOPI-HFRS), from which this work extends, addresses this by jointly optimizing preference, health, and diversity through Pareto-based optimization. However, this approach relies on static, per-step tradeoff solutions that fail to capture the sequential nature of dietary decision-making. We introduce MORL-A2C, a sequential decision-making extension to MOPI-HFRS targeting the health-preference axis. Leveraging frozen GNN embeddings, MORL-A2C formulates recommendation as a K-step reranking problem using an Advantage Actor-Critic algorithm with a scalarized relevance/health reward. The policy is warm-started via behavior cloning against a dot-product ranker derived from frozen embeddings. We also identify and correct a non-trivial bug in the MOPI-HFRS evaluation pipeline that understated baseline performance; all results are reported against the corrected baseline. On the macro-nutrient benchmark, MORL-A2C achieves a modest reduction in ranking quality (Recall@20: 25.64% to 23.61%, NDCG@20: 23.52% to 20.64%) in exchange for a substantial improvement in health alignment (H-Score@20: 46.05% to 69.57%), with consistent trends on the full-nutrient benchmark. These findings validate that policy-driven sequential optimization can effectively navigate the health-preference trade-off in multi-objective food recommendation.

</details>


### [845] [Scaling Linear Mode Connectivity and Merging to Billion Parameter Pretrained Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23607)
*Tianyi Li,Zhiqiang Shen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A scalable dual-learning framework (Dual-Learned Matching) enables near-barrier-free linear mode connectivity and merging for billion-parameter pretrained transformers by jointly learning functionality-preserving weight transformations from both model endpoints toward a shared linear interpolation path.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LMC methods optimize the interpolation path from only one model endpoint, limiting scalability and effectiveness for large pretrained transformers.

Method: Applies properly parameterized functionality-preserving weight transformations to align functionally equivalent solutions, with a dual learning procedure where both models jointly learn transformations toward a shared linear interpolation path.

Result: Near-zero loss barriers on WikiText for medium-sized language models; ViT-L maintains >69% ImageNet top-1 accuracy throughout interpolation; billion-parameter LLMs exhibit only small loss barriers.

Conclusion: Properly resolving parameter symmetries enables large pretrained Transformers to be connected and merged through simple linear paths with substantially improved interpolation performance.

Abstract: Linear mode connectivity (LMC) provides a promising foundation for understanding and merging independently trained neural networks, but existing methods typically optimize the interpolation path from only one model endpoint, limiting their scalability and effectiveness for large pretrained transformers. We propose a novel and scalable framework for enabling LMC-based model merging to {\em billion-parameter pretrained transformers}. Our method applies properly parameterized functionality-preserving weight transformations to align functionally equivalent solutions, and introduces a dual learning procedure in which both models jointly learn their corresponding transformations toward a shared linear interpolation path. This bidirectional optimization substantially reduces interpolation barriers and enables more reliable merging across large-scale architectures. Empirically, we show that our approach achieves near-zero loss barriers on WikiText for language models with medium-sized parameters, representing, to our knowledge, the first demonstration of near-barrier-free linear connectivity at this scale. In the vision domain, ViT-L maintains above 69\% ImageNet top-1 accuracy throughout the interpolation path, while modern billion-parameter LLMs exhibit only small loss barriers. These results suggest that properly resolving parameter symmetries enables large pretrained Transformers to be connected and merged through simple linear paths with substantially improved interpolation performance. Code: https://github.com/VILA-Lab/Dual-Learned-Matching .

</details>


### [846] [Discovering Latent Groups for Robust Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23609)
*Ankur Garg,Ulrich Aïvodji,Samira Ebrahimi Kahou,Vincent Michalski*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Neural classification trees (NCT) address spurious correlations by encoding subgroup structure in a tree-shaped architecture, routing samples to "easy" or "hard" nodes based on prediction correctness, and using these routes as pseudo-labels iteratively, achieving competitive robustness without subgroup supervision while providing interpretable subgroup isolation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Machine learning models exploit spurious correlations, achieving high average accuracy but failing on underrepresented subgroups. Existing methods adjust parameters using subgroup annotations or inferred pseudo-labels but provide no insight into a sample's latent subgroup at inference.

Method: Proposes neural classification trees (NCT) that encode subgroup structure in a tree-shaped architecture. Routes each sample to an "easy" or "hard" node based on prediction correctness, reuses these routes as pseudo-labels for the next iteration, disentangling conflicting subgroups without requiring subgroup supervision.

Result: Evaluated on five benchmarks spanning binary and multi-class spurious correlations. Learned tree topology consistently isolates minority subgroups, provides interpretability with transparent mapping between model architecture and data's latent group structure, and yields competitive robustness with state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: NCT achieves robustness to spurious correlations while offering interpretability through subgroup structure encoded in the tree architecture, all without requiring subgroup annotations.

Abstract: Machine learning models exploit spurious correlations, achieving high average accuracy but failing disproportionately on underrepresented subgroups. Existing methods address this by adjusting network parameters, guided either by subgroup annotations or inferred pseudo-group labels. Yet at inference, these methods produce only a class prediction, with no insight into a sample's latent subgroup. We propose neural classification trees (NCT), a framework that achieves robustness by encoding subgroup structure in its tree-shaped architecture. By routing each sample to an "easy" or "hard" node of this tree -- based on prediction correctness -- and reusing these routes as pseudo-labels for the next iteration, NCT disentangles conflicting subgroups, without requiring subgroup supervision. We evaluate NCT on five benchmarks spanning binary and multi-class spurious correlations. Our experiments show that the learned tree topology provides strong interpretability by consistently isolating minority subgroups, which provides a transparent mapping between the model architecture and the data's latent group structure, while yielding competitive robustness with state-of-the-art methods.

</details>


### [847] [DiT-Reward: Generative Representations for Text-to-Image Reward Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23626)
*Yuanming Yang,Guoqing Ma,Bo Wang,Yuan Zhang,Wei Tang,Chenyi Li,Haoyang Huang,Nan Duan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DiT-Reward converts a pretrained text-to-image Diffusion Transformer into a reward model that outperforms HPSv3 across four preference benchmarks (85.6% on HPDv2, 77.6% on HPDv3), achieves 1.65x inference speedup, and improves policy optimization for Stable Diffusion 3.5 Large via Flow-GRPO.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether generative representations learned for image generation can also serve as effective reward models for evaluating generated images, bridging the gap between generation and evaluation.

Method: DiT-Reward converts a pretrained text-to-image Diffusion Transformer into a reward model by processing near-clean image latents and aggregating text-conditioned image representations across transformer layers. It uses a lightweight learned head on top of a frozen generative backbone, with probing across depth to identify optimal layers.

Result: Outperforms HPSv3 on all four preference benchmarks (HPDv2: 85.6%, HPDv3: 77.6%); achieves 1.65x inference speedup with comparable peak memory; shows positive scaling with generative backbone capacity; strongest reward performance in middle-to-late layers with multi-stage combination benefits.

Conclusion: Pretrained generative DiTs provide transferable representations for both reward modeling and policy optimization, demonstrating that generative representations can effectively support evaluation tasks.

Abstract: Can representations learned for image generation also support the evaluation of generated images? We study text-to-image reward prediction as a downstream task of generative representation learning. To this end, we introduce DiT-Reward, which converts a pretrained text-to-image Diffusion Transformer into a reward model by processing near-clean image latents and aggregating text-conditioned image representations across transformer layers. Under the same training data mixture as HPSv3, DiT-Reward outperforms HPSv3 on all four evaluated preference benchmarks, reaching 85.6% on HPDv2 and 77.6% on HPDv3. When the generative backbone is frozen, a lightweight learned head can still extract meaningful preference predictions from its representations. Probing across depth further reveals that downstream reward performance is strongest in the middle-to-late layers and benefits from combining representations across different stages. We also observe consistent positive scaling with generative backbone capacity. Finally, when used to optimize Stable Diffusion 3.5 Large with Flow-GRPO, DiT-Reward outperforms HPSv3 along the matched training trajectory, with particularly clear gains in realism. Direct latent scoring also achieves a 1.65x inference speedup over HPSv3 with comparable peak memory. These results show that pretrained generative DiTs provide transferable representations for reward modeling and policy optimization.

</details>


### [848] [Muown Implicitly Performs Angular Step-size Decay](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23637)
*Florian Hübler,Kai Lion,Antonio Orvieto,Niao He*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces AngularMuown, an improved matrix-aware optimizer that makes the angular step size explicit in Muown, achieving better performance across small to large-scale models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Matrix-aware optimizers like Muon and Muown show strong empirical performance, but Muown's directional update mechanism and step-size behavior are not well understood. The authors aim to explain this theoretically and improve upon it.

Method: The authors show Muown's directional update is equivalent to a Riemannian step on normalized directions, with magnitude modulating angular step size. They decouple this by introducing AngularMuown, which optimizes normalized directions directly with an explicit schedulable angular multiplier separate from radial magnitude updates.

Result: AngularMuown outperforms Muown, currently leading the per-optimizer category of the modded nanoGPT speedrunning competition, and scales effectively to Qwen2-0.5B and 1.1B parameter mixture-of-experts models.

Conclusion: Making the angular step size explicit in Muown yields better performance and stability, and the method scales beyond small models to larger architectures.

Abstract: Matrix-aware optimizers such as Muon and Muown have recently shown strong empirical performance for pre-training Transformers. In particular, Muown separates each weight matrix into row magnitudes and an un-normalized direction variable, updating the former with Adam and the latter with Muon. We show that the directional update of Muown is equivalent to a Riemannian step on the normalized directions, while the magnitude of the un-normalized parameterization only modulates the angular step size. This explains the step-size stability of Muown and suggests making the angular step size explicit. The resulting method, AngularMuown, optimizes directly over the normalized directions and uses a schedulable angular multiplier decoupled from the radial magnitude update.
